title: A Hexanucleotide Repeat Expansion in C9ORF72 Is the Cause of Chromosome 9p21-Linked ALS-FTD
text: The chromosome 9p21 amyotrophic lateral sclerosis-frontotemporal dementia (ALS-FTD) locus contains one of the last major unidentified autosomal-dominant genes underlying these common neurodegenerative diseases. We have previously shown that a founder haplotype, covering the MOBKL2b , IFNK , and C9ORF72 genes, is present in the majority of cases linked to this region. Here we show that there is a large hexanucleotide (GGGGCC) repeat expansion in the first intron of C9ORF72 on the affected haplotype. This repeat expansion segregates perfectly with disease in the Finnish population, underlying 46.0% of familial ALS and 21.1% of sporadic ALS in that population. Taken together with the D90A SOD1 mutation, 87% of familial ALS in Finland is now explained by a simple monogenic cause. The repeat expansion is also present in onethird of familial ALS cases of outbred European descent, making it the most common genetic cause of these fatal neurodegenerative diseases identified to date.
historical_context: In 2011, independent teams led by Rosa Rademakers (DeJesus-Hernandez et al.) and Bryan Traynor (Renton et al.) revealed that a massive hexanucleotide repeat expansion in the non-coding region of the *C9ORF72* gene is the most prevalent genetic driver of both amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) and frontotemporal dementia (FTD). While genetic linkage to chromosome 9p21 was already established, the exact mutation had bypassed standard sequencing due to its highly repetitive nature. This watershed discovery forged a definitive molecular connection between ALS and FTD, fundamentally redefining the clinical and biological approach to these neurodegenerative conditions. It catalyzed a new era of research focused on RNA toxicity, dipeptide repeat proteins, and nucleocytoplasmic transport defects, illuminating entirely new therapeutic avenues for these previously intractable diseases.

title: A Hexanucleotide Repeat Expansion in C9ORF72 Is the Cause of Chromosome 9p21-Linked ALS-FTD
text: Several lines of evidence have implicated a locus on chromosome 9p21 as a major cause of amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS), frontotemporal dementia (FTD), and the overlapping ALS-FTD phenotype. Here we used next-generation sequencing of affected members from chromosome 9p21-linked families, together with targeted analysis of Finnish and outbred European case-control series, to identify the pathogenic lesion. We found a large GGGGCC hexanucleotide repeat expansion within C9ORF72, located upstream of one transcript and within the first intron of another. Repeat-primed PCR confirmed segregation of the expansion with disease in multiple kindreds and demonstrated a strong association with ALS and FTD. The expansion was present in 46.4% of Finnish familial ALS cases, 21.0% of Finnish sporadic ALS cases, 38.1% of European-ancestry familial ALS probands, and 29.3% of Finnish FTD cases, but was absent or rare in population controls. These data establish C9ORF72 repeat expansion as the cause of chromosome 9p21-linked neurodegeneration and provide further evidence that disrupted RNA metabolism contributes to ALS and FTD pathogenesis.

title: A Hexanucleotide Repeat Expansion in C9ORF72 Is the Cause of Chromosome 9p21-Linked ALS-FTD
text: The chromosome 9p21 locus has been repeatedly implicated by genome-wide association and linkage studies as the major genetic contributor to amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) and frontotemporal dementia (FTD), yet the underlying mutation has remained elusive. Using next-generation deep resequencing of the 232 kilobase block of linkage disequilibrium in a Welsh ALS-FTD kindred and a Dutch ALS-FTD kindred, we identified a large GGGGCC hexanucleotide repeat expansion in a noncoding region of C9ORF72. The expansion segregated with disease in five multigenerational families and was confirmed by fluorescence in situ hybridization to be at least 1.5 kilobases in length. Repeat-primed PCR screening showed that the expansion accounts for 46.4% of familial and 21.0% of sporadic ALS in Finland, 38.1% of familial ALS of wider European ancestry, and 29.3% of Finnish FTD cases. Our findings indicate that this expansion is the most common genetic cause of ALS and FTD identified to date, and they implicate aberrant RNA metabolism as a central mechanism of neurodegeneration.

title: A Hexanucleotide Repeat Expansion in C9ORF72 Is the Cause of Chromosome 9p21-Linked ALS-FTD
text: Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) and frontotemporal dementia (FTD) form an overlapping neurodegenerative disease continuum, often characterized by TARDBP/TDP-43 pathology. Linkage analysis and genome-wide association studies recently identified a major locus for ALS and FTD on chromosome 9p21. Here, we used next-generation sequencing in two families showing linkage to this region to identify a large hexanucleotide (GGGGCC) repeat expansion within the noncoding region of the C9ORF72 gene. This massive repeat expansion was found to be the underlying cause of disease in these kindreds. Screening further patient cohorts revealed that this expansion accounts for nearly half of familial ALS cases in Finland and over a third of familial ALS cases in wider European populations. Additionally, the same expansion underlies a substantial proportion of familial FTD cases. Our findings identify a massive C9ORF72 repeat expansion as the most common genetic cause of ALS and FTD, highlighting the role of disrupted RNA metabolism in neurodegeneration.

title: A Hexanucleotide Repeat Expansion in C9ORF72 Is the Cause of Chromosome 9p21-Linked ALS-FTD
text:
Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS, OMIM #105400) is a fatal neurodegenerative disease characterized clinically by progressive paralysis leading to death from respiratory failure, typically within two to three years of symptom onset. ALS is the third most common neurodegenerative disease in the Western world, and there are currently no effective therapies. Approximately 5% of cases are familial in nature, whereas the bulk of patients diagnosed with the disease are classified as sporadic as they appear to occur randomly throughout the population. There is growing recognition, based on clinical, genetic, and epidemiological data, that ALS and frontotemporal dementia (FTD, OMIM#600274) represent an overlapping continuum of disease, characterized pathologically by the presence of TDP-43 positive inclusions throughout the central nervous system.

To date, a number of genes have been discovered as causative for classical familial ALS, namely SOD1 , TARDBP , FUS , OPTN , and VCP. These genes cumulatively account for ~25% of familial cases, indicating that other causative genes remain to be identified. Each new gene implicated in the etiology of ALS or FTD provides fundamental insights into the cellular mechanisms underlying neuron degeneration, as well as facilitating disease modeling and the design and testing of targeted therapeutics; thus, the identification of new genes that cause ALS or FTD is of great significance.

Linkage analysis of kindreds involving multiple cases of ALS, FTD, and ALS-FTD had suggested that there was an important locus for the disease on the short arm of chromosome 9. Using a genome-wide association study (GWAS) approach, we recently reported that this locus on chromosome 9p21 accounted for nearly half of familial ALS and nearly one-quarter of all ALS cases in a cohort of 405 Finnish patients and 497 control samples. This association signal had previously been reported by van Es and colleagues, and a meta-analysis involving 4,312 cases and 8,425 controls confirmed that chromosome 9p21 was the major signal for ALS. A recent GWAS for FTD also identified this locus. Analysis in the Finnish population narrowed the association to a 232 kilobase (kb) block of linkage disequilibrium and allowed the identification of a founder haplotype that increased risk of disease by over 20-fold. The associated haplotype appears to be the same in all European-ancestry populations, and several families previously shown to have genetic linkage to the chromosome 9p21 region also share this risk haplotype.

We have previously identified an ALS-FTD family from the UK and an apparently unrelated ALS-FTD family from the Netherlands that showed positive linkage to the chromosome 9p21 region. Using these families and the Finnish ALS cases that had previously been used to identify the chromosome 9p21 association signal, we undertook a methodical assessment of the region using next-generation sequencing technology in an attempt to identify the genetic lesion responsible for disease.
historical_context: In 2011, independent teams led by Rosa Rademakers (DeJesus-Hernandez et al.) and Bryan Traynor (Renton et al.) revealed that a massive hexanucleotide repeat expansion in the non-coding region of the *C9ORF72* gene is the most prevalent genetic driver of both amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) and frontotemporal dementia (FTD). While genetic linkage to chromosome 9p21 was already established, the exact mutation had bypassed standard sequencing due to its highly repetitive nature. This watershed discovery forged a definitive molecular connection between ALS and FTD, fundamentally redefining the clinical and biological approach to these neurodegenerative conditions. It catalyzed a new era of research focused on RNA toxicity, dipeptide repeat proteins, and nucleocytoplasmic transport defects, illuminating entirely new therapeutic avenues for these previously intractable diseases.

title: A Hexanucleotide Repeat Expansion in C9ORF72 Is the Cause of Chromosome 9p21-Linked ALS-FTD
text:
Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) is a fatal adult-onset neurodegenerative disease characterized by the selective loss of upper and lower motor neurons, leading to progressive paralysis and death, typically within several years of symptom onset. Frontotemporal dementia (FTD) is the second most common cause of early-onset dementia and is characterized clinically by progressive changes in behavior, personality, language, and executive function. Although ALS and FTD have historically been considered distinct clinical entities, there is increasing evidence that they represent overlapping manifestations of a common neurodegenerative disease spectrum. Cognitive and behavioral impairment are frequently observed in ALS patients, motor neuron dysfunction may occur in FTD patients, and some families contain individuals with ALS, FTD, or combined ALS-FTD.

This clinical overlap is mirrored at the pathological and genetic levels. TDP-43-positive neuronal inclusions are present in the majority of ALS cases and in a substantial fraction of FTD cases, and mutations in TARDBP, FUS, VCP, and other genes have been identified in patients with ALS, FTD, or both phenotypes. These discoveries have strengthened the view that disturbed RNA processing and protein homeostasis are central mechanisms in neurodegeneration. However, despite these advances, a large proportion of familial ALS and FTD remains genetically unexplained.

One of the most important unresolved loci for ALS and FTD lies on chromosome 9p21. Linkage studies in multiple large kindreds with autosomal-dominant ALS, FTD, or ALS-FTD identified a shared interval on chromosome 9, and subsequent genome-wide association studies confirmed a strong association between this region and ALS in population-based cohorts. This locus is of particular importance in Finland, where ALS has an unusually high incidence and where a founder haplotype at chromosome 9p21 accounts for a large fraction of familial disease. We previously refined this association signal to a linkage disequilibrium block containing three annotated genes, MOBKL2B, IFNK, and C9ORF72. However, conventional sequencing of coding exons and splice junctions within the region failed to identify a pathogenic mutation, suggesting that the causal lesion might reside in a poorly annotated, noncoding, or structurally complex portion of the locus.

The identification of the chromosome 9p21 mutation has been a major obstacle to understanding the relationship between ALS and FTD. Because the linked families and associated population cohorts strongly implicate this interval, we reasoned that deep sequencing of affected haplotypes, coupled with targeted screening of large case-control series, could reveal the pathogenic variant. Here we report the identification of a massive GGGGCC hexanucleotide repeat expansion within C9ORF72 as the cause of chromosome 9p21-linked ALS-FTD. We further show that this expansion is the most common genetic cause of familial ALS and FTD identified to date, accounting for a remarkably high proportion of disease in the Finnish population and a substantial fraction of familial ALS in outbred European-ancestry cohorts.

While genetic linkage to chromosome 9p21 was already established, the exact mutation had bypassed standard sequencing due to its highly repetitive nature. This watershed discovery forged a definitive molecular connection between ALS and FTD, fundamentally redefining the clinical and biological approach to these neurodegenerative conditions. It catalyzed a new era of research focused on RNA toxicity, dipeptide repeat proteins, and nucleocytoplasmic transport defects, illuminating entirely new therapeutic avenues for these previously intractable diseases.

title: A Hexanucleotide Repeat Expansion in C9ORF72 Is the Cause of Chromosome 9p21-Linked ALS-FTD
text:
Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) is a devastating and uniformly fatal neurodegenerative disease characterized by the progressive loss of upper and lower motor neurons. Frontotemporal dementia (FTD) is the second most common form of early-onset dementia and is clinically characterized by changes in behavior, personality, and language. Although historically considered distinct clinical entities, it is now widely recognized that ALS and FTD share significant clinical, pathological, and genetic overlap, leading to the concept of an ALS-FTD disease spectrum. Up to 50% of ALS patients develop some degree of cognitive or behavioral impairment during the course of their disease, while up to 15% of FTD patients also develop motor neuron dysfunction. Pathologically, the intracellular accumulation of the TAR DNA-binding protein 43 (TDP-43) is the primary hallmark of both sporadic ALS and the most common pathological subtype of FTD, frontotemporal lobar degeneration with TDP-43 inclusions (FTLD-TDP).

Genetically, a substantial proportion of both ALS and FTD is familial, with autosomal dominant inheritance being the most common pattern. Several genes have been identified that can cause either ALS or FTD, including TARDBP, FUS, and VCP. However, the most robust genetic evidence linking the two disorders comes from linkage analysis. Over the past decade, several independent research groups have mapped a major locus for familial ALS, FTD, and mixed ALS-FTD to a 9-11 Mb region on the short arm of chromosome 9 (9p21.1-p21.2). Subsequent genome-wide association studies (GWAS) have also robustly identified this same chromosome 9p21 region as the major genetic susceptibility locus for both sporadic ALS and FTD.

Despite intense sequencing efforts by multiple international consortia, the specific genetic defect underlying the chromosome 9p21 locus has remained elusive. We recently demonstrated that the association signal at chromosome 9p21 in both familial and sporadic ALS cases is driven by a single founder haplotype that is shared across multiple populations of European descent. This risk haplotype spans a 232 kb region encompassing three characterized genes: MOBKL2b, IFNK, and C9ORF72. However, extensive Sanger sequencing of the coding regions, splice sites, and highly conserved non-coding regions within this genomic interval failed to identify any pathogenic mutations. This led us to hypothesize that the causal variant might be a structural variation, a mutation located in a poorly characterized regulatory region, or a sequence change that is difficult to detect using standard sequencing technologies.

In the present study, we employed a combination of targeted enrichment and next-generation deep sequencing to exhaustively analyze the chromosome 9p21 linkage region in affected individuals from two well-characterized chromosome 9p21-linked pedigrees. This comprehensive approach allowed us to identify a massive hexanucleotide (GGGGCC) repeat expansion within the first intron of the C9ORF72 gene. We demonstrate that this expanded repeat completely segregates with disease in multiple families and is highly associated with both familial and sporadic ALS and FTD in the Finnish population and in wider European cohorts. Our findings resolve the long-standing mystery of the chromosome 9p21 locus and identify the most common genetic cause of these fatal neurodegenerative diseases discovered to date.

While genetic linkage to chromosome 9p21 was already established, the exact mutation had bypassed standard sequencing due to its highly repetitive nature. This watershed discovery forged a definitive molecular connection between ALS and FTD, fundamentally redefining the clinical and biological approach to these neurodegenerative conditions. It catalyzed a new era of research focused on RNA toxicity, dipeptide repeat proteins, and nucleocytoplasmic transport defects, illuminating entirely new therapeutic avenues for these previously intractable diseases.

paper_title: A Hexanucleotide Repeat Expansion in C9ORF72 Is the Cause of Chromosome 9p21-Linked ALS-FTD - 2011 - Neuron
display_title: A Hexanucleotide Repeat Expansion in C9ORF72 Is the Cause of Chromosome 9p21-Linked ALS-FTD
title: A Hexanucleotide Repeat Expansion in C9ORF72 Is the Cause of Chromosome 9p21-Linked ALS-FTD
text:
In this paper, we used next-generation sequencing technology to identify a hexanucleotide repeat expansion within the C9ORF72 gene as the cause of chromosome 9p21-linked ALS-FTD and subsequently confirmed the presence of this large expansion in a substantial proportion of familial ALS and FTD cases. Overall, the hexanucleotide repeat expansion was found in nearly one-half of Finnish familial ALS cases and in more than one-third of familial ALS cases of wider European ancestry. Our data indicate that the repeat expansion is more than twice as common as mutations in the SOD1 gene as a cause of familial ALS and more than three times as common as TARDBP , FUS , OPTN , and VCP mutations combined. Taken together with the D90A SOD1 mutation, our data show that nearly 90% of familial ALS in Finland is now explained by a simple monogenic cause.

We present five pieces of genetic data demonstrating that the hexanucleotide repeat expansion is pathogenic for neurodegeneration. First, the hexanucleotide expansion segregated with disease within two multigenerational kindreds that have been convincingly linked to the region. Second, the hexanucleotide expansion was highly associated with disease in the same cohort of ALS cases and controls that was used to identify the chromosome 9p21 region within the Finnish population. Furthermore, the association signal based on the presence or absence of the expansion was many times greater than that indicated by the surrounding SNPs (p value based on expansion = 8.1 x 10^-38 versus 9.11 x 10^-11 based on the most associated SNP rs3849942 in the initial Finnish ALS GWAS). Third, the hexanucleotide repeat expansion was not found in 409 population-matched control subjects or in 300 diverse population samples screened in our laboratory. Fourth, we found that a large proportion of apparently unrelated familial ALS and FTD cases carried the same hexanucleotide repeat expansion within C9ORF72 . Within this cohort of European-ancestry familial samples, we identified three additional multigenerational families within which the repeat expansion segregated perfectly with disease. Fifth, FISH analysis demonstrated that the repeat expansion is large in size (at least 1.5 kb to be visualized by this technique), and such long expansions are typically pathogenic. Finally, another group independently discovered the same genetic mutation to be the cause of chromosome 9p21-linked FTD/ALS.

Our data indicate that both ALS and FTD phenotypes are associated with the C9ORF72 GGGGCC hexanucleotide repeat expansion. Several members of the GWENT#1 and DUTCH#1 pedigrees manifested clinical signs of isolated motor neuron dysfunction or isolated cognitive decline, whereas other affected members developed mixed ALS-FTD symptomatology over the course of their illness. It is interesting to note that the frequency of the repeat expansion was almost identical in our ALS and FTD case cohorts, suggesting that carriers of the mutant allele are equally at risk for both forms of neurodegeneration. Our data support the notion that the observed clinical and pathological overlap between ALS and FTD forms of neurodegeneration may be driven in large part by the C9ORF72 hexanucleotide repeat expansion.

The identification of the cause of chromosome 9p21-linked neurodegeneration allows for future screening of population-based cohorts to further unravel the overlap between ALS and FTD and to identify additional genetic and environmental factors that push an individual's symptoms toward one end of the ALS/FTD clinical spectrum. Some early observations may already be made: among our Finnish FTD cohort, we identified several patients carrying the pathogenic repeat expansion who presented with nonfluent progressive aphasia. This suggests that the difficulties with speech production that are commonly observed in ALS patients may in some cases be partially attributable to cortical degeneration in addition to tongue and bulbar musculature weakness secondary to hypoglossal motor neuron degeneration. It is also interesting to note that age of symptom onset varied widely in patients carrying the pathogenic hexanucleotide expansion, including some individuals who developed weakness in their ninth decade of life. The genetic and/or environmental factors underlying this variability remain to be determined.

Our development of a rapid, reliable method of screening individuals for the repeat expansion will have immediate clinical utility by allowing early identification of ALS patients at increased risk of cognitive impairment, and of FTD cases at increased risk of progressive paralysis. In the longer term, the identification of the genetic lesion underlying chromosome 9p21-linked ALS and FTD, together with the observed high frequency in these patient populations, makes it an ideal target for drug development aimed at amelioration of the disease process.

Broadly speaking, pathogenic repeat expansions are thought to cause disease through haploinsufficiency, in which expression or splicing of the target gene is perturbed, or through the generation of abnormal amounts of toxic RNA that disrupt normal cellular pathways. We favor the second as a mechanism in chromosome 9 FTD/ALS, given the large size of the expansion visualized by FISH and its noncoding localization within the C9ORF72 gene. RNA generated from such pathogenic repeat expansions are thought to disrupt transcription by sequestering normal RNA and proteins involved in transcription regulation, and disruption of RNA metabolism has already been implicated in the pathogenesis of ALS associated with mutations in TDP-43 and FUS. Interestingly, an index family studied previously demonstrating aberrant RNA metabolism of an astroglial gene, EAAT2, is in fact a chromosome 9 hexanucloitude mutation carrier. This might provide early evidence that aberrant RNA metabolism occurs as part of the pathogenic mechanism. However, knowing the pattern of distribution of C9ORF72 expression is likely to be key in understanding cell vulnerability and local expression of the hexanucleotide repeat expansion, which is probably influenced by the promoter of the C9ORF72 gene. We did not find consistent differences in expression between cases and controls. This may represent the true biological effect of the GGGGCC hexanucleotide repeat expansion on C9ORF72 expression, or alternatively it may reflect the small number of samples analyzed or tissue-to-tissue variation in expression of this gene. More definitive experimental testing is required in the future to resolve the effect of the hexanucleotide repeat expansion on C9ORF72 expression, as well as additional molecular biology investigation to understand the precise mechanism by which the hexanucleotide repeat may disrupt RNA metabolism.

An important aspect of understanding a pathogenic repeat expansion focuses on its stability. Preliminary evidence suggests that the C9ORF72 hexanucleotide repeat expansion may be unstable. First, minor anticipation has been noted in pedigrees that originally identified the locus with earlier generations being relatively unaffected by disease, perhaps reflecting expanding repeat number over successive generations. Interestingly, anticipation was not observed within the five families in which we found the hexanucleotide repeat expansion. Second, although there was strong concordance between the presence of the chromosome 9p21 founder risk haplotype and the presence of the hexanucleotide expansion in an individual, the expansion was also present in ALS cases that did not carry this haplotype. These data are consistent with the expansion occurring on multiple occasions on multiple haplotype backgrounds. Taken together, these observations suggest that the C9ORF72 repeat region has some degree of instability. This instability may be particularly relevant for sporadic ALS, where the apparent random nature of the disease in the community could be a consequence of stochastic expansion in the number of repeats. It is noteworthy that a sizeable proportion of the Finnish ALS cases that carried the repeat expansion was clinically classified as sporadic.

In summary, our data demonstrate that a massive hexanucleotide repeat expansion within C9ORF72 is the cause of chromosome 9p21-linked ALS, FTD, and ALS-FTD. Furthermore, this expansion accounts for an unprecedented proportion of ALS cases in Finland and in familial ALS cases of European ancestry, and it provides additional evidence supporting the role of disrupted RNA metabolism as a cause of neurodegeneration.
historical_context: In 2011, independent teams led by Rosa Rademakers (DeJesus-Hernandez et al.) and Bryan Traynor (Renton et al.) revealed that a massive hexanucleotide repeat expansion in the non-coding region of the *C9ORF72* gene is the most prevalent genetic driver of both amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) and frontotemporal dementia (FTD). While genetic linkage to chromosome 9p21 was already established, the exact mutation had bypassed standard sequencing due to its highly repetitive nature. This watershed discovery forged a definitive molecular connection between ALS and FTD, fundamentally redefining the clinical and biological approach to these neurodegenerative conditions. It catalyzed a new era of research focused on RNA toxicity, dipeptide repeat proteins, and nucleocytoplasmic transport defects, illuminating entirely new therapeutic avenues for these previously intractable diseases.

display_title: A Hexanucleotide Repeat Expansion in C9ORF72 Is the Cause of Chromosome 9p21-Linked ALS-FTD
title: A Hexanucleotide Repeat Expansion in C9ORF72 Is the Cause of Chromosome 9p21-Linked ALS-FTD
text:
We have identified a large GGGGCC hexanucleotide repeat expansion within the first intron of C9ORF72 as the genetic lesion responsible for chromosome 9p21-linked ALS-FTD. This repeat expansion was discovered by deep resequencing of the minimal associated interval and was subsequently shown to segregate with disease in multiple families previously linked to this region. The expansion was absent from unaffected family members in the kindreds examined and was present at a strikingly high frequency in both familial and apparently sporadic ALS in the Finnish population. Furthermore, the same mutation was detected in a large proportion of familial ALS cases of European descent and in Finnish patients with FTD, thereby establishing C9ORF72 repeat expansion as the most common genetic cause of ALS and ALS-FTD identified to date.

The data presented here resolve a long-standing question in the genetics of ALS and FTD. Linkage studies had repeatedly implicated chromosome 9p21 in families with ALS, FTD, or a combination of both phenotypes, and genome-wide association studies had independently identified the same region as the strongest common signal for ALS. Previous haplotype analyses narrowed the disease interval and demonstrated the existence of a shared founder haplotype, but conventional sequencing of coding regions had failed to reveal a causative point mutation. The discovery of a noncoding repeat expansion explains why the lesion escaped standard approaches, while also unifying linkage, association, and family-based segregation data within a single genetic mechanism. The repeat expansion lies near the 5' end of C9ORF72, within an intronic region common to major transcripts, and its position is consistent with the possibility that it alters gene expression, RNA processing, or produces a toxic RNA species.

The population frequency of the expansion is remarkable. In Finland, the mutation accounts for nearly half of familial ALS and approximately one-fifth of apparently sporadic ALS. Together with the known enrichment of the D90A SOD1 mutation in this population, these findings indicate that the majority of familial ALS in Finland can now be attributed to simple monogenic causes. The presence of the C9ORF72 repeat expansion in sporadic cases is also important. Some of these patients may reflect unrecognized familial disease, small family size, premature death of relatives, or reduced penetrance. However, the high frequency observed suggests that genetic testing for this expansion will have immediate relevance beyond classical multiplex families. The identification of the expansion in more than one-third of outbred European familial ALS cases further demonstrates that this is not solely a Finnish founder phenomenon, although the shared haplotype argues that many European cases may derive from an ancestral mutation.

Our results also provide compelling genetic support for the concept that ALS and FTD are clinically distinct manifestations of a common neurodegenerative disease spectrum. The C9ORF72 repeat expansion was identified in families containing both ALS and FTD, in isolated ALS cases, and in Finnish patients presenting with FTD. Among the Finnish FTD expansion carriers, clinical presentations included both behavioral-variant FTD and progressive nonfluent aphasia, and a substantial fraction had a personal or family history of ALS. These observations are consistent with neuropathological studies showing TDP-43-positive inclusions in both disorders and suggest that the same molecular lesion can produce degeneration in motor, frontal, and temporal neural systems. The basis for this clinical variability remains unknown. It may reflect genetic modifiers, repeat length, somatic instability, environmental exposures, or stochastic differences in neuronal vulnerability.

The repeat-primed PCR assay used in this study provides a rapid and robust method for detecting expanded alleles, but it does not determine the true size of large expansions. The assay can classify individuals into expanded and nonexpanded groups, and the separation between affected expansion carriers and control alleles was clear in our cohorts. Nevertheless, FISH analysis indicated that the repeat expansion may comprise at least several hundred repeat units in affected individuals, far beyond the range measurable by repeat-primed PCR. Accurate sizing of the expansion will require Southern blotting or other methods capable of resolving large, GC-rich repeat regions. Such studies will be necessary to determine whether repeat length correlates with age at onset, phenotype, penetrance, disease duration, or tissue-specific pathology.

Although the genetic evidence that the C9ORF72 repeat expansion causes disease is strong, the pathogenic mechanism remains to be determined. Several models may be considered. First, the expansion could reduce expression of one or more C9ORF72 transcripts, resulting in haploinsufficiency. The location of the repeat near the promoter and within the first intron is compatible with altered transcription initiation, elongation, splicing, or chromatin state. Our preliminary expression analyses did not provide conclusive evidence for a consistent reduction in C9ORF72 RNA in frontal cortex, and immunocytochemical studies of patient fibroblasts suggested possible reduction and relocalization of C9ORF72 protein but were limited by small sample size and antibody specificity. Thus, whether loss of C9ORF72 function contributes to disease remains unresolved.

A second possibility is that the expanded repeat gives rise to a toxic RNA gain of function. Several repeat expansion disorders are caused by accumulation of abnormal repeat-containing RNA, which can form nuclear foci and sequester RNA-binding proteins. The high GC content and repetitive nature of the C9ORF72 expansion make this an attractive hypothesis. If such RNA foci are present in patient tissue, they may disrupt RNA metabolism in vulnerable neurons and glia. A third possibility is that the expansion could promote repeat-associated non-ATG translation, generating abnormal dipeptide repeat proteins. This mechanism has not yet been demonstrated for this locus, but the location and structure of the expansion justify investigation of unconventional translation products. These mechanisms are not mutually exclusive, and disease may arise through a combination of decreased C9ORF72 function and toxic effects mediated by expanded repeat RNA or repeat-derived products.

The identification of this repeat expansion has immediate implications for clinical genetics. The high frequency of the mutation in familial ALS, ALS-FTD, and FTD indicates that diagnostic testing for C9ORF72 expansions should be considered in patients with these phenotypes, particularly when there is a family history of either motor neuron disease or frontotemporal dementia. At the same time, counseling will require caution. Penetrance has not been fully defined, repeat length has not yet been accurately measured in large numbers of individuals, and the expansion was observed at low frequency in control samples. Longitudinal study of asymptomatic carriers and careful phenotyping of families will be needed to clarify age-dependent penetrance, clinical variability, and the predictive value of repeat size or haplotype background.

This discovery also creates a direct route toward disease modeling and therapeutic development. Cellular and animal models carrying the expanded repeat will be required to determine whether disease is mediated by loss of C9ORF72, toxic RNA, abnormal repeat-associated proteins, or a combination of mechanisms. Such models should also allow testing of antisense oligonucleotides, RNA-targeting strategies, and approaches designed to restore normal C9ORF72 expression. The large effect size and high prevalence of this mutation make it an especially important target for translational studies.

In summary, a GGGGCC repeat expansion in C9ORF72 accounts for a major proportion of chromosome 9p21-linked ALS, ALS-FTD, and FTD. This finding explains the strongest known genetic association signal for ALS, establishes a common molecular basis for clinically overlapping ALS and FTD syndromes, and substantially expands the fraction of disease attributable to identifiable genetic causes. Future work defining the size, stability, penetrance, and cellular consequences of the expansion will be essential for understanding disease pathogenesis and for developing targeted therapies for this devastating neurodegenerative disease spectrum.

paper_title: A Hexanucleotide Repeat Expansion in C9ORF72 Is the Cause of Chromosome 9p21-Linked ALS-FTD - 2011 - Neuron
display_title: A Hexanucleotide Repeat Expansion in C9ORF72 Is the Cause of Chromosome 9p21-Linked ALS-FTD
title: A Hexanucleotide Repeat Expansion in C9ORF72 Is the Cause of Chromosome 9p21-Linked ALS-FTD
text:
The identification of the massive hexanucleotide (GGGGCC) repeat expansion within the first intron of the *C9ORF72* gene represents a landmark discovery in the genetics of neurodegenerative diseases. For over a decade, researchers have sought the elusive genetic lesion underlying the chromosome 9p21 linkage signal, which has consistently emerged as the most significant locus associated with both amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) and frontotemporal dementia (FTD). Our findings definitively demonstrate that this repeat expansion is the causative mutation, perfectly segregating with the disease in multiple large kindreds and explaining an unprecedented proportion of both familial and sporadic cases. The scale of this genetic contribution is particularly striking in the Finnish population, where the expansion accounts for nearly half of all familial ALS cases and over one-fifth of sporadic cases. When considered alongside the previously identified D90A *SOD1* mutation, we can now attribute approximately 87% of familial ALS in Finland to a simple monogenic cause. Furthermore, our analysis reveals that this mutation is not confined to an isolated genetic isolate; it is responsible for roughly one-third of familial ALS cases across outbred European populations, establishing it as the most common genetic cause of ALS identified to date.

The profound impact of the *C9ORF72* repeat expansion extends beyond ALS, providing a unifying genetic explanation for the long-observed clinical and pathological continuum between ALS and FTD. Historically, these two devastating conditions were viewed as distinct entities, characterized by either upper and lower motor neuron degeneration or frontal and temporal lobe atrophy, respectively. However, the discovery of TDP-43 positive inclusions in the majority of both ALS and FTD cases hinted at a shared molecular etiology. Our genetic data firmly cement this connection. The high frequency of the *C9ORF72* expansion in our FTD cohort, combined with the presence of mixed ALS-FTD phenotypes within families carrying the expansion, confirms that ALS and FTD can represent different clinical manifestations of the same underlying genetic defect. The phenotypic variability observed among expansion carriers—even within the same family—suggests that additional genetic or environmental modifiers may influence disease presentation, dictating whether a patient develops predominantly motor symptoms, cognitive impairment, or a combination of both. Exploring these modifying factors will be a crucial next step in understanding the phenotypic heterogeneity of *C9ORF72*-linked disease.

The mechanisms by which this intronic repeat expansion precipitates neurodegeneration remain to be fully elucidated, but several hypotheses merit careful consideration. A primary question is whether the mutation acts through a loss-of-function or a gain-of-function mechanism. Our preliminary expression and immunocytochemistry analyses suggest that *C9ORF72* protein levels may be reduced in patient-derived cell lines, pointing toward a potential loss-of-function effect resulting from haploinsufficiency. The repeat expansion, located in the first intron, might disrupt normal transcription, mRNA splicing, or export, leading to decreased synthesis of the *C9ORF72* protein. While the physiological function of *C9ORF72* is currently unknown, its evolutionary conservation and widespread expression in the central nervous system imply a significant role in neuronal homeostasis. If haploinsufficiency is a driving factor, therapeutic strategies aimed at restoring *C9ORF72* expression could be beneficial. However, the dominant inheritance pattern and the nature of the mutation as a large repeat expansion strongly invoke alternative or concurrent gain-of-function mechanisms, akin to those observed in other microsatellite expansion disorders such as myotonic dystrophy or fragile X-associated tremor/ataxia syndrome.

In the context of a gain-of-function mechanism, the transcribed hexanucleotide repeat RNA could accumulate and form toxic nuclear foci. These RNA foci could sequester essential RNA-binding proteins, thereby disrupting global RNA processing, splicing, and translation within the neuron. Given the established role of RNA-binding proteins like TDP-43 and FUS in ALS and FTD pathogenesis, an RNA toxicity model for the *C9ORF72* expansion is particularly compelling. It is conceivable that the expanded RNA transcripts directly interact with and dysregulate the function of these or other critical RNA-binding proteins, leading to the characteristic TDP-43 pathology observed in these patients. Alternatively, the expanded repeats might undergo repeat-associated non-ATG (RAN) translation, producing toxic dipeptide repeat proteins that accumulate in the cytoplasm or nucleus and exert deleterious effects on cellular function. Disentangling the relative contributions of loss-of-function and gain-of-function mechanisms will require the development of sophisticated cellular and animal models, including induced pluripotent stem cell (iPSC)-derived motor neurons and genetically modified mice.

The sheer size of the *C9ORF72* expansion—estimated to be hundreds or even thousands of repeats in affected individuals, compared to typically fewer than 20 to 30 repeats in healthy controls—poses significant technical challenges for molecular diagnosis and research. The repeat-primed PCR assay we utilized provides a rapid and robust method for detecting the presence of a pathogenic expansion, allowing for reliable classification of patient samples. However, this technique cannot accurately determine the absolute length of large expansions. Traditional methods like Southern blotting will be necessary to fully characterize the size distribution of the repeat and to investigate potential correlations between repeat length and clinical variables, such as age of onset, disease duration, and phenotypic presentation. Anticipation—the phenomenon of increasing disease severity or decreasing age of onset in successive generations—is a hallmark of many repeat expansion disorders. While our current data do not definitively prove anticipation in *C9ORF72*-linked families, the underlying instability of such large repetitive sequences makes it a plausible feature that requires systematic investigation in large, multigenerational pedigrees.

The identification of the *C9ORF72* repeat expansion has immediate and transformative implications for clinical practice, genetic counseling, and therapeutic development. Diagnostic testing for this mutation will rapidly become a standard component of the clinical evaluation for patients presenting with ALS, FTD, or related syndromes, particularly those with a family history of neurodegenerative disease. The remarkably high frequency of the expansion, even in apparently sporadic cases, suggests that genetic screening may be warranted in a broader patient population. For asymptomatic individuals in families carrying the mutation, the availability of predictive genetic testing introduces complex ethical and psychological considerations. Genetic counseling protocols must be carefully adapted to address the uncertainties surrounding incomplete penetrance, variable expressivity, and the current lack of disease-modifying therapies. Patients and their families will need comprehensive support to navigate the implications of testing positive for a highly penetrant, fatal neurodegenerative condition.

Looking forward, the discovery of the *C9ORF72* expansion opens entirely new avenues for therapeutic intervention. If RNA toxicity is the primary driver of disease, strategies to target and degrade the expanded transcripts—such as antisense oligonucleotides (ASOs) or RNA interference (RNAi) approaches—hold immense promise. ASOs have shown encouraging results in preclinical models of other neurodegenerative diseases, including *SOD1*-linked ALS and spinal muscular atrophy, and they represent a logical therapeutic modality for *C9ORF72*-linked disease. Developing ASOs that selectively target the repeat expansion while preserving wild-type *C9ORF72* expression will be a key objective. Alternatively, if haploinsufficiency contributes significantly to pathogenesis, gene therapy approaches aimed at restoring *C9ORF72* function might be required. Identifying the normal cellular role of the *C9ORF72* protein will be essential for evaluating the feasibility and safety of such interventions. Furthermore, understanding the downstream molecular pathways disrupted by the repeat expansion could reveal novel pharmacological targets for neuroprotection.

In conclusion, our discovery of the *C9ORF72* hexanucleotide repeat expansion resolves a major mystery in the field of neurodegeneration and fundamentally reshapes our understanding of the genetic landscape of ALS and FTD. By demonstrating that a single, highly prevalent genetic lesion unites these two devastating disorders, we have provided a solid foundation for future mechanistic studies and targeted drug discovery efforts. The journey from genetic mapping to gene identification has been arduous, but the ultimate prize—the ability to develop rationally designed therapies that address the root cause of the disease—is now within reach. The *C9ORF72* expansion is not merely another gene on a growing list; it is a paradigm-shifting discovery that will accelerate the pace of research and bring us closer to the day when effective treatments are available for patients confronting these universally fatal conditions. The unprecedented frequency of this mutation demands a massive, coordinated research effort to unravel its biology and translate this genetic breakthrough into meaningful clinical benefits.

paper_title: A Pneumonia Outbreak Associated with a New Coronavirus of Probable Bat Origin - 2020 - Nature
display_title: A Pneumonia Outbreak Associated with a New Coronavirus of Probable Bat Origin
title: A Pneumonia Outbreak Associated with a New Coronavirus of Probable Bat Origin
text: Since the outbreak of severe acute respiratory syndrome (SARS) 18 years ago, a large number of SARS-related coronaviruses (SARSr-CoVs) have been discovered in their natural reservoir host, bats. Previous studies have shown that some bat SARSr-CoVs have the potential to infect humans. Here we report the identification and characterization of a new coronavirus (2019-nCoV), which caused an epidemic of acute respiratory syndrome in humans in Wuhan, China. The epidemic, which started on 12 December 2019, had caused 2,794 laboratory-confirmed infections including 80 deaths by 26 January 2020. Full-length genome sequences were obtained from five patients at an early stage of the outbreak. The sequences are almost identical and share 79.6% sequence identity to SARS-CoV. Furthermore, we show that 2019-nCoV is 96% identical at the whole-genome level to a bat coronavirus. Pairwise protein sequence analysis of seven conserved non-structural proteins domains show that this virus belongs to the species of SARSr-CoV. In addition, 2019-nCoV virus isolated from the bronchoalveolar lavage fluid of a critically ill patient could be neutralized by sera from several patients. Notably, we confirmed that 2019-nCoV uses the same cell entry receptor—angiotensin converting enzyme II (ACE2)—as SARS-CoV.
historical_context: Published in early 2020 by Peng Zhou, Shi Zheng-Li, and colleagues, this landmark paper provided the first comprehensive identification and genomic characterization of SARS-CoV-2, the virus responsible for the COVID-19 pandemic. The authors sequenced the full genome of the novel virus from patients in Wuhan, confirmed its use of the ACE2 receptor for cellular entry (analogous to the 2003 SARS-CoV), and traced its probable evolutionary lineage to bat coronaviruses, highlighting a 96% sequence identity to the bat coronavirus RaTG13. This work was a monumental achievement for global public health; by rapidly openly sharing the viral genome and its receptor usage, it laid the immediate groundwork for the unprecedented, rapid-fire development of diagnostic tests, antiviral drugs, and life-saving mRNA vaccines within a matter of months. It remains one of the most rapidly influential papers in the history of biomedical science.

title: A Pneumonia Outbreak Associated with a New Coronavirus of Probable Bat Origin
text: An outbreak of pneumonia of unknown cause emerged in Wuhan, Hubei province, China, in December 2019 and was epidemiologically linked to a local seafood market. Here we report the identification and characterization of the causative agent from seven patients with severe disease. Using pan-coronavirus PCR, metagenomic sequencing and targeted amplification, we detected a previously unknown betacoronavirus, tentatively named 2019-nCoV, in bronchoalveolar lavage fluid and oral swabs, and obtained five nearly identical full-length genomes. The virus shares 79.6% genome sequence identity with SARS-CoV and 96.2% identity with a bat coronavirus, RaTG13, indicating that it belongs to the species SARS-related coronavirus and suggesting a probable bat origin. Serological analyses showed virus-specific IgG or IgM responses in infected patients, and patient sera neutralized the isolated virus in vitro. We successfully isolated 2019-nCoV in Vero E6 and Huh7 cells, where infected cells displayed typical coronavirus morphology. Functional entry assays demonstrated that 2019-nCoV uses angiotensin-converting enzyme 2 from several species, including humans, but not mouse ACE2, as its receptor. These findings establish the genetic, serological and cellular-entry characteristics of 2019-nCoV and provide tools for diagnosis, surveillance and further investigation.

title: A Pneumonia Outbreak Associated with a New Coronavirus of Probable Bat Origin
text:
Since the SARS outbreak nearly two decades ago, severe acute respiratory syndrome-related coronaviruses (SARSr-CoV) have been widely discovered in bats and have generally been thought to pose a substantial risk of a future disease outbreak. Here we report on a recent cluster of acute respiratory illness in Wuhan, Hubei province, central China, which was determined to be caused by a previously unidentified coronavirus. Samples from seven patients with severe pneumonia, six of whom were sellers or deliverymen from a local seafood market, were sent to the laboratory at the Wuhan Institute of Virology for the diagnosis of the causative pathogen. Using metagenomic next-generation sequencing of bronchoalveolar lavage fluid and targeted PCR, we obtained the full-length genome sequence of a novel coronavirus, which we tentatively call 2019-nCoV. The virus shared 79.6% sequence identity to SARS-CoV across the genome, but was 96.2% identical at the whole-genome level to a coronavirus (BatCoV RaTG13) previously detected in Rhinolophus affinis from Yunnan province, indicating that 2019-nCoV may have originated in bats. We also show that 2019-nCoV is able to use ACE2 proteins from humans, Chinese horseshoe bats, civets and pigs—but not mice—as an entry receptor to enter ACE2-expressing cells, suggesting that ACE2 is probably the cellular receptor through which 2019-nCoV enters cells. The disease and viral spread of this outbreak warrant further investigation.</content>

title: A Pneumonia Outbreak Associated with a New Coronavirus of Probable Bat Origin
text: Coronaviruses have caused two large-scale pandemics in the past two decades, SARS and Middle East respiratory syndrome (MERS). It has generally been thought that SARSr-CoV—which is mainly found in bats—could cause a future disease outbreak. Here we report on a series of cases caused by an unidentified pneumonia disease outbreak in Wuhan, Hubei province, central China. This disease outbreak—which started from a local seafood market—has grown substantially to infect 2,761 people in China, is associated with 80 deaths and has led to the infection of 33 people in 10 additional countries as of 26 January 2020. Typical clinical symptoms of these patients are fever, dry cough, breathing difficulties (dyspnoea), headache and pneumonia. Disease onset may result in progressive respiratory failure owing to alveolar damage (as observed by transverse chest computerized-tomography images) and even death. The disease was determined to be caused by virus-induced pneumonia by clinicians according to clinical symptoms and other criteria, including a rise in body temperature, decreases in the number of lymphocytes and white blood cells (although levels of the latter were sometimes normal), new pulmonary infiltrates on chest radiography and no obvious improvement after treatment with antibiotics for three days. It appears that most of the early cases had contact history with the original seafood market; however, the disease has now progressed to be transmitted by human-to-human contact.

title: A Pneumonia Outbreak Associated with a New Coronavirus of Probable Bat Origin
text:
Coronaviruses (CoVs) are enveloped, positive-sense RNA viruses that infect a broad range of mammalian and avian hosts and have repeatedly crossed species barriers to cause human disease. The outbreak of severe acute respiratory syndrome (SARS) in 2002–2003, which was traced to animal markets in southern China, revealed the pandemic potential of bat-borne SARS-related coronaviruses (SARSr-CoVs). Since then, extensive surveillance has identified a large and genetically diverse reservoir of SARSr-CoVs in horseshoe bats, including viruses that are closely related to SARS-CoV and some that can use human angiotensin converting enzyme II (ACE2) for cell entry.

In December 2019, a cluster of patients with pneumonia of unknown cause was reported in Wuhan, Hubei province, China. Many of the earliest patients had epidemiological links to the Huanan seafood market, where live animals were also sold, raising concern that a zoonotic pathogen had entered the human population. The clinical presentation and winter occurrence of the outbreak were reminiscent of SARS, and suggested that a coronavirus should be considered among the possible causative agents.

Here we report the identification, genome sequencing and initial characterization of a novel coronavirus from patients with severe pneumonia in Wuhan. We show that this virus, provisionally named 2019-nCoV, belongs to the SARSr-CoV species, is closely related to a bat coronavirus, can be isolated in cell culture, elicits antibody responses in infected patients and uses ACE2 as a cellular entry receptor.

text:
Coronaviruses (CoVs) are enveloped, positive-sense, single-stranded RNA viruses that belong to the subfamily Orthocoronavirinae within the family Coronaviridae. While most human coronaviruses cause only mild respiratory diseases, the emergence of the severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus (SARS-CoV) in 2002 and the Middle East respiratory syndrome coronavirus (MERS-CoV) in 2012 demonstrated that these viruses can cross species barriers to cause highly lethal pneumonia in humans. The natural reservoirs for both SARS-CoV and MERS-CoV are bats, with intermediate hosts facilitating their transmission to humans. Since the SARS outbreak, extensive surveillance of bat populations has revealed the vast genetic diversity of SARS-related coronaviruses (SARSr-CoVs) circulating in the wild. Notably, some of these bat SARSr-CoVs have been shown to use the human angiotensin-converting enzyme II (ACE2) receptor for cell entry, suggesting a continuous potential for spillover events.

In late December 2019, an outbreak of a mysterious and severe respiratory disease was reported in Wuhan, Hubei Province, China. The initial cluster of patients was epidemiologically linked to a local seafood market, presenting with clinical symptoms strongly resembling viral pneumonia, including fever, cough, and dyspnea. The rapid spread of the disease and its clinical severity raised immediate public health alarms. In this study, we detail the urgent diagnostic investigation and pathogen discovery efforts undertaken during the early stages of this outbreak. We report the isolation, genomic characterization, and preliminary receptor usage analysis of a novel coronavirus, tentatively named 2019-nCoV, establishing its probable bat origin and its relationship to the SARSr-CoV lineage.

text: The study provides a detailed report on 2019-nCoV, the likely aetiological agent responsible for the ongoing epidemic of acute respiratory syndrome in China and other countries. Virus-specific nucleotide-positive and viral-protein seroconversion was observed in all patients tested and provides evidence of an association between the disease and the presence of this virus. However, there are still many urgent questions that remain to be answered. The association between 2019-nCoV and the disease has not been verified by animal experiments to fulfil the Koch's postulates to establish a causative relationship between a microorganism and a disease. We do not yet know the transmission routine of this virus among hosts. It appears that the virus is becoming more transmissible between humans. We should closely monitor whether the virus continues to evolve to become more virulent. Owing to a shortage of specific treatments and considering the relatedness of 2019-nCoV to SARS-CoV, some drugs and pre-clinical vaccines against SARS-CoV could probably be used to treat this virus. Finally, considering the wide spread of SARSr-CoV in their natural reservoirs, future research should be focused on active surveillance of these viruses for broader geographical regions. In the long term, broad-spectrum antiviral drugs and vaccines should be prepared for emerging infectious diseases that are caused by this cluster of viruses in the future. Most importantly, strict regulations against the domestication and consumption of wildlife should be implemented.

title: A Pneumonia Outbreak Associated with a New Coronavirus of Probable Bat Origin
text:
Our study identifies a new coronavirus, 2019-nCoV, as the causative agent associated with the outbreak of viral pneumonia in Wuhan. The nearly identical genome sequences obtained from five patients at the early stage of the outbreak indicate a recent introduction of this virus into humans. Although 2019-nCoV is related to SARS-CoV and belongs to the species SARSr-CoV, the virus forms a distinct lineage together with the bat coronavirus RaTG13, to which it shows 96.2% whole-genome identity. These results provide strong evidence that bats are the probable natural reservoir of 2019-nCoV, although the possibility that one or more intermediate hosts participated in transmission to humans requires further investigation.

The detection of viral RNA in BALF and oral swabs from patients, together with virus isolation in cell culture, seroconversion and neutralization by patient sera, fulfils several key criteria for linking this virus to the disease. The successful isolation of infectious virus will facilitate the development of diagnostics, antiviral assays and vaccine studies. Our qPCR assay based on the receptor-binding domain of the S gene can distinguish 2019-nCoV from other known human coronaviruses and from closely related bat SARSr-CoVs, but additional targets, including RdRp and E genes, should be incorporated for routine detection.

Notably, 2019-nCoV uses ACE2 from humans and several animal species for cell entry, as does SARS-CoV. This finding may help to explain its capacity for human infection and suggests that surveillance of ACE2-compatible animal hosts is urgently needed. Further epidemiological, clinical and experimental studies are required to define transmission routes, pathogenic mechanisms and the risk of continued spread.
historical_context: Published in early 2020 by Peng Zhou, Shi Zheng-Li, and colleagues, this landmark paper provided the first comprehensive identification and genomic characterization of SARS-CoV-2, the virus responsible for the COVID-19 pandemic. The authors sequenced the full genome of the novel virus from patients in Wuhan, confirmed its use of the ACE2 receptor for cellular entry (analogous to the 2003 SARS-CoV), and traced its probable evolutionary lineage to bat coronaviruses, highlighting a 96% sequence identity to the bat coronavirus RaTG13. This work was a monumental achievement for global public health; by rapidly openly sharing the viral genome and its receptor usage, it laid the immediate groundwork for the unprecedented, rapid-fire development of diagnostic tests, antiviral drugs, and life-saving mRNA vaccines within a matter of months. It remains one of the most rapidly influential papers in the history of biomedical science.

text: Our study identifies a novel coronavirus, 2019-nCoV, as the etiological agent responsible for the acute respiratory syndrome outbreak in Wuhan, China. The remarkable genomic similarity between 2019-nCoV and the bat coronavirus RaTG13 (96.2% identity) provides compelling evidence that bats are the probable natural reservoir for this emerging pathogen. Despite the sequence divergence in the receptor-binding domain of the spike protein compared to SARS-CoV, our results unequivocally demonstrate that 2019-nCoV utilizes the same cellular entry receptor, human ACE2. This finding has immediate implications for understanding viral pathogenesis and highlights the potential for cross-species transmission of SARS-related coronaviruses from wildlife to humans. The rapid human-to-human transmission and the detection of the virus in patient bronchoalveolar lavage fluid underscore the critical need for robust surveillance, rapid diagnostic tests, and the development of targeted therapeutics and vaccines. While the exact intermediate host remains to be definitively identified, the emergence of 2019-nCoV serves as a stark reminder of the ongoing threat posed by zoonotic coronaviruses. Future studies must focus on elucidating the structural basis of ACE2 interaction, monitoring viral evolution, and investigating potential antiviral compounds to mitigate the impact of this unprecedented global health crisis.

paper_title: A Programmable Dual-RNA-Guided DNA Endonuclease in Adaptive Bacterial Immunity - 2012 - Science
display_title: A Programmable Dual-RNA-Guided DNA Endonuclease in Adaptive Bacterial Immunity
title: A Programmable Dual-RNA-Guided DNA Endonuclease in Adaptive Bacterial Immunity
text: CRISPR/Cas systems provide bacteria and archaea with adaptive immunity against viruses and plasmids by using crRNAs to guide the silencing of invading nucleic acids. We show here that in a subset of these systems, the mature crRNA base-paired to trans-activating tracrRNA forms a two-RNA structure that directs the CRISPR-associated protein Cas9 to introduce double-stranded (ds) breaks in target DNA. At sites complementary to the crRNA-guide sequence, the Cas9 HNH nuclease domain cleaves the complementary strand while the Cas9 RuvC-like domain cleaves the non-complementary strand. The dual-tracrRNA:crRNA, when engineered as a single RNA chimera, also directs sequence-specific Cas9 dsDNA cleavage. Our study reveals a family of endonucleases that use dual-RNAs for site-specific DNA cleavage and highlights the potential to exploit the system for RNA-programmable genome editing. A two-RNA structure directs an endonuclease to cleave target DNA.
historical_context: In 2012, Martin Jinek, Krzysztof Chylinski, Ines Fonfara, Michael Hauer, Jennifer Doudna, and Emmanuelle Charpentier demonstrated that the bacterial CRISPR-Cas9 immune system could be elegantly repurposed as a simple, programmable gene-editing tool. They showed that the Cas9 endonuclease is guided by two RNAs (crRNA and tracrRNA) to cleave specific DNA sequences, and they subsequently engineered a single-guide RNA (sgRNA) that fused both. This brilliant simplification allowed researchers to easily target and edit any specific DNA sequence. The breakthrough culminated in the 2020 Nobel Prize in Chemistry for Doudna and Charpentier. The Nobel committee praised their development of "a method for genome editing," emphasizing how the CRISPR/Cas9 genetic scissors have revolutionized the life sciences, unlocking new paradigms for plant breeding, innovative cancer therapies, and the ultimate pursuit of curing inherited diseases.

paper_title: A Programmable Dual-RNA-Guided DNA Endonuclease in Adaptive Bacterial Immunity - 2012 - Science
display_title: A Programmable Dual-RNA-Guided DNA Endonuclease in Adaptive Bacterial Immunity
title: A Programmable Dual-RNA-Guided DNA Endonuclease in Adaptive Bacterial Immunity
text: Bacteria and archaea use CRISPR/Cas systems to defend against foreign genetic elements by means of small RNAs that direct sequence-specific silencing. Here we show that in the Streptococcus pyogenes Type II CRISPR/Cas system, Cas9 is an RNA-guided DNA endonuclease activated by a duplex formed between tracrRNA and crRNA. This dual-RNA structure directs Cas9 to cleave double-stranded DNA at sites specified by complementarity between the crRNA spacer and a target protospacer, together with an adjacent NGG protospacer adjacent motif. The HNH and RuvC-like nuclease domains of Cas9 cleave the complementary and non-complementary DNA strands, respectively, generating site-specific double-stranded breaks. Target recognition requires a PAM-proximal seed sequence and PAM-dependent DNA binding. We further demonstrate that Cas9 can be programmed with a single chimeric guide RNA, providing a simple, versatile strategy for RNA-directed DNA cleavage and genome targeting.

They showed that the Cas9 endonuclease is guided by two RNAs (crRNA and tracrRNA) to cleave specific DNA sequences, and they subsequently engineered a single-guide RNA (sgRNA) that fused both. This brilliant simplification allowed researchers to easily target and edit any specific DNA sequence. The breakthrough culminated in the 2020 Nobel Prize in Chemistry for Doudna and Charpentier. The Nobel committee praised their development of "a method for genome editing," emphasizing how the CRISPR/Cas9 genetic scissors have revolutionized the life sciences, unlocking new paradigms for plant breeding, innovative cancer therapies, and the ultimate pursuit of curing inherited diseases.

title: A Programmable Dual-RNA-Guided DNA Endonuclease in Adaptive Bacterial Immunity
text: Bacteria and archaea have evolved RNA-mediated adaptive defense systems called CRISPR (Clustered Regularly Interspaced Short Palindromic Repeats)/Cas (CRISPR-associated) that protect organisms from invading viruses and plasmids. These defense systems rely on small RNAs for sequence-specific detection and silencing of foreign nucleic acids. CRISPR/Cas systems are composed of cas genes organized in operons and a CRISPR array consisting of unique genome-targeting sequences (called spacers) interspersed with identical repeats. CRISPR/Cas mediated immunity occurs in three steps. In the adaptive phase, bacteria and archaea harboring one or more CRISPR loci respond to viral and plasmid challenge by integrating short fragments of foreign sequence (protospacers) into the host chromosome at the proximal end of the CRISPR array. In the expression and interference phases, transcription of the repeat-spacer element into precursor CRISPR RNA (pre-crRNA) molecules followed by enzymatic cleavage yields the short crRNAs that can base pair with complementary protospacer sequences of invading viral or plasmid targets. Target recognition by crRNAs directs the silencing of the foreign sequences by means of Cas proteins that function in complex with the crRNAs. There are three types of CRISPR/Cas systems. The Type I and III systems share some overarching features: specialized Cas endonucleases process the pre-crRNAs, and once mature, each crRNA assembles into a large multi-Cas protein complex capable of recognizing and cleaving nucleic acids complementary to the crRNA. In contrast, Type II systems process pre-crRNAs by a different mechanism in which a trans-activating crRNA (tracrRNA) complementary to the repeat sequences in pre-crRNA triggers processing by the double-stranded RNA-specific ribonuclease RNase III in the presence of the Cas9 (formerly Csn1) protein. Cas9 is thought to be the sole protein responsible for crRNA-guided silencing of foreign DNA. We show here that in Type II systems, Cas9 proteins constitute a family of enzymes that require a base-paired structure formed between the activating tracrRNA and the targeting crRNA to cleave target double-stranded (ds) DNA. Site-specific cleavage occurs at locations determined by both base-pairing complementarity between the crRNA and the target protospacer DNA and a short motif (referred to as the protospacer adjacent motif, or PAM) juxtaposed to the complementary region in the target DNA. Our study further demonstrates that the Cas9 endonuclease family can be programmed with single RNA molecules to cleave specific DNA sites, thereby raising the exciting possibility of developing a simple and versatile RNA-directed system to generate dsDNA breaks (DSBs) for genome targeting and editing.
historical_context: In 2012, Martin Jinek, Krzysztof Chylinski, Ines Fonfara, Michael Hauer, Jennifer Doudna, and Emmanuelle Charpentier demonstrated that the bacterial CRISPR-Cas9 immune system could be elegantly repurposed as a simple, programmable gene-editing tool. They showed that the Cas9 endonuclease is guided by two RNAs (crRNA and tracrRNA) to cleave specific DNA sequences, and they subsequently engineered a single-guide RNA (sgRNA) that fused both. This brilliant simplification allowed researchers to easily target and edit any specific DNA sequence. The breakthrough culminated in the 2020 Nobel Prize in Chemistry for Doudna and Charpentier. The Nobel committee praised their development of "a method for genome editing," emphasizing how the CRISPR/Cas9 genetic scissors have revolutionized the life sciences, unlocking new paradigms for plant breeding, innovative cancer therapies, and the ultimate pursuit of curing inherited diseases.

paper_title: A Programmable Dual-RNA-Guided DNA Endonuclease in Adaptive Bacterial Immunity - 2012 - Science
display_title: A Programmable Dual-RNA-Guided DNA Endonuclease in Adaptive Bacterial Immunity
title: A Programmable Dual-RNA-Guided DNA Endonuclease in Adaptive Bacterial Immunity
text:
Bacteria and archaea have evolved diverse defense systems that limit infection by viruses and other mobile genetic elements. Among these, clustered regularly interspaced short palindromic repeats (CRISPR) and CRISPR-associated (Cas) proteins constitute an adaptive immune pathway in which short fragments of foreign DNA are integrated into CRISPR loci and later used as sequence-specific guides to silence matching invaders. CRISPR loci are transcribed and processed into small CRISPR RNAs (crRNAs), each containing a guide sequence derived from a previously encountered element. Together with Cas proteins, these RNAs direct recognition and destruction of complementary nucleic acids, thereby providing heritable resistance to reinfection.

CRISPR/Cas systems are broadly distributed and mechanistically diverse. In Type I and Type III systems, crRNAs function within multisubunit protein complexes that recognize foreign nucleic acids and recruit or activate nuclease activities. By contrast, Type II systems are genetically more compact and are defined by the presence of Cas9, a large multidomain protein containing predicted HNH and RuvC-like nuclease domains. Genetic studies have implicated Cas9 in both crRNA biogenesis and DNA interference, and have shown that Type II immunity requires a short protospacer-adjacent motif (PAM) in the target DNA. However, the molecular basis for target recognition and cleavage in Type II systems has remained unclear.

A distinctive feature of some Type II CRISPR/Cas systems is the requirement for a trans-activating CRISPR RNA (tracrRNA), a small noncoding RNA that base pairs with repeat sequences in the pre-crRNA transcript and promotes RNase III-dependent crRNA maturation. Whether tracrRNA functions only during crRNA processing or also participates directly in target DNA interference has not been established. Similarly, although Cas9 is required for interference in cells, it has not been determined whether Cas9 itself is the crRNA-guided DNA endonuclease, how its two nuclease domains contribute to cleavage, or what RNA and DNA features are sufficient to program its activity.

Here we reconstitute the Streptococcus pyogenes Type II CRISPR/Cas system in vitro and show that Cas9 is a dual-RNA-guided DNA endonuclease. We find that mature crRNA and tracrRNA together direct Cas9 to cleave double-stranded DNA at sites specified by crRNA complementarity and an adjacent PAM. We define the strand-specific contributions of the HNH and RuvC-like domains, identify RNA and target-sequence requirements for cleavage, and show that the two natural RNAs can be engineered into a single chimeric guide RNA. These results reveal the mechanism of Type II CRISPR DNA interference and establish Cas9 as an RNA-programmable enzyme for site-specific DNA cleavage.
historical_context: In 2012, Martin Jinek, Krzysztof Chylinski, Ines Fonfara, Michael Hauer, Jennifer Doudna, and Emmanuelle Charpentier demonstrated that the bacterial CRISPR-Cas9 immune system could be elegantly repurposed as a simple, programmable gene-editing tool. They showed that the Cas9 endonuclease is guided by two RNAs (crRNA and tracrRNA) to cleave specific DNA sequences, and they subsequently engineered a single-guide RNA (sgRNA) that fused both. This brilliant simplification allowed researchers to easily target and edit any specific DNA sequence. The breakthrough culminated in the 2020 Nobel Prize in Chemistry for Doudna and Charpentier. The Nobel committee praised their development of "a method for genome editing," emphasizing how the CRISPR/Cas9 genetic scissors have revolutionized the life sciences, unlocking new paradigms for plant breeding, innovative cancer therapies, and the ultimate pursuit of curing inherited diseases.

title: A Programmable Dual-RNA-Guided DNA Endonuclease in Adaptive Bacterial Immunity
text:
Bacteria and archaea face a constant onslaught from bacteriophages and invasive plasmids. To defend against these pervasive genetic elements, prokaryotes have evolved an array of sophisticated defense mechanisms. Among these, the clustered regularly interspaced short palindromic repeats (CRISPR) and CRISPR-associated (Cas) proteins constitute a unique adaptive immune system. CRISPR-Cas systems capture short fragments of foreign DNA, termed spacers, and integrate them into the host genome within CRISPR loci. These loci are transcribed and processed into small guide RNAs (crRNAs), which then direct Cas nucleases to recognize and destroy invading nucleic acids bearing complementary sequences.

CRISPR-Cas systems are incredibly diverse and have been classified into three major types (I, II, and III) based on their distinct suite of Cas proteins and mechanisms of action. In Type I and Type III systems, target recognition and cleavage are typically mediated by large, multi-protein interference complexes, such as Cascade or the Csm/Cmr complexes. In striking contrast, the Type II CRISPR-Cas systems are remarkably simple, relying on a single large, multidomain protein, Cas9, to execute both crRNA biogenesis and target destruction.

Recent studies of the Type II system from Streptococcus pyogenes have revealed that crRNA maturation requires a second, trans-encoded small RNA called tracrRNA. The tracrRNA contains a sequence highly complementary to the repeat region of the pre-crRNA transcript. Base pairing between the tracrRNA and pre-crRNA creates a double-stranded RNA structure that is recognized and cleaved by the host enzyme RNase III in the presence of Cas9, generating mature crRNAs. While the role of Cas9 and tracrRNA in crRNA processing is well established, the exact molecular mechanism by which Cas9 utilizes these RNAs to mediate DNA interference has remained unclear. It has been hypothesized that Cas9 acts as the sole effector nuclease, but its direct DNA cleavage activity has not been biochemically characterized in vitro.

In this study, we set out to elucidate the mechanism of DNA targeting and cleavage by the S. pyogenes Cas9 protein. We biochemically demonstrate that Cas9 is a DNA endonuclease that introduces double-stranded breaks at target sites complementary to the crRNA. Unexpectedly, we discovered that Cas9 relies on a dual-RNA guide consisting of both the mature crRNA and the tracrRNA to achieve site-specific DNA recognition and cleavage. Furthermore, we define the distinct roles of Cas9's two conserved nuclease domains, HNH and RuvC, in DNA strand cleavage. Finally, we show that the dual-tracrRNA:crRNA structure can be engineered into a single chimeric guide RNA, creating a highly programmable, RNA-guided DNA endonuclease system with profound potential for broad genome editing applications.
historical_context: In 2012, Martin Jinek, Krzysztof Chylinski, Ines Fonfara, Michael Hauer, Jennifer Doudna, and Emmanuelle Charpentier demonstrated that the bacterial CRISPR-Cas9 immune system could be elegantly repurposed as a simple, programmable gene-editing tool. They showed that the Cas9 endonuclease is guided by two RNAs (crRNA and tracrRNA) to cleave specific DNA sequences, and they subsequently engineered a single-guide RNA (sgRNA) that fused both. This brilliant simplification allowed researchers to easily target and edit any specific DNA sequence. The breakthrough culminated in the 2020 Nobel Prize in Chemistry for Doudna and Charpentier. The Nobel committee praised their development of "a method for genome editing," emphasizing how the CRISPR/Cas9 genetic scissors have revolutionized the life sciences, unlocking new paradigms for plant breeding, innovative cancer therapies, and the ultimate pursuit of curing inherited diseases.

title: A Programmable Dual-RNA-Guided DNA Endonuclease in Adaptive Bacterial Immunity
text: In summary, we identify a DNA interference mechanism involving a dual-RNA structure that directs a Cas9 endonuclease to introduce site-specific double-stranded breaks in target DNA. The tracrRNA:crRNA-guided Cas9 protein utilizes distinct endonuclease domains, HNH and RuvC-like, to cleave the two strands in the target DNA. Target recognition by Cas9 requires both a seed sequence in the crRNA and a GG dinucleotide-containing PAM sequence adjacent to the crRNA-binding region in the DNA target. We further show that the Cas9 endonuclease can be programmed with guide RNA engineered as a single transcript to target and cleave any dsDNA sequence of interest. The system is efficient, versatile and programmable by changing the DNA target-binding sequence in the guide chimeric RNA. Zinc-Finger Nucleases (ZFNs) and Transcription-Activator Like Effector Nucleases (TALENs) have attracted considerable interest as artificial enzymes engineered to manipulate genomes. We propose an alternative methodology based on RNA-programmed Cas9 that could offer considerable potential for gene targeting and genome editing applications.
