title: A Programmable Dual-RNA-Guided DNA Endonuclease in Adaptive Bacterial Immunity
text: Our results establish Cas9 as an RNA-guided endonuclease whose activity is specified by a dual-RNA structure rather than by crRNA alone. In this system, tracrRNA performs a second function beyond pre-crRNA maturation: it enables Cas9 to bind target DNA and position the crRNA guide for sequence-specific recognition. DNA cleavage is executed by two distinct nuclease domains within Cas9, with the HNH domain cutting the strand complementary to the crRNA and the RuvC-like domain cutting the opposite strand to generate a double-stranded break near the PAM. Targeting is further constrained by PAM recognition and by a seed region proximal to the PAM, providing a mechanism for efficient discrimination of cognate protospacers and escape variants. The ability to replace the natural tracrRNA:crRNA pair with a single chimeric RNA demonstrates that the essential targeting information can be encoded in a compact programmable guide. Thus, Type II CRISPR/Cas systems define a simple class of RNA-directed DNA endonucleases with broad potential for site-specific genome manipulation.

paper_title: A Strong Candidate for the Breast and Ovarian Cancer Susceptibility Gene BRCA1 - 1994 - Science
display_title: A Strong Candidate for the Breast and Ovarian Cancer Susceptibility Gene BRCA1
title: A Strong Candidate for the Breast and Ovarian Cancer Susceptibility Gene BRCA1
text: A strong candidate for the 17q-linked BRCA1 gene, which influences susceptibility to breast and ovarian cancer, has been identified by positional cloning methods. Probable predisposing mutations have been detected in five of eight kindreds presumed to segregate BRCA1 susceptibility alleles. The mutations include an 11-base pair deletion, a 1-base pair insertion, a stop codon, a missense substitution, and an inferred regulatory mutation. The BRCA1 gene is expressed in numerous tissues, including breast and ovary, and encodes a predicted protein of 1863 amino acids. This protein contains a zinc finger domain in its amino-terminal region, but is otherwise unrelated to previously described proteins. Identification of BRCA1 should facilitate early diagnosis of breast and ovarian cancer susceptibility in some individuals as well as a better understanding of breast cancer biology.

The cloning of *BRCA1* concluded an intense international race and definitively proved that inherited mutations in tumor suppressor genes could confer a profound lifetime risk for common cancers. It fundamentally reshaped modern oncology, medical genetics, and preventative medicine. The discovery enabled predictive genetic testing, empowering high-risk families with preventive strategies—such as prophylactic surgeries or enhanced screening—and laid the mechanistic foundation for targeted treatments like PARP inhibitors in homologous recombination-deficient tumors.

title: A Strong Candidate for the Breast and Ovarian Cancer Susceptibility Gene BRCA1
text:
Breast cancer is one of the most common and important diseases affecting women. Current estimates indicate that one in eight American women who reach age 95 will develop breast cancer. Treatment of advanced breast cancer is often futile and disfiguring, making early detection a high priority in medical management of the disease. Ovarian cancer, although less frequent than breast cancer, is often rapidly fatal and is the fourth most common cause of cancer mortality in American women.

Genetic factors contribute to an ill-defined proportion of breast cancer incidence, estimated to be about 5% of all cases but approximately 25% of cases diagnosed before age 30. Breast cancer has been subdivided into two types, early-onset and late-onset, a division that is based on an inflection in the age-specific incidence curve around age 50. Mutation of one gene, BRCA1, is thought to account for approximately 45% of families with significantly high breast cancer incidence and at least 80% of families with increased incidence of both early-onset breast cancer and ovarian cancer. Intense efforts to isolate the BRCA1 gene have proceeded since it was first mapped to chromosome arm 17q in 1990. A second locus, BRCA2, recently mapped to chromosome arm 13q, appears to account for a proportion of early-onset breast cancer roughly equal to that resulting from BRCA1. Unlike BRCA1, however, BRCA2 may not influence ovarian cancer risk. The remaining susceptibility to early-onset breast cancer is likely attributable to unmapped genes for familial cancer and rare germline mutations in genes such as TP53, which encodes the tumor suppressor protein p53. It has also been suggested that heterozygote carriers of defective forms of the gene predisposing to ataxia telangiectasia are at higher risk for breast cancer. Late-onset breast cancer is often familial in origin, although the risks in relatives are not as high as those for early-onset breast cancer. The percentage of such cases that are due to genetic susceptibility is unknown.

Like many other genes involved in familial cancer, BRCA1 appears to encode a tumor suppressor, a protein that acts as a negative regulator of tumor growth. Cancer-predisposing alleles typically carry mutations that cause loss or reduction of gene function. Predisposition to cancer is inherited as a dominant genetic trait, whereas the predisposing allele generally behaves as a recessive allele in somatic cells. Thus, a single inherited copy of the mutant allele causes predisposition, and loss or inactivation of the wild-type allele occurs in the tumor cells.

The cloning of *BRCA1* concluded an intense international race and definitively proved that inherited mutations in tumor suppressor genes could confer a profound lifetime risk for common cancers. It fundamentally reshaped modern oncology, medical genetics, and preventative medicine. The discovery enabled predictive genetic testing, empowering high-risk families with preventive strategies—such as prophylactic surgeries or enhanced screening—and laid the mechanistic foundation for targeted treatments like PARP inhibitors in homologous recombination-deficient tumors.

title: A Strong Candidate for the Breast and Ovarian Cancer Susceptibility Gene BRCA1
text:
Breast and ovarian cancers occur commonly in the general population, but their occurrence in multiple members of some families, often at unusually early ages, has long suggested the existence of inherited susceptibility alleles of large effect. The observation that breast and ovarian cancer can segregate together in the same kindreds further suggested that at least one such allele might influence the development of both tissues. Genetic linkage studies provided support for this view by mapping a major breast and ovarian cancer susceptibility locus, BRCA1, to the long arm of chromosome 17.

The isolation of BRCA1 has presented a formidable problem in positional cloning. The genetic interval defined by recombinant chromosomes was still large enough to contain many genes, and no obvious biochemical function could be assigned to the susceptibility locus from the cancer phenotype alone. Thus, the search for BRCA1 required the construction of a detailed physical map across the candidate region, the systematic identification of expressed sequences contained within that region, and the comparison of candidate transcripts with DNA from individuals carrying inherited susceptibility haplotypes.

A gene within the BRCA1 interval can be considered a strong candidate only if it satisfies several independent criteria. It should lie within the smallest region defined by meiotic recombination in linked kindreds. It should be expressed in tissues relevant to the disease phenotype, including breast or ovary. Most critically, it should contain rare sequence alterations in affected kindreds that segregate with the predisposing chromosome and are absent from appropriate control populations. Because cancer-predisposing alleles may include protein-truncating mutations, missense substitutions, splice alterations, or mutations affecting transcript production or stability, analysis of both genomic DNA and complementary DNA is required.

We have applied these criteria to expressed sequences from the 17q21 interval containing BRCA1. A transcript assembled from complementary DNA clones, hybrid-selected sequences, and polymerase chain reaction products encodes a predicted protein of 1863 amino acids and contains an amino-terminal zinc finger motif. This gene is expressed in several tissues, including breast and ovary, and extends across approximately 100 kb of genomic DNA. We now describe mutations in this gene in five kindreds presumed to segregate BRCA1 susceptibility alleles. These mutations include frameshift, nonsense, missense, and inferred regulatory alterations. Their location within the genetic interval, their predicted effects on gene function, and their cosegregation with disease-associated haplotypes strongly indicate that this gene is BRCA1.

It fundamentally reshaped modern oncology, medical genetics, and preventative medicine. The discovery enabled predictive genetic testing, empowering high-risk families with preventive strategies—such as prophylactic surgeries or enhanced screening—and laid the mechanistic foundation for targeted treatments like PARP inhibitors in homologous recombination-deficient tumors.

title: A Strong Candidate for the Breast and Ovarian Cancer Susceptibility Gene BRCA1
text:
Breast cancer is one of the most common and devastating malignancies affecting women, with a lifetime risk approaching one in nine in developed countries. While the majority of breast cancer cases are thought to be sporadic, arising from a combination of environmental and somatic genetic factors, approximately 5 to 10 percent of cases are attributable to strongly penetrant, dominantly inherited susceptibility genes. Families carrying these genetic predispositions frequently exhibit a high incidence of early-onset breast cancer, and often, an associated increase in the risk of ovarian cancer.

In 1990, genetic linkage analysis of numerous large kindreds segregating early-onset breast cancer provided the first compelling evidence for the existence of a major breast cancer susceptibility locus, designated BRCA1. This groundbreaking work mapped the BRCA1 gene to the long arm of chromosome 17 (17q21). Subsequent genetic studies refined this localization and established that mutations in the BRCA1 locus are responsible for disease in approximately 45 percent of families with multiple cases of breast cancer only, and in an overwhelming majority (greater than 80 percent) of families with a history of both breast and ovarian cancer. The identification of BRCA1 is therefore of immense clinical and biological importance, promising to elucidate the fundamental mechanisms of breast and ovarian tumorigenesis and to provide a means for the early detection and clinical management of individuals at high genetic risk.

Despite intense and highly competitive positional cloning efforts over the past four years, the isolation of the BRCA1 gene has proved exceptionally challenging. The defined genetic interval on chromosome 17q21 was remarkably gene-rich, and the lack of visible chromosomal rearrangements or clearly defined large deletions in patients complicated the search. To isolate the responsible gene, we initiated a systematic approach encompassing the construction of a high-resolution physical map, the exhaustive isolation of candidate transcribed sequences, and the rigorous screening of these candidates for disease-segregating mutations in affected families.

Here, we report the successful identification and characterization of a strong candidate for the BRCA1 gene. Through the integration of genetic and physical mapping techniques, we isolated a large transcription unit from the minimal BRCA1 interval that is expressed in breast and ovarian tissues. By screening DNA from members of eight independent kindreds linked to chromosome 17q, we identified five distinct, potentially disease-causing mutations within this gene. These sequence alterations—including frameshifts, a nonsense mutation, a missense mutation, and a presumptive regulatory mutation—co-segregate precisely with the cancer susceptibility trait. Furthermore, we characterize the genomic structure of the candidate gene and present its full-length complementary DNA sequence, revealing a novel protein containing a structurally conserved zinc finger domain. These findings strongly support the conclusion that we have isolated the long-sought BRCA1 gene.

The discovery enabled predictive genetic testing, empowering high-risk families with preventive strategies—such as prophylactic surgeries or enhanced screening—and laid the mechanistic foundation for targeted treatments like PARP inhibitors in homologous recombination-deficient tumors.

title: A Strong Candidate for the Breast and Ovarian Cancer Susceptibility Gene BRCA1
text:
Most mutant tumor suppressor genes identified to date encode proteins that are absent, nonfunctional, or reduced in function. The majority of TP53 mutations are missense; some of these have been shown to produce abnormal p53 molecules that interfere with the function of the wild-type product. A similar dominant-negative mechanism of action has been proposed for some adenomatous polyposis coli (APC) alleles that produce truncated molecules and for point mutations in the Wilms tumor gene (WT1), which alter DNA binding of the WT1 protein. The nature of three mutations observed in the BRCA1 coding sequence is consistent with production of either dominant-negative proteins or nonfunctional proteins. All three mutations are located in the COOH-terminal half of the protein. The regulatory mutation inferred in kindred 2035 cannot be dominant-negative; rather, this mutation likely causes reduction or complete loss of BRCA1 expression from the affected allele. Similarly, the 11-bp deletion in kindred 1901 likely produces a nonfunctional product.

The BRCA1 protein contains a C3HC4 zinc finger domain similar to domains found in numerous nucleic acid binding proteins. The first 180 amino acids of BRCA1 contain five more basic residues than acidic residues. In contrast, the remainder of the molecule is very acidic, with a net excess of 70 acidic residues. The excess negative charge is particularly concentrated near the COOH-terminus. Thus, one possibility is that BRCA1 encodes a transcription factor with an NH2-terminal DNA binding domain and a COOH-terminal "acidic blob" domain with transactivational activity. Interestingly, the product of another familial tumor suppressor gene, WT1, also contains zinc finger domains, and these are altered by many cancer-predisposing mutations in the gene. The WT1 gene encodes a transcription factor, and alternative splicing of exons that encode parts of the zinc finger domains alters the DNA binding properties of WT1. Some alternatively spliced forms of WT1 mRNA generate WT1 proteins that act as transcriptional repressors. Differential splicing of BRCA1 may alter the zinc finger motif, raising the possibility that a regulatory mechanism similar to that occurring in WT1 may apply to BRCA1.

The identification of a gene that (i) falls within the interval known from genetic studies to include BRCA1 and (ii) contains frameshift, nonsense, and regulatory mutations that cosegregate with predisposing BRCA1 alleles strongly indicates that this gene is BRCA1. The observation of potential predisposing mutations in individuals whose early-onset breast or ovarian cancer was not ascertained by family history supports the view that many early-onset cases are due to mutations at the BRCA1 locus. The role of BRCA1 in cancer progression may now be addressed with molecular precision. The large size and fragmented nature of the coding sequence will make exhaustive searches for new mutations challenging. Nevertheless, the percentage of total breast and ovarian cancer caused by mutant BRCA1 alleles will soon be estimated, and individual mutation frequencies and penetrances may be established. This in turn may permit accurate genetic screening for predisposition to a common, deadly disease. Although such research represents an advance in medical and biological knowledge, it also raises numerous ethical and practical issues, both scientific and social, that must be addressed by the medical community.

paper_title: A Strong Candidate for the Breast and Ovarian Cancer Susceptibility Gene BRCA1 - 1994 - Science
display_title: A Strong Candidate for the Breast and Ovarian Cancer Susceptibility Gene BRCA1
title: A Strong Candidate for the Breast and Ovarian Cancer Susceptibility Gene BRCA1
text:
The genetic and molecular evidence presented here strongly suggests that the transcription unit described in this report is BRCA1. The gene lies within the genetically defined interval on chromosome 17q21, is expressed in tissues relevant to the inherited cancer phenotype, and contains rare sequence alterations in individuals from kindreds segregating breast and ovarian cancer susceptibility. The most compelling evidence is the detection of five different candidate predisposing mutations in eight kindreds chosen because of linkage or haplotype sharing at 17q21. Three of these mutations are frameshift or nonsense changes that would be expected to truncate the BRCA1 protein, one is an inferred regulatory mutation that appears to extinguish expression of one allele, and one is a missense substitution that may alter protein function. These findings are consistent with the behavior expected for a tumor suppressor gene involved in inherited cancer predisposition.

The diversity of the observed mutations indicates that BRCA1 susceptibility alleles are unlikely to be represented by a single common founder mutation. Rather, deleterious alleles may occur throughout the coding region or in sequences that control transcription, splicing, or RNA stability. This conclusion has important implications for genetic diagnosis. Screening strategies limited to a small number of nucleotide changes will identify some carriers but will not be sufficient for comprehensive testing. The large size of the gene, the presence of multiple coding exons, and the complexity of alternative splicing near the 5' end will complicate mutation detection. Nevertheless, the identification of BRCA1 provides the essential molecular substrate for carrier testing in high-risk families and for studies of risk in populations not represented in the present sample.

Several observations argue that loss or reduction of BRCA1 function is likely to be the common mechanism by which these alleles confer susceptibility. The 11-bp deletion in exon 2 would remove the zinc finger motif near the NH2-terminus, whereas the nonsense and insertion mutations would delete portions of the COOH-terminal region. The apparent regulatory mutation in kindred 2035 would be expected to reduce the amount of functional BRCA1 transcript produced from the inherited allele. In each case, the effect is compatible with a recessive mode of action in tumor cells, in which the inherited mutant allele is followed by somatic loss or inactivation of the remaining wild-type copy. Direct examination of tumors from mutation carriers will be necessary to test this prediction and to determine whether loss of heterozygosity, second intragenic mutation, or another mechanism eliminates normal BRCA1 function.

The relation between BRCA1 genotype and clinical phenotype remains incompletely defined. Kindreds carrying different mutations vary in age of breast cancer diagnosis, frequency of ovarian cancer, and occurrence of bilateral disease. Some of these differences may reflect ascertainment, family size, or chance clustering of tumors, but they may also indicate that distinct BRCA1 alleles have different tissue-specific effects. The existence of unaffected elderly carriers further indicates that penetrance is not absolute. Modifier genes, reproductive factors, environmental exposures, and stochastic events may all influence whether a carrier develops breast cancer, ovarian cancer, both diseases, or neither.

The predicted BRCA1 protein contains an NH2-terminal zinc finger domain but otherwise has no clear similarity to known proteins. This feature suggests a role in nucleic acid binding, transcriptional regulation, or protein interactions, but the biochemical function of BRCA1 remains unknown. Conservation of BRCA1-related sequences in several mammalian species should facilitate functional studies in experimental systems. Isolation of BRCA1 therefore represents not only a step toward clinical identification of inherited susceptibility, but also an entry point into the molecular pathways that restrain tumor development in breast and ovarian epithelium.

title: A Strong Candidate for the Breast and Ovarian Cancer Susceptibility Gene BRCA1
text:
The identification of *BRCA1* as a strong candidate for the 17q-linked breast and ovarian cancer susceptibility gene marks a pivotal milestone in cancer genetics. Our positional cloning efforts culminated in the isolation of a large transcription unit, spanning approximately 100 kb of genomic DNA and encoding a 1863-amino acid protein. The presence of potentially predisposing mutations in five of eight families clearly segregating 17q-linked cancer susceptibility provides compelling evidence that this gene is indeed *BRCA1*. The nature of these mutations—which include a frameshift deletion, a nonsense mutation, a single base-pair insertion, a missense mutation at a highly conserved residue, and a likely regulatory mutation—strongly supports the hypothesis that *BRCA1* functions as a tumor suppressor gene, where loss of wild-type function predisposes individuals to oncogenesis.

The predicted *BRCA1* protein contains an amino-terminal C3HC4 zinc finger domain, a motif often found in proteins that interact with nucleic acids and regulate transcription. This suggests that *BRCA1* might govern the expression of other genes involved in cell cycle progression, DNA repair, or apoptosis. Aside from this zinc finger, the lack of significant homology to previously characterized proteins indicates that *BRCA1* may define a novel pathway in the regulation of mammary and ovarian epithelial cell growth. The broad tissue expression pattern of *BRCA1*, despite its specific association with breast and ovarian cancer predisposition, suggests that its tumor-suppressive function might either be redundant in other tissues or uniquely critical to the hormonal or developmental context of the breast and ovary. The complex alternative splicing observed at the 5' end of the transcript further hints at an intricate regulatory mechanism that warrants future investigation.

The diverse array of mutations identified in our study also offers preliminary insights into genotype-phenotype correlations. For instance, the nonsense mutation found in Kindred 2082, which truncates a substantial portion of the carboxy-terminus, is associated with a strikingly high incidence of ovarian cancer and an older average age of breast cancer onset. Conversely, mutations occurring earlier in the sequence, such as the zinc finger-disrupting 11-bp deletion in Kindred 1901 and the potential regulatory mutation in Kindred 2035, appear to correlate with a very early age of breast cancer onset. While these observations are based on a limited number of families, they raise the intriguing possibility that different *BRCA1* alleles confer distinct risks for breast versus ovarian cancer, and influence the penetrance and age of onset of the disease.

The clinical implications of cloning *BRCA1* are profound. With the sequence in hand, it is now possible to develop molecular diagnostics for the identification of individuals at high risk for breast and ovarian cancer. Such screening could facilitate intensive surveillance and early intervention strategies, potentially reducing the high mortality associated with these malignancies. Moreover, uncovering the physiological role of the *BRCA1* protein will inevitably enhance our fundamental understanding of breast and ovarian cancer biology. Future functional studies must prioritize determining the specific cellular pathways regulated by *BRCA1*, investigating the mechanism by which the mutant alleles promote tumorigenesis, and ultimately exploring targeted therapeutic avenues capable of restoring or bypassing *BRCA1* function in susceptible patients.

paper_title: Bacteriophage Genes That Inactivate the CRISPR-Cas Bacterial Immune System - 2013 - Nature
display_title: Bacteriophage Genes That Inactivate the CRISPR-Cas Bacterial Immune System
title: Bacteriophage Genes That Inactivate the CRISPR-Cas Bacterial Immune System
text: A widespread system used by bacteria for protection against potentially dangerous foreign DNA molecules consists of the clustered regularly interspaced short palindromic repeats (CRISPR) coupled with cas (CRISPR-associated) genes. Similar to RNA interference in eukaryotes, these CRISPR/Cas systems use small RNAs for sequence-specific detection and neutralization of invading genomes. Here we describe the first examples of genes that mediate the inhibition of a CRISPR/Cas system. Five distinct 'anti-CRISPR' genes were found in the genomes of bacteriophages infecting Pseudomonas aeruginosa. Mutation of the anti-CRISPR gene of a phage rendered it unable to infect bacteria with a functional CRISPR/Cas system, and the addition of the same gene to the genome of a CRISPR/Cas-targeted phage allowed it to evade the CRISPR/Cas system. Phage-encoded anti-CRISPR genes may represent a widespread mechanism for phages to overcome the highly prevalent CRISPR/Cas systems. The existence of anti-CRISPR genes presents new avenues for the elucidation of CRISPR/Cas functional mechanisms and provides new insight into the co-evolution of phages and bacteria.
historical_context: Published in 2013 by Joseph Bondy-Denomy, Alan Davidson, and colleagues, this paper unveiled the existence of "anti-CRISPRs"—small proteins encoded by bacteriophages that directly neutralize the CRISPR-Cas immune systems of their bacterial hosts. Discovering these inhibitors in Pseudomonas phages, the authors highlighted a relentless evolutionary arms race between bacteria and their viral pathogens. This was a paradigm shift for the field of genome editing. It elucidated how viruses bypass CRISPR immunity while simultaneously handing scientists a crucial "off-switch" for CRISPR technologies. Today, these anti-CRISPR proteins are widely utilized to refine the precision of CRISPR-Cas9 genome editing, mitigate off-target effects, and safely regulate gene therapies in mammalian cells.

paper_title: Bacteriophage Genes That Inactivate the CRISPR-Cas Bacterial Immune System - 2013 - Nature
display_title: Bacteriophage Genes That Inactivate the CRISPR-Cas Bacterial Immune System
title: Bacteriophage Genes That Inactivate the CRISPR-Cas Bacterial Immune System
text: Bacteria and their phages are locked in a continuing evolutionary contest, and CRISPR/Cas systems provide adaptive immunity by using small CRISPR RNAs to guide destruction of invading DNA. Although phage mutation can permit escape from this immunity, whether phages encode dedicated CRISPR/Cas antagonists has been unknown. Here we show that several Pseudomonas aeruginosa Mu-like phages carry genes that profoundly inhibit the type I-F CRISPR/Cas system. Prophages bearing these genes enabled normally CRISPR-sensitive phages and protospacer-containing plasmids to escape targeting. Expression of individual phage genes was sufficient to block immunity against multiple protospacers and in several P. aeruginosa strains, but did not inhibit an Escherichia coli type I-E system. Anti-CRISPR activity did not alter cas gene expression, crRNA biogenesis or crRNA stability, indicating inhibition after assembly of the crRNA-Cas machinery. Mutational and codon-variation analyses demonstrated that the activity requires protein production. Finally, deletion or transfer of an anti-CRISPR gene respectively abolished or conferred phage replication in CRISPR-competent cells. These findings reveal a genetic route by which mobile elements counter adaptive bacterial immunity.

Discovering these inhibitors in Pseudomonas phages, the authors highlighted a relentless evolutionary arms race between bacteria and their viral pathogens. This was a paradigm shift for the field of genome editing. It elucidated how viruses bypass CRISPR immunity while simultaneously handing scientists a crucial "off-switch" for CRISPR technologies. Today, these anti-CRISPR proteins are widely utilized to refine the precision of CRISPR-Cas9 genome editing, mitigate off-target effects, and safely regulate gene therapies in mammalian cells.

title: Bacteriophage Genes That Inactivate the CRISPR-Cas Bacterial Immune System
text: The CRISPR/Cas immune system is a widespread bacterial defence that uses small CRISPR RNAs (crRNAs) complexed with Cas proteins to cleave invading nucleic acids derived from phages and other mobile genetic elements. Although phage-encoded inhibitors have been described for most major bacterial anti-phage defences, no genes that counteract CRISPR/Cas activity have previously been identified. Here we investigate the type I-F CRISPR/Cas system of Pseudomonas aeruginosa and report the discovery of five distinct families of phage-encoded 'anti-CRISPR' genes that profoundly inhibit this system in vivo. Anti-CRISPR genes are clustered at a single position within the genomes of closely related Mu-like phages, and each is required for the host phage to evade CRISPR/Cas-mediated immunity. The encoded proteins act after crRNA biogenesis and have no effect on crRNA or Cas protein amounts, indicating that they target a step subsequent to crRNA–Cas complex formation. These findings reveal a new arms race that may shape the evolution of bacterial and phage genomes.
historical_context: Published in 2013 by Joseph Bondy-Denomy, Alan Davidson, and colleagues, this paper unveiled the existence of "anti-CRISPRs"—small proteins encoded by bacteriophages that directly neutralize the CRISPR-Cas immune systems of their bacterial hosts. Discovering these inhibitors in Pseudomonas phages, the authors highlighted a relentless evolutionary arms race between bacteria and their viral pathogens. This was a paradigm shift for the field of genome editing. It elucidated how viruses bypass CRISPR immunity while simultaneously handing scientists a crucial "off-switch" for CRISPR technologies. Today, these anti-CRISPR proteins are widely utilized to refine the precision of CRISPR-Cas9 genome editing, mitigate off-target effects, and safely regulate gene therapies in mammalian cells.

title: Bacteriophage Genes That Inactivate the CRISPR-Cas Bacterial Immune System
text: Bacteria and archaea utilize CRISPR/Cas systems to defend against foreign genetic elements, including bacteriophages. Despite the prevalence of these adaptive immune systems, the mechanisms by which phages might evade CRISPR/Cas targeting have remained largely unknown. Here, we identify a widespread mechanism of phage-encoded inhibition of the CRISPR/Cas system. Through a screen of Pseudomonas aeruginosa lysogens, we discovered phages that robustly resist type I-F CRISPR/Cas immunity. We identified five distinct "anti-CRISPR" genes encoded within these phage genomes. The expression of any single anti-CRISPR gene is sufficient to completely inactivate the P. aeruginosa CRISPR/Cas system, allowing successful phage replication despite the presence of targeted protospacer sequences. We demonstrate that these anti-CRISPR proteins function downstream of crRNA biogenesis, likely by directly interfering with the crRNA-Cas ribonucleoprotein complex. The discovery of anti-CRISPR genes uncovers a fundamental evolutionary arms race between phages and bacterial adaptive immunity and suggests that such evasion mechanisms may drive the remarkable diversification of CRISPR/Cas systems in nature.

Today, these anti-CRISPR proteins are widely utilized to refine the precision of CRISPR-Cas9 genome editing, mitigate off-target effects, and safely regulate gene therapies in mammalian cells.

title: Bacteriophage Genes That Inactivate the CRISPR-Cas Bacterial Immune System
text: Predation by phages presents an important challenge to bacterial survival, and bacteria have evolved numerous mechanisms to resist phage infection. One such system is the CRISPR/Cas immune system, which is found in 48% of Eubacteria and 95% of Archaea. CRISPR loci contain multiple repeated sequences of approximately 30 base pairs, separated by variable 'spacer' sequences of similar length, which are often identical to segments of phage genomes or other mobile genetic elements. The large single transcript from a CRISPR locus is processed in the repeat regions into small CRISPR RNAs (crRNAs) that are complexed with Cas proteins. Using the crRNAs as guides, crRNA/Cas complexes cleave foreign DNA molecules at sites bearing complementarity to the crRNAs, thus engendering resistance to phages and other invading DNA molecules. Cas proteins also mediate an adaptive function by incorporating short sequences (that is, a new spacer) from newly encountered foreign genomes into CRISPR loci so that these genomes will be destroyed in subsequent encounters. The relatively recent elucidation of CRISPR/Cas functions and their obvious similarities to RNA interference (RNAi) systems of eukaryotes have led to vigorous investigation of these systems.
historical_context: Published in 2013 by Joseph Bondy-Denomy, Alan Davidson, and colleagues, this paper unveiled the existence of "anti-CRISPRs"—small proteins encoded by bacteriophages that directly neutralize the CRISPR-Cas immune systems of their bacterial hosts. Discovering these inhibitors in Pseudomonas phages, the authors highlighted a relentless evolutionary arms race between bacteria and their viral pathogens. This was a paradigm shift for the field of genome editing. It elucidated how viruses bypass CRISPR immunity while simultaneously handing scientists a crucial "off-switch" for CRISPR technologies. Today, these anti-CRISPR proteins are widely utilized to refine the precision of CRISPR-Cas9 genome editing, mitigate off-target effects, and safely regulate gene therapies in mammalian cells.

title: Bacteriophage Genes That Inactivate the CRISPR-Cas Bacterial Immune System
text: Viruses that infect bacteria are under intense selective pressure to circumvent host defences, and bacteriophages encode inhibitors of restriction-modification systems, abortive infection pathways and other anti-phage mechanisms. Clustered regularly interspaced short palindromic repeats (CRISPR) and CRISPR-associated (cas) genes comprise a highly prevalent adaptive immune system that protects bacteria and archaea from phage and plasmid DNA. In these systems, short sequences acquired from foreign genetic elements are incorporated into CRISPR loci and transcribed into small CRISPR RNAs that guide Cas proteins to complementary invader sequences. Although phages can escape CRISPR/Cas targeting through mutation of the protospacer or protospacer adjacent motif, this route is intrinsically target-specific and should be vulnerable to acquisition of additional spacers. The apparent absence of phage-encoded inhibitors of CRISPR/Cas systems has therefore been notable. Here we use a naturally active type I-F CRISPR/Cas system in Pseudomonas aeruginosa to identify bacteriophage genes that inhibit CRISPR/Cas immunity. We show that diverse genes from related Mu-like phages act as anti-CRISPR proteins, function against multiple CRISPR targets, and are required for targeted phages to replicate in CRISPR/Cas-positive bacteria.

Discovering these inhibitors in Pseudomonas phages, the authors highlighted a relentless evolutionary arms race between bacteria and their viral pathogens. This was a paradigm shift for the field of genome editing. It elucidated how viruses bypass CRISPR immunity while simultaneously handing scientists a crucial "off-switch" for CRISPR technologies. Today, these anti-CRISPR proteins are widely utilized to refine the precision of CRISPR-Cas9 genome editing, mitigate off-target effects, and safely regulate gene therapies in mammalian cells.

title: Bacteriophage Genes That Inactivate the CRISPR-Cas Bacterial Immune System
text:
Bacteriophages and their bacterial hosts are locked in a continuous evolutionary arms race. To survive the constant threat of viral predation, bacteria have evolved a diverse arsenal of defense mechanisms, including restriction-modification systems, abortive infection pathways, and surface receptor alterations. The most recently discovered, and perhaps most sophisticated, of these defenses is the clustered regularly interspaced short palindromic repeats (CRISPR) and CRISPR-associated (Cas) proteins. CRISPR/Cas systems function as an adaptive immune system, utilizing small guide RNAs derived from previously encountered invaders to direct the sequence-specific nucleolytic destruction of foreign nucleic acids.

Given the intense selective pressure exerted by bacterial defenses, phages have rapidly evolved countermeasures. For instance, diverse phage-encoded proteins are known to specifically inhibit restriction-modification systems. However, despite the widespread distribution of CRISPR/Cas systems across the bacterial domain, specific viral genes dedicated to inactivating this RNA-guided immunity have not yet been identified. It has been widely assumed that phages escape CRISPR/Cas targeting primarily through the accumulation of point mutations within the targeted protospacer regions. In this study, we screened bacteriophages infecting the opportunistic pathogen Pseudomonas aeruginosa to identify specific, genetically encoded inhibitors of the type I-F CRISPR/Cas system.
historical_context: Published in 2013 by Joseph Bondy-Denomy, Alan Davidson, and colleagues, this paper unveiled the existence of "anti-CRISPRs"—small proteins encoded by bacteriophages that directly neutralize the CRISPR-Cas immune systems of their bacterial hosts. Discovering these inhibitors in Pseudomonas phages, the authors highlighted a relentless evolutionary arms race between bacteria and their viral pathogens. This was a paradigm shift for the field of genome editing. It elucidated how viruses bypass CRISPR immunity while simultaneously handing scientists a crucial "off-switch" for CRISPR technologies. Today, these anti-CRISPR proteins are widely utilized to refine the precision of CRISPR-Cas9 genome editing, mitigate off-target effects, and safely regulate gene therapies in mammalian cells.

title: Bacteriophage Genes That Inactivate the CRISPR-Cas Bacterial Immune System
text: The adaptive nature of the CRISPR/Cas system and the widespread occurrence of CRISPR regions in bacterial genomes suggest that this system could be the most powerful weapon possessed by bacteria to resist invasion by foreign DNA. Before our work, the only known mechanism for phages to evade CRISPR/Cas systems was by mutation, which is a low-frequency event. Here we have provided the first demonstration that the in vivo activity of a CRISPR/Cas system is profoundly inhibited by any one of five different 'anti-CRISPR' genes. The existence of anti-CRISPR genes is one possible explanation of how phages have continued to proliferate despite the ubiquity and potency of CRISPR/Cas systems. The possibility that anti-CRISPR genes are diverse and widespread among phages and other mobile genetic elements may account for the large diversity of CRISPR/Cas systems and the existence of multiple CRISPR/Cas system types within single bacterial strains. This proliferation of CRISPR/Cas systems may be driven by the concomitant proliferation and diversification of anti-CRISPR genes. This newly discovered arms race may have a profound effect on the evolution of both phage and bacterial genomes, and knowledge of anti-CRISPR genes will be crucial for understanding this process. The failure to detect anti-CRISPR genes until now may be due only to a lack of systematic searches using naturally functioning in vivo systems. Future studies to discover more anti-CRISPR genes and explain the mechanisms of their inhibition of CRISPR/Cas systems will provide new inroads for illumination of CRISPR/Cas function.

Discovering these inhibitors in Pseudomonas phages, the authors highlighted a relentless evolutionary arms race between bacteria and their viral pathogens. This was a paradigm shift for the field of genome editing. It elucidated how viruses bypass CRISPR immunity while simultaneously handing scientists a crucial "off-switch" for CRISPR technologies. Today, these anti-CRISPR proteins are widely utilized to refine the precision of CRISPR-Cas9 genome editing, mitigate off-target effects, and safely regulate gene therapies in mammalian cells.

title: Bacteriophage Genes That Inactivate the CRISPR-Cas Bacterial Immune System
text:
The discovery of multiple, unrelated phage proteins that inhibit the P. aeruginosa type I-F CRISPR/Cas system demonstrates that CRISPR immunity, like other bacterial anti-phage defences, is subject to direct antagonism by mobile genetic elements. These anti-CRISPR genes are sufficient to permit targeted phages to replicate in bacteria bearing an intact CRISPR/Cas system, and are required for productive infection by an anti-CRISPR phage that carries functional protospacers. Thus, in addition to mutation of protospacers or protospacer adjacent motifs, phages can use dedicated inhibitory proteins to evade sequence-specific bacterial immunity.

The anti-CRISPR proteins described here do not reduce cas gene transcription, CRISPR locus expression or processed crRNA abundance, indicating that they act after assembly or maintenance of the crRNA-Cas effector complex. This suggests that these proteins may interfere with target recognition, DNA binding, recruitment of Cas3, or target cleavage. Their lack of activity against a type I-E system further indicates that anti-CRISPR proteins can be highly specific for particular CRISPR/Cas subtypes, a property that should facilitate their use as probes of CRISPR/Cas mechanism.

The presence of related genes in prophages and in other predicted mobile elements suggests that anti-CRISPR activity may also promote horizontal gene transfer between bacterial strains. Such genes could allow incoming DNA to overcome resident CRISPR spacers that would otherwise block transfer. The sequence diversity and sparse database representation of these proteins indicate that many additional anti-CRISPR genes remain to be identified. Their discovery expands the known molecular strategies used in phage-bacterial co-evolution and reveals CRISPR/Cas immunity as a regulatory target for phage-encoded proteins.

This was a paradigm shift for the field of genome editing. It elucidated how viruses bypass CRISPR immunity while simultaneously handing scientists a crucial "off-switch" for CRISPR technologies. title: Bacteriophage Genes That Inactivate the CRISPR-Cas Bacterial Immune System
text:
Our discovery of bacteriophage-encoded anti-CRISPR genes reveals an unprecedented mechanism by which viruses subvert the adaptive immune system of bacteria. While the CRISPR-Cas system provides a formidable defense against foreign nucleic acids, our findings demonstrate that phages have co-evolved a sophisticated counter-strategy to ensure their survival. We identified five distinct families of anti-CRISPR proteins from *Pseudomonas aeruginosa* phages that specifically neutralize the Type I-F CRISPR-Cas system. The complete lack of sequence homology among these proteins and to any other known proteins underscores the immense, yet largely unexplored, genetic diversity of the viral world and suggests multiple independent evolutionary origins for anti-CRISPR activity.

Crucially, our mechanistic experiments demonstrate that these anti-CRISPR genes exert their inhibitory effects without interfering with the transcription or processing of CRISPR RNAs (crRNAs), nor do they affect the expression of the Cas proteins themselves. Instead, they act at a post-biogenesis step, preventing the fully assembled crRNA-Cas complex from effectively recognizing and cleaving target DNA. This points to direct protein-protein interactions between the anti-CRISPR proteins and specific components of the Cas machinery. The fact that expressing an anti-CRISPR gene allows a phage to evade CRISPR immunity entirely, regardless of the targeted sequence, highlights the broad potency of these inhibitors. The presence of homologous anti-CRISPR genes within active bacterial pathogenicity islands further implicates these elements in facilitating horizontal gene transfer. Ultimately, these anti-CRISPR systems provide powerful new tools for dissecting the precise molecular mechanisms of CRISPR-Cas function and genome engineering applications.

Discovering these inhibitors in Pseudomonas phages, the authors highlighted a relentless evolutionary arms race between bacteria and their viral pathogens. This was a paradigm shift for the field of genome editing. It elucidated how viruses bypass CRISPR immunity while simultaneously handing scientists a crucial "off-switch" for CRISPR technologies. Today, these anti-CRISPR proteins are widely utilized to refine the precision of CRISPR-Cas9 genome editing, mitigate off-target effects, and safely regulate gene therapies in mammalian cells.

paper_title: CD47 Is Up-Regulated on Circulating Hematopoietic Stem Cells and Leukemia Cells to Avoid Phagocytosis - 2009 - Cell
display_title: CD47 Is Up-Regulated on Circulating Hematopoietic Stem Cells and Leukemia Cells to Avoid Phagocytosis
title: CD47 Is Up-Regulated on Circulating Hematopoietic Stem Cells and Leukemia Cells to Avoid Phagocytosis
text: Macrophages clear pathogens and damaged or aged cells from the blood stream via phagocytosis. Cell-surface CD47 interacts with its receptor on macrophages, SIRP alpha, to inhibit phagocytosis of normal, healthy cells. We find that mobilizing cytokines and inflammatory stimuli cause CD47 to be transiently up-regulated on mouse hematopoietic stem cells (HSCs) and progenitors just prior to and during their migratory phase, and that the level of CD47 on these cells determines the probability that they are engulfed in vivo. CD47 is also constitutively up-regulated on mouse and human myeloid leukemias and over-expression of CD47 on a myeloid leukemia line increases its pathogenicity by allowing it to evade phagocytosis. We conclude that CD47 up-regulation is an important mechanism that provides protection to normal HSCs during inflammation-mediated mobilization, and that leukemic progenitors co-opt this ability in order to evade macrophage killing.
historical_context: Published back-to-back with the Majeti et al. AML study in 2009, this paper by Siddhartha Jaiswal, Irving Weissman, and colleagues demystified the physiological role of CD47 in normal stem cell biology and its malicious hijacking in leukemia. They observed that normal hematopoietic stem cells (HSCs) transiently up-regulate CD47 during mobilization to shield themselves from macrophage-mediated destruction, a survival pathway that leukemia cells aggressively co-opt to evade immune surveillance. Alongside its companion paper, this cornerstone discovery cemented the foundational paradigm of macrophage immune checkpoints. By demonstrating that CD47 blockade permits the innate immune system to clear circulating cancer cells, it pioneered a major new branch of cancer immunotherapy aimed at stimulating phagocytosis, deeply influencing modern oncology.

paper_title: CD47 Is Up-Regulated on Circulating Hematopoietic Stem Cells and Leukemia Cells to Avoid Phagocytosis - 2009 - Cell
display_title: CD47 Is Up-Regulated on Circulating Hematopoietic Stem Cells and Leukemia Cells to Avoid Phagocytosis
title: CD47 Is Up-Regulated on Circulating Hematopoietic Stem Cells and Leukemia Cells to Avoid Phagocytosis
text: Hematopoietic stem cells (HSCs) enter the blood during stress and must pass through macrophage-rich tissues. We show here that mouse HSCs and progenitors transiently up-regulate CD47 during cyclophosphamide/G-CSF mobilization or LPS-induced inflammation, and that circulating human HSCs likewise express higher CD47 than sessile marrow HSCs. Although CD47 deficiency does not grossly impair mobilization, CD47-null HSCs fail to engraft wild-type recipients and are rapidly phagocytosed, while CD47 heterozygous HSCs have reduced fitness during inflammatory challenge in a macrophage-dependent manner. We further find that CD47 is constitutively increased in murine and human myeloid leukemias, particularly AML and blast crisis CML. In MOLM-13 leukemia cells, the level of CD47 determines tumor engraftment, selection in vivo, and resistance to macrophage phagocytosis through SIRP alpha. Thus, leukemias co-opt a physiological HSC protection pathway to evade innate immune clearance.

Alongside its companion paper, this cornerstone discovery cemented the foundational paradigm of macrophage immune checkpoints. By demonstrating that CD47 blockade permits the innate immune system to clear circulating cancer cells, it pioneered a major new branch of cancer immunotherapy aimed at stimulating phagocytosis, deeply influencing modern oncology.

title: CD47 Is Up-Regulated on Circulating Hematopoietic Stem Cells and Leukemia Cells to Avoid Phagocytosis
text: Macrophages clear damaged, foreign, and aged cells from the circulation by recognizing altered surface molecules, while CD47 on healthy cells serves as a "don't eat me" signal through its interaction with macrophage SIRP alpha. Hematopoietic stem and progenitor cells (HSPCs) and leukemic stem cells migrate through macrophage-lined vasculature of the marrow, spleen, and liver, where they are potentially vulnerable to phagocytic clearance. Here we show that CD47 is transiently up-regulated on mouse HSPCs during mobilization and inflammation, and is constitutively elevated on circulating human HSCs and on nearly all mouse and human myeloid leukemias examined, including AML and blast-crisis CML. CD47-deficient and heterozygous HSCs are cleared by macrophages and exhibit reduced fitness during transplantation and inflammatory stress. Conversely, enforced CD47 expression on a non-engrafting human AML line confers tumorigenicity in immunodeficient mice in a dose-dependent, macrophage-sensitive manner. CD47 thus represents a compelling therapeutic target for myeloid malignancies.

title: CD47 Is Up-Regulated on Circulating Hematopoietic Stem Cells and Leukemia Cells to Avoid Phagocytosis
text: Hematopoietic stem cells (HSCs) frequently migrate to ectopic niches during normal development and in response to physiological stress, necessitating navigation through macrophage-lined vascular networks. We discovered that HSCs transiently up-regulate CD47, an immunoglobulin-like protein that binds to SIRPα to inhibit phagocytosis, specifically during experimental mobilization and inflammatory stress. Ectopic expression or increased surface density of CD47 provides HSCs with robust protection against macrophage-mediated clearance. Intriguingly, we found that both mouse and human myeloid leukemias similarly up-regulate CD47 to evade innate immunosurveillance. Functional studies using the human leukemia cell line MOLM-13 demonstrated that enforced CD47 expression promotes in vivo tumor engraftment and growth, whereas blocking CD47 significantly enhances macrophage-mediated phagocytosis of these leukemic cells. Our findings illuminate a shared immune-evasion mechanism between mobilized HSCs and leukemic cells, highlighting CD47 as a potent determinant of stem cell fitness and a compelling therapeutic target in leukemia.

Alongside its companion paper, this cornerstone discovery cemented the foundational paradigm of macrophage immune checkpoints. By demonstrating that CD47 blockade permits the innate immune system to clear circulating cancer cells, it pioneered a major new branch of cancer immunotherapy aimed at stimulating phagocytosis, deeply influencing modern oncology.

paper_title: CD47 Is Up-Regulated on Circulating Hematopoietic Stem Cells and Leukemia Cells to Avoid Phagocytosis - 2009 - Cell
display_title: CD47 Is Up-Regulated on Circulating Hematopoietic Stem Cells and Leukemia Cells to Avoid Phagocytosis
title: CD47 Is Up-Regulated on Circulating Hematopoietic Stem Cells and Leukemia Cells to Avoid Phagocytosis
text:
HSCs have the ability to migrate to ectopic niches in fetal and adult life via the blood stream. Once in the blood, HSCs home to VCAM-1+ endothelia using integrin alpha 4 beta 1, then navigate the vascular beds of the marrow, spleen and liver before returning to potential niches that secrete SDF-1, for which HSC have responsive CXCR4 chemokine receptors. Macrophages lining vascular sinusoids at these sites function to remove damaged cells and foreign particles from the blood stream, sensing altered surface molecules. During inflammatory responses, macrophages become more phagocytically active in order to more effectively clear offending pathogens. At these times, host cells are at increased risk of clearance and newly arriving stem cells might require additional protection against phagocytosis.

Like normal HSCs, leukemic stem and progenitor cells also have an intrinsic ability to migrate via the circulation to ectopic marrow sites. By analogy with the normal HSCs, the leukemic stem cells (LSCs) must also be able to navigate through macrophage lined vasculature of spleen, liver, and marrow as they travel to ectopic niches.

CD47 is an immunoglobulin-like protein that is known to interact functionally with integrins and thrombspondin-1. It has been implicated in functions as diverse as neutrophil migration, axon extension, and T-cell co-stimulation. In addition, CD47 is capable of interacting with its receptor SIRP alpha on macrophages to negatively regulate phagocytosis. Lack of autonomous CD47 expression results in phagocytosis of red blood cells, as well as T-cells and whole bone marrow cells in a transplant setting. Thus, CD47 functions as a 'Don't eat me' signal to ensure that autologous cells are not inappropriately phagocytosed.

While the absence of CD47 is known to result in phagocytosis, the effect of differential CD47 expression is unclear. IAP+/- platelets and erythrocytes may be more prone to phagocytosis than their wild-type counterparts, which suggests that CD47 down-regulation might lead to clearance of these cells as they age. However, no formal exploration of the role of CD47 expression on normal or leukemic hematopoietic stem and progenitor cells, which are also physiologically migrating cells, has been undertaken. Hence, we performed experiments to understand the effect of differential CD47 expression on these cells.

They observed that normal hematopoietic stem cells (HSCs) transiently up-regulate CD47 during mobilization to shield themselves from macrophage-mediated destruction, a survival pathway that leukemia cells aggressively co-opt to evade immune surveillance. Alongside its companion paper, this cornerstone discovery cemented the foundational paradigm of macrophage immune checkpoints. By demonstrating that CD47 blockade permits the innate immune system to clear circulating cancer cells, it pioneered a major new branch of cancer immunotherapy aimed at stimulating phagocytosis, deeply influencing modern oncology.

paper_title: CD47 Is Up-Regulated on Circulating Hematopoietic Stem Cells and Leukemia Cells to Avoid Phagocytosis - 2009 - Cell
display_title: CD47 Is Up-Regulated on Circulating Hematopoietic Stem Cells and Leukemia Cells to Avoid Phagocytosis
title: CD47 Is Up-Regulated on Circulating Hematopoietic Stem Cells and Leukemia Cells to Avoid Phagocytosis
text:
The hematopoietic system is maintained by rare stem cells that reside primarily in specialized bone marrow niches, yet these cells are not permanently sessile. In response to injury, infection, or cytokine-mediated mobilization, hematopoietic stem and progenitor cells enter the circulation and seed distant hematopoietic tissues. This migratory behavior exposes stem cells to a markedly different immune environment. Blood, spleen, liver, and marrow sinusoids contain abundant macrophages that continuously survey passing cells and remove aged, damaged, infected, or foreign elements by phagocytosis. Thus, circulating stem cells must preserve their regenerative capacity while avoiding inappropriate innate immune clearance.

One mechanism by which normal cells are protected from macrophage engulfment is expression of CD47, also known as integrin-associated protein. CD47 binds signal regulatory protein alpha (SIRP alpha) on macrophages and delivers an inhibitory signal that restrains phagocytosis. Loss of CD47 renders erythrocytes and lymphocytes susceptible to macrophage-mediated elimination, indicating that CD47 functions as a broadly used marker of self. However, it has not been clear whether regulated CD47 expression contributes to the physiological behavior of hematopoietic stem cells, particularly during inflammatory states in which both stem cell trafficking and macrophage activation are increased.

Malignant hematopoietic cells face a related but pathological problem. Leukemias arise within macrophage-rich tissues and disseminate through the blood, yet many evade destruction despite expressing abnormal or stress-associated features that could mark them for innate immune clearance. Cancer immunosurveillance has been studied largely in the context of lymphocytes and natural killer cells, whereas the possibility that macrophages impose a selective barrier on developing leukemias has been less well defined. If leukemic progenitors could acquire or constitutively maintain anti-phagocytic signals normally used by healthy stem cells, this would provide a simple mechanism by which malignant clones gain a survival advantage in the hematopoietic environment.

Here we show that CD47 is dynamically up-regulated on normal hematopoietic stem and progenitor cells during cytokine-induced mobilization and inflammatory stress, and that the amount of CD47 on these cells determines their susceptibility to macrophage clearance in vivo. We further show that mouse and human myeloid leukemias constitutively express elevated CD47, and that increasing CD47 expression on leukemia cells promotes tumor growth by protecting them from macrophage phagocytosis. These findings identify CD47 as a regulated determinant of hematopoietic stem cell fitness and as a mechanism co-opted by leukemia to evade innate immune surveillance.

They observed that normal hematopoietic stem cells (HSCs) transiently up-regulate CD47 during mobilization to shield themselves from macrophage-mediated destruction, a survival pathway that leukemia cells aggressively co-opt to evade immune surveillance. Alongside its companion paper, this cornerstone discovery cemented the foundational paradigm of macrophage immune checkpoints. By demonstrating that CD47 blockade permits the innate immune system to clear circulating cancer cells, it pioneered a major new branch of cancer immunotherapy aimed at stimulating phagocytosis, deeply influencing modern oncology.

paper_title: CD47 Is Up-Regulated on Circulating Hematopoietic Stem Cells and Leukemia Cells to Avoid Phagocytosis - 2009 - Cell
display_title: CD47 Is Up-Regulated on Circulating Hematopoietic Stem Cells and Leukemia Cells to Avoid Phagocytosis
title: CD47 Is Up-Regulated on Circulating Hematopoietic Stem Cells and Leukemia Cells to Avoid Phagocytosis
text:
The clearance of senescent, damaged, or apoptotic cells is primarily orchestrated by the innate immune system, specifically through the action of professional phagocytes such as macrophages. To navigate this highly vigilant environment and prevent the destruction of healthy tissues, cells must display markers that explicitly instruct macrophages to refrain from engulfment. These markers function as "don't eat me" signals, counterbalancing the "eat me" signals frequently exposed on dying cells.

CD47, also known as integrin-associated protein (IAP), is a transmembrane glycoprotein that serves as a critical "don't eat me" signal. It binds to its cognate receptor, signal regulatory protein alpha (SIRPα), which is heavily expressed on macrophages and dendritic cells. The engagement of SIRPα by CD47 initiates a signaling cascade that actively suppresses the cytoskeletal rearrangements required for phagocytosis, protecting the CD47-bearing cell from being engulfed. The physiological importance of this interaction is illustrated by the rapid clearance of CD47-deficient red blood cells and platelets by splenic macrophages when transfused into wild-type hosts.

While the role of CD47 in protecting circulating erythrocytes is well established, its function in regulating the survival of other migrating cell populations remains largely unexplored. Hematopoietic stem and progenitor cells (HSPCs) primarily reside within the protective niches of the bone marrow. However, under conditions of physiological stress, infection, or the administration of mobilizing cytokines, HSPCs exit the marrow and migrate into the peripheral blood, eventually seeding extramedullary sites such as the spleen and liver. During this migratory phase, HSPCs must pass through macrophage-rich sinusoidal networks, making them highly vulnerable to phagocytic clearance.

In this study, we investigated the mechanisms by which migrating HSPCs evade innate immune surveillance. We demonstrate that CD47 expression is transiently and robustly upregulated on both murine and human HSPCs during cytokine- or inflammation-induced mobilization, acting as an essential survival mechanism against macrophage-mediated phagocytosis. Strikingly, we discovered that this physiological response is hijacked by neoplastic cells. CD47 is constitutively overexpressed on a wide range of murine and human myeloid leukemias, conferring a critical survival advantage to leukemic progenitors by allowing them to evade immune clearance. Furthermore, overexpression of CD47 in a human leukemia cell line dramatically increases its pathogenicity in vivo, revealing CD47 upregulation as a fundamental mechanism of immune evasion in cancer.

They observed that normal hematopoietic stem cells (HSCs) transiently up-regulate CD47 during mobilization to shield themselves from macrophage-mediated destruction, a survival pathway that leukemia cells aggressively co-opt to evade immune surveillance. Alongside its companion paper, this cornerstone discovery cemented the foundational paradigm of macrophage immune checkpoints. By demonstrating that CD47 blockade permits the innate immune system to clear circulating cancer cells, it pioneered a major new branch of cancer immunotherapy aimed at stimulating phagocytosis, deeply influencing modern oncology.

paper_title: CD47 Is Up-Regulated on Circulating Hematopoietic Stem Cells and Leukemia Cells to Avoid Phagocytosis - 2009 - Cell
display_title: CD47 Is Up-Regulated on Circulating Hematopoietic Stem Cells and Leukemia Cells to Avoid Phagocytosis
title: CD47 Is Up-Regulated on Circulating Hematopoietic Stem Cells and Leukemia Cells to Avoid Phagocytosis
text:
Physiological significance of CD47 up-regulation

We demonstrate here for the first time that HSPCs up-regulate CD47 in response to an insult that induces mobilization. This then leads to the surprising finding that HSPCs themselves are regulated by phagocytosis in vivo. Hence, our results indicate that protection from phagocytosis is essential for HSC survival during migration to the periphery after a strong mobilizing or inflammatory stimulus. A recent report indicates that HSPCs can circulate through the lymphatic system as well. It will be interesting to see if these HSPCs up-regulate CD47 to evade the numerous sinusoidal macrophages in lymph nodes.

Since leukemias up-regulate CD47 as well, it appears they co-opt this normal physiological response to stress induced mobilization as a means to protect themselves at the expense of normal, non-neoplastic progenitors. In this manner, leukemic stem cells could quickly gain a survival advantage over normal stem cells, leading to their eventual take-over of the hematopoietic environment.

The regulatory mechanisms for CD47 expression in mobilization and leukemia remain unknown. Given that CD47 is up-regulated and down-regulated in such a temporally precise manner during mobilization suggests that there is tight control over its expression in normal physiology. Leukemias, however, seem to have constitutively high CD47 expression. The only published regulator of CD47 expression is the transcription factor nuclear respiratory factor 1 (Nrf1). It remains to be determined if Nrf1 or other regulatory elements are involved in the progression of human myeloid leukemia, and if there is a common regulatory pathway that operates in mobilization as well.

Macrophages are important mediators of tumor immunosurveillance

Many examples of tumor clearance by T, B, and NK cells have been described in the literature, indicating that a healthy immune system is essential for regulating nascent tumor growth. However, to date, few examples have been produced indicating that macrophage-mediated phagocytosis can check tumor development. Collectively, our studies reveal that ectopic overexpression of CD47 can enable otherwise immunogenic tumor cells to grow rapidly in a T, B, and NK-cell deficient host. Furthermore, this is likely to reflect a mechanism used by primary human myeloid leukemias to evade the host immune system since CD47 is up-regulated in nearly all sufficiently advanced murine and human myeloid leukemias examined thus far.

This form of immune evasion is particularly important since these cancers occupy sites of high macrophage infiltration. The leukemias studied here are clonal, yet found throughout the marrow (and in mouse, spleen and liver), all tissues with abundant intra-tissue macrophages as well as macrophage-lined sinusoids at the entry site of circulating blood cells. CD47 was first cloned as an ovarian tumor cell marker, indicating that it may play a role in preventing phagocytosis of other tissue cancers as well. Furthermore, solid tumors often metastasize to macrophage rich tissues such as liver, lung, bone, and lymph nodes, indicating that they must be able to escape macrophage-mediated killing in those tissues. Preventing CD47-SIRP alpha interaction could be doubly effective since antigens from phagocytosed tumor cells may be presented by profession antigen presenting cells such as macrophages or dendritic cells to activate an adaptive immune response, leading to further tumor destruction.

CD47 may be up-regulated because some cancers are more immunogenic than normal tissue

But why would leukemic progenitor cells require additional protection against phagocytosis? Like aged neutrophils or erythrocytes, tumor cells can display signs of cellular 'damage' and/ or glycosidic variations on their cell surface. There are well-established data that tumors can be recognized by NK cells via ligands that mark 'stressed self'. We propose that leukemic cells likewise express stress ligands that mark them for phagocytosis; by up-regulating CD47, this innate immunity check on tumor growth can be circumvented. One of these pro-phagocytic ligands, calreticulin, is known to be up-regulated in aged cells. It has been shown that its effects can be counteracted by expression of CD47. It remains to be seen if this or other stress ligands are expressed on nascent leukemic cells to mark them for destruction.

Therapeutics targeting CD47 may be clinically useful for treating cancer

Others have shown that antibodies that cross-link CD47 on the surface of chronic lymphocytic leukemia cells can induce a caspase-independent cell death. This phenomenon, while promising, appears to function primarily by inducing apoptosis, not necessarily by promoting phagocytosis. In fact, we show in a companion paper that anti-CD47 blocked CD47 hi human AML cells are phagocytosed by macrophages, without first becoming apoptotic.

In addition, our recent results in human AML indicate that increasing CD47 transcript levels are associated with shortened survival, allowing the level of expression of this gene to potentially be used as an independent prognostic variable. Combined with the data presented here where we show that CD47 expression levels affect the fitness of both normal HSC and leukemic cells, we propose that CD47 is a compelling target for therapeutic strategies that reduce its functional interaction with macrophage SIRP alpha and thus render these cancers susceptible to innate and adaptive immune system clearance.

AML study in 2009, this paper by Siddhartha Jaiswal, Irving Weissman, and colleagues demystified the physiological role of CD47 in normal stem cell biology and its malicious hijacking in leukemia. They observed that normal hematopoietic stem cells (HSCs) transiently up-regulate CD47 during mobilization to shield themselves from macrophage-mediated destruction, a survival pathway that leukemia cells aggressively co-opt to evade immune surveillance. Alongside its companion paper, this cornerstone discovery cemented the foundational paradigm of macrophage immune checkpoints. By demonstrating that CD47 blockade permits the innate immune system to clear circulating cancer cells, it pioneered a major new branch of cancer immunotherapy aimed at stimulating phagocytosis, deeply influencing modern oncology.

paper_title: CD47 Is Up-Regulated on Circulating Hematopoietic Stem Cells and Leukemia Cells to Avoid Phagocytosis - 2009 - Cell
display_title: CD47 Is Up-Regulated on Circulating Hematopoietic Stem Cells and Leukemia Cells to Avoid Phagocytosis
title: CD47 Is Up-Regulated on Circulating Hematopoietic Stem Cells and Leukemia Cells to Avoid Phagocytosis
text:
We demonstrate here that CD47 expression is dynamically regulated on hematopoietic stem and progenitor cells in response to mobilizing and inflammatory stimuli, and that this regulation has a direct functional consequence for stem cell survival in vivo. The increase in CD47 was transient, occurring at the time when HSPCs expand and enter the circulation, and returned toward baseline as mobilization resolved. These data indicate that CD47 is not simply a constitutive marker of hematopoietic cells, but rather a regulated protective signal that is induced when stem and progenitor cells are most likely to encounter macrophages in the marrow, spleen, liver, and circulation.

The requirement for CD47 in this setting appears to be protection from phagocytosis rather than an essential role in migration. CD47 deficient stem and progenitor cells were able to appear in peripheral tissues after mobilization, and IAP-/- mice did not display a major defect in the overall movement of KLS cells or GMPs. However, CD47 deficient HSCs failed to engraft wild-type recipients, were efficiently phagocytosed by macrophages in vitro, and were unable to seed wild-type marrow in parabiosis experiments. Moreover, HSCs expressing only one allele of CD47 had reduced fitness after inflammatory challenge, and this disadvantage was corrected by macrophage depletion. Thus, the level of CD47 expression determines the probability that an HSPC will be retained or eliminated when it enters macrophage-rich environments.

These findings suggest a physiological model in which inflammation creates two linked pressures on the hematopoietic system. First, inflammatory signals stimulate HSPC proliferation and migration, thereby increasing the number of cells that must traffic through vascular beds populated by active macrophages. Second, the same inflammatory state increases macrophage phagocytic activity. Up-regulation of CD47 may therefore serve as a temporally restricted adaptation that allows normal HSPCs to migrate during stress while avoiding inappropriate clearance. This model also raises the possibility that other migratory hematopoietic populations, including cells that circulate through lymphatic tissues, may similarly regulate CD47 when they encounter sinusoidal macrophages.

Leukemic cells appear to appropriate this normal protective mechanism. CD47 was increased in multiple murine myeloid leukemias and in human AML and blast crisis CML, but not uniformly in earlier or less aggressive myeloproliferative disorders. In human disease, the increase in CD47 accompanying progression from chronic phase CML to blast crisis suggests that fixed high expression of CD47 may be a late event associated with malignant evolution. Because leukemic stem and progenitor cells share the migratory behavior of normal HSCs and must occupy the same macrophage-rich hematopoietic organs, constitutive expression of a signal that normally protects migrating HSCs would provide a substantial selective advantage.

Our experimental studies with MOLM-13 cells directly support this interpretation. MOLM-13 cells with low endogenous CD47 failed to grow efficiently in immunodeficient mice, whereas ectopic expression of murine CD47 allowed these cells to engraft multiple hematopoietic tissues and cause disease. Direct intrafemoral injection did not rescue control cells, indicating that the failure of low CD47 cells was not explained simply by an inability to reach a marrow site. Instead, tumorigenic potential correlated with CD47 expression level. High-expressing cells were more pathogenic, low-expressing populations underwent selection for higher CD47 in vivo, and suppression of CD47 expression after transplant markedly reduced disseminated growth. These results demonstrate that CD47 is not merely a marker of leukemic cells but can be a determinant of their survival and expansion.

Macrophages are the principal mediators of this selective pressure. Depletion of macrophages prevented enrichment for CD47 hi MOLM-13 variants and allowed low CD47 cells to engraft at levels comparable to CD47-expressing cells. In vitro and short-term in vivo phagocytosis assays further showed that the likelihood of engulfment was inversely related to CD47 expression. Finally, blockade of SIRP alpha eliminated the protective effect of CD47, indicating that the CD47-SIRP alpha interaction is the relevant pathway through which leukemic cells inhibit phagocytosis. Together, these results identify macrophage-mediated phagocytosis as a significant form of tumor immunosurveillance, even in hosts lacking T, B, and NK cell function.

Why leukemic cells require such protection remains an important question. Tumor cells may display pro-phagocytic signals that distinguish them from normal tissue, analogous to the altered surface features of aged erythrocytes or damaged cells. Stress-associated ligands such as calreticulin can promote phagocytosis and may be counterbalanced by CD47. We propose that leukemic transformation creates or exposes such prophagocytic signals, and that increased CD47 expression permits malignant cells to evade this innate checkpoint. The regulatory mechanism responsible for CD47 up-regulation is also unknown. The precise induction and resolution of CD47 expression during normal mobilization implies tight transcriptional or post-transcriptional control, whereas leukemic cells maintain constitutively high expression. Defining whether Nrf1 or other regulatory pathways control CD47 in both inflammation and leukemia will be important for understanding how this physiological program becomes fixed in cancer.

These observations have therapeutic implications. Because CD47 expression can determine the fitness of both normal HSCs and leukemic cells, therapies that interrupt CD47-SIRP alpha signaling may render leukemic cells susceptible to macrophage-mediated clearance. Such an approach could have additional benefit if phagocytosed tumor material is processed and presented by macrophages or dendritic cells, thereby engaging adaptive immunity. The association of increased CD47 transcript levels with poorer survival in human AML further suggests that CD47 may serve both as a prognostic marker and as a functional target. Our findings therefore support the development of strategies that block the CD47-mediated "don't eat me" signal in leukemia and potentially in other cancers that disseminate to macrophage-rich tissues.

Alongside its companion paper, this cornerstone discovery cemented the foundational paradigm of macrophage immune checkpoints. By demonstrating that CD47 blockade permits the innate immune system to clear circulating cancer cells, it pioneered a major new branch of cancer immunotherapy aimed at stimulating phagocytosis, deeply influencing modern oncology.

paper_title: CD47 Is an Adverse Prognostic Factor and Therapeutic Antibody Target on Human Acute Myeloid Leukemia Stem Cells - 2009 - Cell
display_title: CD47 Is an Adverse Prognostic Factor and Therapeutic Antibody Target on Human Acute Myeloid Leukemia Stem Cells
title: CD47 Is an Adverse Prognostic Factor and Therapeutic Antibody Target on Human Acute Myeloid Leukemia Stem Cells
text: Acute myelogenous leukemia (AML) is organized as a cellular hierarchy initiated and maintained by a subset of self-renewing leukemia stem cells (LSC). We hypothesized that increased CD47 expression on human AML LSC contributes to pathogenesis by inhibiting their phagocytosis through the interaction of CD47 with an inhibitory receptor on phagocytes. We found that CD47 was more highly expressed on AML LSC than their normal counterparts, and that increased CD47 expression predicted worse overall survival in 3 independent cohorts of adult AML patients. Furthermore, blocking monoclonal antibodies directed against CD47 preferentially enabled phagocytosis of AML LSC and inhibited their engraftment in vivo . Finally, treatment of human AML LSC-engrafted mice with anti-CD47 antibody depleted AML and targeted AML LSC. In summary, increased CD47 expression is an independent poor prognostic factor that can be targeted on human AML stem cells with blocking monoclonal antibodies capable of enabling phagocytosis of LSC.
historical_context: Led by Ravi Majeti, Irving Weissman, and colleagues in 2009, this pivotal paper identified the CD47 protein as a critical "don't eat me" signal heavily overexpressed on human acute myeloid leukemia (AML) stem cells. The team demonstrated that heightened CD47 expression correlated directly with poor clinical outcomes, and that blocking CD47 with monoclonal antibodies unleashed macrophages to phagocytose and eradicate leukemia cells both *in vitro* and *in vivo*. This work was a massive breakthrough in cancer immunology. It broadened the horizon of immunotherapy beyond T-cells to engage the innate immune system (macrophages) and established the CD47-SIRPα axis as a universal innate immune checkpoint in cancer, igniting a wave of clinical trials for CD47-blocking therapies across various hematologic and solid malignancies.
