title: CD47 Is an Adverse Prognostic Factor and Therapeutic Antibody Target on Human Acute Myeloid Leukemia Stem Cells
text: We report here the identification of increased CD47 expression on human acute myeloid leukemia (AML) stem cells (LSC) compared to normal hematopoietic stem and multipotent progenitor cells. Analysis of three independent adult AML cohorts demonstrated that high CD47 expression was associated with FLT3-ITD mutations and independently predicted worse event-free and overall survival. In primary AML specimens, differential CD47 expression enabled prospective separation of leukemic cells from residual normal progenitors. We hypothesized that CD47 contributes to AML pathogenesis by inhibiting macrophage phagocytosis through signal regulatory protein alpha (SIRP alpha). Consistent with this hypothesis, blocking monoclonal antibodies directed against CD47, or antibody-mediated disruption of SIRP alpha, preferentially enabled phagocytosis of AML LSC but not normal CD34+ cells in vitro. Treatment of AML-engrafted immunodeficient mice with anti-CD47 antibody rapidly eliminated circulating and bone marrow leukemia by a phagocyte-dependent mechanism and prevented secondary engraftment. These results establish CD47 as an adverse prognostic factor and provide a rationale for anti-CD47 antibody therapy in AML.

text: Acute myeloid leukemia (AML) is organized as a cellular hierarchy maintained by leukemia stem cells (LSC), which must be eliminated to eradicate disease. These findings establish CD47 as an adverse prognostic factor and therapeutic antibody target in human AML.

title: CD47 Is an Adverse Prognostic Factor and Therapeutic Antibody Target on Human Acute Myeloid Leukemia Stem Cells
text: Acute myeloid leukemia (AML) is organized hierarchically, maintained by leukemia stem cells (LSC) that evade standard therapies. Eradicating these cells requires identifying specific molecular targets. Here we show that CD47, a transmembrane protein functioning as a 'don't eat me' signal through its interaction with macrophage receptor SIRPα, is highly expressed on human AML LSC relative to normal hematopoietic stem cells. Clinical analysis of adult AML patients reveals that elevated CD47 expression is a strong adverse prognostic factor associated with worse event-free and overall survival. Functionally, blocking the CD47-SIRPα interaction with specific monoclonal antibodies promotes the phagocytosis of AML LSC by macrophages in vitro. In vivo, anti-CD47 antibody treatment clears leukemia cells from peripheral blood and bone marrow, prevents engraftment, and specifically targets LSC in xenograft models. Thus, CD47 is a critical immune evasion mechanism and a promising therapeutic target for AML.

title: CD47 Is an Adverse Prognostic Factor and Therapeutic Antibody Target on Human Acute Myeloid Leukemia Stem Cells
text:
According to the cancer stem cell model, tumors are organized as a cellular hierarchy maintained by a small pool of self-renewing cancer stem cells which must be eliminated in order to eradicate the tumor . For the development of cancer stem cell-targeted therapies, it is necessary to identify molecules and pathways that are preferentially expressed in these cancer stem cells and that are critical for pathogenesis.

To date, human acute myeloid leukemia (AML) stem cells (LSC) are the most well studied cancer stem cell population . AML is an aggressive malignancy with five year overall survival between 30-40%, and much lower for those over age 65 . Cytogenetic abnormalities are prognostic in adults with AML, however, up to 50% have a normal karyotype . In these patients, the presence of specific molecular mutations can provide prognostic information, particularly internal tandem duplications within the fms -related tyrosine kinase 3 gene ( FLT3 -ITD) .

In published reports assaying a variety of subtypes of AML, LSC were found to be negative for expression of lineage markers (Lin -), positive for expression of CD34, and negative for expression of CD38 . We have recently shown that the Lin -CD34+CD38 -CD90 -fraction of human cord blood contains a non-HSC multipotent progenitor (MPP), and have hypothesized that this MPP is the cell of origin for human AML . Consistent with this hypothesis, we have shown that preleukemic mutations occur in a clonal HSC population, eventually leading to the development of LSC at the MPP stage in AML or the granulocyte-macrophage progenitor (GMP) stage in myeloid blast crisis CML .

We report here the identification of higher expression of CD47 on AML LSC compared to their normal counterparts, HSC and MPP, a finding corroborated by microarray gene expression analysis . CD47 is a widely expressed transmembrane protein . CD47 serves as the ligand for signal regulatory protein alpha . We hypothesize that increased expression of CD47 on human AML contributes to pathogenesis by inhibiting phagocytosis of these cells through the interaction of CD47 with SIRP α .
historical_context: Led by Ravi Majeti, Irving Weissman, and colleagues in 2009, this pivotal paper identified the CD47 protein as a critical "don't eat me" signal heavily overexpressed on human acute myeloid leukemia (AML) stem cells. The team demonstrated that heightened CD47 expression correlated directly with poor clinical outcomes, and that blocking CD47 with monoclonal antibodies unleashed macrophages to phagocytose and eradicate leukemia cells both *in vitro* and *in vivo*. This work was a massive breakthrough in cancer immunology. It broadened the horizon of immunotherapy beyond T-cells to engage the innate immune system (macrophages) and established the CD47-SIRPα axis as a universal innate immune checkpoint in cancer, igniting a wave of clinical trials for CD47-blocking therapies across various hematologic and solid malignancies.

display_title: CD47 Is an Adverse Prognostic Factor and Therapeutic Antibody Target on Human Acute Myeloid Leukemia Stem Cells
title: CD47 Is an Adverse Prognostic Factor and Therapeutic Antibody Target on Human Acute Myeloid Leukemia Stem Cells
text:
Acute myelogenous leukemia (AML) is an aggressive malignancy of the hematopoietic system characterized by the accumulation of immature myeloid blasts and failure of normal blood formation. Although many patients achieve morphologic remissions with induction chemotherapy, relapse remains common and is thought to result from persistence of rare leukemia stem cells (LSC) capable of self-renewal and re-initiation of disease. These cells are enriched within the Lin-CD34+CD38- fraction of many AML specimens, a compartment that also contains normal hematopoietic stem cells (HSC) and multipotent progenitors (MPP), creating a major therapeutic challenge: the identification of mechanisms that distinguish malignant from normal stem cells.

Several cell-surface molecules have been reported to be preferentially expressed on AML LSC, including CD44, CD96, CD123, and CLL-1. Such molecules are useful both as markers for disease assessment and as candidate therapeutic targets. However, most current therapies for AML do not specifically eliminate LSC, and few strategies directly exploit innate immune mechanisms that may normally recognize and remove malignant cells. Phagocytosis by macrophages is regulated by the balance of pro-phagocytic and anti-phagocytic signals. CD47 is a widely expressed cell-surface protein that binds signal regulatory protein alpha (SIRPα) on phagocytes and delivers an inhibitory “don’t eat me” signal. This interaction has been implicated in the protection of normal cells from macrophage-mediated clearance, suggesting that increased CD47 expression could provide malignant cells with a mechanism of immune evasion.

We hypothesized that human AML LSC upregulate CD47 to inhibit phagocytosis and thereby promote leukemia pathogenesis. This model predicts that CD47 expression should distinguish AML LSC from normal hematopoietic stem and progenitor cells, correlate with adverse clinical outcome, and represent a therapeutic target whose blockade enables macrophage-mediated elimination of LSC. Here we show that CD47 is more highly expressed on AML LSC than on normal HSC and MPP, that increased CD47 expression is an independent adverse prognostic factor in adult AML, and that blocking monoclonal antibodies directed against CD47 preferentially enable phagocytosis and in vivo targeting of human AML LSC.
historical_context: Led by Ravi Majeti, Irving Weissman, and colleagues in 2009, this pivotal paper identified the CD47 protein as a critical "don't eat me" signal heavily overexpressed on human acute myeloid leukemia (AML) stem cells. The team demonstrated that heightened CD47 expression correlated directly with poor clinical outcomes, and that blocking CD47 with monoclonal antibodies unleashed macrophages to phagocytose and eradicate leukemia cells both *in vitro* and *in vivo*. This work was a massive breakthrough in cancer immunology. It broadened the horizon of immunotherapy beyond T-cells to engage the innate immune system (macrophages) and established the CD47-SIRPα axis as a universal innate immune checkpoint in cancer, igniting a wave of clinical trials for CD47-blocking therapies across various hematologic and solid malignancies.

title: CD47 Is an Adverse Prognostic Factor and Therapeutic Antibody Target on Human Acute Myeloid Leukemia Stem Cells
text:
Acute myeloid leukemia (AML) is a devastating hematologic malignancy characterized by the accumulation of immature myeloid blasts in the bone marrow and blood. Despite intensive chemotherapy, the overall prognosis for adult AML patients remains poor, largely due to high rates of relapse. It is now widely recognized that AML is hierarchically organized and driven by a rare subpopulation of cells termed leukemia stem cells (LSCs). AML LSCs possess self-renewal capacity and are inherently resistant to conventional chemotherapies, allowing them to persist as minimal residual disease and initiate relapse. Consequently, developing novel therapies that specifically eradicate the AML LSC population is essential for achieving durable cures.

The innate immune system, particularly macrophages, plays a vital role in clearing abnormal or damaged cells. To evade macrophage-mediated destruction, normal cells display "don't eat me" signals. CD47 is a widely expressed transmembrane protein that interacts with signal regulatory protein alpha (SIRPα), an inhibitory receptor on macrophages. The binding of CD47 to SIRPα initiates a signaling cascade that potently inhibits phagocytosis.

We previously demonstrated that CD47 is upregulated on murine hematopoietic stem cells during mobilization to protect them from macrophage clearance, and is also highly expressed on various murine and human leukemias. We hypothesized that increased CD47 expression on human AML LSCs contributes to disease pathogenesis by allowing these cells to evade innate immune surveillance. Here, we investigate the clinical and therapeutic significance of CD47 in human AML. We find that CD47 is significantly upregulated on human AML LSCs compared to normal hematopoietic stem cells. Furthermore, we demonstrate that high CD47 expression is an independent, adverse prognostic factor for overall and event-free survival in three independent cohorts of adult AML patients. Crucially, we show that disruption of the CD47-SIRPα interaction using blocking monoclonal antibodies directed against CD47 enables the preferential phagocytosis of AML LSCs by macrophages in vitro and inhibits leukemic engraftment in vivo. These findings establish CD47 as an important prognostic marker and a novel, highly promising therapeutic target for the elimination of AML LSCs.

This work was a massive breakthrough in cancer immunology. It broadened the horizon of immunotherapy beyond T-cells to engage the innate immune system (macrophages) and established the CD47-SIRPα axis as a universal innate immune checkpoint in cancer, igniting a wave of clinical trials for CD47-blocking therapies across various hematologic and solid malignancies.

paper_title: CD47 Is an Adverse Prognostic Factor and Therapeutic Antibody Target on Human Acute Myeloid Leukemia Stem Cells - 2009 - Cell
display_title: CD47 Is an Adverse Prognostic Factor and Therapeutic Antibody Target on Human Acute Myeloid Leukemia Stem Cells
title: CD47 Is an Adverse Prognostic Factor and Therapeutic Antibody Target on Human Acute Myeloid Leukemia Stem Cells
text:
We report here the identification of higher expression of CD47 on AML LSC compared to their normal counterparts and hypothesize that increased expression of CD47 on human AML contributes to pathogenesis by inhibiting phagocytosis of these cells through the interaction of CD47 with SIRP α . Consistent with this hypothesis, we demonstrate that increased expression of CD47 in human AML is associated with decreased overall survival. We also demonstrate that disruption of the CD47-SIRP α interaction with monoclonal antibodies directed against CD47 preferentially enables phagocytosis of AML

LSC by macrophages, inhibits engraftment, and targets AML LSC in vivo . Together, these results establish the rationale for considering the use of an anti-CD47 monoclonal antibody as a novel therapy for human AML.

The enabling of phagocytosis by blocking monoclonal antibodies directed against CD47 is a novel mechanism of action for a therapeutic monoclonal antibody in the treatment of cancer. Currently approved antibody therapies are believed to act via stimulation of antibodydependent cellular cytotoxicity (ADCC), disruption of critical receptor-ligand interactions, or through unknown mechanisms . Blocking anti-CD47 monoclonal antibodies would treat human AML by enabling phagocytosis and elimination of AML LSC . In support of this mechanism of action in vivo , we show that treatment of human AML LSC-engrafted mice with anti-CD47 antibody results in rapid phagocytosis of AML cells , and,, that depletion of phagocytes with clodronate abrogates this effect .

As demonstrated here, CD47 contributes to pathogenesis by conferring a survival advantage to LSC and progeny blasts through evasion of phagocytosis by the innate immune system. Moreover, some dendritic cells express SIRP α , and we propose that increased CD47 expression on AML LSC also serves to prevent the activation of adaptive T cell immune responses.

AML LSC are enriched in the Lin -CD34+CD38 -fraction, which in normal bone marrow contains HSC and MPP. The identification of cell surface molecules that can distinguish between leukemic and normal stem cells is essential for flow cytometry-based assessment of minimal residual disease (MRD) and for the development of prospective separation strategies for use in cellular therapies. Several candidate molecules have recently been identified, including CD123 , CD44 , CD96 , CLL-1 (van Rhenen et al., 2007), and now CD47. We demonstrate that not only is CD47 more highly expressed on AML LSC compared to normal HSC and MPP, but also that this differential expression can be used to separate normal HSC/MPP from leukemia cells. This is the first demonstration of the prospective separation of normal HSC from leukemia cells in the same patient sample, and offers the possibility of leukemia-depleted autologous HSC transplantation therapies.

Cell surface molecules preferentially expressed on AML LSC compared to their normal counterparts are candidates for targeting with therapeutic monoclonal antibodies. Thus far, several molecules have been targeted on AML including CD33 , CD44 , CD123 , and now CD47. Here we report that a monoclonal antibody directed against CD47 targets AML LSC in vivo , as shown by direct reduction in the percentage of human CD34+ LSC-enriched leukemia cells in the bone marrow, and complete elimination of engraftment in secondary transplants .

Targeting of leukemia cells and cell lines with anti-CD47 antibodies has previously been reported to directly induce apoptosis. Treatment of primary human B-CLL cells was shown to induce caspase-independent cell death , while a different anti-CD47 antibody was shown to induce apoptosis of several hematopoietic cell lines . These reports raise the alternative hypothesis that anti-CD47 antibodies induce apoptosis of AML LSC, which are then recognized by macrophages and phagocytosed. However, several caveats must be considered when comparing these prior reports to our current study. First, the report on B-CLL involved a mature lymphocytic neoplasm, which is very biologically different from immature aggressive AML, and demonstrated apoptosis not with soluble antibody, but only with cross-linking of antibody, which can result in different effects. Second, the additional reports utilized cell lines and not primary leukemia cells, which are very biologically distinct regarding both proliferation and cell death. Ultimately, we feel that it is not possible to extrapolate the effect of anti-CD47 antibodies from these reports to primary human AML cells.

Several lines of evidence suggest that targeting of CD47 with a monoclonal antibody acts by disrupting the CD47-SIRP α interaction, thereby preventing a phagocytic inhibitory signal, rather than by acting through induction of apoptosis, ADCC, or other mechanisms. First, two blocking anti-CD47 antibodies enabled AML LSC phagocytosis, while one non-blocking antibody did not, even though all three bind the cells similarly. Second, an anti-mouse SIRP α antibody also enabled phagocytosis of human AML LSC by mouse macrophages, demonstrating phagocytosis without direct binding of antibody to AML LSC . Third, in the case of the B6H12.2 antibody used for most of our experiments, no direct induction of apoptosis of primary AML LSC was detected when added either as a soluble antibody or as an immobilized plate-bound antibody. Fourth, phagocytosis of AML LSC was detected as early as 15 minutes after addition of anti-CD47 antibody, while no apoptosis was detected at 2 hours. In fact, only minimal Annexin V-positive staining was detected on Jurkat cells 2 hours after incubation with immobilized plate-bound anti-CD47 antibody . Fifth, if phagocytosis were occurring secondary to apoptosis, then depletion of phagocytes with clodronate should not inhibit the effect of the antibody, which would still directly kill the leukemia cells. However, clodronate did inhibit the ability of anti-CD47 antibody to deplete human AML , indicating that enabling of phagocytosis is the most likely mechanism. Finally, the isotype-matched anti-CD45 antibody, which also binds LSC, failed to produce the same effects, making ADCC less likely . In fact, the B6H12.2 antibody is mouse isotype IgG1, which is less effective at engaging mouse Fc receptors than antibodies of isotype IgG2a or IgG2b . For human clinical therapies, blocking CD47 on AML LSC with humanized monoclonal antibodies should promote LSC phagocytosis through a similar mechanism, as indicated by the human macrophage-mediated in vitro phagocytosis .

Higher CD47 expression is detected on AML LSC; however, CD47 is expressed on normal tissues, including bone marrow HSC. We identified a preferential effect of anti-CD47 antibodies in enabling the phagocytosis of AML LSC compared to normal bone marrow CD34 + cells by human macrophages in vitro . In fact, no increased phagocytosis of normal CD34+ cells compared to isotype control was detected, suggesting that blocking CD47 with monoclonal antibodies is a viable therapeutic strategy for human AML. We speculate that this difference is due to the presence of as yet unknown stimuli for phagocytosis on AML LSC that are lacking on normal CD34+ cells. We have now administered a blocking anti-mouse CD47 antibody to normal mice on a schedule similar to the anti-human CD47 antibody, and find coating of all bone marrow cells with no reduction in HSC, no liver or kidney toxicity, and only isolated neutropenia in complete blood counts and Supplemental Tables 3,4), suggesting that this antibody has no unacceptable toxicity and does not deplete normal mouse HSC.

The experimental evidence presented here provides the rationale for anti-CD47 monoclonal antibodies as monotherapy for AML. However, such antibodies may be equally, if not more effective as part of a combination strategy. The combination of a blocking anti-CD47 antibody with a second antibody able to bind an LSC-specific molecule (for example CD96) and engage Fc receptors on phagocytes may result in a synergistic stimulus for phagocytosis and specific elimination of AML LSC . Furthermore, combinations of monoclonal antibodies to AML LSC that include blocking anti-CD47 and human IgG1 antibodies directed against two other cell surface antigens will be more likely to eliminate leukemia cells with pre-existing epitope variants or antigen loss that are likely to recur in patients treated with a single antibody.

This work was a massive breakthrough in cancer immunology. It broadened the horizon of immunotherapy beyond T-cells to engage the innate immune system (macrophages) and established the CD47-SIRPα axis as a universal innate immune checkpoint in cancer, igniting a wave of clinical trials for CD47-blocking therapies across various hematologic and solid malignancies.

paper_title: CD47 Is an Adverse Prognostic Factor and Therapeutic Antibody Target on Human Acute Myeloid Leukemia Stem Cells - 2009 - Cell
display_title: CD47 Is an Adverse Prognostic Factor and Therapeutic Antibody Target on Human Acute Myeloid Leukemia Stem Cells
title: CD47 Is an Adverse Prognostic Factor and Therapeutic Antibody Target on Human Acute Myeloid Leukemia Stem Cells
text:
The results presented here identify CD47 as both a clinically relevant marker and a therapeutic target on human AML LSC. We found that CD47 was more highly expressed on AML LSC than on normal HSC and MPP, and that increased expression of CD47 was associated with inferior clinical outcome in three independent cohorts of adult AML patients. Moreover, disruption of the CD47-SIRP alpha interaction with blocking monoclonal antibodies enabled phagocytosis of AML LSC in vitro and eliminated leukemia in xenotransplanted mice through a macrophage-dependent mechanism. Together, these data support a model in which AML LSC exploit increased CD47 expression as a mechanism of immune evasion, and suggest that therapeutic blockade of this pathway may permit the innate immune system to recognize and eliminate the cells responsible for leukemia propagation.

Several lines of evidence indicate that CD47 expression has biological significance in AML rather than simply representing a surface marker associated with disease. First, CD47 expression was elevated on the LSC-enriched fraction of multiple AML specimens compared to the corresponding normal progenitor populations. Second, within a single specimen containing both leukemic and residual normal progenitors in the Lin -CD34+CD38 -compartment, the CD47 hi cells contained the FLT3 -ITD mutation and were part of the leukemic clone, whereas the CD47 lo cells lacked the mutation and reconstituted normal hematopoiesis. This observation suggests that increased CD47 expression can distinguish leukemic from normal progenitors in some cases, and is consistent with the hypothesis that CD47 upregulation is acquired during leukemic transformation or progression. Finally, the functional consequence of CD47 expression was demonstrated by the ability of blocking anti-CD47 antibodies to permit phagocytosis of AML LSC, while non-blocking anti-CD47 antibody and control antibodies did not.

The association of increased CD47 expression with adverse outcome further supports the importance of this pathway in human AML. High CD47 expression predicted worse overall survival in a cohort containing diverse cytogenetic and molecular subtypes, and this finding was validated in two independent cohorts of patients with normal karyotype AML. In the validation cohort, increased CD47 expression was associated with both inferior event-free survival and overall survival. Although high CD47 expression correlated with FLT3 -ITD, a known adverse prognostic marker in AML, the prognostic effect of CD47 was also observed among patients lacking FLT3 -ITD and remained significant in multivariable analysis. These findings indicate that CD47 expression provides prognostic information beyond established clinical and molecular features. Since CD47 expression is linked here to a defined mechanism of resistance to phagocytosis, the adverse clinical association may reflect a contribution of immune evasion to disease persistence, relapse, and poor survival.

The cancer stem cell model predicts that durable therapeutic responses require elimination of the self-renewing population capable of maintaining and reinitiating the disease. AML has provided the clearest experimental evidence for such a population, as human AML LSC can be prospectively enriched and assayed by transplantation into immunodeficient mice. Most conventional therapies are evaluated by their ability to reduce bulk blast burden, but relapse may result from persistence of LSC that survive treatment. Our data demonstrate that anti-CD47 antibody treatment targets the LSC compartment functionally, as measured by loss of secondary transplantability. This is a stringent assay of leukemia-propagating activity and suggests that CD47 blockade may not merely debulk leukemia, but may eliminate the cells required for disease maintenance. The reduction in CD34+ leukemia cells in treated mice is consistent with this conclusion, although the secondary transplantation assay provides the most direct evidence.

The mechanism of anti-CD47 antibody activity appears to be distinct from direct induction of apoptosis. Prior reports have shown that antibodies to CD47 can induce apoptosis in some malignant hematopoietic cells, particularly under conditions of immobilization or cross-linking. In contrast, the blocking anti-CD47 antibody used here did not induce Annexin V positivity in primary AML LSC under the conditions tested, including when plate-bound. Phagocytosis was detectable rapidly after antibody treatment, at time points preceding detectable apoptosis. In vivo, GFP-labeled human AML cells were found within mouse phagocytes shortly after anti-CD47 antibody administration, and depletion of phagocytes with liposomal clodronate impaired the ability of anti-CD47 antibody to eliminate leukemia. These findings indicate that the dominant mechanism of leukemia clearance in this system is the enabling of phagocytosis through disruption of an inhibitory signal delivered by CD47 to SIRP alpha.

The ability of anti-CD47 antibody to spare normal hematopoietic cells in these assays is an important feature of this therapeutic strategy. CD47 is expressed at low levels on most normal tissues, including hematopoietic cells, raising the possibility that systemic blockade could cause toxicity. However, blocking anti-CD47 antibodies enabled phagocytosis of AML LSC but not normal human bone marrow CD34+ cells in vitro. In addition, treatment of wild type mice with a blocking anti-mouse CD47 antibody resulted in coating of bone marrow cells without reduction in marrow cellularity, KLS cells, or HSC, and without evidence of anemia or hepatic or renal damage. Isolated neutropenia was observed, indicating that hematopoietic toxicity will require careful evaluation. Nevertheless, the differential susceptibility of AML cells and normal progenitors to phagocytosis suggests that blockade of CD47 is not sufficient by itself to trigger engulfment of all CD47-expressing cells.

One possible explanation for this selectivity is that phagocytosis depends on the balance between inhibitory signals, such as CD47, and prophagocytic signals that are more abundant on leukemia cells than on normal progenitors. In this model, increased CD47 expression allows AML LSC to counteract prophagocytic stimuli generated during transformation, thereby preserving survival in the presence of innate immune effector cells. Blocking CD47 removes this inhibitory signal and exposes leukemia cells to macrophage-mediated clearance. Normal hematopoietic cells, which may express fewer prophagocytic signals, would be less susceptible despite antibody coating. Identification of the relevant prophagocytic signals on AML LSC will be important for understanding the selectivity of CD47 blockade and may suggest rational combinations with agents that increase leukemia cell recognition.

The studies described here also have implications for the development of clinical biomarkers. Since CD47 expression can be measured by gene expression profiling or flow cytometry, it may be possible to incorporate CD47 into risk stratification algorithms, particularly for patients with normal karyotype AML. CD47 expression may also identify patients most likely to benefit from therapies targeting the CD47-SIRP alpha axis. However, the relationship between CD47 expression level and therapeutic response remains to be defined. Although high CD47 expression is associated with poor outcome and AML LSC express increased CD47 compared to normal progenitors, even cases with lower expression may remain dependent on CD47-mediated inhibition of phagocytosis. Future studies should determine whether CD47 expression, SIRP alpha genotype, macrophage abundance, or additional prophagocytic markers predict response to CD47 blockade.

Several limitations should be considered. The clinical analyses were retrospective and relied on previously reported patient cohorts, and therefore prospective validation will be necessary to establish CD47 as a prognostic biomarker in current therapeutic settings. The functional studies used xenotransplantation into immunodeficient mice, a system that permits assay of human AML LSC but does not fully recapitulate the human immune microenvironment. Moreover, the therapeutic antibodies used in these experiments were not optimized for clinical application, and antibody dose, schedule, pharmacokinetics, and potential toxicity will require further investigation. Despite these limitations, the convergence of expression, prognostic, in vitro functional, and in vivo therapeutic data provides strong support for CD47 as a biologically relevant target in AML.

In summary, increased CD47 expression on human AML LSC represents a mechanism by which leukemia cells evade phagocytosis and contributes to adverse clinical outcome. Blocking monoclonal antibodies directed against CD47 disrupt this protective signal, enable macrophage-mediated phagocytosis of AML LSC, reduce leukemic engraftment, and eliminate leukemia-propagating activity in vivo. These findings provide evidence that the innate immune checkpoint mediated by CD47 and SIRP alpha can be therapeutically targeted in AML. More broadly, they suggest that successful cancer stem cell-directed therapy may require not only inhibition of intrinsic survival and self-renewal pathways, but also reversal of immune evasion mechanisms that protect these cells from host clearance.

paper_title: CD47 Is an Adverse Prognostic Factor and Therapeutic Antibody Target on Human Acute Myeloid Leukemia Stem Cells - 2009 - Cell
display_title: CD47 Is an Adverse Prognostic Factor and Therapeutic Antibody Target on Human Acute Myeloid Leukemia Stem Cells
title: CD47 Is an Adverse Prognostic Factor and Therapeutic Antibody Target on Human Acute Myeloid Leukemia Stem Cells
text:
The cancer stem cell hypothesis posits that to achieve long-term remission and ultimate cure in malignancies like acute myeloid leukemia (AML), therapies must effectively eradicate the rare, self-renewing subpopulation of leukemia stem cells (LSC) that drive and maintain the disease. Our study provides compelling evidence that the cell-surface molecule CD47 is not only a critical functional marker of human AML LSC but also a highly significant prognostic factor and a potent therapeutic target. We have demonstrated that CD47 is preferentially upregulated on AML LSC compared to their normal hematopoietic stem cell (HSC) and multipotent progenitor (MPP) counterparts. This differential expression strongly suggests a pathogenic role for CD47 in leukemogenesis, enabling LSC to evade innate immune surveillance by signaling through the inhibitory macrophage receptor SIRPα. By circumventing macrophage-mediated phagocytosis, AML LSC secure a crucial survival advantage within the bone marrow niche, allowing them to propagate the leukemic clone and ultimately overwhelm normal hematopoiesis.

The clinical relevance of CD47 upregulation is profoundly illustrated by our extensive prognostic analysis. Across multiple, independent cohorts of adult AML patients, encompassing diverse cytogenetic and molecular subtypes, increased CD47 mRNA expression consistently predicted worse event-free and overall survival. The prognostic power of CD47 was robust even when stratifying patients within the clinically challenging subset of normal karyotype AML. Notably, while CD47 expression correlated with the presence of the FLT3-ITD mutation—a well-established marker of poor prognosis—multivariate analysis confirmed that elevated CD47 remains an independent predictor of adverse clinical outcomes. This independence indicates that CD47-mediated immune evasion is a fundamental, perhaps convergent, mechanism of leukemic progression that operates across various mutational backgrounds. The identification of CD47 as a prognostic biomarker offers immediate clinical utility, providing physicians with an additional tool to stratify risk and tailor aggressive consolidative therapies, such as allogeneic stem cell transplantation, for patients most likely to experience disease relapse.

Beyond its prognostic value, our findings firmly establish CD47 as an actionable therapeutic target on human AML LSC. We hypothesized that disrupting the CD47-SIRPα interaction using blocking monoclonal antibodies would strip the leukemia cells of their "don't eat me" signal, thereby unleashing the phagocytic capacity of macrophages. Our in vitro experiments unequivocally supported this hypothesis; treatment with blocking anti-CD47 antibodies (B6H12.2 and BRIC126) enabled robust phagocytosis of AML LSC by both human and murine macrophages, whereas non-blocking antibodies and isotype controls had no effect. Crucially, this antibody-mediated phagocytosis was selective for leukemic cells, as normal CD34+ bone marrow cells were spared. Furthermore, our mechanistic investigations rigorously ruled out direct antibody-induced apoptosis as the primary mode of action. Unlike previously reported observations with immobilized anti-CD47 antibodies on other malignant cell types, soluble B6H12.2 did not induce Annexin V positivity or apoptosis in primary human AML LSC prior to phagocytic engulfment. This confirms that the therapeutic efficacy of our anti-CD47 blockade relies fundamentally on the restoration of macrophage-mediated innate immune clearance.

The translation of these in vitro findings to in vivo preclinical models yielded dramatic results. Using our robust xenotransplantation model of human AML LSC in NOG mice, we demonstrated that anti-CD47 antibody therapy not only clears circulating leukemic blasts but also effectively targets the bone marrow-resident leukemia stem cells. The pre-coating strategy showed that antibody engagement alone is sufficient to prevent leukemic engraftment. More importantly, the therapeutic treatment strategy, wherein antibody was administered to mice with established human AML, resulted in rapid and near-complete elimination of the leukemic burden. The profound reduction in bone marrow engraftment and the histological evidence of leukemic clearance alongside phagocytosing macrophages vividly illustrate the in vivo potency of this approach. The critical role of macrophages in executing this therapeutic effect was further validated by our macrophage depletion experiments using liposomal clodronate, which completely abrogated the antileukemic activity of the anti-CD47 antibody.

Perhaps the most significant finding regarding the therapeutic potential of anti-CD47 therapy is its ability to directly target and eliminate the LSC compartment. Eradicating the bulk blast population is often achievable with standard induction chemotherapy, but relapse occurs because the quiescent LSC are spared. Our secondary transplantation assays provided definitive functional proof that anti-CD47 antibody treatment exhausts the AML LSC pool. Bone marrow from treated mice completely failed to engraft secondary recipients, confirming that the self-renewing capacity of the leukemia had been extinguished. This LSC-depleting capability is the hallmark of a curative therapeutic strategy and positions anti-CD47 blockade as a transformative approach in AML treatment, potentially overcoming the high relapse rates associated with current cytotoxic regimens.

A major theoretical concern with targeting a widely expressed molecule like CD47 is the potential for unacceptable off-target toxicity, particularly the destruction of normal hematopoietic cells and erythrocytes. However, our evaluation of a blocking anti-mouse CD47 antibody in a syngeneic, serially transplantable mouse model of AML provides highly encouraging safety data. Despite achieving complete antibody coating of bone marrow cells, daily administration of the anti-CD47 antibody for two weeks did not perturb overall bone marrow cellularity, HSC frequency, or cause anemia in wild-type mice. The only observed hematological toxicity was an isolated, manageable neutropenia, with no biochemical evidence of hepatic or renal damage. This favorable therapeutic window is likely due to the fact that while CD47 blockade removes a negative signal, phagocytosis generally requires the concomitant presence of positive "eat me" signals—such as calreticulin or altered glycosylation—which are often upregulated on stressed or malignant cells but absent on healthy, resting cells. Thus, the preferential phagocytosis of AML LSC by anti-CD47 therapy appears to be driven by an imbalance of these signals unique to the malignant state.

Future investigations should aim to elucidate the precise molecular mechanisms that drive CD47 upregulation in leukemic stem cells. It remains to be determined whether this overexpression is a direct consequence of specific oncogenic drivers, such as FLT3-ITD, or if it represents a generalized cellular stress response co-opted during malignant transformation. Understanding the transcriptional and epigenetic regulation of the CD47 gene in the context of normal hematopoiesis versus leukemogenesis could reveal additional nodes for therapeutic intervention. Furthermore, exploring the synergy between anti-CD47 antibodies and existing standard-of-care chemotherapies or emerging targeted agents is a critical next step. Combining innate immune checkpoint blockade with therapies that induce cellular stress and upregulate pro-phagocytic "eat me" signals could dramatically enhance LSC eradication and provide durable cures for patients suffering from this intractable disease.

In conclusion, our study identifies the CD47-SIRPα axis as a critical immune checkpoint in human AML and establishes CD47 as a powerful, independent prognostic indicator. By demonstrating that blocking anti-CD47 monoclonal antibodies can specifically enable macrophage-mediated phagocytosis and eradication of AML LSC in vivo without severe toxicity, we provide a strong preclinical rationale for the development of CD47-targeted therapies. Given that LSC share many features across different types of leukemia and potentially solid tumors, CD47 upregulation may represent a universal mechanism of immune evasion. If these findings successfully translate to the clinic, anti-CD47 antibodies could emerge as a foundational component of novel, stem cell-directed immunotherapies, offering a radically new approach to curing AML and potentially other devastating malignancies.

paper_title: Caspase-11 Cleaves Gasdermin D for Non-Canonical Inflammasome Signalling - 2015 - Nature
display_title: Caspase-11 Cleaves Gasdermin D for Non-Canonical Inflammasome Signalling
title: Caspase-11 Cleaves Gasdermin D for Non-Canonical Inflammasome Signalling
text: Intracellular lipopolysaccharide from Gram-negative bacteria including Escherichia coli, Salmonella typhimurium, Shigella flexneri, and Burkholderia thailandensis activates mouse caspase-11, causing pyroptotic cell death, interleukin-1β processing, and lethal septic shock. How caspase-11 executes these downstream signalling events is largely unknown. Here we show that gasdermin D is essential for caspase-11-dependent pyroptosis and interleukin-1β maturation. A forward genetic screen with ethyl N-nitrosourea-mutagenized mice links Gsdmd to the intracellular lipopolysaccharide response. Macrophages from Gsdmd-/- mice generated by gene targeting also exhibit defective pyroptosis and interleukin-1β secretion induced by cytoplasmic lipopolysaccharide or Gram-negative bacteria. In addition, Gsdmd-/- mice are protected from a lethal dose of lipopolysaccharide. Mechanistically, caspase-11 cleaves gasdermin D, and the resulting amino-terminal fragment promotes both pyroptosis and NLRP3-dependent activation of caspase-1 in a cell-intrinsic manner. Our data identify gasdermin D as a critical target of caspase-11 and a key mediator of the host response against Gram-negative bacteria.
historical_context: In 2015, Jianbin Shi, Feng Shao, and colleagues published this groundbreaking study identifying Gasdermin D (GSDMD) as the executioner of pyroptosis, a highly inflammatory form of programmed cell death. They determined that intracellular lipopolysaccharide (LPS) activates caspase-11, which then directly cleaves GSDMD. The cleaved N-terminal fragment of GSDMD oligomerizes to punch pores in the cell membrane, driving cell lysis and the massive release of pro-inflammatory cytokines like IL-1β. This paper permanently altered immunology by solving the enduring mystery of how inflammasomes actually execute cell death. Establishing the Gasdermin protein family as the central pore-forming agents of pyroptosis opened entirely novel therapeutic avenues for targeting sepsis, autoimmune disorders, and severe inflammatory conditions.

paper_title: Caspase-11 Cleaves Gasdermin D for Non-Canonical Inflammasome Signalling - 2015 - Nature
display_title: Caspase-11 Cleaves Gasdermin D for Non-Canonical Inflammasome Signalling
title: Caspase-11 Cleaves Gasdermin D for Non-Canonical Inflammasome Signalling
text: Cytoplasmic lipopolysaccharide (LPS) activates caspase-11 to induce pyroptotic cell death and NLRP3-dependent caspase-1 activation, but the substrate that executes these responses has remained unknown. Here we identify gasdermin D (Gsdmd) as an essential mediator of non-canonical inflammasome signalling. An ENU mutagenesis screen uncovered a damaging Gsdmd mutation that selectively abolished LPS-induced interleukin-1beta secretion and pyroptosis while leaving canonical inflammasome responses intact. Gene-targeted Gsdmd-deficient macrophages phenocopied Casp11 deficiency after cytoplasmic LPS delivery or Gram-negative bacterial infection, and human GSDMD was similarly required for caspase-4-dependent LPS cytotoxicity. Caspase-11 directly cleaved Gsdmd at Asp276 to generate an amino-terminal 30-kDa fragment whose expression was sufficient to kill cells. Mutation of this cleavage site prevented LPS-induced pyroptosis, caspase-1 processing and interleukin-1beta release. Gsdmd-deficient mice were also resistant to LPS-induced lethal septic shock. Thus gasdermin D is a conserved, critical caspase substrate that couples cytoplasmic LPS sensing to inflammatory cell death and sepsis.

paper_title: Caspase-11 Cleaves Gasdermin D for Non-Canonical Inflammasome Signalling - 2015 - Nature
display_title: Caspase-11 Cleaves Gasdermin D for Non-Canonical Inflammasome Signalling
title: Caspase-11 Cleaves Gasdermin D for Non-Canonical Inflammasome Signalling
text: Inflammatory caspases (caspase-1, caspase-4, caspase-5 and caspase-11) are essential effectors of antimicrobial host defence and lethal endotoxemia, where they drive pyroptosis and the maturation of interleukin (IL)-1 family cytokines. Cytoplasmic lipopolysaccharide (LPS) directly engages caspase-11 (or its human orthologues caspase-4 and caspase-5) to trigger pyroptosis and non-canonical NLRP3-dependent activation of caspase-1, yet how these caspases execute cell death has remained unknown. Here we use an unbiased ethyl N-nitrosourea mutagenesis screen and gene-targeted mice to identify gasdermin D (Gsdmd) as an indispensable mediator of non-canonical inflammasome signalling. title: Caspase-11 Cleaves Gasdermin D for Non-Canonical Inflammasome Signalling
text:
Pyroptosis plays an important role in anti-bacterial innate immune defence and lethal endotoxemia, but how inflammatory caspases 1, 4, 5 and 11 cause pyroptosis has remained unknown. Our data reveal that proteolytic cleavage of Gsdmd by mouse caspase-11 or human caspase-4 is essential for pyroptosis of innate immune cells and endothelial cells harbouring LPS-tainted cytoplasm. Cleaved Gsdmd also triggers NLRP3-dependent activation of caspase-1 through a cell-intrinsic pathway, although the exact mechanism responsible for NLRP3 activation remains unclear. NLRP3 senses diverse stimuli that perturb intracellular homeostasis, so its activation may be an indirect consequence of the effects of the Gsdmd p30 fragment. Future studies will need to address exactly what the p30 fragment does in cells. The redefined non-canonical inflammasome signalling pathway refutes the previous hypothesis that a caspase-1/11 complex is responsible for caspase-1 activation.

The partial contribution of Gsdmd to caspase-1-dependent pyroptosis suggests the existence of other pro-pyroptotic caspase-1 substrates. Caspase-1 can trigger pyroptosis in the absence of Gsdmd, which is not unexpected because non-mammalian vertebrates lack Gsdmd yet still exhibit caspase-1-dependent pyroptosis. The co-emergence of caspase-4/5/11 and Gsdmd genes in the early stages of mammalian evolution might indicate co-evolution, perhaps in response to the need for additional anti-pathogen responses against Gram-negative bacteria.

Like other caspases, caspase-11 cleaves multiple substrates. However, our in vitro and in vivo data show that caspase-11 relies exclusively on Gsdmd to promote pyroptosis, caspase-1 activation and LPS-induced lethal sepsis. Thus Gsdmd is revealed as an unexpected, but critical facet of the anti-bacterial response.

Establishing the Gasdermin protein family as the central pore-forming agents of pyroptosis opened entirely novel therapeutic avenues for targeting sepsis, autoimmune disorders, and severe inflammatory conditions.

paper_title: Caspase-11 Cleaves Gasdermin D for Non-Canonical Inflammasome Signalling - 2015 - Nature
display_title: Caspase-11 Cleaves Gasdermin D for Non-Canonical Inflammasome Signalling
title: Caspase-11 Cleaves Gasdermin D for Non-Canonical Inflammasome Signalling
text:
Our genetic and biochemical data identify gasdermin D as the critical effector through which caspase-11 executes non-canonical inflammasome signalling. A recessive ENU mutation in Gsdmd, as well as targeted deletion of Gsdmd, abolished pyroptosis, caspase-1 processing and IL-1β secretion in response to cytoplasmic LPS and Gram-negative bacteria, while leaving TLR priming and canonical inflammasome responses largely intact. Thus Gsdmd functions downstream of LPS sensing by caspase-11 and upstream of both membrane rupture and NLRP3-dependent cytokine maturation.

Mechanistically, caspase-11 directly cleaves Gsdmd at a conserved aspartate residue to generate an amino-terminal fragment that is sufficient to induce cell death. The inability of the D276A cleavage mutant to restore LPS responsiveness demonstrates that proteolytic maturation of Gsdmd is not merely correlative, but is required for non-canonical inflammasome signalling. The I105N mutation further indicates that the amino-terminal region contains essential determinants of Gsdmd activity. These findings support a model in which full-length Gsdmd is an inactive precursor whose carboxy-terminal region restrains a latent pro-pyroptotic amino-terminal domain until caspase-mediated cleavage releases this activity.

Gsdmd-dependent activation of NLRP3 appears to be cell intrinsic rather than a consequence of paracrine stimulation by pyroptotic cell products. How Gsdmd p30 engages the NLRP3 pathway remains to be determined, but membrane damage or ion flux induced by the amino-terminal fragment may provide the relevant trigger. The conservation of GSDMD cleavage by human caspase-4 and caspase-5 further suggests that this mechanism is a central mammalian host-defence pathway against cytosolic LPS. Finally, resistance of Gsdmd-/- mice to lethal endotoxemia establishes Gsdmd as a key in vivo mediator of caspase-11-driven sepsis.

title: Caspase-11 Cleaves Gasdermin D for Non-Canonical Inflammasome Signalling
text:
Our study fundamentally redefines the mechanism of non-canonical inflammasome signaling by identifying gasdermin D (Gsdmd) as the critical executor of caspase-11-dependent pyroptosis. Using an unbiased forward genetic screen and CRISPR/Cas9-mediated gene targeting, we established that Gsdmd is absolutely required for both pyroptotic cell death and interleukin-1β secretion in response to cytoplasmic lipopolysaccharide (LPS) and Gram-negative bacterial infections. We demonstrated that upon activation by intracellular LPS, caspase-11 directly cleaves pro-Gsdmd to release an active, 30-kDa N-terminal fragment. This single proteolytic event unleashes the pro-pyroptotic potential of Gsdmd, completely independent of the classical NLRP3-caspase-1 signaling axis.

Strikingly, our data reveal that Gsdmd cleavage serves as the vital molecular bridge linking the non-canonical caspase-11 pathway to the canonical NLRP3 inflammasome. The generation of the active Gsdmd N-terminal fragment triggers a cell-intrinsic signal that concurrently executes pyroptosis and activates NLRP3, leading to caspase-1-dependent processing of pro-IL-1β. Furthermore, we showed that human caspase-4 and caspase-5 share this conserved mechanism, highlighting the evolutionary importance of the gasdermin family in mammalian innate immunity. The profound protection against LPS-induced lethal septic shock observed in Gsdmd-deficient mice underscores the physiological relevance of this pathway in vivo. By identifying Gsdmd as the primary physiological substrate of caspase-11, our findings solve a major outstanding puzzle in innate immunity. Future investigations into the exact biochemical mechanism by which the Gsdmd N-terminal domain induces membrane pore formation and cytolysis will be crucial for developing novel therapeutic interventions against severe endotoxemia and autoinflammatory diseases.

paper_title: Cerebral Organoids Model Human Brain Development and Microcephaly - 2013 - Nature
display_title: Cerebral Organoids Model Human Brain Development and Microcephaly
title: Cerebral Organoids Model Human Brain Development and Microcephaly
text: The complexity of the human brain has made it difficult to study many brain disorders in model organisms, and highlights the need for an in vitro model of human brain development. We have developed a human pluripotent stem cell-derived 3D organoid culture system, termed cerebral organoid, which develops various discrete though interdependent brain regions. These include cerebral cortex containing progenitor populations that organize and produce mature cortical neuron subtypes. Furthermore, cerebral organoids recapitulate features of human cortical development, namely characteristic progenitor zone organization with abundant outer radial glial stem cells. Finally, we use RNAi and patient-specific iPS cells to model microcephaly, a disorder that has been difficult to recapitulate in mice. We demonstrate premature neuronal differentiation in patient organoids, a defect that could explain the disease phenotype. Our data demonstrate that 3D organoids can recapitulate development and disease of even this most complex human tissue.
historical_context: In 2013, Madeline Lancaster, Jürgen Knoblich, and colleagues pioneered a revolutionary method for culturing three-dimensional, self-organizing tissue models of the developing human brain, aptly termed "cerebral organoids." Grown from human pluripotent stem cells, these neural structures recapitulated core features of human cortical development, including distinct progenitor zones and organized neurogenesis. Leveraging patient-derived stem cells, the authors effectively modeled microcephaly, exposing premature neuronal differentiation as a key pathological mechanism. This work was a massive leap forward for neuroscience and developmental biology. It provided, for the first time, an accessible, physiologically relevant 3D model to scrutinize the unique complexities of human brain evolution, intricate neurodevelopmental disorders, and viral infections, effectively circumventing the severe limitations of standard 2D cultures and non-primate animal models.

title: Cerebral Organoids Model Human Brain Development and Microcephaly
text: Human brain development displays features not readily accessible in animal models, including expanded progenitor zones and outer radial glia. Here, we describe a three-dimensional culture method that allows human pluripotent stem cells to self-organize into cerebral organoids containing discrete, interdependent brain regions. These tissues form neuroepithelial ventricular zones, dorsal cortical domains, choroid plexus, retina, and ventral forebrain identities, and cortical regions display radial glia, intermediate progenitors, rudimentary cortical plate organization, and spontaneously active neurons. Importantly, human organoids recapitulate aspects of species-specific cortical architecture, including an outer subventricular zone-like region populated by outer radial glia, which is not evident in comparable mouse organoids. We further use patient-derived induced pluripotent stem cells carrying loss-of-function CDK5RAP2 mutations, together with rescue and knockdown experiments, to model primary microcephaly. Mutant organoids exhibit reduced neuroepithelial growth, altered spindle orientation, and premature neuronal differentiation at the expense of progenitors, suggesting that failed expansion of radial glia contributes to human brain hypoplasia.

paper_title: Cerebral Organoids Model Human Brain Development and Microcephaly - 2013 - Nature
display_title: Cerebral Organoids Model Human Brain Development and Microcephaly
title: Cerebral Organoids Model Human Brain Development and Microcephaly
text:
Mammalian brain development begins with expansion of the neuroepithelium to generate radial glia (RG). These RGs divide at the apical surface within the ventricular zone (VZ) to generate neurons and intermediate progenitors (IPs). IPs populate the adjacent subventricular zone (SVZ) while neurons migrate through the intermediate zone (IZ) to populate specific layers within the cortical plate (CP). In humans, the organization of progenitor zones is markedly more elaborate; the SVZ is split by an inner fiber layer (IFL) into inner (ISVZ) and outer SVZ (OSVZ). The OSVZ represents an entirely separate progenitor layer populated by IPs and a unique stem cell termed outer radial glia (oRG), which are only present to a limited degree in rodents. Both the IFL and OSVZ are completely absent in mice. These key differences allow for the striking expansion in neuronal output and brain size seen in humans.

Primary microcephaly (MCPH) is a neurodevelopmental disorder in which brain size is markedly reduced. Autosomal recessive mutations have been identified in several genes, all of which encode proteins localizing to the mitotic spindle apparatus. Heretofore, MCPH pathogenesis has primarily been examined in mouse models. However, mouse mutants for several of the known genes have failed to recapitulate the severely reduced brain size seen in human patients.

Given the dramatic differences between mice and humans, methods that recapitulate paradigms of human brain development in vitro have enormous potential. While significant progress has been made for in vitro models of whole organ development for other systems, such as intestine, pituitary, and retina, a 3D culture model of the developing brain as a whole has not been established. Previous studies have modeled certain isolated neural tissues in vitro, including dorsal cerebral cortical tissue, which could recapitulate many aspects of early development. However, later events such as the formation of discrete cortical layers with stereotypic inside-out organization as well as human characteristics, such as the presence of oRG cells and the unique organization of progenitor zones, were not described.

Here, we describe a 3D culture system for deriving brain tissue in vitro. These so-called cerebral organoids develop a variety of regional identities organized as discrete domains capable of influencing one another. Furthermore, cerebral cortical regions display an organization similar to the developing human brain at early stages, as well as the presence of a considerable oRG population. Moreover, cerebral cortical neurons mature to form various pyramidal identities with modest spatial separation. Finally, we utilize patient-derived iPSCs and shRNA in these organoids to model CDK5RAP2 dependent pathogenesis of microcephaly, a disorder that has been difficult to model in mice.
historical_context: In 2013, Madeline Lancaster, Jürgen Knoblich, and colleagues pioneered a revolutionary method for culturing three-dimensional, self-organizing tissue models of the developing human brain, aptly termed "cerebral organoids." Grown from human pluripotent stem cells, these neural structures recapitulated core features of human cortical development, including distinct progenitor zones and organized neurogenesis. Leveraging patient-derived stem cells, the authors effectively modeled microcephaly, exposing premature neuronal differentiation as a key pathological mechanism. This work was a massive leap forward for neuroscience and developmental biology. It provided, for the first time, an accessible, physiologically relevant 3D model to scrutinize the unique complexities of human brain evolution, intricate neurodevelopmental disorders, and viral infections, effectively circumventing the severe limitations of standard 2D cultures and non-primate animal models.

paper_title: Cerebral Organoids Model Human Brain Development and Microcephaly - 2013 - Nature
display_title: Cerebral Organoids Model Human Brain Development and Microcephaly
title: Cerebral Organoids Model Human Brain Development and Microcephaly
text:
The human brain displays a degree of anatomical complexity and cellular diversity that has been difficult to model experimentally. Although animal models have provided essential insight into conserved mechanisms of neural induction, patterning and neurogenesis, many features of human cortical development are either absent or greatly reduced in commonly used model organisms. These include the pronounced expansion of neural progenitor populations, the organization of germinal zones, and the abundance of outer radial glia, all of which are thought to contribute to the evolutionary enlargement of the human cerebral cortex. Consequently, disorders that affect human brain growth can be difficult to reproduce in animals, limiting the ability to study their developmental origins.

Human pluripotent stem cells provide a complementary system for examining human development in vitro. Directed differentiation protocols have made it possible to generate many neural cell types, including cortical neurons, from embryonic stem cells and induced pluripotent stem cells. However, most approaches rely on exogenous patterning signals and produce relatively homogeneous populations or simple neural rosettes. Such systems have been valuable for defining cell-intrinsic differentiation programs, but they do not fully capture the three-dimensional architecture, regional heterogeneity, or long-range tissue interactions that characterize early brain development. Recent work in other organ systems has shown that pluripotent stem cells can self-organize into complex structures when provided with appropriate culture conditions, suggesting that intrinsic developmental programs may be sufficient to generate higher-order tissue organization.

Here we describe a three-dimensional culture system that allows human pluripotent stem cells to generate complex neural tissues, which we term cerebral organoids. Rather than imposing specific regional identities with patterning factors, this method promotes neuroectoderm formation, embeds developing tissues in an extracellular matrix scaffold, and maintains them in spinning culture to improve nutrient exchange. Under these conditions, organoids develop multiple discrete but interdependent brain regions, including dorsal cortical tissues with organized progenitor zones and neurons. These tissues display features of human cortical development, including outer radial glia and an organization resembling the outer subventricular zone.

We further apply this system to model primary microcephaly, a disorder of reduced brain size whose severity has been difficult to recapitulate in mice. Using patient-derived induced pluripotent stem cells carrying loss-of-function mutations in CDK5RAP2, together with RNA interference and rescue experiments, we find that mutant tissues undergo premature neuronal differentiation at the expense of progenitor expansion. These findings indicate that cerebral organoids can recapitulate aspects of human brain development and provide a tractable system for investigating human-specific neurodevelopmental disease mechanisms.

Leveraging patient-derived stem cells, the authors effectively modeled microcephaly, exposing premature neuronal differentiation as a key pathological mechanism. paper_title: Cerebral Organoids Model Human Brain Development and Microcephaly - 2013 - Nature
display_title: Cerebral Organoids Model Human Brain Development and Microcephaly
title: Cerebral Organoids Model Human Brain Development and Microcephaly
text:
The human brain is arguably the most complex and highly organized biological structure in existence. Its remarkable cognitive abilities arise from a dramatic evolutionary expansion in size, accompanied by the intricate regionalization and lamination of the cerebral cortex. Understanding the precise molecular and cellular mechanisms that govern human brain development is not only a fundamental goal of developmental biology but is also essential for uncovering the underlying causes of a wide spectrum of devastating neurodevelopmental, psychiatric, and neurodegenerative disorders.

Historically, our knowledge of brain development has relied heavily on studies in model organisms, predominantly the mouse. While these animal models have provided invaluable insights into the basic principles of neurogenesis, neuronal migration, and circuit assembly, they possess significant limitations when attempting to model the unique features of the human brain. The human cerebral cortex, for example, is highly folded (gyrencephalic) and contains a massively expanded outer subventricular zone (OSVZ) populated by specialized outer radial glia (oRG) cells, features that are largely absent in the smooth (lissencephalic) rodent brain. Consequently, many human neurodevelopmental disorders, such as primary microcephaly, exhibit severe phenotypic discrepancies when modeled in mice, often failing to recapitulate the profound brain size reduction seen in patients. This underscores the critical need for an in vitro model system that can faithfully recapitulate the unique complexities of early human brain development.

Recent advancements in stem cell biology have demonstrated the remarkable capacity of mammalian pluripotent stem cells to self-organize into complex, three-dimensional (3D) tissue structures in vitro. By carefully modulating culture conditions to mimic the embryonic environment, researchers have successfully generated functional 3D organoids resembling optic cup, pituitary gland, and intestinal epithelia. Building upon these conceptual breakthroughs, we sought to develop a robust and reproducible 3D culture system capable of generating highly organized, human brain-like tissues from human embryonic stem cells (hESCs) and induced pluripotent stem cells (iPSCs).

In this study, we describe the establishment of a novel 3D organoid culture system that leverages the intrinsic self-organizing properties of human pluripotent stem cells to generate cerebral organoids. We show that these organoids can grow to remarkable sizes and develop discrete, interdependent brain regions, including structures resembling the dorsal cortex, ventral forebrain, choroid plexus, and retina. Crucially, the cortical regions within these organoids recapitulate key organizational features of early human cortical development, including a distinct progenitor zone architecture and the presence of abundant outer radial glia. Finally, we demonstrate the utility of this powerful new system for disease modeling by generating cerebral organoids from patient-specific iPSCs to model primary microcephaly. By investigating these patient-derived organoids, we uncover a disease-specific defect in neural progenitor proliferation and premature neuronal differentiation, providing novel mechanistic insights into the pathogenesis of this severe neurodevelopmental disorder.

Grown from human pluripotent stem cells, these neural structures recapitulated core features of human cortical development, including distinct progenitor zones and organized neurogenesis. Leveraging patient-derived stem cells, the authors effectively modeled microcephaly, exposing premature neuronal differentiation as a key pathological mechanism. This work was a massive leap forward for neuroscience and developmental biology. It provided, for the first time, an accessible, physiologically relevant 3D model to scrutinize the unique complexities of human brain evolution, intricate neurodevelopmental disorders, and viral infections, effectively circumventing the severe limitations of standard 2D cultures and non-primate animal models.

paper_title: Cerebral Organoids Model Human Brain Development and Microcephaly - 2013 - Nature
display_title: Cerebral Organoids Model Human Brain Development and Microcephaly
title: Cerebral Organoids Model Human Brain Development and Microcephaly
text:
We have established a novel approach to studying human neurodevelopmental processes through in vitro culture of cerebral organoids from human pluripotent stem cells. This method recapitulates not only fundamental mechanisms of mammalian neurodevelopment, but also displays characteristics of human brain development. We are hopeful that this method will allow the study of a variety of neurodevelopmental processes specific to human brain development.

Furthermore, a primary goal in neuroscience is to understand the roots of human neurological disease. We have modeled at least some aspects of microcephaly in these cerebral organoids. The finding that progenitor zones in patient-derived tissues display premature neural differentiation at the expense of early progenitors supports a model in which the founder population of RG progenitors fails to properly expand in patient tissues, thereby leading to an overall smaller brain. This may also explain why mouse models have been unable to recapitulate the severity of the disorder in humans. It is hypothesized that the mouse founder population of neural progenitors do not undergo expansion to the same extent as in human before the onset of neurogenesis. Thus, a disruption of the founder population in mice would not lead to as severe an effect as that seen in humans. Overall, our findings suggest we can utilize this in vitro culture system to model aspects of human neurodevelopment and neurological disease and hopefully provide novel insight into pathogenesis of these disorders.

text:
Here we describe a method for generating complex 3D brain tissue from human pluripotent stem cells. These cerebral organoids develop large neuroepithelial regions and form discrete brain identities, including dorsal and ventral forebrain, choroid plexus, retina, and meninges-like tissues. Importantly, these regions are not randomly interspersed, but rather appear as organized domains that can influence one another, as illustrated by interneuron migration from ventral to dorsal forebrain regions. Thus, cerebral organoids provide a system in which early regional specification and local tissue interactions can occur in vitro.

The cerebral cortical regions of organoids recapitulate several fundamental features of mammalian corticogenesis, including radial glial organization, interkinetic nuclear migration, apical mitosis, generation of intermediate progenitors, and the production of neurons with rudimentary layer identity. Furthermore, organoids display features that are especially characteristic of the developing human cortex, namely an OSVZ-like region containing abundant oRG cells separated from the ventricular zone by a fiber layer. These findings suggest that intrinsic human developmental programs are sufficient to generate aspects of cortical organization that are poorly represented in rodent models.

Finally, we demonstrate the utility of cerebral organoids for modeling neurodevelopmental disease. Patient-derived CDK5RAP2 mutant organoids showed reduced neuroepithelial expansion, altered spindle orientation, and premature neuronal differentiation. Rescue and RNAi experiments support a specific role for CDK5RAP2 in maintaining early neural progenitor expansion. Although organoids lack vasculature and do not fully reproduce mature cortical architecture, this system offers a tractable human model for investigating brain development and disorders of brain size.

Our development of the cerebral organoid culture system marks a significant technological leap in our ability to model human brain development and disease in vitro. By optimizing 3D growth conditions without the restrictive use of exogenous patterning factors, we have empowered human pluripotent stem cells to rely on intrinsic self-organizing cues, resulting in the generation of complex, heterogeneous, and highly interdependent brain regions. Crucially, these organoids successfully recapitulate the intricate progenitor zone organization that is uniquely characteristic of the human developing cerebral cortex, notably the abundant presence of outer radial glia (oRG) and an inner fiber layer (IFL)—features critically absent in standard rodent models.

Mechanistically, we discovered that this reduced tissue size is driven by premature neuronal differentiation at the expense of progenitor cell maintenance, likely due to defective horizontal spindle orientation during critical early proliferative divisions. This provides a compelling cellular mechanism for the pathogenesis of microcephaly. Ultimately, human cerebral organoids offer an unprecedented, physiologically relevant platform to investigate uniquely human aspects of neurodevelopment and to dissect the complex etiology of diverse neurological and psychiatric disorders.

paper_title: Checkpoint Blockade Cancer Immunotherapy Targets Tumour-Specific Mutant Antigens - 2014 - Nature
display_title: Checkpoint Blockade Cancer Immunotherapy Targets Tumour-Specific Mutant Antigens
title: Checkpoint Blockade Cancer Immunotherapy Targets Tumour-Specific Mutant Antigens
text: The immune system influences the fate of developing cancers by not only functioning as a tumour promoter that facilitates cellular transformation, promotes tumour growth and sculpts tumour cell immunogenicity, but also as an extrinsic tumour suppressor that either destroys developing tumours or restrains their expansion. Yet, clinically apparent cancers still arise in immunocompetent individuals in part as a consequence of cancer-induced immunosuppression. In many individuals, immunosuppression is mediated by cytotoxic T-lymphocyte associated antigen-4 (CTLA-4) and programmed death-1 (PD-1), two immunomodulatory receptors expressed on T cells. Monoclonal-antibody-based therapies targeting CTLA-4 and/or PD-1 (checkpoint blockade) have yielded significant clinical benefits—including durable responses—to patients with different malignancies. However, little is known about the identity of the tumour antigens that function as the targets of T cells activated by checkpoint blockade immunotherapy and whether these antigens can be used to generate vaccines that are highly tumour-specific. Here we use genomics and bioinformatics approaches to identify tumour-specific mutant proteins as a major class of T-cell rejection antigens following anti-PD-1 and/or anti-CTLA-4 therapy of mice bearing progressively growing sarcomas, and we show that therapeutic synthetic long-peptide vaccines incorporating these mutant epitopes induce tumour rejection comparably to checkpoint blockade immunotherapy. Although mutant tumour-antigen-specific T cells are present in progressively growing tumours, they are reactivated following treatment with anti-PD-1 and/or anti-CTLA-4 and display some overlapping but mostly treatment-specific transcriptional profiles, rendering them capable of mediating tumour rejection. These results reveal that tumour-specific mutant antigens are not only important targets of checkpoint blockade therapy, but they can also be used to develop personalized cancer-specific vaccines and to probe the mechanisms of cancer immunotherapy.
historical_context: Published in 2014 by Matthew Gubin, Robert Schreiber, and colleagues—bolstered by significant advances in neoantigen discovery—this critical paper forged the definitive mechanistic link between immune checkpoint blockade (such as anti-PD-1/CTLA-4 therapies) and tumor-specific mutant antigens (neoantigens). Utilizing advanced genomics and mass spectrometry, the researchers proved that the immune system, once "unleashed" by checkpoint inhibitors, selectively identifies and obliterates cancer cells based on unique, mutation-derived neoantigens. This specific study was a milestone because it definitively explained *why* checkpoint blockade succeeds and why tumors bearing high mutational burdens are far more responsive. It securely laid the foundation for the rapidly expanding field of personalized cancer vaccines and neoantigen-targeted immunotherapies.

display_title: Checkpoint Blockade Cancer Immunotherapy Targets Tumour-Specific Mutant Antigens
title: Checkpoint Blockade Cancer Immunotherapy Targets Tumour-Specific Mutant Antigens
text: Checkpoint blockade with antibodies to CTLA-4 and PD-1 can induce durable tumour regression, yet the tumour antigens targeted by the responding T cells remain poorly defined. Here we show that therapeutic blockade of PD-1 and/or CTLA-4 rejects two independent methylcholanthrene-induced sarcomas through CD4+ and CD8+ T cells, interferon-γ and Batf3-dependent antigen cross-presentation. Using complementary DNA capture sequencing, MHC class I binding prediction and epitope filtering, we analysed 2,796 expressed non-synonymous mutations in the d42m1-T3 sarcoma and identified two dominant H-2Kb-restricted tumour-specific mutant antigens, mLama4 and mAlg8. Both epitopes were recognized by freshly isolated CD8+ tumour-infiltrating lymphocytes from anti-PD-1-treated mice, stimulated tumour-specific cytotoxic T-cell lines, stabilized H-2Kb on RMA-S cells and were detected directly in H-2Kb peptide eluates from d42m1-T3 but not unrelated F244 tumours. T cells specific for mLama4 and mAlg8 accumulated in tumours before rejection and were present before therapy, but checkpoint blockade altered their number, phenotype and transcriptional state. Anti-PD-1 induced metabolic and type I interferon-associated programmes, anti-CTLA-4 enhanced cell-cycle, NFAT and JAK-STAT pathways, and combined treatment produced the strongest effector profile, including increased IFNγ, Granzyme A, Granzyme B and Fas ligand. Therapeutic vaccination with synthetic long peptides containing mLama4 and mAlg8 plus poly(I:C) rejected established d42m1-T3 tumours as effectively as anti-PD-1, while remaining tumour specific. These data demonstrate that tumour-specific mutant antigens are principal targets of checkpoint blockade and provide a rational basis for individualized cancer vaccines and immune monitoring.

title: Checkpoint Blockade Cancer Immunotherapy Targets Tumour-Specific Mutant Antigens
text: The therapeutic efficacy of checkpoint blockade immunotherapy with monoclonal antibodies (mAbs) against programmed cell death 1 (PD-1) and cytotoxic T-lymphocyte-associated antigen 4 (CTLA-4) has been firmly established for the treatment of several human cancers, yet the antigenic targets responsible for tumour rejection have remained poorly defined. Here we use genomics, bioinformatics and mass spectrometry to identify the tumour-specific mutant antigens (TSMA) that drive CD8+ T-cell responses underlying checkpoint blockade-mediated rejection of two distinct progressor 3-methylcholanthrene-induced (MCA) sarcomas in syngeneic mice. By integrating cDNA capture sequencing with three major histocompatibility complex (MHC) class I epitope-binding algorithms and applying filters based on predicted proteasomal processing and differential binding to wild-type sequences, we identify two dominant H-2Kb-restricted epitopes encoded by mutations in Lama4 and Alg8 as the principal targets of tumour-infiltrating CD8+ T cells in anti-PD-1-treated mice bearing d42m1-T3 tumours. The mutant Lama4 and Alg8 peptides are presented by H-2Kb in vivo as confirmed by tetramer staining and direct mass spectrometric detection in immunoaffinity-purified MHC class I eluates, and therapeutic vaccination with synthetic long peptides encoding these epitopes plus poly(I:C) induces tumour rejection with efficacy comparable to that of anti-PD-1 therapy itself. RNA sequencing of antigen-specific tumour-infiltrating lymphocytes reveals that anti-PD-1 and anti-CTLA-4 each engage distinct, predominantly non-overlapping transcriptional programs converging on enhanced effector function, with dual blockade producing synergistic activation of T-cell-mediated anti-tumour pathways. These findings demonstrate that TSMA are major targets of checkpoint blockade immunotherapy, provide a mechanistic framework for the distinct and complementary actions of anti-PD-1 and anti-CTLA-4, and support the use of TSMA-based vaccines as a clinically tractable strategy for personalized cancer immunotherapy.

text: Immune checkpoint blockade therapies, including anti-PD-1 and anti-CTLA-4, induce durable responses in a subset of patients across diverse cancer types; however, the precise antigenic targets driving these responses and the underlying mechanisms of therapeutic efficacy remain incompletely understood. Using progressor 3-methylcholanthrene-induced (MCA) sarcomas that are sensitive to checkpoint blockade, we demonstrate that therapy-induced tumour rejection is dependent on CD8+ T cells and cross-presenting dendritic cells, culminating in immunologic memory. The identification and therapeutic application of TSMA provide a compelling rationale for the development of personalized cancer vaccines to complement or enhance checkpoint blockade therapies in the clinic.
