text:
In this study, we used two distinct progressor 3-methylcholanthrene-induced (MCA) sarcoma cell lines (d42m1-T3 and F244) and asked whether they expressed sufficient immunogenicity to be controlled by checkpoint blockade immunotherapy. Both sarcoma lines were rejected in wild-type mice treated therapeutically with anti-PD-1 and/or anti-CTLA-4. Rejection was immunologic because (1) it was ablated by administration of monoclonal antibodies (mAbs) that either deplete CD4+ or CD8+ cells or neutralize interferon-γ (IFNγ); (2) it did not occur in Rag2-/- mice lacking T, B and natural killer T (NKT) cells or Batf3-/- mice lacking CD8α+ CD103+ dendritic cells required for tumour antigen cross-presentation to CD8+ T cells; and (3) it induced a memory response that protected mice against rechallenge with the same tumour cells that had been injected into naive mice.

On the basis of our previous success using genomics approaches to identify tumour-specific mutant antigens (TSMA) responsible for the spontaneous rejection of highly immunogenic, unedited MCA sarcomas, we asked whether a similar approach could identify antigens responsible for anti-PD-1-mediated rejection of d42m1-T3 progressor tumours. To increase the robustness and accuracy of our epitope predictions, we modified our method as follows: (1) mutation calls from complementary DNA capture sequencing were translated to corresponding protein sequences, pipelined through three major histocompatibility complex (MHC) class I epitope-binding algorithms and a median binding affinity was calculated for each predicted epitope; (2) epitopes were prioritized on the basis of predicted median binding affinities; and (3) filters were applied to the prioritized epitope list to eliminate those predicted to be poorly processed by the immunoproteasome and to deprioritize those from hypothetical proteins or those that displayed lower binding affinity to class I than their corresponding wild-type sequences.

title: Checkpoint Blockade Cancer Immunotherapy Targets Tumour-Specific Mutant Antigens
text:
Cancer immunoediting describes the process by which the immune system can protect the host against tumour development while also shaping the immunogenicity of cancers that ultimately arise. In experimental models, tumour rejection antigens have long been inferred from transplantation, vaccination and T-cell transfer studies, yet the molecular identity of the antigens recognized during effective tumour immunity has often remained uncertain. This question has become more urgent with the clinical development of antibodies that block inhibitory receptors on T cells. Monoclonal antibodies targeting cytotoxic T-lymphocyte associated antigen-4 (CTLA-4) or programmed death-1 (PD-1) can induce durable tumour regressions in patients with cancer, but the antigens targeted by the T cells unleashed by these therapies are incompletely defined.

One attractive class of antigens is generated by tumour-specific somatic mutations. Such mutant antigens are not expressed by normal tissues and therefore should escape central tolerance while conferring a high degree of tumour specificity. Recent advances in massively parallel sequencing and computational prediction of peptide binding to major histocompatibility complex (MHC) molecules now make it possible to survey the mutational landscape of an individual tumour and nominate candidate mutant epitopes. However, whether tumour-specific mutant antigens represent the dominant targets of checkpoint blockade therapy, whether mutant-antigen-specific T cells pre-exist in progressively growing tumours, and whether these antigens can be exploited therapeutically have not been established.

Here we combine exome sequencing, RNA sequencing, epitope prediction, peptide-MHC tetramer analysis and functional T-cell assays to identify mutant antigens targeted during checkpoint blockade-induced rejection of a methylcholanthrene-induced mouse sarcoma. We further test whether synthetic long-peptide vaccines incorporating these epitopes can reproduce the therapeutic effects of checkpoint blockade and use antigen-specific T cells to define the molecular programs induced by anti-PD-1 and/or anti-CTLA-4 therapy.

text:
The immune system plays a dual and seemingly paradoxical role in cancer development, acting both to suppress tumor outgrowth and to sculpt tumor immunogenicity. While the immune system can successfully eliminate many nascent neoplastic cells, clinically apparent cancers ultimately emerge in immunocompetent individuals. This escape is largely facilitated by the ability of developing tumors to subvert normal immune regulation, creating a profoundly immunosuppressive microenvironment. A central mechanism of this tumor-induced immunosuppression involves the co-option of immune checkpoint pathways, which are critical for maintaining self-tolerance and preventing autoimmunity under physiological conditions.

Among the best-characterized immune checkpoints are cytotoxic T-lymphocyte associated antigen-4 (CTLA-4) and programmed death-1 (PD-1), both of which act as key inhibitory receptors on T cells. Monoclonal antibodies designed to block these inhibitory receptors—a therapeutic approach known as checkpoint blockade immunotherapy—have revolutionized clinical oncology. By releasing the brakes on the immune system, antibodies targeting CTLA-4 and/or PD-1 have generated durable, life-prolonging clinical responses in patients across a diverse array of advanced malignancies.

However, despite these remarkable clinical successes, a fundamental question remains largely unanswered: what are the specific targets of the T cells that mediate tumor rejection following checkpoint blockade? While some studies have implicated shared, overexpressed tumor-associated antigens (TAAs), it has long been hypothesized that tumor-specific mutant antigens (neoantigens), generated by somatic mutations during tumorigenesis, might serve as the primary targets of the reactivated immune response. In this study, we utilize advanced genomics, bioinformatics, and mass spectrometry to systematically identify the exact mutated antigens targeted by T cells following checkpoint blockade in a murine sarcoma model. We demonstrate that tumor-specific mutant antigens are indeed major targets of both anti-PD-1 and anti-CTLA-4 therapies and show that these antigens can be successfully formulated into highly effective, personalized therapeutic vaccines.

This specific study was a milestone because it definitively explained *why* checkpoint blockade succeeds and why tumors bearing high mutational burdens are far more responsive. It securely laid the foundation for the rapidly expanding field of personalized cancer vaccines and neoantigen-targeted immunotherapies.

title: Checkpoint Blockade Cancer Immunotherapy Targets Tumour-Specific Mutant Antigens
text:
This report documents that TSMA are targets of checkpoint blockade immunotherapy and can be used in vaccines that therapeutically induce tumour rejection as effectively as checkpoint blockade therapy. The ability to rapidly and accurately identify TSMA using genomics and bioinformatics approaches and use them to generate MHC tetramers to identify tumour-specific T cells provides a significant advantage to the fields of tumour immunology and cancer immunotherapy. This approach has not only facilitated the identification of the antigenic targets of T cells affected by checkpoint blockade therapy but also has provided insights into the molecular changes that occur within the tumour-antigen-specific T-cell population that give rise to the anti-tumour effects of anti-PD-1 and/or anti-CTLA-4. Our findings provide some of the first experimental support for the clinical observations that (1) checkpoint blockade therapy amplifies, in some cases, pre-existing anti-tumour T-cell responses; (2) whereas anti-CTLA-4 treatment eliminates regulatory T cells, promotes T-cell priming, and renders the host more susceptible to autoimmunity, anti-PD-1 promotes T-cell activation acting as a rheostat of immune effector function; and (3) dual blockade of CTLA-4 and PD-1 is particularly effective in promoting enhanced anti-tumour effector functions.

The mutational loads of the MCA sarcomas used in this study are high and similar to those of ultraviolet- and carcinogen-induced human cancers. For these types of tumours, it is likely that TSMA vaccines targeting multiple antigens will be possible, thereby providing better coverage of the tumour cell population in part due to dealing more effectively with tumour heterogeneity. Additionally, the combination of a TSMA vaccine and checkpoint blockade may facilitate the immune system's ability to recognize less immunogenic TSMA as well as shared, tumour-associated antigens (TAA) via mechanisms that mimic epitope spreading. However, recent studies have shown that human tumours containing far fewer mutations (for example, 26 mutations) can be sensitive to TSMA-based immunotherapy even for tumour-specific antigens that are targets for class II restricted CD4+ T cells. Our study thus provides a strong argument to actively pursue the use of TSMA as targets for cancer immunotherapy, as a means to identify patients who would best benefit from such therapy, and as components of MHC tetramers that can be used to identify tumour-specific T cells as biomarkers of successful anti-tumour responses.
historical_context: Published in 2014 by Matthew Gubin, Robert Schreiber, and colleagues—bolstered by significant advances in neoantigen discovery—this critical paper forged the definitive mechanistic link between immune checkpoint blockade (such as anti-PD-1/CTLA-4 therapies) and tumor-specific mutant antigens (neoantigens). Utilizing advanced genomics and mass spectrometry, the researchers proved that the immune system, once "unleashed" by checkpoint inhibitors, selectively identifies and obliterates cancer cells based on unique, mutation-derived neoantigens. This specific study was a milestone because it definitively explained *why* checkpoint blockade succeeds and why tumors bearing high mutational burdens are far more responsive. It securely laid the foundation for the rapidly expanding field of personalized cancer vaccines and neoantigen-targeted immunotherapies.

paper_title: Checkpoint Blockade Cancer Immunotherapy Targets Tumour-Specific Mutant Antigens - 2014 - Nature

title: Checkpoint Blockade Cancer Immunotherapy Targets Tumour-Specific Mutant Antigens
text:
Our findings identify tumour-specific mutant antigens as dominant targets of T cells mobilized by checkpoint blockade immunotherapy. By combining exome/cDNA sequencing, epitope prediction, tetramer-based detection, functional T-cell assays and direct mass spectrometric validation of MHC-associated peptides, we found that two H-2Kb-restricted mutant epitopes, derived from Lama4 and Alg8, account for major CD8+ T-cell responses associated with rejection of d42m1-T3 sarcomas. These antigens were not shared by an unrelated sarcoma, were not detected as corresponding wild-type peptides, and stimulated T cells in a mutation-specific manner. Thus, checkpoint blockade can unmask effective immunity directed against private somatic mutations that distinguish the tumour from normal tissues.

The observation that mLama4- and mAlg8-specific T cells were present in progressively growing tumours before therapy indicates that failure of tumour control was not due simply to absence of relevant tumour-reactive T cells. Rather, these cells persisted in a dysfunctional state that could be altered by blockade of PD-1 and/or CTLA-4. Anti-PD-1, anti-CTLA-4 and the combination each produced tumour rejection, but they did so through partially distinct transcriptional and functional programmes in antigen-specific TIL. Anti-PD-1 was associated prominently with metabolic and type I interferon-related changes, whereas anti-CTLA-4 enhanced proliferative, NFAT/JAK-STAT and effector pathways. Combined treatment produced the strongest induction of cytolytic and inflammatory molecules. These results suggest that different checkpoint receptors restrain tumour-specific T cells through non-identical mechanisms, and that therapeutic combinations may act by converging on a shared outcome while engaging distinct cellular pathways.

A further implication of this work is that mutant epitopes identified as targets of checkpoint blockade can be converted into effective therapeutic vaccines. Synthetic long peptides containing mLama4 and mAlg8, administered with poly(I:C), induced rejection of established tumours with efficacy comparable to anti-PD-1 treatment and protected mice in a tumour-specific manner. The superiority of long peptides over minimal epitopes is consistent with the requirement for antigen processing and efficient presentation by professional antigen-presenting cells. These data provide experimental support for personalized vaccination strategies based on expressed somatic mutations.

Several issues remain unresolved. The sarcomas studied here contain many mutations and are responsive to checkpoint blockade; tumours with lower mutational burdens or impaired antigen presentation may behave differently. In addition, only a small number of predicted high-affinity epitopes proved immunogenic, underscoring the need for improved methods to predict naturally processed, therapeutically relevant neoantigens. Nevertheless, these results establish a direct mechanistic link between checkpoint blockade, mutant-antigen-specific T cells and tumour rejection, and provide a framework for using cancer genomics to guide individualized immunotherapy.

title: Checkpoint Blockade Cancer Immunotherapy Targets Tumour-Specific Mutant Antigens
text:
Our study fundamentally elucidates the mechanisms underlying the efficacy of checkpoint blockade immunotherapy by demonstrating that tumour-specific mutant antigens (TSMAs), or neoantigens, serve as the major targets for the reactivated CD8+ T cell response. Using a sophisticated genomics and bioinformatics pipeline, we successfully identified two dominant mutant epitopes (mLama4 and mAlg8) in progressively growing murine sarcomas that are specifically recognized by tumour-infiltrating T cells following anti-PD-1 or anti-CTLA-4 therapy. This confirms that the immune system's ability to eradicate established tumours upon checkpoint blockade is largely dependent on the recognition of these unique, mutation-derived peptides, which arise from the inherent genomic instability of the cancer cells.

Importantly, our findings reveal that T cells specific for these neoantigens are already present within the tumour microenvironment prior to therapy, but they exist in a dysfunctional or exhausted state, characterized by elevated expression of inhibitory receptors like LAG-3 and TIM-3. Treatment with checkpoint inhibitors effectively rescues these cells, triggering distinct, treatment-specific transcriptional and metabolic reprogramming. While both anti-PD-1 and anti-CTLA-4 lead to tumour rejection, they achieve this through non-overlapping mechanisms: anti-PD-1 primarily alters metabolic pathways and IL-2 signaling, whereas anti-CTLA-4 enhances proliferative and effector T cell pathways. The combination of both therapies results in a synergistic enhancement of effector functions, highlighting the complementary nature of these two clinical approaches.

Furthermore, we demonstrate that therapeutic vaccination using synthetic long peptides incorporating these identified mutant epitopes induces tumour rejection rates comparable to those achieved by checkpoint blockade itself. This indicates that targeting these specific neoantigens is sufficient to drive robust anti-tumour immunity. These results hold profound implications for the future of clinical oncology. By combining whole-exome sequencing with advanced epitope prediction algorithms, it is highly feasible to identify patient-specific neoantigens. Our work provides a strong preclinical foundation for the development of personalized cancer vaccines, which could be used either as standalone therapies or, more likely, in synergistic combination with checkpoint inhibitors to maximize antitumour efficacy and minimize toxicity in patients with advanced malignancies.

It securely laid the foundation for the rapidly expanding field of personalized cancer vaccines and neoantigen-targeted immunotherapies.

paper_title: Comparative Landscape of Genetic Dependencies in Human and Chimpanzee Stem Cells - 2023 - Cell
display_title: Comparative Landscape of Genetic Dependencies in Human and Chimpanzee Stem Cells
title: Comparative Landscape of Genetic Dependencies in Human and Chimpanzee Stem Cells
text: Comparative studies of great apes provide a window into our evolutionary past, but the extent and identity of cellular differences that emerged during hominin evolution remain largely unexplored. We established a comparative loss-of-function approach to evaluate whether changes in human cells altered requirements for essential genes. By performing genome-wide CRISPR interference screens in human and chimpanzee pluripotent stem cells, we identified 75 genes with species-specific effects on cellular proliferation. These genes comprised coherent processes, including cell cycle progression and lysosomal signaling, which we determined to be human-derived by comparison with orangutan cells. Human-specific robustness to CDK2 and CCNE1 depletion persisted in neural progenitor cells and cerebral organoids, supporting the G1-phase length hypothesis as a potential evolutionary mechanism in human brain expansion. Our findings demonstrate that evolutionary changes in human cells reshaped the landscape of essential genes and establish a platform for systematically uncovering latent cellular and molecular differences between species.
historical_context: This was one of the major publications during Richard She's postdoc years in Jonathan Weissman's lab. After spending his Ph.D. working on budding yeast, Richard spent 3 months living with wild chimpanzees in Uganda while deciding what to do next. This experience inspired this project, which aims to compare what makes humans special relative our closest living relatives in the animal kingdom, the chimpanzees. The broad idea behind the She Lab's research is that we can grow human and chimpanzee stem cells in a petri dish side by side, differentiate them into neurons, and then ask what makes the human cells different at the cellular and molecular level. This paper establishes the proof of concept of that platform, showing that human cells are uniquely resistant to CRISPR-mediated disruption of key cell cycle genes like CDK2 and Cyclin E1.

title: Comparative Landscape of Genetic Dependencies in Human and Chimpanzee Stem Cells
text: Comparative studies of humans and chimpanzees have identified many genetic and regulatory differences, but it remains difficult to determine which changes alter cellular physiology. Here, we developed a comparative CRISPR-interference screening framework to map genetic dependencies in homologous human and chimpanzee pluripotent stem cells. Genome-wide screens and validation across multiple independently derived lines showed that, despite a largely conserved essential gene set, stem cells from the two species could be distinguished by their responses to genetic perturbation. Comparisons with orangutan PSCs further indicated that altered G1/S control evolved along the human lineage. These results establish loss-of-function profiling as a functional approach for uncovering recently evolved molecular differences between closely related species.

title: Comparative Landscape of Genetic Dependencies in Human and Chimpanzee Stem Cells
text: To understand the evolutionary origins of unique human traits, it is critical to identify molecular pathways with divergent functions in conserved developmental cell types. We performed genome-wide CRISPR-interference (CRISPRi) screens in human and chimpanzee pluripotent stem cells (PSCs) to systematically map the comparative landscape of genetic dependencies. Despite high overall conservation between the species, we identified 75 genes with strongly divergent effects on cellular proliferation. These species-specific genetic dependencies are organized into coherent functional pathways. Notably, we discovered that human PSCs have evolved a unique robustness to the depletion of key cell cycle regulators, including Cyclin E1 and CDK2, which control the G1/S transition. This human-specific robustness to G1/S perturbations persists in neural progenitor cells and cerebral organoids. Our functional genomics approach provides a robust framework for identifying recently evolved gene networks, connecting molecular changes to evolutionary differences in human development.

paper_title: Comparative Landscape of Genetic Dependencies in Human and Chimpanzee Stem Cells - 2023 - Cell
display_title: Comparative Landscape of Genetic Dependencies in Human and Chimpanzee Stem Cells
title: Comparative Landscape of Genetic Dependencies in Human and Chimpanzee Stem Cells
text:
Comparative studies of humans and chimpanzees, our closest extant relatives, have long sought to define the evolutionary origins of unique human features. Within seven million years, humans evolved numerous specializations, from bipedalism to the threefold expansion of the cerebral cortex. Many of these novel human traits emerge from changes in cell behavior during development. These changes in cell behavior may in turn reflect underlying differences in molecular circuits that emerged during the short time scale of hominin evolution. However, we currently lack a framework for systematically identifying which molecular pathways play divergent roles in conserved developmental cell types.

Current approaches for studying the molecular basis of human evolution include reconstructing candidate mutations at specific loci in model organisms, but only a handful of mutations in non-coding regulatory regions and coding genes have been examined in detail. Among conserved non-coding elements with unexpected changes in the human lineage, specific loci have been linked to gene expression changes in distal limbs, increased sweat gland number, and increased neural proliferation. Among coding changes, two human-specific coding mutations in FOXP2 have been proposed to contribute to human language capabilities based on functional studies in mouse models and human genetics, and three modern human-specific mutations in KIF18A and KNL1 prolong metaphase and reduce segregation errors in neural progenitor cells. In addition, recent duplications and subsequent modifications of ARHGAP11B and NOTCH2NL have been implicated in the expansion of the human cortex, supporting predictions that human-specific mutations may influence proliferation of neural progenitor cells during development. Nonetheless, connecting individual candidate mutations to evolved human traits remains challenging because most mutations are neutral or low effect size, analyses are low throughput, and we lack a detailed understanding of the divergence in cellular and developmental phenotypes that ultimately give rise to species differences.

In parallel, high-throughput genomics-based approaches have described gene regulatory changes that may contribute to species differences. Because ape primary tissue is largely inaccessible during early development, recent studies have employed stem cell derived models as an experimentally tractable system for comparative analyses of species differences during development. Thousands of cell type-specific gene expression differences have been identified in pluripotent stem cells, cardiomyocytes, endoderm, neural crest, and cortical neurons. However, these gene expression differences comprise a mixture of neutral changes, causal changes, and indirect downstream consequences, and genes that mediate species differences may have conserved expression. Therefore, it can be difficult to ascertain which molecular changes, among hundreds or thousands, drive differences in cellular physiology.

The history of developmental genetics provides a template for linking the function of individual genes to organismal phenotypes. Early mutagenesis screens in Drosophila melanogaster identified genes critical for body axis patterning. Many of these genes belonged to highly conserved cell signaling pathways that also coordinate development in vertebrates, such as Wnt, Hedgehog, and BMP. More recent efforts in organismal screening involve several international consortia that have generated large collections of knockout mice to investigate more complex vertebrate phenotypes. The success of these genetic approaches has resulted in the functional annotation of many of the genes that guide mammalian development. However, despite the conservation of many core developmental principles from fruit flies to mice to humans, these shared molecular functions do not account for how our species evolved to be different.

To apply functional genomics approaches to questions of species divergence, we harnessed recent advances in CRISPR-based technologies that have enabled genome-scale perturbation screens across thousands of human cell lines. These efforts have mapped landscapes of genetic dependencies with an enrichment of essential genes in coherent pathways that typically cluster by cell type of origin. Extending this approach to studies of comparative evolution could reveal genes or cellular processes with divergent functional roles in homologous cell types. Illuminating the extent and identity of recently evolved genetic dependencies would complement individual candidate gene approaches, descriptive comparative genomics analyses, and single species loss-of-function studies. However, whether genetic dependencies diverged in closely related hominin species and how this knowledge could reveal previously unappreciated differences in cellular physiology remains unexplored.

To evaluate the extent of conservation and divergence in genetic dependencies between human and chimpanzee, we established a comparative loss-of-function screening approach in pluripotent stem cells (PSCs). PSCs are a model for the earliest stages of development, capturing features of the inner cell mass of the blastocyst, including the capacity to differentiate into all germ layers at a stage that precedes species differences in developmental timing and cell type composition. The state of pluripotency is well conserved between human and chimpanzee PSCs at the level of the transcriptome, epigenome, and cell fate potential, providing a homologous cell type for species comparison. In addition, PSCs have greater levels of open chromatin and gene expression than somatic cells, enabling large-scale study of gene function for genes later expressed in diverse cell types. As PSCs are poised to self-renew or differentiate into all germ layers based on environmental cues, we reasoned that changes in proliferation in PSCs could provide a sensitive measure for species-specific responses to a wide range of genetic perturbations.

Performing genetic screens using an in vitro model confers several advantages that could support isolation of molecular and cellular species differences. First, the ability to grow large numbers of PSCs enables a pooled library approach with multiple redundant library elements targeting each gene. Second, laboratory cell culture provides a well-defined and highly controlled environment, which minimizes extrinsic sources of variation. Lastly, the scalability of pooled screening allows for retesting of each cellular phenotype in PSCs derived from multiple individuals of each species to account for individual variation within a species. Thus, we conducted genome-wide CRISPR-interference (CRISPRi) screens in human and chimpanzee PSCs. Despite high levels of conservation, our screens revealed that genetic dependencies can diverge in short evolutionary time scales, that species differences are organized into coherent pathways and protein complexes, and that human-specific changes have evolved in gene networks promoting G1/S progression in PSCs and neural progenitor cells. In addition to these specific insights, our study establishes a broadly applicable experimental approach for uncovering latent molecular differences between closely related species.

After spending his Ph.D. working on budding yeast, Richard spent 3 months living with wild chimpanzees in Uganda while deciding what to do next. This experience inspired this project, which aims to compare what makes humans special relative our closest living relatives in the animal kingdom, the chimpanzees. The broad idea behind the She Lab's research is that we can grow human and chimpanzee stem cells in a petri dish side by side, differentiate them into neurons, and then ask what makes the human cells different at the cellular and molecular level. This paper establishes the proof of concept of that platform, showing that human cells are uniquely resistant to CRISPR-mediated disruption of key cell cycle genes like CDK2 and Cyclin E1.

paper_title: Comparative Landscape of Genetic Dependencies in Human and Chimpanzee Stem Cells - 2023 - Cell
display_title: Comparative Landscape of Genetic Dependencies in Human and Chimpanzee Stem Cells
title: Comparative Landscape of Genetic Dependencies in Human and Chimpanzee Stem Cells
text:
Humans and chimpanzees differ in anatomy, cognition, life history, and disease susceptibility, yet their genomes are highly similar. This paradox has motivated decades of effort to identify the molecular changes that accompanied human evolution. Comparative genomics has nominated human-specific coding substitutions, accelerated noncoding elements, structural variants, and changes in gene expression or chromatin state. These studies have provided a rich catalogue of candidate loci. However, distinguishing the subset of changes that alter cellular function from the much larger background of neutral divergence remains a central challenge. Most comparative measurements are descriptive: they reveal that a gene or regulatory element differs between species, but do not establish whether that difference affects a cellular phenotype, acts within a pathway, or contributes to organismal traits.

Functional genetics offers a complementary strategy. Rather than beginning with molecular differences and asking which are important, loss-of-function perturbations begin with cellular behavior and ask which genes are required to maintain it. In model organisms, genetic screens have defined the pathways that control development, growth, and survival. In human cells, pooled CRISPR screens have similarly revealed that essentiality is not an intrinsic property of a gene, but a context-dependent feature of the surrounding cellular network. Genes that are dispensable in one genetic or environmental state can become essential in another, exposing latent dependencies that are not obvious from baseline expression or sequence alone. This principle has been exploited extensively in cancer biology, where comparisons across cell lines have uncovered vulnerabilities produced by oncogenic mutations, lineage state, and pathway rewiring.

Whether evolutionary divergence between closely related species has similarly reshaped genetic dependencies is largely unknown. Humans and chimpanzees diverged recently enough that most genes, pathways, and developmental programs are deeply conserved, but long enough ago for regulatory and cellular differences to accumulate. Comparative studies of primate pluripotent stem cells (PSCs), neural progenitor cells, and cerebral organoids have shown broad conservation of cell identity and differentiation trajectories, alongside species differences in gene regulation and developmental timing. These systems therefore provide an opportunity to ask a more direct question: have recent evolutionary changes altered the genes and pathways that human and chimpanzee cells require for proliferation, survival, or differentiation?

Several features make PSCs an attractive starting point for such an analysis. PSCs can be derived and propagated from multiple individuals of each species under controlled culture conditions, reducing the confounding effects of tissue availability and environmental history. They can be engineered with CRISPR interference (CRISPRi) machinery, enabling scalable and reversible repression of endogenous genes without inducing DNA double-strand breaks. They also retain the ability to differentiate into developmentally relevant cell types, allowing discoveries made in the pluripotent state to be tested in neural progenitors and organoids. At the same time, PSCs are rapidly dividing cells with specialized cell cycle properties, making them well suited for detecting perturbations that affect proliferation and self-renewal.

Cell cycle regulation is of particular interest in the context of human evolution. Expansion of the human cerebral cortex is thought to have depended in part on changes in the behavior of neural progenitor cells, including the balance between proliferative self-renewal and neurogenic differentiation. One influential model proposes that the length of G1 phase influences this balance: shorter G1 phases favor continued progenitor divisions, whereas longer G1 phases increase the probability of neuronal differentiation. Experimental manipulation of cyclins and cyclin-dependent kinases in animal models can alter progenitor proliferation and cortical size, supporting the plausibility of this mechanism. However, whether the molecular architecture of G1/S control changed specifically in the human lineage has remained unclear. Standard comparative transcriptomic or epigenomic approaches can identify species differences in the expression of cell cycle regulators, but they cannot determine how robustly human and chimpanzee cells tolerate perturbation of those regulators.

A systematic functional comparison must also contend with several sources of variation that can masquerade as species differences. Individual genetic background, culture adaptation, reprogramming history, karyotypic abnormalities, and differences in p53 pathway status can all influence the outcome of proliferation-based screens. In addition, CRISPR reagents designed for the human genome may not target orthologous sequences equally well in nonhuman primates. Finally, inferring whether a trait is human-derived or chimpanzee-derived requires comparison to an outgroup species, because a difference between two species alone does not reveal which lineage changed. These considerations argue for an experimental framework that uses multiple independently derived cell lines per species, perturbation reagents matched across genomes, validation of candidate effects, and phylogenetic comparison beyond the human-chimpanzee pair.

Here, we developed a comparative CRISPRi screening platform to map genetic dependencies in human and chimpanzee PSCs. We first performed genome-wide loss-of-function screens to identify genes whose repression differentially affected proliferation in the two species. We then constructed a targeted validation library with sgRNAs matched between human and chimpanzee genomes and screened an expanded panel of p53-responsive PSC lines from multiple individuals. This approach identified a stringent set of 75 genes with species-specific effects on cellular proliferation. These genes were organized into coherent biological processes and protein complexes, including lysosomal V-ATPase-associated signaling, UFMylation, chromatin regulation, and cell cycle control, indicating that recent evolutionary divergence altered functional pathways rather than isolated genes.

We further investigated two classes of species-specific dependencies. Human PSCs displayed increased sensitivity to depletion of ATP6AP1, linking species differences in growth to altered reliance on lysosome-associated mTORC1 signaling. Conversely, human PSCs were unexpectedly robust to depletion of the G1/S regulators CDK2, CDK4, and Cyclin E1, whereas chimpanzee PSCs required these factors for normal proliferation. Mechanistic experiments indicated that elevated CDK1 activity could compensate for loss of Cyclin E-CDK2 activity in chimpanzee cells, suggesting that altered network architecture can buffer human cells against perturbations to canonical G1/S regulators. Importantly, human-specific robustness to CDK2 and Cyclin E1 depletion persisted in neural progenitor cells and cerebral organoids, connecting species-specific genetic dependencies in stem cells to a developmental context relevant to cortical expansion.

Finally, by extending selected perturbations to orangutan PSCs, we inferred the phylogenetic origin of several dependencies. Human-specific robustness to perturbations of G1/S regulators was not shared by chimpanzee or orangutan cells, supporting the inference that this property evolved along the human lineage. Together, these results establish comparative loss-of-function screening as a strategy for discovering cellular and molecular changes that emerged during recent primate evolution. More broadly, they show that closely related species can differ not only in the expression or sequence of genes, but in the functional dependencies that reveal how conserved pathways have been rewired.

display_title: Comparative Landscape of Genetic Dependencies in Human and Chimpanzee Stem Cells
title: Comparative Landscape of Genetic Dependencies in Human and Chimpanzee Stem Cells
text:
The evolutionary trajectory that led to the emergence of modern humans involved profound anatomical, cognitive, and behavioral changes, distinguishing our species from our closest living relatives, the great apes. Understanding the biological basis of these human-specific traits is one of the most compelling challenges in evolutionary biology and genetics. While comparative genomics has successfully identified millions of genetic differences—including single nucleotide variants, structural variants, and gene duplications—that distinguish humans from chimpanzees and other primates, translating this vast catalog of genetic variation into functional understanding remains incredibly difficult. The vast majority of these genetic changes reside in non-coding regions and are presumed to be largely neutral. Even among those changes that alter protein sequence or gene expression, determining which specific molecular alterations drove evolutionary adaptations, rather than merely reflecting genetic drift, poses a significant hurdle.

Historically, efforts to bridge the gap between genotype and phenotype in human evolution have relied heavily on comparative transcriptomics and epigenomics. By profiling gene expression and chromatin accessibility across various tissues and developmental stages in humans, chimpanzees, and macaques, researchers have identified thousands of genes with species-specific expression patterns. These studies have provided valuable insights into the regulatory networks that have been rewired during primate evolution, particularly in tissues like the brain. However, gene expression profiling inherently captures both direct regulatory changes and widespread indirect downstream effects, making it difficult to pinpoint the core driver genes within complex networks. More importantly, differential expression does not necessarily equate to differential functional importance. A gene might be expressed at identical levels in human and chimpanzee cells, yet the cells might rely on that gene to entirely different degrees due to rewiring in interacting pathways or compensatory mechanisms. Thus, purely observational approaches cannot directly evaluate the functional consequence of losing a gene's activity in one species versus another.

In model organisms such as yeast, flies, and mice, loss-of-function genetic screens have long served as the gold standard for defining the networks of genes essential for cellular survival, proliferation, and differentiation. The recent advent of CRISPR-Cas9 technology, and specifically CRISPR interference (CRISPRi), has revolutionized our ability to perform highly specific, scalable, genome-wide loss-of-function screens in mammalian cells. By precisely repressing transcription at targeted loci, CRISPRi allows for the systematic cataloging of genetic dependencies—the specific genes that a cell type absolutely requires for its growth and survival. Projects like the Cancer Dependency Map (DepMap) have utilized these tools to uncover massive landscapes of genetic dependencies across hundreds of human cancer cell lines, revealing how specific mutations or cellular states alter a cell's reliance on particular genes and pathways.

Despite these technological advances, the concept of a "genetic dependency" has primarily been explored within the context of disease or developmental stage within a single species. Whether the fundamental essentiality of a gene can diverge between closely related species—and whether comparative functional genomics can uncover such species-specific dependencies—has remained largely unexplored. Given that humans and chimpanzees diverged from a common ancestor only six to eight million years ago, their genomes are remarkably similar, sharing approximately 99% sequence identity in alignable regions. At the cellular level, pluripotent stem cells (PSCs) from humans and chimpanzees display nearly identical morphologies, share core pluripotency transcriptional networks, and exhibit similar capacities for directed differentiation into diverse somatic lineages. Because of this profound similarity, it has been widely assumed that the core requirements for basic cellular processes, such as proliferation and survival, would be strictly conserved between the two species.

We hypothesized, however, that the evolutionary changes which shaped human-specific physiological traits might have subtly but significantly rewired fundamental cellular pathways, altering the dependencies of human cells on specific genes. If true, a comparative loss-of-function approach could uncover "species-specific genetic dependencies," providing a functional window into the molecular pathways that diverged during human evolution. To test this, we established a robust platform for comparative functional genomics by engineering CRISPRi machinery into multiple human and chimpanzee PSC lines. PSCs provide an ideal, highly scalable in vitro model for this comparison, as they capture the earliest stages of embryonic development, a period during which many critical species-specific developmental trajectories are established.

In this study, we perform paired, genome-wide CRISPRi screens in human and chimpanzee PSCs to systematically evaluate the essentiality of nearly every protein-coding gene in the primate genome. We demonstrate that, while the vast majority of essential genes are perfectly conserved, a stringent subset of genes exhibits profound, reproducible, species-specific effects on cellular proliferation. We discover that these divergent dependencies do not simply occur randomly but are highly enriched for genes operating within coherent functional modules, including specific metabolic signaling complexes and core regulators of the cell cycle.

Through rigorous secondary validation screens across an expanded panel of human and chimpanzee individuals, we pinpoint exactly 75 genes whose essentiality diverged during recent evolutionary history. Remarkably, we find that human PSCs have evolved a unique robustness to the depletion of several critical regulators of the G1/S cell cycle transition, including cyclin-dependent kinase 2 (CDK2) and Cyclin E1 (CCNE1). In contrast, chimpanzee PSCs retain a strict reliance on these factors for cell cycle progression. Through functional and pharmacological assays, we link this differential dependency to a broader rewiring of cell cycle checkpoint enforcement and progression in human cells.

Furthermore, we explore the developmental implications of these findings by extending our CRISPRi platform to differentiating neural progenitor cells (NPCs) and three-dimensional cerebral organoids. We show that the human-specific robustness to cell cycle perturbations persists in the neural lineage. The regulation of the G1-phase length has long been proposed as a key developmental mechanism controlling the balance between progenitor expansion and neuronal differentiation during cortical development. Our functional genomics data reveal that the molecular inputs governing this critical cell cycle transition have significantly diverged between humans and chimpanzees. Finally, by integrating orangutan stem cells into our functional assays, we infer the phylogenetic origin of these divergent dependencies, demonstrating that the human-specific robustness to CDK2 and CCNE1 depletion represents a derived trait that emerged specifically along the hominin lineage. Collectively, our findings establish comparative functional genomics as a powerful new paradigm for dissecting the molecular basis of human evolution, revealing that the fundamental landscape of essential genes is not static but can be dynamically reshaped during recent evolutionary history.

paper_title: Comparative Landscape of Genetic Dependencies in Human and Chimpanzee Stem Cells - 2023 - Cell
display_title: Comparative Landscape of Genetic Dependencies in Human and Chimpanzee Stem Cells
title: Comparative Landscape of Genetic Dependencies in Human and Chimpanzee Stem Cells
text:
Loss-of-function screens have provided fundamental insights into the genes that regulate the development of model organisms. Here, we applied genetic screens to human and chimpanzee PSCs to examine whether the requirements for essential genes could differ in closely related species. By performing paired genome-wide CRISPRi screens, we uncovered a landscape of divergent genetic dependencies. Despite human and chimpanzee PSCs being similar in their cellular morphology, response to in vitro differentiation protocols, and core set of essential genes, we identified 75 genes with divergent roles in controlling cellular proliferation. We observed that many of these genes were organized in coherent protein complexes and biochemical pathways. By contrast, existing state-of-the-art comparative approaches, including RNA-seq and chromatin state profiling have identified thousands of differentially expressed and accessible genes between humans and nonhuman primates, but are unable to directly evaluate the role of each gene in key cellular processes such as survival, proliferation, and differentiation. In addition, limited coherence among differentially expressed genes makes it difficult to pinpoint divergent pathways or protein complexes. Our data thus comprise a rich resource that interfaces with existing studies of gene regulation and chromatin states and provide a functional genomics guide for future candidate gene approaches.

How might the genetic dependencies we observed in PSCs relate to organismal differences that manifest during development? Intriguingly, one of the strongest observations that emerged from our unbiased genome-wide screening approach was human-specific robustness to depletion of cell cycle factors, which persisted in neural progenitor cells. This finding aligns with long-standing hypotheses that changes in cell cycle regulation could play a role in human-specific cortical expansion. The G1-phase length hypothesis proposes that factors which lengthen G1 duration in NPCs increase the probability of differentiation towards non-proliferative neuronal fates, while factors reducing G1 length promote proliferative self-renewal of NPCs. Indeed, loss of CDK2 or CDK4 in mouse NPCs prolongs G1 length and causes premature neuronal differentiation at the expense of self-renewal. Conversely, exogenous overexpression of CDK4 and Cyclin D1 in mouse and ferret reduces G1 length, promotes self-renewing divisions in basal progenitor cells, and results in increased brain size and cortical area, while preserving a structurally normal, six-layered cortex. In humans, mutations that promote cyclin D2 stability lead to megalencephaly. These studies underscore the influence of inputs to the G1/S transition on brain expansion during development. However, whether this developmental mechanism changed specifically in recent human evolution remained unexplored. Our demonstration that human NPCs are more likely than chimpanzee NPCs to continue cycling upon equivalent repression of CDK2 or Cyclin E1 connects proposed developmental mechanisms to molecular changes that occurred in human evolution. Although physiological stressors do occur during development that influence the size of the neural progenitor pool, it remains unknown how external environmental stimuli or the intrinsic tempo of differentiation might differ between humans and chimpanzees and interface with these evolved molecular differences.

The endeavor to study the molecular basis of human evolution has been compared to searching for needles in a haystack, as human-specific genetic variants and gene expression changes are numerous and predominantly neutral. By contrast, our finding that human and chimpanzee PSCs exhibit distinct genetic dependencies, even for genes that lack clear expression or protein-coding sequence divergence, provides a complementary approach for isolating recently evolved functional changes in human gene networks. This conceptual advance mirrors the progression of cancer genetics research from sequencing and transcriptomics efforts such as TCGA to functional genetics-based efforts such as DepMap. Moreover, while driver mutations can be identified in tumors based on their independent recurrence, human evolution has occurred only once, highlighting the added value of a functional genomics platform. We expect that loss-of-function profiling can be extended to cellular models of genetic variation and disease risk within humans to identify shared vulnerabilities and convergent pathway level differences. Lastly, our approach can be readily applied in differentiated cell types and interfaced with higher dimensional measurements of cell phenotypes, opening the door to future efforts for understanding molecular control of species differences across stages of development.

While our study systematically maps species-specific genetic dependencies in pluripotent stem cells, it is unclear which dependencies will persist across diverse differentiation trajectories and whether new cell type-specific dependencies will arise. We applied a principled and systematic exploration of the changes in the genetic landscape that evolved during the short divergence time between humans and chimpanzees. However, to further connect human-specific robustness to depletion of cell cycle regulators to physiological differences in brain development, we will need to understand how naturally occurring environmental factors can produce species differences in NPC cell cycle properties and proliferative potential. In addition, the in vitro models of NPCs that we employed produce homologous cell types to those found in vivo, but may exhibit differences in metabolism, spatial architecture, and maturation speed. Finally, our primary screen unexpectedly included two P53-non-responsive chimpanzee cell lines, which we accounted for by constructing four additional P53-responsive cell lines in the validation screen. The frequency of pro-survival adaptations in PSCs highlights the need for multiple representatives of each species and quality controls (such as the Nutlin-3a assay) to distinguish species differences from line-to-line variation.

paper_title: Comparative Landscape of Genetic Dependencies in Human and Chimpanzee Stem Cells - 2023 - Cell
display_title: Comparative Landscape of Genetic Dependencies in Human and Chimpanzee Stem Cells
title: Comparative Landscape of Genetic Dependencies in Human and Chimpanzee Stem Cells
text:
In this study, we established a comparative CRISPRi screening framework to identify genetic dependencies that diverged during recent human and chimpanzee evolution. By performing genome-wide and validation screens across multiple independently derived PSC lines, we found that most growth-associated gene functions are conserved between species, but that a discrete set of perturbations produces reproducible species-specific effects. These differences were sufficiently coherent to distinguish human from chimpanzee stem cells by their sgRNA depletion profiles alone. Thus, although human and chimpanzee PSCs share core features of pluripotency, they differ in the molecular circuits that buffer or enforce proliferation under genetic perturbation.

Our results provide evidence that species-specific genetic dependencies are not simply a consequence of differential expression or coding divergence in the perturbed genes themselves. A small number of candidates, including ASPM and KATNA1, carry evolutionary signatures that are consistent with direct changes in gene function, and other genes displayed quantitative expression differences between species. However, the broader set of validated dependencies was depleted for both coding divergence and species-biased expression. This observation suggests that many functional differences between closely related species may emerge from changes in pathway state, network compensation, or upstream regulatory architecture rather than from obvious changes in the target gene. In this respect, comparative perturbation screening complements gene expression and comparative genomics approaches by identifying genes whose functional importance has changed even when their sequence or expression appears largely conserved.

The species-specific dependencies identified here were organized into biological modules, including UFMylation, the MOZ acetyltransferase complex, lysosomal signaling, and cell cycle control. This pathway-level coherence provides an internal validation of the screening approach and indicates that evolutionary changes have altered requirements for entire molecular processes. The human-specific requirement for ATP6AP1 and ATP6AP2 illustrates this principle. Although core V-ATPase function was essential in both species and lysosomal acidification was not detectably impaired by ATP6AP1 depletion, human cells exhibited a stronger loss of mTORC1 signaling after perturbation of ATP6AP1. These findings suggest that human PSCs acquired increased reliance on specific accessory components of the lysosomal signaling machinery, potentially reflecting broader changes in metabolic or growth factor responsiveness during early development.

The most prominent species difference that we identified involved regulators of G1/S progression. Human PSCs were unexpectedly robust to depletion of CDK2, CCNE1, and CDK4, whereas chimpanzee PSCs required these genes for efficient proliferation and cell cycle progression. Mechanistically, our data support a model in which human cells can compensate for reduced Cyclin E-CDK2 activity through increased CDK1 activity. CDK1 overexpression was sufficient to rescue the effects of CDK2 or CCNE1 depletion in chimpanzee PSCs, suggesting that a shift in the balance of cyclin-dependent kinase activity can alter the dependency landscape of pluripotent cells. The larger effects of RBL1 repression in chimpanzee cells and the species-specific response to FAM122A depletion further support the conclusion that human and chimpanzee PSCs differ in how they regulate entry into S phase and respond to checkpoint perturbation.

These cell cycle differences may be relevant to developmental features that distinguish humans from other apes. The length of G1 has long been proposed to influence neural progenitor behavior, with shorter or more permissive G1 progression favoring proliferative divisions and expansion of progenitor populations. Our findings show that human-specific robustness to CDK2 and CCNE1 depletion is not restricted to PSCs but persists in NPCs and cerebral organoids. In chimpanzee NPCs and organoids, depletion of these regulators increased G1 accumulation and reduced organoid growth, whereas human cells maintained proliferation under the same perturbations. These results do not demonstrate that the genes identified here caused human cortical expansion, but they provide experimental support for the idea that human neural progenitors possess derived mechanisms that stabilize proliferative cell cycle progression.

The inclusion of orangutan PSCs allowed us to infer the evolutionary direction of selected differences. Human robustness to depletion of CDK2, CDK4, and CCNE1 was not shared by chimpanzee or orangutan cells, arguing that altered G1/S dependency emerged on the human lineage. Similarly, sensitivity to ATP6AP1 depletion appeared to be human-derived. By contrast, other phenotypes, including the proliferative effect of KAT6A repression and altered sensitivity to UFL1 depletion, were more consistent with changes on the chimpanzee lineage. These observations emphasize that comparative studies between humans and chimpanzees should not presume that all differences are human-derived. Instead, phylogenetic sampling is required to distinguish ancestral, human-specific, and chimpanzee-specific states.

Several limitations remain. PSCs and stem-cell-derived neural models provide controlled and scalable systems for functional comparisons, but they do not recapitulate all aspects of embryonic development in vivo. Growth-based CRISPRi screens are also most sensitive to perturbations that alter proliferation or survival and may miss species differences related to differentiation, morphology, electrophysiology, or cell-cell interactions. In addition, CRISPRi reduces gene expression rather than producing complete loss of function, and the consequences of partial repression may differ from those of genetic mutation in organisms. Future work applying comparative perturbation screens across additional cell types, developmental stages, and great ape species should extend the principles established here and help connect molecular dependencies to organismal traits.

Together, our findings demonstrate that genetic dependencies have diverged over the short evolutionary interval separating humans and chimpanzees. These changes are reproducible across individuals, organized into functional modules, and can reveal latent cellular differences not apparent from gene expression or sequence analysis alone. Comparative functional genomics therefore provides a powerful route for identifying the molecular processes that changed during hominin evolution and for prioritizing candidate pathways that may have contributed to the emergence of human-specific developmental phenotypes.

paper_title: Comparative Landscape of Genetic Dependencies in Human and Chimpanzee Stem Cells - 2023 - Cell
display_title: Comparative Landscape of Genetic Dependencies in Human and Chimpanzee Stem Cells
title: Comparative Landscape of Genetic Dependencies in Human and Chimpanzee Stem Cells
text:
Our comparative loss-of-function approach using genome-wide CRISPR interference reveals a previously unappreciated landscape of evolutionary divergence in essential gene networks between humans and chimpanzees. By systematically perturbing the genomes of closely related primate pluripotent stem cells (PSCs), we discovered 75 genes that exhibit species-specific effects on cellular proliferation. This finding challenges the assumption that fundamental developmental pathways and the core essential-ome are rigidly conserved among closely related species. Instead, our data demonstrate that genetic dependencies can shift significantly within the relatively short evolutionary timespan separating humans and chimpanzees. Importantly, these differences do not manifest as random noise but are organized into coherent functional modules and protein complexes, indicating systematic evolutionary modifications of specific cellular pathways.

A striking example of this divergence is the human-specific dependency on the lysosomal V-ATPase accessory proteins, ATP6AP1 and ATP6AP2. While core V-ATPase function remains strictly essential across species for maintaining lysosomal pH, human cells have evolved an increased reliance on ATP6AP1 for mediating mTORC1 signaling. This suggests that the integration of nutrient sensing and growth factor signaling at the lysosomal surface has been rewired during human evolution, potentially to support the unique metabolic demands of human development.

Even more profoundly, our genetic screens uncovered a critical human-specific robustness to the depletion of central G1/S cell cycle regulators, including CDK2, CDK4, and Cyclin E1. Chimpanzee PSCs and neural progenitor cells (NPCs) strictly require these factors for timely G1/S transition, accumulating in the G1 phase upon their depletion. In stark contrast, human PSCs and NPCs seamlessly compensate for the loss of Cyclin E1-CDK2 activity, maintaining normal cell cycle progression. Our pharmacological and genetic rescue experiments suggest that this robustness is mediated, at least in part, by higher endogenous levels of CDK1 in human cells, which allows them to bypass the loss of CDK2 or upstream regulators like FAM122A and CHK1. This evolutionary rewiring results in human cells exhibiting a more relaxed S phase and G2/M checkpoint compared to chimpanzee cells.

The persistence of this human-specific robustness to cell cycle perturbations in NPCs and 3D cerebral organoids has profound implications for understanding human brain evolution. The 'G1-phase length hypothesis' posits that the duration of the G1 phase acts as a critical determinant of cell fate in the developing neocortex: a shorter G1 phase promotes continued proliferative divisions of neural progenitors, whereas a lengthening G1 phase triggers neurogenic differentiation. By acquiring an enhanced ability to bypass cell cycle checkpoints and maintain rapid G1/S transitions even in the face of genetic or metabolic stress, human neural progenitors may possess a heightened capacity to sustain prolonged proliferative expansion. This molecular resilience could be a foundational mechanism contributing to the massive expansion of the cerebral cortex—a defining hallmark of the human species.

To trace the evolutionary origins of these distinct genetic dependencies, our three-way comparison including orangutan PSCs was instrumental. By applying the principle of maximum parsimony, we deduced that the dependence on CDK2, CDK4, and Cyclin E1 represents the ancestral great ape state, still present in orangutans and chimpanzees. The robustness to depletion of these cell cycle factors is therefore a derived trait that evolved uniquely along the hominin lineage after the divergence from the chimpanzee ancestor. Similarly, the specific requirement for ATP6AP1 is also a human-derived trait. Conversely, the specific responses to genes like KAT6A in chimpanzee PSCs indicate that significant molecular innovations also occurred independently on the chimpanzee lineage. These findings emphasize that both lineages experienced substantial cellular rewiring, preventing us from viewing the chimpanzee cellular state simply as a proxy for the human ancestor.

While comparative genomics has traditionally focused on identifying changes in DNA sequence or steady-state gene expression, our functional genomics approach uniquely isolates the phenotypic consequences of these evolutionary changes. Many of the species-specific genetic dependencies we identified do not exhibit obvious signatures of positive selection in their coding sequences or striking differences in basal gene expression. This underscores the complexity of evolutionary biology: critical species differences can arise from subtle alterations in protein-protein interactions, shifts in complex stoichiometry, or rewiring of post-translational signaling networks that elude standard descriptive genomic analyses.

In conclusion, our study establishes an experimentally tractable platform for identifying latent molecular differences that have shaped human evolution. By shifting the comparative paradigm from sequence and expression to functional dependency, we have uncovered a new dimension of species divergence. The discovery that human cells have uniquely evolved robustness in cell cycle regulation provides a compelling molecular mechanism that may underpin the evolutionary expansion of the human brain. Moving forward, applying this comparative functional genomics framework to other specialized cell types, such as mature neurons, cardiomyocytes, or immune cells, will undoubtedly yield further insights into the molecular adaptations that make us uniquely human. This approach not only illuminates our evolutionary past but also highlights the potential limitations of using non-human primate models for understanding highly derived human cellular phenotypes and diseases.

paper_title: Crystal Structure of the Beta2 Adrenergic Receptor-Gs Protein Complex - 2011 - Nature
display_title: Crystal Structure of the Beta2 Adrenergic Receptor-Gs Protein Complex
title: Crystal Structure of the Beta2 Adrenergic Receptor-Gs Protein Complex
text: G protein-coupled receptors (GPCRs) are responsible for the majority of cellular responses to hormones and neurotransmitters as well as the senses of sight, olfaction and taste. The paradigm of GPCR signalling is the activation of a heterotrimeric GTP binding protein (G protein) by an agonist-occupied receptor. The β2 adrenergic receptor (β2AR) activation of Gs, the stimulatory G protein for adenylyl cyclase, has long been a model system for GPCR signalling. Here we present the crystal structure of the active state ternary complex composed of agonist-occupied monomeric β2AR and nucleotide-free Gs heterotrimer. The principal interactions between the β2AR and Gs involve the amino- and carboxy-terminal α-helices of Gs, with conformational changes propagating to the nucleotide-binding pocket. The largest conformational changes in the β2AR include a 14 Å outward movement at the cytoplasmic end of transmembrane segment 6 (TM6) and an α-helical extension of the cytoplasmic end of TM5. The most surprising observation is a major displacement of the α-helical domain of Gαs relative to the Ras-like GTPase domain. This crystal structure represents the first high-resolution view of transmembrane signalling by a GPCR.
historical_context: In 2011, Søren Rasmussen, Brian Kobilka, and colleagues achieved a monumental milestone in structural biology by solving the high-resolution crystal structure of the β2-adrenergic receptor actively engaged with its stimulatory G protein (Gs). This study successfully captured the elusive, highly dynamic moment of transmembrane signaling, revealing the exact structural rearrangements caused by an extracellular hormone or drug that trigger the intracellular G protein. This work was a capstone achievement that directly yielded the 2012 Nobel Prize in Chemistry for Brian Kobilka and Robert Lefkowitz. The Nobel committee lauded their extensive studies of G-protein-coupled receptors (GPCRs), noting that dissecting this vast family of receptors—which mediate nearly half of all prescription drugs—is vital for understanding cellular environmental sensing and designing superior, targeted therapeutics.

paper_title: Crystal Structure of the Beta2 Adrenergic Receptor-Gs Protein Complex - 2011 - Nature
display_title: Crystal Structure of the Beta2 Adrenergic Receptor-Gs Protein Complex
title: Crystal Structure of the Beta2 Adrenergic Receptor-Gs Protein Complex
text: G protein-coupled receptors (GPCRs) regulate a broad range of physiological processes by coupling extracellular agonist binding to activation of heterotrimeric G proteins. The β2 adrenergic receptor (β2AR) has long served as a model for understanding this process, but the structural basis for nucleotide exchange and signal transduction has remained unclear. Here we describe the crystal structure of the agonist-bound β2AR in complex with nucleotide-free Gs, determined at 3.2 Å resolution. Crystallization was enabled by stabilization of the complex in maltose neopentyl glycol detergent, fusion of T4 lysozyme to the receptor amino terminus, lipidic cubic phase crystallization, and a camelid nanobody that bridges the Gαs and Gβ subunits. The structure reveals a large outward displacement of receptor transmembrane helix 6 and extension of helix 5, forming an extensive interface with the Ras-like domain of Gαs. The carboxy-terminal α5 helix of Gαs inserts into the activated receptor core, while the Gαs α-helical domain undergoes a pronounced rotation away from the nucleotide-binding pocket. These observations provide a structural framework for GPCR-mediated G protein activation and suggest new opportunities for targeting receptor-G protein interfaces.

title: Crystal Structure of the Beta2 Adrenergic Receptor-Gs Protein Complex
text: G protein-coupled receptors (GPCRs) constitute the largest family of cell-surface signalling proteins, transducing extracellular stimuli into intracellular responses through heterotrimeric G proteins. Despite decades of biochemical and pharmacological characterization of the β2 adrenergic receptor (β2AR) and its stimulatory G protein Gs, the structural basis for receptor-mediated G protein activation has remained elusive. Here we present the crystal structure of the agonist-occupied β2AR in complex with the nucleotide-free Gs heterotrimer at 3.2 Å resolution. To obtain diffraction-quality crystals, the unstructured amino terminus of the β2AR was replaced with T4 lysozyme, the complex was stabilized with the high-affinity agonist BI-167107, and a camelid nanobody (Nb35) was used to lock the interface between the Gα and Gβ subunits. The structure reveals a 14 Å outward movement of transmembrane helix 6 and substantial rearrangements at the cytoplasmic surface of the receptor, which together create a cavity that accommodates the carboxy-terminal α5-helix of Gαs. Most strikingly, the α-helical domain of Gαs is rotated approximately 127° relative to its Ras-like domain, exposing the empty nucleotide-binding pocket. These observations provide a structural framework for understanding transmembrane signalling and nucleotide exchange in GPCR-G protein complexes.

title: Crystal Structure of the Beta2 Adrenergic Receptor-Gs Protein Complex
text: G protein-coupled receptors (GPCRs) are ubiquitous membrane proteins responsible for transducing extracellular signals into intracellular responses via heterotrimeric G proteins. Despite significant progress in structural studies of inactive GPCRs, the structural basis for the active state and GPCR-G protein coupling has remained elusive. Here we present the 3.2 Å crystal structure of the human β2 adrenergic receptor (β2AR) in complex with the stimulatory G protein, Gs. To stabilize this highly dynamic complex for crystallogenesis, we utilized a high-affinity agonist, a customized detergent, T4 lysozyme fusion, and an application of a camelid nanobody (Nb35) that binds the G protein heterotrimer interface. Strikingly, the α-helical domain of Gαs undergoes a massive displacement relative to the Ras-like domain, revealing the mechanism of GDP release and nucleotide exchange. This structure provides the first high-resolution view of transmembrane signaling by a GPCR, offering critical insights into the mechanism of receptor-mediated G protein activation.

paper_title: Crystal Structure of the Beta2 Adrenergic Receptor-Gs Protein Complex - 2011 - Nature
display_title: Crystal Structure of the Beta2 Adrenergic Receptor-Gs Protein Complex
title: Crystal Structure of the Beta2 Adrenergic Receptor-Gs Protein Complex
text:
The β2 adrenergic receptor (β2AR) has been a model system for the large and diverse family of G protein-coupled receptors (GPCRs) for over 40 years. It was one of the first GPCRs to be characterized by radioligand binding, and it was the first neurotransmitter receptor to be cloned and structurally determined by crystallography. The β2AR was initially identified based on its physiological and pharmacological properties, but it was not known if receptors and G proteins were separate entities, or parts of the same protein. Subsequent biochemical studies led to the isolation and purification of functional β2AR and Gs, the stimulatory G protein that activates adenylyl cyclase, and the reconstitution of this signalling complex in phospholipid vesicles. The cooperative interactions of β2AR and Gs observed in ligand binding assays formed the foundation of the ternary complex model of GPCR activation. In the ternary complex consisting of agonist, receptor and G protein, the affinity of the receptor for agonist is enhanced and the specificity of the G protein for guanine nucleotides changes in favour of GTP over GDP. The GPCR field has evolved markedly since these initial studies. Isolation of the genes and cDNAs for the β2AR and other GPCRs using protein sequencing and expression cloning led to the expansion of the family by homology cloning. More recently, sequencing of the human genome led to the identification of over 800 GPCR genes. Experimental tools for identifying protein-protein interactions and for expression and silencing of genes have revealed a complex network of cellular signalling and regulatory pathways including G protein-independent activation of cytosolic kinases. Nevertheless, the β2AR continues to be a relevant model for most aspects of GPCR pharmacology, signalling and regulation.

Notwithstanding the remarkable advances in this field, we still know relatively little about the structural basis for transmembrane signalling by GPCRs. The G protein cycle for the β2AR-Gs complex is well established. Agonist binding to the β2AR promotes interactions with GDP-bound Gs αβγ heterotrimer, leading to the exchange of GDP for GTP, and the functional dissociation of Gs into Gα-GTP and Gβγ subunits. The separate Gα-GTP and Gβγ subunits can modulate the activity of different cellular effectors (channels, kinases or other enzymes). The intrinsic GTPase activity of Gαs leads to hydrolysis of GTP to GDP and the reassociation of Gα-GDP and Gβγ subunits, and the termination of signalling. The active state of a GPCR can be defined as that conformation that couples to and stabilizes a nucleotide-free G protein. In this agonist-β2AR-Gs ternary complex, Gs has a higher affinity for GTP than GDP, and the β2AR has an approximately 100-fold higher affinity for agonists than does β2AR alone. In an effort to understand the structural basis for GPCR signalling, we crystallized the β2AR-Gs complex.
historical_context: In 2011, Søren Rasmussen, Brian Kobilka, and colleagues achieved a monumental milestone in structural biology by solving the high-resolution crystal structure of the β2-adrenergic receptor actively engaged with its stimulatory G protein (Gs). This study successfully captured the elusive, highly dynamic moment of transmembrane signaling, revealing the exact structural rearrangements caused by an extracellular hormone or drug that trigger the intracellular G protein. This work was a capstone achievement that directly yielded the 2012 Nobel Prize in Chemistry for Brian Kobilka and Robert Lefkowitz. The Nobel committee lauded their extensive studies of G-protein-coupled receptors (GPCRs), noting that dissecting this vast family of receptors—which mediate nearly half of all prescription drugs—is vital for understanding cellular environmental sensing and designing superior, targeted therapeutics.

paper_title: Crystal Structure of the Beta2 Adrenergic Receptor-Gs Protein Complex - 2011 - Nature
display_title: Crystal Structure of the Beta2 Adrenergic Receptor-Gs Protein Complex
title: Crystal Structure of the Beta2 Adrenergic Receptor-Gs Protein Complex
text:
G protein-coupled receptors (GPCRs) constitute the largest family of signalling receptors in the human genome and mediate cellular responses to hormones, neurotransmitters, sensory stimuli and chemokines. The central event in GPCR signalling is the conversion of extracellular ligand binding into intracellular activation of a heterotrimeric guanine nucleotide-binding protein. In the classical model, an agonist-bound receptor stabilizes a nucleotide-free state of the G protein, thereby promoting GDP release and allowing GTP binding to initiate downstream signalling. Despite extensive biochemical, pharmacological and biophysical studies, the structural basis by which an activated receptor catalyses nucleotide exchange has remained poorly understood.

The β2 adrenergic receptor (β2AR) and Gs, the stimulatory G protein for adenylyl cyclase, have provided one of the most intensively studied systems for understanding GPCR activation. Agonist binding to β2AR produces a high-affinity ternary complex with Gs that is destabilized by guanine nucleotides, a property that has long served as a functional signature of receptor activation. Previous crystal structures of GPCRs, including inactive-state structures of β2AR and other family A receptors, have revealed the architecture of the seven-transmembrane helical bundle and the ligand-binding pocket. More recently, active-state receptor conformations have been stabilized either by covalent modification, by retinal photoactivation in rhodopsin, or by nanobodies that mimic aspects of G protein binding. These structures have provided important insight into receptor activation, particularly movements of the cytoplasmic ends of transmembrane helices 5 and 6. However, they do not reveal how an intact heterotrimeric G protein engages an activated receptor or how this interaction is coupled to rearrangement of the nucleotide-binding pocket.

Structural studies of the GPCR-G protein complex have been limited by the dynamic and transient nature of this signalling intermediate. The receptor and G protein must be maintained in an agonist-bound, nucleotide-free state, while preserving a membrane protein complex with a large soluble component in detergent or lipidic phases suitable for crystallography. Additional challenges arise from the intrinsic conformational flexibility of the Gα subunit, particularly the relative orientation of its Ras-like GTPase and α-helical domains, which together form the guanine nucleotide-binding site.

Here we describe the crystallographic determination of the agonist-bound β2AR-Gs complex. The structure was enabled by stabilization of the complex with a high-affinity agonist, removal of nucleotide, detergent optimization, fusion of T4 lysozyme to the receptor amino terminus, and use of a camelid nanobody that binds the G protein heterotrimer. The resulting structure provides a high-resolution view of a GPCR in complex with its cognate heterotrimeric G protein and reveals the conformational changes in both receptor and G protein that underlie transmembrane signal transduction.
