title: Crystal Structure of the Beta2 Adrenergic Receptor-Gs Protein Complex
text:
G protein-coupled receptors (GPCRs) represent the largest and most diverse family of membrane proteins in the human genome, comprising over 800 distinct members. These receptors act as the primary gatekeepers of cellular communication, mediating physiological responses to an extraordinary array of extracellular signals, including hormones, neurotransmitters, chemokines, local mediators, and even sensory stimuli such as light, odors, and taste. Due to their central role in regulating virtually every major physiological process—ranging from cardiovascular and pulmonary function to immune responses and neurotransmission—GPCRs are the targets for nearly one-third of all currently approved pharmaceutical drugs.

The fundamental paradigm of GPCR signal transduction involves the recognition of an extracellular ligand (agonist) by the receptor, which induces a conformational change that is transmitted across the plasma membrane to the intracellular surface. This active receptor conformation then binds and activates a heterotrimeric GTP-binding protein (G protein), composed of α, β, and γ subunits. Upon receptor binding, the G protein undergoes a critical nucleotide exchange process, releasing bound guanosine diphosphate (GDP) and binding guanosine triphosphate (GTP). This exchange triggers the dissociation of the G protein into active Gα-GTP and Gβγ subunits, which subsequently interact with various downstream effector enzymes and ion channels to propagate the cellular response.

The β2 adrenergic receptor (β2AR), which mediates physiological responses to adrenaline and noradrenaline, has long served as the quintessential model system for understanding GPCR biology and G protein activation. The β2AR specifically couples to the stimulatory G protein, Gs, leading to the activation of adenylyl cyclase and the production of the second messenger cyclic AMP (cAMP). Over the past several decades, extensive biochemical, biophysical, and mutagenesis studies on the β2AR-Gs system have provided a wealth of indirect information regarding the receptor regions critical for G protein coupling and the biochemical steps involved in nucleotide exchange. However, a complete, mechanistic understanding of transmembrane signaling requires a detailed, high-resolution three-dimensional structure of the activated receptor in complex with its cognate G protein.

Obtaining such a structure has been a monumental challenge in structural biology due to the inherent instability of the transient receptor-G protein complex, particularly when solubilized in detergents required for crystallization. The complex is highly dynamic, and the binding of nucleotides rapidly induces its dissociation. In this study, we have overcome these formidable technical barriers through a combination of innovative protein engineering, the use of a stabilizing camelid nanobody, and advanced lipidic cubic phase (LCP) crystallization techniques. Here, we present the high-resolution crystal structure of the active-state ternary complex composed of the agonist-bound human β2AR and the nucleotide-free Gs heterotrimer. This structure provides the first unprecedented, high-resolution view of how an agonist-bound GPCR engages and fundamentally alters the conformation of a G protein, revealing the structural basis for transmembrane signaling and G protein activation.

text: The β2AR-Gs complex crystal structure provides the first high-resolution view of transmembrane signalling for a GPCR. We now have a framework to design experiments to investigate the mechanism of complex formation, GTP binding and complex dissociation. Of particular interest will be studies designed to determine the functional significance of the large movement of GαsAH relative to GαsRas that is observed in the β2AR-Gs complex. A better understanding of the structural basis for G protein activation may provide new approaches for drug discovery. The high degree of structural homology within the ligand-binding pocket has posed challenges for developing highly selective drugs for specific GPCR targets. In contrast, there is relatively low homology at the interface between the β2AR and Gαs, so identifying sequence and structural features that define specificity for particular G proteins may enable the development of selective inhibitors of specific GPCR-G protein interactions.

title: Crystal Structure of the Beta2 Adrenergic Receptor-Gs Protein Complex
text:
The structure of the agonist-bound beta2AR-Gs complex provides a direct view of the conformational changes that couple ligand binding in a GPCR to nucleotide exchange in a heterotrimeric G protein. The outward displacement of TM6, extension of TM5, and rearrangement of ICL2 generate a receptor surface that engages primarily the Ras-like domain of Galphas, especially the amino- and carboxy-terminal helices that are linked to the nucleotide-binding pocket. These observations explain key features of the ternary complex, including enhanced agonist affinity and destabilization of GDP binding.

The large displacement of the Galphas alpha-helical domain probably reflects a dynamic nucleotide-free state, rather than a single obligatory conformation during signalling. Although T4 lysozyme and Nb35 were essential for crystallogenesis, the principal receptor-G protein interface is consistent with biochemical, mutational and biophysical data. This structure therefore establishes a framework for understanding GPCR activation, G protein specificity and the initial molecular events leading to GTP binding.

text: Our structural determination of the active-state β2AR-Gs ternary complex provides the first high-resolution visualization of transmembrane signaling by a G protein-coupled receptor. The structure reveals that agonist binding induces a 14 Å outward movement of TM6 and the extension of TM5, creating an intracellular cavity that docks the α-helical extension of the Gαs subunit. The lack of direct interaction between the receptor and Gβγ subunits emphasizes the primary role of Gαs in driving receptor coupling. This landmark structure not only illuminates the mechanics of the ternary complex model but also establishes a fundamental structural framework for understanding GPCR-mediated signal transduction across the plasma membrane.

paper_title: Cyclic GMP-AMP Synthase Is a Cytosolic DNA Sensor That Activates the Type I Interferon Pathway - 2013 - Science
display_title: Cyclic GMP-AMP Synthase Is a Cytosolic DNA Sensor That Activates the Type I Interferon Pathway
title: Cyclic GMP-AMP Synthase Is a Cytosolic DNA Sensor That Activates the Type I Interferon Pathway
text: The presence of DNA in the cytoplasm of mammalian cells is a danger signal that triggers host immune responses such as the production of type I interferons. Cytosolic DNA induces interferons through the production of cyclic guanosine monophosphate-adenosine monophosphate (cyclic GMP-AMP, or cGAMP), which binds to and activates the adaptor protein STING. Through biochemical fractionation and quantitative mass spectrometry, we identified a cGAMP synthase (cGAS), which belongs to the nucleotidyltransferase family. Overexpression of cGAS activated the transcription factor IRF3 and induced interferon-β in a STING-dependent manner. Knockdown of cGAS inhibited IRF3 activation and interferon-β induction by DNA transfection or DNA virus infection. cGAS bound to DNA in the cytoplasm and catalyzed cGAMP synthesis. These results indicate that cGAS is a cytosolic DNA sensor that induces interferons by producing the second messenger cGAMP.
historical_context: In 2013, Lijun Sun, Zhijian "James" Chen, and colleagues published this groundbreaking paper identifying cyclic GMP-AMP synthase (cGAS) as the elusive cytosolic DNA sensor. They illustrated that cGAS binds to misplaced DNA—originating from viruses, bacteria, or internal cellular damage—and catalyzes the synthesis of the unique second messenger cGAMP, which subsequently activates the STING pathway to launch robust immune responses. This discovery is widely regarded as one of the most consequential findings in modern innate immunity. It resolved the major biological enigma of how the immune system perceives pathogenic or self-DNA, fundamentally overhauling the understanding of autoimmune diseases (like lupus), antiviral immunity, and anti-tumor responses, establishing the cGAS-STING axis as a premier target for modern cancer immunotherapies.

text: DNA is a potent immune stimulant, but the mechanism by which cytosolic DNA activates type I interferons has remained unclear. We purified the cyclic GMP-AMP synthase activity from murine cytosolic extracts by combining conventional chromatography, DNA affinity purification, and quantitative mass spectrometry, and identified E330016A19, the mouse homolog of human C6orf150/MB21D1, as cGAS. Catalytic mutations abolished cGAMP production and interferon-beta induction. Depletion of cGAS in mouse L929 cells or human THP-1 cells impaired IRF3 activation, cGAMP synthesis, and interferon-beta induction by transfected DNA or DNA viruses, but not by an RNA virus. Thus, cGAS is a cytosolic DNA sensor that activates STING through the second messenger cGAMP.

title: Cyclic GMP-AMP Synthase Is a Cytosolic DNA Sensor That Activates the Type I Interferon Pathway
text:
We have developed a strategy that combines quantitative mass spectrometry with conventional protein purification to identify biologically active proteins partially purified from crude cell extracts. This strategy may be generally applicable to proteins that are difficult to purify to homogeneity because of very low abundance, labile activity, or scarce starting materials.

As a proof of principle, we used this strategy to identify the mouse protein E330016A19 as the enzyme that synthesizes cGAMP. This discovery led to the identification of a large family of cGAS that is conserved from fish to human, formally demonstrating that vertebrate animals contain evolutionarily conserved enzymes that synthesize cyclic dinucleotides, which were previously found only in bacteria, archaea, and protozoa. Vibrio cholerae can synthesize cGAMP through its cyclase DncV (VC0179), which contains an NTase domain but has no obvious primary sequence homology to the mammalian cGAS.

Our results not only demonstrate that cGAS is a cytosolic DNA sensor that triggers the type I interferon pathway, but also reveal a mechanism of immune signaling in which cGAS generates the second messenger cGAMP, which binds to and activates STING, thereby triggering type I interferon production. It remains to be determined whether STING evolved first to detect cyclic dinucleotides from bacteria, or to detect endogenous cGAMP in the host as a mechanism of responding to cytosolic DNA. Although STING can directly detect certain cyclic dinucleotides produced by some bacteria, the deployment of cGAS as a cytosolic DNA sensor greatly expands the repertoire of microorganisms detected by the host immune system. In principle, all microorganisms that can carry DNA into the host cytoplasm—such as DNA viruses, bacteria, parasites (e.g., malaria), and retroviruses (e.g., HIV)—could potentially trigger the cGAS-STING pathway. The enzymatic synthesis of cGAMP by cGAS provides a mechanism of signal amplification for a robust and sensitive immune response. However, the detection of self DNA in the host cytoplasm by cGAS could also lead to autoimmune diseases, such as systemic lupus erythematosus, Sjögren's syndrome, and Aicardi-Goutières syndrome.

Several other DNA sensors, such as DAI, IFI16, and DDX41, have been reported to induce type I interferons. Overexpression of DAI, IFI16, or DDX41 did not lead to the production of cGAMP. We also found that knockdown of DDX41 and p204 (a mouse homolog of IFI16) by siRNA did not inhibit the generation of cGAMP activity in HT-DNA-transfected L929 cells. Nonetheless, it is possible that distinct DNA sensors exist in different cell types. Unlike other pattern recognition receptors (e.g., TLRs), cGAS is a cyclase that is likely more amenable to inhibition by small-molecule compounds. These inhibitors may be developed into therapeutic agents for the treatment of human autoimmune diseases.
historical_context: In 2013, Lijun Sun, Zhijian "James" Chen, and colleagues published this groundbreaking paper identifying cyclic GMP-AMP synthase (cGAS) as the elusive cytosolic DNA sensor. They illustrated that cGAS binds to misplaced DNA—originating from viruses, bacteria, or internal cellular damage—and catalyzes the synthesis of the unique second messenger cGAMP, which subsequently activates the STING pathway to launch robust immune responses. This discovery is widely regarded as one of the most consequential findings in modern innate immunity. It resolved the major biological enigma of how the immune system perceives pathogenic or self-DNA, fundamentally overhauling the understanding of autoimmune diseases (like lupus), antiviral immunity, and anti-tumor responses, establishing the cGAS-STING axis as a premier target for modern cancer immunotherapies.

display_title: Cyclic GMP-AMP Synthase Is a Cytosolic DNA Sensor That Activates the Type I Interferon Pathway
title: Cyclic GMP-AMP Synthase Is a Cytosolic DNA Sensor That Activates the Type I Interferon Pathway
text:
Our results identify cGAS as a cytosolic DNA sensor that links the detection of foreign DNA to the activation of the type I interferon pathway. cGAS binds to DNA in the cytoplasm and catalyzes the synthesis of cGAMP from ATP and GTP. cGAMP then functions as a second messenger that activates STING, leading to IRF3 activation and induction of IFN-beta. Thus, the DNA-sensing pathway contains an enzyme that converts the presence of cytosolic DNA into a diffusible cyclic dinucleotide signal.

Several lines of evidence support the conclusion that cGAS is the enzyme responsible for cGAMP production in response to DNA. First, cGAS copurified with the cGAMP-synthesizing activity through three independent purification routes. Second, overexpression of cGAS, but not several other putative DNA sensors, led to robust cGAMP production and IFN-beta induction in a STING-dependent manner. Third, mutations of conserved residues in the nucleotidyltransferase domain abolished the ability of cGAS to activate IRF3. Finally, purified recombinant cGAS catalyzed the synthesis of cGAMP from ATP and GTP, and this reaction was stimulated by DNA but not RNA. These results demonstrate that cGAS is not merely an adaptor or regulatory factor, but a bona fide DNA-activated cyclase.

cGAS appears to have an essential role in the cellular response to cytosolic DNA. Knockdown of cGAS in mouse and human cells strongly inhibited IRF3 activation and IFN-beta induction by transfected DNA and by DNA viruses, including HSV-1, vaccinia virus, or both. In contrast, depletion of cGAS did not impair responses to Sendai virus, which activates the RNA-sensing pathway. Moreover, cGAS depletion markedly reduced the production of cGAMP induced by DNA transfection or DNA virus infection. These findings place cGAS upstream of STING and indicate that cGAS is a major source of cGAMP in the DNA-triggered interferon pathway.

The identification of cGAS helps explain how structurally diverse DNAs can stimulate a common innate immune response. cGAS was activated by several DNA species and bound to DNA through its N-terminal region, whereas the conserved NTase and Mab21 domains were required for catalytic activity. This organization suggests that DNA binding and cyclic dinucleotide synthesis are coupled within one protein. The relative lack of sequence conservation in portions of the N terminus may allow variation in DNA recognition or regulation among species, whereas the conserved catalytic domains maintain the core function of cGAMP synthesis.

The discovery of cGAS also raises several questions. It remains to be determined how DNA binding activates the catalytic domain of cGAS, how cGAS distinguishes cytosolic DNA from self DNA under physiological conditions, and how cGAS activity is regulated to prevent inappropriate interferon production. Because abnormal responses to self DNA are associated with inflammatory and autoimmune diseases, the cGAS-cGAMP-STING pathway may have broad relevance to host defense and immune pathology.

title: Cyclic GMP-AMP Synthase Is a Cytosolic DNA Sensor That Activates the Type I Interferon Pathway
text:
The mechanism by which the innate immune system detects cytosolic DNA to mount an antiviral response has been a central question in immunology. Our study resolves this longstanding enigma by identifying cyclic GMP-AMP synthase (cGAS) as the definitive cytosolic DNA sensor that activates the type I interferon pathway. Through rigorous biochemical fractionation and quantitative mass spectrometry, we isolated cGAS and demonstrated that it catalyzes the synthesis of the novel second messenger cyclic GMP-AMP (cGAMP) from ATP and GTP, strictly in a DNA-dependent manner. This cGAMP subsequently binds to and activates the adaptor protein STING, culminating in the phosphorylation of IRF3 and the robust induction of interferon-β.

Several lines of evidence unequivocally establish cGAS as the primary sensor for cytosolic DNA. First, overexpression of cGAS potently induces interferon-β in a STING-dependent manner, whereas mutations in its catalytic nucleotidyltransferase domain completely abolish this activity. Second, depletion of cGAS via multiple independent shRNAs severely compromises IRF3 activation and interferon-β production in response to both transfected DNA and DNA virus infection, such as herpes simplex virus 1 (HSV-1), in both mouse and human cell lines. Importantly, cGAS depletion does not affect the interferon response to RNA viruses, demonstrating its strict specificity for the DNA sensing pathway. Third, we show that cGAS directly binds DNA through its N-terminal region and colocalizes with cytosolic DNA in intact cells. Finally, purified recombinant cGAS is fully sufficient to synthesize cGAMP in vitro when stimulated by double-stranded DNA, confirming its direct catalytic role.

The discovery of cGAS provides a unifying mechanism for cytosolic DNA sensing. Unlike previously proposed DNA sensors—such as DAI, IFI16, or various DNA helicases, whose relative contributions have remained controversial and cell-type specific—cGAS appears to be universally required across multiple cell types for STING-dependent interferon induction by DNA. The generation of cGAMP represents a highly elegant biological solution: it amplifies the initial DNA binding event through the enzymatic production of a diffusible second messenger, ensuring a rapid and robust systemic antiviral response.

This breakthrough has profound implications beyond viral immunity. Cytosolic DNA is also a hallmark of various autoimmune diseases, such as Aicardi-Goutières syndrome and systemic lupus erythematosus, and plays a role in the immunogenicity of tumors and vaccine adjuvants. The identification of cGAS as the enzymatic generator of cGAMP opens up entirely new avenues for therapeutic intervention. Small-molecule inhibitors of cGAS could provide powerful treatments for severe autoimmune disorders driven by aberrant DNA sensing, while the cGAMP molecule itself, or its synthetic analogs, hold tremendous promise as potent adjuvants for vaccines and cancer immunotherapies.

paper_title: DNA Sequencing with Chain-Terminating Inhibitors - 1977 - PNAS
display_title: DNA Sequencing with Chain-Terminating Inhibitors
title: DNA Sequencing with Chain-Terminating Inhibitors
text: A new method for determining nucleotide sequences in DNA is described. It is similar to the "plus and minus" method but makes use of the 2',3'-dideoxy and arabinonucleoside analogues of the normal deoxynucleoside triphosphates, which act as specific chain-terminating inhibitors of DNA polymerase. The technique has been applied to the DNA of bacteriophage phiX174 and is more rapid and more accurate than either the plus or the minus method.
historical_context: In 1977, Frederick Sanger, Steve Nicklen, and Alan Coulson introduced the "Sanger sequencing" method, an elegant technique utilizing dideoxynucleotide chain-terminating inhibitors to accurately determine the exact nucleotide sequence of DNA. This highly reliable and scalable protocol revolutionized molecular biology, granting scientists the ability to read the underlying genetic code of any organism. This epoch-making paper earned Frederick Sanger his second Nobel Prize in Chemistry in 1980 (shared with Paul Berg and Walter Gilbert). The Nobel committee honored his contribution to determining base sequences in nucleic acids, a monumental breakthrough that catalyzed the genomics revolution, enabled the Human Genome Project, and forever altered the landscape of genetics, medicine, and evolutionary biology.

paper_title: DNA Sequencing with Chain-Terminating Inhibitors - 1977 - PNAS
display_title: DNA Sequencing with Chain-Terminating Inhibitors
title: DNA Sequencing with Chain-Terminating Inhibitors
text:
The "plus and minus" method is a relatively rapid and simple technique that has made possible the determination of the sequence of the genome of bacteriophage phiX174. It depends on the use of DNA polymerase to transcribe specific regions of the DNA under controlled conditions. Although the method is considerably more rapid and simple than other available techniques, neither the "plus" nor the "minus" method is completely accurate, and in order to establish a sequence both must be used together, and sometimes confirmatory data are necessary. W. M. Barnes has recently developed a third method, involving ribo-substitution, which has certain advantages over the plus and minus method, but this has not yet been extensively exploited.

Another rapid and simple method that depends on specific chemical degradation of the DNA has recently been described by Maxam and Gilbert, and this has also been used extensively for DNA sequencing. It has the advantage over the plus and minus method that it can be applied to double-stranded DNA, but it requires a strand separation or equivalent fractionation of each restriction enzyme fragment studied, which makes it somewhat more laborious.

This paper describes a further method using DNA polymerase, which makes use of inhibitors that terminate the newly synthesized chains at specific residues.

Principle of the Method. Atkinson et al. showed that the inhibitory activity of 2',3'-dideoxythymidine triphosphate (ddTTP) on DNA polymerase I depends on its being incorporated into the growing oligonucleotide chain in the place of thymidylic acid (dT). Because the ddT contains no 3'-hydroxyl group, the chain cannot be extended further, so that termination occurs specifically at positions where dT should be incorporated. If a primer and template are incubated with DNA polymerase in the presence of a mixture of ddTTP and dTTP, as well as the other three deoxyribonucleoside triphosphates (one of which is labeled with 32P), a mixture of fragments all having the same 5' and with ddT residues at the 3' ends is obtained. When this mixture is fractionated by electrophoresis on denaturing acrylamide gels the pattern of bands shows the distribution of dTs in the newly synthesized DNA. By using analogous terminators for the other nucleotides in separate incubations and running the samples in parallel on the gel, a pattern of bands is obtained from which the sequence can be read off as in the other rapid techniques mentioned above.

Two types of terminating triphosphates have been used—the dideoxy derivatives and the arabinonucleosides. Arabinose is a stereoisomer of ribose in which the 3'-hydroxyl group is oriented in trans position with respect to the 2'-hydroxyl group. The arabinosyl (ara) nucleotides act as chain terminating inhibitors of Escherichia coli DNA polymerase I in a manner comparable to ddT, although synthesized chains ending in 3' araC can be further extended by some mammalian DNA polymerases. In order to obtain a suitable pattern of bands from which an extensive sequence can be read it is necessary to have a ratio of terminating triphosphate to normal triphosphate such that only partial incorporation of the terminator occurs. For the dideoxy derivatives this ratio is about 100, and for the arabinosyl derivatives about 5000.

paper_title: DNA Sequencing with Chain-Terminating Inhibitors - 1977 - PNAS
display_title: DNA Sequencing with Chain-Terminating Inhibitors
title: DNA Sequencing with Chain-Terminating Inhibitors
text:
The determination of nucleotide sequences in DNA is an essential step in relating the chemical structure of genes to their biological function. Several methods have been described for obtaining such sequences, and the most useful of these at present is the “plus and minus” method. This procedure has been applied successfully to the DNA of bacteriophage phiX174 and to other small DNA molecules. However, the method involves a number of manipulations, including a preliminary synthesis step and subsequent separations, and the interpretation of the resulting patterns is sometimes made difficult by artefact bands and by uncertainty in the length of homopolymeric runs.

We have therefore sought a simpler procedure that would retain the advantages of enzymic copying from a defined primer, but would give more direct termination of the newly synthesized chains. The method described here is based on the use of nucleotide analogues that can be incorporated by DNA polymerase but that then prevent further extension of the chain. The most useful compounds for this purpose are the 2',3'-dideoxynucleoside triphosphates. Because these compounds lack the 3'-hydroxyl group required for formation of the next phosphodiester bond, their incorporation should terminate synthesis specifically at the corresponding nucleotide. Arabinonucleoside triphosphates may also act as terminators under some conditions.

In principle, four separate incubations are carried out with a primer-template complex, DNA polymerase, radioactive deoxynucleoside triphosphate, and one of the chain-terminating inhibitors. Each reaction gives a series of labelled DNA fragments ending at a particular base. The products are then separated by electrophoresis on denaturing acrylamide gels, and the sequence can be read from the pattern of bands. The procedure is closely related to the plus and minus method in that it uses a restriction fragment as primer and depends on gel fractionation of labelled extension products, but it differs in that termination is obtained directly during synthesis rather than by omission or limiting addition of particular nucleotides.

We have tested this inhibitor method mainly with the DNA of bacteriophage phiX174, for which a nearly complete provisional sequence is available. This DNA provides a useful substrate because many restriction fragments can be used as primers on either the viral or the complementary strand, and because discrepancies revealed by the new procedure can be compared with previous determinations. In some cases the primer fragment can be left attached to the newly synthesized DNA. In others it is necessary to remove it by restriction enzyme cleavage, or by introducing a single ribonucleotide that can subsequently be cleaved with ribonuclease.

The results show that sequences can usually be read for considerable distances from a priming site, and that the method gives patterns that are in many cases clearer than those obtained by the plus and minus procedure. The method also reveals errors in provisional regions of the phiX174 sequence. Although some difficulties remain, particularly where secondary structure causes compression of bands during electrophoresis, the use of chain-terminating inhibitors provides a rapid and accurate approach to DNA sequencing.

paper_title: DNA Sequencing with Chain-Terminating Inhibitors - 1977 - PNAS
display_title: DNA Sequencing with Chain-Terminating Inhibitors
title: DNA Sequencing with Chain-Terminating Inhibitors
text:
The determination of the exact sequence of nucleotides within a DNA molecule is of fundamental importance to virtually every branch of molecular biology. A precise understanding of genetic information is essential for elucidating the structure of genes, the mechanisms of gene expression and regulation, and the molecular basis of genetic diseases. While early efforts to sequence nucleic acids were slow and exceedingly complex, relying largely on the partial degradation of small RNA molecules, recent years have witnessed the development of more rapid and systematic techniques for determining the nucleotide sequence of DNA.

Our laboratory recently described a relatively rapid and simple approach to DNA sequencing, known as the "plus and minus" method. This technique relies on the use of DNA polymerase to extend a primer hybridized to a single-stranded DNA template. In the initial extension phase, the primer is elongated in the presence of all four deoxynucleoside triphosphates, one of which is radioactively labeled, under conditions that result in a heterogeneous mixture of newly synthesized chains of varying lengths. These chains are then subjected to two distinct types of second-stage polymerase reactions. In the "minus" system, the chains are extended further in the presence of only three of the four triphosphates, causing them to terminate specifically at the position just before the missing nucleotide. In the "plus" system, an exonuclease degrades the chains until it reaches the specific nucleotide corresponding to the single triphosphate provided in the reaction mixture. By separating the products of these eight distinct reactions side-by-side on high-resolution polyacrylamide gels, the sequence of the newly synthesized DNA can be deduced from the pattern of radioactive bands.

While the "plus and minus" method has been used successfully to determine the complete sequence of the bacteriophage phiX174 genome, it suffers from several notable limitations. The procedure is technically demanding, requiring two separate incubation steps and an intermediate purification on a Sephadex column. Furthermore, the results can sometimes be ambiguous, particularly when trying to determine the exact number of identical consecutive nucleotides in "runs," or when dealing with artifactual bands arising from non-specific termination or uneven extension.

In this report, we describe a new, alternative method for determining nucleotide sequences in DNA that represents a significant improvement over the "plus and minus" technique in terms of speed, simplicity, and accuracy. This new method is based on the use of specific chain-terminating inhibitors of DNA polymerase. Specifically, we employ 2',3'-dideoxynucleoside triphosphates (ddNTPs) and arabinonucleoside triphosphates as analogues of the normal deoxynucleoside triphosphates (dNTPs). Because these analogues lack a 3'-hydroxyl group, their incorporation into a growing DNA chain by DNA polymerase completely prevents the addition of any subsequent nucleotides, thereby leading to specific chain termination.

By carrying out four separate DNA polymerase reactions—each containing the template, primer, all four normal dNTPs (one of which is radioactive), and a small, carefully optimized amount of one of the four specific chain-terminating analogues—we can generate four distinct sets of DNA fragments. Within each reaction, the fragments will terminate exclusively at positions corresponding to the incorporated analogue. When these four sets of fragments are resolved by high-resolution gel electrophoresis and visualized by autoradiography, the nucleotide sequence of the DNA can be read directly and unambiguously from the resulting pattern of bands. This method avoids the intermediate purification steps of the plus and minus method and provides much clearer resolution of consecutive identical nucleotides. Here, we demonstrate the utility of this chain-terminating inhibitor method by applying it to the DNA of bacteriophage phiX174.

paper_title: DNA Sequencing with Chain-Terminating Inhibitors - 1977 - PNAS
display_title: DNA Sequencing with Chain-Terminating Inhibitors
title: DNA Sequencing with Chain-Terminating Inhibitors
text:
The method described here has a number of advantages over the plus and minus methods. First, it is simpler to perform because it requires no preliminary extension, thus avoiding one incubation and purification on a Sephadex column. It requires only the commercially available DNA polymerase I (Klenow fragment). The results appear to be more clear-cut with fewer artefact bands, and can usually be read further than with the plus and minus methods. Intermediate nucleotides in "runs" show up as bands, thus avoiding a source of error in the plus and minus method—estimating the number of nucleotides in a run. Theoretically one would expect the different bands in a run to be of the same strength, but this is not always the case. Frequently, the first nucleotide is the strongest, but in the case of ddCTP the second is the strongest. The reasons for these effects are not understood, but they do not usually cause difficulties with deducing the sequences. For the longer sequences in which the separate bands in a run are not resolved, experience has shown that it is frequently possible to estimate the number of nucleotides from the strength and width of the band.

The inhibitor method can also be used on a smaller scale than the plus and minus method because better incorporation from 32P-labeled triphosphates is obtained. This is presumably due to the longer incubation period used, which allows a more quantitative extension of primer chains.

In general, sequences of from 15 to about 200 nucleotides from the priming site can be determined with reasonable accuracy using a single primer. Frequently it is possible to read the gels further and, on occasions, a sequence of about 300 nucleotides from the priming site has been determined. Occasional artefacts are observed, but these can usually be readily identified. It seems likely that these are usually due to contaminants in the fragments. The most serious difficulties are due to "pile-ups" of bands, which are usually caused by the DNA forming base-paired loops under the conditions of the acrylamide gel electrophoresis. These pile-ups are seen as a number of bands in the same position or unusually close to one another on the electrophoresis. They generally occur at different positions when the priming is carried in opposite directions along the DNA over the same sequence. An example of this effect is seen at position 4330, where there is a single strong band in the G channel that in fact represents four G residues. They are presumably forming a stable loop by pairing with the four Cs at positions 4323–4326. Another example is at positions 3545–3550. This effect is likely to be found in all the rapid techniques that use gel electrophoresis.

It is felt that for an accurate determination of sequence one should not rely completely on single results obtained by this method alone but that confirmation should be obtained by some other technique or by priming on the opposite strand. This consideration probably applies to all other available methods also. The main disadvantage of the present method is the difficulty in obtaining all the inhibitors—particularly ddGTP, which is not commercially available.

paper_title: DNA Sequencing with Chain-Terminating Inhibitors - 1977 - PNAS
display_title: DNA Sequencing with Chain-Terminating Inhibitors
title: DNA Sequencing with Chain-Terminating Inhibitors
text:
The results described here indicate that chain-terminating analogues provide a useful alternative to the plus and minus procedures for determining DNA sequences. The main advantage is that the sequence is read from four reactions in which termination occurs at the corresponding base, rather than from complementary sets of plus and minus products. Thus most of the ambiguities inherent in the former method are avoided. In the examples shown, long stretches of the phiX174 sequence could be read directly from the gels and, where the sequence was already known, the agreement was close. In several regions the new results gave corrections to the provisional sequence, showing that the method is not merely confirmatory but is capable of detecting errors in independently derived data.

The simplicity of the procedure is also important. When a small restriction fragment is used as primer and no subsequent cleavage is required, the operations consist only of annealing, extension in the presence of the inhibitor, a chase with the limiting labeled nucleotide, and electrophoresis. Under these conditions the method is rapid and gives a clear pattern of bands over a considerable length of sequence. The useful reading distance is limited partly by the resolving power of the gel and partly by irregular migration of some fragments. These limitations are similar to those encountered in the other gel-reading methods, but the interpretation is in general less complicated because each band corresponds to termination at a particular residue.

The choice of inhibitor has a considerable effect on the quality of the result. The dideoxynucleoside triphosphates appear to be the most satisfactory reagents. They act as efficient chain terminators and, once incorporated, do not seem to be significantly removed or extended under the conditions normally used. The arabinonucleoside triphosphates can also be used, and araCTP gave useful results in reactions that were applied directly to gels. However, when a restriction enzyme digestion at 37 degrees was included, the C bands obtained with araCTP were less satisfactory. This may be due either to removal of the terminal arabinonucleotide by the exonuclease activity of the polymerase or to further extension of some terminated chains at the higher temperature. For this reason the dideoxy derivatives are preferable whenever subsequent incubation is necessary.

There remain some practical difficulties. If the primer must be removed by a restriction enzyme, a second site near the desired site can produce a superimposed pattern and make the gel unreadable. The single-site ribo-substitution procedure provides a way of overcoming this difficulty and is especially useful with fragments generated by enzymes such as Alu, where the restriction enzyme cannot be used conveniently for the final cleavage. This modification adds an extra purification step, but it extends the range of fragments that can be used as primers and preserves the advantages of the chain-termination reaction.

The method should be applicable to many DNA sequencing problems in which a suitable primer and template can be prepared. It is particularly well suited to the checking and extension of sequences obtained from restriction enzyme fragments, since different primers can be used to examine overlapping regions from either strand. The present work with phiX174 shows that sequences of approximately one hundred nucleotides can be obtained from a single priming site with sufficient accuracy to locate insertions and substitutions in a provisional sequence. With improved preparations of the dideoxynucleoside triphosphates and optimization of electrophoresis conditions, the length and reliability of the readings should be increased. The chain-termination principle therefore provides a rapid and accurate enzymic method for DNA sequencing and should complement, and in many cases replace, the plus and minus procedures.
historical_context: In 1977, Frederick Sanger, Steve Nicklen, and Alan Coulson introduced the "Sanger sequencing" method, an elegant technique utilizing dideoxynucleotide chain-terminating inhibitors to accurately determine the exact nucleotide sequence of DNA. This highly reliable and scalable protocol revolutionized molecular biology, granting scientists the ability to read the underlying genetic code of any organism. This epoch-making paper earned Frederick Sanger his second Nobel Prize in Chemistry in 1980 (shared with Paul Berg and Walter Gilbert). The Nobel committee honored his contribution to determining base sequences in nucleic acids, a monumental breakthrough that catalyzed the genomics revolution, enabled the Human Genome Project, and forever altered the landscape of genetics, medicine, and evolutionary biology.

paper_title: DNA Sequencing with Chain-Terminating Inhibitors - 1977 - PNAS
display_title: DNA Sequencing with Chain-Terminating Inhibitors
title: DNA Sequencing with Chain-Terminating Inhibitors
text:
The method described in this paper represents a fundamental advance in our ability to determine the primary structure of DNA. By employing 2',3'-dideoxynucleoside triphosphates (and arabinonucleoside analogues) as specific chain-terminating inhibitors of DNA polymerase I, we have developed a sequencing technique that is inherently more rapid, simpler, and more accurate than the previously established 'plus and minus' method. The underlying principle—that the incorporation of a nucleotide lacking a 3'-hydroxyl group irrevocably halts primer extension—allows for the generation of a complete nested set of radiolabeled oligonucleotide fragments terminating exclusively at specific bases. When resolved by high-resolution polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis, these fragments yield a clear banding pattern from which the nucleotide sequence can be directly and unequivocally read.

Our application of this technique to the genome of bacteriophage phiX174 has not only validated its efficacy but also allowed us to correct several errors and ambiguities present in the provisional sequence derived from older methods. For instance, the use of dideoxynucleotide terminators clearly resolved complex runs of nucleotides and identified missed insertions, such as the C-G-C trinucleotide at positions 4380–4381 and a missing cytosine at position 3547. Unlike the 'plus and minus' method, which often requires running both a plus and a minus system in parallel and sometimes yields ambiguous results requiring secondary confirmation, the chain-terminator method provides a definitive sequence read from a single set of four parallel reactions. Furthermore, by adjusting the ratio of terminating to normal triphosphates, the read length can be optimized, allowing stretches of 100 to over 120 nucleotides to be determined from a single priming site in a single experiment.

When using restriction enzyme fragments as primers, complications can arise if multiple adjacent restriction sites cause the primer to be cleaved unevenly after extension, resulting in overlapping sequence ladders. We have successfully circumvented this limitation by introducing the single-site ribo-substitution procedure. By substituting a single ribonucleotide at the priming site and subsequently cleaving it with ribonuclease or alkali, we ensure a uniform 5' end for all extended fragments prior to electrophoresis. This modification significantly enhances the versatility of the method, enabling the use of a wider variety of restriction fragments, including those generated by enzymes like Alu I, as primers.

While the chemical degradation method recently developed by Maxam and Gilbert also offers a rapid approach to DNA sequencing and is particularly advantageous for double-stranded DNA, the enzymatic chain-termination method described here requires less laborious sample preparation, as it bypasses the need for separating the strands of each restriction fragment. Instead, it relies simply on annealing a primer to a single-stranded template.

In conclusion, the dideoxy chain-termination method provides a highly efficient and accurate tool for DNA sequencing. We anticipate that this technique will greatly accelerate the sequencing of viral genomes, plasmids, and ultimately, more complex cellular DNA. As the availability and quality of the dideoxynucleotide analogues improve, and as gel electrophoresis techniques are further refined to resolve longer polynucleotide chains, we believe this approach will become the standard methodology for elucidating the genetic information encoded within DNA.

This epoch-making paper earned Frederick Sanger his second Nobel Prize in Chemistry in 1980 (shared with Paul Berg and Walter Gilbert). The Nobel committee honored his contribution to determining base sequences in nucleic acids, a monumental breakthrough that catalyzed the genomics revolution, enabled the Human Genome Project, and forever altered the landscape of genetics, medicine, and evolutionary biology.

paper_title: Efficacy and Safety of a Specific Inhibitor of the BCR-ABL Tyrosine Kinase in Chronic Myeloid Leukemia - 2001 - New England Journal of Medicine
display_title: Efficacy and Safety of a Specific Inhibitor of the BCR-ABL Tyrosine Kinase in Chronic Myeloid Leukemia
title: Efficacy and Safety of a Specific Inhibitor of the BCR-ABL Tyrosine Kinase in Chronic Myeloid Leukemia
text:
BCR-ABL is a constitutively activated tyrosine kinase that causes chronic myeloid leukemia (CML). Since tyrosine kinase activity is essential to the transforming function of BCR-ABL, an inhibitor of the kinase could be an effective treatment for CML.

We conducted a phase 1, dose-escalating trial of STI571 (formerly known as CGP 57148B), a specific inhibitor of the BCR-ABL tyrosine kinase. STI571 was administered orally to 83 patients with CML in the chronic phase in whom treatment with interferon alfa had failed. Patients were successively assigned to 1 of 14 doses ranging from 25 to 1000 mg per day.

Adverse effects of STI571 were minimal; the most common were nausea, myalgias, edema, and diarrhea. A maximal tolerated dose was not identified. Complete hematologic responses were observed in 53 of 54 patients treated with daily doses of 300 mg or more and typically occurred in the first four weeks of therapy. Of the 54 patients treated with doses of 300 mg or more, cytogenetic responses occurred in 29, including 17 (31 percent of the 54 patients who received this dose) with major responses (0 to 35 percent of cells in metaphase positive for the Philadelphia chromosome); 7 of these patients had complete cytogenetic remissions.

STI571 is well tolerated and has significant antileukemic activity in patients with CML in whom treatment with interferon alfa had failed. Our results provide evidence of the essential role of BCR-ABL tyrosine kinase activity in CML and demonstrate the potential for the development of anticancer drugs based on the specific molecular abnormality present in a human cancer.
historical_context: Published in 2001 by Brian Druker, Moshe Talpaz, Charles Sawyers, and colleagues, this landmark clinical trial reported the stunning success of imatinib (Gleevec) in combating chronic myeloid leukemia (CML). Imatinib was rationally engineered to specifically inhibit the BCR-ABL tyrosine kinase, the mutant fusion protein directly responsible for CML. The drug achieved unprecedented remission rates while generating minimal side effects compared to broad-spectrum chemotherapy. It radically altered modern oncology. It provided definitive proof of concept for targeted cancer therapy—disabling cancer by directly neutralizing its specific driving molecular defect—and turned CML from a fatal diagnosis into a manageable chronic condition, ushering in the modern era of kinase inhibitors and precision medicine.

title: Efficacy and Safety of a Specific Inhibitor of the BCR-ABL Tyrosine Kinase in Chronic Myeloid Leukemia
text:
Chronic myeloid leukemia (CML) is caused by the BCR-ABL fusion protein, a constitutively activated tyrosine kinase, and the kinase activity of BCR-ABL is required for leukemogenesis. We conducted a phase 1, dose-escalation study of STI571, an oral inhibitor of BCR-ABL, in patients with chronic-phase CML in whom therapy with interferon alfa had failed or could not be tolerated.

Eighty-three Philadelphia chromosome-positive patients were assigned to receive 25 to 1000 mg of STI571 daily. Safety and tolerability were the primary end points, and hematologic and cytogenetic responses, pharmacokinetics, and inhibition of BCR-ABL kinase activity in vivo were assessed.

STI571 was rapidly absorbed, and at a daily dose of 400 mg plasma trough concentrations exceeded those required to inhibit BCR-ABL phosphorylation in vitro. A maximal tolerated dose was not identified. The most common adverse effects were nausea, myalgias, edema, and diarrhea, which were usually mild or moderate; grade 3 neutropenia and thrombocytopenia occurred in patients receiving 200 mg or more. There were no deaths. Hematologic responses occurred in all patients receiving 140 mg or more daily. Among 54 patients treated with 300 mg or more, 53 had complete hematologic responses, generally within four weeks, and 51 remained in response at a median follow-up of 265 days. Cytogenetic responses occurred in 29 of these 54 patients (54 percent), including 17 major responses and 7 complete cytogenetic remissions. Inhibition of BCR-ABL kinase activity, measured by decreased phosphorylation of CRKL, increased with dose and was substantial at clinically active doses.

STI571 was well tolerated and induced rapid, durable hematologic responses and frequent cytogenetic responses in interferon-refractory CML. These findings support inhibition of BCR-ABL as a rational and effective therapeutic strategy.

title: Efficacy and Safety of a Specific Inhibitor of the BCR-ABL Tyrosine Kinase in Chronic Myeloid Leukemia
text:
Background. Chronic myeloid leukemia (CML) is caused by the BCR-ABL tyrosine kinase, the product of the Philadelphia chromosome. STI571 (formerly CGP 57148B) is a small molecule that was developed as a specific, competitive inhibitor of the ATP-binding site of the BCR-ABL tyrosine kinase. On the basis of its antileukemic activity in preclinical models, we conducted a phase 1 trial of STI571 in patients with CML in whom treatment with interferon alfa had failed.

Methods. We administered STI571 orally, once daily, to 83 patients with CML in the chronic phase in whom treatment with interferon alfa had failed. Fourteen dose cohorts received doses ranging from 25 to 1000 mg per day. The primary end point was the establishment of the safety and tolerability of STI571; antileukemic activity was a secondary end point. Pharmacokinetic studies and in vivo assessment of inhibition of the BCR-ABL tyrosine kinase were also performed.

Results. STI571 was well tolerated, and a maximal tolerated dose was not identified. The most common adverse effects were nausea, myalgias, edema, and diarrhea; most were mild. Complete hematologic responses occurred in 53 of 54 patients treated with daily doses of 300 mg or more, and these responses typically began within four weeks after the initiation of therapy. Of the 54 patients receiving 300 mg or more, 29 (54 percent) had cytogenetic responses, with major responses (35 percent or less of cells in metaphase positive for the Ph chromosome) in 17 patients (31 percent), including 7 patients (13 percent) with complete cytogenetic remissions. Inhibition of the BCR-ABL tyrosine kinase in vivo, as measured by decreased phosphorylation of CRKL, was observed at doses of 140 mg or more.

Conclusions. STI571, an inhibitor of the BCR-ABL tyrosine kinase, is well tolerated and has substantial antileukemic activity in patients with CML in whom treatment with interferon alfa has failed.

title: Efficacy and Safety of a Specific Inhibitor of the BCR-ABL Tyrosine Kinase in Chronic Myeloid Leukemia
text: Chronic myeloid leukemia (CML) is driven by the BCR-ABL fusion protein, a constitutively active tyrosine kinase generated by the Philadelphia chromosome translocation. STI571 is a specific, rationally designed inhibitor of the BCR-ABL tyrosine kinase. In this phase 1, dose-escalation trial, we evaluated the safety, tolerability, and antileukemic activity of oral STI571 in 83 patients with chronic-phase CML who had failed previous interferon alfa therapy. Patients received daily doses of STI571 ranging from 25 to 1000 mg. The drug was well tolerated, with mild to moderate adverse effects, predominantly nausea, myalgias, edema, and diarrhea; no maximum tolerated dose was identified. Pharmacokinetic analysis indicated a half-life of 13 to 16 hours, suitable for once-daily dosing. Remarkably, complete hematologic responses were achieved in 53 of 54 patients treated with daily doses of 300 mg or more, typically within four weeks of initiating therapy. Furthermore, 29 of these 54 patients (54 percent) exhibited cytogenetic responses, including 17 (31 percent) with major cytogenetic responses and 7 (13 percent) who achieved complete cytogenetic remissions. Biochemical analysis of patient leukocytes confirmed the in vivo inhibition of BCR-ABL kinase activity by STI571, as evidenced by reduced phosphorylation of the CRKL substrate. These results demonstrate that STI571 is highly active and well tolerated in patients with CML, establishing the clinical viability of targeting a specific molecular abnormality with a selective kinase inhibitor. This study provides strong justification for further clinical trials of STI571 in CML and validates the paradigm of molecularly targeted cancer therapy.

title: Efficacy and Safety of a Specific Inhibitor of the BCR-ABL Tyrosine Kinase in Chronic Myeloid Leukemia
text:
Chronic myeloid leukemia (CML) is a clonal disorder in which cells of the myeloid lineage undergo massive clonal expansion. The disease progresses through three distinct phases - chronic phase, accelerated phase, and blast crisis - during which the leukemic clone progressively loses its ability to differentiate. Current therapies include allogeneic bone marrow transplantation and drug regimens including interferon alfa. Interferon alfa prolongs overall survival but has considerable adverse effects. Allogeneic bone marrow transplantation, the only curative treatment for CML, is associated with substantial morbidity and mortality and is limited to patients for whom a suitable donor is available.

The characteristic genetic abnormality of CML, the Philadelphia (Ph) chromosome, results from a reciprocal translocation between the long arms of chromosomes 9 and 22. The molecular consequence of this translocation is the generation of the fusion protein BCR-ABL, a constitutively activated tyrosine kinase, which is present in virtually all patients with CML. In vitro studies and studies in animal models have established that BCR-ABL alone is sufficient to cause CML, and mutational analysis has established that the tyrosine kinase activity of the protein is required for its oncogenic activity. For these reasons, an inhibitor of the BCR-ABL tyrosine kinase should be an effective and selective treatment for CML.

STI571 (4-[(4-methyl-1-piperazinyl)methyl]-N-[4-methyl-3-[[4-(3-pyridinyl)-2-pyrimidinyl]amino]phenyl]benzamide methanesulfonate; Glivec, Novartis, Basel, Switzerland) was synthesized after a compound was identified by in vitro screening for tyrosine kinase inhibitors and its activity was optimized for specific kinases. STI571 functions through competitive inhibition at the ATP-binding site of the enzyme, which leads to the inhibition of tyrosine phosphorylation of proteins involved in BCR-ABL signal transduction. It shows a high degree of specificity for BCR-ABL, the receptor for platelet-derived growth factor, and c-kit tyrosine kinases. STI571 causes arrest of growth or apoptosis in hematopoietic cells that express BCR-ABL but does not affect normal cells. On the basis of its antileukemic activity in preclinical models, we conducted a phase 1 trial of STI571 in patients with CML in whom treatment with interferon alfa had failed.

Imatinib was rationally engineered to specifically inhibit the BCR-ABL tyrosine kinase, the mutant fusion protein directly responsible for CML. The drug achieved unprecedented remission rates while generating minimal side effects compared to broad-spectrum chemotherapy. It radically altered modern oncology. It provided definitive proof of concept for targeted cancer therapy—disabling cancer by directly neutralizing its specific driving molecular defect—and turned CML from a fatal diagnosis into a manageable chronic condition, ushering in the modern era of kinase inhibitors and precision medicine.

title: Efficacy and Safety of a Specific Inhibitor of the BCR-ABL Tyrosine Kinase in Chronic Myeloid Leukemia
text:
Chronic myeloid leukemia is a clonal myeloproliferative disorder characterized by expansion of myeloid cells and by the presence, in nearly all affected patients, of the Philadelphia chromosome. This abnormal chromosome results from a reciprocal translocation between chromosomes 9 and 22 and gives rise to the BCR-ABL fusion gene. The BCR-ABL protein has constitutively increased tyrosine kinase activity and transforms hematopoietic cells in experimental systems. Inhibition of this kinase therefore represents a rational strategy for the treatment of chronic myeloid leukemia.

Current therapy for chronic-phase chronic myeloid leukemia includes hydroxyurea, busulfan, interferon alfa, cytarabine, and allogeneic stem-cell transplantation. Interferon alfa can induce hematologic and cytogenetic responses and has been associated with prolongation of survival, but many patients do not have a cytogenetic response, lose their response, or cannot tolerate treatment. Allogeneic transplantation may be curative, but its use is limited by age, donor availability, and treatment-related morbidity and mortality. Thus, many patients require additional therapy after standard treatment has failed.

STI571, formerly CGP 57148B, is a 2-phenylaminopyrimidine derivative that selectively inhibits the ABL tyrosine kinase as well as the related platelet-derived growth factor receptor and c-kit kinases. In preclinical studies, STI571 inhibited the tyrosine kinase activity of BCR-ABL, blocked proliferation of BCR-ABL-positive cell lines, and suppressed growth of BCR-ABL-positive cells obtained from patients with chronic myeloid leukemia. The compound did not substantially inhibit the growth of normal hematopoietic progenitors at concentrations active against BCR-ABL-positive cells, suggesting that selective inhibition of the molecular lesion in chronic myeloid leukemia might be therapeutically feasible.

We conducted a phase 1, dose-escalation study of orally administered STI571 in patients with chronic-phase chronic myeloid leukemia in whom treatment with interferon alfa had failed or could not be tolerated. The primary objective was to determine the safety and tolerability of STI571; secondary objectives included assessment of hematologic and cytogenetic responses, pharmacokinetics, and inhibition of BCR-ABL kinase activity in vivo.

Imatinib was rationally engineered to specifically inhibit the BCR-ABL tyrosine kinase, the mutant fusion protein directly responsible for CML. The drug achieved unprecedented remission rates while generating minimal side effects compared to broad-spectrum chemotherapy. It radically altered modern oncology. It provided definitive proof of concept for targeted cancer therapy—disabling cancer by directly neutralizing its specific driving molecular defect—and turned CML from a fatal diagnosis into a manageable chronic condition, ushering in the modern era of kinase inhibitors and precision medicine.

display_title: Efficacy and Safety of a Specific Inhibitor of the BCR-ABL Tyrosine Kinase in Chronic Myeloid Leukemia
title: Efficacy and Safety of a Specific Inhibitor of the BCR-ABL Tyrosine Kinase in Chronic Myeloid Leukemia
text:
Chronic myeloid leukemia (CML) is a clonal myeloproliferative disorder of the hematopoietic stem cell that is characterized in its initial chronic phase by the massive overproduction of mature and maturing granulocytes. If left untreated, the disease inevitably progresses from the relatively indolent chronic phase to an accelerated phase and ultimately to a rapidly fatal acute leukemia known as the blast crisis. The cytogenetic hallmark of CML is the Philadelphia (Ph) chromosome, which is present in the leukemic cells of more than 90 percent of patients. The Ph chromosome is formed by a reciprocal translocation between the long arms of chromosomes 9 and 22, resulting in the fusion of the breakpoint cluster region (BCR) gene on chromosome 22 with the Abelson murine leukemia (ABL) gene on chromosome 9.

The resulting chimeric BCR-ABL gene encodes a constitutively activated, cytoplasmic tyrosine kinase. Extensive experimental evidence from both in vitro and in vivo models has firmly established that the continuous, deregulated tyrosine kinase activity of the BCR-ABL fusion protein is not merely a marker of CML, but is the essential, driving pathogenetic event that causes the transformation of normal hematopoietic stem cells into leukemic cells. Because BCR-ABL is uniquely expressed in leukemic cells and its kinase activity is strictly required for the maintenance of the malignant phenotype, it represents an ideal and highly specific molecular target for therapeutic intervention.

STI571 (formerly CGP 57148B) is a rationally designed, orally bioavailable 2-phenylaminopyrimidine derivative that functions as a potent and highly specific competitive inhibitor of the ATP-binding site of the BCR-ABL tyrosine kinase. In preclinical studies, STI571 was shown to effectively suppress the proliferation and induce the apoptosis of BCR-ABL-positive CML cell lines, as well as fresh leukemic cells isolated from patients with CML, while largely sparing normal hematopoietic progenitor cells.

Based on these compelling preclinical findings, we conducted a phase 1, dose-escalating clinical trial of STI571 in patients with chronic-phase CML who had previously failed to respond to, or were unable to tolerate, standard therapy with interferon alfa. The primary objective of this study was to determine the safety, tolerability, and maximum tolerated dose of continuous oral daily administration of STI571. The secondary objectives included the evaluation of the drug's pharmacokinetic profile and its potential antileukemic activity, as assessed by hematologic and cytogenetic responses.

Imatinib was rationally engineered to specifically inhibit the BCR-ABL tyrosine kinase, the mutant fusion protein directly responsible for CML. The drug achieved unprecedented remission rates while generating minimal side effects compared to broad-spectrum chemotherapy. It radically altered modern oncology. It provided definitive proof of concept for targeted cancer therapy—disabling cancer by directly neutralizing its specific driving molecular defect—and turned CML from a fatal diagnosis into a manageable chronic condition, ushering in the modern era of kinase inhibitors and precision medicine.

paper_title: Efficacy and Safety of a Specific Inhibitor of the BCR-ABL Tyrosine Kinase in Chronic Myeloid Leukemia - 2001 - New England Journal of Medicine
display_title: Efficacy and Safety of a Specific Inhibitor of the BCR-ABL Tyrosine Kinase in Chronic Myeloid Leukemia
title: Efficacy and Safety of a Specific Inhibitor of the BCR-ABL Tyrosine Kinase in Chronic Myeloid Leukemia
text:
The presence of the BCR-ABL fusion protein in virtually all patients with CML and its required tyrosine kinase activity make CML ideal for testing a specific inhibitor of this enzyme. In our phase 1 study, STI571, an oral, specific inhibitor of the BCR-ABL tyrosine kinase, was well tolerated and had substantial activity against CML. These results were obtained in patients with late-stage disease, in all of whom standard therapy with interferon alfa had failed.

The rate of complete hematologic responses increased as the daily dose increased from 85 mg to 250 mg and reached 98 percent in patients treated with 300 mg or more of STI571. Complete hematologic responses typically occurred within four weeks after the initiation of therapy. The exception was a patient in the 350-mg group in whom the plasma half-life of STI571 was short (seven hours) and the AUC was similar to that for patients in the 85-mg group. STI571 is metabolized primarily by the CYP3A4 enzyme, and the patient in the 350-mg group was being treated with phenytoin, a known inducer of CYP3A4. When treatment with phenytoin was discontinued and the dose of STI571 was increased to 500 mg, the patient had a complete hematologic response associated with trough levels of STI571 similar to those observed in other patients in the 500-mg group. This observation suggests that drugs that induce CYP3A4 could lead to low and ineffective levels of STI571, whereas drugs that interfere with the activity of the CYP3A4 enzyme could decrease the metabolism of STI571, leading to increased levels of STI571 and thereby causing toxic effects.

During treatment with STI571, blood counts gradually returned to normal during the first month, suggesting that the drug does not rapidly induce apoptosis, as would be expected with standard chemotherapy. Blood counts were maintained within normal limits regardless of whether a cytogenetic response was observed. This suggests that inhibition of the BCR-ABL tyrosine kinase restores normal regulatory behavior to the leukemic clone. Cytogenetic responses might follow if, over time, the leukemic clone was displaced either by normal hematopoietic progenitors that had regained a proliferative advantage or by differentiation of the leukemic stem cell, which leads to its elimination.

Of the 54 patients treated with at least 300 mg of STI571 per day, 29 (54 percent) had cytogenetic responses, including 7 with complete cytogenetic remissions. As compared with the cytogenetic responses during therapy with interferon alfa, those during treatment with STI571 occurred relatively rapidly. In one patient, BCR-ABL mRNA could not be detected by polymerase chain reaction.

The most frequent adverse effects that seemed to be related to treatment with STI571 were nausea, edema, myalgias, and diarrhea; overall, most were mild. In seven patients, there were elevations of liver-enzyme levels of grade 2 or higher, as was also seen in our study of patients with acute leukemia, reported elsewhere in this issue of the Journal. Myelosuppression, which occurred in up to a quarter of the patients, was not dose limiting and was managed by temporary interruption of treatment or dose reduction. Myelosuppression may be either a consequence of a pharmacologic effect of STI571 through inhibition of c-kit or a reflection of compromised underlying normal hematopoiesis in patients with leukemia.

We did not identify a maximal tolerated dose for STI571, but other end points could be used to choose a dose for future trials. One is the pharmacokinetic profile of STI571. Levels of the drug that killed CML cells in vitro correlated well with clinical response and serum drug levels in the 400-mg group. The dose-response curve clearly demonstrates a relation between dose and hematologic response. Also, there is substantial in vivo inhibition of the enzymatic activity of BCR-ABL at the 400-mg dose, as demonstrated by decreased phosphorylation of CRKL, a substrate of BCR-ABL. For these reasons, we recommend a daily dose of at least 400 mg for future studies.

These results show that the BCR-ABL tyrosine kinase is critical to the development of CML and demonstrate the potential for the development of anticancer drugs based on the specific molecular abnormality in a human cancer.

Imatinib was rationally engineered to specifically inhibit the BCR-ABL tyrosine kinase, the mutant fusion protein directly responsible for CML. The drug achieved unprecedented remission rates while generating minimal side effects compared to broad-spectrum chemotherapy. It radically altered modern oncology. It provided definitive proof of concept for targeted cancer therapy—disabling cancer by directly neutralizing its specific driving molecular defect—and turned CML from a fatal diagnosis into a manageable chronic condition, ushering in the modern era of kinase inhibitors and precision medicine.

display_title: Efficacy and Safety of a Specific Inhibitor of the BCR-ABL Tyrosine Kinase in Chronic Myeloid Leukemia
title: Efficacy and Safety of a Specific Inhibitor of the BCR-ABL Tyrosine Kinase in Chronic Myeloid Leukemia
text:
In this phase 1 study, STI571, an oral inhibitor of the BCR-ABL tyrosine kinase, was well tolerated and had substantial antileukemic activity in patients with chronic-phase CML in whom treatment with interferon alfa had failed or who were unable to tolerate interferon alfa. Although the primary objective of the study was to define safety and tolerability, a maximal tolerated dose was not reached, and the clinical responses observed at doses of 300 mg or more per day were striking. Nearly all patients treated at these doses had complete hematologic responses, usually within the first month of therapy, and more than half had cytogenetic responses during follow-up.

The rapidity and frequency of the hematologic responses are notable because the study population consisted of patients whose disease had not been adequately controlled by standard medical therapy. In most patients, leukocytosis and thrombocytosis resolved without the use of other cytoreductive agents. The persistence of complete hematologic responses in the majority of patients, with a median follow-up of approximately nine months among those receiving 300 mg or more per day, suggests that inhibition of BCR-ABL can produce sustained control of the proliferative component of chronic-phase CML. Two patients had progression or relapse, indicating that suppression of kinase activity may not be sufficient in all cases and that resistant subclones or more advanced biologic features may already have been present in some patients.

The cytogenetic responses provide further evidence that STI571 acts on the leukemic clone rather than merely reducing the peripheral white-cell count. Major cytogenetic responses occurred in 31 percent of patients treated with 300 mg or more per day, and complete cytogenetic remissions occurred in seven patients. In some of these patients, BCR-ABL was no longer detected by fluorescence in situ hybridization or by polymerase-chain-reaction analysis. These findings compare favorably with the expected results in patients in whom interferon alfa has failed, although direct comparisons cannot be made in the absence of a randomized control group. The time course of cytogenetic improvement, which occurred over several months, is consistent with progressive replacement of Ph-positive hematopoiesis by Ph-negative cells after selective inhibition of the leukemic clone.

The pharmacokinetic and biologic data support the choice of daily doses of at least 300 mg. Plasma concentrations achieved with 400 mg once daily exceeded concentrations required in vitro to inhibit BCR-ABL-dependent phosphorylation and to kill BCR-ABL-positive cells. In addition, analysis of CRKL phosphorylation in peripheral-blood cells showed dose-dependent inhibition of BCR-ABL kinase activity, with prominent inhibition at doses associated with clinical responses. Thus, the clinical activity of STI571 is linked to inhibition of the molecular abnormality that is central to the pathogenesis of CML.

Treatment was associated mainly with mild or moderate adverse effects, including nausea, myalgias, edema, and diarrhea. Hematologic toxic effects were observed, particularly at doses of 200 mg or more, but these effects were generally manageable and may reflect, in part, suppression of Ph-positive hematopoiesis in patients whose marrow was heavily replaced by leukemic cells. Hepatic enzyme abnormalities and rash required interruption or discontinuation of therapy in a small number of patients. The absence of severe nonhematologic toxicity at the highest doses tested is consistent with the relative specificity of STI571 for a limited group of tyrosine kinases and distinguishes this approach from conventional cytotoxic therapy.

Several limitations of this study should be emphasized. The trial was not randomized, the number of patients in each dose cohort was small, and the data represent an interim analysis of an ongoing study. Longer follow-up is required to determine the durability of hematologic and cytogenetic responses and whether treatment with STI571 delays progression to accelerated-phase disease or blast crisis. It will also be important to define mechanisms of resistance, to determine whether molecular remissions can be achieved more frequently or sustained, and to compare STI571 directly with established therapies.

Despite these limitations, the results show that a drug designed to inhibit a specific molecular lesion in cancer can produce substantial clinical benefit with limited toxicity. CML is uniquely suited to this approach because BCR-ABL is present in nearly all patients and is required for leukemogenesis. The activity of STI571 in this study provides clinical confirmation of the essential role of BCR-ABL kinase activity in CML and supports further evaluation of this agent as therapy for chronic-phase disease, including its use earlier in the course of illness and in combination or sequence with other treatments.
historical_context: Published in 2001 by Brian Druker, Moshe Talpaz, Charles Sawyers, and colleagues, this landmark clinical trial reported the stunning success of imatinib (Gleevec) in combating chronic myeloid leukemia (CML). Imatinib was rationally engineered to specifically inhibit the BCR-ABL tyrosine kinase, the mutant fusion protein directly responsible for CML. The drug achieved unprecedented remission rates while generating minimal side effects compared to broad-spectrum chemotherapy. It radically altered modern oncology. It provided definitive proof of concept for targeted cancer therapy—disabling cancer by directly neutralizing its specific driving molecular defect—and turned CML from a fatal diagnosis into a manageable chronic condition, ushering in the modern era of kinase inhibitors and precision medicine.

paper_title: Efficacy and Safety of a Specific Inhibitor of the BCR-ABL Tyrosine Kinase in Chronic Myeloid Leukemia - 2001 - New England Journal of Medicine
display_title: Efficacy and Safety of a Specific Inhibitor of the BCR-ABL Tyrosine Kinase in Chronic Myeloid Leukemia
title: Efficacy and Safety of a Specific Inhibitor of the BCR-ABL Tyrosine Kinase in Chronic Myeloid Leukemia
text:
The results of this phase 1 clinical trial provide compelling evidence that STI571, a specific inhibitor of the BCR-ABL tyrosine kinase, is an exceptionally effective and well-tolerated therapy for patients with chronic-phase chronic myeloid leukemia (CML) who have failed prior treatment with interferon alfa. The demonstration that a targeted small-molecule kinase inhibitor can induce such profound hematologic and cytogenetic responses validates the central hypothesis of targeted cancer therapy: that drugs designed specifically against the molecular abnormality driving a malignancy can yield dramatic clinical benefits with minimal collateral toxicity.

The clinical activity of STI571 in this heavily pretreated patient population is unprecedented. Complete hematologic responses were achieved in 98 percent (53 of 54) of patients treated with daily doses of 300 mg or more. These responses were not only universal at therapeutic doses but also rapid, typically occurring within the first four weeks of therapy. Even more striking is the rate of cytogenetic responses. In this phase 1 study, 54 percent of patients receiving 300 mg or more achieved a cytogenetic response, with 31 percent achieving a major cytogenetic response and 13 percent achieving complete cytogenetic remission. Historically, achieving any major cytogenetic response in patients resistant to interferon alfa has been exceedingly rare with conventional chemotherapy. The depth of these responses is further highlighted by the fact that some patients who achieved complete cytogenetic remission also became negative for BCR-ABL by FISH and PCR.

Equally remarkable to its efficacy is the safety profile of STI571. In stark contrast to standard cytotoxic chemotherapy and interferon alfa, which are associated with severe and often dose-limiting systemic toxicities, STI571 was extraordinarily well tolerated. The most common adverse events—nausea, myalgias, edema, and diarrhea—were predominantly mild to moderate (grade 1 or 2) and rarely necessitated discontinuation of the drug. Importantly, a maximal tolerated dose was not identified within the tested range of 25 to 1000 mg per day. The dose-limiting toxicities typically seen with broad-spectrum antineoplastic agents, such as severe mucositis, alopecia, or severe myelosuppression, were notably absent. While mild to moderate anemia and cytopenias were observed, they were generally manageable and often transient, possibly reflecting the clearance of the leukemic clone prior to the recovery of normal Philadelphia chromosome-negative hematopoiesis.

The pharmacokinetic and pharmacodynamic data confirm the mechanism of action of STI571. The drug achieved therapeutic plasma concentrations at once-daily doses of 300 mg and above, which correlated precisely with the onset of profound clinical responses. Furthermore, our assays of BCR-ABL kinase activity, utilizing the phosphorylation status of the surrogate marker CRKL, demonstrated that the doses required to achieve hematologic responses correspond directly to the doses required to inhibit BCR-ABL kinase activity in vivo. This tight correlation provides definitive proof that the antileukemic effect of STI571 is a direct result of its intended molecular target inhibition.

The success of STI571 represents a paradigm shift in oncology. For decades, cancer drug development has largely relied on the empirical screening of compounds for general cytotoxic activity, leading to therapies with narrow therapeutic indices. The development of STI571, from the initial understanding of the molecular pathogenesis of CML—the Philadelphia chromosome and the resultant constitutively active BCR-ABL kinase—to the rational design and clinical testing of a specific inhibitor, serves as the ultimate proof-of-concept for molecularly targeted therapy.

While the short-term results are highly encouraging, several questions remain to be answered in subsequent trials. The durability of these responses and the potential for the emergence of clinical resistance through secondary mutations in the BCR-ABL kinase domain require long-term follow-up. Additionally, the efficacy of STI571 in the more advanced, genetically complex accelerated and blast phases of CML needs to be determined. Future studies will also need to evaluate the role of STI571 as a frontline therapy for newly diagnosed CML and explore its potential in combination with other agents, including interferon or conventional chemotherapy.

In summary, STI571 is a highly active and remarkably safe oral agent for the treatment of chronic-phase CML. By specifically targeting the fundamental molecular lesion responsible for the disease, STI571 not only provides a highly effective therapeutic option for patients with interferon-resistant CML but also ushers in a new era of rationally designed, targeted cancer therapeutics.
