Imatinib was rationally engineered to specifically inhibit the BCR-ABL tyrosine kinase, the mutant fusion protein directly responsible for CML. The drug achieved unprecedented remission rates while generating minimal side effects compared to broad-spectrum chemotherapy. It radically altered modern oncology. It provided definitive proof of concept for targeted cancer therapy—disabling cancer by directly neutralizing its specific driving molecular defect—and turned CML from a fatal diagnosis into a manageable chronic condition, ushering in the modern era of kinase inhibitors and precision medicine.

paper_title: Embryonic Stem Cell Lines Derived from Human Blastocysts - 1998 - Science
display_title: Embryonic Stem Cell Lines Derived from Human Blastocysts
title: Embryonic Stem Cell Lines Derived from Human Blastocysts
text: Human blastocyst-derived, pluripotent cell lines are described that have normal karyotypes, express high levels of telomerase activity, and express cell surface markers that characterize primate embryonic stem cells but do not characterize other early lineages. After undifferentiated proliferation in vitro for 4 to 5 months, these cells still maintained the developmental potential to form trophoblast and derivatives of all three embryonic germ layers, including gut epithelium (endoderm); cartilage, bone, smooth muscle, and striated muscle (mesoderm); and neural epithelium, embryonic ganglia, and stratified squamous epithelium (ectoderm). These cell lines should be useful in human developmental biology, drug discovery, and transplantation medicine.
historical_context: In 1998, James Thomson and colleagues achieved a historic breakthrough by deriving the first stable human embryonic stem cell (hESC) lines from human blastocysts. They proved that these cells could be sustained in an undifferentiated state indefinitely while harboring the pluripotent capacity to develop into derivatives of all three embryonic germ layers. This discovery redefined biology and medicine. It birthed the entire field of regenerative medicine, arming researchers with an unprecedented tool to investigate human developmental biology, model intricate genetic diseases *in vitro*, and chart pathways for cell-replacement therapies targeting conditions like Parkinson's disease, spinal cord injuries, and diabetes.

title: Embryonic Stem Cell Lines Derived from Human Blastocysts
text: Human embryonic stem (ES) cell lines were derived from donated in vitro fertilized blastocysts and maintained in an undifferentiated state after prolonged culture. Five lines, each from a separate embryo, showed characteristic primate ES cell morphology, normal XX or XY karyotypes, high telomerase activity, and expression of SSEA-3, SSEA-4, TRA-1-60, TRA-1-81, and alkaline phosphatase. The cells could be cryopreserved and expanded without evidence of replicative crisis. After injection into immunodeficient mice, all lines formed teratomas containing derivatives of endoderm, mesoderm, and ectoderm. These human ES cells provide a renewable model for human developmental biology and a potential source of differentiated cells for drug discovery and transplantation.

title: Embryonic Stem Cell Lines Derived from Human Blastocysts
text: Embryonic stem (ES) cells are derived from totipotent cells of the early mammalian embryo and are capable of unlimited, undifferentiated proliferation in vitro. In chimeras with intact embryos, mouse ES cells contribute to a wide range of adult tissues, including germ cells, providing a powerful approach for introducing specific genetic changes into the mouse germ line. The term 'ES cell' was introduced to distinguish these embryo-derived pluripotent cells from teratocarcinoma-derived pluripotent embryonal carcinoma (EC) cells. Given the historical introduction of the term 'ES cell' and the properties of mouse ES cells, we proposed that the essential characteristics of primate ES cells should include (i) derivation from the preimplantation or periimplantation embryo, (ii) prolonged undifferentiated proliferation, and (iii) stable developmental potential to form derivatives of all three embryonic germ layers even after prolonged culture. For ethical and practical reasons, in many primate species, including humans, the ability of ES cells to contribute to the germ line in chimeras is not a testable property. Nonhuman primate ES cell lines provide an accurate in vitro model for understanding the differentiation of human tissues. We now describe human cell lines that fulfill our proposed criteria to define primate ES cells.

title: Embryonic Stem Cell Lines Derived from Human Blastocysts
text: Embryonic stem (ES) cells are pluripotent cell lines derived from the inner cell mass of the preimplantation embryo. Mouse ES cells can be maintained indefinitely in an undifferentiated state and, when returned to the embryo, can contribute to all tissues of the adult, including the germ line. These properties have made mouse ES cells central to experimental mammalian genetics and to the analysis of early developmental decisions. However, early development differs substantially among mammals, and the extrapolation from mouse to human development is necessarily incomplete. Primate ES cell lines, recently derived from rhesus monkey and common marmoset blastocysts, resemble human embryonal carcinoma cells in morphology and marker expression, but retain a normal karyotype and a broader developmental potential. The derivation of analogous cell lines from human blastocysts would provide a reproducible in vitro system for studying early human development and for identifying conditions that direct differentiation to clinically important cell types. Such cells could also provide genetically normal, expandable starting material for drug discovery, toxicology, and transplantation medicine. We report here the derivation and characterization of pluripotent human ES cell lines from donated blastocysts produced by in vitro fertilization.

title: Embryonic Stem Cell Lines Derived from Human Blastocysts
text: Embryonic stem (ES) cells, first established from the inner cell mass of mouse blastocysts nearly two decades ago, are pluripotent cell lines that can be cultured indefinitely in an undifferentiated state while retaining the capacity to differentiate into all embryonic germ layers. Mouse ES cells have provided an invaluable model for studying the mechanisms of early mammalian development and have revolutionized genetics through the creation of transgenic and knockout models. Despite these advances, the derivation of analogous cell lines from human embryos has proven elusive. Previous efforts have successfully isolated pluripotent cells from human teratocarcinomas (embryonal carcinoma or EC cells) and from human primordial germ cells (embryonic germ or EG cells). While these human lines share some properties with mouse ES cells, they are often karyotypically abnormal or exhibit constrained developmental potential. The availability of true human ES cell lines, possessing both a normal diploid karyotype and the full developmental potential to form all somatic lineages, would offer an unprecedented window into human developmental biology. Furthermore, such cell lines could provide a limitless source of human cells and tissues for drug discovery, pharmacological testing, and transplantation medicine. Here, we report the successful derivation and continuous in vitro culture of embryonic stem cell lines from human blastocysts.

display_title: Embryonic Stem Cell Lines Derived from Human Blastocysts
title: Embryonic Stem Cell Lines Derived from Human Blastocysts
text:
Human ES cells should offer insights into developmental events that cannot be studied directly in the intact human embryo but that have important consequences in clinical areas, including birth defects, infertility, and pregnancy loss. Particularly in the early postimplantation period, knowledge of normal human development is largely restricted to the description of a limited number of sectioned embryos and to analogies drawn from the experimental embryology of other species. Although the mouse is the mainstay of experimental mammalian embryology, early structures including the placenta, extraembryonic membranes, and the egg cylinder all differ substantially from the corresponding structure of the human embryo. Human ES cells will be particularly valuable for the study of the development and function of tissues that differ between mice and humans. Screens based on the in vitro differentiation of human ES cells to specific lineages could identify gene targets for new drugs, genes that could be used for tissue regeneration therapies, and teratogenic or toxic compounds.

Elucidating the mechanisms that control differentiation will facilitate the efficient, directed differentiation of ES cells to specific cell types. The standardized production of large, purified populations of euploid human cells such as cardiomyocytes and neurons will provide a potentially limitless source of cells for drug discovery and transplantation therapies. Many diseases, such as Parkinson's disease and juvenile-onset diabetes mellitus, result from the death or dysfunction of just one or a few cell types. The replacement of those cells could offer lifelong treatment. Strategies to prevent immune rejection of the transplanted cells need to be developed but could include banking ES cells with defined major histocompatibility complex backgrounds or genetically manipulating ES cells to reduce or actively combat immune rejection. Because of the similarities to humans and human ES cells, rhesus monkeys and rhesus ES cells provide an accurate model for developing strategies to prevent immune rejection of transplanted cells and for demonstrating the safety and efficacy of ES cell-based therapies. Substantial advances in basic developmental biology are required to direct ES cells efficiently to lineages of human clinical importance. However, progress has already been made in the in vitro differentiation of mouse ES cells to neurons, hematopoietic cells, and cardiac muscle. Progress in basic developmental biology is now extremely rapid; human ES cells will link this progress even more closely to the prevention and treatment of human disease.

It birthed the entire field of regenerative medicine, arming researchers with an unprecedented tool to investigate human developmental biology, model intricate genetic diseases *in vitro*, and chart pathways for cell-replacement therapies targeting conditions like Parkinson's disease, spinal cord injuries, and diabetes.

paper_title: Embryonic Stem Cell Lines Derived from Human Blastocysts - 1998 - Science
display_title: Embryonic Stem Cell Lines Derived from Human Blastocysts
title: Embryonic Stem Cell Lines Derived from Human Blastocysts
text:
The cell lines described here satisfy the criteria proposed for primate embryonic stem cells. They were derived from the inner cell mass of preimplantation human embryos, proliferated for prolonged periods without loss of an undifferentiated morphology, retained normal karyotypes, expressed high levels of telomerase activity, and maintained the capacity to form differentiated derivatives of endoderm, mesoderm, and ectoderm. Although contribution to the germ line cannot be used as a practical or ethical test for human ES cells, the combination of origin, stable growth, marker expression, and multilineage differentiation provides strong evidence that these cells represent the human counterpart of primate ES cells.

The establishment of these lines also emphasizes important species differences in early development. Human ES cells, like nonhuman primate ES cells and human EC cells, expressed SSEA-3, SSEA-4, TRA-1-60, TRA-1-81, and alkaline phosphatase, but did not express SSEA-1 in the undifferentiated state. This marker pattern differs from that of mouse ES and EC cells, in which SSEA-1 is expressed and SSEA-3 and SSEA-4 are not. These differences suggest that conclusions drawn from mouse ES cells may not always be directly applicable to human development. Human ES cells should therefore provide a complementary experimental system for defining the molecular events that regulate early human cell fate, lineage commitment, and differentiation.

The prolonged proliferation of these cells in culture, together with high telomerase activity, suggests that they may provide a renewable source of normal human cells with broad developmental potential. This property distinguishes them from most differentiated human somatic cells, which have a finite replicative life-span in vitro. The ability to cryopreserve and recover the cell lines further increases their utility as reproducible experimental material. Because the lines were not derived by single-cell cloning, however, some variation among undifferentiated cells cannot be excluded, and future studies will be required to determine the extent to which individual cells within a line share identical developmental potential.

The differentiation observed in teratomas and in culture indicates that human ES cells can give rise to diverse embryonic cell types and, under some conditions, trophoblast. Defined culture conditions that direct differentiation toward specific lineages will be required before these cells can be used effectively for drug discovery, toxicology, or transplantation. In addition, issues of immunologic compatibility, genetic stability during prolonged culture, and the elimination of undifferentiated cells from differentiated preparations must be addressed. Nevertheless, the derivation of human ES cell lines provides a new experimental foundation for the study of human development and for the generation of specialized human cell types in vitro.

title: Embryonic Stem Cell Lines Derived from Human Blastocysts
text:
The derivation of human embryonic stem (ES) cell lines from blastocysts represents a major methodological and conceptual advance in developmental biology. Consistent with previous work in nonhuman primates, our results demonstrate that these cells can be maintained in an undifferentiated state for prolonged periods while retaining their pluripotency. The ability of these cell lines to differentiate into derivatives of all three embryonic germ layers—endoderm, mesoderm, and ectoderm—underscores their remarkable developmental potential. Unlike somatic cells, which undergo replicative senescence, these human ES cells express high levels of telomerase activity, suggesting they possess an extended, if not indefinite, replicative life-span in culture.

This extended replicative capacity, coupled with their pluripotency, positions human ES cell lines as a powerful in vitro model for understanding fundamental aspects of human embryogenesis and early development. Currently, much of our understanding of early mammalian development is derived from murine models. While invaluable, significant developmental differences exist between mice and humans, making human-specific models essential for accurate biological insights.

Beyond basic developmental biology, these cell lines hold profound implications for drug discovery and toxicology. Human ES cells could provide a continuous source of normal human cells and tissues for high-throughput screening of pharmacological compounds, potentially reducing the reliance on animal testing and offering more accurate predictions of human responses to drugs.

Perhaps most significantly, human ES cells offer unprecedented potential for transplantation medicine. The ability to direct the differentiation of these cells into specific cell types, such as neural cells for neurodegenerative diseases, cardiomyocytes for heart failure, or pancreatic beta cells for diabetes, could revolutionize the treatment of numerous devastating conditions. However, realizing this clinical potential will require overcoming significant hurdles, including the challenge of directing differentiation uniformly and addressing the potential for immune rejection of transplanted tissues. Future research must focus on defining the signaling pathways that govern ES cell differentiation and exploring strategies to mitigate immune responses, such as genetic modification or the establishment of diverse ES cell banks. In conclusion, the establishment of human ES cell lines provides a transformative tool that will undoubtedly catalyze major advances in both basic research and clinical applications.

paper_title: Enzymatic Amplification of Beta-Globin Genomic Sequences and Restriction Site Analysis for Diagnosis of Sickle Cell Anemia - 1985 - Science
display_title: Enzymatic Amplification of Beta-Globin Genomic Sequences and Restriction Site Analysis for Diagnosis of Sickle Cell Anemia
title: Enzymatic Amplification of Beta-Globin Genomic Sequences and Restriction Site Analysis for Diagnosis of Sickle Cell Anemia
text: Two new methods were used to establish a rapid and highly sensitive prenatal diagnostic test for sickle cell anemia. The first involves the primer-mediated enzymatic amplification of specific beta-globin target sequences in genomic DNA, resulting in the exponential increase (220,000 times) of target DNA copies. In the second technique, the presence of the beta^A and beta^S alleles is determined by restriction endonuclease digestion of an end-labeled oligonucleotide probe hybridized in solution to the amplified beta-globin sequences. The beta-globin genotype can be determined in less than 1 day on samples containing significantly less than 1 microgram of genomic DNA.
historical_context: Published in 1985 by Randall Saiki, Stephen Scharf, Fred Faloona, Kary Mullis, and colleagues, this paper stands as the first peer-reviewed publication demonstrating the Polymerase Chain Reaction (PCR). The authors deployed PCR to exponentially amplify specific genomic DNA sequences for the diagnosis of sickle cell anemia. This invention permanently altered all of the life sciences. Kary Mullis was awarded the 1993 Nobel Prize in Chemistry for his invention of the PCR method. The Nobel committee underscored how PCR made it remarkably simple to amplify minute quantities of DNA, accelerating basic molecular biology, medical diagnostics, forensics, and evolutionary studies by empowering scientists to synthesize millions of copies of a target DNA segment in mere hours.

paper_title: Enzymatic Amplification of Beta-Globin Genomic Sequences and Restriction Site Analysis for Diagnosis of Sickle Cell Anemia - 1985 - Science
display_title: Enzymatic Amplification of Beta-Globin Genomic Sequences and Restriction Site Analysis for Diagnosis of Sickle Cell Anemia
title: Enzymatic Amplification of Beta-Globin Genomic Sequences and Restriction Site Analysis for Diagnosis of Sickle Cell Anemia
text: We describe a rapid and sensitive procedure for diagnosing sickle cell anemia by direct analysis of genomic DNA. A 110-base pair segment of the beta-globin gene spanning the polymorphic Dde I site altered by the beta^S mutation was amplified by repeated cycles of primer-directed DNA synthesis. The amplified product was then examined by solution hybridization with a labeled oligomer and sequential digestion with Dde I and Hinf I, producing allele-specific cleavage fragments. The method correctly distinguished AA, AS, and SS samples, required as little as 20 nanograms of DNA, and permitted beta-globin genotyping in less than 10 hours.

text: Two new techniques have been developed for the rapid prenatal diagnosis of sickle cell anemia. The first is a method for amplifying specific beta-globin genomic sequences with oligonucleotide primers and the Klenow fragment of DNA polymerase I. Twenty cycles of the polymerase chain reaction (PCR) yield an approximately 220,000-fold amplification of a 110-base pair segment spanning the sixth codon of the beta-globin gene. Together, these procedures permit a diagnosis to be made from as little as 20 nanograms of genomic DNA in less than 10 hours, and they may be generally applicable to the detection of other genetic and infectious diseases.

title: Enzymatic Amplification of Beta-Globin Genomic Sequences and Restriction Site Analysis for Diagnosis of Sickle Cell Anemia
text:
Recent advances in recombinant DNA technology have made possible the molecular analysis and prenatal diagnosis of several human genetic diseases. Fetal DNA obtained by amniocentesis or chorionic villus sampling can be analyzed by restriction enzyme digestion, with subsequent electrophoresis, Southern transfer, and specific hybridization to cloned gene or oligonucleotide probes. With polymorphic DNA markers linked genetically to a specific disease locus, segregation analysis must be carried out with restriction fragment length polymorphisms (RFLPs) found to be informative by examining DNA from family members.

Many of the hemoglobinopathies, however, can be detected by more direct methods in which analysis of the fetus alone is sufficient for diagnosis. For example, the diagnosis of hydrops fetalis (homozygous alpha-thalassemia) can be made by documenting the absence of any alpha-globin genes by hybridization with an alpha-globin probe. Homozygosity for certain beta-thalassemia alleles can be determined in Southern transfer experiments by using oligonucleotide probes that form stable duplexes with the normal beta-globin gene sequence but form unstable hybrids with specific mutants.

Sickle cell anemia can also be diagnosed by direct analysis of fetal DNA. DNA obtained by amniocentesis or chorionic villus sampling can be treated with a restriction endonuclease (for example, Dde I and Mst II) that recognizes a sequence altered by the S mutation. This generates beta^A- and beta^S-specific restriction fragments that can be resolved by Southern transfer and hybridization with a beta-globin probe. We have developed a procedure for the detection of the sickle cell mutation that is very rapid and is at least two orders of magnitude more sensitive than standard Southern blotting. There are two special features to this protocol. The first is a method for amplifying specific beta-globin DNA sequences with the use of oligonucleotide primers and DNA polymerase. The second is the analysis of the beta-globin genotype by solution hybridization of the amplified DNA with a specific oligonucleotide probe and subsequent digestion with a restriction endonuclease. These two techniques increase the speed and sensitivity, and lessen the complexity of prenatal diagnosis for sickle cell anemia; they may also be generally applicable to the diagnosis of other genetic diseases and in the use of DNA probes for infectious disease diagnosis.

Kary Mullis was awarded the 1993 Nobel Prize in Chemistry for his invention of the PCR method. The Nobel committee underscored how PCR made it remarkably simple to amplify minute quantities of DNA, accelerating basic molecular biology, medical diagnostics, forensics, and evolutionary studies by empowering scientists to synthesize millions of copies of a target DNA segment in mere hours.

title: Enzymatic Amplification of Beta-Globin Genomic Sequences and Restriction Site Analysis for Diagnosis of Sickle Cell Anemia
text:
Prenatal diagnosis of sickle cell anemia has depended on the analysis of fetal DNA by restriction endonuclease digestion, Southern transfer, and hybridization with cloned globin gene probes. These methods are reliable, but require microgram quantities of relatively intact DNA and several days to complete after DNA has been prepared. In many prenatal samples, particularly those obtained early in gestation, the amount and quality of DNA may be limiting. A more rapid and sensitive procedure for detecting the beta-globin genotype would therefore be useful both for clinical diagnosis and for the analysis of defined human gene sequences.

Sickle cell anemia results from a single base substitution in the sixth codon of the beta-globin gene. This mutation changes the beta^A sequence to beta^S and eliminates a Dde I restriction site present in the normal allele. The same nucleotide change can be detected indirectly by restriction fragment length polymorphism analysis, but such assays generally require Southern blotting and large amounts of genomic DNA. We reasoned that if the small segment of the beta-globin gene containing the diagnostic base substitution could be selectively increased in copy number, allele detection could be performed by a simple oligonucleotide-based assay rather than by conventional genomic hybridization.

We have combined two procedures to achieve this goal. The first is primer-mediated enzymatic amplification of a defined genomic sequence. Two synthetic oligonucleotides complementary to opposite strands of the beta-globin gene are used to prime DNA synthesis across the region containing the sickle cell mutation. Repeated cycles of denaturation, primer annealing, and polymerase extension result in the exponential accumulation of the intervening sequence. The second procedure is oligomer restriction analysis, in which an end-labeled oligonucleotide probe is hybridized in solution to the amplified DNA and then digested with restriction enzymes that distinguish the beta^A and beta^S sequences.

Here we show that a 110-base pair segment of the beta-globin gene can be amplified approximately 220,000-fold from human genomic DNA. The amplified products can be analyzed by oligomer restriction digestion to determine whether a sample is homozygous normal, heterozygous, or homozygous for the sickle cell allele. The combined procedure permits beta-globin genotyping in less than one day and with substantially less starting DNA than is required for standard Southern blot analysis.

The Nobel committee underscored how PCR made it remarkably simple to amplify minute quantities of DNA, accelerating basic molecular biology, medical diagnostics, forensics, and evolutionary studies by empowering scientists to synthesize millions of copies of a target DNA segment in mere hours.

title: Enzymatic Amplification of Beta-Globin Genomic Sequences and Restriction Site Analysis for Diagnosis of Sickle Cell Anemia
text:
The diagnosis of genetic disorders at the DNA level has been significantly advanced by the use of restriction fragment length polymorphisms (RFLPs) and cloned gene probes. For sickle cell anemia, a severe autosomal recessive disease caused by a single nucleotide substitution (A to T) in the sixth codon of the beta-globin gene, prenatal diagnosis has traditionally relied on the analysis of fetal DNA obtained through amniocentesis. The mutation underlying the sickle cell allele (beta^S) serendipitously abolishes a recognition site for the restriction endonucleases Dde I and Mst II, which is present in the normal (beta^A) allele. This sequence variation allows for the direct detection of the beta^S mutation by restriction enzyme digestion and Southern blotting, bypassing the need for family linkage studies. Despite the elegance of this approach, the standard Southern blotting technique is labor-intensive, time-consuming, and requires relatively large quantities of high-molecular-weight genomic DNA, which can be difficult to obtain in certain clinical situations.

Recent advances in the chemical synthesis of oligonucleotides have provided a new class of highly specific hybridization probes. Under appropriately stringent conditions, short synthetic oligonucleotides can discriminate between sequences that differ by as little as a single base pair. We have previously demonstrated that such probes can be used to detect the sickle cell mutation directly in genomic DNA without relying on a fortuitous restriction site alteration. However, the sensitivity of oligonucleotide hybridization is limited by the complexity of the human genome and the small fraction of total DNA represented by a single-copy gene.

To overcome these limitations and increase the speed and sensitivity of genetic analysis, we have developed a novel method for the targeted amplification of specific genomic DNA sequences in vitro. This technique, termed the polymerase chain reaction (PCR), uses two flanking oligonucleotide primers and repeated cycles of DNA polymerase-mediated extension to achieve a massive, exponential increase in the number of copies of the target sequence. Here, we demonstrate the application of PCR to amplify a segment of the beta-globin gene, combined with a rapid solution hybridization and restriction digestion assay, to provide a highly sensitive and efficient method for the prenatal diagnosis of sickle cell anemia.

Kary Mullis was awarded the 1993 Nobel Prize in Chemistry for his invention of the PCR method. The Nobel committee underscored how PCR made it remarkably simple to amplify minute quantities of DNA, accelerating basic molecular biology, medical diagnostics, forensics, and evolutionary studies by empowering scientists to synthesize millions of copies of a target DNA segment in mere hours.

paper_title: Enzymatic Amplification of Beta-Globin Genomic Sequences and Restriction Site Analysis for Diagnosis of Sickle Cell Anemia - 1985 - Science

title: Enzymatic Amplification of Beta-Globin Genomic Sequences and Restriction Site Analysis for Diagnosis of Sickle Cell Anemia
text:
Diagnostic applications of the PCR-OR system. When currently available methods are used, the completion of a prenatal diagnosis for sickle cell anemia takes a period of several days after the DNA is isolated. With 1 microgram of genomic DNA, the beta-globin genotype can be determined by the PCR-OR method in less than 10 hours; 20 cycles of amplification requires about 2 hours (each full cycle takes 6 to 7 minutes in our protocol), the oligomer restriction procedure involving liquid hybridization and enzyme digestions require an additional 2 hours, and the electrophoresis takes about an hour. Autoradiographic exposure for 4 hours is sufficient to generate a strong signal.

Because this method includes a liquid hybridization protocol and involves the serial addition of reagents to a single tube, it is simpler to perform than the standard Southern transfer and hybridization procedure. Prior to electrophoresis, all of the reactions can be done in two small microcentrifuge tubes and could readily be automated. The sensitivity, as well as the speed and simplicity, of this procedure is also important for clinical applications. Twenty nanograms of starting material can provide an easily detectable result in an overnight autoradiographic exposure. This sensitivity makes the PCR-OR method particularly valuable in cases where poor DNA yields are obtained from prenatal samples. In addition, DNA samples of poor quality (very low average molecular weight) can give excellent results in the PCR-OR protocol.

The PCR method is likely to be generally applicable for specific gene amplification since a fragment encoding a portion of the HLA-DQalpha locus has recently been amplified with this procedure. We have carried out PCR amplification on a 110-bp beta-globin sequence with an overall efficiency per cycle of about 85 percent. We have also amplified longer beta-globin fragments (up to 267 bp), but the yield was lower under our standard conditions. Efficient amplification of a 267-bp fragment required some variation in the PCR procedure. In principle, increasing the number of PCR cycles should yield even greater amplification than that reported here (~220,000-fold after 20 cycles).

Our method for the diagnosis of sickle cell anemia involves the coupling of the PCR procedure with that of oligomer restriction. It was designed to distinguish between two alleles that differ by a polymorphic restriction site. The PCR-OR method is applicable as well to the diagnosis of other diseases where the lesion directly affects a restriction enzyme site or where the polymorphic site is in strong linkage disequilibrium with the disease causing locus. If the polymorphism is in linkage equilibrium with the disease, PCR-OR requires family studies to follow the inheritance of the disease locus. In the case of the beta-globin locus, the presence of the invariant Hinf I restriction site adjacent to the polymorphic Dde I site allows a sequential digestion procedure to identify both the beta^A and beta^S alleles. In principle, this approach does not require that the two sites be immediately adjacent but only that the cleavage product generated by digestion at the polymorphic site dissociate from the target to prevent cutting at the invariant site. Since the restriction enzyme digestion conditions used here are fairly stringent for hybridization, we estimate that the polymorphic and invariant sites could perhaps be separated by as much as 20 bp.

The application of the PCR method to prenatal diagnosis does not necessarily depend on a polymorphic restriction site or on the use of radioactive probes. In fact, the significant amplification of target sequences achieved by the PCR method allows the use of nonisotopically labeled probes. Amplified target sequences could also be analyzed by a number of other procedures including those involving the hybridization of small labeled oligomers which will form stable duplexes only if perfectly matched and the recently reported method based on the electrophoretic shifts of duplexes with base pair mismatches. The ability of the PCR procedure to amplify a target DNA segment in genomic DNA raises the possibility that its use may extend beyond that of prenatal diagnosis to other areas of molecular biology.

Kary Mullis was awarded the 1993 Nobel Prize in Chemistry for his invention of the PCR method. The Nobel committee underscored how PCR made it remarkably simple to amplify minute quantities of DNA, accelerating basic molecular biology, medical diagnostics, forensics, and evolutionary studies by empowering scientists to synthesize millions of copies of a target DNA segment in mere hours.

paper_title: Enzymatic Amplification of Beta-Globin Genomic Sequences and Restriction Site Analysis for Diagnosis of Sickle Cell Anemia - 1985 - Science
display_title: Enzymatic Amplification of Beta-Globin Genomic Sequences and Restriction Site Analysis for Diagnosis of Sickle Cell Anemia
title: Enzymatic Amplification of Beta-Globin Genomic Sequences and Restriction Site Analysis for Diagnosis of Sickle Cell Anemia
text:
The results described here show that a defined human genomic sequence can be amplified sufficiently to permit direct restriction site analysis on very small amounts of DNA. By combining primer-mediated amplification of a 110-bp segment of the beta-globin gene with oligomer restriction analysis, the beta^A and beta^S alleles can be distinguished without the need for Southern transfer, cloned probes, or the analysis of linked polymorphic markers. The genotypes assigned by this procedure were in agreement with those obtained by conventional Mst II restriction fragment analysis, and the autoradiographic signals obtained after 20 cycles of amplification were strong enough to allow scoring of the samples after short exposures.

The principal advantage of this method is the increase in sensitivity produced by enzymatic amplification before allele analysis. Under the conditions used, 20 cycles of PCR resulted in an estimated 220,000-fold increase in the amount of beta-globin target sequence. This permits the analysis of a small fraction of an amplified sample and indicates that DNA quantities far below those ordinarily required for Southern blotting can be used. The ability to determine genotype from 20 ng of genomic DNA, with the likelihood that still smaller samples could be examined by using a larger proportion of the amplification reaction, is of particular importance for prenatal diagnosis. Chorionic villus sampling and amniocentesis may yield limited amounts of fetal material, and a procedure requiring less DNA reduces the constraints imposed by sample size.

The second important feature of the method is that the amplified DNA is examined by a restriction assay in solution. The oligomer restriction procedure depends on the formation of a probe-target duplex in which a single base substitution abolishes cleavage at the Dde I site. The use of the adjacent invariant Hinf I site provides an internal alternative cleavage product, so that both alleles give positive and distinguishable signals. Thus, an AA sample produces the 8-nt fragment, an SS sample produces the 3-nt fragment, and an AS sample produces both. This design avoids reliance on the absence of a band alone and makes the interpretation of heterozygous and homozygous samples straightforward.

Some low-level background products were observed. In AA samples, a faint 3-nt fragment was present in addition to the expected 8-nt product. This may reflect incomplete Dde I digestion or retention of the 8-nt fragment in the probe-target duplex long enough to allow subsequent cleavage by Hinf I. In SS samples, a faint 8-nt fragment was detected. Evidence from digestion of the template DNA with Mbo I before amplification indicates that this signal is due to amplification of homologous delta-globin sequences, which retain the Dde I site and can hybridize weakly to the RS06 probe. Although these background bands did not prevent correct genotype assignment, they show that amplification primers and diagnostic probes must be chosen with attention to closely related sequences in the genome. For clinical application, the assay conditions should be standardized to maintain a large difference between the allele-specific signal and any cross-hybridizing background.

The time required for the combined PCR and OR procedure is also a substantial improvement over existing methods. With 20 amplification cycles and a short autoradiographic exposure, a diagnosis can be made in less than 1 day, and in favorable cases in less than 10 hours. Conventional restriction fragment analysis requires more DNA and several additional steps, including electrophoresis of genomic fragments, transfer to a membrane, and hybridization. The present method therefore reduces both the amount of starting material and the time between specimen collection and interpretation.

This approach should not be limited to sickle cell anemia. Any inherited or acquired sequence change that creates or destroys a restriction site, or that can be placed within a restriction site by the design of an appropriate probe, should in principle be amenable to the same analysis after amplification. More generally, amplification of selected DNA segments followed by specific probe-based detection may be useful for the diagnosis of other genetic diseases and for the detection of infectious agents present in small amounts. The combination of specificity conferred by oligonucleotide primers, sensitivity conferred by repeated enzymatic copying, and allele discrimination conferred by the oligomer restriction assay provides a rapid and direct method for DNA diagnosis.

paper_title: Enzymatic Amplification of Beta-Globin Genomic Sequences and Restriction Site Analysis for Diagnosis of Sickle Cell Anemia - 1985 - Science
display_title: Enzymatic Amplification of Beta-Globin Genomic Sequences and Restriction Site Analysis for Diagnosis of Sickle Cell Anemia
title: Enzymatic Amplification of Beta-Globin Genomic Sequences and Restriction Site Analysis for Diagnosis of Sickle Cell Anemia
text:
The enzymatic amplification of specific genomic sequences using the polymerase chain reaction (PCR) represents a significant technical breakthrough in molecular biology, with immediate and profound implications for genetic analysis and medical diagnostics. In this study, we have demonstrated the efficacy of PCR in exponentially amplifying beta-globin genomic sequences from unpurified DNA, facilitating the rapid and highly sensitive diagnosis of sickle cell anemia. The ability to achieve a 200,000-fold amplification of a specific target sequence directly from clinical samples overcomes a major limitation of previous methodologies, which often required large amounts of high-quality DNA and complex, time-consuming hybridization procedures.

The robustness of this amplification technique is rooted in its conceptual simplicity and precision. By using two oligonucleotide primers that flank the region of interest, one complementary to the plus strand and the other to the minus strand, repeated cycles of denaturation, primer annealing, and enzymatic extension result in an exponential accumulation of the target fragment. This method dramatically increases the signal-to-noise ratio in subsequent detection assays, such as restriction site analysis, making it possible to identify single base-pair mutations with unprecedented clarity and reliability.

In the context of sickle cell anemia, the mutation responsible for the disease alters a DdeI restriction site. The amplified DNA can be directly subjected to restriction endonuclease digestion, and the resulting fragments analyzed by Southern blotting or even simple gel electrophoresis, depending on the amplification efficiency. This streamlined approach not only reduces the time required for diagnosis from weeks to hours but also significantly minimizes the risk of diagnostic errors that can arise from low-signal intensity or cross-hybridization in traditional genomic Southern blots.

The implications of PCR extend far beyond the diagnosis of sickle cell anemia. This technology provides a universal tool for the amplification of any known DNA sequence, opening up new avenues for research in molecular genetics, evolutionary biology, and infectious disease diagnostics. For instance, PCR could be adapted for the rapid detection of viral or bacterial DNA in clinical specimens, offering a highly sensitive alternative to culture-based methods. Furthermore, the ability to amplify DNA from minute quantities of starting material makes it possible to analyze forensic samples or ancient DNA that were previously inaccessible to molecular analysis.

While the current methodology utilizes the Klenow fragment of E. coli DNA polymerase I, which is heat-labile and must be replenished after each denaturation step, the fundamental principle is established. Future refinements, such as the introduction of a thermostable DNA polymerase, could fully automate the process, further enhancing its utility and applicability. We anticipate that as the technique is optimized and automated, it will become a standard procedure in clinical laboratories for prenatal diagnosis, carrier screening, and the identification of genetic susceptibility to complex diseases.

In conclusion, the polymerase chain reaction is a versatile and extraordinarily powerful technique that fundamentally changes how we approach the analysis of genomic DNA. By providing a means to isolate and amplify specific DNA segments in vitro, PCR bypasses many of the traditional challenges associated with molecular cloning and direct genomic analysis. Its application to the diagnosis of sickle cell anemia is merely the first demonstration of its broad utility, heralding a new era of rapid, precise, and highly sensitive genetic testing.

paper_title: Establishment in Culture of Pluripotential Cells from Mouse Embryos - 1981 - Nature
display_title: Establishment in Culture of Pluripotential Cells from Mouse Embryos
title: Establishment in Culture of Pluripotential Cells from Mouse Embryos
text: Pluripotential cells are present in a mouse embryo until at least an early post-implantation stage, as shown by their ability to take part in the formation of chimaeric animals and to form teratocarcinomas. Until now it has not been possible to establish progressively growing cultures of these cells in vitro, and cell lines have only been obtained after teratocarcinoma formation in vivo. We report here the establishment in tissue culture of pluripotent cell lines which have been isolated directly from in vitro cultures of mouse blastocysts. These cells are able to differentiate either in vitro or after inoculation into a mouse as a tumour in vivo. They have a normal karyotype.
historical_context: In 1981, Martin Evans and Matthew Kaufman successfully isolated and cultured pluripotential stem cells from the inner cell mass of mouse blastocysts, with Gail Martin independently publishing parallel findings. These embryonic stem (ES) cells could be sustained in culture and, pivotally, could reintegrate into living embryos to generate chimeric mice. This work established the bedrock for mammalian genetic engineering. Martin Evans was awarded the 2007 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine (shared with Mario Capecchi and Oliver Smithies). The Nobel committee acknowledged their discoveries of principles for introducing specific gene modifications in mice via embryonic stem cells, an innovation that led to the creation of "knockout mice"—now an indispensable pillar for decoding gene function and modeling human disease.

paper_title: Establishment in Culture of Pluripotential Cells from Mouse Embryos - 1981 - Nature
display_title: Establishment in Culture of Pluripotential Cells from Mouse Embryos
title: Establishment in Culture of Pluripotential Cells from Mouse Embryos
text: Previous attempts to establish cultures of pluripotential cells directly from mouse embryos have been unsuccessful. We report here a method for deriving such cells from blastocysts maintained in implantational delay and subsequently cultured on inactivated STO fibroblast feeders. The inner cell mass gave rise to colonies of rapidly proliferating cells closely resembling embryonal carcinoma cells in morphology and growth requirements. These embryo-derived EK cells retained normal 40XX or 40XY karyotypes, excluding contamination by established embryonal carcinoma lines, and have been maintained for more than 30 passages. After injection into syngeneic mice they formed teratocarcinomas, and in vitro they generated embryoid bodies and differentiated tissues. These cultures provide a direct link between the early embryo and pluripotential cells in culture.

paper_title: Establishment in Culture of Pluripotential Cells from Mouse Embryos - 1981 - Nature
display_title: Establishment in Culture of Pluripotential Cells from Mouse Embryos
title: Establishment in Culture of Pluripotential Cells from Mouse Embryos
text: Pluripotential cell lines have been established directly from mouse blastocysts. Embryos of the 129 SvE strain were placed into implantational delay by ovariectomy and Depo-Provera treatment, recovered after 4-6 days, and cultured intact on tissue culture plastic. The resulting egg cylinder-like structures were dispersed and passaged on mitomycin C-inactivated STO feeder layers, where actively proliferating colonies closely resembling embryonal carcinoma (EC) cells appeared. These embryo-derived (EK) cells have a normal diploid karyotype, form teratocarcinomas when injected into syngeneic mice, differentiate into a complex of tissues via embryoid bodies, and share cell-surface antigens and protein synthesis patterns with EC cells. Fifteen independent lines have been obtained from 129 and outbred stocks.

title: Establishment in Culture of Pluripotential Cells from Mouse Embryos
text: Pluripotential cells capable of prolonged growth in vitro have previously only been obtained from teratocarcinomas as embryonal carcinoma (EC) cells. We report the successful establishment of pluripotential cell cultures directly from normal early mouse embryos. By utilizing blastocysts in delayed implantation, we derived cell lines that closely resemble EC cells in morphology, growth, and surface antigen expression. Crucially, unlike most EC lines, these embryo-derived (EK) cells maintain a completely normal karyotype. When injected into mice, they form complex teratocarcinomas, and in vitro, they differentiate into various tissues. These directly derived pluripotential cells offer a powerful new system for studying early mammalian development and differentiation, bypassing the abnormal chromosomal complement typical of teratocarcinoma-derived cells.

paper_title: Establishment in Culture of Pluripotential Cells from Mouse Embryos - 1981 - Nature
display_title: Establishment in Culture of Pluripotential Cells from Mouse Embryos
title: Establishment in Culture of Pluripotential Cells from Mouse Embryos
text:
Previous attempts to obtain cultures of pluripotential cells directly from a mouse embryo have been unsuccessful, although cells with a similar appearance have been reported to be present transiently. We considered that success might depend on three critical factors: (1) the exact stage at which pluripotential cells capable of growth in tissue culture exist in the embryo; (2) explantation of a sufficiently large number of these precursor cells from each embryo; and (3) tissue culture in conditions most conducive to multiplication rather than differentiation of these embryonic cells. These considerations have been discussed at greater length elsewhere. An indication of the stage of embryonic development might be gained by a comparison of the properties of embryonic cells at various stages with established cultures of embryonal carcinoma (EC) cells. Cell-surface antigen expression and the patterns of protein synthesis revealed by two-dimensional electrophoresis have suggested that neither the cells of the 6.5-day ectoderm nor those of the 3.5-day inner cell mass show homology with EC cells, but that epiblast cells of the early post-implantation embryo at 5.5 days post coitum may do so (the day of finding coital plug is termed day 0). Cells from embryos of an early post-implantation stage seem to be the best candidates for direct progenitors of pluripotential cells in culture. As these embryos are difficult to isolate, and as the cell number in the isolated epiblast is small, we chose an alternative route to obtain embryo cells at this stage of development.

Mouse blastocysts may be induced to enter a state of diapause just before implantation. This delay in implantation depends on the maternal hormonal conditions, and may be induced experimentally by ovariectomy at an appropriate stage. Embryos in implantational delay hatch from the zona but remain free-floating in the uterine lumen. A gradual increase in cell number occurs, and the primary endoderm may be formed but no further development takes place until implantation occurs, under the control of hormonal stimuli.

title: Establishment in Culture of Pluripotential Cells from Mouse Embryos
text:
The early mouse embryo contains cells which retain a wide developmental potential. This has been shown most directly by the production of chimaeric animals after the introduction of cells from the inner cell mass or early post-implantation embryo into host blastocysts, and by the ability of such cells, when transplanted to an ectopic site, to give rise to teratocarcinomas. In the latter tumours, embryonal carcinoma (EC) cells provide a continuously growing population which can differentiate to form many somatic tissues, and which therefore offers an experimental system for studying the first steps of mammalian development in vitro.

The origin of EC cells has nevertheless placed an important limitation on their use. They are obtained after the formation of a tumour in vivo and after selection during serial passage, and may therefore have acquired genetic or epigenetic changes not present in normal embryonic cells. Many EC cell lines have abnormal karyotypes and differ in their capacity to differentiate or to contribute to normal development. It has consequently remained uncertain how far the properties of EC cells reflect those of the pluripotential cells of the embryo from which they are presumed to arise.

Attempts to grow such embryonic cells directly in culture have not previously yielded permanent, progressively growing cell lines. The inner cell mass of cultured blastocysts can proliferate for a short time and produce cell types resembling those of the early egg cylinder, but the pluripotential component has not been maintained as an undifferentiated culture. A direct method for isolating these cells would complete the experimental relationship between the embryo, EC cells and differentiated derivatives, and would provide cells of known embryonic origin for genetic and developmental studies.

We describe here the establishment of cell lines from mouse blastocysts cultured in vitro. The cells resemble feeder-dependent EC cells in morphology and growth, but are derived directly from normal embryos, have normal male or female karyotypes, and retain the ability to differentiate both in culture and as teratocarcinomas after injection into syngeneic mice.

text:
The early mammalian embryo provides a unique system for studying the progressive restriction of cellular potential during development. In the mouse, the cells of the inner cell mass of the blastocyst are pluripotential, capable of contributing to all somatic tissues and the germ line of an adult animal, as demonstrated by the formation of chimaeric mice following their injection into host blastocysts. Additionally, when transplanted to extra-uterine sites, early embryos can form teratocarcinomas, which are complex tumours comprising an array of differentiated tissues alongside a population of undifferentiated, pluripotential stem cells. These stem cells, known as embryonal carcinoma (EC) cells, can be established as permanent lines in tissue culture and serve as a widely used model for early embryonic development. EC cells share many biochemical and immunological properties with the undifferentiated cells of the early embryo and, remarkably, can also participate in normal embryogenesis when reintroduced into a developing blastocyst.

Despite the functional equivalence between EC cells and the normal embryonic cells from which they are derived, the transition from a normal embryo to a teratocarcinoma is accompanied by poorly understood changes. EC cell lines often exhibit karyotypic abnormalities and may lose their ability to differentiate fully or colonize the germ line. The isolation of pluripotential cells directly from the early embryo, bypassing the intermediate stage of in vivo tumour formation, would circumvent these issues and provide a more direct model of normal embryogenesis. Previous attempts to culture mouse embryos in vitro have generally resulted in the differentiation of the inner cell mass into a limited range of cell types, primarily extra-embryonic endoderm, with the eventual loss of pluripotential cells. We now report that under appropriate culture conditions, it is possible to isolate and maintain continuously proliferating lines of pluripotential cells directly from in vitro cultures of mouse blastocysts. These embryo-derived cell lines, which we term EK cells, retain a normal diploid karyotype and exhibit the full developmental potential characteristic of EC cells, including the ability to differentiate in vitro and form teratocarcinomas in vivo.

title: Establishment in Culture of Pluripotential Cells from Mouse Embryos
text:
We have demonstrated here that it is possible to isolate pluripotential cells directly from early embryos and that they behave in a manner equivalent to EC cells isolated from teratocarcinomas. The network of inter-relationships between the mouse embryo and pluripotential cells derived from it has previously lacked only the direct link between the embryo and cells in culture for completion. We have now demonstrated this.

Teratocarcinoma cells are now being widely used as a model for the study of developmental processes of early embryonic cell commitment and differentiation. Their use as a vehicle for the transfer into the mouse genome of mutant alleles, either selected in cell culture or inserted into the cells via transformation with specific DNA fragments, has been presented as an attractive proposition. In many of these studies the use of pluripotential cells directly isolated from the embryos under study should have great advantages. We have now shown that these EK cell lines are readily established from cultures of single blastocysts and so far have 15 lines of independent embryonic origin, some of which have been isolated from non-129, outbred mouse stocks. We are now studying the chimaeric mice formed from these cells.

title: Establishment in Culture of Pluripotential Cells from Mouse Embryos
text:
The results show that cells with the growth characteristics and differentiative capacity of embryonal carcinoma cells can be obtained directly from normal mouse embryos without an intervening tumour stage. The delayed blastocyst culture system seems to provide a useful means of increasing the number of appropriate embryonic cells while preventing further organized development. Subsequent transfer to feeder layers then permits their continued proliferation in an undifferentiated state.

The normal karyotype of the cultures, including the recovery of both XX and XY lines, is important evidence that these cells are not contaminants from established EC cultures. Their ability to form teratocarcinomas after inoculation into syngeneic mice, and to give rise to embryoid bodies and differentiated tissues in vitro, indicates that they retain pluripotentiality after prolonged passage. Thus the malignant growth of EC cells is not a prerequisite for the establishment of pluripotential cell lines, although the culture requirements and morphology of EK cells are closely similar to those of feeder-dependent EC cells.

These observations suggest that the embryo contains cells capable of indefinite multiplication in vitro if isolated at, or brought to, an appropriate developmental state. Such cultures should provide a direct experimental system for analysing early mammalian differentiation, the relationship between normal embryonic cells and EC cells, and the conditions controlling the maintenance or loss of pluripotentiality.

title: Establishment in Culture of Pluripotential Cells from Mouse Embryos
text:
The successful establishment of pluripotential cell lines directly from mouse embryos marks a significant methodological advance, providing a robust in vitro system for the study of early mammalian development. These embryonic stem (ES) cells, which can be maintained in a proliferative, undifferentiated state, retain the remarkable capacity to differentiate into a wide variety of cell types representative of all three germ layers. This inherent pluripotency closely mirrors the properties of embryonal carcinoma (EC) cells derived from teratocarcinomas, but without the complications of deriving cells from a tumor environment, thereby offering a more authentic model of normal embryonic processes.

Our findings indicate that the key to maintaining these cells lies in the precise culture conditions, which seem to substitute for the regulatory environment of the intact embryo. The ability to cultivate these cells indefinitely opens up new possibilities for genetic manipulation, including the potential introduction of targeted genetic modifications into the germ line. Future studies will be required to fully elucidate the developmental pathways these cells can traverse and their precise relationship to the inner cell mass. Ultimately, these early embryo-derived cell lines provide a powerful tool for exploring the molecular and cellular mechanisms governing mammalian embryogenesis.

paper_title: Ferroptosis - An Iron-Dependent Form of Nonapoptotic Cell Death - 2012 - Cell
display_title: Ferroptosis - An Iron-Dependent Form of Nonapoptotic Cell Death
title: Ferroptosis - An Iron-Dependent Form of Nonapoptotic Cell Death
text: Nonapoptotic forms of cell death may facilitate the selective elimination of some tumor cells or be activated in specific pathological states. The oncogenic RAS-selective lethal small molecule erastin triggers a unique iron-dependent form of nonapoptotic cell death that we term ferroptosis. Ferroptosis is dependent upon intracellular iron, but not other metals, and is morphologically, biochemically, and genetically distinct from apoptosis, necrosis, and autophagy. We identify the small molecule ferrostatin-1 as a potent inhibitor of ferroptosis in cancer cells and glutamate-induced cell death in organotypic rat brain slices, suggesting similarities between these two processes. Indeed, erastin, like glutamate, inhibits cystine uptake by the cystine/glutamate antiporter (system xc-), creating a void in the antioxidant defenses of the cell and ultimately leading to iron-dependent, oxidative death. Thus, activation of ferroptosis results in the nonapoptotic destruction of certain cancer cells, whereas inhibition of this process may protect organisms from neurodegeneration.
historical_context: In 2012, Scott Dixon, Brent Stockwell, and colleagues defined a novel, non-apoptotic form of regulated cell death which they designated "ferroptosis." Fueled by iron-dependent lipid peroxidation and mechanistically distinct from apoptosis, necrosis, and autophagy, ferroptosis is triggered by the inhibition of the cystine/glutamate antiporter (system xc−) or glutathione peroxidase 4 (GPX4). This discovery has been deeply influential. It carved out a brand-new paradigm in cell death research, exposing profound links between cellular metabolism, oxidative stress, and pathology. Ferroptosis is now intensely investigated as a fundamental driver in neurodegenerative diseases and ischemia-reperfusion injury, as well as a potent vulnerability to exploit in therapy-resistant cancers.

paper_title: Ferroptosis - An Iron-Dependent Form of Nonapoptotic Cell Death - 2012 - Cell
display_title: Ferroptosis - An Iron-Dependent Form of Nonapoptotic Cell Death
title: Ferroptosis - An Iron-Dependent Form of Nonapoptotic Cell Death
text: Nonapoptotic cell death pathways are increasingly recognized as regulated processes with distinct developmental and pathological functions. Here we describe ferroptosis, an iron-dependent form of cell death triggered in oncogenic RAS mutant tumor cells by the RAS-selective lethal compounds erastin and RSL3. Ferroptosis was accompanied by sustained cytosolic and lipid reactive oxygen species accumulation and was suppressed by iron chelation and antioxidants, but not by inhibitors of apoptosis, necroptosis, autophagy, or other known death programs. Genetic screens identified RPL8, IREB2, ATP5G3, CS, TTC35, and ACSF2 as specific mediators of erastin-induced ferroptosis, implicating iron regulation, translation, and lipid metabolism. We further identified ferrostatin-1, a potent small-molecule inhibitor that blocks lipid ROS accumulation and protects both cancer cells and organotypic hippocampal slice cultures from glutamate-induced toxicity. Erastin inhibits cystine uptake by system xc-, thereby weakening antioxidant defenses and enabling iron-dependent lethal oxidation. These findings define ferroptosis as a distinct regulated death mechanism with potential relevance to cancer therapy and neuroprotection.

title: Ferroptosis - An Iron-Dependent Form of Nonapoptotic Cell Death
text: Cell death is essential for normal physiology, and while apoptosis is well understood, other regulated forms of nonapoptotic cell death remain poorly characterized. Here, we describe ferroptosis, a uniquely iron-dependent form of nonapoptotic cell death triggered by the small molecules erastin and RSL3, which were originally identified for their selective lethality to oncogenic RAS-mutant cancer cells. Our findings suggest that ferroptosis represents a broad vulnerability in both cancer and neurodegeneration that can be pharmacologically exploited.

paper_title: Ferroptosis - An Iron-Dependent Form of Nonapoptotic Cell Death - 2012 - Cell
display_title: Ferroptosis - An Iron-Dependent Form of Nonapoptotic Cell Death
title: Ferroptosis - An Iron-Dependent Form of Nonapoptotic Cell Death
text:
Cell death is crucial for normal development, homeostasis, and the prevention of hyperproliferative diseases such as cancer. It was once thought that almost all regulated cell death in mammalian cells resulted from the activation of caspase-dependent apoptosis. More recently, this view has been challenged by the discovery of several regulated nonapoptotic cell death pathways activated in specific disease states, including poly(ADP-ribose) polymerase-1 (PARP-1) and apoptosis-inducing factor 1 (AIF1)-dependent parthanatos, caspase-1-dependent pyroptosis, and receptor-interacting protein kinase 1 (RIPK1)-dependent necroptosis. We hypothesized that additional regulated forms of nonapoptotic cell death likely remain to be discovered that mediate cell death in other developmental or pathological circumstances.

The RAS family of small GTPases (HRAS, NRAS, and KRAS) are mutated in ~30% of all cancers. Finding compounds that are selectively lethal to RAS mutant tumor cells is therefore a high priority. We previously identified two structurally unrelated small molecules, named erastin and RSL3, that were selectively lethal to oncogenic RAS mutant cell lines and that we refer to together as RAS-selective lethal (RSL) compounds. Using affinity purification, we identified voltage-dependent anion channels 2 and 3 (VDAC2/3) as direct targets of erastin, but not RSL3. Short hairpin RNA (shRNA) and complementary DNA (cDNA) overexpression studies demonstrated that VDAC2 and VDAC3 are necessary, but not sufficient, for erastin-induced death, indicating that additional unknown targets are required for this process.

The type of cell death activated by the RSLs has been enigmatic. Classic features of apoptosis, such as mitochondrial cytochrome c release, caspase activation, and chromatin fragmentation, are not observed in RSL-treated cells. RSL-induced death is, however, associated with increased levels of intracellular reactive oxygen species (ROS) and is prevented by iron chelation or genetic inhibition of cellular iron uptake. In a recent systematic study of various mechanistically unique lethal compounds, the prevention of cell death by iron chelation was a rare phenomenon, suggesting that few triggers can access iron-dependent lethal mechanisms.

We have explored the hypothesis that RSLs such as erastin activate a lethal pathway that is different from apoptosis, necrosis, and other well-characterized types of regulated cell death. We find that erastin-induced death involves a unique constellation of morphological, biochemical, and genetic features, which led us to propose the name ferroptosis as a description for this phenotype. We identified a specific small-molecule inhibitor of ferroptosis (ferrostatin-1) that prevents ferroptosis in cancer cells, as well as glutamate-induced cell death in postnatal rat brain slices. Our results suggest an underlying similarity between diverse forms of iron-dependent, nonapoptotic death and that the manipulation of ferroptosis may be exploited to selectively destroy RAS mutant tumor cells or to preserve neuronal cells exposed to specific oxidative conditions.
historical_context: In 2012, Scott Dixon, Brent Stockwell, and colleagues defined a novel, non-apoptotic form of regulated cell death which they designated "ferroptosis." Fueled by iron-dependent lipid peroxidation and mechanistically distinct from apoptosis, necrosis, and autophagy, ferroptosis is triggered by the inhibition of the cystine/glutamate antiporter (system xc−) or glutathione peroxidase 4 (GPX4). This discovery has been deeply influential. It carved out a brand-new paradigm in cell death research, exposing profound links between cellular metabolism, oxidative stress, and pathology. Ferroptosis is now intensely investigated as a fundamental driver in neurodegenerative diseases and ischemia-reperfusion injury, as well as a potent vulnerability to exploit in therapy-resistant cancers.

paper_title: Ferroptosis - An Iron-Dependent Form of Nonapoptotic Cell Death - 2012 - Cell
display_title: Ferroptosis - An Iron-Dependent Form of Nonapoptotic Cell Death
title: Ferroptosis - An Iron-Dependent Form of Nonapoptotic Cell Death
text:
The regulated elimination of cells is essential for development, tissue homeostasis, and the response to injury. Apoptosis has served as the principal model for genetically controlled cell death, and much is known about the caspase-dependent machinery that executes this process. Yet many cells die in ways that do not exhibit the canonical morphological or biochemical features of apoptosis. Such nonapoptotic death processes have been implicated in degenerative disease, ischemic injury, and tumor suppression, but in most cases the molecular events that initiate and execute these forms of death remain poorly understood.

The possibility that cancer cells might be selectively vulnerable to nonapoptotic death is of particular interest. Many tumors acquire mutations that disable apoptotic pathways, thereby limiting the efficacy of therapies that rely on the intact engagement of this machinery. We previously identified small molecules, termed RAS-selective lethal (RSL) compounds, that kill engineered tumor cells expressing oncogenic RAS while sparing matched isogenic cells lacking this oncogene. One such compound, erastin, induces a form of death that is not blocked by caspase inhibition and is associated with the accumulation of reactive oxygen species. These observations suggested that RSL compounds may reveal a regulated lethal mechanism distinct from apoptosis and exploitable in apoptosis-resistant cancer cells.

Oxidative stress has long been associated with cell death, but the specific chemical and genetic pathways that convert reactive oxygen species into lethal signals remain obscure. Iron is a particularly plausible mediator of oxidative damage because it can participate in redox reactions and is frequently dysregulated in cancer and in pathological states of the nervous system. Notably, several forms of glutamate-induced neurotoxicity are oxidative and iron dependent. In these settings, excess glutamate can inhibit cystine uptake through the cystine/glutamate antiporter system xc-, depleting cysteine-derived antioxidant capacity and sensitizing cells to oxidative damage. Whether such neurotoxic processes are mechanistically related to the death induced by RSL compounds has not been established.

Here, we define a form of regulated, iron-dependent, nonapoptotic cell death that we term ferroptosis. We show that erastin triggers the accumulation of lethal lipid reactive oxygen species, that this process is morphologically, biochemically, and genetically distinct from apoptosis, necrosis, and autophagy, and that it depends on a discrete set of genes linked to iron regulation, metabolism, and lipid biology. We identify ferrostatin-1 as a potent inhibitor of this process and show that it suppresses both erastin-induced death in cancer cells and glutamate-induced death in organotypic brain slices. Finally, we demonstrate that erastin inhibits system xc--mediated cystine uptake, connecting RSL-induced cancer cell death to an oxidative mechanism previously implicated in neurodegeneration.

This discovery has been deeply influential. It carved out a brand-new paradigm in cell death research, exposing profound links between cellular metabolism, oxidative stress, and pathology. Ferroptosis is now intensely investigated as a fundamental driver in neurodegenerative diseases and ischemia-reperfusion injury, as well as a potent vulnerability to exploit in therapy-resistant cancers.

text:
The regulated elimination of cells is essential for the development and maintenance of multicellular organisms, and its dysregulation is a hallmark of numerous human diseases. Apoptosis, the most extensively characterized form of programmed cell death, is driven by a cascade of caspase proteases and is critical for normal tissue turnover and the removal of damaged cells. The execution of apoptosis requires a specific genetic program, including the activation of proapoptotic Bcl-2 family members such as Bax and Bak. In cancer, the evasion of apoptosis is a fundamental mechanism of tumor progression and a major cause of resistance to conventional chemotherapeutics. Consequently, there is intense interest in identifying alternative, nonapoptotic pathways of cell death that could be exploited to selectively eradicate apoptosis-resistant cancer cells.

In addition to apoptosis, several other forms of regulated cell death have been described, including various forms of necrosis (such as necroptosis) and autophagic cell death. These processes are morphologically and biochemically distinct from apoptosis and are mediated by different molecular machineries. Recent high-throughput screening efforts have identified small molecules capable of inducing nonapoptotic death in specific genetic contexts. We previously discovered a structurally diverse class of lethal compounds, collectively termed RAS-selective lethal (RSL) compounds, which include erastin and RSL3. These molecules were initially identified for their ability to selectively kill human tumor cells harboring oncogenic mutations in the HRAS, KRAS, or NRAS genes. Subsequent studies revealed that RSL-induced cell death does not exhibit the classic features of apoptosis, such as caspase activation or chromatin condensation, nor does it require Bax or Bak.

Instead, RSL-induced cell death is characterized by its dependence on intracellular iron and can be completely suppressed by iron chelators. Furthermore, this form of death is associated with the accumulation of reactive oxygen species (ROS), suggesting an oxidative mechanism. However, the precise nature of this death pathway, its relationship to other known forms of cell death, and its broader physiological and pathological relevance remain poorly defined. In this study, we demonstrate that erastin triggers a unique, iron-dependent form of nonapoptotic cell death that we have termed "ferroptosis." We show that ferroptosis is morphologically, biochemically, and genetically distinct from apoptosis, necrosis, and autophagy. Through systematic modulatory profiling and genetic screening, we uncover the regulatory network governing ferroptosis and identify a potent small-molecule inhibitor of this process, ferrostatin-1. Our findings indicate that ferroptosis is caused by the lethal accumulation of lipid-based ROS resulting from the inhibition of cystine uptake and that this pathway may also underlie the excitotoxic cell death observed in certain neurodegenerative conditions.

Fueled by iron-dependent lipid peroxidation and mechanistically distinct from apoptosis, necrosis, and autophagy, ferroptosis is triggered by the inhibition of the cystine/glutamate antiporter (system xc−) or glutathione peroxidase 4 (GPX4). This discovery has been deeply influential. It carved out a brand-new paradigm in cell death research, exposing profound links between cellular metabolism, oxidative stress, and pathology. Ferroptosis is now intensely investigated as a fundamental driver in neurodegenerative diseases and ischemia-reperfusion injury, as well as a potent vulnerability to exploit in therapy-resistant cancers.
