paper_title: Ferroptosis - An Iron-Dependent Form of Nonapoptotic Cell Death - 2012 - Cell
display_title: Ferroptosis - An Iron-Dependent Form of Nonapoptotic Cell Death
title: Ferroptosis - An Iron-Dependent Form of Nonapoptotic Cell Death
text:
Ferroptotic death is morphologically, biochemically, and genetically distinct from apoptosis, various forms of necrosis, and autophagy. This process is characterized by the overwhelming, iron-dependent accumulation of lethal lipid ROS. Unlike other forms of apoptotic and nonapoptotic death, this requirement for ROS accumulation appears to be universal. In at least some cells, NOX family enzymes make important contributions to this process. Indeed, although we cannot exclude the possibility of a death-inducing protein or protein complex activated downstream of ROS accumulation, we posit that the executioners of death in cancer cells undergoing ferroptosis are these ROS themselves. An important prediction of this model is that, under anoxic conditions, ferroptosis will be inactive. However, even here, agents such as erastin that may prevent uptake of essential amino acids by system L are likely to be toxic to cells.

Using an shRNA library targeting most known genes encoding mitochondrial proteins, we identified specific roles for RPL8, IREB2, ATP5G3, TTC35, CS, and ACSF2 in erastin-induced ferroptosis. A plausible hypothesis to emerge from these data is that CS and ACSF2 are required to synthesize a specific lipid precursor necessary for death. Just as important, the high resolution of the arrayed approach (1 hairpin/well, minimum 5 hairpins/gene) provides confidence that the various mitochondrial genes not identified in our screen, including many implicated in apoptotic and other nonapoptotic death pathways (BID, BAK1, BAX, AIFM1, PPIF, HTRA2, ENDOG, and PGAM5), are truly not required for erastin-induced ferroptosis. This screening collection will be a valuable resource for future studies of the role of the mitochondria in cell physiology.

In cancer cells, inhibition of system xc--mediated cystine uptake by erastin, SAS, or glutamate may be sufficient to initiate iron-dependent ferroptosis. Inhibition of system xc- is, however, not necessary; RSL3 does not inhibit cystine uptake and yet triggers an otherwise similar iron- and ROS-dependent ferroptotic death program. Thus, RSL3 likely modulates the activity of a target lying downstream of or in parallel to system xc-. Importantly, this may enable RSL3 to activate ferroptosis in cells or conditions in which cystine uptake via system xc- is not limiting for survival. Lanperisone, another recently identified oncogenic RAS-selective lethal small molecule that causes nonapoptotic, iron-dependent death in mouse Kras mutant tumor cells, may also inhibit the function of system xc- or another target in the ferroptotic pathway. Other compounds that behave as RSLs, such as PEITC, oncrasin, and piperlongumine, trigger mitochondrial cytochrome c release, caspase activation, and other features of apoptosis not observed in cancer cells undergoing ferroptosis. Certain tumor cells are highly resistant to apoptosis. Thus, agents such as erastin, RSL3, and lanperisone that can trigger nonapoptotic death may exhibit a unique spectrum of clinical activity.

In some brain cell populations, inhibition of system xc- by glutamate triggers oxidative cell death dependent on iron and lipid ROS as well as Ca2+ influx, mitochondrial damage, mitochondrial ROS production, and chromatin fragmentation. These latter events, beginning with Ca2+ influx, are not required for RSL-induced ferroptosis in cancer cells, perhaps because heightened activity of NOX or other pro-oxidant enzymes or basally altered membrane lipid composition is sufficient to promote death in the absence of these additional features. Regardless, the oxidative death pathways triggered in cancer cells and brain cells by blockade of cystine uptake both appear to access a core iron- and ROS-dependent ferroptotic mechanism, accounting for the ability of Fer-1 and CPX to attenuate death in both cases.

The specific role of iron in ferroptosis remains unclear. Ferroptosis cannot be explained by a simple increase in H2O2-dependent, iron-catalyzed ROS production (i.e., Fenton chemistry), as H2O2-induced death is distinct from RSL-induced ferroptosis. Rather, our results are most consistent with one or more iron-dependent enzymes functioning as part of the core oxidative lethal mechanism. The void created in the antioxidant defenses of the cell by the inhibition of cystine uptake by erastin may be required to unleash the activity of these enzymes. Thus, for better or worse, the aberrantly elevated levels of iron that are observed in some cancer cells and pathological neuronal populations may predispose to ferroptotic death in situations of cystine or cysteine limitation.

Fueled by iron-dependent lipid peroxidation and mechanistically distinct from apoptosis, necrosis, and autophagy, ferroptosis is triggered by the inhibition of the cystine/glutamate antiporter (system xc−) or glutathione peroxidase 4 (GPX4). This discovery has been deeply influential. It carved out a brand-new paradigm in cell death research, exposing profound links between cellular metabolism, oxidative stress, and pathology. Ferroptosis is now intensely investigated as a fundamental driver in neurodegenerative diseases and ischemia-reperfusion injury, as well as a potent vulnerability to exploit in therapy-resistant cancers.

paper_title: Ferroptosis - An Iron-Dependent Form of Nonapoptotic Cell Death - 2012 - Cell
display_title: Ferroptosis - An Iron-Dependent Form of Nonapoptotic Cell Death
title: Ferroptosis - An Iron-Dependent Form of Nonapoptotic Cell Death
text:
The results presented here identify ferroptosis as a regulated form of cell death that is distinct from apoptosis, necrosis, and autophagy. Ferroptosis is initiated by a specific set of lethal stimuli, requires intracellular iron, and proceeds through the accumulation of oxidative damage, including lipid ROS. This process lacks the defining morphological and biochemical features of apoptosis and necrosis, is not suppressed by inhibitors of caspases, RIPK1, lysosomal function, or autophagy, and is governed by a genetic network that differs from that required for apoptotic death. Thus, ferroptosis expands the known repertoire of regulated cell death mechanisms and suggests that mammalian cells contain additional death programs that may be engaged under particular metabolic and pathological conditions.

Several observations suggest that ferroptosis is not simply nonspecific oxidative toxicity. First, erastin and RSL3 cluster together by modulatory profiling and are separated from many other oxidative and nonoxidative lethal agents. Second, erastin-induced death requires a discrete collection of genes, including IREB2, RPL8, ATP5G3, CS, TTC35, and ACSF2, whose silencing protects against erastin but not against diverse unrelated lethal stresses. Third, ferroptosis can be suppressed even several hours after erastin treatment by antioxidants, iron chelators, or MEK inhibition, indicating that cell death depends on the continued operation of a regulated oxidative process rather than an immediate catastrophic injury. These data support the idea that ferroptosis is a specific iron-dependent death pathway in which metabolic state, iron handling, and lipid oxidation converge.

Our data also define system xc- as a key upstream node in erastin-induced ferroptosis. Erastin, like glutamate and sulfasalazine, inhibits Na+-independent cystine uptake, and this blockade can be bypassed by beta-mercaptoethanol or attenuated by increased expression of SLC7A11. Because cystine import is required for cysteine availability and glutathione synthesis, inhibition of system xc- likely creates a deficiency in the antioxidant capacity of the cell. In this setting, iron-dependent reactions and ROS-generating enzymes can drive lipid oxidation beyond a survivable threshold. The ability of RSL3 to induce ferroptosis without inhibiting cystine uptake suggests that ferroptosis can be initiated at multiple points, either by disabling antioxidant defenses upstream or by perturbing downstream components that control lethal lipid oxidation.

The involvement of iron metabolism is a central feature of this pathway. Iron chelation suppresses both ROS accumulation and death, exogenous iron potentiates erastin lethality, and IREB2-dependent control of iron uptake, storage, and metabolism modulates sensitivity to ferroptosis. These findings suggest that a bioavailable iron pool is required to amplify or execute the oxidative reactions that kill cells. The precise chemical species responsible for this toxicity remain to be established, but the dependence on lipid ROS and the activity of Fer-1 are consistent with a model in which iron-dependent lipid peroxidation is a critical lethal event. The requirement for ACSF2, CS, and glutamine metabolism further suggests that the production or remodeling of specific lipid substrates may be important for sensitizing membranes to ferroptotic damage.

The identification of ferrostatin-1 provides both a probe for ferroptosis and a starting point for therapeutic exploration. Fer-1 is structurally and functionally distinct from several canonical antioxidants and suppresses ferroptosis with high potency. Structure-activity relationships indicate that both the aromatic amine groups and the lipophilic N-cyclohexyl moiety are important for activity, supporting the hypothesis that Fer-1 acts as a membrane-localized radical scavenger. The ability of Fer-1 to block glutamate-induced toxicity in organotypic hippocampal slice cultures suggests that ferroptosis-like mechanisms are not restricted to transformed cells and may operate in neural tissue exposed to excitotoxic or oxidative stress. This observation links a small-molecule-defined cancer cell death process to long-studied forms of iron-dependent neurotoxicity.

Ferroptosis may therefore have opposing therapeutic implications in cancer and degenerative disease. In tumors, especially those with oncogenic RAS or altered redox metabolism, induction of ferroptosis may provide a means to exploit metabolic vulnerabilities that are not addressed by apoptosis-inducing agents. The RAS-selective activity of erastin, the sensitivity of some cancer cells to system xc- inhibition, and the contribution of NOX and PPP-dependent ROS production all suggest that ferroptosis may be particularly relevant in cells with high oxidative burden or enhanced dependence on cystine import. Conversely, in pathological states such as stroke, epilepsy, trauma, or neurodegeneration, inhibition of ferroptosis may protect vulnerable cells from iron-dependent oxidative death.

Important questions remain. The molecular target of RSL3, the complete set of lipid species involved in ferroptotic execution, and the relative contribution of NOX enzymes, mitochondrial metabolism, and other ROS sources in different cell types require further definition. Likewise, it will be necessary to determine whether ferroptosis occurs in vivo under physiological or disease-relevant conditions and whether its modulation can be achieved with useful selectivity. Nonetheless, the present findings establish ferroptosis as a distinct, druggable, iron-dependent form of nonapoptotic cell death and provide a framework for understanding how perturbations in amino acid transport, antioxidant defense, iron metabolism, and lipid oxidation can be integrated into a regulated lethal process.

paper_title: Ferroptosis - An Iron-Dependent Form of Nonapoptotic Cell Death - 2012 - Cell
display_title: Ferroptosis - An Iron-Dependent Form of Nonapoptotic Cell Death
title: Ferroptosis - An Iron-Dependent Form of Nonapoptotic Cell Death
text:
The identification and characterization of ferroptosis as a distinct, iron-dependent form of nonapoptotic cell death introduces a new paradigm in our understanding of cellular demise. Historically, cell death has been broadly categorized into apoptosis, necrosis, and autophagy, each characterized by specific morphological features and biochemical pathways. Our findings clearly distinguish ferroptosis from these established pathways; it lacks the morphological hallmarks of apoptosis, does not involve caspase activation, and is not prevented by inhibitors of apoptosis, necrosis, or autophagy. Instead, ferroptosis is driven by an iron-dependent accumulation of lethal lipid reactive oxygen species (ROS), a process that can be specifically induced by small molecules like erastin and RSL3, and selectively inhibited by lipophilic antioxidants such as ferrostatin-1.

The discovery of this pathway provides a mechanistic explanation for the previously poorly understood cell death observed in cells harboring oncogenic RAS mutations when subjected to certain specific lethal compounds. Erastin, for instance, triggers ferroptosis by inhibiting the cystine/glutamate antiporter (system xc-), leading to depletion of intracellular glutathione and subsequent loss of function of glutathione peroxidase 4 (GPX4), a key lipid repair enzyme. The requirement for iron in this process, likely functioning as a catalyst for lipid peroxidation through Fenton chemistry or as a necessary cofactor for lipoxygenases, highlights a profound vulnerability in cellular lipid metabolism that can be pharmacologically exploited.

The implications of ferroptosis extend significantly beyond cancer biology. While the induction of ferroptosis offers a compelling strategy for the targeted elimination of specific tumor cells, particularly those that are resistant to apoptosis or conventional chemotherapies, the aberrant activation of ferroptosis may also play a critical role in various pathological states. We postulate that iron-dependent cell death could be a driving factor in neurodegenerative diseases, ischemic organ damage, and other conditions characterized by iron accumulation and oxidative stress. Consequently, inhibitors of ferroptosis, such as ferrostatin-1, may possess substantial therapeutic potential as neuroprotective or tissue-preserving agents.

Future research must focus on untangling the complete biochemical execution network of ferroptosis. Key questions remain regarding the specific lipid species that undergo peroxidation, the precise source and regulatory mechanisms of the reactive iron pool, and the downstream effectors that directly compromise membrane integrity leading to cell rupture. Furthermore, understanding the physiological role of ferroptosis, whether it serves as a natural tumor suppression mechanism or as a physiological response to specific environmental stresses, will be crucial.

Moreover, the characterization of genetic regulators that sensitize or protect cells from ferroptosis will be essential. Exploring the intersection between ferroptosis and other metabolic pathways, such as lipid biosynthesis and amino acid metabolism, will undoubtedly yield deeper insights into cellular homeostasis. Ultimately, mapping this complex network will facilitate the development of more precise and potent modulators of ferroptosis, expanding the therapeutic arsenal against a diverse array of intractable human diseases.

In conclusion, our work delineates ferroptosis as a unique and actionable cell death modality. By establishing the core characteristics and pharmacological modulators of this pathway, we provide a foundation for future investigations into its role in normal physiology and disease. The modulation of ferroptosis—either inducing it to treat cancer or inhibiting it to prevent degenerative diseases—represents a highly promising frontier in therapeutics, emphasizing the critical interplay between iron metabolism, redox biology, and cell survival.

paper_title: General Nature of the Genetic Code for Proteins - 1961 - Nature
display_title: General Nature of the Genetic Code for Proteins
title: General Nature of the Genetic Code for Proteins
text:
There is now a mass of indirect evidence which suggests that the amino-acid sequence along the polypeptide chain of a protein is determined by the sequence of the bases along some particular part of the nucleic acid of the genetic material. Since there are twenty common amino-acids found throughout Nature, but only four common bases, it has often been surmised that the sequence of the four bases is in some way a code for the sequence of the amino acids. In this article we report genetic experiments which, together with the work of others, suggest that the genetic code is of the following general type:
(a) A group of three bases (or, less likely, a multiple of three bases) codes one amino-acid.
(b) The code is not of the overlapping type.
(c) The sequence of the bases is read from a fixed starting point. This determines how the long sequences of bases are to be correctly read off as triplets. There are no special 'commas' to show how to select the right triplets. If the starting point is displaced by one base, then the reading into triplets is displaced, and thus becomes incorrect.
(d) The code is probably 'degenerate'; that is, in general, one particular amino-acid can be coded by one of several triplets of bases.
historical_context: In 1961, Francis Crick, Leslie Barnett, Sydney Brenner, and Richard Watts-Tobin published this foundational paper confirming the structural mechanics of the genetic code. Executing brilliant genetic experiments with bacteriophage T4 and proflavine-induced frameshift mutations, they rigorously deduced that the genetic code is read in non-overlapping triplets (codons) from a fixed starting point, and that it is degenerate, meaning multiple triplets can encode the same amino acid. This 1961 paper was a logical tour de force that definitively mapped the structural framework dictating how genetic information is physically translated into proteins.

paper_title: General Nature of the Genetic Code for Proteins - 1961 - Nature
display_title: General Nature of the Genetic Code for Proteins
title: General Nature of the Genetic Code for Proteins
text:
It is now generally supposed that the sequence of bases in the nucleic acid of a gene determines, by some rule or code, the sequence of amino-acids in the corresponding protein. The nature of this rule is still obscure. In particular, it is not known how many bases are required to specify one amino-acid, whether successive coding units overlap, or how the correct reading of the message is maintained along the length of the gene.

Several theoretical schemes have been proposed. Some require that neighbouring amino-acids should be specified by overlapping groups of bases. Others suppose that the message contains special punctuation marks, or that only certain groups of bases have meaning. A further possibility is that the message is read from a fixed point in successive groups, so that an addition or deletion of bases would disturb the reading of all following groups until a compensating alteration restored the proper register.

This last idea gives a simple genetic test. Mutations produced by acridines appear to add or remove bases, and such mutations should therefore be suppressible by a second nearby alteration of the opposite kind. We describe here experiments in the rII region of bacteriophage T4 which test this prediction, and which bear directly on the size and general character of the genetic code.

This 1961 paper was a logical tour de force that definitively mapped the structural framework dictating how genetic information is physically translated into proteins.

paper_title: General Nature of the Genetic Code for Proteins - 1961 - Nature
display_title: General Nature of the Genetic Code for Proteins
title: General Nature of the Genetic Code for Proteins
text:
The discovery that genetic information is encoded in the linear sequence of four nucleotide bases in deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA) posed the fundamental problem of how this sequence dictates the specific amino-acid sequences of proteins. The rules governing this translation constitute the genetic code. While DNA replication is well understood, the general nature of the genetic code has remained a subject of intense speculation.

Key questions include the number of bases required to specify an amino-acid (the coding ratio), whether the sequence is overlapping or non-overlapping, and how the reading frame is established along a continuous nucleotide strand. Recent studies on tobacco mosaic virus mutants and human haemoglobin variants suggest the code is non-overlapping, since single mutations typically alter only one amino-acid. If the code is non-overlapping, a mechanism must ensure the bases are read in the correct groupings. In an overlapping code, special "comma" sequences might separate words, but this restricts the allowed sequences. Alternatively, the sequence could be read sequentially from a fixed starting point. Here, we report genetic experiments on the rII region of bacteriophage T4 which provide compelling evidence that the genetic code is non-overlapping, is read from a fixed starting point in groups of three (or a multiple thereof), and is most likely degenerate, meaning multiple triplets can code for the same amino-acid.

This 1961 paper was a logical tour de force that definitively mapped the structural framework dictating how genetic information is physically translated into proteins.

paper_title: General Nature of the Genetic Code for Proteins - 1961 - Nature
display_title: General Nature of the Genetic Code for Proteins
title: General Nature of the Genetic Code for Proteins
text:
Our theory leads to one very clear prediction. Suppose one could examine the amino-acid sequence of the 'pseudo-wild' protein produced by one of our double mutants of the (+ with -) type. Conventional theory suggests that since the gene is only altered in two places, only two amino-acids would be changed. Our theory, on the other hand, predicts that a string of amino-acids would be altered, covering the region of the polypeptide chain corresponding to the region on the gene between the two mutants. A good protein on which to test this hypothesis is the lysozyme of the phage, at present being studied chemically by Dreyer and genetically by Streisinger.

At the recent Biochemical Congress at Moscow, the audience of Symposium I was startled by the announcement of Nirenberg that he and Matthaei had produced polyphenylalanine (that is, a polypeptide all the residues of which are phenylalanine) by adding polyuridylic acid (that is, an RNA the bases of which are all uracil) to a cell-free system which can synthesize protein. This implies that a sequence of uracils codes for phenylalanine, and our work suggests that it is probably a triplet of uracils.

It is possible by various devices, either chemical or enzymatic, to synthesize polyribonucleotides with defined or partly defined sequences. If these, too, will produce specific polypeptides, the coding problem is wide open for experimental attack, and in fact many laboratories, including our own, are already working on the problem. If the coding ratio is indeed 3, as our results suggest, and if the code is the same throughout Nature, then the genetic code may well be solved within a year.

This 1961 paper was a logical tour de force that definitively mapped the structural framework dictating how genetic information is physically translated into proteins.

paper_title: General Nature of the Genetic Code for Proteins - 1961 - Nature
display_title: General Nature of the Genetic Code for Proteins
title: General Nature of the Genetic Code for Proteins
text:
The experiments described here give, we believe, a simple interpretation of a rather diverse set of genetic facts. They suggest that the genetic message is read in successive groups from a fixed point, and that a displacement of this reading by the addition or loss of bases destroys the subsequent interpretation of the message. The restoration of function by a second mutation of the opposite kind, and especially by three mutations of like kind, is difficult to explain except on some such basis.

It should be emphasized that the evidence is not a direct chemical reading of the code. We have not identified the bases altered in any one mutant, nor have we shown the amino-acid changes in the presumed product of the B cistron. The argument therefore depends on the regularity of the genetic behaviour and on the assumption, now a very plausible one, that this cistron determines a polypeptide chain. Within these limits the results strongly favour a coding ratio of three, or conceivably a multiple of three, and they make the triplet interpretation the simplest one.

The same experiments also make improbable a comma system in which special bases or triplets mark the divisions between coding units. The facts are more naturally explained if the divisions are imposed by the starting point itself. A mutation which changes the number of bases then changes the reading frame, while a second compensating mutation can restore it beyond the intervening region. The pseudo-wild character of many double and triple mutants is just what would be expected if a short stretch of amino-acid sequence had been altered while the remainder of the chain was again correctly read.

Finally, the extent over which frame-shifted sequences may be tolerated argues that the code is unlikely to contain only twenty meaningful triplets. Some degeneracy is therefore probable. The exact dictionary of the code remains to be found, but its general form now appears experimentally accessible.

paper_title: General Nature of the Genetic Code for Proteins - 1961 - Nature
display_title: General Nature of the Genetic Code for Proteins
title: General Nature of the Genetic Code for Proteins
text:
The results presented here provide compelling evidence for the general nature of the genetic code, specifically supporting the hypothesis that it is a triplet code, read continuously from a fixed starting point. By analyzing the effects of acridine-induced mutations in the rII region of bacteriophage T4, we have demonstrated that these mutations likely represent the addition or deletion of a single base pair, which causes a shift in the reading frame of the genetic message. The ability to restore wild-type function by combining three mutations of the same sign (either three additions or three deletions) is a crucial finding; it strongly indicates that the fundamental coding unit, the codon, must consist of three nucleotides, or a multiple thereof.

Furthermore, our data indicate that the code is non-overlapping. If the code were overlapping, a single base change would be expected to alter multiple adjacent amino acids, which is inconsistent with biochemical evidence from other studies. The concept of a degenerate code is also supported by our findings, as it appears that multiple different triplets can code for the same amino acid, reducing the lethality of certain point mutations. The suppression of reading frame shifts by compensatory mutations further emphasizes the continuous nature of translation. These findings fundamentally shape our understanding of how genetic information is translated into protein structure, effectively ruling out overlapping codes and providing a clear framework for the future elucidation of the specific nucleotide assignments for each amino acid.

paper_title: Genome-Scale CRISPR-Cas9 Knockout Screening in Human Cells - 2014 - Science
display_title: Genome-Scale CRISPR-Cas9 Knockout Screening in Human Cells
title: Genome-Scale CRISPR-Cas9 Knockout Screening in Human Cells
text: The simplicity of programming the CRISPR-associated nuclease Cas9 to modify specific genomic loci suggests a new way to interrogate gene function on a genome-wide scale. We show that lentiviral delivery of a genome-scale CRISPR-Cas9 knockout (GeCKO) library targeting 18,080 genes with 64,751 unique guide sequences enables both negative and positive selection screening in human cells. First, we used the GeCKO library to identify genes essential for cell viability in cancer and pluripotent stem cells. Next, in a melanoma model, we screened for genes whose loss is involved in resistance to vemurafenib, a therapeutic that inhibits mutant protein kinase BRAF. Our highest-ranking candidates include previously validated genes NF1 and MED12 as well as novel hits NF2, CUL3, TADA2B, and TADA1. We observe a high level of consistency between independent guide RNAs targeting the same gene and a high rate of hit confirmation, demonstrating the promise of genome-scale screening with Cas9.
historical_context: In 2014, Ofir Shalem, Neville Sanjana, Feng Zhang, and colleagues (published back-to-back with a parallel study by Wang et al. from the Sabatini lab) proved that the CRISPR-Cas9 system could be effectively scaled to execute unbiased, genome-wide knockout screens in human cells. By deploying a massive library of single-guide RNAs (sgRNAs) via lentiviruses, they rapidly isolated genes essential for cell viability and drug resistance. This paper was a game-changer because it elevated CRISPR from a targeted editing tool into an industrial-scale discovery engine. text:

Genome-scale loss-of-function screens have provided a wealth of information in diverse model systems. Because the targeting specificity of Cas9 is conferred by short guide sequences, which can be easily generated at large scale by array-based oligonucleotide library synthesis, we sought to explore the potential of Cas9 for pooled genome-scale functional screening.

text:
The ability to perturb gene expression on a genome-wide scale is a foundational tool for uncovering gene function and identifying therapeutic targets in human cells. For the past decade, RNA interference (RNAi), primarily through short hairpin RNA (shRNA) libraries, has been the standard method for such large-scale functional screens. However, RNAi suffers from inherent limitations, including incomplete silencing of target genes and substantial off-target effects, which can confound the interpretation of screening results and obscure the identification of essential genes or novel drug resistance mechanisms.

The clustered regularly interspaced short palindromic repeats (CRISPR)-associated endonuclease Cas9 has recently emerged as a powerful technology for targeted genome editing. By simply altering the sequence of a short guide RNA (sgRNA), Cas9 can be programmed to induce double-strand breaks at specific genomic loci, generating loss-of-function mutations through error-prone non-homologous end joining. We hypothesized that the high efficiency and specificity of Cas9-mediated editing could be harnessed to conduct genome-wide knockout screens in human cells. Here, we present the development and application of a genome-scale CRISPR-Cas9 knockout (GeCKO) library, demonstrating its utility for identifying both essential genes and mechanisms of drug resistance.

It usurped RNA interference (RNAi) as the premier gold standard for functional genomics, empowering the precise, high-throughput discovery of novel drug targets and genetic dependencies underlying human disease.

title: Genome-Scale CRISPR-Cas9 Knockout Screening in Human Cells
text: GeCKO screening provides a mechanistically distinct method to RNAi for systematic perturbation of gene function. Whereas RNAi reduces protein expression by targeting RNA, GeCKO introduces loss-of-function mutations into genomic DNA. While some indel mutations are expected to maintain the reading frame, homozygous knockout yields high screening sensitivity, which is especially important in cases where incomplete knockdown retains gene function. In addition, RNAi is limited to transcripts, whereas Cas9:sgRNAs can target elements across the entire genome, including promoters, enhancers, introns, and intergenic regions. Furthermore, catalytically inactive mutants of Cas9 can be tethered to different functional domains to broaden the repertoire of perturbation modalities, including genome-scale gain-of-function screening using Cas9-activators and epigenetic modifiers. In the GeCKO screens presented here, the efficiency of complete knockout, the consistency of distinct sgRNAs, and the validation rate for top screen hits demonstrate the potential of Cas9:sgRNA-based technology to transform functional genomics.

paper_title: Genome-wide Association Study of 14,000 Cases of Seven Common Diseases and 3,000 Shared Controls - 2007 - Nature
display_title: Genome-wide Association Study of 14,000 Cases of Seven Common Diseases and 3,000 Shared Controls
title: Genome-wide Association Study of 14,000 Cases of Seven Common Diseases and 3,000 Shared Controls
text: There is increasing evidence that genome-wide association (GWA) studies represent a powerful approach to the identification of genes involved in common human diseases. We describe a joint GWA study (using the Affymetrix GeneChip 500K Mapping Array Set) undertaken in the British population, which has examined ~2,000 individuals for each of 7 major diseases and a shared set of ~3,000 controls. Case-control comparisons identified 24 independent association signals at P < 5 × 10⁻⁷: 1 in bipolar disorder, 1 in coronary artery disease, 9 in Crohn's disease, 3 in rheumatoid arthritis, 7 in type 1 diabetes and 3 in type 2 diabetes. On the basis of prior findings and replication studies thus-far completed, almost all of these signals reflect genuine susceptibility effects. We observed association at many previously identified loci, and found compelling evidence that some loci confer risk for more than one of the diseases studied. Across all diseases, we identified a large number of further signals (including 58 loci with single-point P values between 10⁻⁵ and 5 × 10⁻⁷) likely to yield additional susceptibility loci. The importance of appropriately large samples was confirmed by the modest effect sizes observed at most loci identified. This study thus represents a thorough validation of the GWA approach. It has also demonstrated that careful use of a shared control group represents a safe and effective approach to GWA analyses of multiple disease phenotypes; has generated a genome-wide genotype database for future studies of common diseases in the British population; and shown that, provided individuals with non-European ancestry are excluded, the extent of population stratification in the British population is generally modest. Our findings offer new avenues for exploring the pathophysiology of these important disorders. We anticipate that our data, results and software, which will be widely available to other investigators, will provide a powerful resource for human genetics.
historical_context: Published in 2007 by the Wellcome Trust Case Control Consortium, this massive, collaborative initiative launched the modern era of Genome-Wide Association Studies (GWAS). By aggressively analyzing the DNA of 17,000 individuals across seven major common diseases—including bipolar disorder, coronary heart disease, type 1 and 2 diabetes, and rheumatoid arthritis—using dense microarray genotyping, the researchers surfaced numerous robust, replicable genetic risk variants. It completely overhauled the field of complex trait genetics. It provided hard proof that common diseases are intensely polygenic (driven by many variants of small effect) and instituted the rigorous statistical thresholds and collaborative frameworks now required to map the genetic architecture of human disease.

paper_title: Genome-wide Association Study of 14,000 Cases of Seven Common Diseases and 3,000 Shared Controls - 2007 - Nature
display_title: Genome-wide Association Study of 14,000 Cases of Seven Common Diseases and 3,000 Shared Controls
title: Genome-wide Association Study of 14,000 Cases of Seven Common Diseases and 3,000 Shared Controls
text: Genome-wide association (GWA) studies offer a potentially powerful means of identifying genetic variants that contribute to common human disease, but their practical utility, design requirements and susceptibility to bias have required empirical assessment. We report the main experiment of the Wellcome Trust Case Control Consortium, comprising genome-wide analyses of 2,000 cases for each of seven major familial diseases--bipolar disorder, coronary artery disease, Crohn's disease, hypertension, rheumatoid arthritis, type 1 diabetes and type 2 diabetes--together with 3,000 shared controls from the 1958 British Birth Cohort and UK Blood Services. Samples were genotyped with the Affymetrix GeneChip 500K Mapping Array Set and called with CHIAMO, a new bayesian genotype-calling algorithm. After stringent quality control, 16,179 individuals and 469,557 SNPs were retained for association analyses, supplemented by bayesian tests, multilocus imputation and expanded reference group comparisons. The two control collections showed little evidence of systematic allele-frequency differences, supporting the use of common controls. Although geographical structure within Britain was detectable, its overall effect on association statistics was small after exclusion of individuals with appreciable non-European ancestry. We identified numerous strong disease associations, including signals at 9p21.3 for coronary artery disease, ATG16L1 and additional loci including IRGM, NKX2-3 and PTPN2 for Crohn's disease, shared autoimmune susceptibility regions involving IL2RA, PTPN2 and 12q24, and established and novel loci for type 1 and type 2 diabetes. Hypertension produced no association below the primary significance threshold, although several suggestive signals were observed. Across diseases, most convincing effects were modest in size and explained only a small fraction of familial aggregation. These data demonstrate the effectiveness of large GWA studies for discovering common disease loci, emphasize the importance of rigorous genotype and sample quality control, and indicate that still larger studies and extensive replication will be required to define the remaining genetic architecture of common disease.

title: Genome-wide Association Study of 14,000 Cases of Seven Common Diseases and 3,000 Shared Controls
text: Despite more than a decade of intensive research, the genetic basis of common human diseases remains largely unresolved. Linkage and candidate gene studies have yielded only a modest number of replicable findings, leaving most of the heritable contribution to common familial disorders unaccounted for. The convergence of three recent advances—the International HapMap resource, dense genotyping chips that interrogate hundreds of thousands of single nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs), and the assembly of large, well-characterized clinical sample collections—now makes adequately powered genome-wide association (GWA) studies feasible. Here we describe the main experiment of the Wellcome Trust Case Control Consortium (WTCCC): a GWA study of 2,000 cases for each of seven common diseases of major public health importance (bipolar disorder, coronary artery disease, Crohn's disease, hypertension, rheumatoid arthritis, type 1 diabetes and type 2 diabetes) together with 3,000 shared controls drawn from the 1958 British Birth Cohort and the UK Blood Services. All 17,000 samples were genotyped with the Affymetrix GeneChip 500K Mapping Array Set, and a new genotype-calling algorithm, CHIAMO, was developed to extract calls of high accuracy. After stringent quality control, 469,557 SNPs in 16,179 individuals were available for analysis. We identified 24 independent association signals at P < 5 × 10⁻⁷: 1 in bipolar disorder, 1 in coronary artery disease, 9 in Crohn's disease, none in hypertension, 3 in rheumatoid arthritis, 7 in type 1 diabetes and 3 in type 2 diabetes. On the basis of prior findings and replication studies thus far, almost all of these signals reflect genuine susceptibility effects. We observed loci that influence susceptibility to multiple autoimmune diseases and obtained suggestive evidence of a sex-differentiated effect in rheumatoid arthritis. Beyond these disease-specific findings, our study addresses important methodological issues affecting all GWA studies, including the choice of controls, the impact of population structure, sample-size requirements and improvements in genotype-calling and analytical methods. The variants identified are characterized by modest effect sizes, consistent with an allelic architecture in which few large effects, a handful of modest effects and many small effects together contribute to common disease risk, and they account for only a small fraction of overall familiality—pointing to the need for still larger studies, extensive fine-mapping and functional validation.

text: The genetic basis of common human diseases remains largely elusive despite extensive research. Genome-wide association (GWA) studies offer a powerful new approach to identify disease-susceptibility variants, driven by advances in the HapMap project, high-density genotyping arrays, and the assembly of large clinical cohorts. Here, the Wellcome Trust Case Control Consortium (WTCCC) presents the results of a massive GWA study involving 14,000 cases across seven major diseases—bipolar disorder, coronary artery disease, Crohn's disease, hypertension, rheumatoid arthritis, type 1 diabetes, and type 2 diabetes (2,000 cases each)—and a shared reference group of 3,000 controls. By genotyping over 500,000 single nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) and implementing rigorous quality control and novel calling algorithms, we robustly demonstrate the efficacy of the case-control design using a common control group. Among these, we replicated several known associations and discovered numerous novel loci, including regions on chromosomes 16p12 for bipolar disorder, 9p21.3 for coronary artery disease, and multiple new genes for Crohn's disease and type 1 diabetes. Notably, our results indicate that some susceptibility loci are shared across multiple autoimmune diseases. The identified variants typically confer modest disease risks, suggesting that the allelic architecture of common diseases involves many loci of small effect rather than a few of large effect. This underscores the necessity for large sample sizes in future studies. Our findings provide fundamental insights into the genetic architecture of complex traits, offer new avenues for investigating disease mechanisms, and firmly establish GWA studies as a robust methodology for dissecting the biological basis of common human diseases.

paper_title: Genome-wide Association Study of 14,000 Cases of Seven Common Diseases and 3,000 Shared Controls - 2007 - Nature
display_title: Genome-wide Association Study of 14,000 Cases of Seven Common Diseases and 3,000 Shared Controls
title: Genome-wide Association Study of 14,000 Cases of Seven Common Diseases and 3,000 Shared Controls
text:
Despite extensive research efforts for more than a decade, the genetic basis of common human diseases remains largely unknown. Although there have been some notable successes, linkage and candidate gene association studies have often failed to deliver definitive results. Yet the identification of the variants, genes and pathways involved in particular diseases offers a potential route to new therapies, improved diagnosis and better disease prevention. For some time it has been hoped that the advent of genome-wide association (GWA) studies would provide a successful new tool for unlocking the genetic basis of many of these common causes of human morbidity and mortality.

Three recent advances mean that GWA studies that are powered to detect plausible effect sizes are now possible. First, the International HapMap resource, which documents patterns of genome-wide variation and linkage disequilibrium in four population samples, greatly facilitates both the design and analysis of association studies. Second, the availability of dense genotyping chips, containing sets of hundreds of thousands of single nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) that provide good coverage of much of the human genome, means that for the first time GWA studies for thousands of cases and controls are technically and financially feasible. Third, appropriately large and well-characterized clinical samples have been assembled for many common diseases.

The Wellcome Trust Case Control Consortium (WTCCC) was formed with a view to exploring the utility, design and analyses of GWA studies. It brought together over 50 research groups from the UK that are active in researching the genetics of common human diseases, with expertise ranging from clinical, through genotyping, to informatics and statistical analysis. Here we describe the main experiment of the consortium: GWA studies of 2,000 cases and 3,000 shared controls for 7 complex human diseases of major public health importance—bipolar disorder (BD), coronary artery disease (CAD), Crohn's disease (CD), hypertension (HT), rheumatoid arthritis (RA), type 1 diabetes (T1D), and type 2 diabetes (T2D). Two further experiments undertaken by the consortium will be reported elsewhere: a GWA study for tuberculosis in 1,500 cases and 1,500 controls, sampled from The Gambia; and an association study of 1,500 common controls with 1,000 cases for each of breast cancer, multiple sclerosis, ankylosing spondylitis and autoimmune thyroid disease, all typed at around 15,000 mainly non-synonymous SNPs. By simultaneously studying seven diseases with differing aetiologies, we hoped to develop insights, not only into the specific genetic contributions to each of the diseases, but also into differences in allelic architecture across the diseases. A further major aim was to address important methodological issues of relevance to all GWA studies, such as quality control, design and analysis. In addition to our main association results, we address several of these issues below, including the choice of controls for genetic studies, the extent of population structure within Great Britain, sample sizes necessary to detect genetic effects of varying sizes, and improvements in genotype-calling algorithms and analytical methods.

paper_title: Genome-wide Association Study of 14,000 Cases of Seven Common Diseases and 3,000 Shared Controls - 2007 - Nature
display_title: Genome-wide Association Study of 14,000 Cases of Seven Common Diseases and 3,000 Shared Controls
title: Genome-wide Association Study of 14,000 Cases of Seven Common Diseases and 3,000 Shared Controls
text:
The identification of inherited factors that influence susceptibility to common disease has long been a central objective of human genetics. Family and twin studies have established substantial heritable components for many common disorders, yet, for most such diseases, progress in identifying the underlying variants has been slow. Linkage approaches have been highly successful for Mendelian disease, but have generally had limited power to detect the modest effects expected for common susceptibility alleles. Candidate-gene studies, although biologically motivated, have often been constrained by incomplete knowledge of disease mechanisms and by inadequate sample sizes, and many reported associations have failed to replicate.

Recent advances in genomic resources and genotyping technology have transformed the prospects for association analysis. Dense maps of single nucleotide polymorphisms, together with information about linkage disequilibrium from the International HapMap Project, now make it possible to survey a substantial fraction of common variation across the genome. At the same time, high-throughput array-based genotyping has enabled studies of a scale not previously feasible. These developments provide an empirical test of whether genome-wide association (GWA) studies can identify reproducible genetic effects for common diseases, and whether those effects will be sufficiently informative to illuminate pathophysiological pathways.

Several features of common disease genetics make large, carefully designed studies essential. The variants most likely to be detected by current platforms are common alleles of modest effect, requiring thousands of cases and controls for adequate power. Conversely, the examination of hundreds of thousands of markers creates a substantial burden of multiple testing and makes stringent quality control indispensable. Subtle differences in DNA handling, genotype calling, population ancestry or control ascertainment can generate apparent associations that are unrelated to disease biology. For these reasons, the success of GWA studies depends not only on marker density and sample size, but also on robust experimental design, uniform analysis procedures and independent evidence for replication.

The use of shared controls offers an efficient strategy for studying multiple diseases, but its reliability has not been extensively evaluated in genome-wide data. Such an approach could increase power and reduce cost, particularly for national collections in which large numbers of well-characterized disease samples are available. However, shared controls also raise several concerns, including the possibility of disease misclassification within the reference group, differences between control collections, and confounding by population structure. These issues are particularly relevant in populations, such as that of Great Britain, that are often treated as relatively homogeneous but nevertheless contain detectable geographical variation in allele frequencies.

Here we report a large collaborative GWA study of seven common familial diseases: bipolar disorder, coronary artery disease, Crohn's disease, hypertension, rheumatoid arthritis, type 1 diabetes and type 2 diabetes. For each disease we genotyped approximately 2,000 cases and compared them with a shared set of approximately 3,000 controls drawn from two British control collections. We used the Affymetrix GeneChip 500K Mapping Array Set, developed a genotype-calling algorithm for the project, and applied extensive quality-control and analytical procedures. The study was designed both to identify susceptibility loci for these diseases and to assess general methodological questions concerning power, population structure, shared controls and the allelic architecture of common human disease.

It completely overhauled the field of complex trait genetics. It provided hard proof that common diseases are intensely polygenic (driven by many variants of small effect) and instituted the rigorous statistical thresholds and collaborative frameworks now required to map the genetic architecture of human disease.

paper_title: Genome-wide Association Study of 14,000 Cases of Seven Common Diseases and 3,000 Shared Controls - 2007 - Nature
display_title: Genome-wide Association Study of 14,000 Cases of Seven Common Diseases and 3,000 Shared Controls
title: Genome-wide Association Study of 14,000 Cases of Seven Common Diseases and 3,000 Shared Controls
text:
Common, complex diseases such as coronary artery disease, diabetes, and autoimmune disorders place a massive burden on global public health. Unlike rare Mendelian disorders, where single highly penetrant mutations cause the disease, the genetic architecture of complex diseases is thought to involve numerous sequence variants, each contributing a relatively modest effect to the overall risk. For many years, the search for these susceptibility genes relied largely on linkage analysis in affected families or candidate gene association studies. While linkage analysis has successfully identified genetic determinants for many rare monogenic traits, it has generally lacked the power to resolve the complex, polygenic basis of common diseases. Conversely, candidate gene studies have been plagued by a lack of reproducibility and the fundamental limitation of relying on prior, often incomplete, biological hypotheses.

The completion of the Human Genome Project, coupled with the detailed mapping of human genetic variation and linkage disequilibrium patterns by the International HapMap Project, has paved the way for a more systematic and unbiased approach to gene discovery: the genome-wide association (GWA) study. By utilizing high-density microarrays to interrogate hundreds of thousands of single nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) across the genome in large cohorts of cases and controls, GWA studies promise to identify novel susceptibility loci without requiring a priori knowledge of disease mechanisms.

Despite the theoretical promise of GWA studies, their practical implementation has been accompanied by significant methodological and logistical challenges. Key concerns have included the requirement for very large sample sizes to achieve adequate statistical power to detect variants with small effect sizes, the potential for confounding due to hidden population stratification, and the formidable costs and quality control issues associated with generating and analyzing millions of genotypes. Furthermore, the use of a shared control group across multiple disease phenotypes, a strategy that offers considerable efficiencies, has been debated due to the potential for misclassification bias.

To address these challenges and to comprehensively explore the genetic basis of common diseases, the Wellcome Trust Case Control Consortium (WTCCC) was established. Here, we present the results of a joint GWA study examining approximately 2,000 individuals for each of seven major diseases—bipolar disorder, coronary artery disease, Crohn's disease, hypertension, rheumatoid arthritis, type 1 diabetes, and type 2 diabetes—alongside a shared set of 3,000 controls drawn from the British population. This study represents one of the largest and most comprehensive GWA efforts to date. We demonstrate that with rigorous quality control, appropriate sample sizes, and careful consideration of population structure, GWA studies can successfully identify novel, robustly replicating susceptibility loci, offering profound new insights into the biology and genetic architecture of common human diseases.

It completely overhauled the field of complex trait genetics. It provided hard proof that common diseases are intensely polygenic (driven by many variants of small effect) and instituted the rigorous statistical thresholds and collaborative frameworks now required to map the genetic architecture of human disease.

paper_title: Genome-wide Association Study of 14,000 Cases of Seven Common Diseases and 3,000 Shared Controls - 2007 - Nature
display_title: Genome-wide Association Study of 14,000 Cases of Seven Common Diseases and 3,000 Shared Controls
title: Genome-wide Association Study of 14,000 Cases of Seven Common Diseases and 3,000 Shared Controls
text:
We have studied seven common familial diseases by genome-wide association analysis in 16,179 individuals. Our findings inform understanding of the genetic basis of the diseases concerned and provide methodological insights relevant to the pursuit of GWA studies in general.

A simple but important observation is that GWA analysis provides a highly effective approach for exploring the genetic underpinnings of common familial diseases. Our yield of novel, highly significant association findings is comparable to, or exceeds, the number of those hitherto-generated by candidate gene or positional cloning efforts. For many of the compelling signals, replication has already been obtained. Additional findings of particular interest include the identification of several loci that seem to influence susceptibility to multiple autoimmune diseases, and the suggestion of a novel locus for RA which shows sex-specific effects.

Our study enables us to make several general recommendations relevant to GWA studies. The first relates to the importance of careful quality control. In such large data sets, small systematic differences can readily produce effects capable of obscuring the true associations being sought. We implemented extensive quality control checks to minimize differences in sample DNA concentration, quality and handling procedures and combined a new genotype-calling algorithm with a set of filtering heuristics. systematic visual inspection of cluster plots for SNPs of interest remains an integral part of the quality control process.

The potential for population structure to undermine inferences in case-control association studies has long been debated but limited empirical data have been available to assess the issue. Our study highlighted several loci which demonstrate substantial geographical variation in allele frequencies across Britain, most probably due to natural selection in ancestral populations. Outside these loci, the effects of population structure are relatively minor, and do not represent a major source of confounding, provided that individuals with appreciable non-European ancestry are excluded. This finding reinforces the logistical and economic benefits of the case-control design over alternatives.

Our study allowed us to address another important methodological issue: the adequacy of using a common set of controls. our study demonstrates that, within the context of large-scale genetic association studies, for British populations at least, this concern has been overstated. The loss of power due to misclassification bias is modest unless the trait of interest is very common. the present study provides a compelling case for both the suitability and efficiency of the common control design in Britain and warrants its serious consideration elsewhere.

In terms of general biological insights, the most profound relate to inferences about the allelic architecture of common traits. The novel variants we have uncovered are characterized by modest effect size and even these estimates are likely to be inflated. We identified no additional common variants of very large effect. The observed distribution of effect sizes is consistent with models that suggest that, for any given trait, there will be few large effects, a handful of modest effects and a substantial number of genes generating small or very small increases in disease risk.

There are several important corollaries. most of the susceptibility effects yet to be uncovered for these diseases are likely to have effects of similar or smaller magnitude to those we have highlighted. Beyond the signals with the strongest evidence for association, there will be many additional susceptibility variants for which the WTCCC provides some evidence, but for which extensive replication will be required. even variants of very small effect can offer fundamental biological insights.

The patterns of allelic architecture uncovered mean that replication efforts will need to feature comparably large sample sizes. Caution is required in reaching negative conclusions on the basis of a single failed attempt at replication, or any set of replication attempts that are inadequately powered.

One of our major design considerations was sample size. our results suggest that such large sample sizes were necessary. Even with 2,000 cases and 3,000 controls, adequate power is restricted to common variants of relatively large effect. the larger the study sample, the more loci can be expected to reach threshold significance values. indeed, there are strong grounds for the prosecution of GWA studies on an even larger scale than ours, and, wherever possible, combining the results from existing GWA scans performed for the same trait.

In our study, T1D and CD, the conditions showing strongest familial aggregation generated the largest number of highly significant associations. it is important to recognize that the association signals so far identified account for only a small proportion of overall familiality. There is a disparity in scale between the modest locus-specific λₛ effects attributable to the identified associations and the estimates of overall familiality. These estimates demonstrate the limited potential of the variants thus far identified to provide clinically useful prediction of disease.

The identification and characterization of the aetiological variants that underlie replicated associations will necessitate extensive fine-mapping and functional validation. We view the WTCCC study and data set as an important first step towards harnessing the powerful molecular genomic tools now available to dissect the biological basis of common disease and translating those findings into improvements in human health.
historical_context: Published in 2007 by the Wellcome Trust Case Control Consortium, this massive, collaborative initiative launched the modern era of Genome-Wide Association Studies (GWAS). By aggressively analyzing the DNA of 17,000 individuals across seven major common diseases—including bipolar disorder, coronary heart disease, type 1 and 2 diabetes, and rheumatoid arthritis—using dense microarray genotyping, the researchers surfaced numerous robust, replicable genetic risk variants. It completely overhauled the field of complex trait genetics. It provided hard proof that common diseases are intensely polygenic (driven by many variants of small effect) and instituted the rigorous statistical thresholds and collaborative frameworks now required to map the genetic architecture of human disease.

paper_title: Genome-wide Association Study of 14,000 Cases of Seven Common Diseases and 3,000 Shared Controls - 2007 - Nature
display_title: Genome-wide Association Study of 14,000 Cases of Seven Common Diseases and 3,000 Shared Controls
title: Genome-wide Association Study of 14,000 Cases of Seven Common Diseases and 3,000 Shared Controls
text:
The results presented here provide strong support for the view that genome-wide association studies can identify reproducible susceptibility loci for common human diseases, provided that the studies are conducted at sufficient scale and with appropriate attention to sample and genotype quality. Across seven clinically distinct disorders we identified 24 independent signals exceeding stringent thresholds for association, most of which are supported by previous observations, biological plausibility or replication studies completed during the course of the project. These findings extend the known genetic architecture of several common diseases, and demonstrate that common variants of modest effect can be detected reliably in population-based case-control designs.

A striking feature of the data is the generally modest magnitude of the effects observed. With the exception of a small number of established loci, most susceptibility variants identified here confer odds ratios well below those expected from earlier candidate gene and linkage studies. This observation has two important implications. First, large sample collections are essential for robust discovery, and many true associations will remain undetected in studies of smaller size. Second, the variants so far identified explain only a fraction of familial aggregation for these disorders. The remaining genetic contribution is likely to reflect additional common variants of still smaller effect, rare variants not well captured by current arrays, structural variation, gene-gene and gene-environment interactions, and more complex effects within already identified loci. Thus, although the present study validates the GWA strategy, it also indicates that larger studies and complementary approaches will be required for a more complete account of inherited susceptibility.

The study also provides evidence that shared control samples can be used effectively in genome-wide analyses of multiple diseases. The two control collections differed in ascertainment and DNA handling, but showed little evidence of systematic allele frequency differences after geographical stratification and quality control. Combining them increased power without introducing detectable bias in the main analyses. Concerns about the inclusion of undiagnosed or future cases among controls are legitimate, particularly for common adult-onset disorders, but the expected reduction in power is modest under plausible assumptions and is outweighed by the gain from a larger reference sample. These results support the use of well-characterized shared control resources where disease collections have comparable ancestry and rigorous checks for technical and population differences are applied.

Population structure within Great Britain was detectable, but its influence on the primary association results was limited after exclusion of individuals with recent non-European ancestry. The observed north-west to south-east gradients and regions with strong geographical differentiation illustrate that even apparently homogeneous populations contain subtle structure. Such structure is of evolutionary and biological interest, particularly where it implicates loci involved in immune response, metabolism or host-pathogen interactions. Nevertheless, the modest genomic inflation observed in the disease scans suggests that population stratification is unlikely to account for the strongest associations reported. Future studies in more heterogeneous populations, or in designs involving more localized case recruitment, will require careful assessment and may need explicit correction for ancestry.

Several biological themes emerge from the association signals. In Crohn's disease, the convergence of ATG16L1, IRGM, IL23R and related loci points to the importance of microbial handling, autophagy and inflammatory regulation in disease pathogenesis. In type 1 diabetes and rheumatoid arthritis, multiple signals implicate immune signalling pathways, particularly those involving antigen presentation, lymphocyte activation and cytokine-mediated regulation. The overlap between autoimmune diseases is particularly notable. Variants near PTPN2, IL2RA and the chromosome 12q24 region, together with established effects at the MHC and PTPN22, indicate that some components of autoimmune susceptibility are shared, whereas others remain disease-specific. This pattern is consistent with a model in which common regulatory pathways influence immune tolerance or activation, while tissue-specific factors shape the clinical phenotype.

For metabolic and cardiovascular disease, the findings likewise point to both expected and unexpected mechanisms. The association between T2D and FTO appears to operate through adiposity, emphasizing the value of genetic studies for dissecting intermediate phenotypes. Signals at CDKAL1, TCF7L2 and related loci highlight beta-cell function as a central component of T2D susceptibility. The CAD association at 9p21.3 is especially compelling, because of the strength of the signal and because the region contains genes involved in cell-cycle control and lies near a locus also implicated in T2D. The relationship between the CAD and T2D signals in this region remains to be resolved, but the finding illustrates how unbiased genome-wide scans can reveal mechanisms not anticipated from conventional risk factor biology.

Some diseases yielded fewer strong associations than others. Hypertension, in particular, produced no signal exceeding our primary threshold, despite a distribution of weaker signals comparable to those seen in the other scans. This may reflect smaller effect sizes, incomplete tagging of relevant variants, greater phenotypic heterogeneity, or misclassification among controls. Bipolar disorder also yielded a single strong signal for which replication is important. These observations should not be interpreted as evidence against a genetic contribution, but rather as evidence that different disorders may require different sample sizes, phenotypic definitions and analytical strategies before robust loci can be detected.

The present work has several limitations. The Affymetrix 500K array captures only a subset of common variation and has limited power for rare alleles, poorly tagged loci and structural variants. Association signals do not by themselves identify causal variants or genes, particularly in regions of extensive linkage disequilibrium. The case collections were designed to maximize power and diagnostic reliability, but differences in ascertainment may influence the spectrum of variants detected. Most analyses focused on single-locus effects, and the study was not powered to provide a comprehensive evaluation of interactions.

Despite these limitations, the data establish a framework for future studies. Dense genotyping, imputation, larger collaborative samples, replication in independent cohorts and functional follow-up will be required to refine loci and identify causal mechanisms. The availability of the data, results and analytical tools from this project should accelerate such work. More broadly, the findings show that systematic genome-wide analysis can transform the study of common disease genetics from a largely hypothesis-driven enterprise into one capable of revealing unanticipated biological pathways and shared mechanisms across diverse disorders.

title: Genome-wide Association Study of 14,000 Cases of Seven Common Diseases and 3,000 Shared Controls
text:
The findings of this genome-wide association study (GWAS) represent a watershed moment in the genetic analysis of complex human diseases. By analyzing 14,000 cases across seven major diseases—bipolar disorder, coronary artery disease, Crohn's disease, hypertension, rheumatoid arthritis, type 1 diabetes, and type 2 diabetes—alongside 3,000 shared controls, we have successfully demonstrated the power and feasibility of large-scale, systematic scans to uncover the genetic architecture of common traits. The identification of numerous novel susceptibility loci across these disparate diseases validates the core hypothesis of the GWAS approach: that common genetic variants with modest effect sizes play a significant role in the etiology of complex diseases, and that these variants can be robustly identified given sufficient sample size and marker density.

Our methodological design, particularly the use of a shared control group for multiple distinct disease cohorts, proved to be highly efficient and statistically sound. This approach substantially reduced the genotyping burden while maximizing statistical power, demonstrating a cost-effective model for future large-scale genetic studies. The rigorous quality control procedures implemented to address potential artifacts, such as population stratification and differential genotyping call rates, were crucial in ensuring the reliability of the observed associations. The high degree of replication of previously established loci provides further confidence in the novel signals identified in this study.

One of the most striking observations is the robust identification of novel loci that point towards previously unsuspected biological pathways. For example, the discovery of new loci associated with Crohn's disease, such as the IL23R and ATG16L1 genes, highlights the critical involvement of specific inflammatory and autophagic pathways in its pathogenesis. Similarly, the identification of novel loci for coronary artery disease and type 2 diabetes opens new avenues for investigating the underlying metabolic and vascular dysfunction. These findings underscore the unbiased nature of GWAS, which, unlike candidate gene studies, does not rely on a priori assumptions about disease mechanisms, thereby enabling genuine biological discovery.

However, the results also highlight the complexity of the genetic landscape. The newly identified loci typically confer relatively small increases in relative risk, often in the range of 1.2 to 1.5. This confirms that the genetic architecture of these common diseases is highly polygenic, involving the cumulative effect of many variants rather than a few major genes. While the individual predictive value of these common variants is currently limited, their true value lies in their ability to pinpoint critical biological pathways and potential therapeutic targets. The functional characterization of these loci, many of which reside in non-coding regulatory regions, represents the next major challenge in the field.

Furthermore, it is clear that the common variants identified thus far explain only a fraction of the estimated heritability for these diseases. This 'missing heritability' suggests that other classes of genetic variation, such as rare variants with larger effect sizes, structural variations, or complex gene-gene and gene-environment interactions, remain to be discovered. Future studies utilizing next-generation sequencing technologies and larger, more diverse cohorts will be essential to capture the full spectrum of genetic variation contributing to disease risk.

In conclusion, this comprehensive study definitively establishes genome-wide association as a powerful tool for dissecting the genetics of complex diseases. The novel loci discovered here provide critical new insights into disease pathogenesis and offer a foundation for functional studies that will ultimately drive the development of improved diagnostics and targeted therapeutics. The success of this collaborative effort also emphasizes the necessity of large consortia and data sharing in modern genetic research, setting a standard for future investigations into the genetic basis of human health and disease. Moreover, as we transition from locus discovery to functional validation, integrating these genetic findings with transcriptomic, proteomic, and epigenomic data will be paramount. Such multi-omic approaches will be required to decipher the precise mechanisms by which non-coding variants influence gene expression and cellular function. Ultimately, the comprehensive map of disease-associated variants generated by studies of this magnitude provides the essential scaffolding upon which personalized medicine will be built, transforming our approach to disease prevention, risk stratification, and tailored treatment strategies.

paper_title: High Burden and Pervasive Positive Selection of Somatic Mutations in Normal Human Skin - 2015 - Science
display_title: High Burden and Pervasive Positive Selection of Somatic Mutations in Normal Human Skin
title: High Burden and Pervasive Positive Selection of Somatic Mutations in Normal Human Skin
text: How somatic mutations accumulate in normal cells is central to understanding cancer development but is poorly understood. We performed ultradeep sequencing of 74 cancer genes in small (0.8 to 4.7 square millimeters) biopsies of normal skin. Across 234 biopsies of sun-exposed eyelid epidermis from four individuals, the burden of somatic mutations averaged two to six mutations per megabase per cell, similar to that seen in many cancers, and exhibited characteristic signatures of exposure to ultraviolet light. Remarkably, multiple cancer genes are under strong positive selection even in physiologically normal skin, including most of the key drivers of cutaneous squamous cell carcinomas. Positively selected mutations were found in 18 to 32% of normal skin cells at a density of ~140 driver mutations per square centimeter. We observed variability in the driver landscape among individuals and variability in the sizes of clonal expansions across genes. Thus, aged sun-exposed skin is a patchwork of thousands of evolving clones with over a quarter of cells carrying cancer-causing mutations while maintaining the physiological functions of epidermis.
historical_context: In 2015, Iñigo Martincorena, Peter Campbell, and colleagues published a startling discovery regarding the genetic reality of normal human tissue. By deeply sequencing small biopsies of sun-exposed, histologically normal eyelid skin, they proved that healthy human skin carries a staggering burden of somatic mutations. Astoundingly, nearly 25% of normal skin cells harbored known cancer-driving mutations (such as in NOTCH1 or TP53) and had formed thousands of microscopic clonal expansions. This work upended traditional models of cancer initiation and aging. It proved that the steady accumulation of cancer-associated mutations is a pervasive, standard aspect of aging, forcing the field to pivot toward understanding how human tissues successfully suppress tumorigenesis despite a landscape saturated with heavily mutated cellular clones.

paper_title: High Burden and Pervasive Positive Selection of Somatic Mutations in Normal Human Skin - 2015 - Science
display_title: High Burden and Pervasive Positive Selection of Somatic Mutations in Normal Human Skin
title: High Burden and Pervasive Positive Selection of Somatic Mutations in Normal Human Skin
text: Current models of cancer evolution assume that driver mutations arise in rare normal cells and expand only after malignant transformation, but the extent of such processes in physiologically normal tissues is poorly defined. We analyzed 234 small biopsies of sun-exposed eyelid epidermis from four individuals by ultradeep sequencing of 74 cancer genes, with whole-genome sequencing of one highly mutated clone. Normal skin carried thousands of somatic mutations with ultraviolet-light signatures and a burden of two to six coding substitutions per megabase per cell. Despite normal histology, many mutations were under strong positive selection. NOTCH1, NOTCH2, NOTCH3, FAT1, TP53, RBM10, and FGFR3 showed evidence of driver activity, and biallelic NOTCH1 inactivation was already frequent. Approximately one quarter of cells carried NOTCH mutations, and hundreds of mutant clones populated each square centimeter of aged skin. Driver clones were usually small, suggesting constraints on expansion, whereas FGFR3-mutant benign-associated clones could be large. Thus normal human skin is a dense mosaic of competing, positively selected clones, revealing early cancer evolution before malignancy.

title: High Burden and Pervasive Positive Selection of Somatic Mutations in Normal Human Skin
text: How somatic mutations accumulate in normal cells before cancer arises has remained largely unobservable, obscured by the polyclonal architecture of healthy tissue. To directly characterize this earliest stage of tumor evolution, we performed ultradeep sequencing of 74 cancer-associated genes across 234 small biopsies of physiologically normal, sun-exposed eyelid epidermis from four individuals, complemented by whole-genome sequencing of one clonal biopsy. We identified 3760 somatic mutations, with a spectrum dominated by ultraviolet-induced damage, and detected clones ranging from 0.01 to several square millimeters in size. Aged sun-exposed skin carried a striking mutation burden of two to six substitutions per megabase per cell, approaching that of many adult cancers. Using a context-aware dN/dS framework, we found pervasive positive selection acting on NOTCH1, NOTCH2, NOTCH3, FAT1, TP53, RBM10, and FGFR3, with biallelic NOTCH1 inactivation already common in normal cells. On average, every square centimeter of aged eyelid skin harbored around 140 clones carrying driver mutations. These results reveal that physiologically normal skin is a battleground of competing mutant clones, with the selective forces and driver landscape closely mirroring those of cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma.

title: High Burden and Pervasive Positive Selection of Somatic Mutations in Normal Human Skin
text: Cancer evolution is driven by the accumulation of somatic mutations, but the early stages of this process in normal tissues remain largely obscured. To investigate the burden, mutational signatures, and clonal architecture of somatic mutations in normal human tissue, we performed ultradeep targeted sequencing of 74 cancer genes in 234 small biopsies of physiologically normal, sun-exposed eyelid epidermis from four individuals.
