We estimate that over 25% of normal skin cells carry driver mutations in NOTCH genes, with NOTCH1 alone mutated in 14-21% of cells. These findings reveal that physiologically normal sun-exposed skin is a patchwork of thousands of evolving clones carrying oncogenic driver mutations, illuminating a vast, pre-malignant landscape that fundamentally alters our understanding of the earliest stages of cancer development.

title: High Burden and Pervasive Positive Selection of Somatic Mutations in Normal Human Skin
text:
The standard narrative of tumor evolution depicts the accumulation of driver mutations in cancer genes, causing waves of expansion of progressively more disordered clones. Central to this model is the presumption that randomly distributed somatic mutations must accumulate in normal cells before transformation, but directly observing them has proved challenging due to the polyclonal composition of normal tissue. Retrospective reconstructions of clonal evolution from sequencing of tumors give only partial insights, leaving us with fundamental gaps in our understanding of the earliest stages of cancer development. Critical but largely unanswered questions include the burden of somatic mutations in normal cells, which mutational processes are operative in normal tissues, the extent of positive selection among competing clones within an organ, and the patterns of clonal expansion induced by the very first driver mutations. These questions have been partially addressed in blood cells, where somatic mutations, including some driver mutations, have been found to accumulate at a low rate with increasing age.

To study the burden, mutational processes and clonal architecture of somatic mutations in normal non-hematological tissue, we focused on sun-exposed skin. Previous studies have reported the existence of clonal patches of skin cells carrying TP53 mutations. Motivated by this, we designed a sequencing strategy capable of detecting such clones by performing ultradeep sequencing of small biopsies and adapting algorithms to detect mutations in a small fraction of cells. We used eyelid epidermis because of its relatively high levels of sun exposure and because it is one of the few body sites to have normal skin excised (blepharoplasty). This procedure is performed for age-related loss of elasticity of the underlying dermis, which can cause eyelid drooping sometimes severe enough to occlude vision, although the epidermis remains physiologically and histologically normal. From four individuals undergoing bilateral blepharoplasty, we obtained the resected eyelids, all of which had normal epidermis free of macroscopic lesions. The donors, three female and one male, ranged from 55 to 73 years of age and had variable histories of sun exposure. Three were of Western European origin and one was of South Asian origin. We separated the underlying dermis and took multiple biopsies of the epidermis from each eyelid. In total, 234 biopsies of 0.79 to 4.71 mm2 in area were analyzed. We sequenced the coding exons of 74 genes implicated in skin and other cancers to an average effective coverage of 500x. We also performed whole-genome sequencing to ~147x depth on one biopsy in which a predominant clone was found by the targeted gene screen.
historical_context: In 2015, Iñigo Martincorena, Peter Campbell, and colleagues published a startling discovery regarding the genetic reality of normal human tissue. By deeply sequencing small biopsies of sun-exposed, histologically normal eyelid skin, they proved that healthy human skin carries a staggering burden of somatic mutations. Astoundingly, nearly 25% of normal skin cells harbored known cancer-driving mutations (such as in NOTCH1 or TP53) and had formed thousands of microscopic clonal expansions. This work upended traditional models of cancer initiation and aging. It proved that the steady accumulation of cancer-associated mutations is a pervasive, standard aspect of aging, forcing the field to pivot toward understanding how human tissues successfully suppress tumorigenesis despite a landscape saturated with heavily mutated cellular clones.

paper_title: High Burden and Pervasive Positive Selection of Somatic Mutations in Normal Human Skin - 2015 - Science
display_title: High Burden and Pervasive Positive Selection of Somatic Mutations in Normal Human Skin
title: High Burden and Pervasive Positive Selection of Somatic Mutations in Normal Human Skin
text:
Cancer arises through the somatic evolution of normal cells. Mutations acquired during life generate genetic diversity within tissues, and those that confer a proliferative or survival advantage can expand clonally. The established cancers that are usually sequenced represent the end products of this process, after many rounds of mutation, selection, and tissue remodeling. Much less is known about the earliest phases of tumor evolution: how many mutations are present in histologically normal cells, whether cancer genes are already under selection before malignant transformation, and how large the resulting clones become while the tissue continues to function normally.

Normal human tissues are not genetically homogeneous. Clonal expansions have been described in blood, esophagus, liver, and skin, and age-associated mosaicism is increasingly recognized as a general feature of somatic biology. However, measuring the full burden and selective consequences of mutations in normal tissues remains technically difficult. Most mutant clones are small, and their variants are diluted among neighboring wild-type cells. Standard sequencing approaches either lack the sensitivity to detect such low-frequency mutations or require prior knowledge of specific lesions. As a result, the relationship between the mutational landscape of normal tissue and that of its corresponding cancers has remained uncertain.

Skin provides a particularly tractable system in which to study these questions. The epidermis is continually renewed, is directly exposed to ultraviolet light, and gives rise to common cancers whose genomes bear strong signatures of sunlight-induced DNA damage. Previous studies using immunohistochemistry and targeted assays identified patches of normal-appearing skin carrying mutations in genes such as TP53, suggesting that cancer-associated clones may be widespread long before tumors appear. Yet these approaches could not determine, in an unbiased manner, the density of driver mutations, the spectrum of genes under selection, or the extent to which normal clonal competition resembles that seen in cutaneous malignancy.

Here we used ultradeep sequencing of cancer-associated genes in small biopsies of physiologically normal, sun-exposed human eyelid epidermis to quantify somatic mutation and selection at high spatial resolution. By detecting low-frequency variants across hundreds of biopsies, we could estimate mutation burden per cell, identify genes with an excess of protein-altering mutations, infer the density of positively selected clones, and compare the driver landscape of normal skin with that of skin cancers. Our results show that aged sun-exposed epidermis is a dense field of evolving mutant clones, many carrying canonical cancer-driver mutations, and that the boundary between normal tissue and cancer is less genetic than it appears from the tumor alone.

This work upended traditional models of cancer initiation and aging. It proved that the steady accumulation of cancer-associated mutations is a pervasive, standard aspect of aging, forcing the field to pivot toward understanding how human tissues successfully suppress tumorigenesis despite a landscape saturated with heavily mutated cellular clones.

paper_title: High Burden and Pervasive Positive Selection of Somatic Mutations in Normal Human Skin - 2015 - Science
display_title: High Burden and Pervasive Positive Selection of Somatic Mutations in Normal Human Skin
title: High Burden and Pervasive Positive Selection of Somatic Mutations in Normal Human Skin
text:
Cancer is an evolutionary process driven by the progressive accumulation of somatic mutations in the genomes of cells. Some of these mutations, known as "drivers," confer a selective growth advantage, allowing the mutant clone to outcompete its neighbors and expand. Over time, the sequential acquisition of driver mutations in a single lineage can lead to malignant transformation, uncontrolled proliferation, and ultimately, tissue invasion and metastasis. Massive parallel sequencing of human cancers has provided an unprecedented catalog of the genes and pathways altered in established tumors, offering profound insights into the later stages of this evolutionary trajectory.

However, our understanding of the very earliest stages of cancer development remains fundamentally incomplete. Because cancer genomics has almost exclusively focused on fully transformed, clinically detectable tumors, the foundational landscape from which these tumors arise—the normal tissue—is largely unexplored. We know remarkably little about the rate at which somatic mutations accumulate in normal human cells over a lifetime, the mutational signatures left by environmental exposures in healthy tissue, or the extent to which normal cells undergo clonal expansion driven by positively selected mutations. Understanding the mutational burden and clonal dynamics of normal tissue is critical, as it defines the starting material for oncogenesis and helps distinguish mutations that are sufficient for transformation from those that merely confer a benign growth advantage.

Skin provides an ideal model system to investigate these early evolutionary dynamics. The epidermis is continuously replenished throughout life and is frequently exposed to mutagens, most notably ultraviolet (UV) radiation from sunlight. Epidemiological data show a clear link between cumulative sun exposure and the incidence of skin cancers, such as cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma (cSCC) and basal cell carcinoma (BCC). Yet, the genomic landscape of the physiologically normal skin cells that endure this chronic exposure is unknown.

To illuminate the hidden evolutionary life of normal cells, we utilized ultradeep targeted sequencing of 74 known cancer genes to characterize the somatic mutation landscape in 234 small biopsies of normal, sun-exposed eyelid epidermis from four individuals. By adapting algorithms to detect subclonal mutations at very low allele frequencies, we quantified the mutation burden, identified mutational signatures, and mapped the size and frequency of mutant clones. Remarkably, we reveal that physiologically normal skin is a complex patchwork of thousands of evolving clones, bearing a mutational burden similar to many cancers and exhibiting pervasive positive selection for driver mutations in key cancer genes.

This work upended traditional models of cancer initiation and aging. It proved that the steady accumulation of cancer-associated mutations is a pervasive, standard aspect of aging, forcing the field to pivot toward understanding how human tissues successfully suppress tumorigenesis despite a landscape saturated with heavily mutated cellular clones.

paper_title: High Burden and Pervasive Positive Selection of Somatic Mutations in Normal Human Skin - 2015 - Science
display_title: High Burden and Pervasive Positive Selection of Somatic Mutations in Normal Human Skin
title: High Burden and Pervasive Positive Selection of Somatic Mutations in Normal Human Skin
text:
We found the frequency of driver mutations in physiologically normal skin cells surprisingly high. For example, there were more NOTCH1 mutations in just 5 cm2 of aged, sun-exposed skin analyzed here than have been identified in more than 5000 cancers sequenced by TCGA (The Cancer Genome Atlas). About 20% of normal skin cells carry driver mutations in NOTCH1, with some but not overwhelming enrichment in the matching cancer (60% of cSCCs have NOTCH1 mutations). Several other cancer genes were under positive selection in normal skin, and we found clones carrying two to three driver mutations that had not acquired malignant potential, raising the question of what combinations of events are sufficient for transformation. These observations may not be entirely unexpected: For cancers to occur with the frequency they do in the general population, there may be a vast underlying reservoir of competing clones part or much of the way to malignant transformation. A rather sobering corollary is that if we had a systemic targeted therapeutic that killed all cells with inactivated NOTCH1, we might successfully treat 60% of cSCCs but with considerable collateral damage to physiologically normal skin.

Studying tumor evolution by sequencing established cancers is akin to inferring the rules of a musical talent quest by identifying similarities across the show's annual winners. Successful aspirants undoubtedly have common properties that identify necessary criteria for victory, but there is no substitute for directly observing the competition in its raw, early, local heats. Here, we have found hundreds of evolving clones per square centimeter of skin; thousands of mutations per skin cell; variability among individuals in the profile of driver mutations; and variability among cancer genes in clonal dynamics. Scaled up across the range of organ systems, cell types, and mutational exposures—and encompassing aging, predisposing diseases, and genetic backgrounds—such studies promise to reveal fundamental insights into the earliest stages of cancer development.

This work upended traditional models of cancer initiation and aging. It proved that the steady accumulation of cancer-associated mutations is a pervasive, standard aspect of aging, forcing the field to pivot toward understanding how human tissues successfully suppress tumorigenesis despite a landscape saturated with heavily mutated cellular clones.

paper_title: High Burden and Pervasive Positive Selection of Somatic Mutations in Normal Human Skin - 2015 - Science
display_title: High Burden and Pervasive Positive Selection of Somatic Mutations in Normal Human Skin
title: High Burden and Pervasive Positive Selection of Somatic Mutations in Normal Human Skin
text:
These data show that physiologically normal human skin is not a uniform population of genetically normal cells, but a dense mosaic of mutant clones shaped by environmental mutagenesis and positive selection. The burden of somatic mutations in aged, sun-exposed epidermis is comparable with that of many cancers, and the mutational spectra closely recapitulate those seen in cutaneous malignancies. Thus, the earliest genetic events in skin cancer are not rare accidents occurring in isolated precursor cells, but common consequences of life-long UV exposure across an apparently normal tissue.

The extent of positive selection is particularly striking. Mutations in NOTCH1, NOTCH2, FAT1, TP53, and other genes were present at high density and in substantial fractions of cells, despite the absence of clinical or histological abnormality. The close overlap between genes under selection in normal skin and those recurrently mutated in cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma indicates that many canonical cancer drivers can confer a selective advantage long before malignant transformation. Conversely, the absence of strong selection for CDKN2A point mutations suggests that some driver events may become advantageous only after earlier stages of clonal evolution have occurred. Cancer development therefore appears to depend not simply on whether a driver mutation is present, but on its order, cellular context, and interaction with the surrounding tissue.

The clone-size distribution provides an important constraint on this model. Although selected mutations were numerous, most mutant clones remained small, and the difference in size between driver and neutral clones was modest. This implies that expansion of mutant keratinocyte clones is limited by mechanisms operating within intact epidermis, potentially including spatial confinement, competition among neighboring clones, and density-dependent regulation. Such constraints may represent a major barrier preventing the frequent driver mutations observed here from progressing to overt cancer.

Our findings also distinguish clonal expansion from malignant potential. Activating FGFR3 mutations produced some of the largest clones, yet are associated with benign seborrheic keratoses rather than invasive cancer. Normal skin therefore contains many clones that are genetically advanced but biologically restrained. Understanding how these restraints fail, and how inherited background, environmental exposure, and local tissue architecture influence the selective landscape, will be central to explaining why only a small minority of mutant clones progress to cancer.

display_title: High Burden and Pervasive Positive Selection of Somatic Mutations in Normal Human Skin
title: High Burden and Pervasive Positive Selection of Somatic Mutations in Normal Human Skin
text:
The observation of a high burden of somatic mutations and pervasive positive selection in phenotypically normal human skin fundamentally alters our understanding of tissue homeostasis and the early stages of oncogenesis. Our sequencing analysis reveals that sun-exposed skin is a complex mosaic of competing mutant clones, with a mutation burden comparable to that seen in many cancers. This striking accumulation of somatic mutations in healthy tissue challenges the traditional view that cancer-associated mutations are exclusive to malignant states.

Crucially, we identified strong signals of positive selection for mutations in several known cancer genes, most notably NOTCH1, NOTCH2, TP53, and FAT1. The expansion of these mutant clones indicates that these genetic alterations confer a distinct proliferative advantage to epidermal cells long before any clinical signs of malignancy appear. The prevalence of NOTCH1 mutations, which far exceeds their frequency in squamous cell carcinomas, suggests an unexpected role for this gene in driving clonal expansion in normal skin, potentially acting as a mechanism of tissue remodeling or response to chronic UV damage.

These findings highlight that the transition from normal tissue to cancer is a continuous, gradual evolutionary process rather than a sudden event. The fact that a significant fraction of normal skin cells carries driver mutations underscores the robust tumor suppressive mechanisms that must exist to prevent these highly mutated clones from progressing to invasive cancer. Future research must determine what additional genetic or environmental factors precipitate the malignant transformation of these pre-existing clones. This high-resolution view of somatic evolution in normal skin provides a critical baseline for understanding cancer development and emphasizes the need for early detection strategies that can distinguish benign clonal expansions from true neoplastic progression.

paper_title: Highly Accurate Protein Structure Prediction with AlphaFold - 2021 - Nature
display_title: Highly Accurate Protein Structure Prediction with AlphaFold
title: Highly Accurate Protein Structure Prediction with AlphaFold
text: Proteins are essential to life, and understanding their structure can facilitate a mechanistic understanding of their function. Through an enormous experimental effort, the structures of around 100,000 unique proteins have been determined, but this represents a small fraction of the billions of known protein sequences. Structural coverage is bottlenecked by the months to years of painstaking effort required to determine a single protein structure. Accurate computational approaches are needed to address this gap and to enable large-scale structural bioinformatics. Predicting the three-dimensional structure that a protein will adopt based solely on its amino acid sequence—the structure prediction component of the 'protein folding problem'—has been an important open research problem for more than 50 years. Despite recent progress, existing methods fall far short of atomic accuracy, especially when no homologous structure is available. Here we provide the first computational method that can regularly predict protein structures with atomic accuracy even in cases in which no similar structure is known. We validated an entirely redesigned version of our neural network-based model, AlphaFold, in the challenging 14th Critical Assessment of protein Structure Prediction (CASP14), demonstrating accuracy competitive with experimental structures in a majority of cases and greatly outperforming other methods. Underpinning the latest version of AlphaFold is a novel machine learning approach that incorporates physical and biological knowledge about protein structure, leveraging multi-sequence alignments, into the design of the deep learning algorithm.
historical_context: Published in 2021 by John Jumper, Demis Hassabis, and the DeepMind team, this paper detailed AlphaFold2, an artificial intelligence system that conquered the 50-year-old grand challenge of protein folding. AlphaFold showcased the unprecedented ability to predict the 3D structures of proteins directly from their amino acid sequences with atomic-level accuracy, directly rivaling labor-intensive experimental techniques like X-ray crystallography and cryo-EM. This work stands as one of the most monumental breakthroughs in the history of computational biology. It instantly revolutionized structural biology, granting scientists immediate access to high-quality structures for nearly all known proteins, thereby exponentially accelerating drug discovery, enzyme design, and the broader understanding of complex biological systems.

paper_title: Highly Accurate Protein Structure Prediction with AlphaFold - 2021 - Nature
display_title: Highly Accurate Protein Structure Prediction with AlphaFold
title: Highly Accurate Protein Structure Prediction with AlphaFold
text: The prediction of protein structures from amino acid sequence has long been a central challenge in structural biology, limiting the use of genomic data to infer molecular mechanism. Here we describe AlphaFold, a neural network method that combines evolutionary information from multiple sequence alignments with physical and geometric constraints to predict three-dimensional protein structures directly from sequence. The model uses a new architecture, Evoformer, to exchange information between residue-pair and sequence-alignment representations, followed by an equivariant structure module that iteratively refines backbone frames and side-chain geometry. In the blind CASP14 assessment, AlphaFold achieved a median backbone accuracy of 0.96 Angstrom r.m.s.d.95, compared with 2.8 Angstrom for the next best method, and produced accurate all-atom models and calibrated residue-level confidence estimates. This accuracy transferred to a large set of recently released Protein Data Bank chains that were excluded from training, including many cases without close structural templates. Analysis of the network suggests that AlphaFold forms and continuously improves a structural hypothesis through depth and recycling, while self-distillation on unlabelled sequences further improves performance. Accuracy remains affected by shallow sequence alignments and by chains whose conformation is dominated by heterotypic contacts. Nevertheless, AlphaFold provides protein coordinates in GPU minutes to hours and offers a practical route to proteome-scale structural modelling and to broader computational approaches for biological structure prediction.
historical_context: Published in 2021 by John Jumper, Demis Hassabis, and the DeepMind team, this paper detailed AlphaFold2, an artificial intelligence system that conquered the 50-year-old grand challenge of protein folding. AlphaFold showcased the unprecedented ability to predict the 3D structures of proteins directly from their amino acid sequences with atomic-level accuracy, directly rivaling labor-intensive experimental techniques like X-ray crystallography and cryo-EM. This work stands as one of the most monumental breakthroughs in the history of computational biology. It instantly revolutionized structural biology, granting scientists immediate access to high-quality structures for nearly all known proteins, thereby exponentially accelerating drug discovery, enzyme design, and the broader understanding of complex biological systems.

paper_title: Highly Accurate Protein Structure Prediction with AlphaFold - 2021 - Nature
display_title: Highly Accurate Protein Structure Prediction with AlphaFold
title: Highly Accurate Protein Structure Prediction with AlphaFold
text: The prediction of a protein's three-dimensional structure from its amino acid sequence has been a grand challenge in computational biology for decades. While evolutionary and physical approaches have made steady progress, predictions have historically fallen short of experimental accuracy unless a close homologous structure was available. Here, we present AlphaFold, a novel machine learning approach that predicts protein structures with unprecedented accuracy. In the 14th Critical Assessment of protein Structure Prediction (CASP14), AlphaFold produced predictions that were vastly more accurate than competing methods, achieving a median backbone accuracy of 0.96 Å r.m.s.d.95, comparable to the width of a single atom. AlphaFold integrates evolutionary, physical, and geometric constraints into its deep learning architecture. At its core is the Evoformer, a novel neural network block that continuously exchanges information between multiple sequence alignment (MSA) and pairwise residue representations, reasoning directly about spatial and evolutionary relationships. This is followed by a structure module that iteratively refines global rigid body frames for each residue to construct precise 3D coordinates in an end-to-end manner. The network is trained with a combination of labeled Protein Data Bank structures and unlabelled sequence data via self-distillation. Our system can accurately predict domain structures, domain packing, and precise side-chain conformations, while also providing reliable per-residue confidence estimates. AlphaFold demonstrates that deep learning can routinely achieve near-experimental accuracy in structure prediction, overcoming a major bottleneck in structural bioinformatics and accelerating biological discovery.

It instantly revolutionized structural biology, granting scientists immediate access to high-quality structures for nearly all known proteins, thereby exponentially accelerating drug discovery, enzyme design, and the broader understanding of complex biological systems.

paper_title: Highly Accurate Protein Structure Prediction with AlphaFold - 2021 - Nature
display_title: Highly Accurate Protein Structure Prediction with AlphaFold
title: Highly Accurate Protein Structure Prediction with AlphaFold
text:
The development of computational methods to predict three-dimensional (3D) protein structures from the protein sequence has proceeded along two complementary paths that focus on either the physical interactions or the evolutionary history. The physical interaction programme heavily integrates our understanding of molecular driving forces into either thermodynamic or kinetic simulation of protein physics or statistical approximations thereof. Although theoretically very appealing, this approach has proved highly challenging for even moderate-sized proteins due to the computational intractability of molecular simulation, the context dependence of protein stability and the difficulty of producing sufficiently accurate models of protein physics. The evolutionary programme has provided an alternative in recent years, in which the constraints on protein structure are derived from bioinformatics analysis of the evolutionary history of proteins, homology to solved structures and pairwise evolutionary correlations. This bioinformatics approach has benefited greatly from the steady growth of experimental protein structures deposited in the Protein Data Bank (PDB), the explosion of genomic sequencing and the rapid development of deep learning techniques to interpret these correlations. Despite these advances, contemporary physical and evolutionary-history-based approaches produce predictions that are far short of experimental accuracy in the majority of cases in which a close homologue has not been solved experimentally and this has limited their utility for many biological applications.

In this study, we develop the first, to our knowledge, computational approach capable of predicting protein structures to near experimental accuracy in a majority of cases. The neural network AlphaFold that we developed was entered into the CASP14 assessment (May–July 2020; entered under the team name 'AlphaFold2' and a completely different model from our CASP13 AlphaFold system). The CASP assessment is carried out biennially using recently solved structures that have not been deposited in the PDB or publicly disclosed so that it is a blind test for the participating methods, and has long served as the gold-standard assessment for the accuracy of structure prediction.
historical_context: Published in 2021 by John Jumper, Demis Hassabis, and the DeepMind team, this paper detailed AlphaFold2, an artificial intelligence system that conquered the 50-year-old grand challenge of protein folding. AlphaFold showcased the unprecedented ability to predict the 3D structures of proteins directly from their amino acid sequences with atomic-level accuracy, directly rivaling labor-intensive experimental techniques like X-ray crystallography and cryo-EM. This work stands as one of the most monumental breakthroughs in the history of computational biology. It instantly revolutionized structural biology, granting scientists immediate access to high-quality structures for nearly all known proteins, thereby exponentially accelerating drug discovery, enzyme design, and the broader understanding of complex biological systems.

paper_title: Highly Accurate Protein Structure Prediction with AlphaFold - 2021 - Nature
display_title: Highly Accurate Protein Structure Prediction with AlphaFold
title: Highly Accurate Protein Structure Prediction with AlphaFold
text:
Proteins carry out most of the molecular functions of the cell, and these functions are determined in large part by three-dimensional structure. Experimental methods such as X-ray crystallography, nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopy and cryogenic electron microscopy have provided a detailed structural view of biology, but they remain difficult to apply at the scale of modern sequence databases. The number of known protein sequences now exceeds the number of experimentally determined structures by orders of magnitude, leaving most protein families without direct structural characterization.

The computational prediction of protein structure from amino-acid sequence has therefore long been a central goal of structural biology. Progress has been driven by two complementary sources of information. First, the accumulation of homologous sequences has made it possible to infer spatial contacts from evolutionary covariation. Second, physical and geometric constraints restrict the conformations that a folded protein can adopt. Existing methods have used these signals to produce increasingly useful models, particularly when related structures are available as templates. However, many predictions remain insufficiently accurate for detailed biological interpretation, especially for proteins without close homologues of known structure, and side-chain placement and domain organization have continued to present major challenges.

Here we describe AlphaFold, a substantially redesigned neural network system for protein structure prediction. AlphaFold uses the primary sequence, multiple sequence alignments and, where available, template information to reason directly over residue relationships and three-dimensional geometry. The model introduces an architecture that exchanges information between evolutionary and pairwise representations, followed by a structure module that iteratively constructs atomic coordinates while preserving the symmetries of three-dimensional space. Rather than treating structure prediction as a sequence of separately optimized steps, the network is trained end to end to produce full-atom models together with calibrated estimates of confidence.

We evaluate AlphaFold in the 14th Critical Assessment of protein Structure Prediction and on recently released Protein Data Bank structures not available during training. These results show that a learned model, when constrained by the appropriate biological and geometric inductive biases, can routinely approach experimental accuracy for single-chain protein structure prediction.

AlphaFold showcased the unprecedented ability to predict the 3D structures of proteins directly from their amino acid sequences with atomic-level accuracy, directly rivaling labor-intensive experimental techniques like X-ray crystallography and cryo-EM. This work stands as one of the most monumental breakthroughs in the history of computational biology. It instantly revolutionized structural biology, granting scientists immediate access to high-quality structures for nearly all known proteins, thereby exponentially accelerating drug discovery, enzyme design, and the broader understanding of complex biological systems.

paper_title: Highly Accurate Protein Structure Prediction with AlphaFold - 2021 - Nature
display_title: Highly Accurate Protein Structure Prediction with AlphaFold
title: Highly Accurate Protein Structure Prediction with AlphaFold
text:
Proteins are the workhorses of biology, mediating almost every fundamental process in living organisms. The function of a protein is intimately linked to its three-dimensional (3D) structure, which is in turn determined by its linear sequence of amino acids. For over half a century, the "protein folding problem"—the challenge of computationally predicting a protein's 3D structure solely from its primary amino acid sequence—has stood as a grand challenge in structural biology and biophysics. Solving this problem would have profound implications, bridging the massive and ever-growing gap between the billions of known genomic sequences and the ~100,000 protein structures painstakingly determined by experimental methods such as X-ray crystallography, nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectroscopy, and cryogenic electron microscopy (cryo-EM).

While experimental structure determination is time-consuming, resource-intensive, and often technically challenging, computational prediction offers a potentially rapid and scalable alternative. Historically, computational methods have fallen broadly into two categories: template-based modeling, which relies on the presence of a known homologous structure, and free-modeling (or ab initio) approaches, which attempt to predict structure from physical principles or statistical potentials when no template is available. Despite steady, incremental progress over the decades, traditional methods have consistently struggled to achieve atomic-level accuracy, particularly in the absence of a close structural homolog.

Recent advances in deep learning, coupled with the availability of large sequence and structure databases, have catalyzed a revolution in the field. Initial neural network-based approaches focused on predicting distances or contacts between pairs of residues, using co-evolutionary signals derived from multiple sequence alignments (MSAs), which were then used to guide structure generation. Here, we present a novel and entirely redesigned deep learning system, AlphaFold, which directly predicts the 3D coordinates of all heavy atoms. By incorporating physical, geometric, and evolutionary constraints into a novel neural network architecture, AlphaFold achieves unprecedented, near-experimental accuracy in protein structure prediction.

This work stands as one of the most monumental breakthroughs in the history of computational biology. It instantly revolutionized structural biology, granting scientists immediate access to high-quality structures for nearly all known proteins, thereby exponentially accelerating drug discovery, enzyme design, and the broader understanding of complex biological systems.

paper_title: Highly Accurate Protein Structure Prediction with AlphaFold - 2021 - Nature
display_title: Highly Accurate Protein Structure Prediction with AlphaFold
title: Highly Accurate Protein Structure Prediction with AlphaFold
text:
The methodology that we have taken in designing AlphaFold is a combination of the bioinformatics and physical approaches: we use a physical and geometric inductive bias to build components that learn from PDB data with minimal imposition of handcrafted features (for example, AlphaFold builds hydrogen bonds effectively without a hydrogen bond score function). This results in a network that learns far more efficiently from the limited data in the PDB but is able to cope with the complexity and variety of structural data.

In particular, AlphaFold is able to handle missing the physical context and produce accurate models in challenging cases such as intertwined homomers or proteins that only fold in the presence of an unknown haem group. The ability to handle underspecified structural conditions is essential to learning from PDB structures as the PDB represents the full range of conditions in which structures have been solved. In general, AlphaFold is trained to produce the protein structure most likely to appear as part of a PDB structure. For example, in cases in which a particular stochiometry, ligand or ion is predictable from the sequence alone, AlphaFold is likely to produce a structure that respects those constraints implicitly.

AlphaFold has already demonstrated its utility to the experimental community, both for molecular replacement and for interpreting cryogenic electron microscopy maps. Moreover, because AlphaFold outputs protein coordinates directly, AlphaFold produces predictions in graphics processing unit (GPU) minutes to GPU hours depending on the length of the protein sequence (for example, around one GPU minute per model for 384 residues). This opens up the exciting possibility of predicting structures at the proteome-scale and beyond—in a companion paper, we demonstrate the application of AlphaFold to the entire human proteome.

The explosion in available genomic sequencing techniques and data has revolutionized bioinformatics but the intrinsic challenge of experimental structure determination has prevented a similar expansion in our structural knowledge. By developing an accurate protein structure prediction algorithm, coupled with existing large and well-curated structure and sequence databases assembled by the experimental community, we hope to accelerate the advancement of structural bioinformatics that can keep pace with the genomics revolution. We hope that AlphaFold—and computational approaches that apply its techniques for other biophysical problems—will become essential tools of modern biology.

paper_title: Highly Accurate Protein Structure Prediction with AlphaFold - 2021 - Nature
display_title: Highly Accurate Protein Structure Prediction with AlphaFold
title: Highly Accurate Protein Structure Prediction with AlphaFold
text:
We have shown that a neural network can predict protein structures with an accuracy that is competitive with experimental structures in a large fraction of cases, including many proteins for which no close structural template is available. This level of accuracy changes the practical role of computational structure prediction. Rather than serving only as a source of approximate folds or hypotheses for subsequent modelling, predicted structures can now often provide detailed atomic models that are immediately useful for interpreting function, designing experiments and analysing large sequence datasets.

The success of AlphaFold suggests that the information required to specify protein structure is frequently present in the combination of amino acid sequence, evolutionary variation and the general constraints of protein geometry. A central feature of the method is that these sources of information are not treated as separate stages in a hand-designed pipeline, but are integrated within a single differentiable model that can repeatedly refine its structural hypothesis. The emergence of coherent intermediate structures within the network indicates that accurate prediction is not obtained simply by copying templates or by predicting independent distances, but by iterative reasoning over a constrained three-dimensional object.

Several limitations remain. Accuracy decreases when the available MSA is very shallow, indicating that evolutionary information is still important for many targets. Moreover, the current system predicts single chains and therefore has difficulty when the native conformation of a chain is largely determined by heterotypic interactions within a complex. Flexible regions, alternative conformations, ligand-dependent states and proteins whose structures depend on environmental context also remain outside the direct scope of the present model. The confidence measures are therefore an essential part of the output, as they provide a way to distinguish regions that can be interpreted with high confidence from regions that require caution or further experimental validation.

The results presented here do not remove the need for experimental structural biology. Experimental methods remain indispensable for determining complexes, conformational ensembles, ligand-bound states and cellular context, and for providing the data on which predictive methods depend. Instead, accurate prediction should complement experiments by focusing attention on the most informative measurements and by enabling structural interpretation in systems for which experimental determination is difficult.

More broadly, AlphaFold demonstrates that incorporating biological and physical structure into machine learning architectures can solve problems that have resisted purely heuristic or simulation-based approaches. Extending these ideas to protein complexes, dynamics, design and interactions with other molecules may substantially expand the reach of structural biology and make structural information available for a much larger fraction of known protein sequences.

paper_title: Highly Accurate Protein Structure Prediction with AlphaFold - 2021 - Nature
display_title: Highly Accurate Protein Structure Prediction with AlphaFold
title: Highly Accurate Protein Structure Prediction with AlphaFold
text:
The development of AlphaFold and its unprecedented performance in the CASP14 assessment marks a historic milestone in structural biology, effectively solving the 50-year-old grand challenge of protein structure prediction. By achieving atomic accuracy that is competitive with experimental structures across a diverse range of protein targets, AlphaFold transforms the landscape of biological research. Our results demonstrate that this end-to-end deep learning system, incorporating novel neural network architectures such as the Evoformer and a structure module that operates directly on 3D coordinates, can successfully model complex physical interactions and evolutionary constraints derived from multiple sequence alignments.

The implications of this breakthrough are profound and far-reaching. The ability to rapidly and accurately predict protein structures at scale essentially democratizes structural biology, removing the significant time and resource barriers associated with traditional experimental methods like X-ray crystallography and cryo-electron microscopy. This vast expansion of structural coverage will dramatically accelerate research in structural bioinformatics, enzymology, and evolutionary biology, enabling scientists to formulate more precise hypotheses regarding protein function and interaction networks.

Furthermore, the high accuracy of AlphaFold predictions holds immense potential for translational applications, particularly in drug discovery and protein design. Accurate structural models are critical for structure-based drug design, allowing for the rapid screening and optimization of small molecule inhibitors against novel therapeutic targets. While challenges remain in predicting the structures of large multi-protein complexes, dynamic conformational ensembles, and interactions with non-protein ligands, the architectural innovations introduced here provide a robust foundation for future advancements.

In conclusion, AlphaFold demonstrates that deep learning can successfully capture the complex, non-linear physical rules governing protein folding. This tool not only accelerates the pace of structural biology but also fundamentally changes how we approach understanding the molecular machinery of life, paving the way for a new era of computationally driven biological discovery and bioengineering.

paper_title: Highly Parallel Genome-wide Expression Profiling of Individual Cells Using Nanoliter Droplets - 2015 - Cell
display_title: Highly Parallel Genome-wide Expression Profiling of Individual Cells Using Nanoliter Droplets
title: Highly Parallel Genome-wide Expression Profiling of Individual Cells Using Nanoliter Droplets
text: Cells, the basic units of biological structure and function, vary broadly in type and state. Single-cell genomics can characterize cell identity and function, but limitations of ease and scale have prevented its broad application. Here we describe Drop-seq, a strategy for quickly profiling thousands of individual cells by separating them into nanoliter-sized aqueous droplets, associating a different barcode with each cell's RNAs, and sequencing them all together. Drop-seq analyzes mRNA transcripts from thousands of individual cells simultaneously while remembering transcripts' cell of origin. We analyzed transcriptomes from 44,808 mouse retinal cells and identified 39 transcriptionally distinct cell populations, creating a molecular atlas of gene expression for known retinal cell classes and novel candidate cell subtypes. Drop-seq will accelerate biological discovery by enabling routine transcriptional profiling at single-cell resolution.
historical_context: In 2015, Evan Macosko, Steven McCarroll, and colleagues debuted Drop-seq, a phenomenal technology for executing single-cell RNA sequencing at an unparalleled scale. By co-encapsulating individual cells and uniquely barcoded microparticles within nanoliter-sized aqueous droplets using microfluidics, researchers could simultaneously capture the transcriptomes of tens of thousands of cells rapidly and cost-effectively. It was a profound leap for modern transcriptomics. Drop-seq (alongside the parallel inDrop method) successfully democratized large-scale single-cell genomics, forcing biology to evolve from analyzing messy bulk tissue averages to mapping intricate cellular ecosystems at single-cell resolution, laying the direct technological groundwork for massive global initiatives like the Human Cell Atlas.

title: Highly Parallel Genome-wide Expression Profiling of Individual Cells Using Nanoliter Droplets
text:
Individual cells are the building blocks of tissues, organs, and organisms. Each tissue contains cells of many types, and cells of each type can switch among biological states. In most biological systems, our knowledge of cellular diversity is incomplete; for example, the cell-type complexity of the brain is unknown and widely debated. To understand how complex tissues work, it will be important to learn the functional capacities and responses of each cell type.

A major determinant of each cell's function is its transcriptional program. Recent advances now enable mRNA-seq analysis of individual cells. However, methods of preparing cells for profiling have been applicable in practice to just hundreds or (with automation) a few thousand cells, typically after first separating the cells by flow sorting or microfluidics and then amplifying each cell's transcriptome separately. Fast, scalable approaches are needed to characterize complex tissues with many cell types and states, under diverse conditions and perturbations.

Here, we describe Drop-seq, a method to analyze mRNA expression in thousands of individual cells by encapsulating cells in tiny droplets for parallel analysis. Droplets - nanoliter-scale aqueous compartments formed by precisely combining aqueous and oil flows in a microfluidic device - have been used as tiny reaction chambers for PCR and reverse transcription. We sought here to use droplets to compartmentalize cells into nanoliter-sized reaction chambers for analysis of all of their RNAs. A basic challenge of using droplets for transcriptomics is to retain a molecular memory of the identity of the cell from which each mRNA transcript was isolated. To accomplish this, we developed a molecular barcoding strategy to remember the cell-of-origin of each mRNA. We critically evaluated Drop-seq, then used it to profile cell states along the cell cycle. We then applied it to a complex neural tissue, mouse retina, and from 44,808 cell profiles identified 39 distinct populations, each corresponding to one or a group of closely related cell types. Our results demonstrate how large-scale single-cell analysis can help deepen our understanding of the biology of complex tissues and cell populations.

By co-encapsulating individual cells and uniquely barcoded microparticles within nanoliter-sized aqueous droplets using microfluidics, researchers could simultaneously capture the transcriptomes of tens of thousands of cells rapidly and cost-effectively. It was a profound leap for modern transcriptomics. Drop-seq (alongside the parallel inDrop method) successfully democratized large-scale single-cell genomics, forcing biology to evolve from analyzing messy bulk tissue averages to mapping intricate cellular ecosystems at single-cell resolution, laying the direct technological groundwork for massive global initiatives like the Human Cell Atlas.

title: Highly Parallel Genome-wide Expression Profiling of Individual Cells Using Nanoliter Droplets
text:
Multicellular tissues contain many kinds of cells, each defined by a combination of developmental history, physiological function, and current state. The identities and activities of these cells are reflected in their mRNA expression programs. Measurements of gene expression in bulk tissue, however, average together the transcripts of many different cells, obscuring rare cell types, transient states, and the gene-expression relationships that distinguish one cell population from another. Single-cell RNA sequencing offers a direct way to measure this diversity, but existing approaches have been limited by the effort and expense required to prepare large numbers of individual libraries.

An ideal method for single-cell transcriptomics would be simple enough for routine use, inexpensive enough for extensive replication, and scalable enough to characterize complex tissues containing many related and rare cell populations. It would preserve the cell of origin for each transcript while allowing thousands of cells to be processed together. It would also count transcripts digitally, so that measurements from individual cells could be compared across experiments and used to infer both cell types and cell states.

Here we describe Drop-seq, a method for highly parallel single-cell RNA-seq. Drop-seq uses microfluidic droplets to isolate individual cells with barcoded microparticles. After cell lysis, mRNAs are captured on the co-encapsulated microparticle, such that all transcripts from a cell acquire the same cellular barcode and each molecule also receives a unique molecular identifier. Thousands of these single-cell transcriptomes attached to microparticles can then be reverse-transcribed, amplified, and sequenced together.

We validate Drop-seq using species-mixing experiments, synthetic RNA controls, and comparisons to bulk and single-cell expression measurements. We then apply the method to the mouse retina, a complex neural tissue with well-studied major cell classes and many incompletely characterized subtypes. Analysis of 44,808 retinal cells identifies 39 transcriptionally distinct populations, including known retinal cell classes and previously unrecognized molecular distinctions among amacrine cells. These results illustrate how routine, large-scale single-cell profiling can create molecular atlases of complex tissues and reveal biological structure that is difficult to observe by lower-throughput approaches.

title: Highly Parallel Genome-wide Expression Profiling of Individual Cells Using Nanoliter Droplets
text:
Tissues are complex ecosystems composed of numerous, diverse cell types that interact to perform physiological functions. Traditional methods for profiling gene expression, such as bulk RNA sequencing, average the transcriptomes of thousands to millions of cells, masking the profound cellular heterogeneity that exists within even the most seemingly uniform tissues. To truly understand tissue architecture, developmental trajectories, and the specific cellular responses to disease or perturbation, it is necessary to resolve gene expression at the fundamental unit of biology: the single cell.

Single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) has emerged as a transformative technology for dissecting cellular diversity, enabling the discovery of rare cell populations, the mapping of continuous developmental states, and the identification of novel gene regulatory networks. However, the broad application of early scRNA-seq methods has been significantly hindered by limitations in throughput, cost, and ease of use. Conventional approaches typically require the physical isolation of individual cells into separate wells of a microtiter plate—often using fluorescence-activated cell sorting (FACS) or manual picking—followed by independent, labor-intensive library preparation reactions for each cell. Even with the advent of integrated microfluidic circuits, processing more than a few hundred cells remains a formidable and expensive undertaking.

To realize the full potential of single-cell genomics, a technology is needed that can rapidly, efficiently, and inexpensively profile thousands or tens of thousands of individual cells in a single experiment. Here, we introduce Drop-seq, a highly scalable, droplet-based microfluidic approach that addresses this bottleneck. Drop-seq isolates individual cells into nanoliter-sized aqueous droplets along with specially designed, barcoded microparticles. By capturing and tagging the mRNA from each cell with a unique barcode before pooling all transcripts for a single, massive parallel sequencing reaction, Drop-seq can profile thousands of cells simultaneously. We demonstrate the power and accuracy of Drop-seq by generating a comprehensive molecular atlas of the mouse retina, analyzing the transcriptomes of nearly 45,000 individual cells and identifying 39 distinct cell populations, thereby proving its capability to systematically deconstruct complex mammalian tissues.

Drop-seq (alongside the parallel inDrop method) successfully democratized large-scale single-cell genomics, forcing biology to evolve from analyzing messy bulk tissue averages to mapping intricate cellular ecosystems at single-cell resolution, laying the direct technological groundwork for massive global initiatives like the Human Cell Atlas.

paper_title: Highly Parallel Genome-wide Expression Profiling of Individual Cells Using Nanoliter Droplets - 2015 - Cell
display_title: Highly Parallel Genome-wide Expression Profiling of Individual Cells Using Nanoliter Droplets
title: Highly Parallel Genome-wide Expression Profiling of Individual Cells Using Nanoliter Droplets
text:
Ascertaining transcriptional variation across individual cells is a valuable way of learning about complex tissues and functional responses, but single-cell analysis has been limited by the time and cost of preparing libraries from many individual cells. A scientist employing Drop-seq can prepare 10,000 single-cell libraries for sequencing in 12 hr, for about 6.5 cents per cell, representing a >100-fold improvement in both time and cost relative to existing methods. A Drop-seq setup can be constructed quickly and inexpensively in a standard biology lab using readily available equipment. We hope that ease, speed, and low cost facilitate exuberant experimentation, careful replication, and many cycles of experiments, analyses, ideas, and more experiments.

In validating Drop-seq, we developed stringent species-mixing experiments to measure single-cell purity and cell doublet rates in our libraries. In another article in this issue, Klein et al. describe a droplet-based approach to single-cell RNA-seq and also use species-mixing experiments to evaluate it. Our results indicate that all methods of isolating single cells from a cell suspension, including Drop-seq, fluorescence activated cell sorting (FACS) and microfluidics, are vulnerable to impurities, and highlight the value of performing species mixing experiments to assess single-cell approaches. In our retina analysis, even relatively impure libraries generated in ''ultra-high-throughput'' modes (100 cells per ul, allowing the processing of 10,000 cells per hour at ~10% doublet and impurity rates) appeared to yield a robust and biologically validated cell classification, but other tissues or applications may require using Drop-seq in purer modes.

Unsupervised computational analysis of Drop-seq data identified 39 transcriptionally distinct retinal cell populations, many representing specific subtypes of the major retinal cell classes. It is a particular strength of the retina that establishing correspondence between cluster and type was in many cases straightforward; an important direction will be to identify cell types and states in other parts of the brain - as well as in other tissues - about which less is currently known.

We see many applications of Drop-seq, beyond the identification of cell types and cell states. Genome-scale genetic studies are identifying many genes whose variation contributes to disease risk, but biology has lacked similarly high-throughput ways of connecting these genes to specific cell populations and unique functional responses. Drop-seq could be used to provide initial insights into how these genes function in the diverse cell types composing each tissue. In addition, coupling Drop-seq to perturbations - such as small molecules, mutations, pathogens, or other stimuli - could generate an information-rich, multi-dimensional readout of the influence of perturbations on many kinds of cells.

The functional implications of a gene's expression are a product not just of that gene's intrinsic properties, but also of the entire cell-level context in which the gene is expressed. We hope Drop-seq enables the abundant and routine discovery of such relationships in many areas of biology.

paper_title: Highly Parallel Genome-wide Expression Profiling of Individual Cells Using Nanoliter Droplets - 2015 - Cell
display_title: Highly Parallel Genome-wide Expression Profiling of Individual Cells Using Nanoliter Droplets
title: Highly Parallel Genome-wide Expression Profiling of Individual Cells Using Nanoliter Droplets
text:
Drop-seq provides a simple strategy for analyzing the transcriptional programs of large numbers of individual cells. By using droplets to isolate cells and barcoded microparticles to retain a molecular memory of cell-of-origin, Drop-seq converts thousands of single-cell transcriptomes into a pooled sequencing library while preserving the information needed to reconstruct each cell's expression profile. This combination of nanoliter-scale compartmentalization, split-and-pool barcode synthesis, and digital counting with UMIs makes single-cell RNA-seq substantially more scalable and accessible than approaches in which each cell must be handled and amplified separately.

The method was accurate enough to distinguish individual human and mouse cells in mixed-species experiments and sensitive enough to recover thousands of genes per cell. These experiments also clarified the principal technical limitations of the approach. Cell-cell doublets increase with cell concentration, and ambient RNA from damaged cells contributes to single-cell impurity. Both effects can be controlled experimentally and measured directly, allowing the user to balance throughput, purity, and cost for a given biological question. The use of UMIs and conservative collapsing of related UMI sequences further supports quantitative interpretation of digital expression measurements.

The analysis of asynchronously dividing HEK and 3T3 cells illustrates that Drop-seq can recover not only stable cell identities but also dynamic cell states. Without synchronizing cells chemically, expression profiles organized cells along the cell cycle and identified many conserved cell-cycle-regulated genes. This suggests that large-scale single-cell data can reveal biological processes that are obscured in bulk measurements, and can do so without perturbing the system to enrich for particular states.

The retinal analysis demonstrates the value of scale for complex tissues. From 44,808 mouse retinal cells, Drop-seq identified 39 transcriptionally distinct populations corresponding to known neuronal and non-neuronal classes, while also resolving finer distinctions among closely related amacrine populations. The recovery of all clusters across independent experimental sessions indicates that these structures were not artifacts of a single preparation or batch. Moreover, the increasing resolution obtained with increasing cell numbers shows why very large datasets are important: rare cell populations and subtle subdivisions become visible only when enough cells have been sampled.

The markers nominated by Drop-seq provide a practical route from transcriptomic classification to anatomical and functional study. Immunohistochemical validation of markers for specific amacrine populations confirmed the ability of the method to identify biologically meaningful cell types, including heterogeneity among nGnG amacrines that had not been evident from prior marker sets. Supervised analysis of retinal ganglion cells further suggests that additional diversity remains within the clusters identified here, and that deeper sampling or targeted enrichment should reveal still finer taxonomic structure.

Drop-seq should therefore be useful wherever cellular diversity or cellular state is central to biological function. Its strengths are greatest when many cells must be profiled across tissues, developmental stages, perturbations, or disease states. The approach does not eliminate the need for anatomical, physiological, or genetic validation, and transcriptomes alone may not fully define cell types. However, by making large-scale single-cell expression profiling routine, Drop-seq provides a general framework for building molecular atlases of complex tissues and for discovering the cell types and states that underlie their biology.
historical_context: In 2015, Evan Macosko, Steven McCarroll, and colleagues debuted Drop-seq, a phenomenal technology for executing single-cell RNA sequencing at an unparalleled scale. By co-encapsulating individual cells and uniquely barcoded microparticles within nanoliter-sized aqueous droplets using microfluidics, researchers could simultaneously capture the transcriptomes of tens of thousands of cells rapidly and cost-effectively. It was a profound leap for modern transcriptomics. Drop-seq (alongside the parallel inDrop method) successfully democratized large-scale single-cell genomics, forcing biology to evolve from analyzing messy bulk tissue averages to mapping intricate cellular ecosystems at single-cell resolution, laying the direct technological groundwork for massive global initiatives like the Human Cell Atlas.

title: Highly Parallel Genome-wide Expression Profiling of Individual Cells Using Nanoliter Droplets
text:
The development of Drop-seq represents a significant technological leap in transcriptomics, enabling highly parallel, genome-wide expression profiling of thousands of individual cells simultaneously. By encapsulating single cells within nanoliter-sized aqueous droplets alongside uniquely barcoded microparticles, we have created a scalable, cost-effective platform that overcomes the throughput limitations of previous single-cell RNA-seq methodologies. Our results demonstrate that Drop-seq can efficiently process tens of thousands of cells in a matter of hours, generating comprehensive single-cell expression profiles that allow for the precise identification and classification of distinct cell populations within complex tissues.

The analytical power of Drop-seq is evident in its application to the mouse retina, where we successfully reconstructed the major retinal cell types purely from high-dimensional transcriptional data, without reliance on a priori known markers. This unbiased, data-driven approach not only identified well-established cellular subsets but also revealed novel sub-populations and subtle transcriptional gradients that had previously been obscured in bulk tissue analyses. The ability to resolve cellular heterogeneity at this scale is critical for understanding tissue architecture, developmental trajectories, and the distinct cellular responses to physiological or pathological stimuli.

The unique barcoding strategy, utilizing split-and-pool synthesis on the microparticles, ensures that every transcript is tagged with both a cell-specific barcode and a unique molecular identifier (UMI). This dual-tagging system provides high quantitative accuracy, allowing for the absolute counting of mRNA transcripts while mitigating amplification biases inherent in library preparation. Although the transcript capture efficiency per cell is lower than some low-throughput methods, the sheer volume of cells analyzed compensates for this, providing robust statistical power for clustering and cell type discovery.

Looking forward, the accessibility and scalability of Drop-seq will democratize large-scale single-cell analysis. It provides a foundational tool for the Human Cell Atlas initiative and similar efforts aimed at cataloging every cell type in the body. Furthermore, the principles of microfluidic droplet compartmentalization and molecular barcoding demonstrated here can be adapted for other single-cell multi-omic applications, including epigenomic and proteomic profiling. In conclusion, Drop-seq fundamentally transforms our approach to studying complex biological systems, offering a high-resolution, high-throughput lens through which to explore the cellular diversity that underpins tissue function and disease pathogenesis. As the technology matures, improvements in capture efficiency and droplet microfluidics will likely yield even deeper insights into lowly expressed transcripts and subtle regulatory networks. Ultimately, Drop-seq serves as a vital platform that will accelerate the systematic deconstruction of complex tissues, facilitating a new generation of discoveries in cellular biology, neurobiology, and precision medicine.

It was a profound leap for modern transcriptomics. Drop-seq (alongside the parallel inDrop method) successfully democratized large-scale single-cell genomics, forcing biology to evolve from analyzing messy bulk tissue averages to mapping intricate cellular ecosystems at single-cell resolution, laying the direct technological groundwork for massive global initiatives like the Human Cell Atlas.

paper_title: Incorporation of Pseudouridine Into mRNA Yields Superior Nonimmunogenic Vector with Increased Translational Capacity and Biological Stability - 2008 - Molecular Therapy
display_title: Incorporation of Pseudouridine Into mRNA Yields Superior Nonimmunogenic Vector with Increased Translational Capacity and Biological Stability
title: Incorporation of Pseudouridine Into mRNA Yields Superior Nonimmunogenic Vector with Increased Translational Capacity and Biological Stability
text: In vitro-transcribed mRNAs encoding physiologically important proteins have considerable potential for therapeutic applications. However, in its present form, mRNA is unfeasible for clinical use because of its labile and immunogenic nature. Here, we investigated whether incorporation of naturally modified nucleotides into transcripts would confer enhanced biological properties to mRNA. We found that mRNAs containing pseudouridines have a higher translational capacity than unmodified mRNAs when tested in mammalian cells and lysates or administered intravenously into mice at 0.015-0.15 mg/kg doses. The delivered mRNA and the encoded protein could be detected in the spleen at 1, 4, and 24 hours after the injection, where both products were at significantly higher levels when pseudouridine-containing mRNA was administered. Even at higher doses, only the unmodified mRNA was immunogenic, inducing high serum levels of interferon-alpha (IFNα). These findings indicate that nucleoside modification is an effective approach to enhance stability and translational capacity of mRNA while diminishing its immunogenicity in vivo. Improved properties conferred by pseudouridine make such mRNA a promising tool for both gene replacement and vaccination.
historical_context: In 2008, Katalin Karikó, Drew Weissman, and colleagues expanded upon their seminal 2005 work to demonstrate that substituting standard uridine with the modified nucleoside pseudouridine not only evades an inflammatory immune response but also drastically amplifies the translation and stability of engineered mRNA *in vivo*. This critical technical optimization definitively proved that modified mRNA could serve as a safe, highly potent vector for protein replacement and vaccination. This pioneering research culminated in the 2023 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine awarded to Karikó and Weissman. The Nobel committee praised their discoveries regarding nucleoside base modifications that revolutionized our grasp of how mRNA interacts with the immune system, paving the way for the unprecedented, rapid deployment of highly effective mRNA vaccines against COVID-19.

title: Incorporation of Pseudouridine Into mRNA Yields Superior Nonimmunogenic Vector with Increased Translational Capacity and Biological Stability
text: In vitro-transcribed mRNA is an attractive nonviral vector because it directs rapid protein synthesis without risk of genomic integration, yet its clinical utility has been limited by poor stability, low translational efficiency, and potent activation of innate immunity. We examined whether incorporation of naturally occurring modified nucleosides could overcome these barriers. Reporter mRNAs in which uridine was replaced with pseudouridine were generated by in vitro transcription and compared with unmodified and other modified transcripts in cell-free systems, cultured mammalian cells, primary cells, and mice. Pseudouridine-containing mRNAs produced substantially more protein than unmodified mRNAs in rabbit reticulocyte lysates and in multiple mammalian cell types, including dendritic cells, splenocytes, fibroblasts, CHO cells, and neurons. The enhancement was independent of cap and poly(A) elements and persisted in RIG-I-deficient cells, indicating that it was not explained by reduced type I interferon signaling. Following systemic delivery to mice, pseudouridine-containing luciferase mRNA yielded higher and longer-lasting protein expression in spleen, while failing to induce serum IFN-alpha or splenic TNF-alpha mRNA. Thus, pseudouridine modification renders mRNA a biologically stable, highly translatable, and nonimmunogenic vector for safe protein expression in vitro and in vivo.

text: In vitro-transcribed mRNA offers a promising nonviral platform for gene replacement and vaccination, but its therapeutic application has been hindered by inefficient translation, poor stability, and strong activation of the innate immune system. We previously reported that incorporation of naturally occurring modified nucleosides into RNA abolishes recognition by Toll-like receptors and RIG-I. Here, we systematically tested mRNAs containing pseudouridine, 5-methylcytidine, N6-methyladenosine, 5-methyluridine, or 2-thiouridine for their translational potential and in vivo immunogenicity. Strikingly, pseudouridine-containing mRNA was translated more efficiently than its unmodified counterpart in rabbit reticulocyte lysates, in cultured mammalian cell lines, and in primary cells including dendritic cells, fibroblasts, splenocytes, and neurons, yielding up to ten-fold more protein. Using RIG-I-deficient mouse embryonic fibroblasts, we excluded a role for type I interferon in this translational enhancement. Following intravenous administration into mice, pseudouridine-modified mRNA accumulated in the spleen, produced markedly higher and more sustained reporter protein expression, and elicited no detectable proinflammatory cytokine response. These findings establish pseudouridine-containing mRNA as a uniquely effective, stable, and nonimmunogenic vector for in vivo protein expression.

title: Incorporation of Pseudouridine Into mRNA Yields Superior Nonimmunogenic Vector with Increased Translational Capacity and Biological Stability
text: In vitro-synthesized mRNA holds great promise for gene therapy due to its rapid protein expression and lack of insertional mutagenesis risks. However, its clinical application has been severely limited by high immunogenicity and relative instability. Recent findings show that incorporating naturally occurring modified nucleosides into RNA suppresses immune activation. Here, we investigate the translational potential and in vivo immunogenicity of mRNAs containing modified nucleosides. We discovered that substituting uridine with pseudouridine (Ψ) in in vitro-transcribed mRNA not only abrogates immune activation but also significantly enhances its translational capacity and biological stability in mammalian cells. This superior translation is independent of cap and poly(A) tail length and does not rely on the evasion of RIG-I-mediated interferon responses, as demonstrated in wild-type and RIG-I(-/-) fibroblasts. Crucially, intravenous administration of Ψ-modified mRNA in mice resulted in robust, prolonged protein expression—primarily in the spleen—without inducing inflammatory cytokines like interferon-alpha and tumor necrosis factor-alpha, which are strongly triggered by unmodified mRNA. Our results demonstrate that Ψ-containing mRNA is a highly efficient, stable, and non-immunogenic vector, representing a powerful new platform for safe and effective in vivo gene replacement and therapeutic vaccination.

title: Incorporation of Pseudouridine Into mRNA Yields Superior Nonimmunogenic Vector with Increased Translational Capacity and Biological Stability
text:
In vitro-synthesized mRNA seems an ideal nonviral gene replacement tool with many inherent advantages, including rapid protein production and efficient transduction of primary cells. mRNA-based therapy also avoids deleterious side effects (e.g., integration into chromosomes) that limit clinical application of most virus- and DNA-based vectors. The first in vivo gene transfer therapy using mRNA was reported in 1990. Subsequently, however, mRNA has rarely been used for introducing genetic material into animals or even into cultured cells. The use of mRNA has been mostly limited to vaccination in which antigen-encoding transcripts were administered in vivo or delivered to dendritic cells (DCs) ex vivo in order to induce cellular and humoral immune responses. However, RNA is unsuitable for gene replacement because of its high immunogenicity and low effectiveness.

Recent studies have demonstrated that RNA activates cells of the innate immune system by stimulating Toll-like receptors (TLRs), specifically TLR3, TLR7, and TLR8. However, when naturally occurring modified nucleosides, for example, pseudouridine (Ψ), 5-methylcytidine (m5C), N6-methyladenosine (m6A), 5-methyluridine (m5U), or 2-thiouridine (s2U) were incorporated into the transcript, most of the TLRs were no longer activated. The immunostimulatory potential of such RNAs as measured by proinflammatory cytokine release and induction of co-stimulatory molecules was greatly diminished. Hartmann et al. also reported that modified nucleosides, including s2U and Ψ, abrogated 5'-triphosphate RNA-mediated activation of another RNA-responsive immune sensor, retinoic acid-inducible protein I (RIG-I). If any of the in vitro transcripts containing nucleoside modifications would remain translatable and also avoid immune activation in vivo, such an mRNA could be developed into a new therapeutic tool for both gene replacement and vaccination. Accordingly, in this report, we tested the nucleoside-modified mRNAs for their translational potentials and in vivo immune characteristics.

Our results reveal that incorporating pseudouridine, a naturally occurring modified nucleoside, into mRNA not only suppresses RNA-mediated immune activation in vitro and in vivo, but also enhances the translational capacity of the RNA. These characteristics and the ease of generating such an RNA by in vitro transcription make Ψ-containing mRNA a unique tool for expression of any protein in vitro and in vivo.

This critical technical optimization definitively proved that modified mRNA could serve as a safe, highly potent vector for protein replacement and vaccination. This pioneering research culminated in the 2023 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine awarded to Karikó and Weissman. The Nobel committee praised their discoveries regarding nucleoside base modifications that revolutionized our grasp of how mRNA interacts with the immune system, paving the way for the unprecedented, rapid deployment of highly effective mRNA vaccines against COVID-19.
