title: Incorporation of Pseudouridine Into mRNA Yields Superior Nonimmunogenic Vector with Increased Translational Capacity and Biological Stability
text:
The ability to deliver genetic information into mammalian cells safely and efficiently remains a central goal of gene therapy. Viral vectors have been developed for this purpose and can mediate robust expression of therapeutic proteins, but their use is limited by immunogenicity, restricted packaging capacity, difficulties in repeated administration, and the risk of insertional mutagenesis when integrating vectors are used. Plasmid DNA avoids some of these concerns, yet it must enter the nucleus and be transcribed before protein can be produced, a requirement that limits its efficacy in many primary and nondividing cells. Thus, alternative vectors that can direct transient and controllable protein expression without altering the genome are needed.

In vitro-transcribed mRNA represents such an alternative. Once delivered to the cytoplasm, mRNA can be translated immediately, does not require nuclear entry, and is incapable of genomic integration. In principle, these properties make mRNA suitable for vaccination, protein replacement, and other applications in which temporary expression is desirable. Despite these advantages, therapeutic use of mRNA has been hindered by two major limitations. First, RNA is considered biologically unstable and is rapidly degraded by ubiquitous ribonucleases. Second, in vitro-transcribed RNA is strongly immunostimulatory. Cellular receptors that detect foreign RNA, including endosomal Toll-like receptors and cytoplasmic RNA sensors, can induce type I interferons and inflammatory cytokines after exposure to unmodified transcripts. These innate immune responses can limit translation of the delivered RNA and raise safety concerns for systemic administration.

A distinguishing feature of cellular RNA is the presence of numerous naturally modified nucleosides. Transfer RNA, ribosomal RNA, small nuclear RNA, and other noncoding RNAs contain modified nucleotides that influence RNA structure, stability, and recognition by proteins. We and others have shown that incorporation of such modifications into RNA can reduce activation of innate immune receptors. Whether the same strategy can improve the functional properties of protein-encoding mRNA, however, has not been established.

Here, we examined whether replacement of canonical nucleotides with naturally occurring modified nucleotides could generate mRNAs with improved biological activity. We tested a panel of nucleoside-modified transcripts in cell-free systems, cultured mammalian cells, and mice. We found that complete substitution of uridine with pseudouridine markedly enhances translational capacity, increases biological stability, and prevents induction of interferon-alpha after systemic delivery. These findings identify pseudouridine-containing mRNA as a promising nonintegrating vector for therapeutic protein expression.

This critical technical optimization definitively proved that modified mRNA could serve as a safe, highly potent vector for protein replacement and vaccination. This pioneering research culminated in the 2023 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine awarded to Karikó and Weissman. The Nobel committee praised their discoveries regarding nucleoside base modifications that revolutionized our grasp of how mRNA interacts with the immune system, paving the way for the unprecedented, rapid deployment of highly effective mRNA vaccines against COVID-19.

title: Incorporation of Pseudouridine Into mRNA Yields Superior Nonimmunogenic Vector with Increased Translational Capacity and Biological Stability
text:
Gene therapy holds immense promise for treating a wide array of genetic and acquired diseases by introducing functional genetic material into patient cells. Historically, efforts have predominantly relied on viral vectors or plasmid DNA to deliver therapeutic genes. However, these traditional DNA-based approaches are fraught with significant challenges, including the risk of insertional mutagenesis, potential oncogene activation, and robust immune responses against viral capsids. Furthermore, for DNA vectors to be effective, they must breach the nuclear envelope and be transcribed, a process that is often inefficient, particularly in non-dividing primary cells.

In vitro-transcribed messenger RNA (mRNA) presents an attractive alternative to DNA-based gene delivery. Because mRNA exerts its function in the cytoplasm, it circumvents the need for nuclear entry, leading to rapid and efficient protein expression even in quiescent cells. Additionally, mRNA does not integrate into the host genome, thereby eliminating the risk of insertional mutagenesis. Despite these theoretical advantages, the clinical translation of mRNA therapeutics has been severely hampered by two major practical limitations: its inherent instability and its potent immunogenicity. Exogenous unmodified RNA is rapidly degraded by ubiquitous ribonucleases and is recognized by the innate immune system as a pathogen-associated molecular pattern, triggering the secretion of pro-inflammatory cytokines such as interferon-alpha (IFN-α) and tumor necrosis factor-alpha (TNF-α). This innate immune activation not only causes toxicity but also profoundly suppresses the translation of the delivered mRNA.

In nature, cellular RNAs are extensively decorated with post-transcriptional nucleoside modifications, such as pseudouridine (Ψ), which are thought to distinguish self from non-self RNA and prevent auto-immune reactions. Building upon our previous findings that mammalian RNA and nucleoside-modified in vitro transcripts evade detection by Toll-like receptors, we sought to systematically investigate the impact of these natural modifications on the biological properties of therapeutic mRNA. Here, we demonstrate that the incorporation of pseudouridine into in vitro-transcribed mRNA dramatically enhances its translational capacity and biological stability while simultaneously abolishing its immunogenicity in vivo, establishing Ψ-modified mRNA as a vastly superior vector for gene replacement and therapeutic applications.

This critical technical optimization definitively proved that modified mRNA could serve as a safe, highly potent vector for protein replacement and vaccination. This pioneering research culminated in the 2023 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine awarded to Karikó and Weissman. The Nobel committee praised their discoveries regarding nucleoside base modifications that revolutionized our grasp of how mRNA interacts with the immune system, paving the way for the unprecedented, rapid deployment of highly effective mRNA vaccines against COVID-19.

title: Incorporation of Pseudouridine Into mRNA Yields Superior Nonimmunogenic Vector with Increased Translational Capacity and Biological Stability
text:
Progress in understanding the molecular details of many human diseases and instant access to human gene collections brings the possibility of using genes for treatment closer to reality. However, safe and effective gene therapy, which would take advantage of all these recent achievements, is still lacking. Unlike viral- and plasmid-based vectors, in vitro-transcribed mRNAs have never been vigorously tested for in vivo gene replacement, only for therapeutic vaccination because they were generally considered labile, inefficient, and immunogenic. Our most significant finding, however, demonstrates that in vitro-transcribed mRNAs containing pseudouridines possess superior translational capacities, increased biological stability, and no immunogenicity. The potential therapeutic advantages of using mRNA, especially Ψ-containing mRNA, over plasmid and viral vectors for delivering genetic material are numerous: (i) improved safety, because RNA is inherently incapable of integrating into the genome, thus preventing deleterious side effects that have stalled other vectors; (ii) lack of inflammatory response to Ψ-containing mRNA, thereby avoiding devastating systemic inflammation that can be fatal with a viral vector; (iii) efficient transduction of primary cells, unlike DNA, RNA does not require cell proliferation for expression of the encoded protein; (iv) rapid protein expression, mRNAs are translated within minutes following entry into the cytoplasm, whereas plasmids require time-consuming nuclear import and transcription; (v) virtually no size limit for the encoded proteins, because long mRNAs can be easily obtained, unlike viral vectors that possess limited packaging capacities; (vi) the extent and duration of the encoded protein expression can be closely controlled because mRNAs have shorter half lives and, unlike other vectors, do not replicate.

Our most surprising result is that mRNAs with Ψ modification have a higher translational capacity than those without modification in all tested mammalian systems. Although further studies are needed to understand the reason for this difference fully, using RIG-I(-/-) MEFs, we excluded a role for RIG-I and type I IFN in this phenomenon. It is possible that protein synthesis might be inhibited by RNA-dependent protein kinase activated by structural motifs present in mRNA-containing uridine but not Ψ modification. Consistent with this interpretation, attenuated translation of nonmodified mRNAs as compared to Ψ-modified mRNAs was observed in mammalian cells and lysates that contain RNA-dependent protein kinase but not observed in wheat germ extracts, which have no RNA-dependent protein kinase.

A likely contributing factor to the enhanced translation observed with Ψ modification is an increase in biological stability of the mRNAs. Indeed, higher resistance to hydrolysis by phosphodiesterases from snake venom and spleen has been reported when uridine was replaced with Ψ in dinucleotide substrates. Previous studies have also demonstrated that Ψ stabilizes RNA secondary structures by promoting base stacking, which could slow degradation. However, stability of mRNAs containing either uridines or pseudouridines was the same when tested by in vitro assays using human skin-associated RNases. Enhanced translation might be another factor that improves stability by protecting the RNA with high ribosome occupancy.

From a potential adverse event standpoint, it is important to note that Ψ, a natural constituent of RNA, likely lacks toxicity. In fact, Ψ is the most common modification found in RNA and natural pathways for metabolism of Ψ-modified mRNA and subsequent disposal into the urine exist.

In previous studies, we demonstrated that bacterial RNA and in vitro transcripts without modified nucleoside constituents stimulated human TLR3, TLR7, and TLR8, while mammalian RNA and in vitro transcripts containing nucleoside modifications including Ψ were minimally or nonstimulatory. We also observed high levels of TNF-α and IFN-α secretion by human primary DCs when exposed to RNAs that lacked nucleoside modification, but not when Ψ-modified RNAs were used. Because mammalian RNA, unlike bacterial RNA, abundantly possesses modified nucleosides, these results collectively demonstrate that nucleoside modifications suppress RNA immunogenicity. This study extends these findings to in vivo conditions by showing induction of IFN-α secretion and an increase of TNF-α mRNA in mice following administration of unmodified, but not Ψ modified, mRNA.

In summary, we demonstrate that mRNA containing Ψ is a new and effective vector to deliver genetic material for protein expression into both cultured cells and animals. The presence of Ψ in mRNA improved its translational capacity and overall stability. It also diminished its immunogenicity in vivo. Administering Ψ-containing mRNA at very low doses (0.015 mg/kg) resulted in robust expression of the encoded protein. We identified spleen as the target organ where the intravenously delivered, lipofectin-complexed mRNA and its encoded protein accumulate. These collective findings are important steps in developing the therapeutic potential of mRNA, such as using modified mRNA as an alternative to conventional vaccination and as a means for expressing clinically beneficial proteins in vivo safely and effectively.

This critical technical optimization definitively proved that modified mRNA could serve as a safe, highly potent vector for protein replacement and vaccination. This pioneering research culminated in the 2023 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine awarded to Karikó and Weissman. The Nobel committee praised their discoveries regarding nucleoside base modifications that revolutionized our grasp of how mRNA interacts with the immune system, paving the way for the unprecedented, rapid deployment of highly effective mRNA vaccines against COVID-19.

paper_title: Incorporation of Pseudouridine Into mRNA Yields Superior Nonimmunogenic Vector with Increased Translational Capacity and Biological Stability - 2008 - Molecular Therapy
display_title: Incorporation of Pseudouridine Into mRNA Yields Superior Nonimmunogenic Vector with Increased Translational Capacity and Biological Stability
title: Incorporation of Pseudouridine Into mRNA Yields Superior Nonimmunogenic Vector with Increased Translational Capacity and Biological Stability
text:
The data presented here demonstrate that the biological properties of in vitro-transcribed mRNA can be substantially improved by the incorporation of a naturally occurring modified nucleoside. Among the modifications tested, pseudouridine was unique in conferring two desirable characteristics to mRNA: it markedly increased translational capacity and, at the same time, diminished innate immune activation. These findings indicate that the limitations that have restricted the therapeutic use of synthetic mRNA are not intrinsic to RNA as a class of vectors, but can be altered by changing the chemical composition of the transcript.

The enhanced translation observed with Ψ-containing mRNA was unexpected, because pseudouridine is not known to be a constituent of natural mRNA. Nevertheless, replacement of uridine with Ψ did not interfere with protein synthesis in mammalian translation systems. On the contrary, Ψ-containing luciferase, β-galactosidase, and GFP mRNAs produced more encoded protein than the corresponding unmodified transcripts in reticulocyte lysates, cultured mammalian cell lines, and several primary cell types. This enhancement was most pronounced in intact cells, where Ψ-containing mRNA reproducibly yielded higher numbers of protein-expressing cells and greater levels of reporter activity. Importantly, the effect was observed with transcripts containing different 5'- and 3'-terminal structures and was additive with cap, translational enhancer sequences, and poly(A) tails. Thus, Ψ modification appears to improve mRNA function through a mechanism distinct from those established structural elements that facilitate translation.

The time-course experiments suggest that increased biological stability contributes to the superior performance of Ψ-containing mRNA. Although luciferase activity from both modified and unmodified transcripts was detectable shortly after RNA delivery, the difference between the two RNAs increased with time. In cultured cells, Ψ-containing transcripts continued to support higher levels of protein expression at later time points, and, in mice, luciferase activity remained detectable at 24 hours only after administration of Ψ-modified mRNA. Northern blot analyses confirmed that administered RNA could be recovered from the spleen in intact and partially degraded forms, supporting the conclusion that chemical modification can alter the persistence of synthetic mRNA in vivo. Whether this increased stability reflects resistance to ribonucleases, altered intracellular trafficking, reduced engagement of antiviral pathways, or improved association with translation complexes remains to be determined.

The increased translational capacity of Ψ-containing mRNA was not simply a consequence of avoiding RIG-I-mediated interferon induction. Although unmodified RNAs containing 5'-triphosphates are capable of activating RIG-I, Ψ-containing mRNA was translated more efficiently in both wild-type and RIG-I-deficient cells. Moreover, capped unmodified transcripts that induced little IFN-β in wild-type cells still translated less efficiently than Ψ-containing counterparts. These results suggest that Ψ directly improves the functional behavior of the RNA in mammalian cells, rather than acting only by suppressing one antiviral signaling pathway. Because modified nucleosides can influence RNA secondary structure, ribosome scanning, codon-anticodon interactions, and RNA-binding protein recognition, any of these properties may contribute to the higher translational output observed here.

Equally important, Ψ-containing mRNA lacked the strong immunostimulatory activity of unmodified RNA following systemic administration. At doses that supported robust splenic protein expression, unmodified mRNA induced high serum IFN-α and increased splenic TNF-α mRNA, whereas Ψ-modified mRNA did not. This result extends previous in vitro observations that nucleoside modifications suppress RNA recognition by TLRs and RIG-I and shows that the same principle applies after in vivo delivery. For gene replacement, such nonimmunogenicity is essential, because repeated or high-dose administration of an inflammatory RNA vector would be expected to limit expression and produce unacceptable toxicity. For vaccination, the ability to tune immunogenicity independently from antigen expression may also be valuable, allowing Ψ-containing mRNA to be combined with selected adjuvants when immune activation is desired.

The present study also reveals that not all modified nucleosides are suitable for therapeutic mRNA design. Some modifications, such as m5C and m5U, were compatible with translation, whereas s2U and especially m6A, when substituted throughout the transcript, severely reduced or abolished protein production. These observations underscore that the position, abundance, and chemical nature of nucleoside modification are critical determinants of mRNA function. In natural RNAs, modifications are placed selectively, but in the present approach every occurrence of a given nucleotide was replaced during transcription. Future studies may identify combinations or partial substitutions that further optimize translation, stability, and immunological properties.

Our in vivo experiments showed preferential reporter expression in the spleen after intravenous delivery of lipofectin-complexed mRNA. This distribution likely reflects the delivery vehicle and route of administration rather than an absolute tissue restriction of Ψ-containing mRNA. Development of improved formulations and targeting strategies should expand the range of tissues that can be transfected efficiently. Nevertheless, the present results establish proof of principle that chemically modified synthetic mRNA can mediate protein expression in vivo while avoiding potent systemic cytokine induction.

Taken together, these findings support Ψ-containing mRNA as a superior vector for transient gene expression. It combines rapid protein production, lack of genomic integration, improved biological stability, and markedly reduced immunogenicity. These attributes make modified mRNA especially attractive for applications in which short-term expression of a therapeutic protein is sufficient or desirable, including protein replacement, regenerative medicine, and immunotherapy. More broadly, the results suggest that nucleoside modification represents a general strategy for converting synthetic RNA from an inherently immunostimulatory molecule into a controllable and efficient platform for gene delivery.
historical_context: In 2008, Katalin Karikó, Drew Weissman, and colleagues expanded upon their seminal 2005 work to demonstrate that substituting standard uridine with the modified nucleoside pseudouridine not only evades an inflammatory immune response but also drastically amplifies the translation and stability of engineered mRNA *in vivo*. This critical technical optimization definitively proved that modified mRNA could serve as a safe, highly potent vector for protein replacement and vaccination. This pioneering research culminated in the 2023 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine awarded to Karikó and Weissman. The Nobel committee praised their discoveries regarding nucleoside base modifications that revolutionized our grasp of how mRNA interacts with the immune system, paving the way for the unprecedented, rapid deployment of highly effective mRNA vaccines against COVID-19.

paper_title: Incorporation of Pseudouridine Into mRNA Yields Superior Nonimmunogenic Vector with Increased Translational Capacity and Biological Stability - 2008 - Molecular Therapy
display_title: Incorporation of Pseudouridine Into mRNA Yields Superior Nonimmunogenic Vector with Increased Translational Capacity and Biological Stability
title: Incorporation of Pseudouridine Into mRNA Yields Superior Nonimmunogenic Vector with Increased Translational Capacity and Biological Stability
text:
The findings presented in this study demonstrate that the incorporation of pseudouridine (Ψ) into in vitro-transcribed (IVT) mRNA fundamentally alters its biological properties, yielding a vastly superior vector for gene transfer. By replacing standard uridine with pseudouridine, we have successfully addressed the two primary obstacles that have historically hindered the therapeutic application of mRNA: its intrinsic immunogenicity and its instability leading to poor translational capacity in vivo. Our results show that Ψ-modified mRNA is highly resistant to RNase degradation, exhibits significantly enhanced translational efficiency, and most importantly, evades activation of the innate immune system.

The failure of unmodified IVT mRNA to serve as an effective therapeutic agent has largely been due to its recognition by Toll-like receptors (TLRs) and other intracellular sensors of the innate immune system. This recognition triggers a robust inflammatory response, characterized by the production of type I interferons and pro-inflammatory cytokines, which not only causes toxicity but also leads to the rapid suppression of protein translation and degradation of the exogenously introduced RNA. By incorporating pseudouridine, a naturally occurring nucleoside modification widely found in mammalian RNA, we effectively camouflage the mRNA from these immune sensors. The modified mRNA does not activate TLR3, TLR7, or TLR8, resulting in a profound reduction in the inflammatory cascade.

Concurrently, the structural stabilization provided by pseudouridine incorporation protects the transcript from rapid enzymatic cleavage, significantly extending its half-life both in vitro and in vivo. This increased stability, combined with the evasion of immune-mediated translational suppression, translates to a massive increase in protein production. In our murine models, the administration of Ψ-modified mRNA encoding reporter genes resulted in protein expression levels that were orders of magnitude higher and significantly more sustained compared to unmodified mRNA.

The implications of these findings for gene therapy and vaccine development are transformative. Unlike DNA-based vectors, mRNA does not integrate into the host genome, eliminating the risk of insertional mutagenesis. Furthermore, mRNA expression is transient and fully reversible, providing a favorable safety profile for clinical applications. The ability to deliver highly efficient, non-immunogenic mRNA opens the door to a wide array of therapeutic interventions.

For vaccine development, Ψ-modified mRNA provides a safe and potent platform for expressing viral antigens to elicit robust cellular and humoral immune responses, without the confounding effects of vector-induced inflammation. In the realm of protein replacement therapy, this technology could be utilized to restore the expression of defective or missing proteins in genetic disorders. Moreover, modified mRNA holds great promise for regenerative medicine, where it could be used to transiently express transcription factors for cellular reprogramming or directed differentiation, avoiding the risks associated with viral vectors.

Future studies must focus on optimizing delivery systems, such as lipid nanoparticles, to further enhance the targeted uptake and intracellular release of the modified mRNA in specific tissues. Additionally, exploring the effects of combining pseudouridine with other naturally occurring nucleoside modifications, or exploring sequence optimization strategies, could further maximize translational output and fine-tune the pharmacokinetics of the mRNA therapeutics.

In conclusion, the simple yet elegant substitution of pseudouridine into IVT mRNA overcomes the critical barriers of immunogenicity and instability. This study establishes a robust, highly efficient, and safe platform for RNA-based gene delivery, heralding a new paradigm in molecular medicine. The superior characteristics of Ψ-modified mRNA position it as a highly promising candidate for a broad spectrum of clinical applications, potentially revolutionizing our approach to vaccination, gene therapy, and regenerative therapies in the years to come. This conceptual breakthrough proves that the innate immune system distinguishes self from non-self RNA largely based on the presence of specific nucleoside modifications. By mimicking these natural modifications, we can hijack the cellular machinery for therapeutic protein production while remaining undetected by host defenses. Ultimately, the pseudouridine-modified mRNA platform offers unparalleled flexibility and rapid development cycles, representing a monumental shift in how we might address both emerging infectious diseases and chronic genetic conditions.

This critical technical optimization definitively proved that modified mRNA could serve as a safe, highly potent vector for protein replacement and vaccination. This pioneering research culminated in the 2023 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine awarded to Karikó and Weissman. The Nobel committee praised their discoveries regarding nucleoside base modifications that revolutionized our grasp of how mRNA interacts with the immune system, paving the way for the unprecedented, rapid deployment of highly effective mRNA vaccines against COVID-19.

paper_title: Induction of Pluripotent Stem Cells from Mouse Embryonic and Adult Fibroblast Cultures by Defined Factors - 2006 - Cell
display_title: Induction of Pluripotent Stem Cells from Mouse Embryonic and Adult Fibroblast Cultures by Defined Factors
title: Induction of Pluripotent Stem Cells from Mouse Embryonic and Adult Fibroblast Cultures by Defined Factors
text: Differentiated cells can be reprogrammed to an embryonic-like state by transfer of nuclear contents into oocytes or by fusion with embryonic stem (ES) cells. Little is known about factors that induce this reprogramming. Here, we demonstrate induction of pluripotent stem cells from mouse embryonic or adult fibroblasts by introducing four factors, Oct3/4, Sox2, c-Myc, and Klf4, under ES cell culture conditions. Unexpectedly, Nanog was dispensable. These cells, which we designated iPS (induced pluripotent stem) cells, exhibit the morphology and growth properties of ES cells and express ES cell marker genes. Subcutaneous transplantation of iPS cells into nude mice resulted in tumors containing a variety of tissues from all three germ layers. Following injection into blastocysts, iPS cells contributed to mouse embryonic development. These data demonstrate that pluripotent stem cells can be directly generated from fibroblast cultures by the addition of only a few defined factors.
historical_context: In 2006, Kazutoshi Takahashi and Shinya Yamanaka published a groundbreaking paper demonstrating that fully mature, differentiated adult somatic cells (mouse fibroblasts) could be completely reprogrammed back into an embryonic-like, pluripotent state through the introduction of just four transcription factors: Oct3/4, Sox2, c-Myc, and Klf4. These induced pluripotent stem (iPS) cells elegantly bypassed the ethical controversies surrounding embryonic stem cells while offering tantalizing personalized regenerative possibilities. This work led directly to Shinya Yamanaka securing the 2012 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine (shared with John Gurdon). The Nobel committee celebrated the revelation that specialized cells can be reversed to an immature state capable of developing into all bodily tissues, a concept that fundamentally rewrote the rules of cellular development and differentiation.

title: Induction of Pluripotent Stem Cells from Mouse Embryonic and Adult Fibroblast Cultures by Defined Factors
text: Embryonic stem (ES) cells can proliferate indefinitely while retaining the ability to differentiate into derivatives of all three germ layers. Somatic nuclei can be reprogrammed by oocytes or by fusion with ES cells, but the defined factors sufficient for inducing pluripotency have remained unclear. We screened 24 ES cell-associated genes using activation of an Fbx15-bgeo reporter as a selectable indicator of reprogramming in mouse fibroblasts. Introduction of all candidates generated ES cell-like colonies, and sequential withdrawal experiments identified four critical factors: Oct3/4, Sox2, c-Myc, and Klf4. These factors induced cells from mouse embryonic fibroblasts and adult tail-tip fibroblasts that expressed many ES cell markers, showed ES cell-like morphology and proliferation, and exhibited epigenetic changes at pluripotency-associated loci. The induced pluripotent stem cells differentiated into tissues of all three germ layers in vitro and in teratomas, and contributed to embryonic development after blastocyst injection. Thus, pluripotent cells can be generated directly from fibroblast cultures by defined factors.

These induced pluripotent stem (iPS) cells elegantly bypassed the ethical controversies surrounding embryonic stem cells while offering tantalizing personalized regenerative possibilities. This work led directly to Shinya Yamanaka securing the 2012 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine (shared with John Gurdon). The Nobel committee celebrated the revelation that specialized cells can be reversed to an immature state capable of developing into all bodily tissues, a concept that fundamentally rewrote the rules of cellular development and differentiation.

title: Induction of Pluripotent Stem Cells from Mouse Embryonic and Adult Fibroblast Cultures by Defined Factors
text: Embryonic stem (ES) cells possess the unique ability to grow indefinitely while maintaining the potential to differentiate into all three germ layers. Generating patient-specific pluripotent cells directly from somatic cells would circumvent both the ethical and immunological issues associated with ES cell therapies. Here, we demonstrate the induction of pluripotent stem cells from mouse embryonic and adult fibroblast cultures by the retroviral introduction of a defined set of just four transcription factors: Oct3/4, Sox2, c-Myc, and Klf4. These findings demonstrate that somatic cells can be reprogrammed to a pluripotent state by a small number of defined factors, opening new avenues for generating patient-specific stem cells.

title: Induction of Pluripotent Stem Cells from Mouse Embryonic and Adult Fibroblast Cultures by Defined Factors
text:
Embryonic stem (ES) cells, which are derived from the inner cell mass of mammalian blastocysts, have the ability to grow indefinitely while maintaining pluripotency and the ability to differentiate into cells of all three germ layers. Human ES cells might be used to treat a host of diseases, such as Parkinson's disease, spinal cord injury, and diabetes. However, there are ethical difficulties regarding the use of human embryos, as well as the problem of tissue rejection following transplantation in patients. One way to circumvent these issues is the generation of pluripotent cells directly from the patients' own cells.

Somatic cells can be reprogrammed by transferring their nuclear contents into oocytes or by fusion with ES cells, indicating that unfertilized eggs and ES cells contain factors that can confer totipotency or pluripotency to somatic cells. We hypothesized that the factors that play important roles in the maintenance of ES cell identity also play pivotal roles in the induction of pluripotency in somatic cells.

Several transcription factors, including Oct3/4, Sox2, and Nanog, function in the maintenance of pluripotency in both early embryos and ES cells. Several genes that are frequently upregulated in tumors, such as Stat3, E-Ras, c-myc, Klf4, and beta-catenin, have been shown to contribute to the long-term maintenance of the ES cell phenotype and the rapid proliferation of ES cells in culture. In addition, we have identified several other genes that are specifically expressed in ES cells.

In this study, we examined whether these factors could induce pluripotency in somatic cells. By combining four selected factors, we were able to generate pluripotent cells, which we call induced pluripotent stem (iPS) cells, directly from mouse embryonic or adult fibroblast cultures.
historical_context: In 2006, Kazutoshi Takahashi and Shinya Yamanaka published a groundbreaking paper demonstrating that fully mature, differentiated adult somatic cells (mouse fibroblasts) could be completely reprogrammed back into an embryonic-like, pluripotent state through the introduction of just four transcription factors: Oct3/4, Sox2, c-Myc, and Klf4. These induced pluripotent stem (iPS) cells elegantly bypassed the ethical controversies surrounding embryonic stem cells while offering tantalizing personalized regenerative possibilities. This work led directly to Shinya Yamanaka securing the 2012 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine (shared with John Gurdon). The Nobel committee celebrated the revelation that specialized cells can be reversed to an immature state capable of developing into all bodily tissues, a concept that fundamentally rewrote the rules of cellular development and differentiation.

paper_title: Induction of Pluripotent Stem Cells from Mouse Embryonic and Adult Fibroblast Cultures by Defined Factors - 2006 - Cell
display_title: Induction of Pluripotent Stem Cells from Mouse Embryonic and Adult Fibroblast Cultures by Defined Factors
title: Induction of Pluripotent Stem Cells from Mouse Embryonic and Adult Fibroblast Cultures by Defined Factors
text:
Embryonic stem (ES) cells, which are derived from the inner cell mass of blastocysts, proliferate indefinitely in culture while maintaining the ability to give rise to all cell types of the body. These properties have made ES cells a useful system for analyzing early development and a potential source of differentiated cells for regenerative medicine. However, ES cells are obtained from preimplantation embryos, and the establishment of patient-specific pluripotent cells by this route remains technically and ethically difficult.

Somatic cells can acquire pluripotent properties after nuclear transfer into oocytes or after fusion with ES cells. These observations indicate that differentiated nuclei are not irreversibly fixed and that oocytes and ES cells contain activities capable of reprogramming somatic chromosomes to an embryonic state. Nevertheless, the molecular nature of these activities has remained largely unknown. Identification of defined factors that can induce pluripotency would provide a direct approach to studying the mechanisms of nuclear reprogramming and might allow the generation of pluripotent cells without using embryos or oocytes.

Several transcription factors have been implicated in the maintenance of ES cell identity. Oct3/4 is essential for the formation and maintenance of the pluripotent lineage, and changes in Oct3/4 expression levels induce differentiation. Sox2 and Nanog also have important roles in sustaining the undifferentiated state of ES cells. In addition, signaling molecules and transcriptional regulators associated with self-renewal, proliferation, and chromatin modification may contribute to the reprogramming activity present in ES cells.

We reasoned that factors required to maintain ES cell identity might also be sufficient, in appropriate combinations, to induce pluripotency in somatic cells. To test this possibility, we selected candidate genes preferentially expressed in ES cells or implicated in ES cell self-renewal and introduced them into mouse fibroblasts carrying a selectable reporter for activation of an ES cell-specific locus.
historical_context: In 2006, Kazutoshi Takahashi and Shinya Yamanaka published a groundbreaking paper demonstrating that fully mature, differentiated adult somatic cells (mouse fibroblasts) could be completely reprogrammed back into an embryonic-like, pluripotent state through the introduction of just four transcription factors: Oct3/4, Sox2, c-Myc, and Klf4. These induced pluripotent stem (iPS) cells elegantly bypassed the ethical controversies surrounding embryonic stem cells while offering tantalizing personalized regenerative possibilities. This work led directly to Shinya Yamanaka securing the 2012 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine (shared with John Gurdon). The Nobel committee celebrated the revelation that specialized cells can be reversed to an immature state capable of developing into all bodily tissues, a concept that fundamentally rewrote the rules of cellular development and differentiation.

display_title: Induction of Pluripotent Stem Cells from Mouse Embryonic and Adult Fibroblast Cultures by Defined Factors
title: Induction of Pluripotent Stem Cells from Mouse Embryonic and Adult Fibroblast Cultures by Defined Factors
text:
Embryonic stem (ES) cells, derived from the inner cell mass of mammalian blastocysts, possess the unique ability to grow indefinitely in culture while maintaining pluripotency—the capacity to differentiate into all somatic cell types. This remarkable dual property makes ES cells an invaluable resource for developmental biology, drug discovery, and regenerative medicine. However, the use of human ES cells is accompanied by significant ethical controversies regarding the destruction of human embryos, as well as biological challenges related to tissue rejection following transplantation.

A potential solution to these issues is the reprogramming of somatic cells back into a pluripotent state, enabling the generation of patient-specific, customized stem cells. It has been well established that the nuclei of differentiated cells can be reprogrammed to an embryonic-like state either by somatic cell nuclear transfer (SCNT) into unfertilized oocytes or by fusion with existing ES cells. These landmark experiments demonstrate that the unfertilized egg and the ES cell contain the necessary trans-acting factors to reset the epigenetic state of a somatic genome. Yet, the identity of these crucial reprogramming factors has remained elusive, and the technical difficulties and ethical concerns associated with SCNT limit its broad application.

We hypothesized that the factors that maintain pluripotency in ES cells might also be capable of inducing pluripotency in somatic cells. By identifying the core transcription factors responsible for establishing the ES cell identity, it might be possible to directly reprogram differentiated cells without the need for oocytes or cell fusion. In this study, we selected 24 candidate genes known to be highly expressed in ES cells and systematically tested their ability to induce pluripotency in mouse embryonic and adult fibroblasts.
historical_context: In 2006, Kazutoshi Takahashi and Shinya Yamanaka published a groundbreaking paper demonstrating that fully mature, differentiated adult somatic cells (mouse fibroblasts) could be completely reprogrammed back into an embryonic-like, pluripotent state through the introduction of just four transcription factors: Oct3/4, Sox2, c-Myc, and Klf4. These induced pluripotent stem (iPS) cells elegantly bypassed the ethical controversies surrounding embryonic stem cells while offering tantalizing personalized regenerative possibilities. This work led directly to Shinya Yamanaka securing the 2012 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine (shared with John Gurdon). The Nobel committee celebrated the revelation that specialized cells can be reversed to an immature state capable of developing into all bodily tissues, a concept that fundamentally rewrote the rules of cellular development and differentiation.

paper_title: Induction of Pluripotent Stem Cells from Mouse Embryonic and Adult Fibroblast Cultures by Defined Factors - 2006 - Cell
display_title: Induction of Pluripotent Stem Cells from Mouse Embryonic and Adult Fibroblast Cultures by Defined Factors
title: Induction of Pluripotent Stem Cells from Mouse Embryonic and Adult Fibroblast Cultures by Defined Factors
text:
Oct3/4, Sox2, and Nanog have been shown to function as core transcription factors in maintaining pluripotency. Among the three, we found that Oct3/4 and Sox2 are essential for the generation of iPS cells. Surprisingly, Nanog is dispensable. In addition, we identified c-Myc and Klf4 as essential factors. These two tumor-related factors could not be replaced by other oncogenes including E-Ras, Tcl1, beta-catenin, and Stat3.

The c-Myc protein has many downstream targets that enhance proliferation and transformation, many of which may have roles in the generation of iPS cells. Of note, c-Myc associates with histone acetyltransferase (HAT) complexes, including TRRAP, which is a core subunit of the TIP60 and GCN5 HAT complexes, CREB binding protein (CBP), and p300. Within the mammalian genome, there may be up to 25,000 c-Myc binding sites, many more than the predicted number of Oct3/4 and Sox2 binding sites. c-Myc protein may induce global histone acetylation, thus allowing Oct3/4 and Sox2 to bind to their specific target loci.

Klf4 has been shown to repress p53 directly, and p53 protein has been shown to suppress Nanog during ES cell differentiation. We found that iPS cells showed levels of p53 protein lower than those in MEFs. Thus, Klf4 might contribute to activation of Nanog and other ES cell-specific genes through p53 repression. Alternatively, Klf4 might function as an inhibitor of Myc-induced apoptosis through the repression of p53 in our system. On the other hand, Klf4 activates p21CIP1, thereby suppressing cell proliferation. This antiproliferation function of Klf4 might be inhibited by c-Myc, which suppresses the expression of p21CIP1. The balance between c-Myc and Klf4 may be important for the generation of iPS cells.

One question that remains concerns the origin of our iPS cells. With our retroviral expression system, we estimated that only a small portion of cells expressing the four factors became iPS cells. The low frequency suggests that rare tissue stem/progenitor cells that coexisted in the fibroblast cultures might have given rise to the iPS cells. Indeed, multipotent stem cells have been isolated from skin. One explanation for the low frequency of iPS cell derivation is that the four factors transform tissue stem cells. However, we found that the four factors induced iPS cells with comparably low efficiency even from bone marrow stroma, which should be more enriched in mesenchymal stem cells and other multipotent cells. Furthermore, cells induced by the three factors were nullipotent. DNA microarray analyses suggested that iPS-MEF4 cells and iPS-MEF3 cells have the same origin. These results do not favor multipotent tissue stem cells as the origin of iPS cells.

There are several other possibilities for the low frequency of iPS cell derivation. First, the levels of the four factors required for generation of pluripotent cells may have narrow ranges, and only a small portion of cells expressing all four of the factors at the right levels can acquire ES cell-like properties. Consistent with this idea, a mere 50% increase or decrease in Oct3/4 proteins induces differentiation of ES cells. iPS clones overexpressed the four factors when RNA levels were analyzed, but their protein levels were comparable to those in ES cells, suggesting that the iPS clones possess a mechanism (or mechanisms) that tightly regulates the protein levels of the four factors. We speculate that high amounts of the four factors are required in the initial stage of iPS cell generation, but, once they acquire ES cell-like status, too much of the factors are detrimental for self-renewal. Only a small portion of transduced cells show such appropriate transgene expression. Second, generation of pluripotent cells may require additional chromosomal alterations, which take place spontaneously during culture or are induced by some of the four factors. Although several iPS-TTFgfp4 clones had largely normal karyotypes, we cannot rule out the existence of minor chromosomal alterations. Site-specific retroviral insertion may also play a role. Southern blot analyses showed that each iPS clone has approximately 20 retroviral integrations. Some of these may have caused silencing or fusion with endogenous genes. Further studies will be required to determine the origin of iPS cells.

Another unsolved question is whether the four factors we identified play roles in reprogramming induced by fusion with ES cells or nuclear transfer into oocytes. Since the four factors are expressed in ES cells at high levels, it is reasonable to speculate that they are involved in the reprogramming machinery that exists in ES cells. Our result is also consistent with the finding that the reprogramming activity resides in the nucleus, but not in the cytoplasm, of ES cells. However, iPS cells were not identical to ES cells, as shown by the global gene-expression patterns and DNA methylation status. It is possible that we have missed additional important factors.

More obscure are the roles of the four factors, especially Klf4 and c-Myc, in the reprogramming observed in oocytes. Both Klf4 and c-Myc are dispensable for preimplantation mouse development. Furthermore, c-myc is not detected in oocytes. In contrast, L-myc is expressed maternally in oocytes. Klf4 and c-Myc might be compensated by these related proteins. It is highly likely that other factors are also required to induce complete reprogramming and totipotency in oocytes.

It is likely that the four factors from the transgenes are required for maintaining the iPS cells since the expression of Oct3/4 and Sox2 from the endogenous genes remained low. We intended to prove this by using transgenes flanked by two loxP sites. However, Cre-mediated recombination caused chromosomal rearrangements. Studies with conditional expression systems are required to answer this question.

We showed that the iPS cells can differentiate in vitro and in vivo even with the presence of the retroviral vectors containing the four factors. We found that Oct3/4 and Sox2 proteins decreased significantly during in vitro differentiation. Retroviral expression has been shown to be suppressed in ES cells and further silenced upon differentiation by epigenetic modifications, such as DNA methylation. The same mechanisms are likely to play roles in transgene repression in iPS cells. In addition, we found that iPS cells possess a mechanism (or mechanisms) that lowers protein levels of the transgenes and Nanog. The same mechanism may be enhanced during differentiation. However, silencing of Oct3/4 in some iPS cells was not complete, which may explain our inability to obtain live chimeric mice after blastocyst microinjection of iPS cells.

An unexpected finding in this study was the efficient activation of Fgf4 and Fbx15 by the combination of the three factors devoid of Sox2. It is also surprising that Nanog is dispensable for induction and maintenance of iPS cells. More detailed analyses of iPS cells will enhance our understanding of transcriptional regulation in pluripotent stem cells.

Our findings may have wider applications, as we have found that transgene reporters with other ES cell marker genes, such as Nanog, can replace the Fbx15 knockin during selection. Nevertheless, the finding is an important step in controlling pluripotency, which may eventually allow the creation of pluripotent cells directly from somatic cells of patients.
historical_context: In 2006, Kazutoshi Takahashi and Shinya Yamanaka published a groundbreaking paper demonstrating that fully mature, differentiated adult somatic cells (mouse fibroblasts) could be completely reprogrammed back into an embryonic-like, pluripotent state through the introduction of just four transcription factors: Oct3/4, Sox2, c-Myc, and Klf4. These induced pluripotent stem (iPS) cells elegantly bypassed the ethical controversies surrounding embryonic stem cells while offering tantalizing personalized regenerative possibilities. This work led directly to Shinya Yamanaka securing the 2012 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine (shared with John Gurdon). The Nobel committee celebrated the revelation that specialized cells can be reversed to an immature state capable of developing into all bodily tissues, a concept that fundamentally rewrote the rules of cellular development and differentiation.

paper_title: Induction of Pluripotent Stem Cells from Mouse Embryonic and Adult Fibroblast Cultures by Defined Factors - 2006 - Cell
display_title: Induction of Pluripotent Stem Cells from Mouse Embryonic and Adult Fibroblast Cultures by Defined Factors
title: Induction of Pluripotent Stem Cells from Mouse Embryonic and Adult Fibroblast Cultures by Defined Factors
text:
In this study, we have shown that pluripotent stem cells can be generated directly from mouse fibroblast cultures by the introduction of a small number of defined factors. Among 24 candidate genes selected on the basis of their roles in ES cells and early embryos, the combination of Oct3/4, Sox2, c-Myc, and Klf4 was sufficient to induce colonies that activated the Fbx15 locus, acquired ES cell-like morphology and proliferation, expressed many ES cell marker genes, and differentiated into derivatives of all three germ layers. These findings demonstrate that the differentiated state of fibroblasts can be reversed without nuclear transfer or cell fusion and indicate that reprogramming can be achieved by defined molecular components.

The present results support the idea that transcriptional networks responsible for maintaining ES cell identity can also participate in the induction of pluripotency. Oct3/4 and Sox2 are core regulators of pluripotent cells, and their requirement in our assay is consistent with their central roles in early embryos and ES cells. Klf4 and c-Myc are not generally viewed as lineage determinants, but both have been implicated in ES cell self-renewal and proliferation. Their inclusion may create a cellular context in which Oct3/4 and Sox2 can activate endogenous pluripotency-associated genes. c-Myc may contribute not only by promoting cell growth but also by altering chromatin structure or cellular metabolism, thereby permitting transcriptional remodeling. Klf4 may cooperate with Oct3/4 and Sox2 in the activation of ES cell-specific genes and may also oppose differentiation-associated programs. Thus, the induction of iPS cells is likely to reflect combined effects on transcription, chromatin, proliferation, and cell survival rather than the action of a single pathway.

Unexpectedly, Nanog was not required for the initial induction of iPS cells in our system. Nanog is essential for maintaining the undifferentiated state of ES cells under certain conditions and is expressed in the inner cell mass and ES cells. However, forced Nanog expression was dispensable in the factor combinations that produced iPS cells, and endogenous Nanog was activated after reprogramming. These observations suggest that Nanog may act downstream of the initial reprogramming events induced by Oct3/4, Sox2, c-Myc, and Klf4, or that its function can be partially substituted during the induction phase. It remains possible that Nanog expression contributes to the stabilization or quality of the pluripotent state after the Fbx15 locus has been activated.

Although iPS cells resemble ES cells in many respects, they are not identical to ES cells. iPS-MEF4 and iPS-MEF10 clones expressed many ES cell marker genes, showed alkaline phosphatase activity, had high telomerase activity, formed embryoid bodies, generated teratomas containing derivatives of all three germ layers, and contributed to embryonic development after blastocyst injection. Nevertheless, endogenous Oct3/4 and Sox2 expression levels were lower than in ES cells, and several ES cell-enriched genes, including Dnmt3a, Dnmt3b, Dnmt3l, Utf1, Tcl1, and the LIF receptor gene, were expressed more strongly in ES cells than in iPS cells. In addition, the Oct3/4 and Nanog promoters were only partially demethylated, and iPS cells required feeder cells for maintenance. These differences suggest that reprogramming by the four factors induces a pluripotent state that is closely related to, but distinct from, that of ES cells.

The comparison between iPS-MEF4 cells and iPS-MEF3 cells emphasizes that activation of some ES cell markers is not sufficient to establish pluripotency. The combination of Oct3/4, Klf4, and c-Myc was able to activate the Fbx15 locus and some ES cell-associated genes, but the resulting colonies had different morphology, failed to activate several important markers, and produced tumors composed of undifferentiated cells rather than tissues of three germ layers. Thus, the formation of G418-resistant colonies in the Fbx15 assay is a useful indicator of reprogramming but does not by itself prove pluripotency. Complete assessment requires gene-expression analysis, epigenetic characterization, and functional differentiation assays. These observations also indicate that Sox2 has an important role in converting partially reprogrammed cells into pluripotent cells.

The use of Fbx15 as a selectable marker was important for the identification of iPS cells, because Fbx15 is specifically expressed in ES cells and early embryos but is dispensable for pluripotency and development. Selection through this locus probably allowed the recovery of cells that had undergone substantial but not necessarily complete reprogramming. The fact that iPS cells are not identical to ES cells may reflect the properties of this selection system. Selection for activation of other loci, such as Oct3/4 or Nanog, may yield cells more similar to ES cells. Alternatively, additional factors, different culture conditions, longer selection periods, or removal of exogenous transgenes may be required to obtain cells that more completely match ES cells.

Several observations argue against the possibility that the iPS cells described here arose from contamination by preexisting ES cells. The starting populations were embryonic or adult fibroblast cultures, each iPS clone had a unique retroviral integration pattern, adult tail-tip fibroblasts gave rise to iPS cells with the expected genetic background, and GFP-labeled iPS cells contributed to embryonic tissues after blastocyst injection. In addition, some iPS clones had normal karyotypes, and subcloning experiments showed that individual iPS cells could give rise to colonies that retained pluripotent differentiation capacity. These data indicate that iPS cells were generated from somatic cells by the introduced factors.

The generation of iPS cells from adult tail-tip fibroblasts is particularly important, because it shows that reprogramming is not limited to embryonic fibroblasts. Adult somatic cells have undergone more prolonged differentiation and environmental exposure, yet they could still be converted into pluripotent cells by the same four factors. This raises the possibility that patient-specific pluripotent cells might be generated from accessible somatic tissues. Such cells could provide useful systems for disease modeling, drug screening, and, in principle, cell replacement therapy without the ethical issues associated with human embryos or the immune rejection problems associated with unrelated donor cells.

Several issues must be resolved before this approach can be considered for therapeutic application. The use of retroviral vectors leads to permanent integration of transgenes and creates the possibility of insertional mutagenesis. In addition, c-Myc is a potent oncogene, and Klf4 has also been implicated in tumor biology. Teratoma formation demonstrates pluripotency but also highlights the tumorigenic potential of undifferentiated cells. Therefore, safer methods for inducing pluripotency will be required, such as transient expression systems, excisable vectors, or replacement of oncogenic factors with less harmful molecules or culture conditions. It will also be important to determine whether iPS cells can generate germline chimeras and whether iPS cells derived under improved conditions are fully equivalent to ES cells at molecular and functional levels.

The present study provides a defined experimental system for dissecting the mechanisms of nuclear reprogramming. Because reprogramming can be initiated by introducing known factors into somatic cells, it should be possible to analyze the sequence of molecular events that leads from a fibroblast state to a pluripotent state. The system may also be useful for identifying additional genes and small molecules that enhance reprogramming efficiency or improve the quality of iPS cells. Our findings show that pluripotency can be induced by a surprisingly small set of factors and suggest that the barriers separating differentiated and pluripotent states can be overcome by defined transcriptional regulators.

paper_title: Induction of Pluripotent Stem Cells from Mouse Embryonic and Adult Fibroblast Cultures by Defined Factors - 2006 - Cell
display_title: Induction of Pluripotent Stem Cells from Mouse Embryonic and Adult Fibroblast Cultures by Defined Factors
title: Induction of Pluripotent Stem Cells from Mouse Embryonic and Adult Fibroblast Cultures by Defined Factors
text:
The results presented in this study demonstrate a profound and paradigm-shifting discovery in cellular biology: the direct reprogramming of somatic cells into a pluripotent state through the introduction of a minimal set of defined transcription factors. By expressing just four factors—Oct3/4, Sox2, c-Myc, and Klf4—we have successfully generated induced pluripotent stem (iPS) cells from mouse embryonic and adult fibroblasts. These iPS cells exhibit the morphology, growth properties, and comprehensive marker expression typical of embryonic stem (ES) cells, and importantly, they possess the developmental potential to differentiate into cellular derivatives of all three embryonic germ layers in vitro and in teratomas in vivo.

This achievement fundamentally challenges the long-held dogma that cellular differentiation is a unidirectional process. Historically, the derivation of pluripotent cells from somatic tissues required complex, undefined interventions such as somatic cell nuclear transfer (SCNT) or fusion with existing ES cells. These methods, while proving that the somatic genome retains the potential for pluripotency, relied on the uncharacterized reprogramming activity present in the oocyte or the ES cell cytoplasm. By identifying a specific combination of four transcription factors sufficient to drive this reversion, we have uncoupled pluripotency from the embryo, proving that the cellular state is far more plastic than previously conceived and is governed by a defined transcriptional network.

The selection of these four specific factors highlights the intricate balance required to establish pluripotency. Oct3/4 and Sox2 are well-established core regulators of the pluripotency network, essential for maintaining the identity of ES cells. Their inclusion was anticipated to be critical for imposing an ES-cell-like transcriptional program. The roles of c-Myc and Klf4, however, appear to be more complex and multifaceted. c-Myc is a potent oncogene known to promote proliferation, open chromatin structures, and globally enhance transcription. Its role in reprogramming may involve facilitating the access of Oct3/4 and Sox2 to their target loci by inducing widespread epigenetic remodeling and driving the rapid cellular proliferation necessary to overcome senescence barriers. Klf4, while possessing complex roles in both tumor suppression and oncogenesis, also interacts with the Oct3/4/Sox2 core network and may help suppress differentiation pathways. The precise temporal and biochemical interactions between these four factors during the reprogramming process remain a critical area for future investigation.

While the generated iPS cells closely resemble ES cells, our data also indicate that they are not entirely identical. Subtle differences exist in global gene expression profiles and the epigenetic status of certain loci. Most notably, the failure to generate adult chimeras with broad tissue contribution suggests that the reprogramming in this first generation of iPS cells may be incomplete or that the continued expression of the exogenous transgenes may interfere with normal developmental pathways. The viral integration sites and the potential for transgene reactivation, particularly the oncogene c-Myc, pose significant challenges and risks for tumor formation, underscoring the need for further refinement of the methodology.

Despite these current limitations, the implications of this technology are vast and transformative. The ability to generate patient-specific pluripotent stem cells directly from readily accessible somatic tissues circumvent the ethical controversies associated with the destruction of human embryos, which has historically hindered ES cell research. This technology provides an unprecedented platform for studying the pathogenesis of complex human diseases in vitro. By deriving iPS cells from patients with specific genetic disorders and directing their differentiation into affected cell types, researchers can create customized cellular models to investigate disease mechanisms and screen for novel therapeutics.

Furthermore, iPS technology holds immense promise for regenerative medicine and personalized cell replacement therapies. Because iPS cells are genetically identical to the donor patient, tissues derived from them would presumably circumvent the problem of immune rejection, a major hurdle in current transplantation medicine. While clinical applications will strictly require the development of safer reprogramming methods—such as the use of non-integrating vectors, recombinant proteins, or small molecules to replace the viral delivery of oncogenes—the conceptual foundation has been firmly established.

Future studies must prioritize understanding the molecular mechanisms of reprogramming. Elucidating how these four factors silence the somatic transcriptional program and reactivate the pluripotency network will require detailed kinetic and epigenetic analyses. Understanding the barriers to reprogramming, which currently proceeds at a low efficiency, will be crucial for improving the robustness of the technique. Additionally, rigorous comparative studies between iPS cells and authentic ES cells are necessary to determine if the reprogrammed state is fully equivalent to embryonic pluripotency.

In conclusion, the induction of pluripotent stem cells from somatic fibroblasts using defined factors represents a major milestone in stem cell biology. It demonstrates that the epigenetic state of a differentiated cell can be entirely erased and reset by a remarkably small number of master regulatory genes. This discovery not only provides profound insights into the plasticity of cellular identity but also opens a new era of disease modeling, drug discovery, and regenerative medicine, bringing the prospect of personalized cellular therapies significantly closer to reality. The impact of this research will inevitably extend into numerous biological disciplines, stimulating new approaches to understanding aging, cellular senescence, and the epigenetic foundations of cancer. The realization that a terminal cell fate is an engineered state, maintained by specific regulatory loops, rather than an irreversible physical limitation, invites bold new strategies for cellular engineering. As we refine the induction process and gain control over the epigenetic landscape, the artificial generation of highly specific, patient-matched cell populations will undoubtedly become a cornerstone of future biomedical science, revolutionizing our therapeutic capabilities.

paper_title: Integrative Genomics Identifies MCU as an Essential Component of the Mitochondrial Calcium Uniporter - 2011 - Nature
display_title: Integrative Genomics Identifies MCU as an Essential Component of the Mitochondrial Calcium Uniporter
title: Integrative Genomics Identifies MCU as an Essential Component of the Mitochondrial Calcium Uniporter
text: Mitochondria from diverse organisms are capable of transporting large amounts of Ca²⁺ via a ruthenium-red-sensitive, membrane-potential-dependent mechanism called the uniporter. Although the uniporter's biophysical properties have been studied extensively, its molecular composition remains elusive. We recently used comparative proteomics to identify MICU1 (also known as CBARA1), an EF-hand-containing protein that serves as a putative regulator of the uniporter. Here, we use whole-genome phylogenetic profiling, genome-wide RNA co-expression analysis and organelle-wide protein coexpression analysis to predict proteins functionally related to MICU1. All three methods converge on a novel predicted transmembrane protein, CCDC109A, that we now call 'mitochondrial calcium uniporter' (MCU). MCU forms oligomers in the mitochondrial inner membrane, physically interacts with MICU1, and resides within a large molecular weight complex. Silencing MCU in cultured cells or in vivo in mouse liver severely abrogates mitochondrial Ca²⁺ uptake, whereas mitochondrial respiration and membrane potential remain fully intact. MCU has two predicted transmembrane helices, which are separated by a highly conserved linker facing the intermembrane space. Acidic residues in this linker are required for its full activity. However, an S259A point mutation retains function but confers resistance to Ru360, the most potent inhibitor of the uniporter. Our genomic, physiological, biochemical and pharmacological data firmly establish MCU as an essential component of the mitochondrial Ca²⁺ uniporter.
historical_context: In 2011, Joshua Baughman, Vamsi Mootha, and colleagues (alongside a concurrent paper by De Stefani et al.) solved a decades-old biochemical cold case by unmasking the molecular identity of the Mitochondrial Calcium Uniporter (MCU). Utilizing a brilliant synthesis of comparative genomics, proteomics, and RNA interference, they pinpointed the exact transmembrane channel responsible for the rapid influx of calcium into the mitochondrial matrix. It was a critical breakthrough for cell biology. Because mitochondrial calcium dynamics dictate bioenergetics, cell death, and critical signaling, identifying MCU finally handed scientists the genetic lever needed to manipulate and decode mitochondrial calcium behavior in health, aging, and neurodegenerative diseases after 50 years of mere electrophysiological observation.

title: Integrative Genomics Identifies MCU as an Essential Component of the Mitochondrial Calcium Uniporter
text: Rapid Ca²⁺ uptake into mitochondria is a central process that shapes cytosolic Ca²⁺ signals and stimulates matrix metabolism, yet the molecular identity of the mitochondrial calcium uniporter has remained unknown. Here we combine phylogenetic profiling, genome-wide mRNA co-expression and mitochondrial protein co-expression to identify an unstudied inner membrane protein, formerly CCDC109A, which we name mitochondrial calcium uniporter (MCU). MCU closely tracks MICU1 across evolution and tissue expression, is detected as a mitochondrial protein in multiple mouse tissues, and physically interacts with MICU1. Silencing MCU in HeLa and HEK-293 cells markedly attenuates mitochondrial Ca²⁺ uptake without disrupting cytosolic Ca²⁺ mobilization, respiration, membrane potential or gross mitochondrial morphology, and diminishes Ca²⁺-dependent stimulation of NAD(P)H. Liver-directed siRNA knockdown of MCU in mice yields mitochondria with intact respiratory state transitions and ADP:O ratios, but a profound and near-complete loss of Ca²⁺ uptake. MCU localizes to the mitochondrial inner membrane, contains two transmembrane domains with both termini facing the matrix, and oligomerizes within an approximately 450 kDa complex. Conserved acidic residues in the intermembrane-space linker are required for activity, and a point mutation in this region confers resistance to Ru360. These genomic, physiological, biochemical and pharmacological data establish MCU as an essential, conserved component of the mitochondrial calcium uniporter.

title: Integrative Genomics Identifies MCU as an Essential Component of the Mitochondrial Calcium Uniporter
text: Mitochondrial calcium (Ca2+) uptake shapes cytosolic Ca2+ signals, stimulates ATP production and, when dysregulated, triggers cell death. For half a century the existence of a ruthenium-red-sensitive Ca2+ uniporter in the inner mitochondrial membrane has been established physiologically, yet its molecular identity has remained unknown. Here, building on our recent identification of MICU1 as a regulator of the uniporter, we apply three complementary genome-wide analyses, phylogenetic profiling across 500 organisms, mRNA co-expression across 81 mouse tissues, and protein co-expression across 14 tissues from our MitoCarta atlas, to nominate a previously uncharacterized protein, CCDC109A, which we name the mitochondrial calcium uniporter (MCU). MCU physically interacts with MICU1, harbours two transmembrane domains with both termini facing the matrix, and assembles into an oligomeric ~450 kDa complex in the inner membrane. Silencing MCU in HeLa and HEK-293 cells, and in mouse liver in vivo, profoundly attenuates mitochondrial Ca2+ uptake and Ca2+-stimulated NAD(P)H production, without disrupting cytosolic Ca2+ signalling, respiratory chain function or membrane potential. Mutation of conserved acidic residues within an intermembrane-space-facing 'DIME' motif abolishes Ca2+ transport, and a single point mutation, S259A, confers resistance to Ru360, the most potent inhibitor of the uniporter. Together, our genomic, biochemical and physiological data establish MCU as an essential, pore-associated component of the mitochondrial Ca2+ uniporter.

title: Integrative Genomics Identifies MCU as an Essential Component of the Mitochondrial Calcium Uniporter
text: Mitochondrial calcium uptake is a critical process that regulates energy production, shapes cytosolic calcium transients, and controls cell death. Despite decades of study, the molecular identity of the mitochondrial calcium uniporter has remained a major unsolved mystery in cell biology. Recently, MICU1 was identified as an essential regulator of the uniporter, but the core pore-forming component remained unknown. Here, we deployed an integrative genomics approach—combining phylogenetic profiling across 500 genomes, genome-wide mRNA co-expression analysis, and organelle-specific protein co-expression—to identify the uncharacterized protein CCDC109A, which we now call mitochondrial calcium uniporter (MCU), as a crucial component of the uniporter complex. We demonstrate that MCU physically interacts with MICU1 and localizes to the mitochondrial inner membrane. Silencing MCU expression in cells and in vivo profoundly attenuates mitochondrial calcium uptake without altering other vital bioenergetic parameters or mitochondrial membrane potential. Biochemical and topological analyses reveal that MCU forms an oligomeric complex with two transmembrane domains, and site-directed mutagenesis indicates that conserved acidic residues within the linker region are essential for its calcium transport activity. Furthermore, a specific point mutation in MCU confers striking resistance to Ru360, the most potent known inhibitor of the uniporter. Together with MICU1, MCU constitutes an evolutionarily conserved protein complex that is essential for mitochondrial calcium uptake, thereby providing a molecular foundation for investigating the role of mitochondrial calcium in physiology and disease.

paper_title: Integrative Genomics Identifies MCU as an Essential Component of the Mitochondrial Calcium Uniporter - 2011 - Nature
display_title: Integrative Genomics Identifies MCU as an Essential Component of the Mitochondrial Calcium Uniporter
title: Integrative Genomics Identifies MCU as an Essential Component of the Mitochondrial Calcium Uniporter
text:
To predict proteins that are functionally related to MICU1 and essential for mitochondrial calcium (Ca²⁺) uptake, we performed three systematic computational analyses. First, we ranked all ~20,000 mammalian genes on the basis of the similarity of their phylogenetic profile to MICU1, where the phylogenetic profile of a gene is defined as the binary vector of presence or absence of its homologues across 500 evolutionarily diverse organisms. Second, we scored all ~20,000 mammalian genes for their expression similarity to MICU1 across 81 mouse cell types and tissues using a genome-wide RNA expression atlas. Third, we scored protein expression similarity between MICU1 and all ~1,100 mitochondrial proteins, based on their pattern of peptide abundance across 14 different mouse tissues. All three computational methods spotlight an unstudied protein (previously called CCDC109A, accession number NM_138357.1) that we now call 'mitochondrial calcium uniporter' (MCU). MCU, which has two predicted transmembrane domains, was first discovered in our proteomic analysis as a mitochondrial protein detected in 12 different mouse tissues. MCU, which bears no sequence similarity to MICU1, is tied as the eighth closest phylogenetic neighbour genome-wide, being either co-present or co-absent with MICU1 in 495 of 500 organisms evaluated (Hamming distance = 5). MCU is also the second highest scoring gene in the genome-wide mRNA co-expression analysis and is the top scoring protein amongst all ~1,100 mitochondrial proteins for protein coexpression with MICU1 across 14 mouse tissues.

Our prediction of a functional relationship between MICU1 and MCU was further corroborated by evidence of a physical interaction. By transfecting green-fluorescent-protein-tagged MCU (MCU-GFP) into cells stably expressing V5-tagged MICU1 (MICU1-V5) and vice versa, we were able to recover both GFP-tagged proteins following immunoprecipitation with an anti-V5 antibody. The interaction was specific as MICU1-V5 was incapable of pulling down two different GFP-tagged inner membrane proteins (UCP2 and MFRN2, also known as SLC25A28). Similar results were also obtained by immunoprecipitating Flag-tagged MCU and probing for endogenous MICU1.

Collectively, our three complementary genomic analyses combined with our biochemical data predict that MCU is functionally related to MICU1 and that it, too, may participate in mitochondrial Ca²⁺ uptake.

Utilizing a brilliant synthesis of comparative genomics, proteomics, and RNA interference, they pinpointed the exact transmembrane channel responsible for the rapid influx of calcium into the mitochondrial matrix. It was a critical breakthrough for cell biology. Because mitochondrial calcium dynamics dictate bioenergetics, cell death, and critical signaling, identifying MCU finally handed scientists the genetic lever needed to manipulate and decode mitochondrial calcium behavior in health, aging, and neurodegenerative diseases after 50 years of mere electrophysiological observation.

title: Integrative Genomics Identifies MCU as an Essential Component of the Mitochondrial Calcium Uniporter
text:
Mitochondria are central integrators of cellular metabolism, death signalling and intracellular Ca²⁺ homeostasis. In many mammalian cells, rapid Ca²⁺ entry into the mitochondrial matrix couples cytosolic Ca²⁺ signals to ATP production by activating dehydrogenases of the tricarboxylic acid cycle, and can also promote cell death when matrix Ca²⁺ loading becomes excessive. This uptake is mediated by the mitochondrial calcium uniporter, a high-capacity, low-affinity transport activity driven by the large membrane potential across the mitochondrial inner membrane. The uniporter is inhibited by ruthenium red and its more potent derivative Ru360, and has been characterized electrophysiologically and biochemically in isolated mitochondria for several decades.

Despite this extensive physiological literature, the molecular identity of the uniporter has remained unknown. This has represented a central gap in mitochondrial biology, because genetic manipulation of the uniporter would allow its contribution to metabolism, signalling and cell death to be tested directly in intact cells and animals. Several properties have constrained the search for its components. The uniporter activity is widely conserved in vertebrate tissues and many eukaryotes, appears to reside in the mitochondrial inner membrane, and is likely to involve low-abundance membrane proteins that are not readily identified by conventional purification.

We recently used comparative organellar proteomics and evolutionary conservation to identify MICU1, an EF-hand-containing mitochondrial protein required for normal mitochondrial Ca²⁺ uptake. MICU1, however, lacks predicted transmembrane helices, suggesting that it is unlikely to itself constitute the ion-conducting pore. We reasoned that additional uniporter components should share a similar evolutionary distribution and expression pattern with MICU1, and that independent genomic strategies might therefore converge on candidates in the same functional pathway.

Here we combine whole-genome phylogenetic profiling, genome-wide messenger RNA co-expression analysis and mitochondrial protein coexpression analysis to nominate a previously uncharacterized inner membrane protein, CCDC109A. We show that this protein, which we rename mitochondrial calcium uniporter (MCU), is essential for mitochondrial Ca²⁺ uptake in cultured cells and in vivo in mouse liver, forms oligomeric complexes in the inner membrane, interacts with MICU1, and contains conserved residues required for transport and Ru360 sensitivity. These findings identify MCU as an essential component of the mitochondrial calcium uniporter.

Utilizing a brilliant synthesis of comparative genomics, proteomics, and RNA interference, they pinpointed the exact transmembrane channel responsible for the rapid influx of calcium into the mitochondrial matrix. It was a critical breakthrough for cell biology. Because mitochondrial calcium dynamics dictate bioenergetics, cell death, and critical signaling, identifying MCU finally handed scientists the genetic lever needed to manipulate and decode mitochondrial calcium behavior in health, aging, and neurodegenerative diseases after 50 years of mere electrophysiological observation.
