title: Integrative Genomics Identifies MCU as an Essential Component of the Mitochondrial Calcium Uniporter
text:
Mitochondria are dynamic organelles that serve not only as the primary site of cellular adenosine triphosphate (ATP) production but also as crucial hubs for metabolic integration and intracellular signaling. A fundamental aspect of mitochondrial biology is their capacity to rapidly accumulate massive amounts of calcium ions (Ca2+) from the cytosol. This process of mitochondrial Ca2+ uptake is essential for shaping cytosolic Ca2+ transients, buffering local Ca2+ microdomains, and regulating a wide array of cellular functions ranging from muscle contraction and neurotransmitter release to apoptosis. Crucially, elevations in matrix Ca2+ allosterically activate key rate-limiting dehydrogenases of the tricarboxylic acid (TCA) cycle, thereby matching mitochondrial energy production to increased cellular demand.

The primary conduit for this rapid Ca2+ influx across the highly impermeable inner mitochondrial membrane is a highly selective ion channel known as the mitochondrial calcium uniporter. First characterized functionally over fifty years ago, the uniporter is driven by the steep electrochemical gradient generated by the respiratory chain. Its activity is dependent on the membrane potential and can be specifically blocked by hexavalent ruthenium compounds such as ruthenium red and Ru360. Despite decades of intense biochemical and biophysical characterization—revealing complex properties such as high Ca2+ selectivity, sigmoidal activation kinetics, and regulation by cytosolic Ca2+ levels—the molecular identity of the uniporter channel has remained one of the most significant unsolved mysteries in cellular physiology.

The recent discovery of MICU1, a calcium-binding EF-hand protein essential for regulating uniporter activity, provided the first molecular handle on this critical transport system. However, MICU1 does not possess the structural features of an ion channel, indicating it likely acts as a regulatory subunit rather than the pore-forming component itself. To uncover the elusive pore of the uniporter, we employed a multifaceted integrative genomics strategy, combining genome-wide phylogenetic profiling, RNA co-expression, and organelle-specific protein co-expression analyses to identify genes functionally linked to MICU1. Here, we report the identification and characterization of a novel transmembrane protein, CCDC109A (which we name the mitochondrial calcium uniporter, or MCU), and demonstrate that it is an essential, pore-forming component of the mitochondrial calcium uniporter complex.
historical_context: In 2011, Joshua Baughman, Vamsi Mootha, and colleagues (alongside a concurrent paper by De Stefani et al.) solved a decades-old biochemical cold case by unmasking the molecular identity of the Mitochondrial Calcium Uniporter (MCU). Utilizing a brilliant synthesis of comparative genomics, proteomics, and RNA interference, they pinpointed the exact transmembrane channel responsible for the rapid influx of calcium into the mitochondrial matrix. It was a critical breakthrough for cell biology. Because mitochondrial calcium dynamics dictate bioenergetics, cell death, and critical signaling, identifying MCU finally handed scientists the genetic lever needed to manipulate and decode mitochondrial calcium behavior in health, aging, and neurodegenerative diseases after 50 years of mere electrophysiological observation.

title: Integrative Genomics Identifies MCU as an Essential Component of the Mitochondrial Calcium Uniporter
text: Our genomic, physiological, biochemical and pharmacological data firmly establish that MCU is an oligomeric, mitochondrial inner membrane protein that is essential for calcium uniporter activity. In our current studies, overexpression of MCU alone did not give rise to a marked gain of Ca²⁺ uptake in HeLa cells, indicating that additional components or chaperones may be limiting in vivo. Although its precise mechanism remains to be elucidated, MCU fulfils multiple criteria for being an integral component of the uniporter, where it could serve as the pore-forming subunit or as a closely associated partner protein. First, loss of MCU leads to a profound defect in mitochondrial Ca²⁺ uptake in three distinct systems: intact cells, permeabilized cells and mouse liver. Second, it has two bona fide transmembrane domains and oligomerizes to form a larger molecular weight complex in the mitochondrial inner membrane. Third, mutations at evolutionarily conserved acidic residues attenuate its activity. Fourth, a point mutation in MCU confers resistance against Ru360, the most potent inhibitor of the uniporter. The current study further supports our previously reported phylogenomic signature of the mitochondrial Ca²⁺ uniporter, and together these studies identify MICU1 and MCU as an evolutionarily conserved duo that are critical for mitochondrial Ca²⁺ uptake. Whereas MICU1 may serve as a regulator, MCU is likely to represent a core component of the uniporter. Future studies will be necessary to establish the exact mechanism by which these proteins function together to orchestrate the mitochondrial Ca²⁺ cycle.

title: Integrative Genomics Identifies MCU as an Essential Component of the Mitochondrial Calcium Uniporter
text:
The molecular identity of the mitochondrial calcium uniporter has remained unknown despite decades of physiological and pharmacological investigation. Our results identify MCU as an essential component of this pathway. Three independent genomic strategies, based on evolutionary distribution, transcript abundance and mitochondrial protein expression, converged on MCU as a protein functionally linked to MICU1. This prediction was strengthened by physical interaction with MICU1 and by the profound loss of mitochondrial Ca²⁺ uptake after MCU silencing in intact cells, permeabilized cells and mouse liver mitochondria.

Several observations argue that MCU acts directly in mitochondrial Ca²⁺ transport rather than indirectly through respiratory or metabolic impairment. Loss of MCU did not compromise mitochondrial membrane potential, respiration, respiratory state transitions, ADP:O ratios or gross mitochondrial morphology. Nevertheless, MCU depletion nearly abolished rapid Ca²⁺ uptake and attenuated Ca²⁺-dependent stimulation of NAD(P)H production, establishing a functional link between MCU-mediated Ca²⁺ entry and mitochondrial metabolism. The in vivo silencing experiments further show that this pathway can be selectively disrupted in an intact animal without overt impairment of mitochondrial bioenergetics.

MCU is an integral inner membrane protein that oligomerizes and migrates within a large complex, consistent with a structural role in the uniporter. The requirement for conserved acidic residues in the intermembrane-space-facing linker, together with the Ru360 resistance conferred by S259A, implicates this region in uniporter activity and inhibitor sensitivity. These findings do not by themselves prove that MCU forms the ion-conducting pore, and additional components may shape gating, selectivity or regulation. However, they establish MCU as an indispensable molecular constituent of the uniporter complex and provide a foundation for mechanistic studies of mitochondrial Ca²⁺ uptake in physiology and disease.

title: Integrative Genomics Identifies MCU as an Essential Component of the Mitochondrial Calcium Uniporter
text:
The identification of MCU as an essential, pore-forming component of the mitochondrial calcium uniporter resolves a long-standing mystery in cellular physiology. For decades, the biophysical properties of mitochondrial calcium uptake have been well-characterized, yet the molecular identity of the channel responsible remained elusive. By employing an integrative genomics approach—combining comparative physiology, phylogenetic profiling, and systematic RNAi screening—we have pinpointed MCU as a critical mediator of this process. Our functional studies demonstrate that silencing MCU drastically impairs mitochondrial calcium uptake, while its overexpression significantly enhances it, confirming its central role in ion transport across the inner mitochondrial membrane.

The discovery of MCU provides a crucial molecular tool for investigating the broader physiological and pathological roles of mitochondrial calcium signaling. Mitochondrial calcium uptake is vital for matching ATP production to cellular demand by stimulating the TCA cycle, but excessive calcium accumulation can trigger the opening of the mitochondrial permeability transition pore (mPTP), leading to cell death. This delicate balance implicates the uniporter in various conditions, including excitotoxicity in neurodegeneration and ischemia-reperfusion injury in the heart.

Future research must now focus on elucidating the structural properties of MCU and its interaction with other regulatory proteins, such as MICU1. Understanding how the uniporter complex is assembled and dynamically regulated by cytoplasmic signaling pathways will be essential for developing targeted therapies that can modulate mitochondrial calcium handling in disease states. In conclusion, the unmasking of MCU opens a new chapter in mitochondrial biology.

paper_title: Isolation of a T-Lymphotropic Retrovirus from a Patient at Risk for Acquired Immune Deficiency Syndrome AIDS - 1983 - Science
display_title: Isolation of a T-Lymphotropic Retrovirus from a Patient at Risk for Acquired Immune Deficiency Syndrome AIDS
title: Isolation of a T-Lymphotropic Retrovirus from a Patient at Risk for Acquired Immune Deficiency Syndrome AIDS
text: A retrovirus belonging to the family of recently discovered human T-cell leukemia viruses (HTLV), but clearly distinct from each previous isolate, has been isolated from a Caucasian patient with signs and symptoms that often precede the acquired immune deficiency syndrome (AIDS). This virus is a typical type-C RNA tumor virus, buds from the cell membrane, prefers magnesium for reverse transcriptase activity, and has an internal antigen (p25) similar to HTLV p24. Antibodies from serum of this patient react with proteins from viruses of the HTLV-I subgroup, but type-specific antisera to HTLV-I do not precipitate proteins of the new isolate. The virus from this patient has been transmitted into cord blood lymphocytes, and the virus produced by these cells is similar to the original isolate. From these studies it is concluded that this virus as well as the previous HTLV isolates belong to a general family of T-lymphotropic retroviruses that are horizontally transmitted in humans and may be involved in several pathological syndromes, including AIDS.
historical_context: In 1983, Françoise Barré-Sinoussi, Jean-Claude Chermann, Luc Montagnier, and colleagues isolated a novel retrovirus from the lymph node of a patient exhibiting early symptoms of what would soon be universally recognized as AIDS. By detecting reverse transcriptase activity and visually observing the virus budding from T-lymphocytes, they achieved the very first identification of the human immunodeficiency virus (HIV). This landmark study resulted in the 2008 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine for Barré-Sinoussi and Montagnier (shared with Harald zur Hausen). The Nobel committee acknowledged their rapid, vital identification of HIV, which was an absolute prerequisite for deciphering the biology of the devastating disease, implementing critical diagnostic blood screening, and ultimately engineering the antiretroviral therapies that converted AIDS from a death sentence into a manageable chronic condition.

title: Isolation of a T-Lymphotropic Retrovirus from a Patient at Risk for Acquired Immune Deficiency Syndrome AIDS
text: The acquired immune deficiency syndrome (AIDS) is associated with severe depression of cellular immunity and alterations of T-lymphocyte subpopulations, suggesting that T cells may be a target for an infectious agent. We report the isolation of a retrovirus from a cervical lymph node of a homosexual patient with persistent multiple lymphadenopathies, a syndrome that may represent an early or mild form of AIDS. Reverse transcriptase activity was detected after culture of lymph node lymphocytes with phytohemagglutinin, T-cell growth factor, and antiserum to human alpha interferon, but not in similarly cultured peripheral blood lymphocytes. The agent was transmitted to normal adult and umbilical cord T lymphocytes and displayed properties of a type-C retrovirus, including a density of 1.16, uridine labeling, and budding particles in electron microscopy. Its reverse transcriptase resembled that of human T-cell leukemia viruses, but its major p25 core protein was immunologically distinct from HTLV-I p24 and p19. The relation of this T-lymphotropic retrovirus to AIDS remains to be determined.

text: A novel retrovirus has been isolated from a cervical lymph node biopsy of a 33-year-old homosexual male presenting with multiple lymphadenopathies, a condition that may represent an early or milder form of the acquired immune deficiency syndrome (AIDS). The virus was propagated in cultures of T lymphocytes from a healthy adult donor and from umbilical cord blood of newborns, with virus production enhanced by inclusion of antiserum to human alpha interferon. Electron microscopy revealed C-type particles budding at the plasma membrane, and the buoyant density of the virus in sucrose was 1.16. The reverse transcriptase exhibited ionic requirements close to those of the human T-cell leukemia virus (HTLV), with an optimal Mg2+ concentration of 5 mM and a strong preference for poly(A)-oligo(dT). The major viral core protein, p25, was immunologically distinct from the p24 and p19 of HTLV-I, although the patient's serum reacted with surface antigens on HTLV-I-infected cells. This isolate appears to represent a new member of the HTLV family that may be involved in AIDS.

text: The acquired immune deficiency syndrome (AIDS) is a devastating disease characterized by a profound depression of cellular immunity, leading to severe opportunistic infections and Kaposi's sarcoma. Epidemiological evidence suggests horizontal transmission by sexual contact, blood transfusion, and intravenous drug use. The specific depletion of T lymphocytes, particularly the T-helper subset, suggests that these cells may be the primary target of an infectious agent. Here, we report the isolation of a novel T-lymphotropic retrovirus from a cultured lymph node biopsy of a homosexual patient presenting with multiple lymphadenopathies, a condition that may represent an early or milder form of AIDS. The virus exhibits reverse transcriptase activity similar to that of the human T-cell leukemia virus (HTLV) family and produces characteristic C-type budding particles. However, the viral core proteins are immunologically distinct from the known p19 and p24 proteins of HTLV-I. Specifically, the major viral protein, p25, is recognized by the patient's serum but not by antibodies against HTLV-I p24. This novel retrovirus is readily propagated in cultures of normal human T lymphocytes. We tentatively conclude that this isolate belongs to a broader family of human T-lymphotropic retroviruses that may be etiologically involved in AIDS and related syndromes.

title: Isolation of a T-Lymphotropic Retrovirus from a Patient at Risk for Acquired Immune Deficiency Syndrome AIDS
text:
The acquired immune deficiency syndrome (AIDS) has recently been recognized in several countries. The disease has been reported mainly in homosexual males with multiple partners, and epidemiological studies suggest horizontal transmission by sexual routes as well as by intravenous drug administration, and blood transfusion. The pronounced depression of cellular immunity that occurs in patients with AIDS and the quantitative modifications of subpopulations of their T lymphocytes suggest that T cells or a subset of T cells might be a preferential target for the putative infectious agent. Alternatively, these modifications may result from subsequent infections. The depressed cellular immunity may result in serious opportunistic infections in AIDS patients, many of whom develop Kaposi's sarcoma. However, a picture of persistent multiple lymphadenopathies has also been described in homosexual males and infants who may or may not develop AIDS. The histological aspect of such lymph nodes is that of reactive hyperplasia. Such cases may correspond to an early or a milder form of the disease. We report here the isolation of a novel retrovirus from a lymph node of a homosexual patient with multiple lymphadenopathies. The virus appears to be a member of the human T-cell leukemia virus (HTLV) family.

The retrovirus was propagated in cultures of T lymphocytes from a healthy adult donor and from umbilical cord blood of newborn humans. Viral core proteins were not immunologically related to the p24 and p19 proteins of subgroup I of HTLV. However, serum of the patient reacted strongly with surface antigen (or antigens) present on HTLV-I-infected cells. Moreover, the ionic requirements of the viral reverse transcriptase were close to that of HTLV. Recently, a type-C retrovirus was also identified in T cells from a patient with hairy cell leukemia. Analysis of the proteins of this virus showed they were related to, but clearly different from, proteins of previous HTLV isolates. Moreover, recent studies of the nucleic acid sequences of this new virus show it is less than 10 percent homologous to the earlier HTLV isolates. This virus was called HTLV-II to distinguish it from all the earlier, highly related viruses termed HTLV-I. The new retrovirus reported here appears to also differ from HTLV-II. We tentatively conclude that this virus, as well as all previous HTLV isolates, belong to a family of T-lymphotropic retroviruses that are horizontally transmitted in humans and may be involved in several pathological syndromes, including AIDS.

By detecting reverse transcriptase activity and visually observing the virus budding from T-lymphocytes, they achieved the very first identification of the human immunodeficiency virus (HIV). This landmark study resulted in the 2008 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine for Barré-Sinoussi and Montagnier (shared with Harald zur Hausen). The Nobel committee acknowledged their rapid, vital identification of HIV, which was an absolute prerequisite for deciphering the biology of the devastating disease, implementing critical diagnostic blood screening, and ultimately engineering the antiretroviral therapies that converted AIDS from a death sentence into a manageable chronic condition.

title: Isolation of a T-Lymphotropic Retrovirus from a Patient at Risk for Acquired Immune Deficiency Syndrome AIDS
text:
Acquired immune deficiency syndrome (AIDS) has recently been described in young adults with severe opportunistic infections, Kaposi's sarcoma, or both, and with marked impairment of cellular immunity. The disease has been observed with particular frequency in homosexual men, but also in hemophiliacs, drug users, and other patients without a known congenital immune defect. In many cases the onset of the syndrome is preceded by persistent lymphadenopathy, asthenia, fever, loss of weight, and serological evidence of previous or continuing infection with herpesviruses. These observations have suggested that AIDS may be caused, or at least favored, by an infectious agent transmitted between humans.

Several features of the disease point especially to an agent acting on T lymphocytes. Patients with AIDS often show a selective decrease in the T-helper lymphocyte population and a profound defect in cell-mediated immune reactions. A persistent infection of lymphoid cells could therefore account both for the early lymphadenopathy and for the later failure of immune surveillance. However, no virus has yet been reproducibly isolated from patients at risk for AIDS, and the relationship of the many viruses found in such patients to the immune defect remains uncertain.

Human T-cell leukemia virus (HTLV) has recently been isolated from cultured T lymphocytes of patients with certain T-cell leukemias and lymphomas. These viruses are type-C retroviruses, contain a reverse transcriptase, and can be propagated in activated human T lymphocytes. Serological studies have indicated that HTLV-related viruses are horizontally transmitted in man and that related antigens may occur in patients with different lymphoproliferative disorders. Since these viruses have a marked tropism for T cells, it was of interest to determine whether a related retrovirus could be detected in patients with early clinical signs of AIDS.

We have therefore examined lymph node and blood lymphocytes from a patient with generalized lymphadenopathy and epidemiological risk for AIDS. The cultures were stimulated with phytohemagglutinin and T-cell growth factor and were also treated with antiserum to human alpha interferon, since endogenous interferon may inhibit the release of retroviruses from chronically infected cells. We report here the isolation and serial transmission to normal lymphocytes of a new T-lymphotropic retrovirus. Although related to HTLV by some structural and antigenic properties, this virus is immunologically distinct from known HTLV-I isolates and may represent a member of a broader family of human T-lymphotropic retroviruses associated with pathological syndromes including AIDS.

By detecting reverse transcriptase activity and visually observing the virus budding from T-lymphocytes, they achieved the very first identification of the human immunodeficiency virus (HIV). This landmark study resulted in the 2008 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine for Barré-Sinoussi and Montagnier (shared with Harald zur Hausen). The Nobel committee acknowledged their rapid, vital identification of HIV, which was an absolute prerequisite for deciphering the biology of the devastating disease, implementing critical diagnostic blood screening, and ultimately engineering the antiretroviral therapies that converted AIDS from a death sentence into a manageable chronic condition.

title: Isolation of a T-Lymphotropic Retrovirus from a Patient at Risk for Acquired Immune Deficiency Syndrome AIDS
text:
The acquired immune deficiency syndrome (AIDS) has recently emerged as a devastating and rapidly spreading epidemiological crisis. Initially recognized in homosexual men and subsequently identified in intravenous drug abusers, hemophiliacs, and Haitian immigrants, AIDS is characterized by a profound, irreversible depletion of the cellular immune system. This severe immunodeficiency leaves patients highly susceptible to opportunistic infections, such as Pneumocystis carinii pneumonia, and unusual malignancies, notably Kaposi's sarcoma. The epidemiological pattern of AIDS, including its transmission via blood products and intimate sexual contact, strongly suggests that the etiologic agent is a transmissible, infectious microorganism.

The clinical manifestations of AIDS, particularly the selective depletion of T-helper lymphocytes, point toward an infectious agent with a specific tropism for human T cells. Retroviruses are prime candidates for such an etiology. In animal models, various retroviruses are known to cause severe immunosuppression and lymphoid depletion, such as the feline leukemia virus in cats. In humans, the only previously identified horizontally transmitted retroviruses belong to the human T-cell leukemia virus (HTLV) family. HTLV isolates, primarily HTLV-I, are associated with aggressive adult T-cell malignancies and display a striking tropism for T-helper lymphocytes (OKT4+), which is the exact same cell population selectively depleted in AIDS. Although HTLV-I is typically oncogenic, producing leukemias and lymphomas by immortalizing infected T cells, we hypothesized that a related, yet distinct, T-lymphotropic retrovirus might be responsible for the cytopathic effects and profound immunodeficiency observed in AIDS patients.

Investigating this hypothesis, we focused on patients exhibiting the early signs of the syndrome, often referred to as the AIDS prodrome or lymphadenopathy syndrome, characterized by persistent, generalized lymph node enlargement and asthenia. Such patients might represent a more active phase of viral replication, before the terminal depletion of the target T cells occurs and opportunist infections mask the underlying cause. In this report, we describe the successful isolation and characterization of a novel, human T-lymphotropic retrovirus from cultured lymph node cells of a homosexual patient presenting with multiple lymphadenopathies. We demonstrate that this virus, while sharing certain morphological and biochemical characteristics with the known HTLV family, is antigenically and biologically distinct, representing a new class of human retrovirus that may play a central role in the pathogenesis of AIDS.

This landmark study resulted in the 2008 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine for Barré-Sinoussi and Montagnier (shared with Harald zur Hausen). The Nobel committee acknowledged their rapid, vital identification of HIV, which was an absolute prerequisite for deciphering the biology of the devastating disease, implementing critical diagnostic blood screening, and ultimately engineering the antiretroviral therapies that converted AIDS from a death sentence into a manageable chronic condition.

Patient 1 had circulating antibodies against the virus, and some of the latter persisted in lymphocytes of his lymph node (or nodes). The virus-producing lymphocytes seemed to have no increased growth potential in vitro compared to the uninfected cells. Therefore, the multiple lymphadenopathies may represent a host reaction against the persistent viral infection rather than hyperproliferation of virus-infected lymphocytes. Other factors, such as repeated infection by the same virus or other bacterial and viral agents may, in some patients, overload this early defense mechanism and bring about an irreversible depletion of T cells involved in cellular immunity.

Its growth in normal adult and cord blood lymphocytes, morphology, density, and reverse transcriptase activity establish its retroviral nature, whereas its lack of reaction with antisera to HTLV-I p19 and p24 indicates that it is not an ordinary HTLV-I isolate. Although these observations do not prove an etiologic role in AIDS, they support further serological and molecular studies in patients at risk and in established cases.

text: The isolation of a novel T-lymphotropic retrovirus from a patient exhibiting signs of pre-AIDS marks a critical advancement in understanding the etiology of Acquired Immune Deficiency Syndrome. This virus, distinguished from previously known human T-cell leukemia viruses (HTLV-I and II) by its unique immunological and morphological characteristics, exhibits a pronounced tropism for human T-lymphocytes. The detection of reverse transcriptase activity and the presence of retroviral particles in the patient's cultured cells provide compelling evidence of viral infection. Given the profound immunodeficiency characterizing AIDS, the identification of a retrovirus targeting the very immune cells depleted in the disease strongly suggests a causal relationship. Further epidemiological and molecular studies are urgently needed to confirm its role as the primary etiological agent of AIDS.

historical_context: This was one of the major publications during Richard She's grad school years in Daniel Jarosz's lab. Long before he got interested in brain organoids, Richard worked on quantitative genetics in budding yeast. This study bridged the frustrating gap between broad statistical GWAS associations and tangible biochemical mechanisms. Most human GWAS hits large regions with many neutral passenger mutations that surround each true causal mutation. However, in yeast, advanced genetic engineering techniques make it possible to fine map these genetic neighborhoods to definitively pinpoint the exact causal mutations responsible for driving complex traits.

text:
Genetics is not destiny. However, the DNA polymorphisms that are inherited in families can affect our risk for disease, the physical dimensions of our bodies, and even our baseline behavioral tendencies. Long before physical genomes could be fully sequenced, the early pioneers of quantitative genetics scoured the genome for mutations responsible for the most extreme manifestations of these traits. By harnessing the power of family pedigrees, investigators linked unique restriction endonuclease sites to disease alleles, creating the first linkage maps of our genomes.

Although linkage was a blessing that allowed geneticists to pinpoint genes responsible Mendelian diseases, it is now a curse for the vast majority of heritable traits, which are influenced by many variants of small effect. Identifying these variants is hampered by the sparsity of meiotic crossovers. Even in a highly outbred human population, the average size of a haplotype block spans dozens of polymorphisms, meaning that each causal polymorphism is inherited along with numerous statistically indistinguishable passenger mutations. Thus, despite rapid advances in identifying polymorphisms, modern genome-wide association studies (GWAS) are often unable to distinguish true causal mutations from nearby neutral variants. Current methods for fine mapping rely on functional profiling of candidate variants within each quantitative trait locus (QTL). However, in virtually all cases, attribution of causality is based on some prior knowledge from directed studies, preventing discovery of fundamentally new variants.

title: Mapping Causal Variants with Single-Nucleotide Resolution Reveals Biochemical Drivers of Phenotypic Change
text:
A central goal of genetics is to identify the exact DNA sequence changes that transform genotype into phenotype. The rapid accumulation of whole-genome sequences has made it increasingly straightforward to discover statistical associations between genetic markers and biological traits. Yet association is not causation. In natural populations, causal variants are embedded within haplotype blocks that contain many linked passenger mutations. As a result, most genome-wide association studies localize phenotypic effects to broad genomic intervals, but cannot determine which nucleotide, gene, or biochemical perturbation actually drives the trait.

Model organisms provide a potential solution because crosses can restrict the number of possible alleles and use meiotic recombination to separate nearby variants. However, even the most powerful inbred crosses have generally stopped short of causal nucleotides. Quantitative trait loci typically contain many polymorphisms, and resolving them requires laborious allele replacement or backcrossing experiments that cannot be scaled across the genome. Thus, a long-standing practical gap has persisted between mapping a locus and understanding the molecular basis of phenotypic variation.

Here we show that this gap can be closed by pushing inbred crossing to its practical limit in Saccharomyces cerevisiae. By combining reduced parental divergence, six generations of recombination, dense genotyping, and quantitative phenotyping, we created a genetic panel in which adjacent variants could be distinguished statistically. This strategy enabled genome-wide mapping of natural causal variants at single-nucleotide resolution, revealing the coding, synonymous, and regulatory changes that collectively shape quantitative traits.

text:
Understanding the sequence determinants that give rise to diversity among individuals and species is the central challenge of genetics. Despite ever-greater numbers of sequenced genomes, most genome-wide association studies (GWAS) cannot distinguish true causal variants from tightly linked passenger mutations spanning many neighboring genes. This inherent limitation stems from the structure of linkage disequilibrium in human populations, which causes extensive blocks of the genome to be inherited together.

We report that this fundamental challenge can be overcome in model organisms by utilizing highly recombined inbred crosses. By pushing the advantages of inbred crossing to its practical limit in the yeast Saccharomyces cerevisiae, we have dramatically improved the statistical resolution of genetic linkage analysis to single nucleotides. This 'super-resolution' mapping approach allowed us to accurately map 370 precise causal variants across 26 distinct quantitative traits. Surprisingly, we found that missense, synonymous, and cis-regulatory mutations collectively gave rise to phenotypic diversity, challenging common assumptions about which types of mutations are functionally important and providing mechanistic insight into the basis of evolutionary divergence. Our comprehensive data also systematically unmasked complex genetic architectures, revealing that multiple closely linked driver mutations frequently act in concert on the same quantitative trait within a single locus. Single-nucleotide mapping thus complements traditional deletion and overexpression screening paradigms and opens new frontiers in understanding the genetic basis of quantitative traits.

paper_title: Mapping Causal Variants with Single-Nucleotide Resolution Reveals Biochemical Drivers of Phenotypic Change - 2018 - Cell
display_title: Mapping Causal Variants with Single-Nucleotide Resolution Reveals Biochemical Drivers of Phenotypic Change
title: Mapping Causal Variants with Single-Nucleotide Resolution Reveals Biochemical Drivers of Phenotypic Change
text:
More than fifty years ago the power of recombination (and the ability to select for rare events) enabled phage geneticists to discover the triplet nature of the genetic code. In the current genomic era, recombination is again poised to serve as a workhorse for genetic discovery. Our data demonstrate that a dense recombination map enables statistical differentiation of individual causal variants from a multitude of confounding, highly linked mutations. We mapped hundreds of genome-wide associations with single nucleotide resolution, allowing us to systematically explore the functional properties of natural genetic variation. We found that numerous missense, synonymous, and cis-regulatory mutations collectively gave rise to phenotypic diversity, in contrast to prevailing models that systematically discount non-coding variation or synonymous mutations within ORFs. Indeed, most traits we examined were genetically complex, and were driven by both coding and non-coding variants.

The consistent impact of synonymous mutations across a diverse panel of traits has strong implications for our fundamental understanding of how genotype determines phenotype. Most measures of positive selection in evolution assume that all synonymous mutations are neutral and can only become fixed via random chance (drift). In contrast, we found that a substantial number of naturally occurring synonymous mutations exert strong effects of growth. These synonymous mutations, in aggregate, deviated substantially from the expected random distribution for such mutations - providing evidence that they have been shaped by selection pressures in the wild. For pragmatic reasons, genome-wide association studies in humans systematically discount any influence of synonymous mutations on phenotype. Yet a handful of targeted investigations have reported that synonymous mutations can modulate phenotypic severity or penetrance. Our data thus challenge an assumption in bioinformatics and clinical diagnostics that is born out of expediency: that synonymous mutations are universally neutral. Instead, we find that synonymous mutations can commonly exert significant impacts on biological traits.

Remarkably, mutations that influenced the same trait tended to cluster in linkage groups. In sexually reproducing organisms this close physical coupling may allow genetically complex traits to persist over long evolutionary timescales. Several attempts to fine-map QTLs in mice, flies, plants, and humans have also uncovered multiple causal variants within a single locus, but such fine mapping has not been possible systematically. Our data suggest that clusters of causal mutations - which confound traditional genome-wide association studies - may be a general feature of natural genetic variation.

Genetics in inbred crosses holds several crucial advantages over efforts to sequence greater numbers of outbred individuals: i) allele frequency is ~50% in the progeny, enabling accurate assessment of alleles that are rare in the population as a whole, ii) the degree of polymorphism in a cross can be carefully controlled through selection of founder parents, and iii) recombination can be enhanced by multiple generations of mating. Our cross balanced the costs of reducing genetic variation with the practical challenges of sequencing and maintaining a large cohort of inbred progeny. Crucially, our results suggest that judicious selection of ecologically and geographically diverse parents preserves a high degree of phenotypic complexity despite reduced genetic divergence.

Extensive modeling of the parameter space of all possible inbred cross designs suggests that achieving single nucleotide mapping resolution could be possible in metazoa. In flies, precision mapping could be achieved with a ~100-fold increase in the number of total meiotic crossovers for the degree of polymorphism in current crosses. It would require a 10,000-fold increase in mice. We also simulated an inbred cross in mice with 1,000,000 F6 individuals and 1,000,000 sites of variation. Even with this extreme number of potential false positive sites, our statistical method retained >95% precision and estimated effect sizes with remarkable accuracy.

Because genotyping and phenotyping such a large cohort of individuals is presently infeasible, a more immediately practical strategy would be to restrict genetic variation between founding parents. One major hurdle to this strategy is the frequency of outcrossing events observed in wild populations, which create mosaic genetic architectures with regions of dense variation. Thus, careful selection of parents, ideally from highly bottlenecked and isolated populations, provides a feasible route.

Our model suggests a second fruitful strategy for high-resolution mapping: increasing the frequency of meiotic crossovers relative to genome size. Among metazoans, social insects such as bees and ants exhibit the highest known recombination rates (>8 times greater than D. melanogaster). Sequential inbreeding could prove challenging in these organisms, but would have far greater mapping power. Alternatively, methods that artificially enhance recombination rates - which have been deployed in a handful of studies - would also yield large dividends. Although the establishment of a dense recombination map is a prerequisite for mapping causal variants, our crossing scheme enables even deeper inspection the genetic architecture of heredity. Because all 1152 segregants in our cross are fully genotyped haploids, they can be crossed to each other in an arrayed format to generate ~250,000 unique diploids without any further sequencing. A genotype to phenotype map at this larger scale would be statistically competent to illuminate how pairwise epistasis among natural genetic variants might contribute to the 'missing heritability' problem.

As sequencing costs fall and methods for enhancing recombination rates advance, the scope of genetic variation accessible to inbred crossing strategies will increase. Naturally occurring polymorphisms are thus poised to provide a parallel genetic toolkit that captures a range of biochemical perturbations not present in traditional deletion, siRNA, and CRISPR-based screens. However, the natural variation mapped in any one cross represents only a fraction of the mutational space available to an organism. To saturate annotation of natural variants and enable truly comprehensive functional genomics, it will be necessary to perform additional crosses between new pairs of parents, as inspired by the famous Heidelberg screens. Many such pairs of wild yeast strains with unique ecological origins and constrained genotypic divergence are presently available and can be crossed with minimal genetic manipulation. The ability to systematically identify the individual polymorphisms that give rise to quantitative traits provides new possibilities for understanding the relationship between genetic variation and phenotypic diversity on a genome-wide scale.

paper_title: Mapping Causal Variants with Single-Nucleotide Resolution Reveals Biochemical Drivers of Phenotypic Change - 2018 - Cell
display_title: Mapping Causal Variants with Single-Nucleotide Resolution Reveals Biochemical Drivers of Phenotypic Change
title: Mapping Causal Variants with Single-Nucleotide Resolution Reveals Biochemical Drivers of Phenotypic Change
text:
The central challenge in quantitative genetics has been to move from loci to mechanisms. Linkage analysis and GWAS have identified thousands of genomic regions associated with human disease, agriculturally important traits, and evolutionary divergence. Yet these associations usually remain descriptive because causal variants are inherited together with nearby passengers. Here we show that this limitation is not intrinsic to genetics. By combining a restricted number of segregating polymorphisms with many generations of inbreeding and dense genotyping, we were able to map quantitative trait nucleotides across the yeast genome with single-nucleotide resolution. This strategy transforms meiotic recombination from a coarse tool for assigning traits to chromosomal intervals into a systematic source of natural allele replacements.

Our results reveal that the genetic architecture of complex traits is richer than can be appreciated from conventional QTL studies. Even in a compact genome and a highly controlled cross, most traits were not explained by one or two loci of large effect. Instead, phenotypic variation commonly arose from many variants of modest effect, often distributed across coherent functional networks. The azole-resistance traits illustrate this principle. A standard analysis would have highlighted a strong QTL at UPC2 and a small number of additional loci, but fine mapping uncovered a broader set of causal polymorphisms that converged on ergosterol biosynthesis, membrane composition, and pleiotropic drug response. Thus, single-nucleotide mapping preserves the statistical power of inbred crosses while adding the mechanistic resolution that is usually obtained only through directed follow-up experiments.

The ability to resolve adjacent variants also uncovered a surprising amount of local genetic complexity. A common assumption in QTL dissection is that one causal mutation explains one locus, with the remaining linked variants functioning as neutral passengers. This simplifying model was frequently incorrect. At SKY1 and IXR1, multiple mutations within a single gene influenced the same trait, sometimes in opposing directions. In such cases, the net effect of a parental haplotype can obscure the contributions of the individual alleles that compose it. The SKY1 example is especially instructive because both parental haplotypes were nearly neutral in 5-FU, whereas recombinant haplotypes exposed the functional consequences of each mutation. These observations argue that naturally evolving genomes should often be interpreted as mosaics of partially compensatory, reinforcing, or buffered variants rather than collections of independent drivers.

We also found that functionally coupled variants were frequently linked across short genomic distances, including in different genes. This observation has implications for how selection acts on eukaryotic genomes. Because recombination continually breaks apart favorable combinations of alleles, physical linkage can preserve beneficial multi-variant architectures across generations. Conversely, linkage may also allow deleterious alleles to persist when they are carried with variants that improve fitness in the same environment. Although yeast lacks operons, our data show that phenotypic units of inheritance can nevertheless extend beyond individual genes. This provides a genetic explanation for why haplotype blocks can retain biological meaning even when the underlying genome is not organized into obvious functional clusters.

The molecular identities of causal variants were also more diverse than expected. Missense variants were enriched, particularly among alleles of large effect, but they accounted for less than half of the causal variants we mapped. Regulatory and synonymous mutations made substantial contributions to phenotypic diversity. The discovery of a synonymous UPC2 variant controlling pan-azole resistance illustrates how natural variation can expose mechanisms that are poorly captured by current computational predictors. Synonymous mutations are often treated as neutral by default, yet they can alter codon optimality, translation, protein abundance, or other features of gene expression. Similarly, promoter mutations such as the PMP3 allele can create conditional changes in expression that become visible only in the relevant environmental context. These findings emphasize that causal inference based solely on variant class or conservation will miss important drivers of quantitative traits.

Our study also demonstrates the value of natural genetic variation as a functional genomics resource. Classical deletion and overexpression collections have been extraordinarily powerful in yeast, but they sample a restricted space of perturbations. Natural alleles are different. They include subtle changes in coding sequence, expression, protein stability, regulation, and genetic background dependence. They also have survived evolutionary filtering, making them enriched for alleles that can alter phenotype without necessarily abolishing gene function. The derived mutations in our cross were frequently beneficial in at least one condition, contrasting with the expectation from random mutagenesis that most new variants are deleterious. Thus, natural variation provides a complementary toolkit for discovering gene function and for learning which molecular changes are compatible with cellular fitness.

Several limitations define the boundaries of this approach. Yeast is unusually favorable for single-nucleotide mapping because it has a small genome, high recombination rate, inexpensive cultivation, and tractable population sizes. Larger genomes, lower recombination rates, and denser polymorphism will all increase the number of individuals required to distinguish neighboring variants. In addition, variants of very small effect, traits with strong environmental noise, rare higher-order epistasis, and phenotypes requiring developmental or tissue-specific contexts will remain challenging. Our design also intentionally restricted parental divergence, which improved mapping precision but may exclude alleles that segregate between more distant lineages. These tradeoffs do not diminish the value of the approach, but they indicate that single-nucleotide mapping will be most powerful when experimental design is matched to genome size, recombination density, and trait heritability.

Despite these constraints, the principles developed here are general. Reducing the density of segregating variation, increasing the number of independent recombination events, and preserving large genotyped cohorts should improve causal mapping in any organism where controlled crosses are possible. In yeast, these principles allowed us to move directly from phenotype to nucleotide, and from nucleotide to biochemical mechanism, across many traits. More broadly, our data suggest that much of the missing mechanistic content in quantitative genetics is not inaccessible; it is hidden within linkage. Once linked variants can be separated with sufficient resolution, complex traits become collections of interpretable molecular perturbations. Single-nucleotide mapping therefore offers a practical path toward a more complete genotype-to-phenotype map, one in which the causal variants underlying phenotypic change can be discovered systematically rather than inferred one locus at a time.

paper_title: Mapping Causal Variants with Single-Nucleotide Resolution Reveals Biochemical Drivers of Phenotypic Change - 2018 - Cell
display_title: Mapping Causal Variants with Single-Nucleotide Resolution Reveals Biochemical Drivers of Phenotypic Change
title: Mapping Causal Variants with Single-Nucleotide Resolution Reveals Biochemical Drivers of Phenotypic Change
text:
The comprehensive fine-mapping of causal genetic variants at single-nucleotide resolution, as detailed in this study, provides unprecedented insight into the biochemical mechanisms that drive phenotypic variation and complex disease susceptibility. By integrating massively parallel reporter assays (MPRAs), CRISPR-Cas9 genome editing, and extensive epigenomic profiling, we have systematically decoded the functional consequences of thousands of non-coding genetic variants. Our approach bridges the critical gap between statistical associations identified in genome-wide association studies (GWAS) and the underlying molecular etiology, demonstrating that a significant proportion of phenotypic change is driven by subtle alterations in regulatory element activity.

Historically, the interpretation of GWAS findings has been severely hampered by linkage disequilibrium, which often implicates large genomic blocks containing numerous highly correlated variants. Identifying the true causal variant among a sea of passengers has remained a formidable challenge. Furthermore, the vast majority of these variants reside in non-coding regions, lacking the straightforward interpretive framework of protein-coding mutations. Our application of high-throughput reporter assays directly tests the regulatory potential of individual alleles in a native cellular context, providing empirical evidence of their functional impact.

Our results reveal that causal non-coding variants predominantly act by disrupting or creating transcription factor (TF) binding sites within enhancers and promoters. This single-nucleotide resolution mapping allows us to pinpoint the precise biochemical events—such as the altered affinity of a specific TF—that initiate the cascade from genetic variation to altered gene expression. Crucially, we observed that the magnitude of expression changes induced by these regulatory variants is often modest, yet highly reproducible. This supports the highly polygenic model of complex traits, where the cumulative effect of many small regulatory tweaks dictates the overall phenotype.

The integration of CRISPR-Cas9 editing proved essential for validating the findings of our episomal reporter assays within the endogenous genomic context. While MPRAs are highly scalable, they remove the variant from its native chromatin environment. By creating precise, targeted base edits at endogenous loci, we confirmed that the regulatory effects observed in vitro consistently translate to significant changes in local gene expression in vivo. This rigorous validation solidifies the role of these specific single-nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) as bona fide drivers of transcriptional variation.

Moreover, our analysis highlights the cell-type-specific nature of regulatory variation. The impact of a causal variant is frequently dependent on the trans-acting environment of the cell, specifically the availability and activity of the TFs whose binding sites are affected. This observation is critical for understanding the tissue-specific manifestations of complex diseases. A variant associated with an autoimmune disorder, for example, may only exhibit its regulatory effect in a specific subset of immune cells where the relevant enhancer is active. Therefore, future fine-mapping efforts must increasingly utilize relevant primary cell types or precisely differentiated in vitro models to capture the true functional landscape of disease-associated variation.

The identification of these causal biochemical drivers also offers new avenues for therapeutic intervention. While targeting transcription factors themselves has historically been challenging, understanding the precise regulatory networks disrupted by disease variants allows for the identification of downstream effectors that may be more amenable to pharmacological modulation. Additionally, the increasing precision of genome editing technologies raises the long-term prospect of directly correcting causal regulatory variants in relevant tissues as a therapeutic strategy.

Despite these advances, significant challenges remain. Our assays primarily evaluate the impact of variants on local enhancer activity and proximal gene expression. Understanding long-range chromatin interactions and how regulatory effects are transmitted over large genomic distances remains a complex area of study. Furthermore, higher-order combinatorial effects between multiple variants within a single regulatory element, or across multiple elements, are not fully captured by our current single-variant approach.

In conclusion, the mapping of causal variants at single-nucleotide resolution represents a major leap forward in our understanding of human genetics. By systematically defining the biochemical consequences of non-coding variation, we move beyond simple statistical association to establish a mechanistic framework for complex traits. This approach not only illuminates the fundamental rules of gene regulation but also provides a crucial foundation for translating genetic discoveries into actionable biological insights and targeted therapeutics. As these high-throughput functional genomics platforms continue to evolve and scale, they will play a central role in realizing the promise of precision medicine. The extensive catalog of functional variants generated by this study will serve as an invaluable resource for the broader scientific community, facilitating the training of advanced computational models aimed at predicting the effects of non-coding mutations. As machine learning algorithms become increasingly sophisticated, training data of this quality and resolution will be essential for developing accurate in silico predictors of variant function. Looking ahead, the expansion of these functional assays to encompass a wider array of cellular contexts, developmental stages, and environmental stimuli will be critical. It is increasingly clear that the penetrance and impact of genetic variation are highly dynamic, heavily influenced by the immediate physiological state of the cell. Ultimately, integrating highly resolved functional variant maps with dynamic physiological data will provide the holistic understanding necessary to decipher the true complexity of the human genome and its contribution to health and disease.
historical_context: This was one of the major publications during Richard She's grad school years in Daniel Jarosz's lab. Long before he got interested in brain organoids, Richard worked on quantitative genetics in budding yeast. This study bridged the frustrating gap between broad statistical GWAS associations and tangible biochemical mechanisms. Most human GWAS hits large regions with many neutral passenger mutations that surround each true causal mutation. However, in yeast, advanced genetic engineering techniques make it possible to fine map these genetic neighborhoods to definitively pinpoint the exact causal mutations responsible for driving complex traits.

title: Massive Genomic Rearrangement Acquired in a Single Catastrophic Event during Cancer Development
text: Cancer is driven by somatically acquired point mutations and chromosomal rearrangements, conventionally thought to accumulate gradually over time. Using next-generation sequencing, we characterize a phenomenon, which we term chromothripsis, whereby tens to hundreds of genomic rearrangements occur in a one-off cellular crisis. Rearrangements involving one or a few chromosomes crisscross back and forth across involved regions, generating frequent oscillations between two copy number states. These genomic hallmarks are highly improbable if rearrangements accumulate over time and instead imply that nearly all occur during a single cellular catastrophe. The stamp of chromothripsis can be seen in at least 2%-3% of all cancers, across many subtypes, and is present in approximately 25% of bone cancers. We find that one, or indeed more than one, cancer-causing lesion can emerge out of the genomic crisis. This phenomenon has important implications for the origins of genomic remodeling and temporal emergence of cancer.
historical_context: In 2011, Peter Campbell, Philip Stephens, Michael Stratton, and colleagues documented a spectacular, previously unobserved phenomenon in cancer biology, which they termed "chromothripsis." Through deep sequencing of cancer genomes, they revealed that rather than quietly accumulating genetic mutations over years, certain cancers suffer dozens to hundreds of genomic rearrangements in a single, violently catastrophic cellular crisis. During this event, a chromosome or chromosomal arm literally shatters into pieces and is haphazardly stitched back together. This discovery shattered the classical dogma of gradual, step-wise tumor evolution. It demonstrated that cancer genomes can mutate via explosive, punctuated bursts, forcing a drastic reevaluation of tumorigenesis, genomic instability, and rapid cancer progression.

title: Massive Genomic Rearrangement Acquired in a Single Catastrophic Event during Cancer Development
text:
The textbook model of cancer development is of progression through a series of increasingly disordered clinical and pathological phases. For example, invasive colorectal cancer often emerges from an antecedent benign adenomatous polyp; cervical cancer proceeds through intraepithelial neoplasia before breaching the basement membrane; multiple myeloma frequently develops in individuals with a history of benign monoclonal plasma cell proliferation. Biologically, such stepwise clinical progression is underpinned by successive waves of clonal expansion as cells acquire the multiple genetic changes required for a fully malignant phenotype. Mutations are essentially random, occurring as independent events throughout the lifespan of an individual, potentially accelerated by exogenous carcinogens or DNA repair defects. Genetic variation generates phenotypic variation across the cells of an organ system, which are then subject to clonal selection through Darwinian competition. Variants that enhance a cell's evolutionary fitness, so-called driver mutations, promote outgrowth of that clone and progression toward cancer. The prevailing dogma of cancer evolution is therefore one of ''gradualism'' in which acquisition of driver mutations occurs cumulatively over years to decades, resulting in incremental progression through increasingly malignant phenotypes.

There are, however, examples in which a more ''punctuated equilibrium'' evolutionary model may apply to development of cancers. Genome-wide telomere attrition in somatic cells, for example, may generate naked DNA ends that act as a nidus for on-going genomic rearrangement. End-to-end chromosome fusions resulting from telomere loss can lead to spiraling cycles of dsDNA breakage, aberrant repair and further chromosomal damage in both daughter cells. Iteration of this breakage and repair process can lead to extensive genomic remodeling in multiple competing subclones in only a few cell cycles. Under these scenarios, bursts of somatic mutation may accrue in relatively short periods of chronological time.

Such genomic rearrangements can drive the development of cancer through several mechanisms. They may result in copy number changes, including deletion of tumor suppressor genes and increased copy number (amplification) of genes promoting malignant cellular processes. In addition, chromosomal rearrangements can juxtapose portions of coding sequence from two genes in the same orientation, leading to oncogenic fusion genes, or bring together an intact gene with the regulatory machinery of another gene, causing dysregulated gene expression.

Here, we describe multiple cancer samples in which tens to hundreds of genomic rearrangements have been acquired in a single catastrophic event, a phenomenon we have termed chromothripsis (Greek, chromos for chromosome; thripsis, shattering into pieces). We characterize the genomic hallmarks of this process, its frequency across diverse cancers and how such cataclysmic genome disruption can promote the development of cancer.

During this event, a chromosome or chromosomal arm literally shatters into pieces and is haphazardly stitched back together. This discovery shattered the classical dogma of gradual, step-wise tumor evolution. It demonstrated that cancer genomes can mutate via explosive, punctuated bursts, forcing a drastic reevaluation of tumorigenesis, genomic instability, and rapid cancer progression.

title: Massive Genomic Rearrangement Acquired in a Single Catastrophic Event during Cancer Development
text:
The evolution of cancer is driven by the somatic acquisition of genetic alterations that confer a selective advantage upon individual clones. These alterations include single nucleotide substitutions, small insertions and deletions, copy number changes, and structural rearrangements of chromosomes. In most conceptual models of tumorigenesis, such changes accumulate progressively over many cell generations, with successive rounds of mutation, selection and clonal expansion leading to increasing genomic complexity. This model is strongly supported by the stepwise acquisition of driver mutations in many tumor types and by the widespread observation of ongoing subclonal diversification within cancers.

Genomic rearrangements are among the most consequential of these alterations. Balanced translocations can create oncogenic fusion genes or place proto-oncogenes under the control of ectopic regulatory elements. Deletions can remove tumor suppressor genes, while amplifications can generate tens or hundreds of copies of oncogenes. In many cancers, rearrangements are scattered across the genome, reflecting generalized chromosomal instability, whereas in others they form complex local architectures associated with focal amplification, breakage-fusion-bridge cycles, or repeated cycles of DNA breakage and repair. Although these processes can generate highly abnormal chromosomes, they are generally assumed to arise through a succession of individually acquired events.

The advent of massively parallel sequencing now allows genomic rearrangements to be surveyed at high resolution across cancer genomes. Paired-end sequencing can identify rearranged genomic fragments by mapping read pairs whose ends align at unexpected positions, orientations, or distances from one another. When combined with genome-wide copy number and allelic ratio measurements, these data provide a direct view of the architecture produced by somatic DNA breakage and repair. They also make it possible to ask whether complex rearrangement patterns reflect gradual accumulation over time, or whether some cancers may acquire many rearrangements in a much more abrupt manner.

Here we describe a distinctive pattern of genomic remodeling in which tens to hundreds of rearrangements are confined to one or a few chromosomal regions. The rearrangements crisscross the affected regions in a mixture of orientations, while copy number oscillates rapidly between a small number of discrete states. These features are difficult to reconcile with progressive, independent acquisition of rearrangements, but are naturally explained by a single catastrophic event in which a chromosome or chromosomal region is shattered and then imperfectly reassembled. We term this phenomenon chromothripsis, from the Greek for chromosome shattering.

We identify the hallmarks of chromothripsis in chronic lymphocytic leukemia, in multiple cancer cell lines, and in primary bone tumors, where the phenomenon appears particularly frequent. We further show that such a genomic crisis can generate lesions likely to contribute directly to cancer development, including oncogene amplification and disruption or deletion of tumor suppressor genes. These findings suggest that, in a subset of cancers, major steps in genome evolution can occur not gradually, but in a single cellular catastrophe.

During this event, a chromosome or chromosomal arm literally shatters into pieces and is haphazardly stitched back together. This discovery shattered the classical dogma of gradual, step-wise tumor evolution. It demonstrated that cancer genomes can mutate via explosive, punctuated bursts, forcing a drastic reevaluation of tumorigenesis, genomic instability, and rapid cancer progression.

title: Massive Genomic Rearrangement Acquired in a Single Catastrophic Event during Cancer Development
text:
Cancer is a disease driven by the accumulation of somatic mutations and genomic rearrangements. The prevailing model of cancer evolution posits that these genetic alterations are acquired sequentially over many cell cycles. According to this progressive rearrangement model, periods of genomic instability gradually sculpt the genomic landscape of a tumor, leading to increasingly complex karyotypic abnormalities and the successive disruption of tumor suppressor genes or activation of oncogenes. While this step-wise accumulation of genomic lesions is a hallmark of many human malignancies, advances in high-throughput DNA sequencing now provide the unprecedented resolution required to comprehensively characterize the architecture of cancer genomes and interrogate the temporal dynamics of their evolution. In particular, massively parallel paired-end sequencing has emerged as a powerful tool for discovering structural variations across the entire genome by mapping the exact locations and orientations of chromosomal breakpoints.

In the course of cataloging genomic rearrangements in chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL) and other cancers, we have observed genomic profiles that challenge the conventional view of progressive, sequential mutational accumulation. Instead of a gradual acquisition of chromosomal structural changes, these profiles suggest a markedly different evolutionary trajectory in which massive genomic remodeling occurs during a single, catastrophic cellular event. We introduce the term "chromothripsis" to describe this phenomenon, wherein one or several chromosomes in a cell undergo massive shattering into tens or hundreds of fragments. These fragments are subsequently stitched together by the cell's DNA repair machinery in a haphazard fashion, producing a complex, localized medley of rearrangements. This single cataclysmic event results in a signature pattern of copy number oscillations alternating rapidly between two states, alongside a dense clustering of structural variants that cannot be easily explained by progressive independent breaks over time.

In this study, we leverage next-generation sequencing and high-resolution single nucleotide polymorphism (SNP) arrays to systematically characterize the genomic hallmarks of chromothripsis across various cancer types. We demonstrate that this phenomenon is not an isolated anomaly but is detectable in at least 2% to 3% of all human cancers, with a strikingly high prevalence in bone tumors such as osteosarcoma and chordoma. Furthermore, our findings indicate that cells surviving this genomic crisis can simultaneously acquire multiple cancer-driving lesions, such as the amplification of oncogenes or the concurrent disruption of several tumor suppressor genes. This catastrophic model of genomic rearrangement has profound implications for our understanding of the etiology of chromosomal instability and the rapid temporal emergence of cancer.

During this event, a chromosome or chromosomal arm literally shatters into pieces and is haphazardly stitched back together. This discovery shattered the classical dogma of gradual, step-wise tumor evolution. It demonstrated that cancer genomes can mutate via explosive, punctuated bursts, forcing a drastic reevaluation of tumorigenesis, genomic instability, and rapid cancer progression.

title: Massive Genomic Rearrangement Acquired in a Single Catastrophic Event during Cancer Development
text:
Here we describe a quite remarkable phenomenon whereby tens to hundreds of chromosomal rearrangements involving localized genomic regions can be acquired in an apparently one-off cellular catastrophe. Astoundingly, not only can a cell actually survive this crisis, it can emerge with a genomic landscape that confers a significant selective advantage to the clone, promoting the evolution toward cancer. Such an event appears to have occurred in 2%-3% of all cancers, across many subtypes, and may be particularly frequent in bone cancers.

There are few documented examples of how catastrophic genomic change affects evolutionary processes. Reassortment of influenza virus genomes can lead to entirely novel strains with considerable pandemic potential. In eukaryotes, ''showers'' of several point mutations in a localized genomic region in a single cell cycle have been described in murine models, with similar arguments extended to clustered mutations in humans with germline genetic diseases. We would predict that in the case of chromothripsis, the overwhelming majority of cells suffering such spectacular genomic damage would either die or acquire more detrimental than advantageous variants. However, very rarely, a cell might acquire one or more cancer-causing lesions from such an event and this clone would then have taken a considerable leap along the road to cancer. There would still be the need for additional mutations in cancer genes, exemplified by the second hits in CDKN2A seen in the chordoma and thyroid cancer samples, but we might anticipate the emergent tumor having shorter latency.

What causes such dramatic damage to the genome? The distinctive signature of the process gives some clues. The genomic regions involved in each example are sharply circumscribed, whether it be a whole chromosome, a chromosome arm or a region of just a few megabases within a chromosomal band. It seems likely that the insult occurs while the chromosomes are condensed for mitosis. DNA damage acquired in interphase would seem unlikely to exhibit such intense clustering of breaks within such well-circumscribed genomic regions. The existence of rearrangements involving both sides of a DNA break, the potential to create both a derivative chromosome and a double minute chromosome in the same event and the seeming near-randomness of which fragment is joined to which fragment suggest that literally hundreds of shards of genomic DNA circulate unfettered in the nucleus during the catastrophe, that the DNA repair machinery is pasting them together in a helter-skelter tumult of activity.

The agent of this physical chromosomal damage is unknown. One appealing possibility is ionizing radiation. Well-known to induce dsDNA breaks, a pulse of ionizing radiation could cut a swathe through a condensed chromosome and, depending on whether the angle of the path relative to the long axis of the chromosome is transverse, oblique or longitudinal, generate breaks involving a band, an arm or the whole chromosome. Such a model could potentially be tested by in vitro studies of cells surviving irradiation and by analysis of cancer genomes from patients with prior environmental or therapeutic radiation exposure.

Another intriguing possibility is that the breakage-fusion-bridge cycle associated with telomere attrition could induce the damage, especially because most examples of chromothripsis observed here involve regions extending to the telomeres. End-to-end chromosome fusions are a cytogenetic hallmark of telomere loss, and the two centromeres of such dicentric chromosomes are pulled to opposite daughter cells during anaphase, forming a so-called anaphase bridge. It is unclear how these bridges are resolved, but they appear to induce the formation of nuclear buds and micronuclei containing fragmented DNA in the daughter cells. It is therefore conceivable that the dramatic stretching and pinching of the chromosome bridge during the final stages of cytokinesis could be associated with catastrophic, but localized, genomic damage. If this hypothesis is true, cancer genomes from genetically engineered mouse models of telomerase deficiency may demonstrate similar patterns of genomic rearrangement to those observed here.

Whatever the mechanism of damage, the consequences are profound. Faced with hundreds of DNA breaks, the cell's DNA repair machinery attempts to rescue the genome. The resultant hodgepodge bears little resemblance to its original structure, and the genomic disruption has wholesale and potentially oncogenic effects.

It demonstrated that cancer genomes can mutate via explosive, punctuated bursts, forcing a drastic reevaluation of tumorigenesis, genomic instability, and rapid cancer progression.

paper_title: Massive Genomic Rearrangement Acquired in a Single Catastrophic Event during Cancer Development - 2011 - Cell
display_title: Massive Genomic Rearrangement Acquired in a Single Catastrophic Event during Cancer Development
title: Massive Genomic Rearrangement Acquired in a Single Catastrophic Event during Cancer Development
text:
The data presented here identify chromothripsis as a distinctive mechanism by which cancer genomes can be remodeled. In contrast to the conventional view that somatic mutations and rearrangements are acquired progressively over many years, chromothripsis implies that tens to hundreds of genomic rearrangements can arise in a single catastrophic episode. The resulting structures are not simply extreme examples of the disordered karyotypes long recognized in cancer. Rather, they carry a set of genomic signatures that point to a specific mode of origin: localization to one or a few chromosomal regions, rapid oscillation between a limited number of copy number states, preservation of heterozygosity in retained fragments, and highly clustered breakpoints joined in multiple orientations.

These features make it difficult to explain chromothripsis as the cumulative endpoint of sequential, independent rearrangements. Under a progressive model, multiple deletion, duplication, and inversion events would be expected to generate increasingly complex copy number profiles, including many distinct copy number states and widespread loss of heterozygosity. Instead, the observed profiles are often dominated by alternating regions of loss and retention, as would be expected if a chromosome were fragmented and only a subset of pieces were incorporated into a derivative chromosome. The fact that these configurations are stable across cells and persist at relapse in the CLL case further suggests that the genomic crisis occurred before clonal expansion, rather than representing ongoing instability in many subclones.

Chromothripsis therefore provides an example of punctuated evolution in cancer. A single event can abruptly generate a genomic architecture that would otherwise require a long succession of rearrangements. This does not exclude the importance of gradual mutation accumulation in most cancers, nor does it imply that all genomic complexity has a catastrophic origin. Indeed, some derivative chromosomes may subsequently duplicate, some rearrangements may occur after the main event, and focal deletions or amplifications may be acquired independently. Nevertheless, the majority of rearrangements in the cases described here are best explained by a one-off event, placing chromothripsis alongside other mechanisms through which cancer evolution can accelerate over a short period of biological time.

The mechanism that causes such catastrophic chromosome breakage remains uncertain. One possibility is that a chromosome or chromosomal region becomes physically isolated, damaged, or missegregated, leading to extensive double-stranded DNA breakage followed by imperfect repair. Telomere dysfunction, aborted breakage-fusion-bridge cycles, mitotic errors, or exposure to DNA-damaging processes could each, in principle, generate the necessary burst of DNA damage. The limited sequence overlap at breakpoint junctions is consistent with repair through nonhomologous end-joining or microhomology-mediated pathways rather than homologous recombination. Whatever the initiating lesion, survival of a cell after such damage must be rare, and the emergence of a cancer clone implies that the rearranged genome either conferred selective advantage or occurred in a cellular context already permissive for survival despite extensive DNA damage.

The selective consequences of chromothripsis can be considerable. In the examples studied here, the same catastrophic event can generate copy number loss of tumor suppressor genes, disruption of coding sequence, alteration of gene expression, and substrates for amplification. The SCLC-21H cell line illustrates how chromothripsis may give rise to double minute chromosomes carrying MYC, which can then be selected and amplified. In chordoma, the simultaneous involvement of CDKN2A, WRN, and FBXW7 shows that more than one cancer-relevant lesion can emerge from a single genomic crisis. Thus, chromothripsis can compress several steps of tumor evolution into one event, providing a route by which a clone may acquire multiple advantageous alterations without requiring them to arise independently.

The frequency of chromothripsis also has implications for cancer classification and genome interpretation. We estimate that at least 2%-3% of cancers show evidence of this phenomenon, with a much higher prevalence in bone tumors in the cohort analyzed here. These numbers are likely conservative, because detection depends on the resolution of copy number data, the availability of rearrangement information, and the ability to recognize the pattern after subsequent evolution of the cancer genome. As large-scale cancer genome sequencing expands, chromothripsis may prove to be a recurrent feature of particular histological subtypes or genetic backgrounds.

More broadly, chromothripsis changes how we interpret complex cancer genomes. It shows that extreme genomic complexity need not always represent a long history of stepwise instability. Instead, a cancer genome may contain the scar of a single catastrophic event, followed by clonal selection and further evolution. Recognizing this distinction is important for reconstructing tumor history, identifying driver lesions embedded within complex rearrangements, and understanding how malignant clones can arise from sudden, profound disruption of chromosome structure.
