By exhaustively cataloging mutations that corrupted the fly's segmentation, they neatly classified developmental genes into gap, pair-rule, and segment-polarity genes, laying bare the hierarchical genetic logic that instructs an amorphous egg how to build a highly structured organism. This seminal work earned them the 1995 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine (shared with Edward B. Lewis). The Nobel committee praised their discoveries concerning the genetic control of early embryonic development, revealing foundational blueprints of embryogenesis that are deeply conserved across the animal kingdom, including humans.

paper_title: Mutations Affecting Segment Number and Polarity in Drosophila - 1980 - Nature
display_title: Mutations Affecting Segment Number and Polarity in Drosophila
title: Mutations Affecting Segment Number and Polarity in Drosophila
text:
The establishment of a segmental pattern is one of the most conspicuous features of insect embryogenesis. In Drosophila the metameric subdivision of the body is first recognized during gastrulation and is later expressed in the regular arrangement of cuticular structures in the larva. Each segment has a characteristic morphology, but all thoracic and abdominal segments share a common organization and polarity. Thus, the larval cuticle provides a simple and reproducible pattern in which alterations of segment number, position and polarity can be recognized with considerable precision.

Many mutations are known which transform the identity of one segment into that of another. These homeotic mutations have shown that segmental identity can be altered without necessarily abolishing segmentation itself. Much less is known about the genes required for the generation of the segmental subdivision. Mutations of this kind would be expected to change the number or arrangement of segments rather than only their regional character. Since the differentiated larval pattern preserves the results of earlier embryonic events, such mutations should allow an analysis of the steps by which the initially continuous egg becomes divided into a series of homologous units.

We have therefore searched systematically for embryonic lethal mutations which alter the cuticular pattern of the Drosophila larva. The mutations described here were isolated without regard to any particular chromosome region or previously known locus. The mutant embryos were examined for reproducible deviations from the normal segmental pattern. Although many embryonic lethal mutations have no obvious effect on the larval cuticle, a small number cause specific and characteristic changes in segmentation.

The phenotypes of these mutants fall into three main groups. In one group, a defined part of each segment is deleted and the remaining part is duplicated with reversed polarity. In a second group, homologous parts of alternating segments are missing, suggesting a repeat unit larger than the individual segment. In a third group, one continuous region of the embryo fails to form the normal sequence of segments. These classes do not correspond to transformations of segment identity, but rather to different alterations in the spatial organization of the embryo.

The mutant phenotypes therefore provide genetic evidence that segmentation is not established in a single step. Instead, the embryo appears to pass through successive levels of organization, involving the whole egg, a double-segmental repeat, and the individual segment. The loci defined by these mutations identify zygotic functions required for this process.

paper_title: Mutations Affecting Segment Number and Polarity in Drosophila - 1980 - Nature
display_title: Mutations Affecting Segment Number and Polarity in Drosophila
title: Mutations Affecting Segment Number and Polarity in Drosophila
text:
The development of a complex, multicellular organism from a single fertilized egg is a highly coordinated process that relies on the precise spatial and temporal regulation of gene expression. In the fruit fly Drosophila melanogaster, early embryogenesis provides a uniquely tractable model system for understanding the genetic mechanisms that govern the initial partitioning of the body plan. The morphological blueprint of the Drosophila larva is characterized by a repeating pattern of homologous units along its antero-posterior axis, specifically three thoracic and eight abdominal segments. While each segment ultimately acquires a unique morphological identity—evident in the specific structures and polarity of the denticle bands on the ventral cuticle—the foundational process of segment formation itself must first subdivide the embryonic field into discrete repeating domains.

Although numerous homeotic mutations have been described that dramatically alter the specific identity of individual segments, these mutations generally do not disrupt the total number or the basic spatial delineation of the segments themselves. This suggests that the genetic processes responsible for defining the initial segmental framework of the embryo are distinct from those that subsequently specify the unique character of each segmental unit. To uncover the genes that construct this fundamental body plan, we have undertaken systematic genetic screens for embryonic lethal mutations that cause specific, reproducible alterations in the basic segmentation pattern of the larva.

In this report, we describe fifteen distinct genetic loci which, when mutated, lead to profound defects in the number, spacing, and polarity of the larval segments. Through a detailed phenotypic analysis of homozygous mutant embryos, we have been able to categorize these loci into three distinct phenotypic classes based on their specific effects on the body plan. These include the gap mutants, which lack large, contiguous stretches of multiple segments; the pair-rule mutants, which exhibit deletions in homologous structures in alternating segments; and the segment-polarity mutants, which possess the normal number of segments but display characteristic pattern deletions and mirror-image duplications within each individual segment. The distinct nature of these mutant phenotypes provides striking genetic evidence that the morphological process of segmentation in Drosophila involves at least three hierarchical levels of spatial organization during early embryonic development. These loci likely represent the majority of the key genes required to establish the segmental body plan of the organism.

This seminal work earned them the 1995 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine (shared with Edward B. Lewis). The Nobel committee praised their discoveries concerning the genetic control of early embryonic development, revealing foundational blueprints of embryogenesis that are deeply conserved across the animal kingdom, including humans.

paper_title: Mutations Affecting Segment Number and Polarity in Drosophila - 1980 - Nature
display_title: Mutations Affecting Segment Number and Polarity in Drosophila
title: Mutations Affecting Segment Number and Polarity in Drosophila
text:
Segmentation in Drosophila proceeds by the transition from a single field into a repeated pattern of homologous smaller subfields. Mutant alleles at the 15 loci we have described interfere with this process at various points. Although each locus has its own distinct phenotype, we were able to distribute the mutations in three classes. In one class only a single large subregion of the embryo is affected, whereas in mutants of the other two classes a reiteration of defects is produced with a repeat length of one or two segments, respectively. This suggests that the process of segmentation involves three different units of spatial organization.

The organization of the egg is thought to be controlled by a monotonic gradient set up during oogenesis under the control of the maternal genome. All the mutants we have described depend on the embryonic rather than the maternal genome. None of them alters the overall polarity of the embryo, the head always being at the anterior and the telson at the posterior end of the egg. The most dramatic alterations of the pattern are produced in the gap mutants and involve one large subregion of the egg. Hunchback and knirps affect the development of thorax and abdomen, respectively, and might be involved in the establishment of these large morphologically unique subregions of the embryo. The large mirror-image duplications found in the posterior pattern of Krüppel embryos are similar to those of bicaudal. The bicaudal phenotype has been interpreted as resulting from an instability in the maternal gradient, and thus Krüppel might be involved in the maintenance of elaboration of this gradient in the posterior egg region after fertilization.

The smallest repeat unit, the individual segment, is affected in mutants at the six segment-polarity loci. The pattern alteration consists of a mirror-image duplication of part of the normal pattern with the remainder deleted. In these mutants the deleted region corresponds in size to more than half a segment. Mutations causing smaller deletions in the segmental pattern do not lead to polarity reversals. The mirror-image duplications produced by the pair-rule mutation runt are also associated with a deletion of more than half a repeat unit, that is, more than one segment. The tendency of partial fields containing less than half the positional values to duplicate is well described for imaginal disks in Drosophila, as well as for the larval epidermis in other insects. On the other hand, the same types of pattern duplication are also produced in conditions which do not involve cell death and regeneration, but rather a reorganization of the positional information in the entire field. More detailed studies of early development in mutant embryos may reveal which mechanism is responsible for the different mutant phenotypes.

Given the evidence for a homology between segments, the existence of a class of mutants affecting corresponding regions in each individual segment is perhaps not surprising. The discovery of a mutant class affecting corresponding regions in every other segment was not expected and suggests the existence at some time during development of homologous units with the size of two segmental anlagen. It is possible that the double segmental unit corresponds to transitory double segmental fields which are established early during embryogenesis and are later subdivided into individual segments. At the blastoderm stage the epidermal primordium giving rise to thorax and abdomen is only about 40 cells long. An initial subdivision into double segments would avoid problems of accuracy encountered in a simultaneous establishment of segment boundaries every three to four cells. A stepwise establishment of segments implies that the borders defining double segments be made before the intervening ones. The mutant phenotypes do not definitely show which, if any, of the segment borders define a primary double segmental division in normal development. The mutant phenotypes which come closest to a pattern one would expect if the transition from the double segment to the individual segment stage were blocked are the patterns of paired and even-skipped. Both suggest the frame meso- and metathorax, 1st and 2nd, 3rd and 4th abdominal segment, etc.

It is also possible that the double segmental units are never defined by distinct borders in normal development. The existence of a double segmental homology unit may merely reflect a continuous property such as a wave with a double segmental period responsible for correct spacing of segmental boundaries. We have not found any mutations showing a repeat unit larger than two segments. This may indicate that the subdivision of the blastoderm proceeds directly by the double segmental repeat with no larger intervening homology units. However, the failure to identify such larger units may reflect the incompleteness of our data.

Drosophila has been estimated to have about 5,000 genes and only a very small fraction of these when mutated result in a change of the segmental pattern of the larva. Some of the loci described here were known previously but only in the case of Krüppel has the embryonic lethal phenotype been recognized as affecting segmentation. The majority of the mutants described here have been isolated in systematic searches for mutations affecting the segmentation pattern of the Drosophila larva. These experiments are still incomplete. Most of the alleles on the second chromosome were isolated in one experiment which yielded an average allele frequency of four or five alleles per locus. From this yield and similar calculations for the third and first chromosomes, we estimate that we have identified almost all segmentation loci on the second chromosome and about 50% each of those on the third and first chromosome. Our sample of 15 loci should therefore represent the majority of the loci affecting segmentation in the Drosophila genome. Thus, in Drosophila it would seem feasible to identify all genetic components involved in the complex process of embryonic pattern formation.

Note added in proof: All known barrel alleles fail to complement hairy, suggesting that the barrel mutations are alleles at the hairy locus.

This seminal work earned them the 1995 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine (shared with Edward B. Lewis). paper_title: Mutations Affecting Segment Number and Polarity in Drosophila - 1980 - Nature
display_title: Mutations Affecting Segment Number and Polarity in Drosophila
title: Mutations Affecting Segment Number and Polarity in Drosophila
text:
The mutations described here identify a set of zygotic genes which are required for the establishment of the segmented pattern of the Drosophila larva. Their phenotypes show that segmentation is not a single event, nor can it be understood simply as the assignment of segmental identities to a pre-existing series of equivalent units. Rather, the pattern seems to be generated through several successive levels of spatial subdivision. The three classes of mutants define defects in regions of different size: continuous portions of the embryo, repeated units of two segments, and fractions of single segments. This order of effects suggests that the normal embryo is first organized along its antero-posterior axis, then subdivided in a double-segmental periodicity, and finally resolved into the individual segmental pattern.

The gap mutants are the simplest to interpret in terms of axial position. In Krüppel, hunchback and knirps a continuous stretch of the body pattern is missing, and the affected region is characteristic for each locus. These mutations therefore appear to disturb the allocation of unique positions along the length of the embryo. The terminal regions are not affected in the same way as the middle regions, and in Krüppel a posterior terminal pattern is followed by a mirror-image arrangement. Such phenotypes are reminiscent of those produced by maternal-effect mutations, but they are caused here by the zygotic genome. It is therefore likely that the maternal information present in the egg is not sufficient by itself to specify the entire segmental array, but must be interpreted by zygotic genes which define broad regions of the embryo.

The pair-rule mutants reveal a second and quite different level of organization. In these embryos the pattern is not deleted in one continuous block, but in a repeated fashion at intervals of two segments. The deleted regions do not correspond to whole segments, and their margins do not coincide with segment boundaries. Moreover, different loci remove different parts of the same double-segmental repeat. These observations make it unlikely that the primary defect is the absence of every other segment as an already determined unit. Instead, the data suggest that, before the final segmental boundaries are established, the embryo contains a periodic pattern with a wavelength corresponding to two future segments. The normal segmental series may be derived by subdivision or refinement of this earlier periodicity.

The combinations with homeotic mutations support this interpretation. In paired and even-skipped embryos, the altered pattern remains pairwise even when the identity of particular segments is changed by Ultrabithorax or Polycomb. Conversely, the homeotic transformations do not correct or otherwise modify the basic deletion pattern caused by the pair-rule mutation. The processes affected by pair-rule genes must therefore be distinct from those which determine segmental identity. They concern the number and position of segmental elements rather than the character assigned to them. The composite structures in paired and knirps larvae further show that segmental identity can be expressed in parts of an abnormal pattern, and that it is not dependent on the formation of normal segmental borders.

The segment polarity mutants define a third level of pattern formation, within the segment itself. In these embryos the normal number of segments, or at least the normal number of repeated segmental elements, is retained, but a defined part of each element is deleted and another part is duplicated with reversed polarity. The phenotype is therefore not a general failure of differentiation, nor a transformation of one segment into another. It is a local alteration of the arrangement within each segmental repeat. This suggests that after the embryo has acquired its segmental periodicity, further genetic functions are required to specify the anterior-posterior polarity and the relative positions of structures within each segment.

The frequent association of deletion and mirror-image duplication is especially informative. It indicates that the remaining part of the pattern can be organized symmetrically when the missing region is not specified. In the segment polarity mutants this occurs within each segment, whereas in Krüppel a similar principle may operate over a much larger part of the embryo. Such mirror-image arrangements suggest that neighbouring cells may acquire their pattern not only from absolute position, but also from local organizing influences which can be duplicated or reversed when a normal boundary or signalling region is absent. The mutants do not yet distinguish whether the affected genes act in the production of such local signals, in the response of cells to them, or in the stabilization of the resulting pattern.

All the genes described here act zygotically, yet their effects are already visible before or shortly after gastrulation. This timing is important because the larval cuticle is a late product of development, whereas the mutations alter the pattern at a much earlier stage. The reduced number of ventral bulges in paired and even-skipped embryos, the short germ bands of Krüppel, runt and knirps, and the temperature-sensitive period of paired all indicate that these loci act during the blastoderm or early gastrula stages. Thus the genetic programme for segmentation is initiated before the visible formation of larval structures, at a time when the blastoderm still consists of a simple epithelium.

The number of loci recovered in our screens suggests that the major zygotic components of segmentation may be relatively few. This conclusion must be cautious, since some genes may have escaped detection because of weak phenotypes, maternal contributions, or functions required earlier for viability. Nevertheless, the mutations obtained fall into coherent phenotypic classes, and many alleles were recovered repeatedly. It seems therefore probable that these loci represent a substantial fraction of the genes whose zygotic function is specifically required to establish the larval segmental pattern.

The phenotypes provide a genetic framework for analysing how a metameric pattern is generated. Maternal determinants may define the global coordinates of the egg. Gap genes then appear to establish broad regions along these coordinates. Pair-rule genes impose a repeated pattern with a two-segment periodicity, and segment polarity genes organize the final pattern within each segment. Homeotic genes, acting in a different process, assign identities to the segments or to parts of them. This hierarchy is inferred from mutant phenotypes rather than from direct observation of gene products, and its steps may overlap in time. Even so, the distinct classes of mutations show that the segmented body plan is assembled by separable genetic processes. The study of these genes should make it possible to determine how positional information is converted into a precise series of segmental units.

paper_title: Mutations Affecting Segment Number and Polarity in Drosophila - 1980 - Nature
display_title: Mutations Affecting Segment Number and Polarity in Drosophila
title: Mutations Affecting Segment Number and Polarity in Drosophila
text:
The systematic identification of fifteen genetic loci that specifically control the number and polarity of Drosophila segments provides a comprehensive framework for understanding the zygotic contribution to embryonic pattern formation. Our results suggest that the subdivision of the antero-posterior axis into a metameric array is not a single-step process but involves a hierarchy of increasingly refined spatial organizational levels. By characterizing the phenotypes of gap, pair-rule, and segment polarity mutants, we can begin to reconstruct the logical sequence of events that transforms the relatively simple information of the maternal gradient into the complex, repetitive structure of the larval epidermis.

The gap genes—*Krüppel*, *hunchback*, and *knirps*—appear to represent the first level of zygotic response to the maternal coordinate system. Their function is required for the establishment of large, continuous body regions. The deletion of up to eight adjacent segments in these mutants suggests that these genes are active in broad domains of the blastoderm. Interestingly, the gap phenotypes do not show a repeated pattern along the axis, implying that they respond to unique, non-repeating values provided by the maternal morphogen gradient. The *Krüppel* phenotype is especially significant in this regard; the mirror-image symmetry observed in these embryos, which lacks a large central portion of the body, reminiscent of the maternal *bicaudal* effect, suggests that *Krüppel* is essential for interpreting the middle range of the maternal gradient. In its absence, the developmental program appears to "collapse" or revert to terminal identities that expand to fill the void. This supports a model where the gap genes subdivide the embryo into a few large, unique domains that serve as the foundation for all subsequent metameric subdivisions.

The discovery of the pair-rule mutants—*even-skipped*, *odd-skipped*, *paired*, *runt*, *barrel*, and *engrailed*—was perhaps the most unexpected finding of our search. These mutants demonstrate that the embryo is organized into repeating units with a periodicity of two segments before individual segments are defined. The fact that homologous parts of the pattern are deleted in every other segment indicates that the developmental machinery "counts" in units of two. The distinct, partially overlapping deletion patterns of loci like *even-skipped* (which affects even-numbered segments) and *odd-skipped* (which affects odd-numbered segments) suggest that the entire segmental array is first covered by a series of double-segment periodicities. This level of organization intervened between the broad domains of the gap genes and the individual segments. The composite nature of the segments in *paired* embryos, as revealed by our combinations with homeotic mutants like *Ultrabithorax*, provides compelling evidence that the establishment of segmental boundaries and the specification of segmental identity are independent processes. The pair-rule genes appear to be responsible for the periodic "rhythm" of the embryo, defining the positions of future segment boundaries based on their location within a double-segment repeat.

The third level of organization is represented by the segment polarity genes. Unlike the gap or pair-rule genes, these six loci—*wingless*, *gooseberry*, *hedgehog*, *patch*, *fused*, and *cubitus interruptus*—affect every segment. The common feature of this class is the deletion of a specific fraction of the pattern in each segment and its replacement by a mirror-image duplication of the remaining part. This suggests that these genes are required to specify the internal organization and polarity of individual segmental units. The mirror-image duplications are particularly telling; they imply that each segment possesses an internal coordinate system or gradient that dictates the arrangement of structures (e.g., denticle bands vs. naked cuticle) and their orientation. When a segment polarity gene is mutated, this internal system is disrupted, and the cells appear to adopt a "default" or duplicated state. The fact that the deletions in *patch* involve structures from two adjacent segments further highlights the importance of these genes in defining the boundaries between segments.

Our results also clarify the relationship between genes affecting segment number and the homeotic genes of the *bithorax* complex. Homeotic mutations transform the identity of segments without altering their number, size, or polarity. Our combinations of *paired* or *even-skipped* with *Ultrabithorax* and *Polycomb* demonstrate that the periodic grouping of segments in pairs occurs regardless of the segments' identity. This confirms that the segmentation genes we have identified operate "upstream" of, or in parallel to, the identity genes. The segmentation genes establish the physical units (the metameres), while the homeotic genes subsequently assign a specific "flavor" or identity to each of those units.

The timing of these genetic activities is consistent with their role as primary architects of the body plan. Our analysis of temperature-sensitive alleles and early morphological defects indicates that these genes are active during the blastoderm stage, a period when the embryo is still a syncytium or a single layer of cells. This is the stage when cell lineage restrictions are established, and our data suggest it is also when the final number and arrangement of segments are definitively determined. The fact that *Krüppel*, *runt*, and *knirps* embryos show germ-band defects as early as fifteen minutes after the onset of gastrulation underscores the immediacy of their impact on developmental topology.

In summary, the transition from an egg to a segmented larva is achieved through a hierarchical refinement of spatial information. The maternal gradient provides the global coordinates, which the zygotic gap genes translate into broad domains. These domains are then subdivided by the pair-rule genes into a periodic, double-segment pattern. Finally, the segment polarity genes define the internal structure and boundaries of each individual segment within that periodic array. This "divide and conquer" strategy allows the embryo to manage the enormous complexity of metameric organization using a relatively small set of genetic interactions. The identification of these fifteen loci likely represents the majority of the genes involved in this fundamental process, providing a molecular map for the spatial organization of the Drosophila embryo.

This seminal work earned them the 1995 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine (shared with Edward B. Lewis). The Nobel committee praised their discoveries concerning the genetic control of early embryonic development, revealing foundational blueprints of embryogenesis that are deeply conserved across the animal kingdom, including humans.

paper_title: Novel Proteinaceous Infectious Particles Cause Scrapie - 1982 - Science
display_title: Novel Proteinaceous Infectious Particles Cause Scrapie
title: Novel Proteinaceous Infectious Particles Cause Scrapie
text: After infection and a prolonged incubation period, the scrapie agent causes a degenerative disease of the central nervous system in sheep and goats. Six lines of evidence including sensitivity to proteases demonstrate that this agent contains a protein that is required for infectivity. Although the scrapie agent is irreversibly inactivated by alkali, five procedures with more specificity for modifying nucleic acids failed to cause inactivation. The agent shows heterogeneity with respect to size, apparently a result of its hydrophobicity; the smallest form may have a molecular weight of 50,000 or less. Because the novel properties of the scrapie agent distinguish it from viruses, plasmids, and viroids, a new term "prion" is proposed to denote a small proteinaceous infectious particle which is resistant to inactivation by most procedures that modify nucleic acids. Knowledge of the scrapie agent structure may have significance for understanding the causes of several degenerative diseases.
historical_context: In 1982, Stanley Prusiner published this highly controversial, iconoclast paper asserting that scrapie, an infectious neurodegenerative disease in sheep, is propelled not by a virus or bacterium, but by a rogue protein completely devoid of nucleic acid. He originated the term "prion" (proteinaceous infectious particle) for this unprecedented agent, theorizing that a misfolded host protein could physically coerce normal proteins into misfolding and aggregating. Prusiner's unyielding pursuit of this theory, which directly defied decades of established biological dogma regarding infectious agents, earned him the 1997 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine. The Nobel committee honored his discovery of prions, validating a fundamentally new principle of infection that remains central to decoding transmissible spongiform encephalopathies and understanding the pathological propagation of misfolded proteins in Alzheimer's and Parkinson's diseases.

paper_title: Novel Proteinaceous Infectious Particles Cause Scrapie - 1982 - Science
display_title: Novel Proteinaceous Infectious Particles Cause Scrapie
title: Novel Proteinaceous Infectious Particles Cause Scrapie
text: A major obstacle to defining the etiology of scrapie has been the unusual resistance of the infectious agent to procedures that inactivate conventional viruses. We developed a rapid hamster bioassay based on incubation time and used it to follow purification of the agent from infected brain. Partially purified preparations contained high titers of infectivity with little DNA or RNA and exhibited pronounced hydrophobicity. Multiple treatments that modify or denature proteins, including proteinase K, trypsin, diethyl pyrocarbonate, sodium dodecyl sulfate, chaotropic salts, phenol, and urea, diminished infectivity. In contrast, nucleases, zinc-catalyzed hydrolysis, psoralens, hydroxylamine, and high doses of ultraviolet irradiation failed to reveal a required nucleic acid. The molecular properties of the agent differ from those of viruses, viroids, and plasmids. These findings suggest that scrapie is caused by a small proteinaceous infectious particle, or prion, although a protected nucleic acid cannot yet be excluded.

Prusiner's unyielding pursuit of this theory, which directly defied decades of established biological dogma regarding infectious agents, earned him the 1997 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine. The Nobel committee honored his discovery of prions, validating a fundamentally new principle of infection that remains central to decoding transmissible spongiform encephalopathies and understanding the pathological propagation of misfolded proteins in Alzheimer's and Parkinson's diseases.

display_title: Novel Proteinaceous Infectious Particles Cause Scrapie
title: Novel Proteinaceous Infectious Particles Cause Scrapie
text: The scrapie agent causes a degenerative neurological disorder in sheep and goats, yet its molecular structure has remained mysterious. To characterize this enigmatic pathogen, we developed an accelerated bioassay based on incubation time in Syrian hamsters, permitting substantial purification of the infectivity. The infectious agent exhibits marked hydrophobicity and an array of anomalous biochemical properties. Notably, it is resistant to numerous treatments that inactivate nucleic acids, including nucleases, ultraviolet irradiation, and psoralens. In contrast, the agent is readily inactivated by proteases and protein-denaturing agents such as sodium dodecyl sulfate, phenol, urea, and chaotropic salts. These results strongly suggest that the pathogen lacks a conventional nucleic acid genome and instead requires a protein for its infectivity. Because of its unprecedented properties, which distinguish it from viruses, plasmids, and viroids, we introduce the term "prion" to denote these novel proteinaceous infectious particles.

paper_title: Novel Proteinaceous Infectious Particles Cause Scrapie - 1982 - Science
display_title: Novel Proteinaceous Infectious Particles Cause Scrapie
title: Novel Proteinaceous Infectious Particles Cause Scrapie
text:
A major, unanswered question in molecular biology concerns the chemical structure of the scrapie agent. Until recently, mysteries surrounding the scrapie agent were so commonplace that investigators had come to accept rather than question its enigmatic properties. The scrapie agent causes a degenerative disorder of the central nervous system in sheep and goats.

The extraordinary resistance of the scrapie agent to formalin was responsible for the inadvertent inoculation of sheep in Scotland. Eighteen thousand animals were vaccinated against louping ill virus with a formalin-treated suspension of ovine brain and spleen that had been contaminated with the scrapie agent. Two years later, 1500 sheep developed scrapie. Subsequently, studies on CNS diseases of sheep provided the foundation for Sigurdsson's concept of slow infections. In 1959, Hadlow suggested that kuru, a CNS degenerative disease of New Guinea highlanders, might be similar to scrapie because the pathologies of these disorders share many features. The transmission of kuru to chimpanzees in 1965 by Gajdusek, Gibbs, and Alpers forced a major reconsideration of the etiology of all degenerative disorders and made scrapie a subject of intense medical interest. Subsequently, Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease (CJD), a progressive, presenile dementia, was shown to be caused by a transmissible agent.

A recent study suggests that there may be similarities between the agents causing scrapie and CJD. Goats inoculated with brain tissue from demented patients dying of CJD developed a neurological disorder 3 to 4 years after inoculation. Five out of ten CJD inocula have produced disease in goats. Experimental CJD in goats is indistinguishable both clinically and neuropathologically from natural scrapie. Monkeys have been used as a common experimental host for scrapie and CJD; curiously, chimpanzees are susceptible to CJD but not scrapie. Numerous attempts to link scrapie epidemiologically to CJD have been unsuccessful. At present, there is no direct evidence that the scrapie agent causes disease in humans.

In contrast to CJD which occurs worldwide, kuru is found only in a small mountainous region of Papua New Guinea. Epidemiological studies of kuru provide evidence for incubation periods of 20 to 30 years. Although considerable evidence implicates cannibalism in the spread of kuru, no direct observations of cannibalistic acts in the endemic region have been recorded. Attempts to transmit kuru by feeding infected brain tissue to chimpanzees have been unsuccessful although one monkey developed a kuru-like illness 36 months after oral ingestion of the kuru agent. In contrast, goats fed scrapie-infected tissue frequently develop disease. Recently, we have taken advantage of the natural cannibalistic activities of hamsters to develop an experimental model of scrapie transmitted by cannibalism. Oral transmission of the scrapie agent appears to be extremely inefficient. Cannibalism requires a dose of agent 10^9 times greater than that needed to produce scrapie by intracerebral injection. These results provide compelling evidence for oral transmission of the scrapie agent and may offer new insights into the spread of kuru by cannibalism among the Fore people and their neighboring tribes.

Hypotheses on the chemical structure of the scrapie agent have included: sarcosporidia parasite, filterable virus, small DNA virus, replicating protein, replicating abnormal polysaccharide within membranes, DNA subvirus controlled by a transmissible linkage substance, provirus consisting of recessive genes generating RNA particles, naked nucleic acid similar to plant viroids, unconventional virus, aggregated conventional virus with unusual properties, replicating polysaccharide, nucleoprotein complex, nucleic acid surrounded by a polysaccharide coat, spiroplasmalike organism, multicomponent system with one component quite small, and membrane-bound DNA.

text:
Scrapie is a transmissible degenerative disease of the central nervous system that occurs naturally in sheep and goats. Related disorders of humans, including kuru and Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease (CJD), share several pathological and biological properties with scrapie. All of these diseases are characterized by a prolonged incubation period, a progressive neurological syndrome, and a fatal outcome. The infectious agents responsible for these disorders have been difficult to define because they do not behave like conventional viruses, bacteria, or fungi. They are unusually resistant to heat and to many procedures that ordinarily inactivate microorganisms, yet they retain the capacity to reproduce within susceptible animals.

For many years the most reasonable assumption was that the scrapie agent, like all other known infectious agents, contained a nucleic acid genome. Attempts to identify such a genome, however, have been unsuccessful. The agent resists ultraviolet irradiation at doses that readily inactivate many small viruses. It is not measurably inactivated by a variety of nucleases, including enzymes that degrade DNA or RNA under conditions where control nucleic acids are destroyed. It also remains infectious after treatments expected to hydrolyze or chemically modify nucleic acids. These observations do not prove that the agent lacks nucleic acid, since a small genome might be shielded by protein or lipid. Nevertheless, they have made it increasingly difficult to assign the scrapie agent to any established class of infectious particle.

The biological analysis of the scrapie agent has been hindered by the absence of a tissue culture assay and by the failure of the native agent to elicit a useful immune response. Infectivity must therefore be measured in animals. Earlier studies in sheep and goats required long incubation periods and large numbers of animals. The adaptation of scrapie to mice and, subsequently, to hamsters provided more tractable systems, but quantitative assays still depended on serial dilution, inoculation, and prolonged observation for neurological disease. The development of a hamster bioassay based on the incubation time interval has made possible more systematic purification and inactivation studies.

Biochemical characterization of the scrapie agent has also been complicated by its distribution among many subcellular fractions and by its apparent heterogeneity in size, density, and charge. Several laboratories have reported partial purification procedures, including fractionation with microsomes, density gradients, ammonium sulfate precipitation, and detergent extraction. These studies established that the agent could be enriched but did not resolve its molecular composition. In particular, they did not determine whether infectivity was associated primarily with nucleic acid, protein, lipid, carbohydrate, or some complex of these macromolecules.

We have used the hamster model to develop a purification procedure for the scrapie agent from brain, where infectious titers are high. Detergent extracts were subjected to nuclease and protease digestion, followed by additional detergent extraction, ammonium sulfate precipitation, and agarose gel electrophoresis in Sarkosyl at low temperature. The resulting preparations permitted direct tests of the chemical requirements for infectivity. In the studies described here, we examine the sensitivity of the purified and partially purified agent to proteases, protein-modifying reagents, protein denaturants, nucleases, ultraviolet irradiation, psoralens, hydroxylamine, and other treatments directed against nucleic acids. The results indicate that a protein is required for infectivity, whereas no comparable evidence implicates a nucleic acid. These findings suggest that the scrapie agent represents a novel class of infectious particle and require reconsideration of the molecular basis of replication in these slow degenerative diseases.

text:
Scrapie, a naturally occurring, transmissible, and inevitably fatal neurodegenerative disease of sheep and goats, has puzzled investigators for decades due to the unprecedented physical and chemical properties of its infectious agent. Like kuru and Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease (CJD) in humans, scrapie is characterized by a prolonged incubation period, sometimes spanning years, followed by progressive neurological dysfunction, ataxia, and characteristic spongiform vacuolation of the central nervous system. Unlike conventional viral or bacterial pathogens, the causative agent of scrapie evokes no recognizable inflammatory or robust immune response in the host, and it has consistently evaded detection by standard microscopic and molecular techniques.

Historically, efforts to isolate and characterize the scrapie agent have been severely hampered by the slow, tedious, and expensive nature of the required animal bioassays. Because no tissue culture system exists that supports the robust replication and titration of the agent, researchers have had to rely exclusively on animal inoculations. The original bioassays in sheep and goats took years to complete. The adaptation of the scrapie agent to mice in 1961 significantly advanced the field, though end-point titrations still required up to a year of observation. More recently, the transmission of the agent to the Syrian golden hamster has led to the development of a more rapid incubation-time assay, which allows for quantitative measurements in approximately 60 to 70 days, finally enabling more systematic biochemical fractionations.

Using these improved assays, substantial progress has been made toward purifying the infectious particles from hamster brain tissue. The scrapie agent has historically exhibited extreme heterogeneity in terms of its size, density, and charge, distributing widely across subcellular fractions. This heterogeneity, we demonstrate, largely arises from the profound hydrophobicity of the infectious particle, causing it to aggregate extensively with cellular membranes and proteins. By utilizing rigorous detergent extractions and sequential enzymatic digestions, we have achieved a 100- to 1000-fold purification of the agent relative to cellular protein.

In the course of these biochemical characterizations, a startling profile of the scrapie agent has emerged. Six independent lines of evidence indicate that a protein component is absolutely required for infectivity. The agent is readily inactivated by proteases like proteinase K and trypsin, as well as by protein-denaturing agents such as sodium dodecyl sulfate, chaotropic salts, phenol, and urea, and by chemical modification with diethyl pyrocarbonate. Conversely, the agent displays a remarkable, almost absolute resistance to procedures that specifically modify or destroy nucleic acids. It is impervious to digestion by a vast array of ribonucleases and deoxyribonucleases, it resists inactivation by psoralens and zinc-catalyzed hydrolysis, and it exhibits extreme resistance to ultraviolet irradiation.

The extraordinary properties of the scrapie agent—namely, its absolute reliance on a protein for infectivity and its steadfast resistance to nucleic acid-inactivating treatments—distinctly separate it from all known viruses, plasmids, and viroids. Given that the dominant characteristics of the agent reflect those of a protein rather than a nucleic acid, we propose the term "prion" to denote a small, proteinaceous infectious particle that resists inactivation by most procedures that modify nucleic acids. The existence of prions challenges the central dogma of biology, posing fundamental questions about how an infectious pathogen can replicate without a nucleic acid genome. Understanding the molecular structure and replicative mechanism of the prion is not only critical for explaining the pathogenesis of scrapie, kuru, and CJD, but it may also provide novel insights into the etiologies of other chronic, degenerative diseases of the human central nervous system.

title: Novel Proteinaceous Infectious Particles Cause Scrapie
text:
The molecular properties of the scrapie agent differ from those of viruses, viroids, and plasmids. Its resistance to procedures that attack nucleic acids, its resistance to inactivation by heat, and its apparent small size all suggest that the scrapie agent is a novel infectious entity. Because the dominant characteristics of the scrapie agent resemble those of a protein, the term "prion" is suggested. Prions are small proteinaceous infectious particles which are resistant to inactivation by most procedures that modify nucleic acids. The term "prion" underscores the requirement of a protein for infection; current knowledge does not allow exclusion of a small nucleic acid within the interior of the particle.

Our data and that of other investigators suggest two possible models for the scrapie agent: (i) a small nucleic acid surrounded by a tightly packed protein coat or (ii) a protein devoid of nucleic acid, that is, an infectious protein. While the first model might seem the most plausible, there is no evidence for a nucleic acid within the agent. The second model is consistent with the experimental data but is clearly heretical. Determination of its molecular structure will be required prior to deciding whether prions represent a distinct subgroup of extraordinarily small viruses or a completely different type of pathogen which lacks a nucleic acid genome.

The first possibility for replication is that prions contain a protected nucleic acid and that, like a viral genome, it codes for the protein shell. Alternatively, prions may contain an oligonucleotide that acts as a regulatory element instead of a coding template. If the postulated nucleic acid within the scrapie agent does not code for the protein in its coat, then this would be a major feature distinguishing prions from viruses.

The second possibility is that prions are, in fact, devoid of nucleic acid. If cellular genes coding for the scrapie prion do exist, then they are highly regulated, not readily activated, and present in various mammalian cells ranging from mice to monkeys. An occasional activation of such cellular genes might explain the sporadic occurrence of CJD. The molecular mechanism by which prions might activate cellular genes which code for their biosynthesis is unknown. Alternatively, prions could code for their own biosynthesis. Unorthodox mechanisms such as reverse translation or protein-directed protein synthesis would allow prions to replicate. We have no precedents for either of these synthetic processes in biology.

The consequences of understanding the structure, function, and replication of prions are significant. A knowledge of the molecular structure of prions may help identify the etiologies of some chronic degenerative diseases of humans. Diseases where prions might play an etiological role include Alzheimer's senile dementia, multiple sclerosis, Parkinson's disease, amyotrophic lateral sclerosis, diabetes mellitus, rheumatoid arthritis, and lupus erythematosus, as well as a variety of neoplastic disorders. The importance of prion research in the potential elucidation of a wide variety of medical illnesses underscores the need for purification of the scrapie agent to homogeneity and the subsequent identification of its macromolecular components. Only then can we determine with certainty whether or not prions are devoid of nucleic acid.

title: Novel Proteinaceous Infectious Particles Cause Scrapie
text:
The data presented here require a reassessment of the nature of the scrapie agent. A protein within the infectious particle is required for biological activity, whereas no comparable evidence has been obtained for an essential nucleic acid. Proteolytic digestion, carbethoxylation, detergents, chaotropic ions, phenol, and urea all diminish infectivity under conditions in which nucleic acids ordinarily retain, or can be recovered with, biological activity. Conversely, nucleases, zinc-catalyzed hydrolysis, hydroxylamine, psoralens, and ultraviolet irradiation at doses sufficient to modify nucleic acids have failed to inactivate the agent. These observations do not prove the absence of all nucleic acid from the infectious particle, but they place severe limits on any nucleic acid that could serve as its genome.

The resistance of the scrapie agent to many procedures that destroy conventional viruses, together with its sensitivity to protein modification, distinguishes it from known animal viruses, plant viroids, and plasmids. The term prion is proposed to emphasize this distinction. By this term we do not imply that all structural features of the agent are now understood. Rather, prion denotes a proteinaceous infectious particle in which a protein is an essential component and in which a conventional nucleic acid genome has not been demonstrated. This designation should be useful until the molecular structure of the agent has been established by direct chemical analysis.

The apparent heterogeneity of the scrapie agent may be explained, at least in part, by its hydrophobicity. Binding to phenyl-Sepharose, loss of titer after removal of detergent, and broad distributions in density and size gradients are all consistent with an infectious particle containing hydrophobic domains that promote aggregation or association with membrane constituents. Such behavior could account for previous failures to assign a single sedimentation coefficient, density, or molecular weight to the agent. The recovery of smaller forms in sulfobetaine 3-14 suggests that the infectious unit may be much smaller than many preparations indicate. If a dissociated particle of 50,000 daltons or less retains infectivity, then the chemical nature of the agent is more unusual than estimates based on aggregated material have suggested.

Several qualifications are necessary. The purification of the scrapie agent is incomplete, and infectivity must still be measured by animal bioassay. Since a highly sensitive in vitro assay is not available, small losses of activity and contaminating host molecules remain difficult to evaluate. Alkali inactivation also deserves further study, for it could reflect modification of either protein or an unidentified nonprotein component. Nevertheless, the concordance of many independent experiments argues against the possibility that the observed effects are artifacts of one reagent, one preparation, or one assay.

The existence of an infectious particle with these properties has important implications for slow degenerative diseases of the central nervous system. Scrapie, kuru, and Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease share long incubation periods and neuropathological features, yet their agents have resisted classification by conventional microbiological criteria. A proteinaceous infectious particle could explain their unusual stability, apparent lack of antigenicity, and failure to yield readily to methods devised for viruses. It also raises new questions concerning replication. The prion may carry information through the conformation, covalent modification, or ordered association of a protein, or it may act upon a host molecule to generate additional infectious material. Elucidating these mechanisms will require more highly purified preparations, identification of the essential protein, and determination of whether host or agent sequences contribute to infectivity. Such studies should clarify not only scrapie but also the pathogenesis of related human dementias.

title: Novel Proteinaceous Infectious Particles Cause Scrapie
text:
The discovery that the scrapie agent is resistant to procedures that alter nucleic acids but susceptible to those that denature proteins marks a profound departure from the accepted paradigms of infectious disease. Our results indicate that this agent depends upon a protein for its infectivity, yet lacks a nucleic acid genome that can be inactivated by standard methodologies such as nuclease digestion, ultraviolet irradiation, or exposure to alkali. Because these novel properties cleanly differentiate the scrapie agent from viruses, plasmids, and viroids, we introduce the term "prion" to denote a small, proteinaceous infectious particle that resists inactivation by most procedures known to modify nucleic acids.

The failure to identify a nucleic acid genome within the prion raises profound questions regarding its replication and transmission. If prions indeed lack nucleic acid, they must replicate through mechanisms fundamentally distinct from all known infectious agents. Several theoretical models might account for this replication. A host gene might encode the prion protein, and the infectious prion could simply induce its own expression or trigger a conformational change in an already existing host precursor. Alternatively, a small, yet undiscovered nucleic acid might be intimately bound and protected by the prion protein, though our exhaustive physical and chemical treatments argue against this unless the protection is extraordinarily robust. Another possibility is that the prion represents an abnormal, self-replicating structural form of a normal cellular protein.

The marked hydrophobicity of the prion protein, as evidenced by its requirement for detergents during purification and its behavior in gel electrophoresis and density gradients, suggests it interacts extensively with cellular membranes. This property likely accounts for the significant heterogeneity in the agent's size, which has historically confounded purification efforts. By recognizing and mitigating this hydrophobicity, we have achieved a substantial purification, allowing for more precise biochemical characterization. The smallest infectious form of the prion appears to have a molecular weight of 50,000 or less, further distinguishing it from conventional viruses.

These findings also carry significant implications for our understanding of human neurodegenerative diseases. The striking neuropathological and clinical similarities between experimental Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease (CJD) in goats and natural scrapie, along with the parallel epidemiological enigma of kuru, strongly suggest these diseases share a common etiology rooted in prion infection. The experimental transmission of scrapie via cannibalism in hamsters, albeit inefficient, directly mirrors the proposed spread of kuru among the Fore people of New Guinea. The unique properties of prions might also shed light on the pathogenesis of other degenerative disorders, such as Alzheimer's disease, where conventional viral etiologies have eluded detection.

In conclusion, the identification of the prion as a fundamentally new class of infectious agent demands a reevaluation of our basic concepts of infectious disease. Future research must inevitably focus on defining the primary structure of the prion protein, elucidating its mechanism of replication, and investigating its potential role in a broader spectrum of degenerative neurological diseases. The resilience and uniqueness of this agent promise to uncover new principles of macromolecular biology and pathogenesis.

paper_title: On the Topography of the Genetic Fine Structure - 1961 - PNAS
display_title: On the Topography of the Genetic Fine Structure
title: On the Topography of the Genetic Fine Structure
text: A small portion of the genetic map of phage T4, the two cistrons of the rII region, has been dissected by overlapping "deletions" into 47 segments. If any branch exists, it cannot be larger than one of these segments. The overlapping deletions are used to map point mutations and the map order established by this method is consistent with the order established by the conventional method that makes use of recombination frequencies. Further dissection has led to the identification of 308 distinct sites of widely varied spontaneous and induced mutability. The distributions throughout the region for spontaneous mutations and those induced by various chemical mutagens are compared. Data are included for nitrous acid and ethyl methane sulfonate acting in vitro, and 2-aminopurine, 2,6-diaminopurine, 5-bromouracil, 5-bromodeoxycytidine, and proflavine acting in vivo. The characteristic hotspots reveal a striking topography.
historical_context: In 1961, Seymour Benzer published his masterful work detailing the ultra-fine mapping of the *rII* region of bacteriophage T4. Exploiting a brilliantly streamlined viral genetics system, Benzer mapped thousands of mutations with a resolution never before seen, successfully drilling genetic mapping down to the scale of individual nucleotide base pairs. He conclusively proved that the gene is a linear array of mutable sites. Benzer's work was revolutionary because it permanently bridged the conceptual divide between abstract Mendelian genetics and physical DNA molecular biology. By coining the concepts of the cistron, muton, and recon, Benzer established that genes are not indivisible beads on a string, but highly complex stretches of DNA capable of internal mutation and recombination.

paper_title: On the Topography of the Genetic Fine Structure - 1961 - PNAS

title: On the Topography of the Genetic Fine Structure
text: The fine structure of the rII region of phage T4 has been examined with respect to its topography, that is, the local differences in mutability among its parts. By means of overlapping deletions, thousands of reverting mutants have been assigned by qualitative recombination tests to a linear series of 47 segments, and distinct mutable sites have then been recognized within these segments. Spontaneous mutations are distributed in a markedly non-random fashion. A few sites recur with very high frequency, whereas many others are represented only once or remain undetected; the observed 1,612 spontaneous mutants define 251 sites and imply many additional sites. Mutations induced by several chemical agents reveal further sites and characteristic spectra, bringing the total mapped to over 300. The results show that genetic sites differ widely in spontaneous mutability and in response to mutagens, and suggest a highly differentiated nucleotide organization within the cistron.

text: This paper builds upon previous topological studies of the rII region in bacteriophage T4 to investigate its genetic topography—specifically, the distribution and mutability of distinct genetic sites within this locus. Using a rapid, qualitative mapping technique based on overlapping deletions, we mapped thousands of independent spontaneous and induced rII mutants. Our analysis reveals a highly non-random distribution of mutations, characterized by distinct "hotspots" of high mutability alongside numerous sites with low or singular occurrences. Furthermore, different mutagens produce unique mutational spectra, indicating distinct chemical specificities at the nucleotide level. Despite identifying over 300 distinct mutable sites, statistical estimates suggest many more remain undiscovered, implying a high degree of complexity within the cistron. These findings demonstrate that the genetic fine structure is topographically differentiated, likely reflecting the underlying nucleotide sequence and its local interactions, and pave the way for understanding mutation at the molecular level.

paper_title: On the Topography of the Genetic Fine Structure - 1961 - PNAS
display_title: On the Topography of the Genetic Fine Structure
title: On the Topography of the Genetic Fine Structure
text:
In an earlier paper, a detailed examination was made of the structure of a small portion of the genetic map of phage T4, the rII region. This region, which controls the ability of the phage to grow in Escherichia coli strain K, consists of two adjacent cistrons, or functional units. Various rII mutants, unable to grow in strain K, have mutations affecting various parts of either or both of these cistrons. The topology of the region; i.e., the manner in which its parts are interconnected, was intensively tested and it was found that the active structure can be described as a string of subelements, a mutation constituting an alteration of a point or segment of the linear array.

This paper is a sequel in which inquiry is made into the topography of the structure, i.e., local differences in the properties of its parts. Specifically, are all the subelements equally mutable? If so, mutations should occur at random throughout the structure and the topography would be trivial. On the other hand, sites or regions of unusually high or low mutability would be interesting topographic features.

The preceding investigation of topology was done by choosing mutants showing no detectable tendency to revert. This avoided any possible confusion between recombination and reverse mutation, so that a qualitative (yes-or-no) test for recombination was possible. The class of non-reverting mutants automatically included those marked by relatively large alterations, which will be referred to as "deletions." Such a mutant is defined for the present purposes as one which intersects (fails to give recombination with) two or more mutants that do recombine with each other. Deletions provided overlaps of the sort needed to test the topology and to divide the map into segments.

The present investigation of topography, however, is concerned with differentiation of the various points in the structure. For this purpose mutants which do revert are of the greater interest, since they are most likely to contain small alterations. As a rule (there are exceptions) an rII mutant that reverts behaves as if its alteration were localized to a point. That is to say, mutants that intersect with the same mutant also intersect with each other. In a cross, recombination can be scored only if it is clearly detectable above the spontaneous reversion noise of the mutants involved. Therefore, the precision with which a mutation can be mapped is limited by its reversion rate. The detailed analysis of topography can best be done with mutants having low, non-zero reversion rates.

Some thousands of such rII mutants, both spontaneous and induced, have been analyzed and the resultant topographic map is presented here.

Exploiting a brilliantly streamlined viral genetics system, Benzer mapped thousands of mutations with a resolution never before seen, successfully drilling genetic mapping down to the scale of individual nucleotide base pairs. He conclusively proved that the gene is a linear array of mutable sites. Benzer's work was revolutionary because it permanently bridged the conceptual divide between abstract Mendelian genetics and physical DNA molecular biology. By coining the concepts of the cistron, muton, and recon, Benzer established that genes are not indivisible beads on a string, but highly complex stretches of DNA capable of internal mutation and recombination.

paper_title: On the Topography of the Genetic Fine Structure - 1961 - PNAS
display_title: On the Topography of the Genetic Fine Structure
title: On the Topography of the Genetic Fine Structure
text:
The genetic map, as ordinarily drawn, is a line upon which mutations are placed in an order and at distances proportional to recombination frequencies. Such a representation has been highly useful, but it leaves open two questions about the fine structure of the genetic material. First, is the order really linear when examined at the smallest intervals now accessible? Second, when many mutations have been placed upon such a line, are the mutable points distributed as if they were equivalent, or does the genetic material have a characteristic local topography?

The rII region of phage T4 offers a favorable object for this inquiry. Mutants of this region grow readily on one bacterial strain, E. coli B, but fail to grow on another, E. coli K. Thus the occurrence of a standard type recombinant in a cross between two rII mutants can be detected with great sensitivity by a simple plating test. Since very large numbers of mutants can be isolated and compared, the region can be examined more closely than has been possible for most genetic systems.

In a previous study the rII region was analyzed with the aid of mutations that behave genetically as deletions. These deletions, by overlapping one another, make it possible to divide the region into consecutive intervals and to assign point mutations to them by qualitative recombination tests. A deletion which covers the point mutation gives no standard type recombinants; one which does not cover it gives a positive result. In this way much of the labor of making quantitative crosses among all possible pairs of point mutants is avoided. The same material also provides a test of the topology of the region, since certain patterns of overlap would be expected if branches or other departures from a line existed.

The present work extends that analysis in two directions. First, by using additional deletions, the rII region has been divided into a larger number of small segments, permitting a closer test of linearity and a more precise placement of point mutations. Second, many independently arising mutations, both spontaneous and induced, have been mapped within these segments and compared with one another. The induced mutations include those produced by several agents acting in the host cell and by others acting directly upon extracellular phage particles.

The resulting map is therefore not merely a topology, in the sense of an order of points, but a topography, in the sense of a landscape of mutable sites. Some sites recur with extraordinary frequency, while others are found only once or not at all in the present collection. Different mutagens reveal different patterns of favored sites. These differences provide a genetic approach to the chemical individuality of positions within a cistron.

paper_title: On the Topography of the Genetic Fine Structure - 1961 - PNAS
display_title: On the Topography of the Genetic Fine Structure
title: On the Topography of the Genetic Fine Structure
text:
The structural and functional organization of genetic material at the molecular level represents one of the central problems in modern biology. While classical genetic analysis successfully established that genes are arranged in a linear sequence along the chromosome, the limit of resolution achieved in higher organisms was insufficient to probe the internal structure of the gene itself. To determine whether the gene is indivisible or composed of a sequence of smaller, mutable elements, one requires a genetic system capable of resolving rare recombination events between extremely closely linked mutations. The rII region of the bacteriophage T4 provides an ideal system for such ultra-fine-scale mapping. Mutations within the rII region prevent the phage from producing progeny in Escherichia coli strain K12(lambda), although they grow normally on E. coli strain B. Because even a single recombinant wild-type particle can be easily detected among millions of mutant progeny by plating on strain K, the resolution of this system is high enough to theoretically separate mutations located at adjacent nucleotide pairs in the DNA molecule.

In a previous study, we demonstrated that the genetic map of the rII region is completely linear and that the functional unit, or cistron, can be genetically dissected into numerous distinct, mutable sites. We introduced a rapid method for topological mapping using a set of well-characterized, overlapping deletion mutations. This method allows any newly isolated point mutation to be unambiguously assigned to a specific segment of the map simply by conducting qualitative spot tests to observe the presence or absence of recombination with the reference deletions, thus circumventing the need for exhaustive, quantitative recombination frequency measurements.

In the present investigation, we have expanded this set of overlapping deletions to divide the rII region into 47 distinct segments. We utilize this high-resolution map to determine the exact topographical distribution of hundreds of spontaneous and induced mutations. Our analysis identifies 308 distinct mutable sites within the two cistrons of the rII region. We find that the distribution of mutations across these sites is highly non-random, with certain specific locations ("hotspots") mutating at frequencies orders of magnitude higher than others. Furthermore, by comparing the spectra of spontaneous mutations to those induced by various chemical mutagens, both in vivo and in vitro, we reveal that each mutagen produces a unique and characteristic topographical fingerprint. These data provide direct evidence that the mutable sites within a gene are chemically heterogeneous and suggest that the sequence of nucleotide base pairs profoundly influences local mutability, offering a critical step toward ultimately translating the genetic map into a specific chemical sequence.

He conclusively proved that the gene is a linear array of mutable sites. Benzer's work was revolutionary because it permanently bridged the conceptual divide between abstract Mendelian genetics and physical DNA molecular biology. By coining the concepts of the cistron, muton, and recon, Benzer established that genes are not indivisible beads on a string, but highly complex stretches of DNA capable of internal mutation and recombination.

paper_title: On the Topography of the Genetic Fine Structure - 1961 - PNAS
display_title: On the Topography of the Genetic Fine Structure
title: On the Topography of the Genetic Fine Structure
text:
One topographic feature, non-random mutability at the various sites, is obvious. Another question is whether mutable sites are distributed at random, or whether there exist portions of the map that are unusually crowded with or devoid of sites. The mapping technique used here defines only the order of sites from one segment to another (but not within a given segment). The distance between sites remains unspecified. However, all mutations in a segment more distal to a given point must be farther away than those in a more proximal segment. If the number of sites in a segment is used as a measure of its length, it can be seen that there is no major discrepancy between these distances and those defined in terms of another measure of distance, recombination frequency. On a gross scale, therefore, there is no evidence for any large portion of the rII region that is unusually crowded or roomy with respect to sites. This does not necessarily mean that some other measure of distance would not reveal such regions, since it is at least conceivable that mutable sites coincide with points highly susceptible to recombination. The distribution of sites on a finer scale, within a small segment, remains to be investigated.

The number of points at which mutations can wreck the activity of a cistron is very large. This would be expected if a cistron dictates the formation of a polypeptide chain and "nonsense" mutations are possible which interrupt the completion of the chain. Such mutations would be effective at any point of the structure, whereas ones which lead to "missense," i.e., the substitution of one amino acid for another, might be effective at relatively special points or regions which are crucial in affecting the active site or folding.

It would be of interest to compare the number of genetic sites to the material embodiment of the rII region in terms of nucleotides. Unfortunately, the size of the latter is not well known. Estimates based upon its length, in units of recombination frequency compared to the length of the entire genetic structure, are uncertain. A more direct attempt has been made using equilibrium sedimentation in a cesium chloride gradient and looking for a change in density of mutants known by genetic evidence to have portions of the rII region deleted (Nomura, Champe, and Benzer). This technique has been successful in characterizing defective mutants of phage lambda and is sufficiently sensitive to detect a decrease of 1 per cent in the amount of DNA per phage particle, but has so far failed with rII mutants. Although other explanations are possible, this result may suggest that the physical structure corresponding to the rII region represents less than 1 per cent of the total DNA of the phage particle, or less than 2,000 nucleotide pairs. If this is so, the number of possible sites would be of the order of at least one-fifth of the number of nucleotide pairs.

The data show that, if each site is characterized by its spontaneous mutability and response to various mutagens, the sites are of many different kinds. Some response patterns are represented only once in the entire structure. According to the Watson-Crick model for DNA, the structure consists of only two types of elements, adenine-thymine (AT) pairs and guanine-hydroxymethylcytosine (GC) pairs. This does not mean, however, that there can only be two kinds of mutable sites, even if a site corresponds to a single base pair. Considering only base pair substitutions, a given AT pair can undergo three kinds of change: AT can be replaced by GC, CG, or TA. Certain of these changes may lead to a mutant phenotype, but some may not. The frequency of observable mutations at a particular AT pair will be determined by the sum of the probabilities for each type of change, each multiplied by a coefficient (either one or zero) according to whether that specific alteration at that particular pair does or does not represent a mutant type. Thus, if the probability that a base pair will be substituted is independent of its neighbors, the various AT sites may have seven different mutation rates. Similarly, there are seven rates possible for the various GC sites, so that it would be possible to account for fourteen classes by this mechanism. Some of these may have spontaneous mutation rates that are similar. If a mutagen induces only certain substitutions, it will facilitate further discriminations between sites but there should still be no more than fourteen classes.

If one allows for interactions between neighbors, the number of possible classes increases enormously. Such interactions are to be expected. As an example, consider the fact that AT pairs are held together much less strongly than are GC pairs. If several AT pairs occur in succession, this segment of the DNA chain will be relatively loose, making it easier to consummate an illicit base pairing during replication. Thus, guanine and adenine, which make a very satisfactory pair of hydrogen bonds but require a larger than normal separation between the backbones, could be more readily accommodated. This would lead, in the next replication, to a replica in which one of the AT pairs has been substituted by a CG pair, with the orientation of purine and pyrimidine reversed. Thus, a region rich in AT pairs will tend to be more subject to substitution. If the same (standard-type) phenotype can be achieved by alternative sequences, the ones containing long stretches of AT pairs would tend to be lost because of their high mutability. In other words, cistrons ought to have evolved in such a way as to eliminate hotspots. The spontaneous hotspots that are observed would be remnants of an incomplete ironing-out process. In fact, a map of the rII region of the related phage T6 also shows hotspots at locations corresponding to r131 and r117. However, while the first of these has a mutability similar to that in T4, the second is lower by a factor of four.

This point is emphasized by the data on reverse mutations. It is not uncommon for an rII mutant to have a reverse mutation rate that is greater than the total forward rate observed for the composite of at least 400 sites. That some of these high-rate reverse mutations represent true reversion (and not "suppressor" mutations) has been established in several cases by the most stringent criteria, including the demonstration that the revertant has exactly the same forward mutation rate at the same site as did the original standard type. It would therefore appear that certain kinds of highly mutable configurations are systematically excluded from the standard form of the rII genetic structure, and a mutation may recreate one of these banned sequences.

In the attempt to translate the genetic map into a nucleotide sequence, the detection of the various sites by forward mutation is necessarily the first step. By studies on the specificity of induction of reverse mutations, one site at a time can be analyzed in the hope of identifying the specific bases involved.
