paper_title: On the Topography of the Genetic Fine Structure - 1961 - PNAS
display_title: On the Topography of the Genetic Fine Structure
title: On the Topography of the Genetic Fine Structure
text:
The results described here show that a genetic structure which is topologically simple may nevertheless possess a highly differentiated topography. The rII region behaves, for the purposes of these tests, as a linear array of sites. Deletions provide overlapping landmarks by which the array can be divided into small segments, and point mutations can then be assigned to these segments by qualitative recombination tests. Within the limits imposed by reversion noise and by the finite size of the segments, no evidence has been obtained for a branch. Thus, the same material which previously served to test the topology now provides a framework in which local differences in mutability can be examined.

The agreement between the order obtained by deletion mapping and that obtained by recombination frequencies is important in this connection. The deletion method does not measure distance in the ordinary genetic sense; it asks only whether two lesions overlap. Nevertheless, the order which it gives is the same as that given by quantitative crossing. This agreement justifies the use of the deletion map as a scaffold for the topographic analysis. It also emphasizes that, for many purposes, a qualitative recombination test can be more powerful than a quantitative one, provided that suitable overlapping deficiencies are available. A large number of mutants can then be placed on the map without making all possible crosses.

The most striking fact about the distribution of spontaneous mutations is its non-randomness. If all mutable sites in the rII region had the same probability of alteration, the repeated recovery of some sites hundreds of times and the failure to recover many others would be very improbable. The data instead require the existence of sites of very different spontaneous mutability. Some sites are conspicuously hot, some are represented only once or twice, and many must still be unobserved. The estimate of undiscovered sites is necessarily conservative, since it assumes that the least frequently observed sites form a uniform low-mutability class. If there are sites still colder than these, as there is no reason to exclude, then the true number of possible sites is larger and the present map is less saturated than the numerical estimate suggests.

It follows that a genetic map of this kind cannot be regarded simply as a list of equally probable positions at which mutation may occur. The apparent frequency with which a site is found depends on at least two things: the existence of a possible alteration which produces the rII phenotype and the probability that such an alteration will occur under the conditions of origin. The present experiments do not distinguish fully between these two factors. A site absent from the collection may be unable to give a detectable rII mutant by a small change, or it may be capable of doing so but only with very low probability. Similarly, a hot site may be hot because the corresponding chemical change is especially favored, because the altered product is readily detected as rII, or because both circumstances apply. The topographic map is therefore a map of observed mutability in the genetic test system, not yet a direct map of chemical structure.

The use of mutagens sharpens this conclusion. Each mutagen produces a spectrum which differs from the spontaneous one and from the spectra of the other mutagens. Certain sites respond strongly to one treatment and weakly or not at all to another. Other sites are common to several spectra. This behavior is difficult to reconcile with a view in which mutation is a uniform accidental break in an otherwise featureless line. It is more naturally interpreted as evidence that different positions in the genetic structure present different chemical opportunities for alteration. The same genetic site may be accessible to more than one chemical process, whereas another site may be characteristic of a single process. The map thus becomes not merely a positional map, but a record of chemical specificity expressed through genetic behavior.

At the same time, the comparison of spectra must be made cautiously. None of the induced collections is saturated. Each contains some spontaneous background. Differences between two spectra at a site represented by only a few mutants may therefore arise by chance. Conversely, the absence of a site from one collection cannot be taken as proof that the corresponding mutagen is unable to produce that alteration. The strongest conclusions are those based on repeated occurrences, especially the distinct hotspots. These show that some local features of the rII region are recognized, in effect, by particular mutagens with high efficiency.

The addition of induced mutants has increased the number of known sites, but has not exhausted the region. This is not paradoxical. A mutagen may uncover sites whose spontaneous mutability is so low that they would scarcely appear in a spontaneous collection of this size, while still leaving many other possible sites unseen. Thus, increasing the variety of mutational agents changes the visible topography as well as increasing the total count of sites. The estimate that at least 120 further sites remain to be found after combining all the material should therefore be understood as a lower limit for the class of sites detectable by the present methods and present mutagens. The complete fine structure is almost certainly richer than the catalog now available.

Several limitations of the analysis should also be kept in mind. A reverting mutant is ordinarily treated as a point mutant, and this is a useful rule for the rII region. But exceptions occur. A small deletion or other extended alteration may revert and may overlap several true point mutations. In such cases, mutations grouped as repeats of a single site may later be divided. For this reason the identification of different sites is a firm conclusion when recombination is observed, whereas the identification of identical sites remains provisional when recombination is not observed. This asymmetry is inherent in the method and is especially important in the most crowded segments.

Another limitation arises from the threshold of detection. A negative spot test means that recombination, if present, is below the level observable in the assay. It does not prove that no recombination of any kind can occur. Thus, the term "site" as used here has an operational meaning. Two mutations at the same site are those which have not been separated by the present test; two mutations at different sites are those which have been separated. As methods are refined, some sites may be subdivided. The topographic features described here are therefore features at the resolution of the present recombination system.

Even with these qualifications, the main result is clear. The rII region contains a large number of distinguishable mutable sites arranged in a linear order, and these sites differ greatly in their tendency to mutate spontaneously and in their responsiveness to particular mutagens. This conclusion brings the genetic analysis close to chemical specificity. The genetic map no longer represents only the order of functional elements; it begins to reveal differences among the elements themselves. Such differences may reflect the sequence of chemical units in the genetic material, the local environment in which they reside, or the consequences of particular changes for the function of the rII cistrons.

The present work therefore extends fine-structure mapping from topology to topography. Topology asks how the parts are connected; topography asks how the parts differ. The answer obtained for the rII region is that the structure is linear to a high degree of resolution, but far from uniform. It has landmarks of mutability, some general and some agent-specific. Continued mapping with larger collections and additional mutagens should reveal still more sites and may eventually permit the genetic positions to be correlated with particular chemical changes. In that case, the pattern of hotspots and cold spots described here will provide a bridge between the formal genetic map and the molecular constitution of the genetic material.

paper_title: On the Topography of the Genetic Fine Structure - 1961 - PNAS
display_title: On the Topography of the Genetic Fine Structure
title: On the Topography of the Genetic Fine Structure
text:
The analysis of the rII region of bacteriophage T4 presented here provides a detailed, high-resolution map of genetic topography. By exploiting the method of overlapping deletions, we have bypassed the labor-intensive requirement of relying solely on recombination frequencies to order mutational sites, allowing for the rapid and definitive assignment of hundreds of independent mutations. This technique has effectively dissected the rII cistrons into 47 distinct segments, creating a robust framework for ordering point mutations.

Our investigation unequivocally demonstrates that the genetic structure is not uniformly mutable. The distribution of spontaneous mutations across the rII region is decidedly non-random, revealing a striking topography characterized by prominent "hotspots" and numerous "cold" sites. At the most mutable spontaneous hotspot, over 500 independent mutations have been isolated, whereas many sites have been hit only once or not at all in our sample. This extreme variation in mutability at different sites strongly implies that the probability of a mutation arising at a specific point is profoundly influenced by local structural or chemical factors within the genetic material.

When analyzing the distribution of induced mutations, it becomes evident that each mutagen produces a unique topographic fingerprint that differs significantly from the spontaneous spectrum. Mutagens such as 5-bromouracil, proflavine, 2-aminopurine, and nitrous acid each exhibit specific affinities for particular sites or regions, creating distinct patterns of hotspots. For instance, sites that are highly mutable spontaneously may remain untouched by a specific chemical mutagen, while other sites, completely quiet in the spontaneous background, emerge as major hotspots under chemical induction. This specificity must reflect the underlying molecular interactions between the mutagen and the nucleotide sequence of the DNA at or near the mutable site.

The varying specificities of these mutagens likely stem from the nature of the chemical alterations they induce. Base analogues such as 5-bromouracil and 2-aminopurine are thought to induce transition mutations by pairing erroneously during DNA replication. In contrast, proflavine, an intercalating agent, is hypothesized to cause the insertion or deletion of single base pairs. The distinct mutational spectra generated by these differing mechanisms support the notion that the specific nucleotide sequence surrounding a mutable site plays a critical role in its susceptibility to specific types of mutational events. The topography of the genetic fine structure, therefore, is not merely a map of mutable points, but a reflection of the intricate chemical microenvironment along the DNA molecule.

Despite identifying 308 distinct sites of mutation within the rII region, statistical estimations suggest that our map is not yet saturated. Based on the distribution of occurrences at the least mutable sites, we project the existence of at least 120 additional sites that have not yet been observed. This calculation brings the maximum degree of saturation to approximately 72 percent. It is highly probable that there exists a substantial class of sites with exceedingly low mutabilities, both spontaneous and induced, which would require the screening of vastly larger numbers of mutants to detect. The current map, while highly detailed, thus represents a major fraction, but not the entirety, of the mutable positions within this genetic segment.

The finding that different mutagens act at different sites is particularly valuable for expanding the map. Because each mutagen samples the genetic structure with a different bias, utilizing a wide array of mutagens increases the likelihood of discovering new sites that might be intrinsically resistant to spontaneous mutation or to the action of other chemicals. The 53 new sites added exclusively through the study of induced mutations underscore the importance of this comprehensive approach.

The non-random distribution of mutations and the specific action of mutagens provide compelling insights into the physical nature of the genetic material. The classical view of the gene as a homogeneous, indivisible unit of mutation is fundamentally incompatible with these findings. Instead, the gene must be viewed as a heterogeneous sequence of subelements—nucleotide pairs—each possessing its own inherent probability of undergoing spontaneous alteration or reacting with a specific mutagen. The extreme hotspots, which mutate at rates orders of magnitude higher than the average site, may represent specific nucleotide configurations that are particularly unstable or uniquely susceptible to errors during replication.

Furthermore, the linear topology of the rII region, as confirmed by the perfect correlation between the map derived from overlapping deletions and that derived from recombination frequencies, supports the Watson-Crick model of DNA as a linear sequence of base pairs. Our data provide no evidence for branching within the genetic structure at a scale larger than any of our defined segments. The absence of complex topological anomalies simplifies the interpretation of genetic mapping data and reinforces the concept of a one-dimensional genetic code.

The operational definition of the cistron as a functional unit is further validated by this fine-structure map. The boundary between the A and B cistrons of the rII region is sharply defined, yet the mutational topography continues continuously across it. This suggests that while functional boundaries exist that limit genetic complementation, the physical structure of the DNA is contiguous, with mutational behavior governed by local sequence context rather than broader functional divisions. The mapping of hundreds of sites within each cistron illustrates the immense informational capacity of these functional units and highlights the complexity of the genetic material required to encode even a single phenotypic trait.

It is also worth considering the implications of our methodology. The use of overlapping deletions to bin mutations into progressively smaller segments represents a significant advance in mapping efficiency. By transforming a quantitative problem—measuring low-frequency recombination—into a series of qualitative yes/no tests, we have constructed a map of unprecedented detail. This approach circumvents the noise and variability inherent in measuring very rare recombination events, relying instead on the unambiguous outcome of whether recombination can occur at all. The success of this method in mapping the T4 rII region suggests it could be widely applicable to the high-resolution mapping of other genetic systems, provided suitable deletion mutants can be isolated.

The techniques and concepts developed in this study establish a foundation for the ultimate goal of decoding the genetic fine structure at the molecular level. By correlating the genetic map with the chemical structure of the DNA, it should eventually be possible to determine the exact nucleotide sequence of the rII region and the specific base changes responsible for each mutation. The distinct mutational spectra of various chemical mutagens, acting as specific chemical probes, will be indispensable in this endeavor. Such a correlation would bridge the gap between abstract genetic loci and physical molecules, fulfilling a major objective of molecular genetics.

In summary, the topographic mapping of the rII region reveals a highly differentiated genetic structure characterized by non-random mutability and specific responses to chemical mutagens. The existence of mutational hotspots and the varying specificities of different mutagens point to the influence of local nucleotide sequences on genetic stability. While the current map identifies over 300 mutable sites, a complete description of the genetic fine structure will require the discovery of the remaining sites and, ultimately, the correlation of this genetic topography with the physical chemistry of the DNA molecule. The precision and scalability of the overlapping deletion method have proven essential for navigating this complex genetic terrain, offering a powerful tool for the continued dissection of the gene.

paper_title: Optogenetic Stimulation of a Hippocampal Engram Activates Fear Memory Recall - 2012 - Nature
display_title: Optogenetic Stimulation of a Hippocampal Engram Activates Fear Memory Recall
title: Optogenetic Stimulation of a Hippocampal Engram Activates Fear Memory Recall
text: A specific memory is thought to be encoded by a sparse population of neurons. These neurons can be tagged during learning for subsequent identification and manipulation. Moreover, their ablation or inactivation results in reduced memory expression, suggesting their necessity in mnemonic processes. However, the question of sufficiency remains: it is unclear whether it is possible to elicit the behavioural output of a specific memory by directly activating a population of neurons that was active during learning. Here we show in mice that optogenetic reactivation of hippocampal neurons activated during fear conditioning is sufficient to induce freezing behaviour. We labelled a population of hippocampal dentate gyrus neurons activated during fear learning with channelrhodopsin-2 (ChR2) and later optically reactivated these neurons in a different context. The mice showed increased freezing only upon light stimulation, indicating light-induced fear memory recall. This freezing was not detected in non-fear-conditioned mice expressing ChR2 in a similar proportion of cells, nor in fear-conditioned mice with cells labelled by enhanced yellow fluorescent protein instead of ChR2. Finally, activation of cells labelled in a context not associated with fear did not evoke freezing in mice that were previously fear-conditioned in a different context, suggesting that light-induced fear memory recall is context-specific. Together, our findings indicate that activating a sparse but specific ensemble of hippocampal neurons that contribute to a memory engram is sufficient for the recall of that memory. Moreover, our experimental approach offers a general method of mapping cellular populations bearing memory engrams.
historical_context: In 2012, Xu Liu, Steve Ramirez, Susumu Tonegawa, and colleagues deployed optogenetics to accomplish something previously relegated to science fiction: the direct, artificial activation of a specific memory. They genetically tagged a distinct subset of hippocampal neurons (the "engram") that were active precisely during the formation of a fear memory. Later, by simply pulsing blue light onto those specific tagged neurons, they artificially triggered the behavioral recall of that fear memory in an entirely different environment. It was a historical milestone in behavioral neuroscience. It delivered definitive, causal proof of the physical manifestation of memory—the engram—proving that memories are housed in specific neural ensembles and that directly firing those ensembles is sufficient to induce memory retrieval.

paper_title: Optogenetic Stimulation of a Hippocampal Engram Activates Fear Memory Recall - 2012 - Nature
display_title: Optogenetic Stimulation of a Hippocampal Engram Activates Fear Memory Recall
title: Optogenetic Stimulation of a Hippocampal Engram Activates Fear Memory Recall
text: The formation and storage of a specific memory is thought to involve defined populations of neurons that constitute a cellular memory engram. Previous studies have shown that selective ablation or inhibition of neurons activated during learning can impair subsequent memory expression, indicating that these cells are necessary for behavioural recall. However, a critical test of the engram hypothesis is whether direct activation of such cells is sufficient to induce the associated behavioural output. Here we used c-fos-tTA transgenic mice combined with a doxycycline-regulated AAV9-TRE-ChR2-EYFP system to label dentate gyrus neurons that were active during contextual fear conditioning and to reactivate them later with light. Activity-dependent labelling was sparse, restricted largely to excitatory dentate gyrus granule cells, and recapitulated endogenous c-Fos induction during training. Optical stimulation of the labelled population in a neutral context induced robust freezing after fear conditioning, whereas stimulation of similarly labelled cells from mice exposed to the context without shock, or of EYFP-expressing control cells, did not. Reducing background labelling or stimulating bilaterally increased the magnitude of light-evoked freezing. Moreover, dentate gyrus cell populations recruited by different contexts showed only chance overlap, and optical activation of cells labelled in a non-conditioned context failed to evoke fear memory recall. These results demonstrate that reactivation of a hippocampal neuronal population recruited during learning is sufficient to elicit the behavioural expression of a fear memory, and support the idea that sparse dentate gyrus ensembles form contextual components of memory engrams.

paper_title: Optogenetic Stimulation of a Hippocampal Engram Activates Fear Memory Recall - 2012 - Nature
display_title: Optogenetic Stimulation of a Hippocampal Engram Activates Fear Memory Recall
title: Optogenetic Stimulation of a Hippocampal Engram Activates Fear Memory Recall
text: A specific memory is thought to be encoded by a sparse population of neurons whose coordinated activity defines a cellular memory engram. Selective ablation or inhibition of such populations has been shown to disrupt memory expression, indicating that these cells are necessary for memory recall. However, the demonstration that a defined neuronal population constitutes the cellular basis of a particular memory requires a mimicry experiment, in which direct reactivation of that population is itself sufficient to elicit the associated behavioural output. Here we test this prediction in the dentate gyrus (DG) of the hippocampus, a region implicated in the formation of contextual memory representations. We used c-fos-tTA transgenic mice in combination with an AAV9-TRE-ChR2-EYFP virus to label, in an activity-dependent and doxycycline-regulated manner, the sparse population of DG granule cells that were active during contextual fear conditioning. In vivo recordings confirmed that these cells could be reliably driven by blue light pulses delivered through an implanted optical fibre, and that optical stimulation also engaged downstream CA3 neurons. When mice that had been fear conditioned in one context were placed in a distinct, neutral context, optical reactivation of the labelled DG ensemble was sufficient to evoke freezing behaviour, whereas mice receiving no shock during training, mice expressing EYFP alone, or mice in which the labelled cells were active in an unrelated context did not freeze. Bilateral stimulation produced freezing approaching that elicited by the conditioned context itself. These findings demonstrate that a sparse population of DG cells active during learning forms a contextual memory engram whose direct reactivation is sufficient for fear memory recall.

text: A fundamental question in neuroscience is whether a defined population of neurons, or a memory engram, is sufficient to elicit the recall of a specific memory. Our findings provide direct causal evidence for the existence of cellular memory engrams and offer a powerful approach to map the complex neural circuits underlying memory formation and retrieval.

text:
An important question in neuroscience is how a distinct memory is formed and stored in the brain. Recent studies indicate that defined populations of neurons correspond to a specific memory trace, suggesting a cellular correlate of a memory engram. Selective ablation or inhibition of such neuronal populations erased the fear memory response, indicating that these cells are necessary for fear memory expression. However, to prove that a cell population is the cellular basis of a specific fear memory engram it is necessary to conduct a mimicry experiment to show that direct activation of such a population is sufficient for inducing the associated behavioural output.

The hippocampus is thought to be critical in the formation of the contextual component of fear memories. Modelling and experimental studies have demonstrated an essential role of the dentate gyrus (DG) of the hippocampus in discriminating between similar contexts. Cellular studies of immediate early gene expression showed that sparse populations of DG granule cells (2-4%) are activated in a given context. Moreover, although the same population of DG granule cells is activated repeatedly in the same environment, different environments or different tasks activate different populations of DG granule cells. These lines of evidence point to the DG as an ideal target for the formation of contextual memory engrams that represent discrete environments.

text:
A central question in neuroscience is how a specific experience is represented in the brain. Since the early proposal of a memory engram, it has been hypothesized that learning induces stable physical changes in a restricted population of neurons, and that subsequent reactivation of this population gives rise to memory recall. Recent studies have begun to identify neuronal ensembles that are recruited during learning. In fear conditioning, sparse populations of neurons in the amygdala or hippocampus can be marked by activity-dependent gene expression, and manipulation of these populations has shown that they are important for memory expression. Ablation or inhibition of neurons activated during training impairs later recall, supporting the idea that such cells are necessary components of a memory trace.

However, necessity does not establish sufficiency. It remains unknown whether direct reactivation of neurons that were naturally active during learning can elicit the behavioural output of the corresponding memory in the absence of the original sensory cues. This question is particularly relevant for contextual fear memory, in which the hippocampus is thought to encode a representation of the environment that becomes associated with an aversive event. Here we combine activity-dependent genetic labelling with optogenetic stimulation to test whether dentate gyrus neurons activated during fear conditioning are sufficient to drive memory recall. By selectively reactivating these cells in a neutral context, we examine whether a defined hippocampal ensemble can function as a cellular substrate of a specific memory.

text:
A fundamental tenet of neuroscience is that specific memories are represented in the brain by the physical and chemical modifications of a sparse network of neurons. This specific neural ensemble, activated during learning and reactivated during retrieval, is known as a memory engram. Recent advances in immediate early gene-based tagging systems have allowed researchers to identify and capture the specific populations of neurons that are active during the encoding of a fear memory. Studies utilizing these techniques have demonstrated that the targeted ablation or pharmacological inhibition of these specific engram-bearing cells results in a significant reduction in memory expression, suggesting that these neural ensembles are necessary for mnemonic processes.

However, a critical question remains unanswered: is the activation of this specific neural ensemble sufficient to drive the behavioural expression of a memory? Can we artificially induce the recall of a specific memory by directly stimulating the very neurons that were active during its formation? To address this, we utilized a transgenic approach to selectively express the light-activated cation channel channelrhodopsin-2 (ChR2) in a specific population of hippocampal dentate gyrus (DG) neurons that were activated during contextual fear conditioning. By subsequently illuminating these tagged neurons with blue light in a novel context, we were able to directly test whether optogenetic reactivation of a defined hippocampal memory engram is sufficient to elicit the behavioral output of that memory, namely, freezing.

title: Optogenetic Stimulation of a Hippocampal Engram Activates Fear Memory Recall
text:
We have shown that optical activation of hippocampal cells that were active during FC elicits freezing behaviour. To our knowledge, this is the first demonstration that directly activating a subset of cells involved in the formation of a memory is sufficient to induce the behavioural expression of that memory. Our results and previous studies that addressed the necessity of similarly sparse cell populations in the amygdala argue that defined cell populations can form a cellular basis for fear memory engrams. The memory engram that we selectively labelled and manipulated is probably contextual in nature, as previous studies have demonstrated that hippocampal interventions affect conditioned freezing responses to a context but not a tone. Indeed, recent findings show that optogenetic inhibition of the hippocampal CA1 region during training or testing inhibited the recall of a contextual fear memory, while leaving auditory-cued fear memory recall intact. However, we cannot completely rule out the possibility that the fear memory recalled in our experiments may have some tone memory component.

Our observation that freezing responses were elicited by optical stimulation in the experimental groups (Exp, Exp-1day and Exp-Bi), but not in the OF-FC or FC-OF group, strongly supports a dual memory engram hypothesis of contextual FC. In this hypothesis, hippocampal cells are recruited to form contextual memory engrams, but these contextual engrams alone do not represent a complete fear memory. For a fear memory to be formed, the information from the contextual memory engram must be transferred to the basolateral amygdala coincidentally with the information representing a foot shock. In the OF-FC or FC-OF scheme, two distinct contextual memory engrams were formed in the DG, which were represented by two distinct sets of DG cells. One of these two contextual engrams (the one for context B) was associated with the representation of the shock, but not the other engram (the one for context C). Because only the latter, but not the former, was labelled by ChR2, optical stimulation could not elicit fear memory expression.

Although we have demonstrated the sufficiency of a DG memory engram for the behavioural expression of a fear memory, we cannot conclude that this engram is necessary for behavioural recall. During contextual FC, it is likely that multiple contextual memory engrams are formed in a series of hippocampal regions. Each of these engrams may contribute to the formation of the complete fear memory in the BLA and may also be capable of reactivating it independently, as we observed in the case of the DG engrams. Because the hippocampus is not a linear feed-forward network but contains several parallel circuits, inhibiting the formation or activation of contextual engrams in one region may not necessarily block the expression of the fear memory. For instance, disruption of contextual memory engrams in the DG could be circumvented by CA1 engrams, which could be generated through the direct input from the entorhinal cortex and may be sufficient to activate the fear memory engram in the BLA. Indeed, we recently generated a mouse mutant, which permitted us to demonstrate that the DG input to the CA3 is dispensable in the formation and retrieval of contextual fear memory.

The approach and methods described in this work will be a powerful tool for mapping multiple component engrams, each contributing to an overall memory. A multifaceted analysis of these engrams and their interplay will reveal the nature of the overall memory engram.

paper_title: Optogenetic Stimulation of a Hippocampal Engram Activates Fear Memory Recall - 2012 - Nature
display_title: Optogenetic Stimulation of a Hippocampal Engram Activates Fear Memory Recall
title: Optogenetic Stimulation of a Hippocampal Engram Activates Fear Memory Recall
text:
Our experiments show that the artificial reactivation of a sparse population of DG neurons that was active during fear conditioning is sufficient to induce the behavioural expression of a fear memory. Previous studies have shown that neuronal populations activated during learning can be subsequently identified, and that their ablation or inhibition can impair memory expression. These findings established an important requirement for such populations in memory recall. Here, by contrast, we directly tested sufficiency. Labelling c-Fos-expressing DG neurons during fear conditioning with ChR2-EYFP and later stimulating these neurons in a neutral context induced freezing only during light delivery. Thus, the activation of a defined population of hippocampal neurons can mimic, at least in part, the natural retrieval of a learned contextual fear memory.

Several observations support the conclusion that the light-induced freezing reflected recall of a specific memory rather than a nonspecific consequence of optical stimulation or ChR2 expression. Mice in which DG neurons were labelled during exposure to the training chamber without shock did not freeze in response to light, although the number and intensity of labelled cells were comparable to those in fear-conditioned mice. Similarly, fear-conditioned mice expressing EYFP alone showed no light-induced freezing. These controls indicate that the behavioural response requires both the appropriate learning episode and the ability to drive the activity of the neurons recruited during that episode. In addition, in vivo recordings confirmed that labelled DG neurons could be reliably activated by the same optical stimulation used in the behavioural experiments, and optical stimulation induced c-Fos expression in downstream CA3 neurons. These results suggest that reactivation of the labelled DG ensemble engages hippocampal circuitry in a manner related to natural contextual recall.

The context-specificity of the effect is particularly consistent with the proposed role of the DG in pattern separation. Distinct contexts activated largely non-overlapping populations of DG granule cells, and optical stimulation of neurons labelled in an open field failed to evoke freezing associated with fear conditioning in a different chamber. This was true whether the open-field labelling occurred before or after fear conditioning. Therefore, the mere activation of a sparse DG population is not sufficient to produce freezing; rather, the population must correspond to the representation associated with the aversive experience. These data support a model in which the DG contributes to the formation of relatively orthogonal contextual representations, and in which a subset of the neurons recruited into such a representation can serve as an entry point for recall of the associated memory.

The magnitude of light-induced freezing was lower than that observed during re-exposure to the conditioned context. This difference may reflect several limitations of the experimental manipulation. First, some labelled cells probably arose from background activity during the off-Dox period and may have diluted or interfered with the fear-conditioning ensemble. Consistent with this idea, shortening the off-Dox period reduced background labelling and increased light-induced freezing. Second, unilateral stimulation may have activated only a fraction of the relevant hippocampal representation. Bilateral stimulation produced stronger freezing, indicating that the size or completeness of the reactivated ensemble influences the behavioural output. Finally, natural contextual recall is likely to recruit a distributed network involving multiple hippocampal subregions, the amygdala and other structures. Artificial stimulation of DG cells may therefore initiate recall through a restricted component of this network rather than reproducing the full sensory and associative state produced by the conditioned context.

Together, these findings provide causal evidence that a sparse, learning-activated neuronal ensemble in the hippocampus is a functional component of a memory engram. The results do not imply that the entire memory is stored exclusively in the DG, but they show that reactivating the DG component of the ensemble is sufficient to trigger a specific learned response. This strategy of activity-dependent labelling combined with optogenetic reactivation should be useful for mapping the distributed cellular components of memory traces, determining how different brain regions cooperate during recall, and examining how engrams are modified during consolidation, extinction or the formation of new associations.

display_title: Optogenetic Stimulation of a Hippocampal Engram Activates Fear Memory Recall
title: Optogenetic Stimulation of a Hippocampal Engram Activates Fear Memory Recall
text:
Our results demonstrate that the direct optogenetic reactivation of a sparse subpopulation of dentate gyrus (DG) neurons that were naturally activated during the encoding of a fear memory is sufficient to induce the behavioral expression of that memory. These findings provide compelling evidence that a specific, sparsely distributed ensemble of hippocampal neurons forms a crucial component of the contextual memory engram. By genetically tagging cells based on their endogenous c-Fos expression during learning and subsequently driving them with light, we have achieved a functional mimicry of a naturally occurring memory recall process.

The necessity of the hippocampus, and specifically the DG, in contextual fear conditioning has been well established through lesion and pharmacological inhibition studies. Furthermore, recent work utilizing targeted ablation or silencing of specific neuronal ensembles has shown that these populations are necessary for the retrieval of fear memories. However, our study bridges a critical gap by addressing the question of sufficiency. By selectively activating only those cells that participated in the initial learning event, we observed a robust, context-specific behavioral response—freezing—in the absence of any external sensory cues associated with the conditioned context. This confirms that driving the activity of this defined neural population is enough to recreate the cognitive and behavioral state associated with the original experience.

The context specificity of this effect is a critical aspect of our findings. Light-induced freezing was absent in control animals where neuronal tagging occurred during exposure to a novel, unconditioned context, even though the total number of tagged cells was comparable. Similarly, tagging cells during a non-shock context exposure did not lead to subsequent light-evoked freezing. These controls rule out the possibility that our optical stimulation simply produced an unspecific motor response or a generalized state of anxiety. Instead, the evoked behavior was tightly linked to the specific informational content acquired during the fear-conditioning event. The observation that distinct contexts activate largely non-overlapping populations of DG neurons further supports the hypothesis that the DG performs pattern separation by assigning unique, orthogonal representations to different environments.

Although the degree of light-induced freezing (~15-35%) was lower than the freezing typically observed upon full re-exposure to the conditioned context (~60%), this discrepancy is not unexpected. Full contextual re-exposure engages a widespread, distributed network involving multiple brain regions, including the CA3, CA1, amygdala, and cortex. Our manipulation, while sufficient to drive a behavioral response, activated only a small fraction of the DG ensemble, artificially bypassing the natural upstream sensory inputs and the complete recruitment of the downstream neural circuitry. The enhancement of the freezing response observed with bilateral stimulation and optimized tagging protocols suggests that driving a larger proportion of the engram increases the fidelity and intensity of the recalled memory.

In conclusion, our experimental strategy provides a direct causal link between the activation of a specific neural ensemble and the expression of a discrete memory. This optogenetic approach to tagging and manipulating active neural populations offers a powerful, generalized method for identifying and characterizing the cellular substrates of memory engrams across the brain. Future studies utilizing this paradigm will be instrumental in dissecting the complex circuits underlying different types of memories, understanding how engrams evolve over time, and exploring how memories might be modified or disrupted in psychiatric and neurodegenerative disorders.

paper_title: Perturb-Seq - Dissecting Molecular Circuits with Scalable Single-Cell RNA Profiling of Pooled Genetic Screens - 2016 - Cell
display_title: Perturb-Seq - Dissecting Molecular Circuits with Scalable Single-Cell RNA Profiling of Pooled Genetic Screens
title: Perturb-Seq - Dissecting Molecular Circuits with Scalable Single-Cell RNA Profiling of Pooled Genetic Screens
text: Genetic screens help infer gene function in mammalian cells, but it has remained difficult to assay complex phenotypes—such as transcriptional profiles—at scale. Here, we develop Perturb-seq, combining single-cell RNA sequencing (RNA-seq) and clustered regularly interspaced short palindromic repeats (CRISPR)-based perturbations to perform many such assays in a pool. We demonstrate Perturb-seq by analyzing 200,000 cells in immune cells and cell lines, focusing on transcription factors regulating the response of dendritic cells to lipopolysaccharide (LPS). Perturb-seq accurately identifies individual gene targets, gene signatures, and cell states affected by individual perturbations and their genetic interactions. We posit new functions for regulators of differentiation, the anti-viral response, and mitochondrial function during immune activation. By decomposing many high content measurements into the effects of perturbations, their interactions, and diverse cell metadata, Perturb-seq dramatically increases the scope of pooled genomic assays.
historical_context: In 2016, Adam Dixit, Aviv Regev, Jonathan Weissman, and colleagues (paralleling related methods from other labs) unveiled "Perturb-seq," a massive technical leap combining CRISPR-Cas9 genome editing with single-cell RNA sequencing. By injecting distinct genetic perturbations into a vast pool of cells and subsequently reading out both the specific CRISPR guide RNA and the entire whole-transcriptome response within each individual cell, the method unleashed functional screens of unprecedented scale. This paper radically advanced functional genomics. It forcefully moved the field beyond simplistic live/dead screens, providing a high-throughput genomic tool to systematically map complex genetic interactions and completely dissect the intricate regulatory circuits underlying cellular function and disease states.

text: Pooled genetic screens are powerful but have been largely restricted to low-content phenotypes, whereas transcriptional profiling of perturbations has remained difficult to scale. We developed Perturb-seq, which couples CRISPR/Cas9-mediated perturbations with massively parallel single-cell RNA sequencing by encoding each single-guide RNA with an expressed guide barcode. This strategy links perturbation identity to whole-transcriptome profiles in individual cells and can be tuned to assay single genes or combinations. We applied Perturb-seq to primary bone marrow-derived dendritic cells responding to lipopolysaccharide and to proliferating K562 cells, analyzing approximately 200,000 cells across six experiments. Using MIMOSCA, a Multi-Input-Multi-Output-Single-Cell-Analysis framework, we inferred effects of individual perturbations and genetic interactions on genes, programs, and cell states. Perturb-seq recovered regulatory modules, transcription factor-specific functions, and distinct mechanisms underlying related fitness phenotypes, providing a scalable framework for dissecting molecular circuits with rich genomic readouts.

paper_title: Perturb-Seq - Dissecting Molecular Circuits with Scalable Single-Cell RNA Profiling of Pooled Genetic Screens - 2016 - Cell
display_title: Perturb-Seq - Dissecting Molecular Circuits with Scalable Single-Cell RNA Profiling of Pooled Genetic Screens
title: Perturb-Seq - Dissecting Molecular Circuits with Scalable Single-Cell RNA Profiling of Pooled Genetic Screens
text:
Genetic screens systematically analyze gene function in mammalian cells. Such screens are designed in either: (1) an individual ("arrayed") format, where each perturbation is delivered and assessed separately; or (2) a pooled format, performed en masse. Pooled readouts measure cell autonomous phenotypes, such as growth, drug resistance, or marker expression. Pooled screens are more efficient and scalable, but have been limited to low-content readouts. Distinguishing between different molecular mechanisms that yield similar phenotypes requires time and labor intensive follow-up.

Bridging the gap between rich profiles and pooled screens has been challenging. In mammalian cells, a few studies transcriptionally profiled hundreds of individual perturbations. In yeast, up to 1,500 knock out (KO) strains have been assessed. Even signature screens were only performed in centralized efforts.

Profiling may particularly help interpret the combined nonlinear effects of multiple factors. Comprehensive analysis of genetic interactions in growth phenotype between pairs of genes has been performed in yeast. In mammals, only small sets of pre-selected pairs have been assessed for cell viability or morphology. One yeast study determined the combined effects of regulators on expression profiles in a circuit of three to five genes. Very few studies have examined higher order interactions and none have coupled those with a high content scalable readout.

To address this challenge, we develop Perturb-seq, combining the modularity of clustered regularly interspaced short palindromic repeats (CRISPR)/Cas9 to perform multi-locus gene perturbation with the scale of massively parallel single cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) as a rich genomic readout. We demonstrate Perturb-seq in primary post-mitotic immune cells and in proliferating cell lines. We develop a computational framework, Multi-Input-Multi-Output-Single-Cell-Analysis (MIMOSCA), to decipher the effect of individual perturbations and the marginal contributions of genetic interactions on the level of each transcript, program, and cell state. Our framework can be extended to other high dimensional molecular phenotypes or diverse cell metadata.
historical_context: In 2016, Adam Dixit, Aviv Regev, Jonathan Weissman, and colleagues (paralleling related methods from other labs) unveiled "Perturb-seq," a massive technical leap combining CRISPR-Cas9 genome editing with single-cell RNA sequencing. By injecting distinct genetic perturbations into a vast pool of cells and subsequently reading out both the specific CRISPR guide RNA and the entire whole-transcriptome response within each individual cell, the method unleashed functional screens of unprecedented scale. This paper radically advanced functional genomics. It forcefully moved the field beyond simplistic live/dead screens, providing a high-throughput genomic tool to systematically map complex genetic interactions and completely dissect the intricate regulatory circuits underlying cellular function and disease states.

title: Perturb-Seq - Dissecting Molecular Circuits with Scalable Single-Cell RNA Profiling of Pooled Genetic Screens
text:
Genetic perturbation has been central to assigning gene function and dissecting cellular circuits. In mammalian cells, pooled genetic screens have made it possible to assay thousands of perturbations in parallel, using enrichment or depletion of guide RNAs to identify genes that affect growth, survival, or selectable marker phenotypes. However, many cellular phenotypes are not readily reduced to a single measurement. Regulatory programs, differentiation trajectories, and responses to environmental cues are encoded in coordinated changes across many genes and cell states, requiring high-content molecular readouts that have typically been restricted to arrayed experiments.

Single-cell RNA sequencing offers a natural readout for such screens, because it measures both gene expression and cell-to-cell heterogeneity at scale. In principle, a single pooled experiment could connect each genetic perturbation to a whole-transcriptome phenotype, while also capturing variation in cell quality, cell cycle, differentiation state, and response kinetics. The key challenge is to recover, for each profiled cell, the identity of the perturbation it carries and to separate its effect from the many technical and biological covariates intrinsic to single-cell measurements.

Here, we develop Perturb-seq, a platform that combines CRISPR-based pooled perturbations with single-cell RNA sequencing. We encode each single-guide RNA with an expressed guide barcode, allowing the perturbation identity and transcriptional state of each cell to be read out together. By varying infection multiplicity, the same framework can assay single perturbations or combinations. We further introduce a computational model to estimate the effects of perturbations on genes, signatures, and cell states, while accounting for cell quality, perturbation efficacy, and endogenous heterogeneity.

We apply Perturb-seq to approximately 200,000 cells across primary bone marrow-derived dendritic cells responding to lipopolysaccharide and K562 cells. These experiments identify transcription factor modules, regulated gene programs, altered cellular states, and genetic interactions. Perturb-seq thus links genotype to rich molecular phenotype in pooled screens, providing a scalable route to causal dissection of mammalian regulatory circuits.

title: Perturb-Seq - Dissecting Molecular Circuits with Scalable Single-Cell RNA Profiling of Pooled Genetic Screens
text:
The systematic perturbation of gene function is a cornerstone of biological research, enabling the elucidation of complex regulatory networks and cellular pathways. Genetic screens, particularly those utilizing the CRISPR-Cas9 system, have revolutionized our ability to identify genes required for cellular survival, proliferation, and resistance to various drugs or toxins. In a typical pooled genetic screen, large libraries of single-guide RNAs (sgRNAs) are introduced into a population of cells, and the functional consequences are measured by determining the enrichment or depletion of specific guides via bulk DNA sequencing. While incredibly powerful for identifying essential genes, these traditional pooled screens are largely limited to simple, one-dimensional phenotypes, such as cell death or the expression of a single fluorescent reporter.

To fully dissect the architecture of molecular circuits, we require the ability to measure high-dimensional, complex phenotypes—such as genome-wide transcriptional profiles—across thousands of distinct genetic perturbations simultaneously. Until recently, mapping the transcriptomic consequences of multiple gene knockouts necessitated arrayed experiments in multi-well plates, an approach that is labor-intensive, expensive, and severely limited in scale.

In this study, we introduce Perturb-seq, a highly scalable platform that seamlessly merges pooled CRISPR-Cas9 genetic screening with single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq). By engineering a lentiviral vector that not only delivers an sgRNA to a cell but also uniquely identifies that perturbation via an expressed guide barcode (GBC) transcript, we can simultaneously read out both the specific genetic manipulation and the resulting whole-transcriptome profile in thousands of individual cells in a single pooled format. We demonstrate the power of Perturb-seq by analyzing over 200,000 single cells across immune cells and human cell lines. Focusing on the complex regulatory response of dendritic cells to lipopolysaccharide, we show that Perturb-seq can accurately identify the individual gene targets, modular gene signatures, and dynamic cell states controlled by various transcription factors. By decomposing these high-content measurements, Perturb-seq dramatically expands the scope and resolution of pooled genomic assays, allowing us to map the functional wiring of the genome at unprecedented scale.

paper_title: Perturb-Seq - Dissecting Molecular Circuits with Scalable Single-Cell RNA Profiling of Pooled Genetic Screens - 2016 - Cell
display_title: Perturb-Seq - Dissecting Molecular Circuits with Scalable Single-Cell RNA Profiling of Pooled Genetic Screens
title: Perturb-Seq - Dissecting Molecular Circuits with Scalable Single-Cell RNA Profiling of Pooled Genetic Screens
text:
We developed Perturb-seq, a method to analyze the transcriptional effect associated with genetic manipulations on genes, processes, and states. Perturb-seq decreases the time and cost associated with assaying the complex effects of large numbers of perturbations, including combinations.

Future Enhancements of Precision and Facility

An important advantage of using Perturb-seq is that higher order interactions can be assayed without further need to generate complex reagents. Due to the Poisson loading of perturbation per cell, the same experiment used for a single perturbation can also uncover the genetic interactions between the perturbed genes. Future work could leverage the ability of Cpf1 to autonomously process an entire array and deliver several sgRNAs (or an unprocessed array) on one construct.

Current and Future Scale of Perturb-Seq

At its current scale, Perturb-seq can be readily applied for targeted screens of a subset of genes of interest and their interactions, as we have done here. In some systems, growth or marker-based screens may first be performed to identify this subset prior to Perturb-seq. Perturb-seq will scale as both the cost of sequencing and of scRNA-seq decreases.

By varying the number of surveyed cells and the sequencing depth, screens can be adjusted to focus on cell states and signatures or on effects on individual genes. Our analysis suggests that a broad survey of transcriptional phenotypes across thousands of perturbations can be performed with a few tens of cells per perturbation.

Genome-wide or large combinatorial screens will also require increased computational bandwidth. MIMOSCA is designed foreseeing screens with millions of cells, with fast, scalable, and parallelizable algorithms. It is publically available with worked examples.

Challenges and Opportunities for Understanding the Vast Space of Possible Genetic Interactions

As we showed, Perturb-seq can in principle dissect higher order effects, but systematic analysis of genetic interactions remains an ambitious goal. First, both the probability of detecting all perturbations and the probability of all perturbations resulting in an effect scale exponentially with the order of perturbations. Our inference framework and future improvements can help deconvolve mixtures of knockouts, and is potentially scalable to higher order interactions. However, while Perturb-seq significantly reduces time and cost, these still scale linearly with the number of perturbations assayed, whereas the size of combination space grows exponentially as the order of combinations grows.

We hypothesize that a plausible alternative strategy exists, combining substantial under-sampling of this vast space with appropriate analytics that make inference possible even when the number of possible combinations is much larger than the number of samples. We are motivated by two biological assumptions: (1) modularity, as we have shown for both the perturbations and the gene targets; and (2) sparsity, such that the majority of gene pairs (or higher order combinations) do not manifest genetic interactions. If sparsity holds for expression phenotypes, a subset of experiments can be performed in which most cells receive a relatively large number of perturbations (e.g., five), and we infer both partially observed and even entirely unobserved interactions at a lower order effects (e.g., two or three). Perturb-seq was designed bearing in mind such future studies, which will leverage group structure in perturbations and their interactions.

A General Framework to Combine Rich Readout with Cellular Metadata

Other CRISPR-based perturbations are readily compatible with Perturb-seq, including CRISPRi as in our companion study, CRISPRa, and alternative editors (e.g., Cpf1). Expressed barcodes can also be used to mark cells derived from a common ancestor for the purposes of lineage tracing. Other measurement platforms, such as multiplex PCR or multiplex protein measurements can help focus on a subset of target transcripts or proteins, respectively. It should also be possible to apply Perturb-seq in vivo, or in co-cultures of perturbed cells in droplets, merging them with scRNA-seq.

We hope that the experiments and analysis provided here and in Adamson et al. will be starting points for future experiments that combine scRNA-seq and pooled screens. These will bridge the gap between genetic screening and molecular follow-up experiments and will facilitate causal studies of how specific genotypes lead to phenotypes.

paper_title: Phase Transitions in the Assembly of Multivalent Signalling Proteins - 2012 - Nature
display_title: Phase Transitions in the Assembly of Multivalent Signalling Proteins
title: Phase Transitions in the Assembly of Multivalent Signalling Proteins
text: Cells are organized on length scales ranging from Angstrom to micrometres. However, the mechanisms by which Angstrom-scale molecular properties are translated to micrometre-scale macroscopic properties are not well understood. Here we show that interactions between diverse synthetic, multivalent macromolecules (including multi-domain proteins and RNA) produce sharp liquid-liquid demixing phase separations, generating micrometre-sized liquid droplets in aqueous solution. This macroscopic transition corresponds to a molecular transition between small complexes and large, dynamic supramolecular polymers. The concentrations needed for phase transition are directly related to the valency of the interacting species. In the case of the actin-regulatory protein called neural Wiskott-Aldrich syndrome protein (N-WASP) interacting with its established biological partners NCK and phosphorylated nephrin, the phase transition corresponds to a sharp increase in activity towards an actin nucleation factor, the Arp2/3 complex. The transition is governed by the degree of phosphorylation of nephrin, explaining how this property of the system can be controlled to regulatory effect by kinases. The widespread occurrence of multivalent systems suggests that phase transitions may be used to spatially organize and biochemically regulate information throughout biology.
historical_context: In 2012, Pilong Li, Michael Rosen, and colleagues published this landmark paper introducing the physical concept of liquid-liquid phase separation (LLPS) directly into mainstream cell signaling. They demonstrated that multivalent signaling proteins possess the ability to undergo rapid, reversible phase transitions, condensing into highly dynamic, liquid-like droplets that act as membraneless organelles. This field-defining paper triggered an absolute explosion of research within cell biology. It permanently altered how scientists conceptualize intracellular organization, supplying a physical mechanism explaining how cells rapidly localize and concentrate biochemical reactions without relying on lipid membranes, and yielding profound insights into everything from basic gene regulation to the devastating protein aggregates observed in ALS and Alzheimer's.

title: Phase Transitions in the Assembly of Multivalent Signalling Proteins
text: Multivalent interactions are common in signalling proteins, but their capacity to drive sharp changes in molecular organization and activity has been unclear. Here we show that weak, modular interactions between synthetic SH3-domain and proline-rich-motif polymers produce concentration- and valency-dependent liquid-liquid phase separation. The dense phase contains both components at approximately equal stoichiometry and at concentrations about 100-fold higher than the surrounding solution, and displays properties consistent with a polymeric gel. Dynamic and small-angle X-ray scattering, fluorescence recovery after photobleaching and cryo-electron microscopy indicate that droplet formation is driven by assembly into large, disordered, crosslinked species, in a transition coupled to sol-gel polymerization. Similar puncta form in cells when multivalent SH3 and ligand arrays are coexpressed. We further show that the natural nephrin-NCK-N-WASP signalling system undergoes analogous phosphorylation-regulated phase transitions, and that entry into the droplet/polymer state sharply enhances Arp2/3-mediated actin assembly. These findings suggest that multivalent proteins can generate switch-like transitions between dispersed and condensed states, providing a physical mechanism for organizing cellular bodies and producing nonlinear signalling responses.

title: Phase Transitions in the Assembly of Multivalent Signalling Proteins
text: Multivalent macromolecules are central to many biological processes, including signal transduction, RNA metabolism and the organization of non-membrane-bound cellular bodies. Yet, despite the well-known capacity of multivalent small molecules and polymers to undergo sharp sol-gel transitions, it has remained unclear whether intracellular multivalent proteins, which typically combine high valency, modest affinity and long flexible linkers, exhibit analogous behaviour. Here we show that engineered repeats of SRC homology 3 (SH3) domains and their proline-rich motif (PRM) ligands, as well as an unrelated RNA-binding system and the natural nephrin-NCK-N-WASP signalling module, undergo sharp liquid-liquid demixing transitions to produce micrometre-scale, protein-rich droplets in vitro and in cells. Light scattering, X-ray scattering, photobleaching and cryo-electron microscopy indicate that the droplet phase contains large, dynamic polymeric assemblies, suggesting that the phase transition is thermodynamically coupled to a molecular sol-gel transition. The phase boundary depends steeply on valency and affinity and can be tuned by tyrosine phosphorylation of nephrin, which in turn switches on Arp2/3-mediated actin assembly. These data suggest that multivalent interactions can generate sharp, regulatable transitions between physically and functionally distinct states, providing a general mechanism for nonlinear behaviour and the assembly of cellular bodies.

title: Phase Transitions in the Assembly of Multivalent Signalling Proteins
text: Interactions between multivalent molecules can generate sharp sol-gel phase transitions, producing macroscopic polymer networks. While extensively studied in classical polymer chemistry and extracellular biology, the occurrence and consequences of such transitions among intracellular multivalent proteins have been unclear. Here, we investigate the assembly of multivalent signaling proteins using engineered and natural systems. We show that combining proteins with multiple SH3 domains and multiple proline-rich motifs leads to a sharp liquid-liquid demixing phase separation. This phase transition is thermodynamically coupled to a molecular sol-gel transition, creating macroscopic, highly concentrated liquid droplets containing dynamic, non-covalent protein polymers. This phenomenon depends strongly on valency and affinity and is reproduced in living cells. Furthermore, we demonstrate that a natural three-component system—nephrin, NCK, and N-WASP—undergoes a similar phase transition, which can be dynamically regulated by nephrin phosphorylation. Crucially, this phase transition sharply increases the system's ability to stimulate Arp2/3-mediated actin assembly. Our findings suggest that macroscopic phase transitions coupled to sol-gel transitions may be a general mechanism for organizing intracellular space and generating switch-like, non-linear responses in biological signaling networks.

paper_title: Phase Transitions in the Assembly of Multivalent Signalling Proteins - 2012 - Nature
display_title: Phase Transitions in the Assembly of Multivalent Signalling Proteins
title: Phase Transitions in the Assembly of Multivalent Signalling Proteins
text: Covalent and non-covalent interactions between multivalent small molecules are central elements of classical polymer chemistry and physics and of supramolecular chemistry. These fields have produced theories and experimental demonstrations of sharp transitions between small assemblies and macroscopic polymer gels (known as sol-gel transitions) as the degree of bonding increases. The transition point (critical point) depends on the physical properties of the monomeric species, including valency and affinity. The polymer can have a variety of physical forms, ranging from phase-separated liquid to crystalline solid. For non-covalent systems, phase separation can strongly influence the sol-gel transition by altering the degree of bonding. In biology, interactions between multivalent entities are found in diverse processes, including extracellular carbohydrate-lectin binding, intracellular signalling, RNA metabolism and chromatin organization in the nucleus. Biological multivalency has been studied most extensively in the context of extracellular ligands binding to cell surface receptors, where antibody-receptor and carbohydrate-lectin systems can assemble into crosslinked networks. These networks are typically precipitates, but liquid-like gels have also been described. Multivalency has been less studied in the context of intracellular molecules, which often share the characteristics of high valency, modest affinity, and long, flexible connections between binding elements. Here we asked whether these systems also undergo sharp transitions to polymers, and if so, what the macroscopic properties of the polymers are and how such transitions could be regulated and affect function.

This field-defining paper triggered an absolute explosion of research within cell biology. It permanently altered how scientists conceptualize intracellular organization, supplying a physical mechanism explaining how cells rapidly localize and concentrate biochemical reactions without relying on lipid membranes, and yielding profound insights into everything from basic gene regulation to the devastating protein aggregates observed in ALS and Alzheimer's.

title: Phase Transitions in the Assembly of Multivalent Signalling Proteins
text:
Many cellular processes require information encoded in weak molecular interactions to be converted into structures and activities on far larger length scales. This problem is particularly acute in signalling, where modular domains and short linear motifs often occur in multiple copies and bind one another with modest affinities. Such architectures are well suited to combinatorial regulation, but they also create the possibility that individual binding events can be coupled to collective physical transitions. How these transitions might organize intracellular space and alter biochemical activity remains poorly understood.

Polymer chemistry predicts that multivalent interactions can drive sharp changes in molecular organization once the number and occupancy of binding sites exceed a critical threshold. In biological systems, related principles have been invoked to explain the assembly of non-membrane-bound cellular bodies, puncta and signalling clusters, but the molecular basis and functional consequences of such behaviour have been difficult to establish. In particular, it is unclear whether phase separation can arise from ordinary folded protein interaction domains, whether the transition is governed by valency in a predictable manner, and whether entry into a condensed phase can directly regulate enzymatic or cytoskeletal activity.

Here we use synthetic multivalent proteins, RNA-binding systems and the physiological nephrin-NCK-N-WASP actin-regulatory pathway to show that multivalent interactions can generate liquid-like phase-separated droplets that contain dynamic supramolecular polymers. We further show that phosphorylation-controlled changes in valency tune the phase boundary and convert molecular assembly into switch-like activation of Arp2/3-mediated actin polymerization.

display_title: Phase Transitions in the Assembly of Multivalent Signalling Proteins
title: Phase Transitions in the Assembly of Multivalent Signalling Proteins
text:
Biological systems are highly organized across multiple length scales, from the Angstrom-scale interactions of individual amino acids to the micrometre-scale compartmentalization of the entire cell. A major challenge in modern cell biology is understanding the precise physical mechanisms by which molecular-level binding events are translated into the assembly of large, macroscopic cellular structures. While it is well known that many signalling proteins contain multiple interaction domains—such as tandem arrays of SH3 domains and proline-rich motifs—the functional consequences of this multivalency have primarily been studied in the context of small, discrete protein complexes.

However, polymer theory suggests that when multivalent molecules interact, they can undergo a sharp transition to form large, cross-linked, dynamic networks. In this study, we demonstrate that interactions between diverse engineered and naturally occurring multivalent macromolecules, including multi-domain proteins and RNA, can drive macroscopic liquid-liquid demixing phase separations in aqueous solution. This process produces micrometre-sized, liquid-like droplets that are highly enriched in the interacting components. We show that this macroscopic phase transition is thermodynamically coupled to a molecular sol-gel transition, driven by the valency of the interacting species.

We apply this principle to the natural tripartite signalling system comprising the transmembrane protein nephrin, the adaptor protein NCK, and the actin-nucleating factor N-WASP. We find that the phase separation of these components is strictly regulated by the degree of nephrin phosphorylation and corresponds directly to a sharp, switch-like activation of the Arp2/3 complex. Our findings suggest that phase transitions driven by multivalent interactions provide a general physical mechanism by which cells can spatially organize and biochemically regulate complex signalling pathways.

display_title: Phase Transitions in the Assembly of Multivalent Signalling Proteins
title: Phase Transitions in the Assembly of Multivalent Signalling Proteins
text: Sharp phase transitions occurring concomitantly with sol-gel transitions may be a general feature of multivalent systems in biology. Many 'cellular bodies'-that is, subcellular compartments that are compositionally distinct from the surrounding cytoplasm or nucleoplasm but that are not membrane bounded-are enriched in multivalent proteins and nucleic acids. These include promyelocytic leukaemia nuclear bodies, Cajal bodies, P bodies and P granules. Moreover, P granules in the Caenorhabditis elegans embryo were recently shown to have liquid-like properties with many of the features that we observed here, including switch-like formation and greatly slowed diffusion of constituent molecules. Many multivalent proteins, in general, can organize into puncta in the cytosol or at membranes. Within puncta, the physical properties of a polymer could impart micrometre-scale structural and dynamic organization and control the chemistry (for example, of catalysis, molecular interactions or structural rearrangements). Our findings provide a mechanism by which multivalent interactions could yield sharp transitions between physically and functionally distinct states, generating nonlinearity in signalling pathways, connecting disparate length scales in the cell, and perhaps contributing to the structure and function of cellular bodies and other two- and three-dimensional compartments.

paper_title: Phase Transitions in the Assembly of Multivalent Signalling Proteins - 2012 - Nature
display_title: Phase Transitions in the Assembly of Multivalent Signalling Proteins
title: Phase Transitions in the Assembly of Multivalent Signalling Proteins
text:
Our findings show that weak interactions among multivalent macromolecules can drive sharp transitions from small soluble complexes to concentrated, dynamic liquid phases containing polymeric networks. This behaviour provides a physical mechanism by which molecular-scale binding events can be converted into micrometre-scale cellular organization. The dependence of the transition on valency, affinity and stoichiometry, together with the ability of monovalent ligands to suppress assembly, indicates that phase separation is a direct consequence of network formation rather than nonspecific aggregation.

The nephrin-NCK-N-WASP system illustrates how such transitions can be coupled to biological regulation. Phosphorylation of nephrin increases the effective valency of the complex and shifts the phase boundary into a concentration range relevant to cells. Thus, kinase activity can regulate not only the recruitment of binding partners but also the physical state of the assembled signalling machinery. The resulting condensed phase concentrates N-WASP and promotes Arp2/3-dependent actin assembly, suggesting that phase transitions can function as biochemical switches.

Because many signalling, RNA-binding and scaffold proteins contain repeated modular interaction elements connected by flexible linkers, similar transitions may be widespread. Regulated phase separation may therefore provide a general strategy for organizing cellular reactions in space and time while preserving rapid molecular exchange.

title: Phase Transitions in the Assembly of Multivalent Signalling Proteins
text:
Our findings reveal that interactions among multivalent signaling proteins can drive macroscopic liquid-liquid phase separations, corresponding to sharp molecular sol-gel transitions. This phenomenon provides a mechanism by which Angstrom-scale protein interactions manifest as micrometer-scale cellular structures, such as signaling puncta. In the nephrin-NCK-N-WASP system, the phase transition directly couples to a dramatic, switch-like increase in Arp2/3-mediated actin nucleation activity. Importantly, this transition threshold is highly sensitive to the degree of nephrin phosphorylation, highlighting how kinase activity can tune the system’s physical state and functional output.

The formation of these dynamic, liquid-like droplets allows cells to locally concentrate specific biochemical components while excluding others, potentially enhancing reaction rates and sequestering signaling pathways. Because multivalent interactions—such as repeating SH3 domains, PRMs, and multiple phosphorylation sites—are ubiquitous across biological systems, liquid-liquid phase separation may be a fundamental and widespread principle for spatial organization. From receptor clustering at the plasma membrane to RNA processing in the nucleus, macroscopic phase transitions offer a versatile means for cells to compartmentalize information and exquisitely regulate biochemical activities in space and time.

historical_context: In 2010, Bertrand Coste, Ardem Patapoutian, and colleagues discovered the Piezo proteins, finally solving the enduring mystery of how cells physically sense mechanical force. Executing an exhaustive RNA interference screen, they pinpointed Piezo1 and Piezo2 as the essential, pore-forming subunits of mechanically activated ion channels, directly responsible for translating physical touch into readable electrical signals. This landmark discovery led directly to Ardem Patapoutian winning the 2021 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine (shared with David Julius). The Nobel committee praised their discoveries of receptors for temperature and touch, highlighting that uncovering Piezo channels definitively decoded the molecular basis of somatosensation, proprioception, and numerous physiological reflexes reliant on mechanical sensing, including blood pressure regulation and bladder control.

text: Mechanotransduction, the conversion of mechanical force into biological signals, has crucial roles in physiology. In mammals, embryonic development, touch, pain, proprioception, hearing, adjustment of vascular tone and blood flow, flow sensing in kidney, lung growth and injury, bone and muscle homeostasis as well as metastasis are all regulated by mechanotransduction. In plants, mechanical force strongly impacts morphogenesis, for example in lateral root formation. Unicellular organisms such as ciliates sense touch and change direction in response to a tactile stimulus. Mechanotransduction in vertebrate inner ear hair cells is extremely rapid, implicating an ion channel directly activated by force. Indeed, calcium-permeable mechanically-activated (MA) cationic currents have been described in various mechanosensitive cells. However, only few MA channels have been identified to date, and definitive candidates in vertebrate mechanosensation has yet to emerge.
