text:
We found that Piezo1 is required for MA currents in Neuro2A cells and Piezo2 is required for a subset of MA currents in DRG neurons. Moreover, overexpressing Piezo1 or Piezo2 in three different cell types gave rise to 17- to 300-fold increase in MA currents. We conclude that Piezos are both necessary and sufficient for the expression of a MA current in various cell types.

Piezo1 and Piezo2 sequences do not resemble those of other known ion channels or other protein classes. The large number of predicted transmembrane domains of Piezo1 and Piezo2 is reminiscent of the structure of voltage-activated sodium channels with 24 transmembrane domains, composed of a 4-fold repeat of 6-transmembrane units. However, pore-containing or repetitive domains have not been observed in Piezo proteins. It may be that Piezo proteins are non-conducting subunits of ion channels required for proper expression or for modulating channel properties, similar to beta subunits of voltage-gated channels or SUR subunits of ATP-sensitive K+ channels. In this case, all the cell types used here would have to express an inactive conducting subunit of an MA channel that requires Piezos to function. Alternatively, Piezo proteins may define a distinct class of ion channels, akin to Orai1, which lacks significant sequence homology to other channels. Piezo1 is also found in the endoplasmic reticulum, so Piezos may act at both the plasma membrane and in intracellular compartments.

We described a role of Piezo2 in rapidly-adapting mechanically-activated currents in somatosensory neurons. Thus Piezo2 has potential roles in touch and pain sensation. Piezo1 and 2 are expressed in various tissues, and their homologs are present throughout animals, plants, and protozoa, raising the possibility that Piezo proteins have a broad role in mechanotransduction.

This landmark discovery led directly to Ardem Patapoutian winning the 2021 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine (shared with David Julius). The Nobel committee praised their discoveries of receptors for temperature and touch, highlighting that uncovering Piezo channels definitively decoded the molecular basis of somatosensation, proprioception, and numerous physiological reflexes reliant on mechanical sensing, including blood pressure regulation and bladder control.

paper_title: Piezo1 and Piezo2 Are Essential Components of Distinct Mechanically Activated Cation Channels - 2010 - Science
display_title: Piezo1 and Piezo2 Are Essential Components of Distinct Mechanically Activated Cation Channels
title: Piezo1 and Piezo2 Are Essential Components of Distinct Mechanically Activated Cation Channels
text:
Mechanically-activated cation currents have been described in many vertebrate cell types, yet the molecular identity of the channels underlying these currents has remained elusive. Our results identify Piezo1 and Piezo2 as large multipass transmembrane proteins required for distinct MA channel activities. Loss of Piezo1 markedly reduced endogenous MA currents in N2A cells, and expression of either Piezo1 or Piezo2 was sufficient to confer robust mechanically-evoked cation currents in multiple cell lines. These observations argue that Piezos are essential components of MA channels, although additional associated proteins may modulate their localization, gating, or adaptation in native cells.

Piezo1- and Piezo2-dependent currents share several properties with previously described MA currents, including non-selective cation permeability, block by gadolinium and ruthenium red, and activation by membrane indentation or stretch. However, the two proteins generate currents with different kinetics. Piezo2 currents inactivate rapidly, whereas Piezo1 currents display slower adaptation. Thus, the diversity of MA current kinetics observed in native cells may reflect, at least in part, expression of different Piezo family members. The presence of Piezo homologs in invertebrates, plants, and protozoa further suggests that this family represents an ancient and broadly used mechanism for coupling mechanical force to ionic signaling.

The expression pattern of Piezos is consistent with roles in multiple mechanotransduction pathways. Piezo1 is present in tissues that experience stretch or pressure, including bladder, colon, lung, kidney, and skin. Piezo2 is enriched in dorsal root ganglia neurons, and its knockdown selectively reduced rapidly-adapting MA currents in these neurons. This selective effect supports a role for Piezo2 in somatosensory mechanotransduction, including touch and possibly noxious mechanical sensation. Future studies should determine whether Piezos form the pore of MA channels, how force gates these proteins, and how their activity contributes to mechanosensory physiology in vivo.

text:
The identification of Piezo1 and Piezo2 solves a long-standing mystery in sensory biology by providing the molecular basis for mechanically-activated (MA) cation currents in vertebrates. While mechanotransduction is essential for fundamental processes such as touch, hearing, and blood pressure regulation, the identity of the primary mammalian transducers has remained elusive for decades. Our results demonstrate that Piezos are both necessary for endogenous MA currents in Neuro2A cells and sufficient to confer mechanosensitivity to several heterologous cell lines that otherwise lack these responses.

Piezo proteins are structurally unique, characterized by an exceptionally large number of predicted transmembrane domains—between 24 and 36. This architectural complexity distinguishes them from other known ion channel families and likely reflects the specialized requirements for sensing tension within the lipid bilayer. The broad conservation of Piezos across diverse eukaryotic kingdoms, including plants and ciliates, suggests that this protein family represents an ancient and evolutionarily successful solution to the fundamental challenge of mechanotransduction.

Functionally, we show that Piezo1 and Piezo2 mediate currents with distinct kinetic signatures. Piezo2 exhibits significantly faster inactivation than Piezo1, a property that is likely tuned to specific physiological needs. In dorsal root ganglia, the specific reduction of rapidly-adapting currents following Piezo2 knockdown strongly implicates it as a primary transducer for touch or noxious mechanical stimuli. In contrast, the wider distribution of Piezo1 in tissues such as the lung, bladder, and kidney suggests it may be involved in sensing fluid flow or organ distension.

Collectively, these findings establish Piezos as essential components of vertebrate MA cation channels. Future work must definitively demonstrate that Piezos form the ion-conducting pore themselves and elucidate how their massive structure couples mechanical force to channel gating. Understanding the mechanosensory role of Piezos will undoubtedly provide profound insights into mammalian physiology and the pathogenesis of various mechanosensory disorders.

paper_title: Positional Cloning of the Mouse Obese Gene and Its Human Homologue - 1994 - Nature
display_title: Positional Cloning of the Mouse Obese Gene and Its Human Homologue
title: Positional Cloning of the Mouse Obese Gene and Its Human Homologue
text: The mechanisms that balance food intake and energy expenditure determine who will be obese and who will be lean. One of the molecules that regulates energy balance in the mouse is the obese (ob) gene. Mutation of ob results in profound obesity and type II diabetes as part of a syndrome that resembles morbid obesity in humans. The ob gene product may function as part of a signalling pathway from adipose tissue that acts to regulate the size of the body fat depot.
historical_context: In 1994, Jeffrey Friedman, Yiying Zhang, and colleagues executed a monumental feat by positionally cloning the *obese* (*ob*) gene in mice and successfully identifying its human equivalent. The gene encoded a completely novel hormone they dubbed "leptin," which is secreted by adipose tissue to communicate satiety to the brain. The discovery of leptin was one of the most consequential events in modern endocrinology and metabolism. It dismantled the long-held assumption that fat was merely inert storage, permanently elevating adipose tissue to the status of an active, highly critical endocrine organ. It delivered the first definitive molecular mechanism for the regulation of body weight and appetite, profoundly directing decades of subsequent research into obesity, metabolism, and eating disorders.

text: We have isolated the mouse obese (ob) gene by positional cloning and identified its human homologue. The gene encodes a 4.5-kb messenger RNA expressed principally in adipose tissue and predicts a conserved 167-amino-acid protein with an amino-terminal signal sequence. The original C57BL/6J ob mutation is a nonsense change at codon 105 and is associated with increased ob RNA, whereas the SM/Ckc-Dac ob2J allele lacks detectable transcript. The conservation and probable secretion of ob suggest an adipose-derived signal regulating body fat stores.

paper_title: Positional Cloning of the Mouse Obese Gene and Its Human Homologue - 1994 - Nature
display_title: Positional Cloning of the Mouse Obese Gene and Its Human Homologue
title: Positional Cloning of the Mouse Obese Gene and Its Human Homologue
text:
OBESITY is the commonest nutritional disorder in Western societies. More than three in ten adult Americans weigh at least 20% in excess of their ideal body weight. Increased body weight is an important public health problem because it is associated with type II diabetes, hypertension, hyperlipidaemia and certain cancers. Although obesity is often considered to be a psychological problem, there is evidence that body weight is physiologically regulated.
The molecular pathogenesis of obesity is unknown. To identify components of the physiological system controlling body weight, we have applied positional cloning technologies in an attempt to isolate mouse obesity genes. Five single-gene mutations in mice that result in an obese phenotype have been described. The first of the recessive obesity mutations, the obese mutation (ob), was identified in 1950. ob is a single gene mutation that results in profound obesity and type II diabetes as part of a syndrome that resembles morbid obesity in humans. Neither the primary defect nor the site of synthesis of the ob gene product is known. Cross-circulation experiments between mutant and wild-type mice suggest that ob mice are deficient for a blood-borne factor that regulates nutrient intake and metabolism, but the nature of this putative factor has not been determined.
We report the cloning and sequencing of the mouse ob gene and its human homologue. ob encodes a 4.5-kilobase (kb) adipose tissue messenger RNA with a highly conserved 167-amino-acid open reading frame. The predicted amino-acid sequence is 84% identical between human and mouse and has features of a secreted protein. A nonsense mutation in codon 105 has been found in the original congenic C57BL/6J ob/ob mouse strain, which expresses a twentyfold increase in ob mRNA. A second ob mutant, the co-isogenic SM/Ckc-Dac ob2J/ob2J strain, does not synthesize ob RNA. These data suggest that the ob gene product may function as part of a signalling pathway from adipose tissue that acts to regulate the size of the body fat depot.
For the positional cloning of mutant genes from mammals, it is necessary first to obtain genetic and physical maps, then to isolate the gene and detect the mutation. Here we describe the successful use of this approach to identify the ob gene.

The gene encoded a completely novel hormone they dubbed "leptin," which is secreted by adipose tissue to communicate satiety to the brain. The discovery of leptin was one of the most consequential events in modern endocrinology and metabolism. It dismantled the long-held assumption that fat was merely inert storage, permanently elevating adipose tissue to the status of an active, highly critical endocrine organ. It delivered the first definitive molecular mechanism for the regulation of body weight and appetite, profoundly directing decades of subsequent research into obesity, metabolism, and eating disorders.

title: Positional Cloning of the Mouse Obese Gene and Its Human Homologue
text:
The regulation of body weight in mammals requires that food intake and energy expenditure be matched over long periods of time. Although transient changes in feeding behaviour and metabolic rate can produce large fluctuations in energy balance, the size of the adipose depot is normally maintained within relatively narrow limits. This observation has led to the proposal that body fat is subject to physiological feedback control, but the molecular nature of the signals that communicate the state of peripheral energy stores to the central nervous system has remained unknown.

Several inherited forms of obesity in the mouse provide genetic models for identifying such signals. Among these, mutations at the obese (ob) locus produce a recessive syndrome of severe obesity, hyperphagia, decreased energy expenditure, insulin resistance and diabetes. The phenotype is evident early in life and is accompanied by numerous endocrine and metabolic abnormalities, including defects in thermoregulation and fertility. These pleiotropic features have suggested that ob acts at an early point in the pathways that regulate energy balance, rather than representing a secondary consequence of increased adiposity alone.

Physiological studies have further indicated that the product of ob may participate in a humoral signalling pathway. Parabiosis experiments, in which the circulation of obese and lean animals was joined, suggested that some forms of obesity result from the absence of a circulating satiety factor, whereas others reflect an inability to respond to such a factor. In particular, the relationship between the ob and diabetes (db) mutations raised the possibility that ob encodes a blood-borne signal and that db encodes a component required for its response. Direct biochemical identification of this putative factor, however, has not been possible.

A molecular approach to the ob locus therefore offers a means of testing the lipostatic model of body-weight regulation. The ob gene had previously been mapped to proximal mouse chromosome 6, close to the molecular markers Pax4 and D6Rck13. Here we describe the positional cloning of ob, the identification of mutations in two independent obese mouse strains, and the cloning of the homologous human gene. The predicted protein has the properties of a secreted polypeptide and is expressed predominantly in adipose tissue, suggesting that adipocytes themselves may produce a conserved signal that regulates the size of the fat depot.
historical_context: In 1994, Jeffrey Friedman, Yiying Zhang, and colleagues executed a monumental feat by positionally cloning the *obese* (*ob*) gene in mice and successfully identifying its human equivalent. The gene encoded a completely novel hormone they dubbed "leptin," which is secreted by adipose tissue to communicate satiety to the brain. The discovery of leptin was one of the most consequential events in modern endocrinology and metabolism. It dismantled the long-held assumption that fat was merely inert storage, permanently elevating adipose tissue to the status of an active, highly critical endocrine organ. It delivered the first definitive molecular mechanism for the regulation of body weight and appetite, profoundly directing decades of subsequent research into obesity, metabolism, and eating disorders.

paper_title: Positional Cloning of the Mouse Obese Gene and Its Human Homologue - 1994 - Nature
display_title: Positional Cloning of the Mouse Obese Gene and Its Human Homologue
title: Positional Cloning of the Mouse Obese Gene and Its Human Homologue
text:
The physiological mechanisms that tightly balance food intake and energy expenditure are fundamental in determining whether an individual will be obese or lean. Disruption of this energy homeostasis leads to obesity, a condition associated with significant morbidity and mortality, including an increased risk for type II diabetes, cardiovascular disease, and hypertension. While the regulation of body weight is influenced by a complex interplay of genetic, environmental, and behavioral factors, evidence from human and animal studies strongly suggests a major genetic component. In the mouse, one of the key genetic determinants regulating energy balance is the obese (ob) gene. The recessive mutation of the ob locus results in a phenotype of profound, early-onset obesity, hyperphagia, decreased energy expenditure, and type II diabetes, closely resembling the severe, morbid obesity syndrome seen in humans.

Classical physiological experiments, including parabiosis studies in rodents, have suggested that the regulation of energy balance involves a feedback loop between the central nervous system and peripheral tissues. According to the lipostasis theory, a circulating factor produced by adipose tissue acts on the hypothalamus to regulate food intake and modulate energy expenditure, effectively signaling the size of the body fat depot to the brain. The profound phenotypic defects observed in ob/ob mice led to the hypothesis that the ob gene encodes this elusive satiety factor, or a critical component of its signaling pathway. Despite extensive study of the physiological and metabolic abnormalities in ob/ob mice, the biochemical nature of the ob gene product and its exact molecular function have remained unknown.

To elucidate the molecular basis of this obesity syndrome, we undertook a positional cloning strategy to isolate the ob gene. In this paper, we describe the high-resolution genetic and physical mapping of the ob locus on mouse chromosome 6. Through the construction of yeast artificial chromosome (YAC) and P1 bacteriophage contigs, followed by exon trapping, we successfully identified the ob gene. We demonstrate that the ob gene is expressed exclusively in white adipose tissue and encodes a highly conserved secreted protein. Furthermore, we identify the specific mutations responsible for the obese phenotype in two independent mutant strains, establishing that this secreted molecule is indeed the product of the ob gene.

The discovery of leptin was one of the most consequential events in modern endocrinology and metabolism. It dismantled the long-held assumption that fat was merely inert storage, permanently elevating adipose tissue to the status of an active, highly critical endocrine organ. It delivered the first definitive molecular mechanism for the regulation of body weight and appetite, profoundly directing decades of subsequent research into obesity, metabolism, and eating disorders.

paper_title: Positional Cloning of the Mouse Obese Gene and Its Human Homologue - 1994 - Nature
display_title: Positional Cloning of the Mouse Obese Gene and Its Human Homologue
title: Positional Cloning of the Mouse Obese Gene and Its Human Homologue
text:
Obesity has been a focus of discussion, with a line of research dating back to Lavoisier and Laplace (1783) implying that energy balance-food intake versus energy output-is physiologically regulated. The site of this regulation has been the subject of nearly one hundred years of debate. Of the brain regions implicated in the regulation of feeding behaviour, the ventromedial nucleus of the hypothalamus (VMH) is considered to be the most important satiety centre in the central nervous system (CNS). The increase in body weight associated with VMH lesions is a result of both increased food intake and decreased energy expenditure.
The energy balance in mammals was therefore postulated to be controlled by a feedback loop in which the amount of stored energy is sensed by the hypothalamus, which adjusts food intake and energy expenditure to maintain a constant body weight. The nature of the inputs to the hypothalamus was unclear. According to the lipostasis theory, the size of the body fat depot is regulated by the CNS, with a product of fat metabolism circulating in plasma and affecting energy balance by interacting with the hypothalamus. The glucostasis theory suggested that the plasma glucose level is a key signal regulating energy stores. A third possibility is that body temperature is an important input to the CNS centres controlling food intake. The inability to identify the putative signal from fat has hindered the validation of the lipostasis theory. Moreover, neither the glucostasis theory nor theories on the thermal regulation of food intake fully account for the precision with which energy balance is regulated in vivo. The possibility that at least one component of the signalling system circulates in the bloodstream was first suggested by Hervey, who showed that the transfer of blood from an animal with a VMH lesion across a vascular graft to an untreated animal (a parabiosis experiment) resulted in a reduction of food intake in the intact animal. The biochemical nature of the putative signal has remained elusive, although it has been suggested that ob is responsible for the generation of this blood-borne factor.
Our results, particularly the evidence that the ob protein product is secreted, suggest that ob may encode this circulating factor. Evidence that the gene described here is ob includes the identification of a nonsense mutation in the C57BL/6J ob/ob strain and a genomic alteration in the SM/Ckc-Dac ob2J/ob2J strain that is associated with an absence of RNA. The data showing that one strain overproduces ob RNA while the other fails to express this gene, make it unlikely that these changes are secondary. This conclusion can be confirmed using transgenic mice to complement the obese mutation. The nature of the mutation in the co-isogenic SM/Ckc-Dac ob2J/ob2J mice is as yet unknown. Preliminary analysis of the gene structure has indicated that the polymorphic BglII site maps ~7 kb upstream of the promoter. As this mutation is co-isogenic, the data strongly suggest that this polymorphism is associated with the mutation, although it is not yet clear whether there is a structural alteration (insertion or deletion) or base change. Further studies will be required to identify the molecular basis for the absence of ob RNA expression in SM/Ckc-Dac ob2J/ob2J mice, as well as the molecular mechanisms that result in the increased expression of ob RNA in C57BL/6J ob/ob mice.
The increased level of ob RNA in the C57BL/6J mutant, which has greatly increased fat cell mass, raises the possibility that the level of expression of this gene signals the size of the adipose depot. This hypothesis is consistent with the lipostasis theory, implying that an increase in the level of the ob signal (as might occur after a prolonged binge of eating) may act directly or indirectly on the CNS to inhibit food intake and/or regulate energy expenditure as part of a homeostatic mechanism to maintain constancy of the adipose mass. Such effects could be explained if the ob protein activated the sympathetic nervous system via interactions with the VMH. Direct effects of ob on the CNS would require that mechanisms exist to allow its passage across the blood-brain barrier. The means by which the CNS effects changes in body weight remain to be elucidated and may include mechanisms independent of effects on food intake and energy expenditure. In addition, ob may have endocrine, paracrine and autocrine effects on different tissues. These and other possibilities will be testable if the active form of the ob protein can be shown to circulate in plasma.
The ob mutation is associated with a myriad of hormonal and metabolic alterations. These include effects on thermoregulation, fertility, adrenal and thyroid function as well as a wide range of biochemical abnormalities. Although we cannot exclude the possibility that many of these pleiotypic effects are the result of the expression of ob in sites not included in our initial survey of mouse tissues, the apparent expression of ob in only adipose tissue suggests that many of these changes are secondary. It has been suggested that the complex ob phenotype reflects an imbalance in the activity of the autonomic nervous system with low sympathetic and high parasympathetic tone. If ob is a signal molecule that regulates body weight, it may act by interacting with CNS (and other) receptors to modulate food intake and the activity of the autonomic nervous system. Parabiosis experiments suggest that the ob receptor is encoded by the mouse db (diabetes) gene, a possibility that can be tested by positional cloning of db or by expression cloning and mapping of the ob receptor.
The extensive homology of the ob gene product among vertebrates suggests that its function is highly conserved. Now that the human homologue of ob is cloned, it will be possible to test for mutations in the human ob gene. As ob heterozygotes have an enhanced ability to survive a prolonged fast, heterozygous mutations at ob may provide a selective advantage in human populations subjected to caloric deprivation. Identification of ob now offers an entry point into the pathways that regulate adiposity and body weight and should provide a fuller understanding of the pathogenesis of obesity.
historical_context: In 1994, Jeffrey Friedman, Yiying Zhang, and colleagues executed a monumental feat by positionally cloning the *obese* (*ob*) gene in mice and successfully identifying its human equivalent. The gene encoded a completely novel hormone they dubbed "leptin," which is secreted by adipose tissue to communicate satiety to the brain. The discovery of leptin was one of the most consequential events in modern endocrinology and metabolism. It dismantled the long-held assumption that fat was merely inert storage, permanently elevating adipose tissue to the status of an active, highly critical endocrine organ. It delivered the first definitive molecular mechanism for the regulation of body weight and appetite, profoundly directing decades of subsequent research into obesity, metabolism, and eating disorders.

paper_title: Positional Cloning of the Mouse Obese Gene and Its Human Homologue - 1994 - Nature
display_title: Positional Cloning of the Mouse Obese Gene and Its Human Homologue
title: Positional Cloning of the Mouse Obese Gene and Its Human Homologue
text:
The identification of the ob gene provides direct molecular evidence for a physiological pathway regulating body weight. The obese phenotype in the mouse has long suggested that food intake and energy expenditure are not determined solely by behaviour, but are subject to genetic and metabolic control. The data reported here indicate that ob encodes an adipose-tissue RNA whose product is likely to be secreted, and that mutations affecting either the coding sequence or expression of this gene result in profound obesity. These findings are consistent with the hypothesis that adipose tissue produces a circulating signal that acts to regulate the size of the body fat depot.

Several observations support the conclusion that the adipose RNA identified by exon trapping is the ob transcript. First, the gene maps within the genetic interval defined by recombination in more than 1,600 meioses segregating the ob mutation. Secondly, its expression is highly enriched in white adipose tissue, the tissue in which such a signal might be expected to originate if it reflected the size or metabolic state of fat stores. Thirdly, the transcript is absent in adipose tissue from SM/Ckc-Dac ob2J/ob2J mice, a co-isogenic strain in which the mutation is closely associated with a genomic alteration upstream of the mRNA start site. Finally, in the original C57BL/6J ob/ob strain, the coding region contains a nonsense mutation that would truncate the predicted protein before the second conserved cysteine. The occurrence of independent lesions in the same gene in two phenotypically similar mutant strains provides strong genetic evidence that this gene is ob.

The nature of the predicted protein is of particular interest. The open reading frame encodes a small, largely hydrophilic polypeptide with an amino-terminal signal sequence. In vitro translation in the presence of microsomal membranes produced a processed, protease-protected form, indicating that the signal sequence can mediate translocation into the microsomal lumen. The absence of other membrane-spanning regions, together with the hydrophilic sequence and the conservation of cysteine residues in the mature protein, is compatible with the idea that ob is secreted. Although direct demonstration of secretion from adipocytes and identification of the circulating protein remain to be established, the structure of the predicted product fits well with previous physiological experiments in which parabiosis suggested that ob/ob mice lack a humoral factor.

The marked increase of ob RNA in C57BL/6J ob/ob adipose tissue suggests that expression of the gene is subject to regulation by metabolic state or by its own product. Because the mutant transcript encodes a truncated and presumably inactive protein, increased RNA abundance may reflect a feedback response to the absence of functional ob activity. Alternatively, the elevated expression could be secondary to the increased mass, altered cellular composition, or changed endocrine state of adipose tissue in the obese animal. Distinguishing among these possibilities will require analysis of ob RNA and protein during development of obesity, after nutritional manipulation, and in other genetic models. Nonetheless, the difference between the coding mutation in C57BL/6J ob/ob mice and the apparent absence of RNA in ob2J/ob2J mice indicates that loss of ob function can occur by more than one molecular mechanism.

The apparent adipose specificity of expression argues that adipose tissue is not merely a passive site of energy storage, but can participate actively in the regulation of whole-body metabolism. A simple model is that the ob product is produced in proportion to adipose mass and acts at a distant site to reduce food intake, increase energy expenditure, or both. In the absence of this signal, the organism would behave as though fat stores were depleted, leading to hyperphagia, reduced energy expenditure, and progressive obesity. The endocrine and neural targets of the ob product are not identified by the present experiments. The central nervous system is an attractive candidate because feeding behaviour and autonomic output are centrally controlled, but peripheral sites involved in nutrient utilization may also respond to the factor. The isolation of the gene now makes it possible to test these alternatives directly.

The high degree of conservation between the mouse and human proteins indicates that the ob gene has an important vertebrate function. The predicted mature proteins are 84% identical, with particularly strong conservation near the amino terminus and around the cysteine residues. This conservation is especially striking because the untranslated regions show much less similarity, suggesting selective pressure on the protein sequence itself. The presence of related sequences in vertebrate genomic DNA further implies that the pathway in which ob participates is ancient. The isolation of the human homologue raises the possibility that variation in this gene, or in genes encoding its receptor or downstream effectors, may contribute to human obesity. Because most human obesity is probably polygenic and environmentally influenced, defects identical to those in the mouse need not be common to be informative.

The positional cloning strategy used here illustrates the utility of combining high-resolution genetic mapping, physical contig construction and exon trapping for the isolation of mammalian disease genes. The ob locus was initially defined only by phenotype, and no prior knowledge of the biochemical defect was available. By reducing the candidate interval and screening genomic clones for expressed sequences, we were able to identify a transcript whose expression pattern and mutant lesions revealed its identity. The success of this approach should encourage the isolation of other mouse obesity genes and of genes underlying complex physiological traits in which candidate-gene approaches have been limited.

There are several limitations to the present study. We have not yet shown that the ob protein circulates in vivo, that recombinant protein can correct the phenotype of ob/ob mice, or that the human homologue has an equivalent biological activity. The site of action of the protein, its receptor, and the pathways through which it alters feeding and metabolism remain unknown. It is also possible that the ob product influences several processes, including pancreatic islet function, insulin sensitivity, thermogenesis and reproductive physiology, all of which are disturbed in ob/ob mice. These questions can now be addressed using the cloned cDNAs, antibodies to the predicted protein, transgenic expression and administration of recombinant protein.

In summary, the cloning of ob identifies a conserved adipose-derived molecule whose loss produces severe obesity and diabetes in the mouse. These results provide molecular support for the concept that the adipose depot communicates with other tissues to maintain energy balance. The ob gene product may therefore represent a component of a feedback loop that monitors fat stores and coordinates nutrient intake and expenditure. Further characterization of this pathway should clarify the biological basis of obesity and may provide new approaches to the diagnosis and treatment of metabolic disease.

paper_title: Positional Cloning of the Mouse Obese Gene and Its Human Homologue - 1994 - Nature
display_title: Positional Cloning of the Mouse Obese Gene and Its Human Homologue
title: Positional Cloning of the Mouse Obese Gene and Its Human Homologue
text:
The successful positional cloning of the mouse *obese* (*ob*) gene and its human homologue represents a significant milestone in our understanding of the molecular basis of energy homeostasis. For decades, the physiological regulation of body weight has been suggested by classical experiments, yet the specific components of the underlying signaling pathways remained elusive. The isolation of *ob* provides the first definitive evidence of a molecular link between adipose tissue and the central nervous system, effectively moving the study of obesity from the realm of descriptive physiology into the modern era of molecular genetics.

The technical success of this positional cloning effort—utilizing a massive genetic cross of 1,606 meioses and a 2.2-megabase physical map of YAC and P1 clones—demonstrates the power of this "forward genetics" approach to identify genes based solely on their mutant phenotype. Because the primary biochemical defect in *ob* mice was unknown, conventional biochemical approaches to isolate the gene product were unsuccessful. By pinpointing the *ob* locus on chromosome 6 and utilizing exon trapping to isolate candidate genes within the 650-kilobase critical region, we have identified a novel, adipose-tissue-specific transcript that is fundamentally disrupted in two independent mutant mouse strains.

The most striking feature of the *ob* gene is its highly restricted expression pattern. Both Northern blot analysis and high-sensitivity RT-PCR demonstrate that *ob* mRNA is expressed exclusively in white adipose tissue. This tissue specificity strongly suggests that the *ob* gene product functions as a sensor for the size of the body's fat depots. The discovery that *ob* encodes a 167-amino-acid protein with a functional N-terminal signal sequence further supports the hypothesis that it is a secreted hormone. Our in vitro translation experiments with microsomal membranes provide clear evidence of translocation and signal-sequence cleavage, resulting in a processed protein of approximately 16 kilodaltons. These findings are perfectly consistent with earlier parabiosis (cross-circulation) experiments, which suggested that *ob/ob* mice are deficient in a circulating factor that normally regulates food intake and body weight.

The characterization of the *ob* mutations in different mouse strains provides deep insight into the gene's function and its place within a homeostatic feedback loop. In the original C57BL/6J *ob/ob* strain, we identified a nonsense mutation at arginine 105. This premature stop codon presumably results in a truncated, non-functional protein. Notably, we observed a twenty-fold increase in *ob* mRNA levels in the adipose tissue of these mice. This massive upregulation suggests the existence of a compensatory feedback mechanism: in the absence of a functional *ob* signal, the adipose tissue attempts to normalize energy balance by overproducing the transcript. In contrast, the *ob2J* strain fails to synthesize any *ob* RNA, likely due to a promoter mutation or large structural rearrangement. The identical phenotype of these two strains—profound obesity, hyperphagia, and type II diabetes—confirms that the absence of the *ob* protein is the primary cause of the morbid obesity syndrome.

The high degree of evolutionary conservation of the *ob* gene across vertebrates is another remarkable finding. The 84% amino-acid identity between the mouse and human proteins implies that the physiological system regulating energy balance has been subject to intense selective pressure throughout vertebrate evolution. The ability to store energy efficiently as fat and to regulate those stores according to environmental availability was undoubtedly a critical survival advantage during periods of famine. The conservation of the human *ob* gene suggests that the same "adipostat" mechanism discovered in mice is also at work in our own species.

The identification of *ob* immediately raises several critical questions regarding human obesity. While the *ob/ob* mouse is a model of monogenic, recessive obesity, human morbid obesity is typically more complex and polygenic. Nevertheless, the *ob* gene provides a molecular entry point into this complexity. It is now possible to determine whether certain cases of human obesity are caused by mutations in the *ob* gene itself or by defects in the regulation of its expression. Furthermore, the secretion of the *ob* protein suggests that its primary site of action is likely the brain, specifically the hypothalamus, which is known to coordinate appetite and metabolic rate. This implies the existence of a specific *ob* receptor, which may correspond to another of the known mouse obesity mutations, such as *diabetes* (*db*).

Historically, the search for the *ob* gene has been guided by the work of researchers like Douglas Coleman, whose parabiosis experiments in the 1970s accurately predicted the nature of the *ob* and *db* defects. Our molecular cloning of *ob* confirms his insightful hypothesis: *ob* encodes the signal, and *db* likely encodes the receiver. The isolation of the *ob* gene product now allows for the production of recombinant protein, which will be essential for testing its therapeutic potential. If the administration of the *ob* protein can normalize the weight and metabolic abnormalities of *ob/ob* mice, it will represent a new paradigm for treating obesity—protein replacement therapy for a hormonal deficiency.

In conclusion, the positional cloning of the *ob* gene has transformed our understanding of body weight regulation. By identifying a secreted factor that is produced specifically by fat cells and whose absence leads to profound obesity, we have defined the first molecular component of the system that balances energy intake and expenditure. This discovery not only provides a long-awaited explanation for the *ob* phenotype in mice but also establishes a new framework for investigating the pathogenesis and treatment of human obesity. The high conservation of the gene and its elegant feedback regulation suggest that we have uncovered a fundamental and ancient biological system for the management of energy reserves.

paper_title: Potent and Specific Genetic Interference by Double-Stranded RNA in Caenorhabditis elegans - 1998 - Nature
display_title: Potent and Specific Genetic Interference by Double-Stranded RNA in Caenorhabditis elegans
title: Potent and Specific Genetic Interference by Double-Stranded RNA in Caenorhabditis elegans
text: Experimental introduction of RNA into cells can be used in certain biological systems to interfere with the function of an endogenous gene. Such effects have been proposed to result from a simple antisense mechanism that depends on hybridization between the injected RNA and endogenous messenger RNA transcripts. RNA interference has been used in the nematode Caenorhabditis elegans to manipulate gene expression. Here we investigate the requirements for structure and delivery of the interfering RNA. To our surprise, we found that double-stranded RNA was substantially more effective at producing interference than was either strand individually. After injection into adult animals, purified single strands had at most a modest effect, whereas double-stranded mixtures caused potent and specific interference. The effects of this interference were evident in both the injected animals and their progeny. Only a few molecules of injected double-stranded RNA were required per affected cell, arguing against stoichiometric interference with endogenous mRNA and suggesting that there could be a catalytic or amplification component in the interference process.
historical_context: In 1998, Andrew Fire, Craig Mello, and colleagues published the seminal discovery of RNA interference (RNAi). They showcased that injecting double-stranded RNA (dsRNA) into the nematode *C. elegans* provoked extremely potent, highly specific silencing of the corresponding gene, vastly outperforming previous single-stranded antisense RNA techniques. This seismic discovery resulted in Fire and Mello securing the 2006 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine. The Nobel committee celebrated their discovery of RNA interference—gene silencing executed by double-stranded RNA. This paper overhauled modern biology by unearthing a fundamental, highly conserved cellular mechanism for gene regulation and viral defense, while simultaneously equipping researchers with a simple, wildly effective tool to knock down gene expression to interrogate gene function on a genome-wide scale.

paper_title: Potent and Specific Genetic Interference by Double-Stranded RNA in Caenorhabditis elegans - 1998 - Nature
display_title: Potent and Specific Genetic Interference by Double-Stranded RNA in Caenorhabditis elegans
title: Potent and Specific Genetic Interference by Double-Stranded RNA in Caenorhabditis elegans
text: RNA-mediated interference in Caenorhabditis elegans has provided a powerful means of reducing gene activity, but the nature of the active interfering molecule has remained unclear. We show here that double-stranded RNA is a potent and specific trigger of genetic interference. Purified sense or antisense strands corresponding to unc-22 had little activity, whereas annealed sense-antisense RNA, or gel-purified double-stranded RNA, produced strong loss-of-function phenotypes at much lower doses. Interference required sequence homology within the double-stranded region and was not enhanced by unrelated double-stranded RNA, indicating that the effect is specific rather than a generalized response to RNA duplexes. Double-stranded RNAs corresponding to unc-54, fem-1, hlh-1 and gfp likewise produced phenotypes or reporter losses expected from inhibition of the target genes. Interference was accompanied by loss of endogenous mex-3 RNA and could spread from the site of injection into germline and somatic tissues. These results identify double-stranded RNA as an efficient, sequence-specific agent of gene silencing in C. elegans.

The Nobel committee celebrated their discovery of RNA interference—gene silencing executed by double-stranded RNA. title: Potent and Specific Genetic Interference by Double-Stranded RNA in Caenorhabditis elegans
text: Experimental introduction of RNA into cells can be used to inhibit the function of endogenous genes in Caenorhabditis elegans. Although the mechanism of this interference is not understood, two puzzling features have been observed: both sense and antisense RNA preparations are sufficient to cause interference, and these effects persist well into the next generation. We report here that double-stranded RNA is substantially more effective at producing genetic interference than either single strand. Purified single strands had only marginal activity, whereas double-stranded mixtures were at least two orders of magnitude more potent. Effects of injected double-stranded RNA were gene-specific, mimicking the phenotype of the corresponding loss-of-function mutation, and could cross cellular boundaries, with injection into the body cavity producing interference in progeny and in somatic tissues. Injection of double-stranded RNA also resulted in a marked decrease in the endogenous messenger RNA. These results indicate that double-stranded RNA could target a specific class of cellular events, and provide a powerful tool for the analysis of gene function in C. elegans and, potentially, in other organisms.

paper_title: Potent and Specific Genetic Interference by Double-Stranded RNA in Caenorhabditis elegans - 1998 - Nature
display_title: Potent and Specific Genetic Interference by Double-Stranded RNA in Caenorhabditis elegans
title: Potent and Specific Genetic Interference by Double-Stranded RNA in Caenorhabditis elegans
text: The phenomenon of RNA interference (RNAi) in Caenorhabditis elegans has been poorly understood, particularly regarding the observation that both sense and antisense RNA preparations can induce gene silencing. Here, we demonstrate that double-stranded RNA (dsRNA) is actually the potent trigger for this specific genetic interference. We found that injecting a mixture of sense and antisense RNA, or purified dsRNA, is at least two orders of magnitude more effective than injecting either single strand alone. The interference is highly specific to the targeted gene, as demonstrated by the precise phenocopying of null mutations for several genes, including unc-22, unc-54, fem-1, and hlh-1. Furthermore, we show that dsRNA injection leads to a drastic reduction in the corresponding endogenous mRNA levels. Remarkably, this dsRNA-mediated interference can cross cellular boundaries, spreading from the site of injection to affect somatic tissues and even the germline, resulting in phenotypic alterations in the subsequent generation. These findings indicate that dsRNA is a powerful and specific tool for functional genomics, revealing a previously unappreciated mechanism for gene silencing that may be widely conserved among eukaryotes.

title: Potent and Specific Genetic Interference by Double-Stranded RNA in Caenorhabditis elegans
text: Despite the usefulness of RNA interference in C. elegans, two features of the process have been difficult to explain. First, sense and antisense RNA preparations are each sufficient to cause interference. Second, interference effects can persist well into the next generation, even though many endogenous RNA transcripts are rapidly degraded in the early embryo. These results indicate a fundamental difference in behaviour between native RNAs (for example, mRNAs) and the molecules responsible for interference. We sought to test the possibility that this contrast reflects an underlying difference in RNA structure. RNA populations to be injected are generally prepared using bacteriophage RNA polymerases. These polymerases, although highly specific, produce some random or ectopic transcripts. DNA transgene arrays also produce a fraction of aberrant RNA products. From these facts, we surmised that the interfering RNA populations might include some molecules with double-stranded character. To test whether double-stranded character might contribute to interference, we further purified single-stranded RNAs and compared interference activities of individual strands with the activity of a deliberately prepared double-stranded hybrid.

text: Experimental inhibition of gene expression by introduced RNA has provided a useful, though incompletely understood, method for analysing gene function. We therefore examined the structural requirements, potency and specificity of RNA-mediated interference in C. elegans. In particular, we compared purified sense, antisense and double-stranded RNA species corresponding to several genes with known mutant phenotypes.

text: The experimental introduction of RNA into living cells has emerged as a valuable technique in certain biological systems to intentionally interfere with the function of a specific endogenous gene. Traditionally, such specific gene-silencing effects have been presumed to result from a straightforward antisense mechanism, which relies on the stoichiometric hybridization between the exogenously injected single-stranded antisense RNA and the complementary endogenous messenger RNA (mRNA) transcripts, thereby preventing translation. This phenomenon of RNA interference has been widely utilized in the nematode Caenorhabditis elegans to experimentally manipulate gene expression and deduce gene function. In this study, we systematically investigate the structural and delivery requirements of the interfering RNA molecules in C. elegans. To our surprise, we discovered that purified double-stranded RNA (dsRNA) was substantially more effective at producing targeted genetic interference than either the sense or antisense strand individually. Moreover, we found that only a few molecules of injected dsRNA were required per affected cell to achieve robust silencing. This remarkable potency argues strongly against a simple stoichiometric antisense mechanism and instead suggests the existence of a catalytic or amplification component inherent to the dsRNA-mediated interference process.

title: Potent and Specific Genetic Interference by Double-Stranded RNA in Caenorhabditis elegans
text:
The use of dsRNA injection adds to the tools available for studying gene function in C. elegans. In particular, it should now be possible functionally to analyse many interesting coding regions for which no specific function has been defined. Although the effects of dsRNA-mediated interference are potent and specific we have observed several limitations that should be taken into account when designing RNA-interference-based experiments. First, a sequence shared between several closely related genes may interfere with several members of the gene family. Second, it is likely that a low level of expression will resist RNA-mediated interference for some or all genes, and that a small number of cells will likewise escape these effects.

Genetic tools are available for only a few organisms. Double-stranded RNA could conceivably mediate interference more generally in other nematodes, in other invertebrates, and, potentially, in vertebrates. RNA interference might also operate in plants: several studies have suggested that inverted-repeat structures or characteristics of dsRNA viruses are involved in transgene-dependent co-suppression in plants.

There are several possible mechanisms for RNA interference in C. elegans. A simple antisense model is not likely: annealing between a few injected RNA molecules and excess endogenous transcripts would not be expected to yield observable phenotypes. RNA-targeted processes cannot, however, be ruled out, as they could include a catalytic component. Alternatively, direct RNA-mediated interference at the level of chromatin structure or transcription could be involved. Interactions between RNA and the genome, combined with propagation of changes along chromatin, have been proposed in mammalian X-chromosome inactivation and plant-gene co-suppression. If RNA interference in C. elegans works by such a mechanism, it would be new in targeting regions of the template that are present in the final mRNA (as we observed no phenotypic interference using intron or promoter sequences). Whatever their target, the mechanisms underlying RNA interference probably exist for a biological purpose. Genetic interference by dsRNA could be used by the organism for physiological gene silencing. Likewise, the ability of dsRNA to work at a distance from the site of injection, and particularly to move into both germline and muscle cells, suggests that there is an effective RNA-transport mechanism in C. elegans.

The Nobel committee celebrated their discovery of RNA interference—gene silencing executed by double-stranded RNA. This paper overhauled modern biology by unearthing a fundamental, highly conserved cellular mechanism for gene regulation and viral defense, while simultaneously equipping researchers with a simple, wildly effective tool to knock down gene expression to interrogate gene function on a genome-wide scale.

title: Potent and Specific Genetic Interference by Double-Stranded RNA in Caenorhabditis elegans
text:
These experiments identify double-stranded RNA as the active trigger for RNA-mediated genetic interference in C. elegans. The striking difference between purified single strands and the corresponding sense-antisense duplex argues that previous activities attributed to sense or antisense RNA may have resulted, at least in part, from small amounts of double-stranded material present in nominally single-stranded preparations. The requirement for homology between the duplex and the affected gene, together with the failure of unrelated dsRNA to potentiate interference, distinguishes this process from a general response to foreign or double-stranded RNA.

The potency of dsRNA-mediated interference is particularly notable. In several experiments, the number of injected RNA molecules was far below the number of endogenous target transcripts that would be present in the affected cells. Moreover, interference could persist after extensive cell division and was transmitted to progeny derived from injected animals. These observations make a simple stoichiometric model, in which injected RNA directly binds and inactivates each target mRNA molecule, unlikely. Instead, the data suggest that dsRNA initiates a process with a catalytic, regenerative, or otherwise amplified component. Such a process could act by promoting degradation of homologous transcripts, by preventing their accumulation, or by affecting the gene or its transcript through an intermediate molecule generated from the injected duplex.

Our observations favour an effect on RNA, although they do not yet define the precise target. Duplexes corresponding to introns or promoter sequences failed to produce detectable interference, whereas dsRNA corresponding to exonic sequence caused a marked reduction in endogenous mex-3 mRNA. These results are most simply explained by post-transcriptional elimination of homologous RNA. Nonetheless, the possibility of effects at the level of the gene, or of nascent transcripts, remains open.

The ability of dsRNA to move from the site of injection to distant tissues and to affect both somatic cells and germline-derived progeny indicates that C. elegans can distribute an interfering signal between cells. The mosaic patterns seen with gfp interference further suggest that delivery, persistence, or amplification of this signal varies with developmental history and tissue type. Thus, dsRNA-mediated interference provides both a powerful experimental method for reducing gene function and a biological phenomenon with unexpected specificity, potency and systemic character.

title: Potent and Specific Genetic Interference by Double-Stranded RNA in Caenorhabditis elegans
text:
Our study identifies double-stranded RNA (dsRNA) as the primary and most potent trigger for specific genetic interference in *C. elegans*. This discovery provides a definitive explanation for the long-standing observation that both sense and antisense RNA preparations can mediate gene silencing; we conclude that the trace amounts of dsRNA contaminating those earlier preparations were the actual interfering agents. The finding that purified single strands possess at most a marginal effect, while a deliberately annealed dsRNA hybrid is at least two orders of magnitude more effective, highlights a fundamental biological distinction in how the cellular machinery recognizes and responds to double-stranded RNA structures.

The characteristics of this interference process strongly suggest that it does not operate through a simple stoichiometric antisense mechanism. The extreme potency of the effect—whereby a few molecules of injected dsRNA are sufficient to silence thousands of copies of endogenous mRNA—argues for the existence of an underlying catalytic or amplification component. Furthermore, the observation that dsRNA causes the near-total elimination of target endogenous mRNA, as demonstrated by our *mex-3* in situ hybridization experiments, identifies the post-transcriptional stability of mRNA as the primary target of the interference pathway. This is further supported by the lack of interference observed when using dsRNA corresponding to non-coding intron or promoter sequences.

One of the most remarkable features of this phenomenon is its systemic nature. The ability of the interference signal to cross cellular boundaries, moving from the site of injection in the body cavity to affect somatic tissues in the progeny, implies an active mechanism for the transport and propagation of the dsRNA signal. Such a robust response, which can persist well into the next generation, suggests that *C. elegans* possesses a specialized and highly efficient biological system for managing dsRNA-mediated information.

While the natural physiological role of this pathway—perhaps in viral immunity or the regulation of mobile genetic elements—remains to be elucidated, its utility as an experimental tool is profound. The ability to precisely and potently knock down specific gene functions will undoubtedly accelerate the study of gene function and genetic networks. Future investigations must focus on identifying the molecular components responsible for detecting, amplifying, and executing this targeted mRNA degradation.

paper_title: Programmable Editing of a Target Base in Genomic DNA without Double-Stranded DNA Cleavage - 2016 - Nature
display_title: Programmable Editing of a Target Base in Genomic DNA without Double-Stranded DNA Cleavage
title: Programmable Editing of a Target Base in Genomic DNA without Double-Stranded DNA Cleavage
text: Current genome-editing technologies introduce double-stranded (ds) DNA breaks at a target locus as the first step to gene correction. Although most genetic diseases arise from point mutations, current approaches to point mutation correction are inefficient and typically induce an abundance of random insertions and deletions (indels) at the target locus from the cellular response to dsDNA breaks. Here we report the development of base editing, a new approach to genome editing that enables the direct, irreversible conversion of one target DNA base into another in a programmable manner, without requiring dsDNA backbone cleavage or a donor template. We engineered fusions of CRISPR/Cas9 and a cytidine deaminase enzyme that retain the ability to be programmed with a guide RNA, do not induce dsDNA breaks, and mediate the direct conversion of cytidine to uridine, thereby effecting a C → T (or G → A) substitution. The resulting 'base editors' convert cytidines within a window of approximately five nucleotides (nt), and can efficiently correct a variety of point mutations relevant to human disease. In four transformed human and murine cell lines, second- and third-generation base editors that fuse uracil glycosylase inhibitor (UGI), and that use a Cas9 nickase targeting the non-edited strand, manipulate the cellular DNA repair response to favor desired base-editing outcomes, resulting in permanent correction of ~ 15-75% of total cellular DNA with minimal (typically ≤ 1%) indel formation. Base editing expands the scope and efficiency of genome editing of point mutations.
historical_context: In 2016, Alexis Komor, David Liu, and colleagues debuted a revolutionary leap in genome engineering known as "base editing." By engineering a fusion between a catalytically impaired Cas9 (incapable of creating double-strand breaks) and a cytidine deaminase enzyme, they built a molecular machine capable of directly and seamlessly converting a C-G base pair into a T-A base pair at a highly specific genomic locus. This paper entirely reshaped the CRISPR field. It brilliantly sidestepped a major limitation of standard CRISPR-Cas9, which relies heavily on messy, error-prone cellular repair mechanisms following a double-strand break. Base editing granted the ability to make precise, clean point mutation corrections without triggering widespread genomic damage, forging realistic, inherently safer therapeutic pathways for correcting the precise point mutations responsible for the vast majority of human genetic diseases.

title: Programmable Editing of a Target Base in Genomic DNA without Double-Stranded DNA Cleavage
text: Current genome editing methods typically introduce double-stranded DNA breaks (DSBs) at target loci, which are repaired by non-homologous end joining or, less efficiently, by homology-directed repair in the presence of a donor template. Although powerful, these processes often generate stochastic insertions or deletions and are poorly suited for the precise correction of point mutations, which underlie many genetic diseases. Here we describe programmable base editing, a strategy for the direct conversion of one DNA base pair into another in genomic DNA without requiring DSBs or donor DNA. We fused a cytidine deaminase to catalytically impaired Cas9 to create an RNA-programmed enzyme that converts cytosine to uracil within the single-stranded DNA bubble generated by Cas9 binding. Optimization of deaminase identity, fusion geometry, and linker length yielded a first-generation base editor that efficiently deaminated target cytosines in vitro. Fusion to uracil DNA glycosylase inhibitor improved editing in human cells, and nicking of the non-edited strand further increased product formation by directing cellular repair of the U:G intermediate. The resulting base editors mediate targeted C to T conversion in mammalian cells with efficiencies up to 37% at endogenous human genomic loci, while producing far fewer indels than Cas9-mediated homology-directed repair. Off-target base editing was confined to a subset of known Cas9 off-target sites and was not accompanied by detectable untargeted C to T mutagenesis near the examined loci. We further demonstrate efficient correction of disease-relevant APOE4 and TP53 mutations in mammalian cells. These findings establish base editing as a programmable approach for precise single-nucleotide genome modification.

text: Current CRISPR/Cas9 genome editing technologies primarily rely on the creation of double-stranded DNA breaks (DSBs), which frequently lead to stochastic insertions or deletions (indels) via non-homologous end joining. For correcting point mutations underlying many genetic diseases, precise base conversion without DSB-induced indels is highly desirable. Here, we describe the development of "base editors," a novel class of engineered proteins capable of direct, programmable conversion of one target DNA base into another without requiring DSBs or donor templates. We constructed these editors by fusing a catalytically inactive Cas9 (dCas9) to a cytidine deaminase enzyme, which catalyzes the conversion of cytosine (C) to uracil (U) within a narrow window of the single-stranded DNA bubble formed by the Cas9-guide RNA complex. To overcome the cellular base-excision repair response to the resulting U:G mismatch, we tethered a uracil DNA glycosylase inhibitor (UGI) to the complex, substantially increasing base editing efficiency in human cells. Finally, to manipulate mismatch repair and favor the desired U:A resolution, we restored nickase activity to the Cas9 domain, directing the cell to replace the unedited G-containing strand. This third-generation base editor (BE3) mediates highly efficient (up to 37%) and specific C-to-T (or G-to-A) conversions at targeted genomic loci in human and mouse cells, with minimal (<1%) indel formation. We demonstrate the utility of this approach by efficiently correcting disease-relevant point mutations, including the APOE4 Alzheimer's risk variant and a dominant-negative p53 mutation. Programmable base editing expands the scope of precision genome engineering for research and therapeutic applications.

title: Programmable Editing of a Target Base in Genomic DNA without Double-Stranded DNA Cleavage
text:
The clustered regularly interspaced short palindromic repeat (CRISPR) system has been widely used to mediate genome editing in a variety of organisms and cell lines. CRISPR/Cas9 protein-RNA complexes localize to a target DNA sequence through base pairing with a guide RNA, and natively create a dsDNA break (DSB) at the locus specified by the guide RNA. In response to DSBs, cellular DNA repair processes mostly result in random insertions or deletions (indels) at the site of DNA cleavage through non-homologous end joining (NHEJ). In the presence of a homologous DNA template, the DNA surrounding the cleavage site can be replaced through homology-directed repair (HDR). HDR competes with NHEJ during the resolution of DSBs, and indels are generally more abundant outcomes than gene replacement. For most known genetic diseases, however, correction of a point mutation in the target locus, rather than stochastic disruption of the gene, is needed to study or address the underlying cause of the disease.

Motivated by this need, researchers have sought to increase the efficiency of HDR and suppress NHEJ. Despite recent progress, current strategies to correct point mutations using HDR under therapeutically relevant conditions remain inefficient (typically ~ 0.1 to 5%), especially in unmodified, non-dividing cells. These observations highlight the need to develop alternative approaches to correct point mutations in genomic DNA that do not require DSBs.

We envisioned that direct conversion of one DNA base to another at a programmable target locus without requiring DSBs could increase the efficiency of gene correction relative to HDR without introducing an excess of random indels. Catalytically-dead Cas9 (dCas9), which contains Asp10Ala and His840Ala mutations that inactivate its nuclease activity, retains its ability to bind DNA in a guide RNA-programmed manner but does not cleave the DNA backbone. In principle, conjugation of dCas9 with an enzymatic or chemical catalyst that mediates the direct conversion of one base to another could enable RNA-programmed DNA base editing.

The deamination of cytosine (C) is catalyzed by cytidine deaminases and results in uracil (U), which has the base-pairing properties of thymine (T). Most known cytidine deaminases operate on RNA, and the few examples that are known to accept DNA require single-stranded (ss) DNA. Recent studies on the dCas9-target DNA complex reveal that at least nine nt of the displaced DNA strand are unpaired upon formation of the Cas9:guide RNA:DNA 'R-loop' complex. Indeed, in the structure of the Cas9 R-loop complex the first 11 nt of the protospacer on the displaced DNA strand are disordered, suggesting that their movement is not highly restricted. It has also been speculated that Cas9 nickase-induced mutations at cytosines in the non-template strand might arise from their accessibility by cellular cytosine deaminase enzymes. We reasoned that a subset of this stretch of ssDNA in the R-loop might serve as an efficient substrate for a dCas9-tethered cytidine deaminase to effect direct, programmable conversion of C to U in DNA.

This paper entirely reshaped the CRISPR field. It brilliantly sidestepped a major limitation of standard CRISPR-Cas9, which relies heavily on messy, error-prone cellular repair mechanisms following a double-strand break. Base editing granted the ability to make precise, clean point mutation corrections without triggering widespread genomic damage, forging realistic, inherently safer therapeutic pathways for correcting the precise point mutations responsible for the vast majority of human genetic diseases.

title: Programmable Editing of a Target Base in Genomic DNA without Double-Stranded DNA Cleavage
text:
The ability to alter genomic DNA at a chosen locus has transformed biological research and created new opportunities for the treatment of genetic disease. Programmable nucleases, including zinc-finger nucleases, transcription activator-like effector nucleases, and RNA-guided CRISPR/Cas9 systems, use sequence-specific DNA recognition to induce double-stranded DNA breaks (DSBs) at targeted genomic sites. These breaks are repaired by endogenous cellular pathways, most commonly by non-homologous end joining (NHEJ), which frequently generates insertions and deletions (indels), or less frequently by homology-directed repair (HDR) when an exogenous donor DNA template is supplied. Although these strategies have enabled efficient gene disruption and, in some cases, precise sequence replacement, they rely on the formation of DSBs and on cellular DNA repair outcomes that are often inefficient, stochastic, and difficult to control.

This limitation is especially consequential for the correction of point mutations. Single-nucleotide substitutions account for a large fraction of known pathogenic human genetic variation, yet current genome-editing approaches are poorly matched to the chemical simplicity of these lesions. To change one base pair into another using nuclease-mediated HDR, cells must first tolerate a DSB, copy information from an exogenous repair template, and avoid competing NHEJ events that can disrupt the target locus. In many therapeutically relevant cell types, HDR is inefficient or absent, while NHEJ is active and can introduce permanent genetic damage. As a result, attempts to correct point mutations using programmable nucleases often produce a mixture of rare desired alleles and more abundant indel-containing alleles. A strategy that directly converts one base into another without requiring DSBs or donor DNA would therefore offer a more natural solution to the problem of single-base genome editing.

Several features of CRISPR/Cas9 make it an attractive platform for such an approach. Cas9 can be programmed with a guide RNA to bind a chosen DNA sequence, and catalytically impaired Cas9 retains this targeting ability while no longer cleaving DNA. During target recognition, Cas9 locally unwinds the DNA duplex and exposes a short segment of single-stranded DNA within the R-loop. This transiently accessible DNA strand presents a potential substrate for enzymes that act on single-stranded nucleic acids. Cytidine deaminases, for example, catalyze the hydrolytic deamination of cytidine to uridine, a chemical change that, if installed in genomic DNA and resolved by replication or repair, can produce a C:G to T:A substitution.

Here we develop base editing, a programmable genome-editing strategy that directly converts targeted cytidines to thymidines without requiring DSB formation or a donor template. We engineer fusions between Cas9 variants and cytidine deaminases, identify architectures that mediate guide RNA-dependent deamination of DNA, and manipulate cellular DNA repair pathways to favor permanent installation of the desired base-pair change. We show that these base editors can efficiently edit endogenous genomic loci in mammalian cells, produce substantially fewer indels than Cas9-mediated HDR, and correct disease-relevant point mutations. These results establish a general framework for genome editing by direct chemical modification of bases rather than by nuclease-induced DNA breakage.

This paper entirely reshaped the CRISPR field. It brilliantly sidestepped a major limitation of standard CRISPR-Cas9, which relies heavily on messy, error-prone cellular repair mechanisms following a double-strand break. Base editing granted the ability to make precise, clean point mutation corrections without triggering widespread genomic damage, forging realistic, inherently safer therapeutic pathways for correcting the precise point mutations responsible for the vast majority of human genetic diseases.

title: Programmable Editing of a Target Base in Genomic DNA without Double-Stranded DNA Cleavage
text:
Current genome-editing technologies, such as zinc finger nucleases, TALENs, and the widely used CRISPR/Cas9 system, rely fundamentally on the introduction of targeted double-stranded (ds) DNA breaks at a specified genomic locus. This initial cleavage event is intended to stimulate the cell's endogenous DNA repair pathways. However, the cellular response to these dsDNA breaks is heavily biased towards the error-prone non-homologous end joining (NHEJ) pathway, which typically induces an abundance of random insertions and deletions (indels) at the target site. While this is useful for gene disruption, it poses a significant challenge for the precise correction of specific genetic mutations. Although homology-directed repair (HDR) can be used to incorporate desired sequence changes from an exogenously supplied donor template, HDR is generally inefficient, highly variable across different cell types and cell states, and is almost always outcompeted by indel formation. Given that the vast majority of known human genetic diseases arise from point mutations rather than large structural variations, there is a critical need for an approach that can efficiently and cleanly correct point mutations without the deleterious consequences associated with dsDNA cleavage.

Here, we report the development of "base editing," a novel and fundamentally different approach to genome editing. This technology enables the direct, irreversible conversion of one specific target DNA base into another in a highly programmable manner, entirely bypassing the requirement for dsDNA backbone cleavage or the introduction of a homologous donor template. To achieve this, we engineered synthetic fusions comprising a catalytically impaired CRISPR/Cas9 and a naturally occurring cytidine deaminase enzyme. These chimeric proteins retain the remarkable ability of Cas9 to be directed to a specific genomic locus using a programmable guide RNA, yet they do not induce dsDNA breaks. Instead, upon binding to the target site, the tethered deaminase mediates the direct chemical conversion of cytidine to uridine on the exposed single-stranded DNA bubble, thereby directly effecting a C → T (or G → A) substitution.

The resulting first-generation base editors successfully convert cytidines within a tightly defined window of approximately five nucleotides, demonstrating the capability to efficiently correct a variety of point mutations relevant to human disease in vitro. However, to translate this biochemical activity into permanent genomic alterations in living mammalian cells, we had to manipulate the cellular DNA repair response, which naturally attempts to revert the engineered U:G mismatch back to a C:G base pair. In four transformed human and murine cell lines, we show that second- and third-generation base editors—which incorporate a uracil glycosylase inhibitor (UGI) and utilize a Cas9 nickase to specifically target the non-edited strand—successfully bias the cellular repair machinery to favor the desired base-editing outcomes. This strategy results in the permanent correction of ~15-75% of total cellular DNA with minimal (typically ≤ 1%) indel formation. Base editing thus dramatically expands the scope, precision, and efficiency of genome editing for the targeted correction of point mutations.
historical_context: In 2016, Alexis Komor, David Liu, and colleagues debuted a revolutionary leap in genome engineering known as "base editing." By engineering a fusion between a catalytically impaired Cas9 (incapable of creating double-strand breaks) and a cytidine deaminase enzyme, they built a molecular machine capable of directly and seamlessly converting a C-G base pair into a T-A base pair at a highly specific genomic locus. This paper entirely reshaped the CRISPR field. It brilliantly sidestepped a major limitation of standard CRISPR-Cas9, which relies heavily on messy, error-prone cellular repair mechanisms following a double-strand break. Base editing granted the ability to make precise, clean point mutation corrections without triggering widespread genomic damage, forging realistic, inherently safer therapeutic pathways for correcting the precise point mutations responsible for the vast majority of human genetic diseases.

title: Programmable Editing of a Target Base in Genomic DNA without Double-Stranded DNA Cleavage
text: The development of base editing advances both the scope and effectiveness of genome editing. The base editors described here offer researchers a choice of editing with very little (< 0.1%) indel formation (BE2), or more efficient editing with ≤ 1% indel formation (BE3). That the product of base editing is, by definition, no longer a substrate likely contributes to editing efficiency by preventing subsequent product transformation, which can hamper traditional Cas9 applications. By removing the reliance on dsDNA cleavage, donor templates, and stochastic DNA repair processes that vary by cell state and cell type, base editing has the potential to expand the type of genome modifications that can be cleanly installed, the efficiency of these modifications, and the type of cells that are amenable to editing. It is likely that engineered Cas9 variants or delivery methods that offer improved DNA specificity or altered PAM specificities can provide additional base editors with improved properties. These results also suggest architectures for the fusion of other DNA-modifying enzymes, including methylases and demethylases, that may enable additional types of programmable genome and epigenome base editing.
