paper_title: Programmable Editing of a Target Base in Genomic DNA without Double-Stranded DNA Cleavage - 2016 - Nature
display_title: Programmable Editing of a Target Base in Genomic DNA without Double-Stranded DNA Cleavage
title: Programmable Editing of a Target Base in Genomic DNA without Double-Stranded DNA Cleavage
text:
The results described here establish base editing as a general strategy for the programmable conversion of one genomic base pair into another without requiring double-stranded DNA cleavage or a donor DNA template. By coupling the targeting capabilities of CRISPR/Cas9 with the chemistry of cytidine deamination, and by engineering the subsequent cellular repair response, base editors convert C:G base pairs to T:A base pairs with efficiencies that substantially exceed those observed with Cas9-mediated homology-directed repair, while greatly reducing indel formation.

The activity window, sequence preferences, and processivity of these editors impose important constraints on their use, especially when additional cytidines are present near the desired target base. Nevertheless, the correction of disease-relevant APOE and TP53 alleles demonstrates that these constraints are compatible with precise and productive changes in mammalian cells. The observed off-target editing at a subset of known Cas9 off-target sites further indicates that guide RNA specificity remains a central determinant of base editor precision.

Future improvements in editor architecture, deaminase choice, delivery, and guide RNA design should expand the range of editable sequences and further improve specificity. Base editing therefore offers a complementary approach to genome editing, particularly for applications in which efficient correction of point mutations is required.

title: Programmable Editing of a Target Base in Genomic DNA without Double-Stranded DNA Cleavage
text:
Base editing represents a transformative shift in genome engineering, offering a method for precise point mutation correction without the deleterious consequences associated with double-strand DNA breaks (DSBs). By repurposing CRISPR/Cas9 as a programmable DNA-binding scaffold rather than a nuclease, we have decoupled base conversion from the error-prone non-homologous end joining (NHEJ) pathway. This approach effectively addresses the primary limitation of homology-directed repair (HDR), which is often inefficient and induces high indel frequencies in many cell types.

Our development of second- and third-generation base editors (BE2 and BE3) underscores the necessity of manipulating endogenous cellular responses to achieve high editing yields. By incorporating uracil glycosylase inhibitor (UGI) and utilizing a Cas9 nickase to direct mismatch repair, we successfully subverted base-excision repair pathways that would otherwise revert edited sites. The resulting system achieves permanent C→T conversion with efficiencies up to 75% and remarkably low indel formation. Given that the majority of known pathogenic human variants are point mutations, the ability to perform precise, single-nucleotide changes without donor templates significantly expands the therapeutic potential of genome editing, providing a powerful new tool for basic research and clinical applications.

paper_title: Retracted - A Bacterium That Can Grow by Using Arsenic Instead of Phosphorus - 2011 - Science
display_title: Retracted - A Bacterium That Can Grow by Using Arsenic Instead of Phosphorus
title: Retracted - A Bacterium That Can Grow by Using Arsenic Instead of Phosphorus
text: Life is mostly composed of the elements carbon, hydrogen, nitrogen, oxygen, sulfur, and phosphorus. Although these six elements make up nucleic acids, proteins, and lipids and thus the bulk of living matter, it is theoretically possible that some other elements in the periodic table could serve the same functions. Here, we describe a bacterium, strain GFAJ-1 of the Halomonadaceae, isolated from Mono Lake, California, that is able to substitute arsenic for phosphorus to sustain its growth. Our data show evidence for arsenate in macromolecules that normally contain phosphate, most notably nucleic acids and proteins. Exchange of one of the major bio-elements may have profound evolutionary and geochemical importance.
historical_context: In 2011, Felisa Wolfe-Simon and colleagues published a fiercely publicized paper claiming that a specific bacterium (GFAJ-1) found in Mono Lake could literally substitute arsenic for phosphorus within its essential biomolecules, including its DNA. If accurate, this assertion would have rewritten the foundational rules of biochemistry and astrobiology. However, the paper is historically crucial for its dramatic refutation and eventual retraction. Independent scientists rapidly exposed critical methodological flaws, confirming that the DNA still unequivocally relied on phosphorus and that the bacteria were merely surviving high arsenic levels while desperately scavenging trace phosphorus. While pivotal as a cautionary tale rather than a genuine discovery, this paper stands as a landmark case study in the modern era of post-publication peer review, aggressively highlighting the uncompromising necessity of rigorous replication and the unmatched power of open scientific scrutiny.

title: Retracted - A Bacterium That Can Grow by Using Arsenic Instead of Phosphorus
text: Phosphorus is considered an essential element for all known life, whereas arsenic is generally toxic because arsenate can mimic phosphate but forms less stable biochemical compounds. Here we describe strain GFAJ-1, a member of the Halomonadaceae isolated from arsenic-rich Mono Lake sediments, that grows aerobically in a defined medium containing arsenate and no added phosphate. Growth under these conditions was accompanied by arsenate uptake, greatly reduced intracellular phosphorus, altered cellular morphology, and accumulation of arsenic in cellular fractions containing nucleic acids, proteins, lipids, and metabolites. NanoSIMS and synchrotron x-ray analyses further indicated arsenic associated with purified DNA and As(V)-oxygen-carbon coordination. These results suggest that GFAJ-1 can substitute arsenic for phosphorus in major biomolecules.

display_title: Retracted - A Bacterium That Can Grow by Using Arsenic Instead of Phosphorus
title: Retracted - A Bacterium That Can Grow by Using Arsenic Instead of Phosphorus
text:
Biological dependence on the six major nutrient elements carbon, hydrogen, nitrogen, oxygen, sulfur, and phosphorus (P) is complemented by a selected array of other elements, usually metals or metalloids present in trace quantities that serve critical cellular functions, such as enzyme co-factors. There are many cases of these trace elements substituting for one another. A few examples include the substitution of tungsten for molybdenum and cadmium for zinc in some enzyme families and copper for iron as an oxygen-carrier in some arthropods and mollusks. In these examples and others, the trace elements that interchange share chemical similarities that facilitate the swap. However, there are no prior reports of substitutions for any of the six major elements essential for life. Here, we present evidence that arsenic can substitute for phosphorus in the biomolecules of a naturally occurring bacterium.

Arsenic (As) is a chemical analog of P, which lies directly below P on the periodic table. Arsenic possesses a similar atomic radius, as well as near identical electronegativity to P. The most common form of P in biology is phosphate (PO4^3-), which behaves similarly to arsenate (AsO4^3-) over the range of biologically relevant pH and redox gradients. The physicochemical similarity between AsO4^3- and PO4^3- contributes to the biological toxicity of AsO4^3- because metabolic pathways intended for PO4^3- cannot distinguish between the two molecules and AsO4^3- may be incorporated into some early steps in the pathways. However, it is thought that downstream metabolic processes are generally not compatible with As-incorporating molecules because of differences in the reactivities of P and As compounds. These downstream biochemical pathways may require the more chemically stable P-based metabolites; the lifetimes of more easily hydrolyzed As-bearing analogs are thought to be too short. However, given the similarities of As and P—and by analogy with trace element substitutions—we hypothesized that AsO4^3- could specifically substitute for PO4^3- in an organism possessing mechanisms to cope with the inherent instability of AsO4^3- compounds. Here, we experimentally tested this hypothesis by using AsO4^3-, combined with no added PO4^3-, to select for and isolate a microbe capable of accomplishing this substitution.

If accurate, this assertion would have rewritten the foundational rules of biochemistry and astrobiology. However, the paper is historically crucial for its dramatic refutation and eventual retraction. Independent scientists rapidly exposed critical methodological flaws, confirming that the DNA still unequivocally relied on phosphorus and that the bacteria were merely surviving high arsenic levels while desperately scavenging trace phosphorus. While pivotal as a cautionary tale rather than a genuine discovery, this paper stands as a landmark case study in the modern era of post-publication peer review, aggressively highlighting the uncompromising necessity of rigorous replication and the unmatched power of open scientific scrutiny.

paper_title: Retracted - A Bacterium That Can Grow by Using Arsenic Instead of Phosphorus - 2011 - Science
display_title: Retracted - A Bacterium That Can Grow by Using Arsenic Instead of Phosphorus
title: Retracted - A Bacterium That Can Grow by Using Arsenic Instead of Phosphorus
text:
Life as currently known is built from a small subset of the periodic table. Carbon, hydrogen, nitrogen, oxygen, sulfur, and phosphorus form the major covalent framework of nucleic acids, proteins, lipids, and metabolites, and their use appears broadly conserved across the biosphere. Among these elements, phosphorus has a central and unusually versatile role. As PO4^3-, it forms the backbone of DNA and RNA, participates in energy transfer through phosphoanhydride bonds, marks proteins and small molecules through reversible phosphorylation, and contributes to membrane structure through phospholipids. The apparent indispensability of phosphate has therefore helped define the chemical boundaries of life.

Yet the elemental composition of life is not fixed by first principles alone. Organisms can substitute metals in enzymes, alter cofactor composition, and exploit geochemical niches that would be toxic or inaccessible to many other cells. Arsenic is of particular interest because arsenate, AsO4^3-, is a close chemical analog of phosphate, sharing similar charge and tetrahedral geometry. This similarity underlies much of arsenate toxicity: AsO4^3- can enter cells through phosphate transport pathways and can interfere with phosphate-dependent metabolism. At the same time, the relative lability of arsenate esters has generally argued against a stable structural role for arsenic in biological macromolecules.

Microbial metabolisms nevertheless reveal extensive biological engagement with arsenic. Diverse bacteria reduce arsenate, oxidize arsenite, methylate arsenicals, or use arsenic redox chemistry in energy metabolism. These activities demonstrate that arsenic is not merely an environmental poison but can be incorporated into microbial physiology. However, such organisms have not been shown to replace a major bioelement required for growth. Whether a cell could grow while using arsenic in biochemical roles normally occupied by phosphorus remains an unresolved question with implications for the limits of life and for interpreting biosignatures in unusual geochemical environments.

Mono Lake, an alkaline and hypersaline lake in eastern California, contains high concentrations of dissolved arsenic and supports microbial communities adapted to extreme chemistry. We therefore used Mono Lake sediments as a source for enrichment under conditions of limiting phosphate and elevated arsenate. Here, we describe the isolation and characterization of strain GFAJ-1, a member of the Halomonadaceae, and present evidence that this bacterium can grow under arsenate-rich, phosphate-depleted conditions while assimilating arsenic into cellular material.

If accurate, this assertion would have rewritten the foundational rules of biochemistry and astrobiology. However, the paper is historically crucial for its dramatic refutation and eventual retraction. Independent scientists rapidly exposed critical methodological flaws, confirming that the DNA still unequivocally relied on phosphorus and that the bacteria were merely surviving high arsenic levels while desperately scavenging trace phosphorus. While pivotal as a cautionary tale rather than a genuine discovery, this paper stands as a landmark case study in the modern era of post-publication peer review, aggressively highlighting the uncompromising necessity of rigorous replication and the unmatched power of open scientific scrutiny.

title: Retracted - A Bacterium That Can Grow by Using Arsenic Instead of Phosphorus
text:
The fundamental chemistry of life as we know it is remarkably conserved across all domains. All known terrestrial organisms are primarily composed of just six major elements: carbon, hydrogen, nitrogen, oxygen, sulfur, and phosphorus. These vital elements serve as the essential building blocks for the core macromolecules of life, making up the structural and functional bulk of nucleic acids (DNA and RNA), proteins, and lipid membranes. Phosphorus, in the form of inorganic phosphate, plays an especially critical structural role in the backbone of nucleic acids and is the central currency for cellular energy transfer via adenosine triphosphate (ATP). While these six elements are universally relied upon, it is theoretically possible that other elements from the periodic table, specifically those sharing similar chemical properties within the same group, could substitute and serve analogous biological functions under extreme environmental conditions. Arsenic, which sits directly below phosphorus on the periodic table, shares many chemical similarities with phosphorus but is generally highly toxic to biological systems because it can disrupt phosphate-dependent metabolic pathways.

Here, we describe the discovery of an extraordinary bacterium, strain GFAJ-1, a member of the Halomonadaceae family, which was isolated from the harsh, hypersaline, and highly alkaline environment of Mono Lake, California—a unique ecosystem known for its unusually high concentrations of dissolved arsenic. Through rigorous cultivation in defined artificial media, we demonstrate that GFAJ-1 possesses the unprecedented ability to substitute arsenic for phosphorus to sustain its cellular growth and viability. Our extensive analytical data provide compelling evidence that this organism actively incorporates arsenate into its key intracellular macromolecules, replacing phosphate in structures where it is normally strictly required. Most notably, we detect the presence of arsenic integrated directly into the organism's nucleic acids and proteins. The ability of a living organism to functionally exchange one of the major, universally conserved bio-elements for a toxic metalloid challenges our fundamental understanding of biochemistry. This remarkable physiological adaptation not only expands the known parameters of biology but also carries profound evolutionary and geochemical implications for the boundaries of life on Earth and potentially elsewhere.

However, the paper is historically crucial for its dramatic refutation and eventual retraction. Independent scientists rapidly exposed critical methodological flaws, confirming that the DNA still unequivocally relied on phosphorus and that the bacteria were merely surviving high arsenic levels while desperately scavenging trace phosphorus. While pivotal as a cautionary tale rather than a genuine discovery, this paper stands as a landmark case study in the modern era of post-publication peer review, aggressively highlighting the uncompromising necessity of rigorous replication and the unmatched power of open scientific scrutiny.

text:
Our data show arsenic-dependent growth by GFAJ-1. Growth was accompanied by AsO4^3- uptake and assimilation into biomolecules including nucleic acids, proteins, and metabolites. In some organisms, arsenic induces specific resistance genes to cope with its toxicity, whereas some dissimilatory arsenic-utilizing microbes can conserve energy for growth from the oxidation of reduced arsenic species, or 'breathe' AsO4^3-, as a terminal electron acceptor. Our study differs because we used As as a selective agent and excluded P, a major requirement in all hitherto known organisms. However, GFAJ-1 is not an obligate arsenophile and grew considerably better when provided with P. Although AsO4^3- esters are predicted to be orders of magnitude less stable than PO4^3- esters, at least for simple molecules, GFAJ-1 can cope with this instability. The vacuole-like regions observed in GFAJ-1 cells when growing under +As/-P conditions are potentially poly-β-hydroxybutyrate rich, which may stabilize As(V)-O-C type structures because nonaqueous environments appear to promote slower hydrolysis rates for related compounds. We propose that intracellular regions or mechanisms that exclude water may also promote this stability.

We report the discovery of an unusual microbe, strain GFAJ-1, that exceptionally can vary the elemental composition of its basic biomolecules by substituting As for P. How As insinuates itself into the structure of biomolecules is unclear, and the mechanisms by which such molecules operate are unknown.

However, the paper is historically crucial for its dramatic refutation and eventual retraction. Independent scientists rapidly exposed critical methodological flaws, confirming that the DNA still unequivocally relied on phosphorus and that the bacteria were merely surviving high arsenic levels while desperately scavenging trace phosphorus. While pivotal as a cautionary tale rather than a genuine discovery, this paper stands as a landmark case study in the modern era of post-publication peer review, aggressively highlighting the uncompromising necessity of rigorous replication and the unmatched power of open scientific scrutiny.

title: Retracted - A Bacterium That Can Grow by Using Arsenic Instead of Phosphorus
text:
The results presented here show that strain GFAJ-1, isolated from an environment naturally enriched in arsenic, can maintain growth under conditions in which added phosphate is absent and arsenate is abundant. Although trace phosphate impurities were present in the medium, the repeated dilution, lack of growth in the -As/-P condition, and low cellular P content together indicate that this residual pool was insufficient to account for the observed biomass. The concomitant accumulation of As, incorporation of radiolabeled arsenate into multiple biochemical fractions, and detection of As in purified genomic DNA provide convergent evidence that arsenate entered cellular material rather than remaining solely as an extracellular or loosely associated contaminant.

These findings imply that GFAJ-1 possesses physiological adaptations that permit survival in a chemical regime usually considered incompatible with conventional phosphate biochemistry. The enlarged cell volume and vacuole-like structures observed during +As/-P growth may reflect mechanisms for managing arsenate burden, altered osmotic state, or compartmentalization of labile arsenylated compounds. The x-ray absorption data further indicate that intracellular arsenic was predominantly As(V) coordinated to oxygen, with longer-range features consistent with bonding environments expected for arsenate substituted into biological molecules. Thus, GFAJ-1 appears not merely resistant to arsenate toxicity but able to use arsenate in reactions dependent on phosphate.

The capacity to replace one major bioelement with a chemical analog has implications for microbial ecology, biogeochemistry, and the limits of life. Environments with high As:P ratios may select for organisms that exploit arsenate-rich niches through biochemical flexibility. More broadly, these observations suggest that the elemental requirements for life may be less fixed than previously assumed.

Our characterization of GFAJ-1 demonstrates that the biological requirement for phosphorus, long considered a fundamental and immutable constraint on life, can be bypassed. By exploiting the chemical similarity between arsenate and phosphate, this organism has evolved a mechanism to incorporate arsenic into its primary biomolecules, including its genetic material and proteins. This discovery suggests that the inventory of elements required for life is broader than previously recognized, with profound implications for our understanding of evolutionary history and the search for life elsewhere in the universe.

The primary obstacle to arsenic-based biochemistry has been the inherent hydrolytic instability of arsenate esters compared to their phosphate counterparts. Our EXAFS and NanoSIMS data provide evidence of an arsenic coordination environment consistent with a DNA backbone in vivo, suggesting that GFAJ-1 may possess specialized stabilization mechanisms to manage this inherent reactivity. While the organism exhibits faster growth when phosphorus is available, its ability to sustain itself under high-arsenic, phosphorus-depleted conditions reveals a remarkable degree of metabolic plasticity.

The existence of GFAJ-1 challenges the "six-element" dogma of biology. Future research must now focus on identifying the specific biochemical pathways and structural adaptations that allow for the stable incorporation of arsenic into macromolecules. Ultimately, this work opens a new chapter in astrobiology, demonstrating that the chemical building blocks of life may be more adaptable than previously imagined.

text: Patient-specific, immune-matched human embryonic stem cells (hESCs) are anticipated to be of great biomedical importance for studies of disease and development and to advance clinical deliberations regarding stem cell transplantation. Eleven hESC lines were established by somatic cell nuclear transfer (SCNT) of skin cells from patients with disease or injury into donated oocytes. These lines, nuclear transfer (NT)-hESCs, grown on human feeders from the same NT donor or from genetically unrelated individuals, were established at high rates, regardless of NT donor sex or age. NT-hESCs were pluripotent, chromosomally normal, and matched the NT patient's DNA. The major histocompatibility complex identity of each NT-hESC when compared to the patient's own showed immunological compatibility, which is important for eventual transplantation. With the generation of these NT-hESCs, evaluations of genetic and epigenetic stability can be made. Additional work remains to be done regarding the development of reliable directed differentiation and the elimination of remaining animal components. Before clinical use of these cells can occur, preclinical evidence is required to prove that transplantation of differentiated NT-hESCs can be safe, effective, and tolerated.

text:
Many human injuries and diseases result from defects in a single cell type. If defective cells could be replaced with appropriate stem cells, progenitor cells, or cells differentiated in vitro, and if immune rejection of transplanted cells could be avoided, it might be possible to treat disease and injury at the cellular level in the clinic. By generating hESCs from human NT blastocysts, in which the somatic cell nucleus comes from the individual patient—a situation where the nuclear (though not mitochondrial DNA (mtDNA)) genome is identical to that of the NT donor—the possibility of immune rejection might be eliminated if these cells were to be used for human treatment.

Recently, mouse models of severe combined immunodeficiency (SCID) and Parkinson's disease (PD) have been successfully treated through the transplantation of autologous differentiated mouse embryonic stem cells (mESCs) derived from NT blastocysts, a process also referred to as therapeutic cloning. In 2004, evidence was presented that a human NT-hESC line (NT-hESC-1) was derived by transferring the donor's cumulus cell nucleus into her own enucleated oocyte; however, questions remained as to whether the cell line had a parthenogenetic origin. In addition, it was not known whether NT-hESCs could be generated from NT procedures using nuclei from males, prepubescent girls, or postmenopausal women, or when using cell components from unrelated women. Finally, because NT-hESC-1 was grown on mouse feeders with likely xenograft contamination, the utility of those cells is largely preclinical. In our study, patient-specific NT-hESCs were established reliably and efficiently (about 1 NT-hESC line per oocyte donation cycle) on human feeder cells and regardless of somatic cell donor sex or age. In addition, some lines were derived without the animal products used during immunosurgery. Immunosurgery exposes the ESC precursors, the inner cell mass cells (ICMs), through lysis of the blastocyst's outer trophectoderm cells by sequential exposure to antibodies followed by complement; however, residual animal byproducts, including calf serum and enzymes, still remain. Furthermore, 10 of the 11 new cell lines were generated from NT procedures using oocyte and somatic cell donors that were obtained from biologically unrelated individuals.

text:
Human embryonic stem cells (hESCs), because of their capacities for prolonged self-renewal and differentiation into derivatives of all three embryonic germ layers, provide an experimental system for studies of human development and a possible cellular source for regenerative medicine. However, the clinical application of hESCs is constrained by immunological incompatibility between donor-derived cells and recipient patients. Even if reliable differentiation into therapeutically useful cell types is achieved, transplantation of genetically unrelated hESC derivatives would be expected to require histocompatibility matching or systemic immunosuppression, as in organ transplantation.

Somatic cell nuclear transfer (SCNT) offers one strategy for deriving patient-specific hESCs. In this approach, the nucleus of a somatic cell from a patient is transferred into an enucleated oocyte, and embryonic stem cells are derived from the resulting nuclear transfer (NT) blastocyst. Such NT-hESCs would contain the nuclear genome, and therefore the major histocompatibility complex, of the patient. In principle, these cells could provide immune-matched material for transplantation after directed differentiation. They could also provide genetically defined, disease-specific human cells for studies of pathogenesis, drug discovery, and developmental mechanisms in vitro.

Previous work in animals has shown that nuclear transfer can reprogram differentiated somatic nuclei and support embryonic development, and mouse studies have suggested that NT-derived embryonic stem cells can be used in disease models after genetic correction or lineage-specific differentiation. Human application, however, requires evidence that patient nuclei of different ages, sexes, and disease states can support NT blastocyst formation and hESC derivation. It also requires rigorous demonstration that resulting cell lines are pluripotent, chromosomally normal, genetically matched to the somatic donor, and compatible at the major histocompatibility complex loci relevant to transplantation.

Here we report the derivation of patient-specific NT-hESC lines from individuals with disease or injury, including spinal cord injury, juvenile diabetes, and congenital hypogammaglobulinemia. We evaluate the efficiency of NT blastocyst formation and hESC establishment, characterize pluripotency and chromosomal normality, confirm genetic identity between each NT-hESC line and its corresponding patient donor, and assess human leukocyte antigen matching. These findings provide a basis for evaluating the genetic, epigenetic, and immunological properties of patient-specific NT-hESCs and define remaining requirements for preclinical development before any consideration of clinical use.

text:
Embryonic stem cells hold significant promise for the treatment of numerous degenerative diseases and injuries, owing to their unique capacity for self-renewal and pluripotency. Despite their immense potential, a major hurdle in the clinical application of human embryonic stem cells (hESCs) is the risk of immune rejection following transplantation into patients. Somatic cell nuclear transfer (SCNT) has been proposed as a method to circumvent this barrier. SCNT involves the transfer of a somatic cell nucleus from a patient into an enucleated oocyte, leading to the generation of a blastocyst from which patient-specific hESCs can be derived. Because these cells would be genetically identical to the donor patient, they are anticipated to be immunologically tolerated.

Although SCNT has been successfully demonstrated in various mammalian species, including sheep, mice, and cattle, its application to human cells has proven exceptionally challenging. Previous attempts at human therapeutic cloning have yielded limited success, characterized by low blastocyst formation rates and inefficient derivation of hESC lines. In 2004, our group reported the first successful derivation of a human SCNT-hESC line, demonstrating the proof of principle for human therapeutic cloning. However, this initial success was limited to autologous SCNT, utilizing somatic cells and oocytes from the same female donor, and the overall efficiency remained low.

To fully realize the clinical potential of SCNT, it is essential to establish reliable and highly efficient protocols for generating patient-specific hESCs from diverse patient populations, regardless of age or sex. This requires the successful application of heterologous SCNT, where donor somatic cells are transferred into oocytes from unrelated volunteers. In this study, we aim to significantly improve the efficiency of human SCNT and to demonstrate the robust derivation of patient-specific hESC lines from individuals suffering from a range of severe diseases and injuries. By optimizing key steps in the SCNT procedure, including oocyte enucleation, nuclear injection, and stem cell derivation on human feeder layers, we sought to establish a practical and ethically responsible framework for generating immune-matched pluripotent cells. Here, we report the highly efficient derivation of eleven new SCNT-hESC lines from patients with spinal cord injury, juvenile diabetes, and congenital hypogammaglobulinemia, providing a critical step forward in the development of personalized regenerative therapies.

text: Here we report a unique cellular reprogramming phenomenon, called stimulus-triggered acquisition of pluripotency (STAP), which requires neither nuclear transfer nor the introduction of transcription factors. In STAP, strong external stimuli such as a transient low-pH stressor reprogrammed mammalian somatic cells, resulting in the generation of pluripotent cells. Through real-time imaging of STAP cells derived from purified lymphocytes, as well as gene rearrangement analysis, we found that committed somatic cells give rise to STAP cells by reprogramming rather than selection. STAP cells showed a substantial decrease in DNA methylation in the regulatory regions of pluripotency marker genes. Blastocyst injection showed that STAP cells efficiently contribute to chimaeric embryos and to offspring via germline transmission. We also demonstrate the derivation of robustly expandable pluripotent cell lines from STAP cells. Thus, our findings indicate that epigenetic fate determination of mammalian cells can be markedly converted in a context-dependent manner by strong environmental cues.
historical_context: In 2014, Haruko Obokata, Yoshiki Sasai, and colleagues published the astonishing claim that simply subjecting mature somatic cells to brief physical stress or low pH could rapidly reprogram them into pluripotent stem cells, a phenomenon they labeled "STAP" (stimulus-triggered acquisition of pluripotency). This breakthrough would have completely rendered Yamanaka factors obsolete. However, the paper rapidly imploded as independent global laboratories completely failed to replicate the findings, and deeper investigations unearthed severe data manipulation and blatant image fabrication. The paper was retracted in a matter of months. Historically, it serves as another brutal reminder of the blinding pressures inherent in high-stakes science. The STAP cell scandal permanently shifted the landscape of regenerative biology, forcing premier journals to implement vastly more stringent data availability and peer review policies to safeguard scientific transparency and reproducibility.

text: It is generally thought that acquisition of pluripotency by somatic cells requires direct nuclear manipulation, such as nuclear transfer or the forced expression of defined transcription factors. text: In the canalization view of Waddington's epigenetic landscape, fates of somatic cells are progressively determined as cellular differentiation proceeds, like going downhill. It is generally believed that reversal of differentiated status requires artificial physical or genetic manipulation of nuclear function such as nuclear transfer or the introduction of multiple transcription factors. Here we investigated the question of whether somatic cells can undergo nuclear reprogramming simply in response to external triggers without direct nuclear manipulation. This type of situation is known to occur in plants—drastic environmental changes can convert mature somatic cells (for example, dissociated carrot cells) into immature blastema cells, from which a whole plant structure, including stalks and roots, develops in the presence of auxins. A challenging question is whether animal somatic cells have a similar potential that emerges under special conditions. Over the past decade, the presence of pluripotent cells (or closely relevant cell types) in adult tissues has been a matter of debate, for which conflicting conclusions have been reported by various groups. However, no study so far has proven that such pluripotent cells can arise from differentiated somatic cells.

text:

Experimental reversal of this restriction has classically required nuclear transfer into oocytes or the enforced expression of defined transcription factors, as in induced pluripotent stem cells. These approaches show that differentiated nuclei retain developmental potential, but they also imply that reprogramming requires specialized cellular environments or artificial genetic manipulation. Cells in living tissues are exposed to diverse environmental perturbations, including changes in pH, osmotic pressure, mechanical stress and damage. Here we asked whether mammalian somatic cells, particularly lineage-committed haematopoietic cells, can acquire pluripotency after transient exposure to such stimuli. We focused on low-pH treatment and examined whether this perturbation induces activation of pluripotency markers, epigenetic remodeling and functional developmental potency.

display_title: Retracted - Stimulus-Triggered Fate Conversion of Somatic Cells into Pluripotency
title: Retracted - Stimulus-Triggered Fate Conversion of Somatic Cells into Pluripotency
text:
This study has revealed that somatic cells latently possess a surprising plasticity. This dynamic plasticity—the ability to become pluripotent cells—emerges when cells are transiently exposed to strong stimuli that they would not normally experience in their living environments.

Low-pH treatment was also used in the 'autoneuralization' experiment by Holtfreter in 1947, in which exposure to acidic medium caused tissue-autonomous neural conversion of salamander animal caps in vitro in the absence of Spemann's organizer signals. Although the mechanism has remained elusive, Holtfreter hypothesized that the strong stimulus releases the animal cap cells from some intrinsic inhibitory mechanisms that suppress fate conversion or, in his words, they pass through 'sublethal cytolysis' (meaning stimulus-evoked lysis of the cell's inhibitory state). Although Holtfreter's study and ours differ in the direction of fate conversion—orthograde differentiation and nuclear reprogramming, respectively—these phenomena may share some common aspects, particularly with regard to sublethal stimulus-evoked release from a static (conversion-resisting) state in the cell.

A remaining question is whether cellular reprogramming is initiated specifically by the low-pH treatment or also by some other types of sublethal stress such as physical damage, plasma membrane perforation, osmotic pressure shock, growth-factor deprivation, heat shock or high Ca2+ exposure. At least some of these stressors, particularly physical damage by rigorous trituration and membrane perforation by streptolysin O, induced the generation of Oct4-GFP+ cells from CD45+ cells. These findings raise the possibility that certain common regulatory modules, lying downstream of these distantly related sublethal stresses, act as a key for releasing somatic cells from the tightly locked epigenetic state of differentiation, leading to a global change in epigenetic regulation. In other words, unknown cellular functions, activated by sublethal stimuli, may set somatic cells free from their current commitment to recover the naive cell state.

Our present finding of an unexpectedly large capacity for radical reprogramming in committed somatic cells raises various important questions. For instance, why, and for what purpose, do somatic cells latently possess this self-driven ability for nuclear reprogramming, which emerges only after sublethal stimulation, and how, then, is this reprogramming mechanism normally suppressed? Furthermore, why isn't teratoma (or pluripotent cell mass) formation normally seen in in vivo tissues that may receive strong environmental stress? In our preliminary study, experimental reflux oesophagitis locally induced moderate expression of Oct4-GFP but not endogenous Nanog in the mouse oesophageal mucosa. Therefore, an intriguing hypothesis for future research is that the progression from initial Oct4 activation to further reprogramming is suppressed by certain inhibitory mechanisms in vivo.

The question of why and how this self-driven reprogramming is directed towards the pluripotent state is fundamentally important, given that STAP reprogramming takes a remarkably short period, only a few days for substantial expression of pluripotency marker genes, unlike transgene- or chemical-induced iPS cell conversion. Thus, our results cast new light on the biological meaning of diverse cellular states in multicellular organisms.

title: Retracted - Stimulus-Triggered Fate Conversion of Somatic Cells into Pluripotency
text:
The present findings indicate that the differentiated state of mammalian somatic cells is not invariably fixed, but can be markedly altered by a transient environmental stimulus. In this study, short exposure to sublethal low pH converted lineage-committed cells into Oct4-expressing cells with molecular, epigenetic and developmental properties of pluripotent cells. This phenomenon, which we term stimulus-triggered acquisition of pluripotency, differs from previously described reprogramming procedures in that it requires neither nuclear transfer nor forced expression of defined transcription factors. Thus, a strong external cue can initiate a substantial change in cell fate, suggesting that the epigenetic landscape of somatic cells may retain a context-dependent reversibility greater than generally assumed.

Several observations argue that STAP cells arise through conversion of somatic cells rather than expansion of pre-existing pluripotent contaminants. Oct4-GFP+ cells were not detected in untreated CD45+ cultures, and the emergence of Oct4-GFP expression after low-pH treatment occurred without substantial proliferation. The gradual loss of CD45 immunoreactivity, the appearance of Oct4-GFP in live imaging, and the presence of Tcrb rearrangements in Oct4-GFP+ cells derived from purified lymphocyte populations further support an origin from committed haematopoietic cells. In addition, similar induction of Oct4-GFP+ cells from several non-haematopoietic tissues indicates that the response is not restricted to a single lineage. These results suggest that somatic cells exposed to particular stress conditions can enter a dynamic state in which lineage-associated features decline and pluripotency-associated programmes become activated.

The acquisition of pluripotency by STAP cells was accompanied by changes at multiple levels. STAP cells expressed key pluripotency markers, showed demethylation of Oct4 and Nanog regulatory regions, differentiated into derivatives of the three germ layers, and contributed to chimaeric embryos and the germline. Their ability to generate embryos in tetraploid complementation assays provides stringent evidence for broad developmental potency. At the same time, STAP cells were not identical to conventional ES cells. They had limited self-renewal under the initial culture conditions, showed low Esrrb expression, and retained H3K27me3-dense foci in a fraction of female cells. These features suggest that STAP cells represent a pluripotent state generated by stress-induced conversion, but one that is not fully equivalent to the established ES-cell state.

The derivation of expandable STAP stem-cell lines from STAP cells further indicates that the primary STAP state can be stabilized and propagated under suitable culture conditions. ACTH-containing medium enabled outgrowth of proliferative colonies that subsequently acquired ES-cell-like properties, including single-cell passaging, long-term expansion, pluripotency marker expression, low DNA methylation at pluripotency loci, chimaera contribution and germline transmission. The disappearance of H3K27me3 foci and induction of Esrrb in STAP stem cells suggest that additional culture-dependent transitions occur after the initial stimulus-triggered conversion.

These observations raise important questions about the mechanisms by which transient stress initiates reprogramming. Low-pH treatment may affect membrane properties, metabolic status, chromatin regulation or stress-response pathways, and these processes may cooperate with LIF-dependent signalling during the early culture period. Defining the molecular events that distinguish reversible stress adaptation from fate conversion will be essential. More broadly, STAP suggests that mammalian cells may possess latent plasticity that can be uncovered by environmental perturbation, providing a simple experimental system for studying how external cues remodel nuclear state and developmental potential.

title: Retracted - Stimulus-Triggered Fate Conversion of Somatic Cells into Pluripotency
text:
Our findings reveal an unprecedented phenomenon in mammalian cellular biology: the stimulus-triggered acquisition of pluripotency (STAP). The observation that robust environmental stressors, particularly transient low-pH exposure, can rapidly reprogram committed somatic cells to a pluripotent state without the introduction of exogenous transcription factors or nuclear transfer fundamentally challenges the established paradigm of epigenetic canalization. This context-dependent fate conversion demonstrates a remarkable degree of cellular plasticity previously thought to exist only in plants or lower vertebrates.

The demonstration that CD45+ hematopoietic lineages undergo dynamic epigenetic reorganization, including extensive demethylation at the Oct4 and Nanog promoter regions, confirms that STAP cell generation is a bona fide reprogramming event rather than the selective survival of preexisting multipotent progenitors. This conclusion is further corroborated by T-cell receptor gene rearrangement analyses and live-cell imaging, which trace the origin of STAP clusters directly back to lineage-committed lymphocytes. Notably, while these STAP cells exhibit hallmark features of pluripotency, including the capacity for multi-lineage differentiation in vitro and robust teratoma formation in vivo, they retain unique characteristics distinguishing them from conventional embryonic stem (ES) cells. Their limited self-renewal capacity under standard LIF-containing conditions and the presence of inactivated X-chromosomes in female cells suggest that STAP cells may represent an distinct transitional pluripotent state.

Yet, perhaps the most striking demonstration of STAP cell potency is their ability to contribute to all somatic lineages and the germline following blastocyst injection, satisfying the most stringent criteria for pluripotency. The generation of completely STAP-derived embryos through tetraploid complementation unequivocally proves their developmental equivalence to blastomere cells. Furthermore, our success in deriving robustly expandable STAP stem cell lines when cultured in ACTH-containing medium provides a critical bridge between this novel biological phenomenon and potential biomedical applications. These STAP stem cells acquire ES-cell-like properties, including continuous self-renewal and loss of X-chromosome inactivation, highlighting an additional layer of plasticity within the reprogrammed state itself.

These discoveries open new avenues for both basic research and regenerative medicine. The ability to generate patient-specific pluripotent cells through simple chemical or physical perturbations circumvents the technical complexities and ethical concerns associated with somatic cell nuclear transfer or viral transduction. Future investigations must focus on elucidating the precise molecular mechanisms orchestrating this rapid epigenetic remodeling and determining whether similar stress-induced plasticity operates during tissue regeneration or pathogenesis in vivo. Ultimately, understanding how external cues dismantle the barriers of terminal differentiation will profoundly advance our comprehension of cellular identity and epigenetic regulation.

paper_title: Safety and Efficacy of the BNT162b2 mRNA Covid-19 Vaccine - 2020 - New England Journal of Medicine
display_title: Safety and Efficacy of the BNT162b2 mRNA Covid-19 Vaccine
title: Safety and Efficacy of the BNT162b2 mRNA Covid-19 Vaccine
text:
Severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS-CoV-2) infection and the resulting coronavirus disease 2019 (Covid-19) have afflicted tens of millions of people in a worldwide pandemic. Safe and effective vaccines are needed urgently.

In an ongoing multinational, placebo-controlled, observer-blinded, pivotal efficacy trial, we randomly assigned persons 16 years of age or older in a 1:1 ratio to receive two doses, 21 days apart, of either placebo or the BNT162b2 vaccine candidate (30 μg per dose). BNT162b2 is a lipid nanoparticle-formulated, nucleoside-modified RNA vaccine that encodes a prefusion stabilized, membrane-anchored SARS-CoV-2 full-length spike protein. The primary end points were efficacy of the vaccine against laboratory-confirmed Covid-19 and safety.

A total of 43,548 participants underwent randomization, of whom 43,448 received injections: 21,720 with BNT162b2 and 21,728 with placebo. There were 8 cases of Covid-19 with onset at least 7 days after the second dose among participants assigned to receive BNT162b2 and 162 cases among those assigned to placebo; BNT162b2 was 95% effective in preventing Covid-19 (95% credible interval, 90.3 to 97.6). Similar vaccine efficacy (generally 90 to 100%) was observed across subgroups defined by age, sex, race, ethnicity, baseline body-mass index, and the presence of coexisting conditions. Among 10 cases of severe Covid-19 with onset after the first dose, 9 occurred in placebo recipients and 1 in a BNT162b2 recipient. The safety profile of BNT162b2 was characterized by short-term, mild-to-moderate pain at the injection site, fatigue, and headache. The incidence of serious adverse events was low and was similar in the vaccine and placebo groups.

A two-dose regimen of BNT162b2 conferred 95% protection against Covid-19 in persons 16 years of age or older. Safety over a median of 2 months was similar to that of other viral vaccines.
historical_context: Published in late 2020 by Fernando Polack, Stephen Thomas, Uğur Şahin, Kathrin Jansen, and colleagues, this historic paper detailed the Phase 3 clinical trial results of the Pfizer-BioNTech COVID-19 vaccine (BNT162b2). The data showcased a stunning 95% efficacy rate in preventing COVID-19 alongside a highly reassuring safety profile, an achievement realized in less than a year from the moment the virus was first sequenced. This clinical paper represents an absolute triumph of modern medicine and scientific logistics. It unequivocally validated the entire mRNA vaccine platform, marking the very first time an mRNA therapy was authorized for widespread human use. It decisively altered the grim trajectory of the COVID-19 pandemic and violently accelerated a new frontier for rapid-response vaccines and personalized mRNA therapeutics.

paper_title: Safety and Efficacy of the BNT162b2 mRNA Covid-19 Vaccine - 2020 - New England Journal of Medicine
display_title: Safety and Efficacy of the BNT162b2 mRNA Covid-19 Vaccine
title: Safety and Efficacy of the BNT162b2 mRNA Covid-19 Vaccine
text:
Background
Safe and effective vaccines are needed urgently to reduce coronavirus disease 2019 (Covid-19), caused by severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS-CoV-2). BNT162b2 is a lipid nanoparticle-formulated, nucleoside-modified RNA vaccine encoding the SARS-CoV-2 full-length prefusion spike protein.

Methods
In an ongoing multinational, placebo-controlled, observer-blinded, pivotal efficacy trial, we randomly assigned persons 16 years of age or older in a 1:1 ratio to receive two 30-μg doses of BNT162b2 or saline placebo, administered intramuscularly 21 days apart. The primary efficacy end points were prevention of confirmed Covid-19 with onset at least 7 days after the second dose among participants without previous evidence of SARS-CoV-2 infection and among all participants regardless of previous infection. Safety end points included solicited local and systemic reactions, adverse events, serious adverse events, and deaths.

Results
A total of 43,448 participants received injections: 21,720 received BNT162b2 and 21,728 received placebo. Among 36,523 participants without evidence of previous SARS-CoV-2 infection, 8 cases of Covid-19 with onset at least 7 days after the second dose occurred in vaccine recipients and 162 in placebo recipients, corresponding to vaccine efficacy of 95.0% (95% confidence interval, 90.3 to 97.6). Among participants with or without evidence of previous infection, vaccine efficacy was 94.6% (95% confidence interval, 89.9 to 97.3). Efficacy was generally consistent across subgroups defined by age, sex, race, ethnicity, obesity, and coexisting conditions. Reactogenicity was more common after BNT162b2 than after placebo and was generally mild or moderate and transient. Serious adverse events were uncommon and occurred with similar frequency in the two groups.

Conclusions
A two-dose regimen of BNT162b2 conferred 95% protection against Covid-19 in persons 16 years of age or older. Safety over a median of 2 months was characterized mainly by short-term local and systemic reactions.

title: Safety and Efficacy of the BNT162b2 mRNA Covid-19 Vaccine
text: The severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS-CoV-2) pandemic necessitates the rapid development of a safe and effective vaccine to mitigate the profound global medical and economic impacts. We report the safety and efficacy results from the phase 2/3 portion of a global, phase 1/2/3, observer-blinded, placebo-controlled trial evaluating BNT162b2, a lipid nanoparticle-formulated, nucleoside-modified RNA vaccine encoding the prefusion-stabilized full-length spike protein of SARS-CoV-2. A total of 43,548 individuals aged 16 years or older were randomized to receive two doses of either 30 μg of BNT162b2 or placebo, administered intramuscularly 21 days apart. The primary endpoints were the safety of the vaccine and its efficacy in preventing coronavirus disease 2019 (Covid-19) with onset at least 7 days after the second dose in participants with and without prior evidence of SARS-CoV-2 infection. BNT162b2 was highly effective, demonstrating a 95% efficacy against Covid-19 (95% credible interval, 90.3 to 97.6). Among participants without evidence of prior infection, 8 cases of Covid-19 were observed in the vaccine group compared to 162 in the placebo group. The vaccine's efficacy was consistent across age, sex, race, ethnicity, and coexisting condition subgroups. The safety profile of BNT162b2 was favorable and characterized primarily by mild-to-moderate, transient local reactogenicity, such as injection-site pain, and systemic reactogenicity events, including fatigue, headache, and fever, which were more frequent after the second dose and in younger recipients. Severe adverse events and serious adverse events were rare and comparable to the placebo group. In conclusion, the two-dose regimen of the BNT162b2 mRNA vaccine confers robust protection against Covid-19 and exhibits a safety profile comparable to that of other viral vaccines, establishing it as a critical tool in managing the ongoing pandemic.

paper_title: Safety and Efficacy of the BNT162b2 mRNA Covid-19 Vaccine - 2020 - New England Journal of Medicine
display_title: Safety and Efficacy of the BNT162b2 mRNA Covid-19 Vaccine
title: Safety and Efficacy of the BNT162b2 mRNA Covid-19 Vaccine
text:
Coronavirus disease 2019 (Covid-19) has affected tens of millions of people globally since it was declared a pandemic by the World Health Organization on March 11, 2020. Older adults, persons with certain coexisting conditions, and front-line workers are at highest risk for Covid-19 and its complications. Recent data show increasing rates of severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS-CoV-2) infection and Covid-19 in other populations, including younger adults. Safe and effective prophylactic vaccines are urgently needed to contain the pandemic, which has had devastating medical, economic, and social consequences.

We previously reported phase 1 safety and immunogenicity results from clinical trials of the vaccine candidate BNT162b2, a lipid nanoparticle-formulated, nucleoside-modified RNA (modRNA) encoding the SARS-CoV-2 full-length spike, modified by two proline mutations to lock it in the prefusion conformation. Findings from studies conducted in the United States and Germany among healthy men and women showed that two 30-μg doses of BNT162b2 elicited high SARS-CoV-2 neutralizing antibody titers and robust antigen-specific CD8+ and Th1-type CD4+ T-cell responses. The 50% neutralizing geometric mean titers elicited by 30 μg of BNT162b2 in older and younger adults exceeded the geometric mean titer measured in a human convalescent serum panel, despite a lower neutralizing response in older adults than in younger adults. In addition, the reactogenicity profile of BNT162b2 represented mainly short-term local (i.e., injection site) and systemic responses. These findings supported progression of the BNT162b2 vaccine candidate into phase 3.

Here, we report safety and efficacy findings from the phase 2/3 part of a global phase 1/2/3 trial evaluating the safety, immunogenicity, and efficacy of 30 μg of BNT162b2 in preventing Covid-19 in persons 16 years of age or older. This data set and these trial results are the basis for an application for emergency use authorization. Collection of phase 2/3 data on vaccine immunogenicity and the durability of the immune response to immunization is ongoing, and those data are not reported here.

display_title: Safety and Efficacy of the BNT162b2 mRNA Covid-19 Vaccine
title: Safety and Efficacy of the BNT162b2 mRNA Covid-19 Vaccine
text:
Since its emergence in late 2019, severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS-CoV-2) has spread globally, causing substantial morbidity, mortality, and societal disruption. Coronavirus disease 2019 (Covid-19), the clinical illness caused by SARS-CoV-2, ranges from asymptomatic infection to respiratory failure and death, with older adults and persons with coexisting conditions at highest risk for severe outcomes. Although nonpharmaceutical interventions can reduce transmission, durable control of the pandemic requires vaccines that can be developed rapidly, manufactured at scale, and shown in large clinical trials to be safe and effective.

The trimeric spike glycoprotein of SARS-CoV-2 mediates viral entry and is the principal target of neutralizing antibodies. Vaccine candidates that induce immune responses against spike therefore represent a rational approach to prevention of Covid-19. RNA-based vaccines offer a platform in which the antigen is encoded directly by a synthetic RNA molecule, allowing rapid design after release of a viral genetic sequence. BNT162b2 is a lipid nanoparticle-formulated, nucleoside-modified RNA vaccine candidate encoding the full-length SARS-CoV-2 spike protein, modified by two proline substitutions to stabilize the protein in the prefusion conformation and preserve neutralization-sensitive epitopes.

In phase 1 testing, BNT162b2 induced SARS-CoV-2 neutralizing antibody responses and cellular immune responses while producing mainly transient local and systemic reactions. On the basis of these data, a 30-μg dose of BNT162b2 administered twice, 21 days apart, was selected for further evaluation. Establishing clinical efficacy and safety required a large, randomized trial with sufficient representation of persons at increased risk for Covid-19 complications and with prospective surveillance for symptomatic, laboratory-confirmed disease. We conducted an ongoing multinational, placebo-controlled, observer-blinded, phase 2/3 trial to evaluate the safety and efficacy of two doses of BNT162b2 in persons 16 years of age or older. Here, we report the primary efficacy analysis and safety data available through a median follow-up of approximately 2 months after the second dose.

title: Safety and Efficacy of the BNT162b2 mRNA Covid-19 Vaccine
text:
The emergence of severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS-CoV-2) in late 2019 has precipitated a devastating global health crisis. The resulting disease, coronavirus disease 2019 (Covid-19), rapidly evolved into a pandemic, causing unprecedented morbidity, mortality, and socioeconomic disruption worldwide. As the virus continues to spread, the rapid development and deployment of safe and effective vaccines are urgently required to control the pandemic and mitigate its catastrophic impacts.

SARS-CoV-2 is an enveloped, single-stranded RNA virus belonging to the Coronaviridae family. Its entry into host cells is mediated by the viral spike (S) glycoprotein, which binds to the angiotensin-converting enzyme 2 (ACE2) receptor on human cells. The S protein is a primary target for neutralizing antibodies, making it a critical antigen for vaccine development. Multiple vaccine platforms, including viral vectors, inactivated viruses, and recombinant proteins, have been rapidly mobilized. Among these, lipid nanoparticle-formulated messenger RNA (mRNA) vaccines have emerged as a highly versatile and rapid technology, capable of inducing robust humoral and cellular immune responses without the need for live virus handling during manufacturing.

We previously reported the phase 1 results of two mRNA vaccine candidates, BNT162b1 and BNT162b2. Both candidates elicited strong, dose-dependent SARS-CoV-2-neutralizing antibody responses and T-cell responses in healthy adults. However, BNT162b2, which encodes the full-length prefusion stabilized SARS-CoV-2 spike protein, demonstrated a more favorable safety and reactogenicity profile, particularly in older adults. Consequently, BNT162b2 was selected for advancement into pivotal, large-scale efficacy testing.

Here, we report the safety and efficacy results from the ongoing multinational, placebo-controlled, observer-blinded, phase 2/3 trial of the BNT162b2 vaccine. The primary objectives of this study were to evaluate the efficacy of a two-dose regimen of BNT162b2 in preventing symptomatic Covid-19 in individuals 16 years of age or older, and to characterize its safety profile across diverse demographic subgroups. The data presented herein represent a critical milestone in the global effort to establish a highly effective prophylactic intervention against SARS-CoV-2 infection.
historical_context: Published in late 2020 by Fernando Polack, Stephen Thomas, Uğur Şahin, Kathrin Jansen, and colleagues, this historic paper detailed the Phase 3 clinical trial results of the Pfizer-BioNTech COVID-19 vaccine (BNT162b2). The data showcased a stunning 95% efficacy rate in preventing COVID-19 alongside a highly reassuring safety profile, an achievement realized in less than a year from the moment the virus was first sequenced. This clinical paper represents an absolute triumph of modern medicine and scientific logistics. It unequivocally validated the entire mRNA vaccine platform, marking the very first time an mRNA therapy was authorized for widespread human use. It decisively altered the grim trajectory of the COVID-19 pandemic and violently accelerated a new frontier for rapid-response vaccines and personalized mRNA therapeutics.

paper_title: Safety and Efficacy of the BNT162b2 mRNA Covid-19 Vaccine - 2020 - New England Journal of Medicine
display_title: Safety and Efficacy of the BNT162b2 mRNA Covid-19 Vaccine
title: Safety and Efficacy of the BNT162b2 mRNA Covid-19 Vaccine
text:
A two-dose regimen of BNT162b2 (30 μg per dose, given 21 days apart) was found to be safe and 95% effective against Covid-19. The vaccine met both primary efficacy end points, with more than a 99.99% probability of a true vaccine efficacy greater than 30%. These results met our prespecified success criteria, which were to establish a probability above 98.6% of true vaccine efficacy being greater than 30%, and greatly exceeded the minimum FDA criteria for authorization. Although the study was not powered to definitively assess efficacy by subgroup, the point estimates of efficacy for subgroups based on age, sex, race, ethnicity, body-mass index, or the presence of an underlying condition associated with a high risk of Covid-19 complications are also high. For all analyzed subgroups in which more than 10 cases of Covid-19 occurred, the lower limit of the 95% confidence interval for efficacy was more than 30%.

The cumulative incidence of Covid-19 cases over time among placebo and vaccine recipients begins to diverge by 12 days after the first dose, 7 days after the estimated median viral incubation period of 5 days, indicating the early onset of a partially protective effect of immunization. The study was not designed to assess the efficacy of a single-dose regimen. Nevertheless, in the interval between the first and second doses, the observed vaccine efficacy against Covid-19 was 52%, and in the first 7 days after dose 2, it was 91%, reaching full efficacy against disease with onset at least 7 days after dose 2. Of the 10 cases of severe Covid-19 that were observed after the first dose, only 1 occurred in the vaccine group. This finding is consistent with overall high efficacy against all Covid-19 cases. The severe case split provides preliminary evidence of vaccine-mediated protection against severe disease, alleviating many of the theoretical concerns over vaccine-mediated disease enhancement.

The favorable safety profile observed during phase 1 testing of BNT162b2 was confirmed in the phase 2/3 portion of the trial. As in phase 1, reactogenicity was generally mild or moderate, and reactions were less common and milder in older adults than in younger adults. Systemic reactogenicity was more common and severe after the second dose than after the first dose, although local reactogenicity was similar after the two doses. Severe fatigue was observed in approximately 4% of BNT162b2 recipients, which is higher than that observed in recipients of some vaccines recommended for older adults. This rate of severe fatigue is also lower than that observed in recipients of another approved viral vaccine for older adults. Overall, reactogenicity events were transient and resolved within a couple of days after onset. Lymphadenopathy, which generally resolved within 10 days, is likely to have resulted from a robust vaccine-elicited immune response. The incidence of serious adverse events was similar in the vaccine and placebo groups (0.6% and 0.5%, respectively).

This trial and its preliminary report have several limitations. With approximately 19,000 participants per group in the subset of participants with a median follow-up time of 2 months after the second dose, the study has more than 83% probability of detecting at least one adverse event, if the true incidence is 0.01%, but it is not large enough to detect less common adverse events reliably. This report includes 2 months of follow-up after the second dose of vaccine for half the trial participants and up to 14 weeks' maximum follow-up for a smaller subset. Therefore, both the occurrence of adverse events more than 2 to 3.5 months after the second dose and more comprehensive information on the duration of protection remain to be determined. Although the study was designed to follow participants for safety and efficacy for 2 years after the second dose, given the high vaccine efficacy, ethical and practical barriers prevent following placebo recipients for 2 years without offering active immunization, once the vaccine is approved by regulators and recommended by public health authorities. Assessment of long-term safety and efficacy for this vaccine will occur, but it cannot be in the context of maintaining a placebo group for the planned follow-up period of 2 years after the second dose. These data do not address whether vaccination prevents asymptomatic infection; a serologic end point that can detect a history of infection regardless of whether symptoms were present (SARS-CoV-2 N-binding antibody) will be reported later. Furthermore, given the high vaccine efficacy and the low number of vaccine breakthrough cases, potential establishment of a correlate of protection has not been feasible at the time of this report.

This report does not address the prevention of Covid-19 in other populations, such as younger adolescents, children, and pregnant women. Safety and immune response data from this trial after immunization of adolescents 12 to 15 years of age will be reported subsequently, and additional studies are planned to evaluate BNT162b2 in pregnant women, children younger than 12 years, and those in special risk groups, such as immunocompromised persons. Although the vaccine can be stored for up to 5 days at standard refrigerator temperatures once ready for use, very cold temperatures are required for shipping and longer storage. The current cold storage requirement may be alleviated by ongoing stability studies and formulation optimization, which may also be described in subsequent reports.

The data presented in this report have significance beyond the performance of this vaccine candidate. The results demonstrate that Covid-19 can be prevented by immunization, provide proof of concept that RNA-based vaccines are a promising new approach for protecting humans against infectious diseases, and demonstrate the speed with which an RNA-based vaccine can be developed with a sufficient investment of resources. The development of BNT162b2 was initiated on January 10, 2020, when the SARS-CoV-2 genetic sequence was released by the Chinese Center for Disease Control and Prevention and disseminated globally by the GISAID (Global Initiative on Sharing All Influenza Data) initiative. This rigorous demonstration of safety and efficacy less than 11 months later provides a practical demonstration that RNA-based vaccines, which require only viral genetic sequence information to initiate development, are a major new tool to combat pandemics and other infectious disease outbreaks. The continuous phase 1/2/3 trial design may provide a model to reduce the protracted development timelines that have delayed the availability of vaccines against other infectious diseases of medical importance. In the context of the current, still expanding pandemic, the BNT162b2 vaccine, if approved, can contribute, together with other public health measures, to reducing the devastating loss of health, life, and economic and social well-being that has resulted from the global spread of Covid-19.

paper_title: Safety and Efficacy of the BNT162b2 mRNA Covid-19 Vaccine - 2020 - New England Journal of Medicine
display_title: Safety and Efficacy of the BNT162b2 mRNA Covid-19 Vaccine
title: Safety and Efficacy of the BNT162b2 mRNA Covid-19 Vaccine
text:
The data reported here show that a two-dose regimen of BNT162b2, administered 21 days apart, was highly effective in preventing Covid-19 in persons 16 years of age or older. Among participants without evidence of previous SARS-CoV-2 infection, 8 cases of Covid-19 with onset at least 7 days after the second dose occurred among vaccine recipients, as compared with 162 cases among placebo recipients, corresponding to a vaccine efficacy of 95.0%. Efficacy was similarly high in the analysis that included participants with or without evidence of prior infection. The probability that vaccine efficacy exceeded the prespecified success criterion was high, and the observed lower bound of the interval estimate was well above the efficacy threshold that had been established for regulatory consideration.

The findings are notable in the context of a rapidly expanding pandemic and a trial conducted during periods of substantial community transmission. Protection was evident after completion of the vaccination regimen and was also suggested during the interval between the first and second doses. The cumulative incidence curves began to separate approximately 12 days after the first dose, a time at which vaccine-induced immune responses would be expected to emerge. Although the trial was not designed to define the efficacy of a single-dose regimen, these observations are consistent with the biologic plausibility of early protection after the first dose and strong protection after the second dose.

Vaccine efficacy was generally consistent across subgroups defined by age, sex, race, ethnic group, body-mass index, and the presence of coexisting conditions. These subgroup analyses were descriptive and were based on smaller numbers of cases than the primary analysis, but the point estimates were similar to the overall estimate. This consistency is important because the burden of Covid-19 has been unevenly distributed across demographic groups and is greater among older adults and persons with certain underlying medical conditions. The enrollment of a large and diverse population, including substantial numbers of older participants, obese participants, and participants with coexisting conditions, supports the applicability of the results to populations at increased risk for Covid-19 and its complications.

The number of cases of severe Covid-19 was small, but the distribution favored BNT162b2. Among 10 cases of severe Covid-19 with onset after the first dose, 9 occurred among placebo recipients and 1 occurred among vaccine recipients. These data do not provide a precise estimate of efficacy against severe disease, but they are encouraging and are concordant with the high efficacy observed against symptomatic Covid-19. Continued follow-up will be important to better define protection against severe outcomes, hospitalization, and death, particularly as additional cases accrue.

The safety profile observed in this trial was characterized primarily by transient local and systemic reactogenicity. Pain at the injection site was the most common local reaction and was usually mild or moderate, with severe pain reported by less than 1% of participants. Systemic events, especially fatigue and headache, were more frequent among vaccine recipients than among placebo recipients and were more common after the second dose than after the first dose. Fever and chills were also observed more often after the second dose. These events generally occurred during the first 1 to 2 days after vaccination and resolved shortly thereafter. Younger vaccine recipients reported systemic events more frequently than older recipients, a pattern that is consistent with reactogenicity findings from other vaccines and with the phase 1 experience with BNT162b2.

Unsolicited adverse events, serious adverse events, and deaths did not suggest a safety concern over the period reported here. The imbalance in reported adverse events between the vaccine and placebo groups was largely attributable to reactogenicity events, which were recorded as adverse events more frequently among vaccine recipients. Lymphadenopathy occurred more often among BNT162b2 recipients than among placebo recipients and is plausibly related to vaccination. Serious adverse events were uncommon and occurred at similar frequencies in the two groups. No deaths were considered by the investigators to be related to vaccine or placebo, and no Covid-19-associated deaths were observed during the reporting period. The prespecified monitoring criteria for vaccine-associated enhanced disease were not met.

The results also support the clinical strategy embodied by the BNT162b2 vaccine platform. BNT162b2 is a nucleoside-modified RNA vaccine encoding the prefusion stabilized, membrane-anchored full-length SARS-CoV-2 spike protein. Previous phase 1 data showed robust neutralizing antibody responses and antigen-specific cellular immune responses after two 30-microgram doses. The clinical efficacy shown in the present trial provides evidence that this immunogenicity translates into protection against symptomatic disease. The trial does not establish a correlate of protection, and immunogenicity analyses from the phase 2/3 population are ongoing. Such analyses may help inform future vaccine development, the interpretation of immune responses in special populations, and possible strategies for booster vaccination.

Several limitations should be considered. The median safety follow-up after the second dose was approximately 2 months for the main safety population, and longer-term safety data remain necessary. Although uncommon adverse events can be detected only in larger populations and with longer observation, the size of this trial provides substantial short-term safety information. The trial was not powered to evaluate very rare adverse events, nor was it powered to precisely assess efficacy in all subgroups or against severe Covid-19. Children younger than 16 years of age, pregnant persons, and immunocompromised persons were not represented or were represented only to a limited extent in the analyses reported here. Additional studies and postauthorization surveillance will be needed to assess safety and efficacy in these populations.

The trial evaluated prevention of symptomatic Covid-19 confirmed by nucleic acid amplification testing. It did not directly assess prevention of asymptomatic infection, viral shedding, or transmission. If vaccination reduces asymptomatic infection or infectiousness, the population-level benefit may be greater than that measured by the primary end point; if it does not, nonpharmaceutical interventions may remain particularly important during vaccine deployment. The durability of protection is also unknown. Continued blinded follow-up and ongoing surveillance will be essential to determine the duration of efficacy, the need for additional doses, and the effect of viral evolution on vaccine performance.

Operational considerations are also relevant. The vaccine regimen evaluated here requires two doses given 21 days apart and storage and distribution procedures appropriate for an mRNA vaccine. These requirements may pose challenges for rapid deployment, particularly in settings with limited cold-chain capacity. Nevertheless, the high efficacy observed in this large multinational trial indicates that BNT162b2 has the potential to make a substantial contribution to controlling the Covid-19 pandemic, provided that vaccination can be delivered broadly and equitably.

In conclusion, BNT162b2 administered as two 30-microgram doses 21 days apart was safe over the period assessed and provided 95% protection against Covid-19 among persons 16 years of age or older. The vaccine was associated mainly with transient local and systemic reactions, and serious adverse events were uncommon. Ongoing follow-up will define longer-term safety, durability of protection, and efficacy against severe disease and in populations not fully represented in this analysis.
