paper_title: Safety and Efficacy of the BNT162b2 mRNA Covid-19 Vaccine - 2020 - New England Journal of Medicine
display_title: Safety and Efficacy of the BNT162b2 mRNA Covid-19 Vaccine
title: Safety and Efficacy of the BNT162b2 mRNA Covid-19 Vaccine
text:
The findings of this global, placebo-controlled, observer-blinded, pivotal phase 3 trial demonstrate that a two-dose regimen of the BNT162b2 mRNA vaccine provides a highly robust 95% efficacy against laboratory-confirmed Covid-19 in persons 16 years of age or older. Given the unprecedented scale and devastating impact of the SARS-CoV-2 pandemic, the rapid development and evaluation of this vaccine represent a critical milestone in global public health. The observed efficacy was remarkably consistent across all assessed subgroups, including those defined by age, sex, race, ethnicity, obesity, and the presence of diverse coexisting medical conditions. This broad-spectrum protection is particularly vital, as older adults and individuals with comorbidities are disproportionately vulnerable to severe Covid-19 complications and mortality.

Importantly, the protection conferred by BNT162b2 appears to emerge rapidly. The analysis of the interval between the first and second doses revealed an estimated vaccine efficacy of 52%, suggesting that even partial immunization initiates a meaningful level of clinical protection as early as 12 days following the primary injection. Furthermore, the trial data provide strong preliminary evidence that BNT162b2 effectively prevents severe Covid-19. Among the 10 cases of severe disease observed after the first dose, 9 occurred in the placebo group compared to only 1 in the vaccine cohort. While the absolute number of severe cases remains low at this interim stage, this trend is highly encouraging and aligns with the overarching goal of alleviating the profound strain on healthcare systems worldwide.

The safety profile of BNT162b2 is equally reassuring and compares favorably to those of widely used, well-established viral vaccines. Reactogenicity was generally mild to moderate, transient, and primarily characterized by local pain at the injection site, fatigue, and headache. These symptoms were typically more pronounced after the second dose and more frequently reported among younger participants compared to their older counterparts. Notably, the incidence of severe local and systemic reactions was low, and no grade 4 local reactions were reported. The overall frequency of severe adverse events and serious adverse events was comparably low in both the BNT162b2 and placebo groups, indicating that the lipid nanoparticle-formulated mRNA platform does not introduce unexpected or unmanageable toxicities. Furthermore, the theoretical concern of vaccine-enhanced disease—a phenomenon historically observed with certain other respiratory virus vaccines—has not materialized in this trial. There was no clinical or statistical evidence to suggest that vaccination predisposed individuals to more severe illness upon subsequent exposure to SARS-CoV-2.

The utilization of a nucleoside-modified RNA platform encoding the prefusion-stabilized spike protein offers several distinct advantages that have been instrumental to the success of BNT162b2. The modRNA approach ensures highly efficient in vivo expression of the target antigen while minimizing innate immune sensing that could otherwise prematurely curtail the translation of the therapeutic RNA. Additionally, the completely synthetic nature of the vaccine avoids the need for complex, time-consuming cell culture or viral replication processes, thereby enabling rapid design iteration and unprecedented scalability of manufacturing. This agility has been crucial in addressing the urgent global demand for billions of doses in a compressed timeframe, showcasing the unique benefits of mRNA technology for rapid pandemic response.

Despite these overwhelmingly positive outcomes, several important limitations and areas for ongoing investigation must be acknowledged. First, while the median follow-up of two months satisfies regulatory requirements for emergency use authorization, the long-term safety and durability of the immune response remain to be fully characterized. The trial will continue to monitor participants for up to two years to capture any potential late-onset adverse events and to determine whether booster doses will be necessary to maintain protective immunity over time. Second, the current data set does not definitively address the vaccine's efficacy in preventing asymptomatic SARS-CoV-2 infection or mitigating viral transmission. Further studies, including longitudinal serological assessments and real-world effectiveness analyses, will be essential to ascertain the extent to which widespread BNT162b2 administration can interrupt community transmission and contribute to herd immunity.

Furthermore, additional data are urgently needed regarding specific subpopulations that were either excluded from or underrepresented in this initial phase 3 cohort. For example, the safety and efficacy of the vaccine in pregnant women, children under the age of 16, and profoundly immunocompromised individuals—such as solid organ transplant recipients or patients undergoing active cytotoxic chemotherapy—require dedicated clinical evaluation. Preliminary steps are already underway to expand trial enrollment into younger pediatric demographics and specialized patient registries to close these knowledge gaps. The unique physiological states of these populations require rigorous empirical data to guide targeted clinical recommendations.

The logistical requirements associated with the distribution and storage of BNT162b2 also present unique challenges. The necessity for ultra-low temperature storage constraints necessitates sophisticated cold-chain infrastructure, which may pose implementation hurdles in resource-limited settings or remote geographical regions. Ongoing stability testing and formulation optimization efforts are critical to eventually relaxing these stringent storage requirements, thereby ensuring equitable global access to this life-saving intervention. Future iterations of the lipid nanoparticle formulation may yield more temperature-stable variants that can be seamlessly integrated into conventional pharmaceutical supply chains.

The broader implications of this successful trial extend beyond the immediate crisis. The clinical validation of a lipid nanoparticle mRNA vaccine represents a monumental leap forward in vaccinology. The unprecedented speed at which this candidate moved from viral sequencing to phase 3 efficacy demonstration provides a robust template for future outbreak preparedness. It confirms that precise, structure-based antigen design coupled with rapid synthetic manufacturing can effectively address novel emerging pathogens.

In conclusion, the data presented herein unequivocally demonstrate that the BNT162b2 mRNA vaccine is both highly effective and well tolerated. The 95% efficacy rate surpasses initial public health expectations and establishes a formidable new tool in the armamentarium against Covid-19. The robust protection observed across diverse demographic and clinical subgroups underscores the vaccine's broad applicability. While continued surveillance will be paramount to addressing outstanding questions regarding long-term durability and transmission dynamics, the immediate availability of BNT162b2 provides a scientifically rigorous and empirically validated pathway toward controlling the SARS-CoV-2 pandemic. The successful deployment of this mRNA technology not only offers hope for the present crisis but also represents a paradigm shift in vaccinology, paving the way for the rapid development of novel prophylactic and therapeutic interventions against future emerging infectious diseases.

paper_title: Safety, Activity, and Immune Correlates of Anti-PD-1 Antibody in Cancer - 2012 - New England Journal of Medicine
display_title: Safety, Activity, and Immune Correlates of Anti-PD-1 Antibody in Cancer
title: Safety, Activity, and Immune Correlates of Anti-PD-1 Antibody in Cancer
text:
Blockade of programmed death 1 (PD-1), an inhibitory receptor expressed by T cells, can overcome immune resistance. We assessed the antitumor activity and safety of BMS-936558, an antibody that specifically blocks PD-1.

We enrolled patients with advanced melanoma, non-small-cell lung cancer, castration-resistant prostate cancer, or renal-cell or colorectal cancer to receive anti-PD-1 antibody at a dose of 0.1 to 10.0 mg per kilogram of body weight every 2 weeks. Response was assessed after each 8-week treatment cycle. Patients received up to 12 cycles until disease progression or a complete response occurred.

A total of 296 patients received treatment through February 24, 2012. Grade 3 or 4 drug-related adverse events occurred in 14% of patients; there were three deaths from pulmonary toxicity. No maximum tolerated dose was defined. Adverse events consistent with immune-related causes were observed. Among 236 patients in whom response could be evaluated, objective responses (complete or partial responses) were observed in those with non-small-cell lung cancer, melanoma, or renal-cell cancer. Cumulative response rates (all doses) were 18% among patients with non-small-cell lung cancer (14 of 76 patients), 28% among patients with melanoma (26 of 94 patients), and 27% among patients with renal-cell cancer (9 of 33 patients). Responses were durable; 20 of 31 responses lasted 1 year or more in patients with 1 year or more of follow-up. To assess the role of intratumoral PD-1 ligand (PD-L1) expression in the modulation of the PD-1-PD-L1 pathway, immunohistochemical analysis was performed on pretreatment tumor specimens obtained from 42 patients. Of 17 patients with PD-L1-negative tumors, none had an objective response; 9 of 25 patients (36%) with PD-L1-positive tumors had an objective response (P = 0.006).

Anti-PD-1 antibody produced objective responses in approximately one in four to one in five patients with non-small-cell lung cancer, melanoma, or renal-cell cancer; the adverse-event profile does not appear to preclude its use. Preliminary data suggest a relationship between PD-L1 expression on tumor cells and objective response.
historical_context: In 2012, Suzanne Topalian, F. Stephen Hodi, and colleagues published the landmark Phase 1 clinical trial results testing the anti-PD-1 antibody nivolumab across a spectrum of solid tumors. The study revealed highly durable, robust tumor responses in patients suffering from advanced melanoma, non-small-cell lung cancer, and renal-cell cancer—malignancies historically notorious for resisting standard therapies. This clinical paper was a turning point because it provided undeniable proof that PD-1 blockade was both safe and exceptionally potent in humans. It cemented immune checkpoint inhibition as a broad, formidable new pillar of oncology alongside surgery, radiation, and chemotherapy, igniting a medical revolution in cancer care that continues to save countless lives.

paper_title: Safety, Activity, and Immune Correlates of Anti-PD-1 Antibody in Cancer - 2012 - New England Journal of Medicine
display_title: Safety, Activity, and Immune Correlates of Anti-PD-1 Antibody in Cancer
title: Safety, Activity, and Immune Correlates of Anti-PD-1 Antibody in Cancer
text:
Background
Programmed death 1 (PD-1) is an inhibitory receptor expressed by activated T cells and is engaged by ligands that may be expressed by tumor cells and stromal cells. Blockade of this pathway may restore antitumor immunity. We evaluated the safety, antitumor activity, pharmacokinetics, and immune correlates of repeated doses of the anti-PD-1 antibody BMS-936558 in patients with advanced cancer.

Methods
In this phase 1 study, patients with advanced melanoma, non-small-cell lung cancer, renal-cell cancer, castration-resistant prostate cancer, or colorectal cancer received BMS-936558 at doses ranging from 0.1 to 10.0 mg per kilogram every 2 weeks. Treatment was continued for up to 2 years unless a complete response, unacceptable toxic effects, or progressive disease occurred. Tumor responses were assessed after each 8-week cycle. PD-1-receptor occupancy on circulating T cells and pretreatment tumor-cell expression of PD-L1 were evaluated in available specimens.

Results
A total of 296 patients were treated, and 236 could be evaluated for efficacy. A maximum tolerated dose was not reached. Grade 3 or 4 treatment-related adverse events occurred in 14% of patients, and 5% discontinued therapy because of treatment-related adverse events. Drug-related pneumonitis occurred in 3% of patients and was fatal in three patients. Objective responses were observed in patients with non-small-cell lung cancer, melanoma, and renal-cell cancer at all dose levels tested. Response rates were 6 to 32% in non-small-cell lung cancer, 19 to 41% in melanoma, and 24 to 31% in renal-cell cancer, according to dose level. Many responses were durable; among patients with at least 1 year of follow-up after response, 20 of 31 had responses lasting 1 year or more. No objective responses were observed in prostate or colorectal cancer. Anti-PD-1 antibody showed linear pharmacokinetics, and receptor occupancy on circulating T cells was sustained. Among 42 patients with evaluable tumors, objective responses occurred in 9 of 25 patients with PD-L1-positive tumors and in none of 17 patients with PD-L1-negative tumors.

Conclusions
Anti-PD-1 antibody produced durable objective responses in several advanced solid tumors, including non-small-cell lung cancer, with manageable but potentially serious immune-related toxic effects. Tumor PD-L1 expression warrants further study as a predictive biomarker.
historical_context: In 2012, Suzanne Topalian, F. Stephen Hodi, and colleagues published the landmark Phase 1 clinical trial results testing the anti-PD-1 antibody nivolumab across a spectrum of solid tumors. The study revealed highly durable, robust tumor responses in patients suffering from advanced melanoma, non-small-cell lung cancer, and renal-cell cancer—malignancies historically notorious for resisting standard therapies. This clinical paper was a turning point because it provided undeniable proof that PD-1 blockade was both safe and exceptionally potent in humans. It cemented immune checkpoint inhibition as a broad, formidable new pillar of oncology alongside surgery, radiation, and chemotherapy, igniting a medical revolution in cancer care that continues to save countless lives.

We enrolled patients with advanced melanoma, non-small-cell lung cancer, castration-resistant prostate cancer, renal-cell cancer, or colorectal cancer to receive anti-PD-1 antibody at a dose of 0.1 to 10.0 mg per kilogram of body weight every 2 weeks. A total of 296 patients received treatment through February 24, 2012. Grade 3 or 4 drug-related adverse events occurred in 14% of patients; there were three deaths from pulmonary toxicity. No maximum tolerated dose was defined. Adverse events consistent with immune-related causes were observed. Of 17 patients with PD-L1-negative tumors, none had an objective response; 9 of 25 patients (36%) with PD-L1-positive tumors had an objective response.

Conclusions

Anti-PD-1 antibody produced objective responses in approximately one in four to one in five patients with non-small-cell lung cancer, melanoma, or renal-cell cancer; the adverse-event profile does not appear to preclude its use. Preliminary data suggest an association between PD-L1 expression on tumor cells and objective response.

title: Safety, Activity, and Immune Correlates of Anti-PD-1 Antibody in Cancer
text: Tumors frequently exploit immune checkpoint pathways, such as the programmed death 1 (PD-1) receptor pathway, to evade immune-mediated destruction. Blocking the interaction between PD-1 and its ligands is a promising immunotherapeutic strategy to enhance T-cell responses and restore antitumor immunity. In this phase 1 study, we evaluated the safety, pharmacokinetics, and clinical activity of BMS-936558, a fully human IgG4 blocking monoclonal antibody targeting PD-1, in patients with advanced, heavily pretreated solid tumors. Patients with melanoma, non-small-cell lung cancer (NSCLC), renal-cell cancer, castration-resistant prostate cancer, and colorectal cancer received intravenous infusions of anti-PD-1 antibody at doses of 0.1 to 10.0 mg per kilogram of body weight every 2 weeks. A total of 296 patients were enrolled. The antibody exhibited a favorable safety profile; grade 3 or 4 treatment-related adverse events occurred in 14% of patients. The most common drug-related adverse events were fatigue, rash, diarrhea, pruritus, and nausea. Immune-related adverse events, including pneumonitis, vitiligo, colitis, and hepatitis, were observed, with mild-to-moderate pneumonitis manageable via treatment interruption or glucocorticoids. Three deaths related to pneumonitis occurred (1%). Antitumor activity was observed across multiple tumor types and dose levels. Among 236 evaluable patients, objective responses were achieved in 18% of those with NSCLC (14 of 76 patients), 28% of those with melanoma (26 of 94 patients), and 27% of those with renal-cell cancer (9 of 33 patients). Notably, the responses were remarkably durable; 20 of 31 responding patients who were followed for 1 year or more maintained their response for 1 year or longer. Preliminary biomarker analysis suggested an association between the expression of the PD-1 ligand PD-L1 on tumor cells in pretreatment biopsy specimens and objective clinical response to PD-1 blockade. In conclusion, anti-PD-1 antibody therapy is safe, tolerable, and induces substantial and highly durable tumor regression in a diverse range of advanced cancers, including NSCLC, thereby broadening the potential applications of immune checkpoint blockade in oncology.

title: Safety, Activity, and Immune Correlates of Anti-PD-1 Antibody in Cancer
text:
Human cancers harbor numerous genetic and epigenetic alterations, generating neoantigens that are potentially recognizable by the immune system. Although an endogenous immune response to cancer is observed in preclinical models and patients, this response is ineffective, because tumors develop multiple resistance mechanisms, including local immune suppression, induction of tolerance, and systemic dysfunction in T-cell signaling. Moreover, tumors may exploit several distinct pathways to actively evade immune destruction, including endogenous 'immune checkpoints' that normally terminate immune responses after antigen activation. These observations have resulted in intensive efforts to develop immunotherapeutic approaches for cancer, including immune-checkpoint-pathway inhibitors such as anti-CTLA-4 antibody (ipilimumab) for the treatment of patients with advanced melanoma.

Programmed death 1 (PD-1) is a key immune-checkpoint receptor expressed by activated T cells, and it mediates immunosuppression. PD-1 functions primarily in peripheral tissues, where T cells may encounter the immunosuppressive PD-1 ligands PD-L1 (B7-H1) and PD-L2 (B7-DC), which are expressed by tumor cells, stromal cells, or both. Inhibition of the interaction between PD-1 and PD-L1 can enhance T-cell responses in vitro and mediate preclinical antitumor activity. In a dose-escalation study, the anti-PD-1 monoclonal antibody BMS-936558 (also known as MDX-1106 and ONO-4538) was administered as a single dose in 39 patients with advanced solid tumors. A favorable safety profile and preliminary evidence of clinical activity were shown in this pilot study, establishing the basis for the current multiple-dose trial involving patients with diverse cancers. We report clinical results for 296 patients in this trial.

title: Safety, Activity, and Immune Correlates of Anti-PD-1 Antibody in Cancer
text:
Therapies that augment antitumor immunity have produced durable tumor regressions in some patients with advanced cancer, but their clinical benefit has been limited by incomplete response rates and mechanism-specific toxic effects. One approach to improving this therapeutic index is to block inhibitory pathways that restrain activated T cells within the tumor microenvironment. Programmed death 1 (PD-1) is an inhibitory receptor expressed on activated T cells, B cells, and myeloid cells. Engagement of PD-1 by its ligands, PD-L1 and PD-L2, attenuates T-cell receptor signaling, cytokine production, and cytolytic function, thereby limiting immune-mediated tissue injury but also providing a mechanism by which tumors may evade immune destruction.

Expression of PD-L1 has been observed in multiple human cancers and may be induced by inflammatory signals in the tumor microenvironment. This pattern suggests that the PD-1–PD-L1 pathway can mediate adaptive immune resistance: an endogenous antitumor immune response may induce a counterregulatory signal that protects tumor cells from elimination. Preclinical studies have shown that blockade of this pathway can enhance antitumor T-cell responses, and early clinical observations have suggested that inhibition of PD-1 may have activity in advanced malignancies.

BMS-936558 is a fully human IgG4 monoclonal antibody that blocks PD-1. We conducted a phase 1 study to evaluate the safety, pharmacokinetics, pharmacodynamics, and antitumor activity of BMS-936558 in patients with selected advanced solid tumors and to explore whether pretreatment tumor-cell expression of PD-L1 was associated with objective response.

title: Safety, Activity, and Immune Correlates of Anti-PD-1 Antibody in Cancer
text:
The immune system is capable of recognizing and eliminating tumor cells; however, tumors often exploit immune checkpoint pathways to evade immune surveillance and destruction. One such pathway is mediated by the programmed death 1 (PD-1) receptor, an inhibitory molecule expressed on activated T cells, B cells, and natural killer cells. When PD-1 engages its ligands, PD-L1 (B7-H1) or PD-L2 (B7-DC), which are frequently upregulated on the surface of various solid tumors, it transduces inhibitory signals that suppress T-cell proliferation, cytokine production, and cytolytic activity. This mechanism of adaptive immune resistance effectively blunts the antitumor immune response, allowing cancer cells to proliferate unchecked.

Preclinical models have demonstrated that blockade of the PD-1–PD-L1 interaction can reinvigorate exhausted T cells and restore their antitumor activity, leading to durable tumor regression. Based on these compelling biological rationales, monoclonal antibodies targeting PD-1 or its ligands have entered clinical development. BMS-936558 is a fully human IgG4 monoclonal antibody that specifically binds to PD-1, thereby preventing its interaction with both PD-L1 and PD-L2.

We conducted a phase 1 dose-escalation and expansion study to evaluate the safety, pharmacokinetics, and clinical activity of BMS-936558 in patients with selected advanced solid tumors. In addition to assessing the tolerability and objective response rates across different tumor types and dose levels, we investigated the potential role of PD-L1 expression on tumor cells as a predictive biomarker for clinical response to PD-1 blockade therapy.

This clinical paper was a turning point because it provided undeniable proof that PD-1 blockade was both safe and exceptionally potent in humans. It cemented immune checkpoint inhibition as a broad, formidable new pillar of oncology alongside surgery, radiation, and chemotherapy, igniting a medical revolution in cancer care that continues to save countless lives.

paper_title: Safety, Activity, and Immune Correlates of Anti-PD-1 Antibody in Cancer - 2012 - New England Journal of Medicine
display_title: Safety, Activity, and Immune Correlates of Anti-PD-1 Antibody in Cancer
title: Safety, Activity, and Immune Correlates of Anti-PD-1 Antibody in Cancer
text:
Our data show that approximately one in four to one in five patients treated with anti-PD-1 antibody had objective responses with durability; these occurred in heavily pretreated patients with diverse tumor types. PD-1 blockade extends the spectrum of clinical activity by immunotherapy beyond immunogenic tumor types, such as melanoma and renal-cell cancer, to treatment-refractory, metastatic non-small-cell lung cancer, a tumor type that is generally not considered to be responsive to immunotherapy. The level of activity seen with anti-PD-1 antibody in patients with lung cancer who had received substantial amounts of prior therapy (55% with at least three lines of previous therapy) and across histologic types is of interest, particularly in the patients with squamous tumors. These unexpected findings underscore the possibility that any neoplasm could be immunogenic with proper immune activation; however, the reason why only a minority of patients had a response (i.e., tumor or patient [host] factors or both) is not known. The full therapeutic potential of PD-1 blockade across other tumor types remains to be defined.

The durability of objective responses across multiple cancer types in patients treated with anti-PD-1 antibody was also notable. Although anti-PD-1 antibody was not compared with other therapies in this study, this durability contrasts with the relatively modest durability of objective responses observed in many patients with non-small-cell lung cancer, melanoma, or renal-cell cancer who are treated with conventional chemotherapy, tyrosine kinase inhibitors, or both. The response durability in heavily pretreated patients with lung cancer who received anti-PD-1 antibody is particularly interesting, because standard salvage therapies historically have shown modest benefit in these patients. As measured by standard RECIST criteria in this study, objective responses were long-lasting, with response durations of 1 year or more in 20 of 31 patients who had a response with 1 year or more of follow-up. In addition, patterns of tumor regression consistent with immune-related patterns of response were observed. That is, index lesions often responded as previously undetected lesions became detectable, a finding that is possibly related to lymphocyte infiltration of previously unknown small nests of tumor cells. Although the full effect of these unconventional response patterns remains to be defined in randomized trials with survival end points, these observations are reminiscent of findings with ipilimumab in which a significant extension of overall survival was observed in treated patients.

Drug-related grade 3 or 4 toxic effects occurred in 14% of patients who received anti-PD-1 antibody, suggesting that therapy can be delivered in an outpatient setting with minimal supportive care. Among adverse events of special interest, pneumonitis was observed, with findings ranging from isolated radiographic abnormalities to progressive, diffuse infiltrates associated with clinical symptoms in a small number of patients. Although three deaths occurred, mild-to-moderate pneumonitis was managed successfully with either observation or glucocorticoids.

A particular challenge in cancer immunotherapy has been the identification of mechanism-based predictive biomarkers that could be used to identify candidates for such treatment and guide disease-management decisions. Our findings suggest that PD-L1 expression in tumors is a candidate molecular marker that warrants further exploration for use in selecting patients for immunotherapy with anti-PD-1 antibody. Our observation of an objective response in 36% of the patients with PD-L1-positive tumors and in none of those with PD-L1-negative tumors suggests that PD-L1 expression on the surface of tumor cells in pretreatment tumor specimens may be associated with an objective response. Although tumor-cell expression of PD-L1 may be driven by constitutive oncogenic pathways, recent research suggests that it may also reflect adaptive immune resistance in response to an endogenous antitumor immune response, which may remain in check unless it is unleashed through blockade of the PD-1-PD-L1 pathway. Although our data on PD-L1 expression are consistent with current knowledge of the role of PD-L1 in tumor immune resistance, additional studies will be needed to define the role of PD-L1 as a potential predictive marker of response to anti-PD-1 antibody therapy.

This study and a companion study of anti-PD-L1 antibody describe clinical activity with these agents that validates the importance of the PD-1-PD-L1 pathway for the treatment of some cancers. The signals of clinical activity in patients with non-small-cell lung cancer, melanoma, and renal-cell cancer treated with anti-PD-1 antibody and the possibility of basing patient selection for such treatment on PD-L1 expression in tumors need to be prospectively assessed. Phase 2 trials involving immunologic and molecular-marker correlates are under way, and phase 3 studies of anti-PD-1 antibody for the treatment of non-small-cell lung cancer, melanoma, and renal-cell cancer are being planned. An assessment of such treatment for other tumor types is also of interest.

This clinical paper was a turning point because it provided undeniable proof that PD-1 blockade was both safe and exceptionally potent in humans. paper_title: Safety, Activity, and Immune Correlates of Anti-PD-1 Antibody in Cancer - 2012 - New England Journal of Medicine
display_title: Safety, Activity, and Immune Correlates of Anti-PD-1 Antibody in Cancer
title: Safety, Activity, and Immune Correlates of Anti-PD-1 Antibody in Cancer
text:
In this phase 1 study, repeated administration of the anti-PD-1 antibody BMS-936558 produced objective antitumor responses in patients with several advanced solid tumors, including non-small-cell lung cancer, melanoma, and renal-cell cancer. Responses were observed in heavily pretreated patients, across dose levels, and in diverse sites of metastatic disease. A substantial proportion of responses were durable, with many continuing for 1 year or longer. These findings provide clinical evidence that blockade of PD-1 can restore antitumor immunity in patients with established cancer and that the PD-1 pathway is an active mechanism of immune resistance in some human tumors.

The activity observed in non-small-cell lung cancer is particularly notable. Lung cancer has not traditionally been considered a tumor type in which immunotherapy has a major role, and previous immune-based approaches have had limited success. In the current study, objective responses were seen in both squamous and nonsquamous non-small-cell lung cancer, including responses that were sustained. This observation broadens the range of tumors in which immune-checkpoint blockade may be relevant and suggests that tumor types characterized by a high burden of somatic mutation may generate antigens capable of supporting an effective immune response once inhibitory signaling is relieved. Responses in melanoma and renal-cell cancer are also consistent with the known sensitivity of these diseases to immune modulation, but the magnitude and durability of benefit seen with PD-1 blockade suggest that this pathway may be distinct from, and potentially complementary to, other immunotherapeutic strategies.

The pattern of response in some patients differed from that typically observed with cytotoxic agents. Several patients had persistent reductions in target lesions despite the appearance of new lesions and were therefore not counted as having objective responses according to conventional criteria. Such findings are compatible with previously described immune-related response patterns and underscore the difficulty of applying standard radiographic criteria to agents that depend on modulation of the host immune response. Although objective response remains an important and conservative end point, future studies of PD-1 blockade should consider response criteria and trial designs that capture delayed or atypical responses without compromising patient safety.

Treatment-related adverse events were generally manageable, and a maximum tolerated dose was not reached within the dose range studied. The most common treatment-related events were constitutional, dermatologic, or gastrointestinal in nature. Events with potential immune-related causes, including colitis, hepatitis, thyroiditis, hypophysitis, vitiligo, and pneumonitis, were observed, a finding consistent with the mechanism of action of PD-1 blockade. Most hepatic, gastrointestinal, and endocrine events were reversible or manageable with treatment interruption, glucocorticoids, or hormone replacement. However, pneumonitis occurred in a small number of patients and was associated with three treatment-related deaths. This toxicity indicates that although the overall adverse-event profile does not appear to preclude further development, careful monitoring for pulmonary symptoms and early intervention are essential in subsequent studies.

The pharmacokinetic and pharmacodynamic findings support the feasibility of sustained PD-1 blockade with dosing every 2 weeks. Serum exposure increased in a dose-proportional manner, and receptor occupancy on circulating T cells was observed across dose levels. The absence of a clear relationship between dose and response in this study should be interpreted cautiously, given the small size of the individual cohorts and the nonrandomized nature of much of the dose expansion. Nonetheless, the observation of activity at multiple doses suggests that biologically meaningful PD-1 inhibition may be achieved without defining a conventional maximum tolerated dose, a feature that distinguishes immune-checkpoint inhibitors from many cytotoxic agents.

The exploratory analysis of PD-L1 expression provides preliminary evidence that intratumoral expression of the PD-1 ligand may identify patients more likely to respond to anti-PD-1 therapy. Objective responses were observed among patients with PD-L1-positive tumors, whereas no objective responses were observed among patients whose tumors were negative for PD-L1 in the specimens tested. These results are consistent with the hypothesis that tumor-cell expression of PD-L1 reflects an adaptive immune-resistance mechanism that can be reversed by PD-1 blockade. However, this analysis was performed in a limited, nonrandom subset of patients, with archival or newly obtained tissue and without a prespecified statistical hypothesis. PD-L1 expression may be heterogeneous within tumors, may vary over time, and may be influenced by prior therapy or local immune activity. Therefore, these findings should be regarded as hypothesis-generating and require prospective validation with standardized assays.

This study has several limitations. It was an early-phase trial, with heterogeneous tumor types, prior treatments, and dose cohorts. The efficacy population was smaller than the total treated population, and follow-up was variable. The study was not designed to compare dose levels, to estimate survival benefit, or to establish the predictive value of PD-L1 expression. In addition, patients with active autoimmune disease or conditions requiring immunosuppression were excluded, and the safety of PD-1 blockade in such populations remains uncertain.

In conclusion, BMS-936558 showed durable antitumor activity in patients with advanced non-small-cell lung cancer, melanoma, and renal-cell cancer, with a toxicity profile that warrants continued clinical development but requires vigilance for immune-mediated adverse events, particularly pneumonitis. The preliminary association between tumor PD-L1 expression and response supports further investigation of biomarkers that may guide patient selection. These results establish PD-1 blockade as a promising therapeutic approach and provide the basis for larger studies to define its efficacy, safety, optimal dosing, and role across tumor types.

The study revealed highly durable, robust tumor responses in patients suffering from advanced melanoma, non-small-cell lung cancer, and renal-cell cancer—malignancies historically notorious for resisting standard therapies. This clinical paper was a turning point because it provided undeniable proof that PD-1 blockade was both safe and exceptionally potent in humans. It cemented immune checkpoint inhibition as a broad, formidable new pillar of oncology alongside surgery, radiation, and chemotherapy, igniting a medical revolution in cancer care that continues to save countless lives.

paper_title: Safety, Activity, and Immune Correlates of Anti-PD-1 Antibody in Cancer - 2012 - New England Journal of Medicine
display_title: Safety, Activity, and Immune Correlates of Anti-PD-1 Antibody in Cancer
title: Safety, Activity, and Immune Correlates of Anti-PD-1 Antibody in Cancer
text:
The results of this study demonstrate that blockade of the PD-1 immune checkpoint with the fully human IgG4 monoclonal antibody BMS-936558 mediates significant and durable antitumor activity across a diverse spectrum of advanced solid tumors. Objective responses were observed in approximately 18% to 28% of patients with heavily pretreated non-small-cell lung cancer, melanoma, and renal-cell cancer. These findings are particularly notable given that the patient population consisted largely of individuals whose disease had progressed despite multiple prior lines of systemic therapy, underscoring the potent therapeutic potential of releasing endogenous immune suppression.

The clinical activity observed with anti-PD-1 therapy distinctively differs from traditional cytotoxic chemotherapy. We noted that objective responses are frequently highly durable, with many lasting one year or more among patients with adequate follow-up. This durability is consistent with the induction of a robust, self-sustaining memory T-cell response capable of providing long-term tumor control. Furthermore, the spectrum of responding histologies expands the horizon of cancer immunotherapy. While melanoma and renal-cell cancer have historically been considered immunogenic, the substantial response rate in non-small-cell lung cancer—a disease generally considered refractory to classical immunotherapeutic approaches—represents a major paradigm shift. The responses in both squamous and nonsquamous lung cancer histologies suggest that PD-1 pathway dependency is a fundamental mechanism of immune evasion across varied tumor microenvironments.

The safety profile of BMS-936558 is generally favorable and manageable. The majority of adverse events were mild to moderate, with grade 3 or 4 drug-related events occurring in only 14% of patients. Most importantly, the adverse events largely align with the mechanism of action, characterized by phenomena with potential immune-related etiologies, such as rash, diarrhea, and endocrine dysfunctions. These immune-related adverse events were typically reversible with prompt recognition and the judicious administration of immunosuppressive agents, predominantly glucocorticoids, without permanently compromising the observed antitumor efficacy. However, the occurrence of drug-related pneumonitis, which was fatal in three cases, highlights the critical need for vigilance. Early identification of respiratory symptoms, immediate discontinuation of the antibody, and aggressive immunosuppressive intervention remain paramount to mitigating this severe pulmonary toxicity.

A maximum tolerated dose was not defined up to the highest tested dose of 10.0 mg per kilogram, and the spectrum and severity of toxicities did not demonstrate a clear dose-dependent relationship. This, coupled with the observation of robust clinical activity across multiple dose levels, suggests a broad therapeutic window for anti-PD-1 therapy. The pharmacokinetic profile, characterized by dose-proportional exposure and a prolonged half-life, supports the feasibility of biweekly or potentially less frequent dosing schedules. Moreover, our pharmacodynamic analysis revealed that near-maximal PD-1 receptor occupancy on circulating CD3+ T cells was achieved even at the lowest tested doses, providing a mechanistic rationale for the lack of a strict dose-response correlation for efficacy.

Perhaps one of the most compelling aspects of this study is the preliminary evidence correlating intratumoral PD-L1 expression with clinical benefit. Our immunohistochemical analysis revealed that all objective responses occurred exclusively in patients whose tumors expressed PD-L1 on the cell surface. Conversely, none of the patients lacking detectable PD-L1 expression experienced an objective response. Although this biomarker analysis was conducted retrospectively in a nonrandomized subset of patients and must be interpreted cautiously, it strongly suggests that PD-L1 expression could serve as a valuable predictive biomarker. The localized expression of PD-L1 by tumor cells or within the tumor microenvironment may delineate the subset of patients whose immune evasion is primarily driven by the PD-1-PD-L1 axis, thereby identifying those most likely to respond to PD-1 blockade. If prospectively validated, this could significantly refine patient selection, minimizing exposure to potential toxicities for those unlikely to benefit while maximizing the therapeutic impact.

In conclusion, BMS-936558 demonstrates manageable safety and remarkable, durable clinical activity in multiple advanced solid tumors, including those historically resistant to immunotherapy. The identification of objective responses in non-small-cell lung cancer challenges previous assumptions regarding the immunogenicity of this disease. The relationship between PD-L1 expression and objective response provides a critical foundation for biomarker-driven clinical development. These promising phase 1 results establish a compelling rationale for the continued, rigorous investigation of PD-1 pathway inhibitors in randomized, phase 3 clinical trials, offering renewed hope for patients with refractory malignancies and marking a significant advance in the field of cancer immunotherapy.

paper_title: Search-and-Replace Genome Editing without Double-Strand Breaks or Donor DNA - 2019 - Nature
display_title: Search-and-Replace Genome Editing without Double-Strand Breaks or Donor DNA
title: Search-and-Replace Genome Editing without Double-Strand Breaks or Donor DNA
text: Most genetic variants that contribute to disease are challenging to correct efficiently and without excess byproducts. Here we describe prime editing, a versatile and precise genome editing method that directly writes new genetic information into a specified DNA site using a catalytically impaired Cas9 fused to an engineered reverse transcriptase, programmed with a prime editing guide RNA (pegRNA) that both specifies the target site and encodes the desired edit. We performed >175 edits in human cells including targeted insertions, deletions, and all 12 types of point mutations without requiring double-strand breaks or donor DNA templates. We applied prime editing in human cells to correct efficiently and with few byproducts the primary genetic causes of sickle cell disease (requiring a transversion in HBB) and Tay-Sachs disease (requiring a deletion in HEXA), to install a protective transversion in PRNP, and to precisely insert various tags and epitopes into target loci. Four human cell lines and primary post-mitotic mouse cortical neurons support prime editing with varying efficiencies. Prime editing shows higher or similar efficiency and fewer byproducts than homology-directed repair, complementary strengths and weaknesses compared to base editing, and much lower off-target editing than Cas9 nuclease at known Cas9 off-target sites. Prime editing substantially expands the scope and capabilities of genome editing, and in principle can correct up to 89% of known genetic variants associated with human diseases.
historical_context: In 2019, Andrew Anzalone, David Liu, and colleagues published this monumental paper introducing "prime editing," an incredibly versatile evolution of the CRISPR toolkit. Prime editors physically fuse a catalytically impaired Cas9 to a reverse transcriptase and deploy an engineered prime editing guide RNA (pegRNA) to write fresh genetic information directly into a target DNA site, elegantly bypassing the need for double-strand breaks or separate donor DNA templates. This paper massively expanded the genome editing landscape. While base editing was restricted to specific single-letter swaps, prime editing unlocked genuine "search-and-replace" capabilities, permitting highly targeted insertions, deletions, and all 12 possible point mutations. This breakthrough vastly broadened the horizon of genetic engineering, theoretically putting the correction of up to 89% of known pathogenic human genetic variants within reach.

paper_title: Search-and-Replace Genome Editing without Double-Strand Breaks or Donor DNA - 2019 - Nature
display_title: Search-and-Replace Genome Editing without Double-Strand Breaks or Donor DNA
title: Search-and-Replace Genome Editing without Double-Strand Breaks or Donor DNA
text: The ability to install any desired genomic change in living cells remains a central goal of genome editing. Current methods that use programmable nucleases can efficiently disrupt genes, but precise correction of pathogenic alleles commonly requires double-strand DNA breaks (DSBs) and donor DNA templates, which can generate mixtures of indels, translocations, and inefficient homology-directed repair outcomes. Base editors avoid DSBs and donor templates, but are presently limited to transition mutations and cannot directly mediate most transversions, targeted insertions, or targeted deletions. Here we describe prime editing, a versatile search-and-replace genome editing strategy that uses a Cas9 nickase fused to an engineered reverse transcriptase and a prime editing guide RNA (pegRNA) that both specifies the target site and encodes the desired edit. The nicked target DNA primes reverse transcription of the pegRNA-encoded sequence, generating an edited DNA flap that can be incorporated by cellular repair. Optimized prime editors install all 12 possible base-to-base conversions, small insertions, small deletions, larger deletions, and combinations of these edits in human cells without requiring DSBs or exogenous donor DNA. Prime editing efficiencies commonly reached 20-50% in HEK293T cells, with substantially fewer indels than Cas9-initiated homology-directed repair, and with low activity at known Cas9 off-target sites. Prime editing also corrected or installed pathogenic alleles including HBB E6V, HEXA 1278+TATC, and CFTR DeltaF508-related edits. These results establish prime editing as a broadly applicable precision genome editing platform with the potential to address most known pathogenic human genetic variants.
historical_context: In 2019, Andrew Anzalone, David Liu, and colleagues published this monumental paper introducing "prime editing," an incredibly versatile evolution of the CRISPR toolkit. paper_title: Search-and-Replace Genome Editing without Double-Strand Breaks or Donor DNA - 2019 - Nature
display_title: Search-and-Replace Genome Editing without Double-Strand Breaks or Donor DNA
title: Search-and-Replace Genome Editing without Double-Strand Breaks or Donor DNA
text:
The ability to make virtually any targeted change in the genome of any living cell or organism is a longstanding aspiration of the life sciences. Despite rapid advances in genome editing technologies, the majority of the >75,000 known human genetic variants associated with diseases remain difficult to correct or install in most therapeutically relevant cell types. Programmable nucleases such as CRISPR-Cas9 make double-strand DNA breaks (DSBs) that can disrupt genes by inducing mixtures of insertions and deletions (indels) at target sites. DSBs, however, are associated with undesired outcomes including complex mixtures of products, translocations, and p53 activation. Moreover, the vast majority of pathogenic alleles arise from specific insertions, deletions, or base substitutions that require more precise editing technologies to correct. Homology-directed repair (HDR) stimulated by DSBs has been widely used to install precise DNA changes. HDR, however, relies on exogenous donor DNA repair templates, typically generates an excess of indels from end-joining repair of DSBs, and is inefficient in most therapeutically relevant cell types (T cells and some types of stem cells being important exceptions). While enhancing the efficiency and precision of DSB-mediated editing remains the focus of promising efforts, these challenges motivate the exploration of alternative precision genome editing strategies.

Base editing can efficiently install the four transition mutations (C → T, G → A, A → G, and T → C) without requiring DSBs in many cell types and organisms, including mammals, but cannot currently perform the eight transversion mutations (C → A, C → G, G → C, G → T, A → C, A → T, T → A, and T → G), such as the T·A-to-A·T mutation needed to directly correct the most common cause of sickle cell disease (HBB E6V). In addition, no DSB-free method has been reported to perform targeted deletions, such as the removal of the 4-base duplication that causes Tay-Sachs disease (HEXA 1278+TATC), or targeted insertions, such as the 3-base insertion required to directly correct the most common cause of cystic fibrosis (CFTR ∆F508). Targeted transversions, insertions, and deletions thus are difficult to install or correct efficiently and without excess byproducts in most cell types, even though they collectively account for most known pathogenic alleles.

Here we describe the development of prime editing, a 'search-and-replace' genome editing technology that mediates targeted insertions, deletions, all 12 possible base-to-base conversions, and combinations thereof in human cells without requiring DSBs or donor DNA templates. Prime editors (PEs), initially exemplified by PE1, use a reverse transcriptase (RT) fused to an RNA-programmable nickase and a prime editing guide RNA (pegRNA) to directly copy genetic information from an extension on the pegRNA into the target genomic locus. PE2 uses an engineered RT to increase editing efficiencies, while PE3 nicks the non-edited strand to induce its replacement and further increase editing efficiency, typically to 20-50% with 1-10% indel formation in human HEK293T cells. Prime editing offers much lower off-target activity than Cas9 at known Cas9 off-target loci, far fewer byproducts and higher or similar efficiency compared to Cas9-initiated HDR, and complementary strengths and weaknesses compared to base editors. By enabling precise targeted insertions, deletions, and all 12 possible classes of point mutations without requiring DSBs or donor DNA templates, prime editing has the potential to advance the study and correction of the vast majority of pathogenic alleles.

This paper massively expanded the genome editing landscape. While base editing was restricted to specific single-letter swaps, prime editing unlocked genuine "search-and-replace" capabilities, permitting highly targeted insertions, deletions, and all 12 possible point mutations. This breakthrough vastly broadened the horizon of genetic engineering, theoretically putting the correction of up to 89% of known pathogenic human genetic variants within reach.

paper_title: Search-and-Replace Genome Editing without Double-Strand Breaks or Donor DNA - 2019 - Nature
display_title: Search-and-Replace Genome Editing without Double-Strand Breaks or Donor DNA
title: Search-and-Replace Genome Editing without Double-Strand Breaks or Donor DNA
text:
The ability to make precise changes at specified genomic sites is a long-standing goal of molecular biology and medicine. CRISPR-Cas nucleases have transformed genome editing by enabling efficient targeted DNA cleavage, but most precise sequence replacement still depends on cellular repair of double-strand breaks (DSBs). DSBs are efficiently resolved by non-homologous end joining, which is useful for gene disruption but often produces heterogeneous insertions and deletions. Installing defined sequence changes by homology-directed repair (HDR) requires an exogenous donor DNA template, competes poorly with end joining in many cell types, and is especially inefficient in non-dividing or terminally differentiated cells. Moreover, DSBs can generate unwanted byproducts including indels, large deletions, translocations, and other genome alterations that complicate biological interpretation and therapeutic development.

Several genome editing approaches have reduced the need for DSBs. Base editors, which combine catalytically impaired Cas proteins with deaminases, can install C•G-to-T•A or A•T-to-G•C transitions efficiently and with few indels. These methods have expanded the range of precise editing, but their chemistry restricts the kinds of mutations that can be made. Base editing cannot directly install transversions, small insertions, or small deletions, and editing outcomes can be limited by bystander bases within the activity window or by the absence of a protospacer adjacent motif (PAM) that appropriately positions the target nucleotide. Many pathogenic variants therefore remain difficult to correct without either making a DSB or relying on inefficient donor-dependent repair.

We sought to develop a genome editing strategy that could directly write new genetic information into a target locus without requiring DSBs or donor DNA templates. We reasoned that a Cas9 nickase could expose a 3′ hydroxyl group at a programmed genomic site, and that this DNA end might be used to prime reverse transcription from an RNA template covalently linked to the guide RNA. Such a guide RNA could both specify the genomic target through its spacer and encode the desired sequence change through an additional reverse transcription template. The resulting edited DNA strand would create a heteroduplex intermediate, whose resolution by endogenous DNA repair could permanently install the edit. We further hypothesized that nicking the non-edited strand could bias repair toward copying the edited strand, analogous to strategies used to improve base editing outcomes.

Here we describe prime editing, a versatile search-and-replace genome editing method that uses a Cas9 H840A nickase fused to an engineered reverse transcriptase and a prime editing guide RNA (pegRNA) that specifies both the target site and the desired edit. We develop prime editors through biochemical validation, yeast reporter assays, mammalian cell optimization, reverse transcriptase engineering, pegRNA design, and non-edited-strand nicking strategies. We show that prime editing can install all 12 possible base-to-base substitutions, targeted insertions, targeted deletions, and combinations of these changes in human cells. We apply prime editing to install or correct disease-relevant alleles including mutations in HBB, HEXA, and PRNP, and demonstrate activity in multiple human cell lines and primary post-mitotic mouse cortical neurons. Comparisons with base editing and Cas9-initiated HDR show that prime editing provides complementary capabilities, broader editing scope, and fewer unwanted byproducts in many settings. These results establish prime editing as a broadly programmable genome editing approach that substantially expands the range of precise sequence changes accessible in living cells.

This breakthrough vastly broadened the horizon of genetic engineering, theoretically putting the correction of up to 89% of known pathogenic human genetic variants within reach.

paper_title: Search-and-Replace Genome Editing without Double-Strand Breaks or Donor DNA - 2019 - Nature
display_title: Search-and-Replace Genome Editing without Double-Strand Breaks or Donor DNA
title: Search-and-Replace Genome Editing without Double-Strand Breaks or Donor DNA
text:
The development of CRISPR-Cas9 programmable nucleases has revolutionized the life sciences by enabling targeted disruption of genes and, in the presence of a donor DNA template, the introduction of specific genetic changes via homology-directed repair (HDR). However, the reliance of these traditional genome editing methods on the generation of DNA double-strand breaks (DSBs) presents significant limitations. The repair of DSBs by the error-prone non-homologous end joining (NHEJ) pathway often yields unpredictable insertions and deletions (indels) at the target locus, compromising editing precision. Furthermore, HDR is generally inefficient, particularly in post-mitotic or non-dividing cells, and can be accompanied by high frequencies of indel byproducts, making precise and efficient gene correction challenging for many therapeutic applications.

To address the limitations of DSB-mediated editing, base editors were developed. Cytidine and adenine base editors, which consist of catalytically impaired Cas9 fused to a nucleobase deaminase enzyme, can efficiently install targeted transition mutations (C·G to T·A, and A·T to G·C) without generating DSBs or requiring a donor DNA template. Base editors represent a major advance in precision genome editing, demonstrating high efficiency and minimal indel formation across various cell types. However, base editing currently cannot install transversions, insertions, or deletions. Moreover, base editors require a strictly positioned protospacer adjacent motif (PAM) to place the target nucleotide within a narrow activity window. If multiple cytidine or adenine bases are present within this window, bystander mutations can occur alongside the desired edit.

Because transversion point mutations, insertions, and deletions collectively account for the majority of known pathogenic human genetic variants, there is a critical need for a precision genome editing technology that can directly and efficiently install any combination of these changes without the detrimental consequences of DSBs. Such a method would ideally write new genetic information into a specified genomic locus directly from an engineered component, bypassing the need for exogenous donor DNA templates and the unpredictable nature of DSB repair.

Here, we describe prime editing, a novel and versatile 'search-and-replace' genome editing approach that directly writes new genetic information into a specified DNA site without generating DSBs or requiring donor DNA. Prime editing is mediated by a prime editor (PE) protein, consisting of a catalytically impaired Cas9 endonuclease fused to an engineered reverse transcriptase. The PE is programmed by a prime editing guide RNA (pegRNA), which acts as both the guide sequence to specify the target locus and the template that encodes the desired edit. Upon binding to the target site, the Cas9 nickase domain nicks the non-edited DNA strand, allowing the reverse transcriptase domain to extend the 3' end of the nicked strand using the pegRNA as a template. This process introduces the new genetic information directly into the genome. We systematically engineered and optimized the PE components and pegRNA design to maximize editing efficiency and purity. We demonstrate that prime editing can efficiently install targeted insertions, deletions, and all twelve possible types of point mutations in human cells with remarkable precision and substantially fewer indel byproducts than Cas9-initiated HDR. We apply prime editing to precisely correct the primary genetic causes of sickle cell disease and Tay-Sachs disease, highlighting its potential to dramatically expand the capabilities of precision genetic medicine.

This paper massively expanded the genome editing landscape. While base editing was restricted to specific single-letter swaps, prime editing unlocked genuine "search-and-replace" capabilities, permitting highly targeted insertions, deletions, and all 12 possible point mutations. This breakthrough vastly broadened the horizon of genetic engineering, theoretically putting the correction of up to 89% of known pathogenic human genetic variants within reach.

paper_title: Search-and-Replace Genome Editing without Double-Strand Breaks or Donor DNA - 2019 - Nature
display_title: Search-and-Replace Genome Editing without Double-Strand Breaks or Donor DNA
title: Search-and-Replace Genome Editing without Double-Strand Breaks or Donor DNA
text:
The ability to insert arbitrary DNA sequences with single-nucleotide precision is an especially enabling prime editing capability. For example, we used PE3 in HEK293T cells to precisely insert into HEK3 a His 6 tag (18 bp, 65% efficiency), a FLAG epitope tag (24 bp, 18% efficiency), and an extended Cre recombinase LoxP site (44 bp, 23% efficiency) with 3.0-5.9% indels. We anticipate many biotechnological and therapeutic applications will be enabled by the ability to efficiently and precisely insert new DNA sequences into target sites in living cells.

Collectively the prime editing experiments described in this study performed 19 insertions up to 44 bp, 23 deletions up to 80 bp, 119 point mutations including 83 transversions, and 18 combination edits at 12 endogenous loci in the human and mouse genomes at locations ranging from 3 bp upstream to 29 bp downstream of a PAM without making explicit DSBs. These results establish prime editing as a remarkably versatile genome editing method. Because 85-99% of insertions, deletions, indels, and duplications in ClinVar are ≤30 bp in length, in principle prime editing can correct up to ~89% of the 75,122 pathogenic human genetic variants in ClinVar.

Prime editing offers many possible choices of pegRNA-induced nick locations, sgRNA-induced second nick locations, PBS lengths, RT template lengths, and which strand to edit first. This flexibility, which contrasts with more limited options typically available for other precision editing methods, allows editing efficiency, product purity, DNA specificity, and other parameters to be optimized to suit a given application.

Much additional research is needed to further understand and improve prime editing in a broad range of cell types and organisms, to assess off-target prime editing in a genome-wide manner, and to further characterize the extent to which prime editors might affect cells. Interfacing prime editing with additional in vitro and in vivo delivery strategies is critical to explore the potential of prime editing to enable applications including the study and treatment of genetic diseases. By enabling precise targeted transitions, transversions, insertions, and deletions in the genomes of mammalian cells without requiring DSBs, donor DNA templates, or HDR, however, prime editing provides a new 'search-and-replace' capability that substantially expands the scope of genome editing.

This breakthrough vastly broadened the horizon of genetic engineering, theoretically putting the correction of up to 89% of known pathogenic human genetic variants within reach.

paper_title: Search-and-Replace Genome Editing without Double-Strand Breaks or Donor DNA - 2019 - Nature
display_title: Search-and-Replace Genome Editing without Double-Strand Breaks or Donor DNA
title: Search-and-Replace Genome Editing without Double-Strand Breaks or Donor DNA
text:
Prime editing establishes a versatile approach for installing precise genetic changes without requiring double-strand DNA breaks or exogenous donor DNA templates. By coupling Cas9 nickase-mediated target recognition to pegRNA-programmed reverse transcription, prime editors directly write new sequence information into genomic DNA. This strategy supports all 12 base-to-base conversions, targeted insertions, targeted deletions, and combinations of these edits, substantially expanding the scope of precision genome editing beyond the capabilities of current base editors and DSB-initiated homology-directed repair.

Several features of prime editing are likely to contribute to its broad utility. First, the edit is encoded by the pegRNA, allowing the same editor protein to mediate many classes of sequence change. Second, the method does not depend on HDR and therefore can operate in cell types in which donor-template repair is inefficient, including post-mitotic primary neurons. Third, because prime editing requires target binding, primer binding, and productive flap resolution, the system can show very low off-target editing at known Cas9 off-target sites. These properties distinguish prime editing from nuclease-based editing, which frequently generates indels and other DSB-associated byproducts, and from base editing, which can be limited by editing windows, bystander nucleotides, and the chemical scope of available deaminases.

Prime editing nevertheless remains an early-stage technology with important limitations. Editing efficiencies vary substantially with genomic locus, cell type, edit position, PBS length, RT template length, and nicking strategy. PE3 can increase editing levels but can also elevate indels, whereas PE3b offers a useful strategy to reduce indel formation when the desired edit creates a selectively nickable allele. The mechanistic determinants of productive flap resolution, pegRNA stability, reverse transcription termination, and repair of the edited heteroduplex remain incompletely understood. Improved pegRNA design rules, editor architectures, delivery systems, and cellular repair modulation should further increase editing efficiency, product purity, and applicability across primary cells and tissues.

The ability of prime editing to correct or install disease-relevant transversions, insertions, and deletions in human cells illustrates its potential for studying and eventually treating genetic disease. Because most pathogenic variants are not simple transition mutations, a DSB-free technology that can precisely install the majority of sequence changes represents an important advance. Continued evaluation of genome-wide off-target effects, RNA or transcriptome perturbations, delivery constraints, and long-term cellular consequences will be essential before therapeutic use. More broadly, prime editing provides a programmable search-and-replace framework for genome manipulation and a foundation for future precision editing technologies.

title: Search-and-Replace Genome Editing without Double-Strand Breaks or Donor DNA
text:
The development of prime editing fundamentally expands the capabilities of precision genome editing, addressing many of the most persistent limitations associated with both Cas9-initiated homology-directed repair (HDR) and base editing. By engineering a system that writes new genetic information directly from a pegRNA into a specified genomic locus without the requirement for double-strand breaks (DSBs) or exogenous donor DNA templates, we have established a highly versatile 'search-and-replace' methodology. The ability of prime editors to efficiently install all twelve possible base-to-base conversions, targeted insertions, and precise deletions—as well as complex combinations of these changes—opens the door to correcting a vast majority of known pathogenic human alleles that were previously recalcitrant to existing technologies.

A central advantage of prime editing lies in its mechanism of action, which uncouples precise genome modification from the inherently unpredictable consequences of DSB repair. By relying on a nickase and an engineered reverse transcriptase, prime editing circumvents the frequent indel byproducts, translocations, and p53 activation commonly associated with Cas9 nuclease activity. Our extensive comparisons in diverse human cell lines demonstrated that prime editing consistently yields substantially higher editing-to-indel ratios than Cas9-mediated HDR. Furthermore, the requirement for three distinct DNA hybridization events—target-spacer complementary, target-primer binding site complementary, and target-reverse transcript product complementary—confers a stringent layer of specificity. Consequently, prime editing exhibits dramatically lower off-target editing activity at known Cas9 off-target loci compared to conventional Cas9 nuclease.

While base editors remain highly efficient for installing specific transition mutations within a defined targeting window, prime editing offers complementary strengths. It is uniquely capable of generating transversions and multi-base changes, and operates without the constraints of a rigid PAM positioning requirement or the complication of bystander edits in sequence contexts rich in the target nucleotide. Although editing efficiencies vary depending on the target site, the specific edit, and the cell type, the system's flexibility allows for extensive pegRNA optimization.

Future optimization efforts will likely focus on enhancing the efficiency of prime editing in primary cells and in vivo models, as well as refining pegRNA design algorithms. Nevertheless, the robustness, precision, and broad applicability of prime editing represent a major step toward the comprehensive manipulation of mammalian genomes.

paper_title: Site-Directed Mutagenesis by Gene Targeting in Mouse Embryo-Derived Stem Cells - 1987 - Cell
display_title: Site-Directed Mutagenesis by Gene Targeting in Mouse Embryo-Derived Stem Cells
title: Site-Directed Mutagenesis by Gene Targeting in Mouse Embryo-Derived Stem Cells
text: We mutated, by gene targeting, the endogenous hypoxanthine phosphoribosyl transferase (HPRT) gene in mouse embryo-derived stem (ES) cells. A specialized construct of the neomycin resistance (neo) gene was introduced into an exon of a cloned fragment of the Hprt gene and used to transfect ES cells. Among the G418-resistant colonies, 1/1000 were also resistant to the base analog 6-thioguanine (6-TG). The G418-resistant, 6-TG-resistant cells were all shown to be Hprt-deficient as the result of homologous recombination with the exogenous, neo-containing, Hprt sequences. We have compared the gene-targeting efficiencies of two classes of neo-Hprt recombinant vectors: those that replace the endogenous sequence with the exogenous sequence and those that insert the exogenous sequence into the endogenous sequence. The targeting efficiencies of both classes of vectors are strongly dependent upon the extent of homology between exogenous and endogenous sequences. The protocol described herein should be useful for targeting mutations into any gene.
historical_context: In 1987, Thomas Doetschman, Oliver Smithies, and colleagues proved that homologous recombination could be harnessed to target and flawlessly modify specific endogenous genes within mouse embryonic stem (ES) cells. Merged with the concurrent breakthroughs of Mario Capecchi, this innovation proved that the mammalian genome was no longer locked, but could be edited at will. This foundational paper laid the direct groundwork for the 2007 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine, awarded to Oliver Smithies, Mario Capecchi, and Martin Evans. The Nobel committee praised them for establishing the very principles required to introduce specific gene modifications in mice. This exact technology birthed the "knockout mouse," fundamentally revolutionizing genetics by granting scientists the power to selectively delete and observe the function of almost any gene in a living mammal, profoundly elevating the study of human disease.

paper_title: Site-Directed Mutagenesis by Gene Targeting in Mouse Embryo-Derived Stem Cells - 1987 - Cell
display_title: Site-Directed Mutagenesis by Gene Targeting in Mouse Embryo-Derived Stem Cells
title: Site-Directed Mutagenesis by Gene Targeting in Mouse Embryo-Derived Stem Cells
text: Gene targeting provides a means for introducing defined alterations into the mammalian genome. We have used homologous recombination to inactivate the endogenous Hprt gene in male mouse embryo-derived stem (ES) cells. Recombinant vectors were constructed in which a redesigned neo gene, optimized for expression in ES cells, was inserted into exon 8 of cloned Hprt sequences. Following electroporation and selection for resistance to G418 and 6-thioguanine, all 38 independently isolated cell lines examined contained targeted disruptions of the Hprt locus and lacked detectable HPRT activity. Both sequence replacement and sequence insertion vectors mediated targeting, with frequencies strongly dependent on the extent of homology between the introduced DNA and the chromosomal target. Under optimal conditions, approximately 1 in 1000 G418-resistant transformants resulted from gene targeting, without detectable random integration of additional vector copies. These results demonstrate that ES cells can be used for site-directed mutagenesis of endogenous genes and should permit the generation of mice carrying predetermined mutations.

title: Site-Directed Mutagenesis by Gene Targeting in Mouse Embryo-Derived Stem Cells
text: Gene targeting via homologous recombination holds tremendous potential for the precise modification of the mammalian genome. In this study, we demonstrate the successful site-directed inactivation of the endogenous hypoxanthine phosphoribosyl transferase (Hprt) gene in mouse embryo-derived stem (ES) cells. By employing custom-designed recombinant vectors containing a neomycin resistance (neo) gene embedded within Hprt sequences, we selectively disrupted the target locus. We optimized our targeting strategy by modifying the neo gene for robust expression in ES cells and utilizing both sequence replacement and insertion vectors. When introduced via electroporation, these vectors facilitated homologous recombination at a remarkable frequency of 1 in 1000 transformants. This targeted disruption rendered the cells resistant to 6-thioguanine, allowing direct selection of Hprt-deficient clones. Southern blot analysis confirmed the precise integration of the neo gene into the intended site. Our findings establish ES cells as a robust platform for gene targeting, paving the way for generating custom-designed mutant mice to study development and disease.

Merged with the concurrent breakthroughs of Mario Capecchi, this innovation proved that the mammalian genome was no longer locked, but could be edited at will. This foundational paper laid the direct groundwork for the 2007 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine, awarded to Oliver Smithies, Mario Capecchi, and Martin Evans. The Nobel committee praised them for establishing the very principles required to introduce specific gene modifications in mice. This exact technology birthed the "knockout mouse," fundamentally revolutionizing genetics by granting scientists the power to selectively delete and observe the function of almost any gene in a living mammal, profoundly elevating the study of human disease.

title: Site-Directed Mutagenesis by Gene Targeting in Mouse Embryo-Derived Stem Cells
text:
Gene targeting—the homologous recombination of DNA sequences residing in the chromosome with newly introduced DNA sequences—provides a means for systematically altering the mammalian genome. A desired alteration would first be introduced into a cloned DNA sequence, and gene targeting would then transfer the alteration into the genome. Gene targeting should be equally effective for correcting or mutating the desired chromosomal locus.

We initiated our analysis of gene targeting in cultured mammalian cells by studying recombination between a defective gene residing in the chromosome and newly introduced plasmid DNA carrying a different mutation in the same gene. For those experiments, we first established cell lines containing a mutant neomycin resistance (neo) gene integrated into the genome of mouse L cells. We were then able to restore the gene via homologous recombination by injecting DNA carrying a different mutation in the neo gene. In the course of these experiments we uncovered two mechanisms for altering chromosomal sequences. The first involved the transfer of information, by homologous recombination, from the newly introduced DNA into the cognate chromosomal sequence. The second involved inducing mutations in the homologous chromosomal sequence by what appears to entail incorrect repair of a heteroduplex formed between the newly introduced DNA and the cognate chromosomal sequence. Each of the two methods has its own advantages. The transfer of information by homologous recombination allows one to mutate or correct the desired chromosomal locus in a defined manner. On the other hand, the frequency of altering chromosomal sequences by heteroduplex-induced mutagenesis promises to be higher than via homologous recombination. This could prove to be a useful method for generating a large number of random mutations in specific genes.

In this current study we have extended our analysis of gene targeting by using an endogenous gene as the target and by using embryo-derived stem (ES) cells as the recipient cell line. The target gene is hypoxanthine phosphoribosyl transferase (HPRT). This gene was selected primarily for two reasons. First, the Hprt gene lies on the X-chromosome. Since ES cells derived from male embryos are hemizygous for Hprt, only a single copy of the Hprt gene needs to be inactivated in order to yield a selectable phenotype. Second, selection procedures have been developed for isolating Hprt-deficient mutants. By far the most common pathway for cells in culture to become resistant to the base analog 6-thioguanine (6-TG) is to acquire a mutation in the Hprt gene.

ES cells were chosen for these experiments because, following inactivation of a chosen gene by gene targeting, they should provide the means to generate mice with the desired mutation. ES cells have been shown to be pluripotent in vitro and in vivo. When reintroduced into mouse blastocysts, these cells contribute efficiently to the formation of chimeras, including contributions to a functional germ line. In addition, it has been shown recently that these cells can be manipulated in vitro without losing their capacity to generate germ-line chimeras. Following transfection with the neo gene and selection for G418-resistance, these ES cells were used to produce germ-line chimeras that stably transmitted G418-resistance to subsequent generations. HPRT-deficient mice were produced from ES cells that were either selected for spontaneous Hprt mutations or selected for Hprt-deficiency following the random insertion of retroviral DNA into the mouse genome.

Here we describe the site-directed inactivation of the endogenous Hprt gene in male ES cells by gene targeting. We examine some parameters that affect the gene-targeting frequency as well as the mechanism of gene inactivation mediated by different recombinant vectors. Under optimal conditions, we find that 1/1000 cells transformed by exogenous DNA can undergo a gene-targeting event. The advantage of inactivating specific genes via gene targeting compared with random mutagenic methods such as chemical mutagenesis or retroviral DNA insertion is twofold. First, the nature of the mutant allele is at the discretion of the experimenter, and second, unlike random mutagenic events, the frequency of the targeting events is sufficiently high to make the procedure applicable to nonselectable genes.

This foundational paper laid the direct groundwork for the 2007 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine, awarded to Oliver Smithies, Mario Capecchi, and Martin Evans. The Nobel committee praised them for establishing the very principles required to introduce specific gene modifications in mice.
