title: Site-Directed Mutagenesis by Gene Targeting in Mouse Embryo-Derived Stem Cells
text:
The ability to introduce defined mutations into the mammalian genome would provide a direct means of relating gene structure to gene function. At present, mutations in cultured mammalian cells are obtained either by selecting rare spontaneous variants or by introducing foreign DNA that integrates at apparently random positions in the genome. These approaches have been useful for isolating selectable mutants and for studying expression of transfected genes, but they do not provide a general method for altering a predetermined chromosomal locus. A procedure that would allow cloned DNA sequences to direct a mutation to their cognate site in the genome would make it possible to construct specific alleles in cultured cells and, in principle, in the mouse.

Homologous recombination offers such a route. In lower eukaryotes, cloned DNA introduced into the cell can recombine efficiently with the corresponding chromosomal sequences, thereby replacing or disrupting the endogenous locus. Similar events have been observed in mammalian cells, but generally at low frequency and against a large background of nonhomologous integration. Thus, a central question is whether homologous recombination can be used practically to mutate a specific gene in mammalian cells, and whether the products of such events can be recovered with sufficient efficiency to permit their routine analysis.

Embryo-derived stem (ES) cells provide a particularly attractive recipient for such experiments. These cells can be propagated in culture while retaining the capacity to contribute to many tissues after introduction into early embryos. If a defined mutation could be introduced into an ES cell chromosome, it should be possible to recover mice carrying that mutation. This would extend somatic cell genetics from cultured cell lines to the intact animal and would provide a general method for analyzing genes whose functions are revealed only during development or physiology.

We have chosen the mouse hypoxanthine phosphoribosyl transferase (Hprt) gene as a target for developing such a procedure. Hprt is useful because loss of gene function can be selected by resistance to the base analog 6-thioguanine (6-TG), whereas acquisition of an introduced neomycin resistance (neo) gene can be selected by resistance to G418. Thus, a targeting vector that both disrupts Hprt and carries neo should, after homologous recombination, generate cells that are resistant to both drugs. The Hprt locus is nevertheless not an especially favorable target. It is expressed at a low level in ES cells, spans more than 33 kb, and contains repetitive sequences that complicate molecular analysis. Success at this locus would therefore suggest that the method is not restricted to unusually favorable genes.

Our strategy was to insert a modified neo gene into exon 8 of a cloned fragment of Hprt and to introduce these recombinant molecules into ES cells. Homologous recombination between the exogenous Hprt sequences and the endogenous locus should transfer the neo insertion into the chromosome, inactivating Hprt and marking the altered allele. Because expression of the neo gene is required to recover targeted cells, we redesigned the neo cassette to function efficiently in ES cells while keeping the amount of nonhomologous DNA small. The cassette contains the Tn5 neo coding sequence, regulatory elements derived from the herpes simplex virus thymidine kinase promoter and a polyoma enhancer, and an improved translation initiation signal.

We also wished to determine how vector design affects the recovery of targeted mutations. Guided by recombination pathways characterized in yeast, we constructed two classes of neo-Hprt recombinant vectors. In one class, the introduced DNA is expected to replace the corresponding endogenous sequence. In the other, the introduced DNA is expected to insert into the locus, creating a duplication of Hprt sequences. These two products would have different genetic uses. Replacement vectors provide a simple means of disrupting a gene. Insertion vectors could permit the introduction of more subtle mutations, such as point mutations or small deletions, linked to a selectable marker elsewhere in the same cloned segment.

In this paper we describe the construction and use of these vectors to mutate the endogenous Hprt gene in mouse ES cells. We compare replacement and insertion vectors, examine the dependence of targeting frequency on the extent of homology, and characterize the resulting mutant cell lines by Southern transfer analysis and enzymatic assay. The results show that homologous recombination can be used to introduce a defined mutation into a specific mouse chromosomal locus in ES cells, and suggest a general route for producing mice carrying predetermined genetic alterations.

title: Site-Directed Mutagenesis by Gene Targeting in Mouse Embryo-Derived Stem Cells
text:
The introduction of exogenous DNA sequences into the mammalian genome has become a powerful tool for analyzing gene expression, regulation, and cellular differentiation. Techniques such as microinjection, viral infection, and DNA transfection allow for the stable integration of cloned genes into cultured cells and the germline of mice. However, a major limitation of these approaches is that the integrated DNA sequences typically insert at random, non-homologous locations within the host genome. Because gene expression is profoundly influenced by the chromosomal environment, this random integration complicates the study of gene regulation in its normal chromosomal context. Furthermore, the inability to target integrations prevents the deliberate modification or disruption of specific endogenous genes, a capability that is essential for directly linking a gene to its physiological function and for generating precise animal models of human genetic diseases.

In contrast, homologous recombination between newly introduced DNA and cognate chromosomal sequences permits the targeted modification of the host genome. This approach, widely termed gene targeting, has been utilized with great success in yeast to generate targeted mutations, gene replacements, and precise chromosomal deletions. Extending this technology to mammalian systems would provide a robust method for directing specific mutations into any cloned gene within the mammalian genome. While homologous recombination has been demonstrated to occur between newly introduced plasmids and homologous chromosomal sequences in cultured mammalian cells, the frequency of such targeted events is generally several orders of magnitude lower than the frequency of random integration. Consequently, detecting the rare cells in which a targeted recombination event has occurred requires stringent selection or highly sensitive screening strategies.

Pluripotent mouse embryo-derived stem (ES) cells provide an ideal system for developing gene targeting techniques with the ultimate goal of generating mutant mice. ES cells are derived from the inner cell mass of preimplantation mouse blastocysts and can be maintained in an undifferentiated state in vitro. Crucially, when reintroduced into a developing blastocyst, these cells can colonize the embryo and contribute to all somatic tissues, as well as the germline, of the resulting chimeric mouse. Therefore, if a specific endogenous gene can be mutated by targeted recombination in cultured ES cells, it should be possible to introduce this defined mutation into the mouse germline. This would enable the derivation of mouse strains harboring precise genetic alterations, greatly facilitating the study of mammalian development and the generation of models for recessive genetic disorders.

In this study, we investigated the feasibility of using homologous recombination to direct a specific mutation into an endogenous gene in mouse ES cells. As our target locus, we chose the gene encoding the purine salvage enzyme hypoxanthine phosphoribosyl transferase (HPRT). The Hprt locus offers a unique advantage for these initial studies: it is X-linked, meaning that male-derived ES cells contain only a single functional copy of the gene. Inactivation of this single allele results in a complete loss of HPRT enzymatic activity. Furthermore, both positive and negative selections can be applied to the Hprt locus. Cells lacking HPRT activity can be readily selected by their resistance to the toxic base analog 6-thioguanine (6-TG).

To mutate the endogenous Hprt gene, we constructed recombinant vectors carrying a cloned fragment of the mouse Hprt gene disrupted by the insertion of a neomycin resistance (neo) cassette. The neo gene serves two purposes: it disrupts the Hprt coding sequence to create a null allele, and it provides a selectable marker (resistance to the antibiotic G418) to identify cells that have stably integrated the vector DNA. By introducing these vectors into ES cells via electroporation and selecting for cells that are simultaneously resistant to both G418 and 6-TG, we successfully isolated clonal cell lines in which the endogenous Hprt gene had been selectively inactivated by homologous recombination. We describe the efficient targeting of the Hprt gene, examine the influence of vector design and the extent of sequence homology on targeting frequencies, and discuss the implications of these findings for the targeted modification of any gene in the mammalian genome.

title: Site-Directed Mutagenesis by Gene Targeting in Mouse Embryo-Derived Stem Cells
text:
We have analyzed 38 independent G418-resistant, 6-TG-resistant ES cell lines. Each of these cell lines was shown to have arisen from inactivation of the endogenous Hprt gene by homologous recombination with the introduced neo-Hprt fragment. Spontaneous formation of G418-resistant, 6-TG-resistant cells was not detected (i.e., occurs at less than 1/10^9 cells per generation).

None of the G418-resistant, 6-TG-resistant cell lines contained extraneous copies of the neo-Hprt recombinant vector integrated randomly within the ES genome. This greatly simplified the analysis of the targeting events in these cell lines and permitted an unambiguous interpretation of the results. The absence of extraneous copies of the input vector in the targeted cells should also simplify interpretation of additional studies that will entail establishing a correlation between the inactivation of specific genes with the resultant phenotypes.

Under our optimal conditions, we have observed a gene-targeting frequency, relative to the frequency of random integration of the input vector, of 1/1000. The parameters that we believe influenced the success of these experiments include using a neo gene that is efficiently expressed in ES cells, maintaining the size of the neo gene at a minimum, using extensive homology between the homing sequence and the target sequence, and removing, prior to transfection, unnecessary and nonhomologous sequences from the input vector.

This gene-targeting frequency is sufficiently high to be used for inactivating nonselectable genes. Direct screening, by Southern transfer analysis, for a gene-targeting event among 1000 candidate cell lines would not be exorbitant. Furthermore, gene-targeting enrichment procedures could be added to the protocol for using the neo gene as a transfection tag. For example, a neo gene lacking an enhancer or a poly(A) addition signal could be positioned within the homing sequence in such a way that homologous recombination with the target gene would juxtapose the defective neo gene with the sequences required for effective expression. Random integration of the same vector into the recipient genome would not normally bring the required sequence sufficiently near the neo gene to yield G418-resistant colonies. Pilot experiments testing such procedures indicate that enrichment of several hundred fold for gene targeting compared with random integration should be attainable.

The gene-targeting frequency was observed to be very sensitive to the extent of homology between the exogenous and cognate endogenous sequence. A two-fold increase in homology increased the gene-targeting frequency by 20-fold. Further increases in the extent of homology may increase the gene-targeting frequency even more.

We have compared two classes of neo-Hprt recombinant vectors, one that replaces endogenous sequences with exogenous sequences and another that inserts exogenous sequences into the endogenous sequence. Both classes exhibit comparable gene-targeting frequencies and are equally sensitive to the extent of homology with the endogenous target. We have termed the former sequence replacement vectors and the latter sequence insertion vectors. In 23/23 G418-resistant, 6-TG-resistant cell lines obtained by introducing the replacement vector, the endogenous Hprt gene was inactivated by sequence replacement. Of the 12 G418-resistant, 6-TG-resistant cell lines obtained with the insertion vector pIV9.3, 9 resulted from sequence insertion. In the remaining 3 the Hprt gene was inactivated by sequence replacement. The latter may result from a crossover occurring at points within the vector sequences rather than at both termini. Though insertion vectors mediate mutagenesis via two pathways, they target predominantly by inserting into the endogenous gene.

The insertion vectors are technically more difficult to build. On the other hand, they may provide the means for generating a wider spectrum of mutant alleles. For example, by placing the neo gene in the 3'-untranslated sequence, it can still be used as a transfection tag. In such a vector, the neo gene could be linked to a wide spectrum of mutations, including point mutations, small insertions, or small deletions, in upstream exons. In the process of insertion of the neo vector, these mutations would be concomitantly transferred into the endogenous gene.

When we initiated these experiments we had two concerns about using the Hprt gene as our target: it is expressed at a low level in ES cells, and it contains many repetitive DNA sequences. As in most cells, HPRT protein represents approximately 1/5,000 of the soluble protein. Furthermore, repetitive DNA sequences are dispersed throughout both the neo-Hprt recombinant vector and the Hprt gene. In fact, it is not a simple task to identify a suitable probe from the Hprt locus for Southern transfer analysis. The success obtained in targeting to the Hprt gene despite these handicaps may indicate that in the future we need not be so concerned with these parameters negatively influencing the gene-targeting frequency.

Conclusion
We have demonstrated that we can inactivate by gene targeting a specific locus in the mouse genome. The protocol we have developed to inactivate the endogenous Hprt gene should be adaptable to other genes as well. We have also shown that ES cells are a suitable host for gene-targeting experiments. It is hoped that this combination of using ES cells as the recipient cell line and site-specific mutagenesis achieved by gene targeting will provide the means for generating mice of any desired genotype. An advantage of this scenario is that the first generation chimera will usually be heterozygous for the targeted mutation and that subsequent breeding can be used to generate the homozygous animal. Thus, only one of the two loci need be inactivated, and recessive lethals can be maintained as heterozygotes. If successful, this technology will be used in the future to dissect the developmental pathway of the mouse as well as to generate mouse models for human genetic diseases.

paper_title: Site-Directed Mutagenesis by Gene Targeting in Mouse Embryo-Derived Stem Cells - 1987 - Cell
display_title: Site-Directed Mutagenesis by Gene Targeting in Mouse Embryo-Derived Stem Cells
title: Site-Directed Mutagenesis by Gene Targeting in Mouse Embryo-Derived Stem Cells
text:
We have shown that an endogenous mammalian gene can be altered in a predetermined manner by homologous recombination between chromosomal sequences and DNA introduced into cultured cells. The experiments described here differ from earlier demonstrations of gene correction or gene disruption in that the target was not an experimentally introduced marker gene, but the native mouse Hprt gene. Thus, the results establish that cloned genomic sequences can be used to direct a defined mutation into the corresponding chromosomal locus. In the present case, insertion of the neo gene into exon 8 of Hprt abolished HPRT activity and conferred resistance to 6-TG. The diagnostic restriction patterns and the absence of detectable HPRT activity in independently isolated clones show that the selectable phenotype resulted from modification of the Hprt locus rather than from random integration of the vector accompanied by an unrelated Hprt mutation.

A central feature of these experiments was the use of ES cells as the recipient cell type. These cells can be propagated, selected, and cloned in vitro, yet retain the developmental potential to contribute to chimeric animals and to the germ line. The ability to carry out gene targeting in such cells therefore provides a route by which mutations designed in vitro may be introduced into the mouse. Since male ES cells carry a single X-linked Hprt gene, the loss of HPRT function provided a convenient selection for targeted events. However, the importance of the system is not limited to Hprt. The neo gene serves both as a mutagen and as a dominant selectable marker, and the observed frequency of targeting indicates that comparable strategies should be applicable to loci for which no direct loss-of-function selection exists. In such cases, selection for G418 resistance followed by molecular screening should allow the recovery of cells carrying the desired chromosomal alteration.

The design of the neo cassette was an important component of the strategy. To use neo as a general tag for chromosomal modification, it must be expressed after integration into many different genomic environments. The pMC1Neo construction, containing the HSV-tk promoter, polyoma enhancer sequences, and an improved translational initiation signal, was markedly more efficient than pRSVNeo or pSV2Neo in ES cells. This difference was especially striking because the same vectors gave comparable results in fibroblasts. Thus, the regulatory sequences that function well in differentiated cells are not necessarily adequate in ES cells. The increased efficiency and colony size obtained with pMC1Neo should be particularly useful when the targeted gene is expressed weakly, as is Hprt, or is transcriptionally inactive.

The frequency of gene targeting depended strongly on the length of homology between the introduced DNA and the chromosomal locus. Among the sequence replacement vectors, increasing the homologous segment from 4.0 kb to 9.1 kb increased the fraction of G418-resistant cells that were also 6-TG-resistant from approximately 1 in 40,000 to approximately 1 in 950. A similar effect was observed with the sequence insertion vectors. These results are consistent with the expectation that longer regions of homology provide more efficient substrates for chromosomal recombination. They also suggest that the practical efficiency of targeting at other loci may be improved by using vectors containing extensive isogenic or closely related genomic sequences flanking the mutation to be introduced.

The two classes of vectors produced different kinds of targeted alleles. Sequence replacement vectors yielded replacement of the endogenous Hprt sequence by the neo-containing exogenous sequence. In contrast, sequence insertion vectors most often inserted the entire vector into the Hprt locus, thereby generating a duplication in which one copy contained the neo insertion and the other retained wild-type Hprt sequences. Nevertheless, insertion vectors could also give rise to replacement products. These findings indicate that linear DNA molecules with homologous ends can participate in more than one recombination pathway in mammalian cells. For some applications, replacement will be the desired outcome because it produces a simple mutant allele. In other cases, insertion events may be useful because they create duplicated chromosomal segments that could subsequently undergo further recombination or be exploited to introduce more complex changes.

Several features of the protocol contributed to the recovery and interpretation of targeted clones. The vectors were linearized and stripped of bacterial plasmid sequences before transfection, thereby reducing nonhomologous DNA and producing molecules favorable for recombination. Electroporation conditions were chosen to give efficient survival and predominantly single-copy integration. In addition, the selection scheme was arranged to allow expression of neo and decay of preexisting HPRT activity before exposure to 6-TG. Because ES cells grow as tight colonies and can cross-feed, dispersion during selection was necessary for reliable scoring. The appearance of bursts of G418-resistant, 6-TG-resistant colonies on individual plates indicates that most targeting events occurred at or near the time of electroporation.

The present work provides a general approach for site-directed mutagenesis in the mouse. The method should permit the introduction of null alleles, subtle coding changes, or regulatory mutations into cloned genes and then into ES cells. Once such cells are shown to retain their developmental potential, they can be used to generate chimeric animals and, through germ-line transmission, mice carrying the engineered mutation. Because the initial chimera or its progeny can be maintained as a heterozygote, even mutations that are recessive lethal should be recoverable and studied. Thus, gene targeting in ES cells offers a means to analyze gene function in development, physiology, and disease with a precision not obtainable by chemical mutagenesis or random insertional mutagenesis.

title: Site-Directed Mutagenesis by Gene Targeting in Mouse Embryo-Derived Stem Cells
text:
The experiments presented in this study provide definitive evidence that endogenous genes in mammalian cells can be specifically inactivated through homologous recombination with newly introduced DNA vectors. By utilizing mouse embryo-derived stem (ES) cells and the hypoxanthine phosphoribosyl transferase (Hprt) gene as a model system, we have established a robust methodology for site-directed mutagenesis that fundamentally circumvents the limitations of random insertional or chemical mutagenesis. The ability to dictate the precise nature of the genetic alteration at a predetermined locus is a transformative advance, offering unprecedented control over the mammalian genome.

A critical component of this success was the rigorous optimization of the neo gene construct. Because gene targeting events are inherently rare, the efficient expression of the selectable marker from within the target locus is paramount. By engineering pMC1Neo to incorporate a highly active mutant polyoma enhancer, an efficient HSV-tk promoter, and an optimized mammalian translation initiation sequence, we significantly augmented the frequency of recovering G418-resistant transformants. This enhancement effectively increases the pool of cells in which the targeting event can be detected, a factor that will be indispensable when extending this technology to genes that are transcriptionally silent in ES cells.

Our comparative analysis of sequence replacement and sequence insertion vectors revealed that both approaches successfully mediate homologous recombination at the endogenous Hprt locus, albeit with mechanistic distinctions. Replacement vectors generated the anticipated allelic substitution, resulting in a clean disruption of the target gene. In contrast, sequence insertion vectors, designed to integrate the entire plasmid into the chromosomal locus, predominantly yielded the expected duplication of homologous sequences. Interestingly, a subset of insertion vectors also produced replacement-type events. This dual outcome indicates that mammalian recombination machinery can resolve these intermediates through multiple pathways, suggesting that the topology of the input DNA significantly influences the final genomic architecture. Regardless of the vector class, the frequency of accurate targeting was strongly correlated with the extent of sequence homology between the exogenous construct and the endogenous gene. The largest vectors evaluated achieved targeting frequencies approaching one in every thousand G418-resistant cells, a rate highly amenable to experimental isolation even in the absence of a secondary selection like 6-thioguanine.

The choice of ES cells as the recipient line is strategically central to the broader biological implications of this work. Because ES cells retain their developmental pluripotency after extensive in vitro manipulation, including electroporation and clonal expansion, they serve as a unique bridge between cultured cells and the whole organism. A targeted mutation introduced into an ES cell can theoretically be incorporated into the germline of a chimeric mouse, allowing for the subsequent breeding of animals carrying the designed genetic defect. This capacity essentially enables the reverse-genetic study of mammalian biology: moving from a cloned DNA sequence to a specific mutation in a living animal.

This protocol represents a generalized strategy that should be broadly applicable for targeting virtually any cloned gene within the mouse genome. The finding that targeting occurs at a workable frequency without requiring a gene-specific selection strategy beyond the acquisition of the neo marker implies that non-selectable genes are now accessible to targeted disruption. This opens the door to systematically dissecting complex developmental pathways by sequentially disabling key regulatory genes and observing the resulting phenotypic consequences in vivo. Furthermore, the ability to engineer precise mutations will facilitate the creation of highly accurate mouse models of human genetic diseases. For instance, the creation of Hprt-deficient mice through this methodology directly models Lesch-Nyhan syndrome, providing an invaluable in vivo system for testing potential gene therapies or pharmacological interventions.

In summary, we have demonstrated that gene targeting in mammalian ES cells is a feasible and practical method for generating site-specific mutations. The efficiency of the targeting reaction is governed by the length of homology and the optimal expression of the selectable marker. This technical achievement marks a pivotal transition in mammalian genetics, transitioning the field from a reliance on spontaneous or randomly induced mutations toward an era of deliberate, rationally designed genomic engineering. If this technology proves as generalizable as our initial results suggest, it will profoundly accelerate our understanding of gene function in mammalian development and disease.

This foundational paper laid the direct groundwork for the 2007 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine, awarded to Oliver Smithies, Mario Capecchi, and Martin Evans. The Nobel committee praised them for establishing the very principles required to introduce specific gene modifications in mice. This exact technology birthed the "knockout mouse," fundamentally revolutionizing genetics by granting scientists the power to selectively delete and observe the function of almost any gene in a living mammal, profoundly elevating the study of human disease.

text: Knowledge of the expression profile and spatial landscape of the transcriptome in individual cells is essential for understanding the rich repertoire of cellular behaviors. historical_context: In 2015, Kok Hao Chen, Xiaowei Zhuang, and colleagues unveiled Multiplexed Error-Robust Fluorescence In Situ Hybridization (MERFISH). This revolutionary imaging technology permitted scientists to simultaneously identify and precisely map the spatial location of thousands of distinct RNA species within individual, fully intact cells by employing combinatorial labeling and error-correcting barcodes. It was a colossal leap forward for transcriptomics and cell biology. Prior to MERFISH, single-cell RNA sequencing strictly required the harsh dissociation of tissue, completely destroying the vital spatial context of cell location. MERFISH spearheaded the explosive field of spatial transcriptomics, seamlessly merging genomics with high-resolution imaging, and allowing researchers to accurately map the complex architectural organization and intricate cell-to-cell signaling networks within intact tissues, such as the mammalian brain.

paper_title: Spatially Resolved, Highly Multiplexed RNA Profiling in Single Cells - 2015 - Science
display_title: Spatially Resolved, Highly Multiplexed RNA Profiling in Single Cells
title: Spatially Resolved, Highly Multiplexed RNA Profiling in Single Cells
text:
System-wide analyses of the abundance and spatial organization of RNAs in single cells promise to transform our understanding in many areas of cell and developmental biology, such as the mechanisms of gene regulation, the heterogeneous behavior of cells, and the development and maintenance of cell fate. Single-molecule fluorescence in situ hybridization (smFISH) has emerged as a powerful tool for studying the copy number and spatial organization of RNAs in single cells either in isolation or in their native tissue context. Taking advantage of its ability to map the spatial distributions of specific RNAs with high resolution, smFISH has revealed the importance of subcellular RNA localization in diverse processes such as cell migration, development, and polarization. In parallel, the ability of smFISH to precisely measure the copy numbers of specific RNAs without amplification bias has allowed quantitative measurement of the natural fluctuations in gene expression, which has in turn elucidated the regulatory mechanisms that shape such fluctuations and their role in a variety of biological processes.

Recent advances in imaging and analysis methods have allowed hundreds of smFISH measurements to be performed in an automated manner, substantially expanding our knowledge of the RNA expression profile and spatial organization in different organisms. However, application of the smFISH approach to many systems-level questions remains limited by the number of RNA species that can be simultaneously measured in single cells. State-of-the-art efforts using combinatorial labeling by either color-based barcodes or sequential hybridization have enabled simultaneous measurements of 10-30 different RNA species in individual cells, yet many interesting biological questions would benefit from the measurement of hundreds to thousands of RNAs within a single cell. For example, analysis of how the expression profile of such a large number of RNAs vary from cell to cell and how these variations correlate among different genes could be used to systematically identify co-regulated genes and map regulatory networks; knowledge of the subcellular organizations of numerous RNAs and their correlations could help elucidate molecular mechanisms underlying the establishment and maintenance of many local cellular structures; and RNA profiling of individual cells in native tissues could allow in situ identification of cell type.

Here we report MERFISH, a highly multiplexed smFISH imaging method that substantially increases the number of RNA species that can be simultaneously imaged in single cells by using combinatorial labeling and sequential imaging with error-robust encoding schemes. We demonstrated this transcriptome imaging approach by simultaneously measuring 140 RNA species using an encoding scheme that can both detect and correct errors and 1001 RNA species using an encoding scheme that can detect but not correct errors. Correlation analyses of the copy number variations and spatial distributions of these genes allowed us to identify groups of genes that are co-regulated and groups of genes that share similar spatial distribution patterns inside the cell.

MERFISH spearheaded the explosive field of spatial transcriptomics, seamlessly merging genomics with high-resolution imaging, and allowing researchers to accurately map the complex architectural organization and intricate cell-to-cell signaling networks within intact tissues, such as the mammalian brain.

title: Spatially Resolved, Highly Multiplexed RNA Profiling in Single Cells
text:
The expression level and spatial organization of RNAs in individual cells provide essential information about cellular state, function, and regulation. Differences in gene expression between cells underlie diverse biological behaviors, even within genetically identical populations, and the subcellular localization of RNAs can influence where proteins are synthesized and how cellular structures are organized. Thus, a complete description of the transcriptome should ideally report both the copy number and the spatial position of many RNA species in single cells.

Several approaches have enabled transcriptome analysis at the single-cell level, but each has important limitations. RNA sequencing can measure thousands of genes in individual cells and has revealed extensive heterogeneity among apparently similar cells. However, sequencing-based measurements require RNA extraction, amplification, and molecular conversion steps that can introduce bias and destroy the native spatial context of the molecules being measured. In contrast, single-molecule fluorescence in situ hybridization (smFISH) directly detects individual RNA molecules in fixed cells, providing absolute copy-number measurements together with subcellular localization. This imaging-based approach has been used to quantify transcriptional bursting, cell-to-cell variation, and RNA localization, but conventional implementations are limited to a small number of genes because only a few spectrally distinct fluorophores can be measured simultaneously.

Increasing the throughput of smFISH to the systems scale would enable a different class of single-cell measurements. If many RNA species could be imaged together, the natural variation in gene expression across individual cells could be used to infer co-regulated gene modules, constrain regulatory networks, and predict functions for poorly characterized genes. At the same time, the spatial distributions of many RNAs could be compared within the same cells, revealing groups of transcripts that share common localization programs. Achieving this goal requires a method that combines the molecular specificity and quantitative accuracy of smFISH with the multiplexing capacity normally associated with sequencing.

Combinatorial labeling provides a route to such highly multiplexed imaging. In this strategy, each RNA species is assigned a binary code word and identified by its pattern of signals across multiple rounds of hybridization and imaging. Because the number of possible code words grows exponentially with the number of hybridization rounds, this approach can, in principle, address thousands of RNA species. However, single-molecule measurements are intrinsically imperfect: hybridization events can be missed, fluorescent spots can be falsely detected, and errors accumulated over many imaging rounds can cause one RNA species to be mistaken for another. Without a mechanism for detecting or correcting these errors, the accuracy of combinatorial RNA imaging would deteriorate rapidly as the number of measured genes increased.

Here we report multiplexed error-robust fluorescence in situ hybridization (MERFISH), a single-molecule imaging method for highly multiplexed RNA profiling in individual cells. By combining combinatorial labeling, sequential hybridization and imaging, and error-robust encoding schemes, MERFISH measures the abundance and position of hundreds to thousands of RNA species in single cells. We demonstrate this approach in human fibroblasts by imaging 140 genes with an error-correcting code and 1001 genes with an error-detecting code, and show that the resulting data can be used to analyze cell-to-cell variation, infer co-varying gene groups, predict gene function, and identify spatially organized classes of RNAs.

It was a colossal leap forward for transcriptomics and cell biology. MERFISH spearheaded the explosive field of spatial transcriptomics, seamlessly merging genomics with high-resolution imaging, and allowing researchers to accurately map the complex architectural organization and intricate cell-to-cell signaling networks within intact tissues, such as the mammalian brain.

paper_title: Spatially Resolved, Highly Multiplexed RNA Profiling in Single Cells - 2015 - Science
display_title: Spatially Resolved, Highly Multiplexed RNA Profiling in Single Cells
title: Spatially Resolved, Highly Multiplexed RNA Profiling in Single Cells
text:
The cellular transcriptome is a highly dynamic and spatially organized network of thousands of different RNA species, the precise regulation of which dictates cellular behavior, state, and identity. Characterizing the expression profile of individual cells is essential for understanding the vast heterogeneity of cell populations within tissues and the complex regulatory circuits that govern them. Single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) has recently emerged as a powerful tool for profiling the transcriptomes of individual cells, providing unprecedented insights into cellular diversity, developmental trajectories, and gene regulatory networks. However, scRNA-seq inherently requires the dissociation of cells from their native environment and the extraction of RNA, which results in the complete loss of spatial information regarding RNA localization.

The spatial organization of RNA within a cell is biologically significant. Subcellular localization of messenger RNAs (mRNAs) is a widespread mechanism used to target the translation of specific proteins to distinct subcellular compartments, thereby facilitating asymmetric cell division, cell migration, and synaptic plasticity. Similarly, the spatial distribution of non-coding RNAs is critical for their regulatory functions. Thus, a comprehensive understanding of the transcriptome requires not only knowing the abundance of each RNA species but also its precise spatial distribution within the native cellular context.

Single-molecule fluorescence in situ hybridization (smFISH) provides an excellent method for determining both the copy number and the spatial localization of specific RNA species within individual intact cells. By using multiple fluorescently labeled oligonucleotide probes that bind to the same target RNA, smFISH enables the reliable detection of individual transcript molecules as bright, diffraction-limited spots. Despite its high sensitivity and quantitative nature, conventional smFISH is limited in its multiplexing capacity. Because it relies on spectral separation, typically only three to four distinct RNA species can be simultaneously imaged in a single cell. To capture a more comprehensive picture of the cellular transcriptome, it is necessary to drastically scale up the multiplexing throughput of smFISH to the hundreds or thousands of genes.

Several sequential hybridization and barcoding approaches have been developed to increase the multiplexing capabilities of FISH. However, scaling these approaches to simultaneously image thousands of distinct RNA species faces a critical challenge: as the number of sequential measurement rounds increases, the accumulation of single-molecule labeling and detection errors severely degrades the accuracy of RNA identification, leading to a high rate of misidentification.

Here, we present multiplexed error-robust fluorescence in situ hybridization (MERFISH), a highly multiplexed single-molecule imaging technology that overcomes these limitations. MERFISH employs combinatorial labeling and sequential imaging coupled with error-robust encoding schemes to accurately identify and spatially map thousands of different RNA species within individual fixed cells. By utilizing error-correcting codes, such as a modified Hamming distance 4 code, MERFISH detects and corrects single-molecule identification errors, ensuring high detection efficiency and a low misidentification rate. We demonstrate the capability of MERFISH to simultaneously image hundreds to over a thousand RNA species in hundreds of single human fibroblast cells. Using the vast amount of spatially resolved, single-cell expression data generated, we identify novel spatial distributions of RNAs, analyze intrinsic cell-to-cell variability in gene expression, and use expression co-variation to uncover regulatory networks and predict the functions of previously unannotated genes.

It was a colossal leap forward for transcriptomics and cell biology. MERFISH spearheaded the explosive field of spatial transcriptomics, seamlessly merging genomics with high-resolution imaging, and allowing researchers to accurately map the complex architectural organization and intricate cell-to-cell signaling networks within intact tissues, such as the mammalian brain.

paper_title: Spatially Resolved, Highly Multiplexed RNA Profiling in Single Cells - 2015 - Science
display_title: Spatially Resolved, Highly Multiplexed RNA Profiling in Single Cells
title: Spatially Resolved, Highly Multiplexed RNA Profiling in Single Cells
text:
In summary, we have developed a highly multiplexed detection scheme for transcriptomic-scale RNA imaging in single cells. Using combinatorial labeling, sequential hybridization and imaging, and two different error-robust encoding schemes, we simultaneously imaged either 140 or 1001 genes in hundreds of individual human fibroblast cells. Of the two encoding schemes presented here, the MHD4 code is capable of both error detection and error correction, and hence can provide a higher calling rate and a lower misidentification rate than the MHD2 code, which instead can only detect but cannot correct errors. MHD2, on the other hand, provides a faster scaling of the degree of multiplexing with the number of bits than MHD4. Other error-robust encoding schemes can also be used for such multiplexed imaging, and experimenters can set the balance between detection accuracy and ease of multiplexing based on the specific requirements of the experiments.

By increasing the number of bits in the code words, it should be possible to further increase the number of detectable RNA species using MERFISH with either MHD4 or MHD2 codes. For example, using the MHD4 code with 32 total bits and four or six '1' bits would increase the number of addressable RNA species to 1,240 or 27,776, respectively - the latter is the approximate scale of the human transcriptome. The predicted misidentification and calling rates are still reasonable for the 32-bit MHD4 code. If more accurate measurements are desired, an additional increase in the number of bits would allow the use of encoding schemes with a Hamming distance greater than 4, further enhancing the error detection and correction capability. While an increase in the number of bits by adding more hybridization rounds would increase the data collection time and potentially lead to sample degradation, these problems could be mitigated by utilizing multiple colors to readout multiple bits in each round of hybridization.

As the degree of multiplexing is increased, it is important to consider the potential increase in the density of RNAs that need to be resolved in each round of imaging. Based on our imaging and sequencing results, we estimate that including the whole transcriptome of the IMR90 cells would lead to a total RNA density of ~200 molecules/µm^3. Using our current imaging and analysis methods, we could resolve 2-3 molecules/µm^3 per hybridization round, which would reach a total RNA density of ~20 molecules/µm^3 after 32 rounds of hybridization. This density should allow all but the top 10% most expressed genes to be imaged simultaneously or a subset of genes with even higher expression levels to be included. By utilizing more advanced image analysis algorithms to better resolve overlapping images of individual molecules, such as compressed sensing, it would be possible to extend the resolvable density by ~4-fold and thus allow nearly the entire transcriptome, except for the top 2% most expressed genes, to be imaged all together. Finally, theoretical predictions indicate that the use of super-resolution imaging could increase the resolvable density to ~10^5 molecules/µm^3, which should be ample to address the entire transcriptome even in cell types with RNA densities substantially higher than that of IMR90. However, it is important to note that RNAs in densely packed structures, such as p-bodies and stress granules, may still elude measurement.

We have illustrated the utility of the data derived from highly multiplexed RNA imaging by using co-variation and correlation analysis to reveal distinct sub-cellular distribution patterns of RNAs, to constrain gene regulatory networks, and to predict functions for many previously unannotated or partially annotated genes with unknown functions. We anticipate that many more quantitative analyses could be applied to such data sets that include the spatial localization and copy number information of many RNA species in individual cells. Given its ability to quantify RNAs across a wide range of abundances without amplification bias while preserving native context, we envision that MERFISH will enable many applications of in situ transcriptomic analyses of individual cells in culture or complex tissues.

It was a colossal leap forward for transcriptomics and cell biology. MERFISH spearheaded the explosive field of spatial transcriptomics, seamlessly merging genomics with high-resolution imaging, and allowing researchers to accurately map the complex architectural organization and intricate cell-to-cell signaling networks within intact tissues, such as the mammalian brain.

paper_title: Spatially Resolved, Highly Multiplexed RNA Profiling in Single Cells - 2015 - Science
display_title: Spatially Resolved, Highly Multiplexed RNA Profiling in Single Cells
title: Spatially Resolved, Highly Multiplexed RNA Profiling in Single Cells
text:
The results presented here establish MERFISH as a highly multiplexed, single-molecule imaging method for quantitative RNA profiling in individual cells. By combining sequential hybridization with error-robust binary encoding, MERFISH substantially extends the multiplexing capacity of smFISH while preserving the key advantages of imaging-based measurements: direct molecular counting, single-cell resolution, and spatial localization of individual RNA molecules. In the 140-gene measurements, the use of a 16-bit MHD4 code allowed single-bit errors to be corrected and double-bit errors to be detected, producing high calling rates and low misidentification rates. The agreement between independently encoded measurements, conventional smFISH, and bulk RNA sequencing indicates that this approach provides accurate and reproducible measurements over a broad range of RNA abundances. The extension to 1001 genes further demonstrates that transcriptome-scale imaging is feasible, even when error correction is sacrificed for increased coding capacity.

A central feature of this approach is that the scaling of the measurement is controlled by the encoding scheme rather than by the number of fluorescent colors. Because only a small number of readout probes are required and because each readout hybridization can be completed rapidly, MERFISH circumvents several practical barriers that have limited previous multiplexed smFISH measurements. The two-step labeling strategy, in which complex encoding probe libraries are synthesized from array-derived oligonucleotide pools and read out with a small set of fluorescent probes, should be adaptable to many different gene panels and cell types. Moreover, the use of misidentification control words provides an internal and direct measure of decoding accuracy, allowing conservative selection of genes for downstream analysis. These features should become increasingly important as the number of imaged RNA species is increased further and as MERFISH is applied to samples with higher autofluorescence, greater molecular crowding, or more complex morphology.

The multiplexing capacity of MERFISH enables analyses that are difficult to perform with lower-throughput imaging methods. In particular, measurements of hundreds to thousands of RNA species in the same single cells allow the natural cell-to-cell fluctuations in gene expression to be used as a source of regulatory information. The co-variation analysis reported here identified groups of genes with shared expression patterns and enriched functional annotations. These groups included genes involved in extracellular matrix organization, vesicle transport, cell motility, cell-cycle progression, and many other processes. The association of unannotated or poorly annotated genes with functionally coherent groups further illustrates the potential of single-cell co-variation analysis to generate hypotheses about gene function. Although such predictions require subsequent experimental validation, the strong enrichment of related GO terms among the identified groups suggests that the correlations reflect biologically meaningful regulatory structure rather than measurement noise alone.

Because MERFISH records the positions of individual RNA molecules, it also provides a direct view of the spatial organization of the transcriptome. The observed clustering of RNAs by spatial distribution revealed groups enriched either near the perinuclear region or near the cell periphery. The perinuclear enrichment of RNAs encoding secreted, extracellular, and membrane-associated proteins is consistent with translation on the endoplasmic reticulum and suggests that spatial RNA profiling can reveal common features of protein biogenesis. Conversely, the enrichment of RNAs encoding actin-binding, cytoskeletal, and motility-related proteins near the cell periphery suggests coordinated localization of transcripts involved in cellular architecture and movement. These results indicate that RNA localization patterns can be systematically classified at large scale and correlated with the cellular functions of the encoded proteins.

Several limitations remain. The current measurements were performed in cultured fibroblast cells and targeted selected gene panels rather than the complete transcriptome. Detection efficiency depends on hybridization efficiency, imaging quality, RNA abundance, and the density of molecules in the sample. The 1001-gene MHD2 measurements illustrate the tradeoff between multiplexing capacity and error correction, with reduced calling rates and increased misidentification relative to the MHD4 measurements. Future improvements in probe design, imaging speed, spot detection, sample registration, and coding strategies should further improve sensitivity, throughput, and accuracy. Increasing the number of bits, using additional colors, or designing codes that better match experimentally measured error profiles could permit still larger gene panels while maintaining robust decoding.

More broadly, MERFISH provides a framework for spatially resolved transcriptomics in single cells and, potentially, in intact tissues. By uniting molecular counting with spatial mapping and large-scale correlation analysis, this approach can connect gene expression, gene regulation, and subcellular organization in the same cells. Such measurements should be particularly useful for identifying cell types in situ, mapping regulatory states across heterogeneous populations, and determining how RNA localization contributes to cellular function. The ability to image hundreds to thousands of RNA species with single-molecule resolution therefore opens a path toward a more complete, spatially organized view of the transcriptome.

paper_title: Spatially Resolved, Highly Multiplexed RNA Profiling in Single Cells - 2015 - Science
display_title: Spatially Resolved, Highly Multiplexed RNA Profiling in Single Cells
title: Spatially Resolved, Highly Multiplexed RNA Profiling in Single Cells
text:
The development of multiplexed error-robust fluorescence in situ hybridization (MERFISH) marks a significant advancement in our ability to perform spatially resolved, system-wide transcriptomic profiling within single cells. By implementing highly multiplexed combinatorial labeling alongside novel error-correcting and error-detecting encoding schemes, we have fundamentally overcome the exponential scaling of misidentification errors that has traditionally plagued sequential smFISH methodologies. Our demonstration of simultaneously mapping 140 RNA species with high accuracy using the modified Hamming distance 4 (MHD4) code, and scaling this to 1001 RNA species utilizing the MHD2 code, underscores the immense potential of this approach for dissecting the complex molecular architecture of single cells in their native spatial context.

A defining strength of MERFISH is its quantitative precision, which we validated by demonstrating excellent agreement with both conventional smFISH and bulk RNA sequencing measurements over a dynamic range of three orders of magnitude. This sensitivity, capable of detecting transcripts present at less than one copy per cell on average, is critical for capturing the complete transcriptional landscape. The error-robust nature of our encoding schemes—particularly the MHD4 code, which enables the detection and correction of single-bit errors—was essential for maintaining a high calling rate while suppressing false positives to negligible levels. Although the 14-bit MHD2 code lacked error-correction capabilities, resulting in a predictable reduction in the overall calling rate, it nonetheless provided reliable relative abundance measurements for a massive thousand-gene network, proving that trade-offs between multiplexing capacity and absolute detection efficiency can be managed effectively based on experimental needs.

The massive single-cell data sets generated by MERFISH offer unparalleled opportunities to unravel gene regulatory networks through the analysis of stochastic cell-to-cell variations in gene expression. Our co-variation analysis of both the 140-gene and 1001-gene panels successfully identified numerous clusters of co-regulated genes, grouping them into functionally distinct modules such as those driving extracellular matrix production, cell cycle progression, and cellular motility. Notably, these analyses allowed us to putatively assign biological functions to dozens of previously unannotated genes based entirely on their tight co-expression with well-characterized gene clusters. This guilt-by-association approach demonstrates how high-throughput single-cell profiling can serve as a powerful hypothesis-generation engine for functional genomics, identifying novel participants in complex regulatory pathways and pinpointing potential transcriptional regulators driving these modular programs.

Furthermore, because MERFISH is an imaging-based technique, it inherently preserves the precise subcellular localization of every detected transcript, adding a crucial spatial dimension absent from traditional single-cell RNA sequencing methods. Our spatial correlation analyses revealed distinct classes of RNA localization, identifying groups of transcripts tightly restricted to the perinuclear region—likely reflecting co-translational translocation at the rough endoplasmic reticulum for secreted and membrane-bound proteins—and other groups specifically enriched at the cell periphery, corresponding predominantly to transcripts encoding cytoskeletal and motility-associated proteins. This robust capacity to link the spatial distribution of mRNAs directly to the function of their encoded proteins opens a new window into understanding how local translation and active RNA transport contribute to the establishment of cellular polarity, structure, and dynamic function.

In conclusion, MERFISH transforms smFISH from a targeted assay into a true systems-biology tool. The combination of error-robust encoding, combinatorial labeling, and high-resolution imaging provides an unprecedented view of the single-cell transcriptome, seamlessly integrating quantitative expression profiling with high-resolution spatial mapping. As we continue to refine optical sectioning and computational algorithms to handle increasingly dense molecular crowding, MERFISH will undoubtedly become instrumental for mapping distinct cell types in complex tissues in situ, resolving local regulatory networks, and illuminating the spatial logic of gene expression in health and disease.

paper_title: Structural and Functional Features of Central Nervous System Lymphatic Vessels - 2015 - Nature
display_title: Structural and Functional Features of Central Nervous System Lymphatic Vessels
title: Structural and Functional Features of Central Nervous System Lymphatic Vessels
text: One of the characteristics of the central nervous system is the lack of a classical lymphatic drainage system. Although it is now accepted that the central nervous system undergoes constant immune surveillance that takes place within the meningeal compartment, the mechanisms governing the entrance and exit of immune cells from the central nervous system remain poorly understood. In searching for T-cell gateways into and out of the meninges, we discovered functional lymphatic vessels lining the dural sinuses. These structures express all of the molecular hallmarks of lymphatic endothelial cells, are able to carry both fluid and immune cells from the cerebrospinal fluid, and are connected to the deep cervical lymph nodes. The unique location of these vessels may have impeded their discovery to date, thereby contributing to the long-held concept of the absence of lymphatic vasculature in the central nervous system. The discovery of the central nervous system lymphatic system may call for a reassessment of basic assumptions in neuroimmunology and sheds new light on the aetiology of neuroinflammatory and neurodegenerative diseases associated with immune system dysfunction.

text: The central nervous system has long been considered devoid of a classical lymphatic drainage system, despite the presence of immune surveillance and the capacity of cerebrospinal fluid-derived antigens to reach peripheral lymph nodes. Here we describe functional lymphatic vessels lining the dural sinuses of the mouse brain. Tracer studies, deep cervical lymph node resection and lymphatic ligation further demonstrate a physical and functional connection between the meningeal lymphatic network and the deep cervical lymph nodes. These findings identify a classical route for fluid and immune cell egress from the central nervous system.

title: Structural and Functional Features of Central Nervous System Lymphatic Vessels
text: The central nervous system (CNS) has historically been considered an immune-privileged organ lacking a classical lymphatic drainage system. In this study, we fundamentally revise this dogma by describing the discovery and characterization of a functional lymphatic vascular network within the mammalian meninges. Using a novel whole-mount preparation of mouse brain meninges, we identified vessels expressing classic lymphatic endothelial markers, including Lyve-1, Prox1, podoplanin, and VEGFR3, running parallel to the dural venous sinuses. These vessels exhibit the structural features of initial lymphatic capillaries, lacking smooth muscle coverage and possessing a discontinuous basement membrane. Through in vivo imaging and tracer injection studies, we demonstrate that the meningeal lymphatic vessels are functionally capable of draining cerebrospinal fluid and transporting both fluid and immune cells directly to the deep cervical lymph nodes. Resection or ligation of these downstream lymph nodes leads to an accumulation of immune cells within the meninges. The identification of this major fluid and leukocyte clearance pathway provides a missing mechanistic link in neuroimmunology and suggests that the dysfunction of meningeal lymphatics may contribute to the pathology of diverse neurological diseases associated with altered immune responses, such as multiple sclerosis and Alzheimer's disease.

title: Structural and Functional Features of Central Nervous System Lymphatic Vessels
text:
Seeking to identify routes responsible for the recirculation of surveying meningeal immune cells, we investigated the meningeal spaces and the immune cells that occupy those spaces. First, a whole-mount preparation of dissected mouse brain meninges was developed and stained by immunohistochemistry for endothelial cells, T cells and major histocompatibility complex II (MHCII)-expressing cells. Labelling of these cells revealed a restricted partitioning of immune cells throughout the meningeal compartments, with a high concentration of cells found in close proximity to the dural sinuses.

The dural sinuses drain blood from both the internal and the external veins of the brain into the internal jugular veins. The exact localization of the T lymphocytes around the sinuses was examined to rule out the possibility of artefacts caused by incomplete intracardial perfusion. Coronal sections of the dura mater were stained for CD3ε (T cells) and for CD31 (endothelial cells). Indeed, the vast majority of the T lymphocytes near the sinuses were abluminal. To confirm this finding, mice were injected intravenously with DyLight 488 lectin or fluorescent anti-CD45 antibody before euthanasia and the abluminal localization was confirmed and quantified. Unexpectedly, a portion of T cells (and of MHCII-expressing cells) was aligned linearly in CD31-expressing structures along the sinuses (only few cells were evident in meningeal blood vessels of similar diameter), suggesting a unique function for these perisinusal vessels.

text:
In addition to the cardiovascular system, lymphatic vessels constitute a distinct vascular network that maintains tissue-fluid homeostasis, supports immune-cell trafficking, and conveys soluble antigens from peripheral tissues to draining lymph nodes. The central nervous system has long been considered an exception to this general organization, as no classical lymphatic vasculature has been described within the brain or its surrounding meninges. This anatomical view has shaped the concept of brain immune privilege, despite accumulating evidence that the central nervous system is under constant immune surveillance and that cerebrospinal fluid-derived antigens and immune cells can reach the cervical lymph nodes.

How immune cells enter and leave the meningeal spaces, and how cerebrospinal fluid communicates with peripheral lymphoid organs, remains incompletely understood. Several routes for cerebrospinal fluid outflow have been proposed, including absorption through arachnoid granulations and drainage along olfactory pathways through the cribriform plate into nasal lymphatics. However, these mechanisms do not fully explain the organization of meningeal immune surveillance or the rapid appearance of central nervous system-derived material in deep cervical lymph nodes.

Here we identify a network of functional lymphatic vessels in the dura mater, running alongside the dural sinuses. These vessels express classical lymphatic endothelial markers, display the structural features of initial lymphatics, drain cerebrospinal fluid and immune cells, and connect the meningeal compartment with the deep cervical lymph nodes.

text:
The central nervous system (CNS) is an immune-privileged site, classically characterized by the presence of the blood-brain barrier and the apparent absence of a conventional lymphatic drainage system. In peripheral tissues, the lymphatic vasculature plays a critical role in immune surveillance and homeostasis by draining interstitial fluid and immune cells to regional lymph nodes, where immune responses are initiated. The long-standing dogma that the CNS lacks a similar lymphatic network has posed a persistent anatomical puzzle: how do immune cells and cerebrospinal fluid (CSF)-derived antigens exit the CNS to reach the cervical lymph nodes to facilitate immune surveillance?

Although it is increasingly recognized that the CNS is not entirely isolated from the immune system and undergoes constant immune surveillance within the meningeal compartments, the precise anatomical routes governing the trafficking of immune cells and the clearance of macromolecules from the CNS remain obscure. Previous studies suggested that CSF drains along olfactory nerve routes through the cribriform plate into nasal mucosa lymphatics, but this pathway does not fully account for the robust movement of immune cells to the deep cervical lymph nodes.

In our pursuit to identify the primary gateways for T-cell trafficking into and out of the meninges, we systematically examined the meningeal compartments of the mouse brain. Here, we report the discovery of a functional lymphatic vascular network lining the dural sinuses. These previously unrecognized meningeal lymphatic vessels express classical lymphatic endothelial cell markers, carry fluid and immune cells from the CSF, and connect directly to the deep cervical lymph nodes.

text:
Drainage of the cerebrospinal fluid into the periphery has been a subject of interest for decades and several routes have been described regarding how cerebrospinal fluid can leave the central nervous system. The newly discovered meningeal lymphatic vessels are a novel path for cerebrospinal fluid drainage and represent a more conventional path for immune cells to egress the central nervous system. Our findings may represent the second step in the drainage of the interstitial fluid from the brain parenchyma into the periphery after it has been drained into the cerebrospinal fluid through the recently discovered glymphatic system.

The presence of a functional and classical lymphatic system in the central nervous system suggests that current dogmas regarding brain tolerance and the immune privilege of the brain should be revisited. Malfunction of the meningeal lymphatic vessels could be a root cause of a variety of neurological disorders in which altered immunity is a fundamental player such as multiple sclerosis, Alzheimer's disease, and some forms of primary lymphoedema that are associated with neurological disorders.

paper_title: Suppression of RNA Recognition by Toll-like Receptors - The Impact of Nucleoside Modification and the Evolutionary Origin of RNA - 2005 - Immunity
display_title: Suppression of RNA Recognition by Toll-like Receptors - The Impact of Nucleoside Modification and the Evolutionary Origin of RNA
title: Suppression of RNA Recognition by Toll-like Receptors - The Impact of Nucleoside Modification and the Evolutionary Origin of RNA
text: DNA and RNA stimulate the mammalian innate immune system through activation of Toll-like receptors (TLRs). DNA containing methylated CpG motifs, however, is not stimulatory. Selected nucleosides in naturally occurring RNA are also methylated or otherwise modified, but the immunomodulatory effects of these alterations remain untested. We show that RNA signals through human TLR3, TLR7, and TLR8, but incorporation of modified nucleosides m5C, m6A, m5U, s2U, or pseudouridine ablates activity. Dendritic cells (DCs) exposed to such modified RNA express significantly less cytokines and activation markers than those treated with unmodified RNA. DCs and TLR-expressing cells are potently activated by bacterial and mitochondrial RNA, but not by mammalian total RNA, which is abundant in modified nucleosides. We conclude that nucleoside modifications suppress the potential of RNA to activate DCs. The innate immune system may therefore detect RNA lacking nucleoside modification as a means of selectively responding to bacteria or necrotic tissue.
historical_context: In 2005, Katalin Karikó, Drew Weissman, and colleagues published this foundational paper overcoming a massive, seemingly insurmountable roadblock in mRNA therapy: the reality that synthetic mRNA injected into the body provoked a severe, highly inflammatory immune response. They discovered that by actively incorporating naturally occurring modified nucleosides (such as pseudouridine) into the synthetic mRNA sequence, they could effectively blind the body's Toll-like receptors to its presence, rendering the mRNA completely stealthy and non-inflammatory. This vital breakthrough served as the cornerstone achievement that secured their 2023 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine. The Nobel committee explicitly cited this 2005 discovery, emphasizing that eliminating this inflammatory hurdle was the exact key that enabled the unprecedented, rapid engineering of the highly successful mRNA vaccines during the COVID-19 pandemic.

text: RNA activates innate immune cells, yet the molecular basis by which self RNA is distinguished from microbial RNA has remained unclear. Here we show that nucleoside modification is a critical determinant of RNA recognition by Toll-like receptors. In vitro-transcribed RNA and bacterial RNA stimulated human dendritic cells and 293 cells expressing human TLR3, TLR7, or TLR8, whereas mammalian RNA species rich in modified nucleosides were weakly stimulatory or inactive. Incorporation of naturally occurring modified nucleosides, including N6-methyladenosine, 5-methylcytidine, 5-methyluridine, 2-thiouridine, pseudouridine, and 2'-O-methyluridine, reduced or abolished RNA-mediated cytokine induction and dendritic cell maturation. Suppression was proportional to the frequency of modified nucleosides, and even a single modification within short oligoribonucleotides diminished immune activation. These findings identify nucleoside modification as a feature by which the innate immune system discriminates among RNAs and suggest that the evolutionary accumulation of RNA modifications limits inappropriate recognition of host RNA while permitting responses to bacterial and mitochondrial RNA.

text: The innate immune system utilizes Toll-like receptors (TLRs) to distinguish pathogenic nucleic acids from host molecules. While DNA recognition relies on unmethylated CpG motifs, the basis for discriminating host from pathogen RNA remains elusive. Here, we demonstrate that the immunostimulatory potential of RNA is governed by naturally occurring nucleoside modifications. Unmodified RNAs, such as bacterial RNA and in vitro transcripts, potently activate dendritic cells and trigger human TLR3, TLR7, and TLR8. In contrast, eukaryotic RNAs, which are highly abundant in post-transcriptional modifications, are significantly less stimulatory. Incorporating specific modified nucleosides—such as 5-methylcytidine, pseudouridine, 2-thiouridine, and N6-methyladenosine—into synthetic transcripts severely suppresses their capacity to activate TLR signaling and induce cytokine secretion. This immune evasion effect correlates with the frequency of modifications present within the RNA. These findings identify nucleoside modification as a fundamental mechanism allowing the innate immune system to avoid auto-reactivity against self-RNA, providing critical insights into autoimmune pathogenesis and the optimization of therapeutic RNA design.

paper_title: Suppression of RNA Recognition by Toll-like Receptors - The Impact of Nucleoside Modification and the Evolutionary Origin of RNA - 2005 - Immunity
display_title: Suppression of RNA Recognition by Toll-like Receptors - The Impact of Nucleoside Modification and the Evolutionary Origin of RNA
title: Suppression of RNA Recognition by Toll-like Receptors - The Impact of Nucleoside Modification and the Evolutionary Origin of RNA
text:
The innate immune system is the first line of defense against invading pathogens. This system utilizes TLRs to recognize conserved pathogen-associated molecular patterns and orchestrate the initiation of immune responses. TLRs are germline-encoded signaling receptors with extracellular leucine-rich repeats and intracellular signaling domains. In humans, ten distinct TLR family members have been identified, and corresponding microbial ligands for most have been identified. Several TLRs recognize and respond to nucleic acids. DNA containing unmethylated CpG motifs, characteristic of bacterial and viral DNA, activate TLR9. Double-stranded (ds)RNA, a frequent viral constituent, has been shown to activate TLR3, single-stranded (ss)RNA activates mouse TLR7, and RNA oligonucleotides with phosphorothioate internucleotide linkages are ligands of human TLR8. Based on structural and sequence similarities, TLR7, TLR8, and TLR9 form a subfamily. Activation of these receptors depends upon endosomal acidification and leads to interferon production. Human TLR7 and TLR8 are stimulated by the synthetic antiviral compound R-848, but a natural ligand has not been identified.

It has been known for decades that selected DNA and RNA molecules have the unique property to activate the immune system. It was discovered only recently that secretion of interferon in response to DNA is mediated by unmethylated CpG motifs acting upon TLR9 present on immune cells. For years, bacterial and mammalian DNA were portrayed as having the same chemical structure, which hampered the understanding of why only bacterial, but not mammalian, DNA is immunogenic. Recently, however, the sequence and structural microheterogeneity of DNA has come to be appreciated. For example, methylated cytidine in CpG motifs of DNA has proven to be the structural basis of recognition for the innate immune system. In light of this finding and given that multiple TLRs respond to RNA, a question emerges as to whether the immunogenicity of RNA is under the control of similar types of modification. This possibility is not unreasonable given that RNA undergoes nearly one hundred different nucleoside modifications. Importantly, the extent and quality of RNA modifications depend on the RNA subtype and correlate directly with the evolutionary level of the organism from which the RNA is isolated. Ribosomal RNA, the major constituent (≈80%) of cellular RNA, contains significantly more nucleoside modifications when obtained from mammalian cells versus bacteria. Human rRNA, for example, has ten times more pseudouridine (Ψ) and 25 times more 2'-O-methylated nucleosides than bacterial rRNA, whereas rRNA from mitochondria, an organelle that is a remnant of eubacteria, has very few modifications. Transfer RNA is the most heavily modified subgroup of RNA. In mammalian tRNAs, up to 25% of the nucleosides are modified, whereas there are significantly less modifications in prokaryotic tRNAs. Bacterial mRNA contains no nucleoside modifications, whereas mammalian mRNAs have modified nucleosides.

In recent years, we have investigated the immunomodulatory effect of RNA on human DCs. These studies demonstrated that in vitro-transcribed RNA activates/matures DCs partially by a mechanism in which double-stranded regions of the RNA signal through TLR3. Recently, it was noted that in vitro transcripts, or total RNA derived from bacteria, but not from eukaryotic cells, can prime DCs for high-level IL-12 secretion. The molecular basis for the discrimination between these various RNAs is not fully understood. In this report, we sought to determine whether naturally occurring nucleoside modifications modulate the immunostimulatory potential of RNA and the role TLRs might play in this process.
