paper_title: The C elegans Heterochronic Gene lin-4 Encodes Small RNAs with Antisense Complementarity to lin-14 - 1993 - Cell
display_title: The C elegans Heterochronic Gene lin-4 Encodes Small RNAs with Antisense Complementarity to lin-14
title: The C elegans Heterochronic Gene lin-4 Encodes Small RNAs with Antisense Complementarity to lin-14
text:
The lin-4 Locus

The C. elegans heterochronic gene lin-4 was identified by chromosomal walking from linked polymorphisms, characterization of the molecular lesion associated with a lin-4 allele, e972, and transformation rescue. lin-4(e972) is most likely a lin-4 null allele, because its lesion removes most of the sequences corresponding to the 693 bp clone that rescues lin-4 function. This observation, together with the fact that lin-14 null alleles are completely epistatic to lin-4(e972) suggests that the sole function of lin-4 is to downregulate lin-14.

The C. elegans lin-4 gene is located within the intron of a gene of unknown function. Although lin-4 is transcribed in the same orientation as that gene, it is unlikely that the expression or function of lin-4 depends on the host gene. This is because genomic clones that contain essentially only intron sequences of the host gene rescue lin-4(e972) extrachromosomally. These rescued animals appear fully wild type, despite the fact that e972 deletes a substantial portion of the host gene in addition to the lin-4 rescuing region, indicating that the activity of the host gene is probably not required for lin-4 function or for other aspects of normal development. Although our data indicate that the 693 bp SalI rescuing fragment contains the entire lin-4 locus, all our rescue experiments were performed using high copy extrachromosomal arrays. Since we have not tested the 693 bp SalI clones in single copy, we cannot rule out the possibility that some additional sequences may be required for full lin-4 activity. Generally, rescue of lin-4 retarded defects by high-copy plasmid arrays was complete, and we did not consistently observe additional developmental defects that might be associated with overexpression of lin-4 from the arrays.

lin-4 Is Unlikely to Encode a Protein

Two lines of evidence strongly suggest that none of the lin-4 genomic sequence encodes protein. First, strategically located frameshift and nonsense mutations, as well as an ATG to ACG mutation, were introduced into several putative open reading frames of the C. elegans genomic clone without affecting rescuing activity. Second, comparison of the C. elegans, C. briggsae, C. remanei, and C. vulgaris lin-4 genomic sequences (which are functionally interchangeable in C. elegans) revealed a high level of nucleic acid sequence conservation but no conserved open reading frames. Regions of conserved sequence do not show a degeneracy at the third base positions of putative codons that would be expected for protein coding sequences, and they contain deletions or insertions that would cause frameshifts. There are no splice donor or acceptor sites that would compensate for these frameshifts. Thus, if a conserved protein product were encoded by the lin-4 locus, its synthesis would need to be directed by unorthodox translation start and stop signals. The 693 bp C. elegans fragment rescues in either orientation, arguing against the generation of a lin-4 protein from the rescuing fragment using translational signals from the vector.

lin-4 Encodes Small RNAs

Northern blots and RNase protection experiments with lin-4 genomic probes detected the lin-4S and lin-4L small RNAs in total RNA of wild-type and rescued lin-4(e972) animals. Several observations argue that either lin-4L RNA or lin-4S RNA or both are the functional lin-4 gene products. First, the ma161 mutation, which results in loss of lin-4 function, affects a conserved C of the lin-4L and lin-4S transcripts. Second, the DNA sequence encoding lin-4L and lin-4S is virtually identical in four nematode species. Finally, the sequence complementarity between the lin-4 transcripts and the lin-14 mRNA suggests a direct base-pairing interaction between lin-4 and lin-14 RNAs and lends credence to the idea that lin-4S, lin-4L, or both may be the active lin-4 gene product. Regions of very high sequence conservation were identified outside the lin-4L- and lin-4S-transcribed regions of the lin-4-rescuing clones. One of these is located between approximately 140 and 420 bp upstream of the transcribed sequences. This region is essential for lin-4 function, but its orientation is not critical, because when it is inverted with respect to the transcribed region, partial rescuing activity is observed. These upstream conserved regions could contain regulatory sequences or encode additional small RNAs of an abundance too low to be detected in our experiments. No other lin-4 products besides lin-4L and lin-4S were consistently detected in our experiments.

lin-4L and lin-4S may be transcribed from the same promoter, given that their 5' ends appear to be identical. lin-4S could be generated by a posttranscriptional processing of lin-4L, or lin-4L may be produced by read-through of a lin-4S termination site. It is possible that either lin-4L or lin-4S is a nonfunctional byproduct of the biogenesis of the other, or that both are derived by the processing of a longer precursor transcript. lin-4L and lin-4S seem to be synthesized in approximately normal levels in a strain lacking the lin-14 3' UTR, suggesting that the generation of the two products likely occurs independently of an interaction with the lin-14 mRNA. The fact that the lengths determined for lin-4S and lin-4L by RNase protection and S1 mapping agree with the lengths estimated by Northern blot analysis indicates that the RNAs are not heavily modified posttranscriptionally (for example by significant splicing, polyadenylation, or covalent linkage to protein). However, these experiments would not necessarily detect slight modification or processing of the RNAs, such as modified nucleotides or cap structures. Further experiments are required to develop a complete description of lin-4 RNA biogenesis and structure.

Antisense Complementarity to the lin-14 3' UTR

Sequence comparison of the lin-4 transcripts with the 3' UTR of lin-14 revealed that a sequence element repeated seven times in the lin-14 3' UTR is complementary to the lin-4 transcripts. The significance of this complementarity to lin-4 function is supported by phylogenetic and genetic observations. First, the seven repeated elements are conserved between the C. elegans and C. briggsae lin-14 sequences, consistent with a conserved lin-4:lin-14 RNA base pairing and the observed conservation of lin-4 function between these species. Second, the lin-4(ma161) loss-of-function mutation is located within a block of lin-14-complementary sequences, and so could affect lin-4 activity by altering or destabilizing a lin-4:lin-14 RNA hybrid. Third, the seven lin-4-complementary elements occur in the lin-14 region deleted by lin-14(gf) alleles. These observations strongly support the hypothesis that lin-4 downregulates LIN-14 protein levels through a direct RNA-RNA interaction with the lin-14 3' UTR.

Although the longest stretch of contiguous sequence complementarity between lin-4 and lin-14 is 10 nt, there is precedence for specific RNA-RNA interactions by such short regions of complementarity. For example, interactions among spliceosomal RNAs and between spliceosomal RNAs and the pre-mRNAs involve similarly short duplexes. Since both lin-4S and lin-4L span the ma161 lesion, and both include sequences complementary to the 3' UTR of lin-14, either could in principle interact with lin-14 RNA. Although our 5' and 3' end-mapping experiments indicate that lin-4S might not contain all the lin-14-complementary sequences, the ambiguity in these data leaves open the possibility that lin-4S contains the entire lin-14-complementary sequence. Since lin-4S is significantly more abundant than lin-4L, it seems likely that lin-4S plays the major role in base pairing with lin-14 RNA, especially if the interaction is stoichiometric. Furthermore, the predicted secondary structure of lin-4L would sequester within a stem most of the bases that are complementary to lin-14, perhaps rendering lin-4L inactive for base-pairing with lin-14 mRNA. We cannot evaluate how the secondary structure of lin-4S might affect its function, since the precise 5' and 3' ends of lin-4S are uncertain, and this variability would significantly alter any proposed secondary structure.

A definitive proof of the proposed antisense pairing between lin-4 and lin-14 RNAs will require the construction of compensatory base-pairing mutations in lin-14 and lin-4. Furthermore, determination of whether the interaction involves a stable complex or a transient (perhaps catalytic) interaction will require careful measurement of lin-4 and lin-14 RNA levels and direct tests for a complex in vivo.

Temporal Regulation of lin-14 by lin-4

If lin-4 RNA inhibits lin-14 translation by an antisense interaction with the lin-14 3' UTR, then the temporal decrease of LIN-14 protein during C. elegans development is probably generated by a stage-specific increase in lin-4 gene expression. According to this view, lin-4 is inactive during late embryogenesis and the early part of the L1, permitting synthesis of a high level of LIN-14 protein, which programs the execution of L1 fates. Later in the L1 stage, lin-4 gene expression is activated, and the lin-4L transcripts, lin-4S transcripts, or both bind to the complementary sequence elements in the 3' UTR of lin-14 mRNA. This RNA-RNA interaction results, through some unknown mechanism, in a decrease in LIN-14 protein between the L1 and L2 stages. The resulting reduced level of LIN-14 protein specifies the expression of L2-specific fates in the L2, and L3-specific fates in the L3. To downregulate lin-14 globally, lin-4 would have to be activated stage-specifically in at least those cells that express lin-14. A developmental activation of lin-4 might involve transcriptional induction or posttranscriptional modification of lin-4 RNA, or both. Little is known about what developmental signal(s) activate lin-4 gene expression, but it is likely that lin-4 is not activated until after a food signal initiates postembryonic development, since LIN-14 protein does not decrease until after feeding. lin-4 may be required only in the L1 to modify lin-14 mRNA into an untranslatable state, or also during later stages to inhibit LIN-14 protein synthesis continuously. Features of this model need to be tested by examining in detail the temporal profile of lin-4 transcript levels and by artificially manipulating the level of active lin-4 RNA during development.

Antisense Regulation via 3' UTRs

There are a number of known examples of natural antisense regulatory mechanisms that affect the stability or translatability of target RNAs. lin-4 RNA probably does not control the stability of lin-14 mRNA because the steady state levels of lin-14 mRNA remain relatively constant during development and are not appreciably altered in a lin-4 mutant background. lin-4 could inhibit LIN-14 protein synthesis indirectly, for example by modifying lin-14 mRNA, or by localizing it to a subcellular compartment (such as the nucleus) where it is inaccessible to the ribosomes. Alternatively, lin-4 RNA may bind to lin-14 mRNA in the cytoplasm and inhibit its translation by interacting directly with components of the translational machinery. Distinguishing among these broad classes of models will require determination of the subcellular location of lin-4 and lin-14 RNAs.

Previously known natural antisense mechanisms that affect translation involve interaction of an antisense RNA with the 5' UTR of the mRNA and appear to affect ribosome binding. By contrast, if lin-4 does directly inhibit translation, then the proposed interaction between lin-4 RNA and the 3' end of lin-14 might represent a novel kind of antisense translational control mechanism. Structural features of the lin-4-lin-14 complex might interfere with a critical interaction between the 3' and 5' ends of the lin-14 mRNA. A regulation of poly(A) length might be involved or an interaction with translation factors, such as poly(A) binding protein, that act via the 3' end. lin-4 RNA may act in conjunction with proteins, but to date there is no genetic evidence for genes other than lin-4 that regulate lin-14 via its 3' UTR. It is noteworthy that lin-4 RNA sequences that are not complementary to lin-14, and hence are looped out of the predicted lin-4:lin-14 hybrid, are completely conserved. This loop may bind cellular proteins involved in the control of lin-14 mRNA. Proteins that interact with lin-4 RNA might also have functions essential for cell viability, and so it may not be surprising that the genes encoding them have apparently not been previously identified by a visible heterochronic phenotype.

The involvement of 3' UTR sequences in mRNA localization, stability, or translation and the developmental roles of posttranscriptional regulatory mechanisms mediated by 3' UTR sequences are becoming increasingly clear. For example, the mRNAs of mouse protamines, Drosophila bicoid, hunchback, and nanos, and nematode fem-3 and tra-2 contain developmentally significant posttranscriptional regulatory sequences in their 3' UTRs. As yet, no antisense regulatory RNAs have been shown to be involved in the above cases, but the possibility has not been ruled out for any of them. Thus, lin-14 may not be the only developmental gene that is posttranscriptionally regulated via its 3' end by a small antisense RNA. Because the regulatory affect of a small antisense RNA such as lin-4 is directed toward the translation of a specific antisense partner mRNA, translational control of gene expression by small antisense RNAs may be a particularly effective strategy for providing a developmental switch of very high specificity.

Small Regulatory RNAs

lin-4 may represent a class of developmental regulatory genes that encode small antisense RNA products, but such genes might be difficult to identify by standard genetic approaches. If lin-4 is active in lin-14 shut-off, it may be among the smallest functional RNAs so far identified. The mouse and human Xist genes are believed to encode active RNAs important in the control of X chromosome inactivation, and the mouse H19 gene also appears to encode an untranslated RNA. However, the products of both these genes are much larger than lin-4 RNA; the Xist RNAs are 15-17 kb and the H19 RNAs are 2.6 kb. Small RNA molecules play a critical role in essential cellular processes such as RNA processing or editing. However, even compared with this group, the lin-4 RNAs are unusually small. At 61 and 22 bases, respectively, lin-4L and lin-4S are shorter than the 80 base guide RNA molecules from Leishmania. Given its small size, lin-4S may interact directly with relatively few cellular proteins.

The scarcity of lin-4 alleles is consistent with the relatively small size of the lin-4 locus. We identified a single allele, ma161, after screening a relatively large number (>20,000) of mutagenized genome equivalents. Other small RNA genes within introns have been identified in C. elegans as part of a genomic sequencing project and in mouse and human. The small target size for mutations that affect such genes without altering the host gene and the lack, in many cases, of distinguishing features in their genomic sequence might contribute to the difficulty of identifying other small RNA genes like lin-4.
historical_context: In 1993, Rosalind Lee, Rhonda Feinbaum, and Victor Ambros published the astonishing revelation that the *lin-4* gene in *C. elegans* completely lacks the code to produce a protein. Instead, it generates a tiny, 22-nucleotide non-coding RNA that heavily regulates the translation of the *lin-14* mRNA through direct antisense complementarity. This marked the very first discovery of a microRNA (miRNA). The finding was a seismic shift for molecular biology. Dismissed for years as a quirky biological oddity specific to worms, miRNAs were eventually proven to be universally conserved and absolutely vital for fine-tuning gene expression across all animal species, including humans. This discovery threw open the doors to an entirely new, massive dimension of post-transcriptional gene regulation, reshaping the scientific understanding of embryonic development, cancer biology, and complex cellular networks.

paper_title: The C elegans Heterochronic Gene lin-4 Encodes Small RNAs with Antisense Complementarity to lin-14 - 1993 - Cell
display_title: The C elegans Heterochronic Gene lin-4 Encodes Small RNAs with Antisense Complementarity to lin-14
title: The C elegans Heterochronic Gene lin-4 Encodes Small RNAs with Antisense Complementarity to lin-14
text:
The lin-4 Gene Product

The molecular analysis of the C. elegans heterochronic gene lin-4 indicates that this locus defines an unusual class of developmental regulatory gene. lin-4 was localized by transformation rescue to a 693 bp genomic fragment, and this fragment is sufficient to restore essentially wild-type development to animals carrying the lin-4(e972) mutation. Since the e972 lesion deletes most or all of the rescuing sequences, and since lin-14 null mutations suppress the lin-4 mutant phenotype, lin-4(e972) is likely to eliminate the activity required for the normal negative regulation of lin-14. Thus the principal, and perhaps sole, genetically defined role of lin-4 in the heterochronic pathway is to reduce lin-14 activity at the proper time in early larval development.

Several features of the lin-4 locus argue against a conventional protein-coding function. The rescuing region is contained within an intron of another transcription unit, yet sequences corresponding to this intron alone can rescue lin-4 mutant animals. Moreover, this rescuing activity is not abolished by mutations that disrupt the most plausible open reading frames. The comparison of lin-4 sequences from four Caenorhabditis species is especially informative, because all four genomic clones rescue lin-4(e972) in C. elegans. The conserved sequences show strong nucleotide conservation without the pattern of codon degeneracy, conserved reading frame, and splice-site organization expected of a protein-coding gene. Although these observations cannot formally exclude every possible unorthodox mode of translation, they make it unlikely that the essential lin-4 product is a polypeptide.

The identification of two small lin-4 RNAs provides a more plausible explanation for lin-4 function. The 61 nt lin-4L RNA and the approximately 22 nt lin-4S RNA are absent from lin-4(e972) animals and are present in wild-type and rescued animals. The point mutation ma161, which causes loss of lin-4 function, changes a conserved nucleotide near the 5' end of both small RNAs. This result links the transcribed small RNA sequences directly to lin-4 activity. In addition, the sequences encoding these RNAs are highly conserved among the Caenorhabditis species examined. These data suggest that one or both of the small RNAs, rather than an encoded protein, constitute the functional lin-4 gene product.

Relationship of lin-4L and lin-4S

The relationship between lin-4L and lin-4S is not yet clear. The two RNAs appear to have the same 5' end, and lin-4S corresponds to the 5' portion of lin-4L. lin-4S could be produced by processing of lin-4L, whereas lin-4L could arise by read-through of a termination site used to generate lin-4S. Alternatively, both RNAs could derive from a longer precursor that has not been detected in our experiments. The agreement between the sizes estimated by Northern blotting, RNase protection, and S1 nuclease mapping suggests that these RNAs are not extensively modified by splicing or polyadenylation, but these methods would not necessarily reveal small terminal modifications or altered nucleotides.

It is possible that only one of the two RNAs is active in lin-14 regulation and that the other is a byproduct of its synthesis. lin-4S is much more abundant than lin-4L in total RNA, which may favor lin-4S as the active species, particularly if the regulatory interaction with lin-14 is stoichiometric. In addition, predicted folding of lin-4L would place some lin-14-complementary residues in an intramolecular stem, which could reduce their availability for pairing with lin-14 mRNA. However, the precise ends and possible structure of lin-4S remain uncertain, and lin-4L may have functions not apparent from abundance alone. Direct tests of each RNA species will be required to determine whether lin-4S, lin-4L, or both are necessary and sufficient for lin-4 activity.

The conserved sequences upstream of the lin-4 RNAs may also be important for expression or processing. Some of this conserved DNA lies outside the transcribed small RNAs but within the rescuing region, and inversion of upstream sequences can still yield partial rescue. These sequences may contain promoter or processing signals, or they may encode additional low-abundance products below the detection limits of the assays used here. Nevertheless, the only consistent lin-4 products detected were lin-4L and lin-4S, and the ma161 lesion in their shared sequence provides strong evidence that these small RNAs are central to lin-4 function.

Antisense Complementarity to lin-14

The most striking feature of the lin-4 RNAs is their complementarity to repeated elements in the lin-14 3' untranslated region. lin-14 is regulated posttranscriptionally: lin-14 mRNA levels remain relatively constant during development, while LIN-14 protein decreases sharply during the L1 stage. Previous genetic and fusion-gene studies showed that sequences in the lin-14 3' UTR are required in cis for this decrease and that lin-4 is required in trans. The present results identify a molecular basis for this relationship. Two short blocks of lin-4 sequence can pair with a sequence repeated seven times in the lin-14 3' UTR, and these repeated elements are located in the region affected by lin-14 gain-of-function mutations.

Several observations support the idea that lin-4 negatively regulates lin-14 through a direct RNA-RNA interaction. First, the complementary sequences in the lin-14 3' UTR are conserved in Caenorhabditis, consistent with their importance for regulation. Second, the ma161 mutation alters a nucleotide within one of the lin-14-complementary regions of lin-4. Third, the repeated complementary elements are within the 3' UTR sequences known to mediate the lin-4-dependent decrease in LIN-14 protein. These independent genetic, phylogenetic, and molecular correlations are most simply explained by a model in which lin-4 RNA base pairs with lin-14 mRNA.

The proposed pairing is short, with no single continuous duplex longer than about 10 nt. Nevertheless, short RNA duplexes can be biologically specific when they occur in an appropriate structural and molecular context. Interactions between small nuclear RNAs and pre-mRNAs provide precedents for regulatory RNA-RNA contacts involving limited complementarity. The seven repeated elements in lin-14 may increase the probability or stability of the interaction with lin-4 RNA, or may allow more than one lin-4 molecule to bind a single lin-14 transcript. The repeated arrangement could also permit partial pairing at multiple sites, thereby reducing the requirement for an extensive uninterrupted duplex.

Definitive proof of the antisense model will require compensatory mutations. A mutation in lin-14 that disrupts pairing should prevent normal downregulation of LIN-14 protein, and a corresponding mutation in lin-4 should restore regulation if base pairing is the essential feature. It will also be important to determine whether lin-4 forms a stable complex with lin-14 mRNA in vivo or acts transiently, perhaps with the assistance of cellular proteins. Such experiments should distinguish whether lin-4 behaves as a simple antisense RNA, a guide for modification of lin-14 mRNA, or part of a larger ribonucleoprotein regulatory complex.

Temporal Regulation of lin-14

The genetic behavior of lin-4 and lin-14 suggests a developmental switch in which high lin-14 activity specifies early L1 programs, and reduced lin-14 activity permits later stage-specific programs. The data presented here suggest that the switch is executed posttranscriptionally by small lin-4 RNAs acting through the lin-14 3' UTR. In this model, lin-4 activity is absent or ineffective during late embryogenesis and the earliest part of the L1 stage, allowing LIN-14 protein to accumulate. Later in the L1, lin-4 activity increases or becomes competent to act, causing LIN-14 protein levels to fall without a corresponding reduction in lin-14 mRNA.

The activation of lin-4 could occur at several levels. lin-4 RNA abundance may increase at a particular time in larval development, or the RNAs may be present but inactive until a processing, localization, or association step occurs. The observation that LIN-14 protein does not decrease until after feeding suggests that lin-4 activation may be coupled to the initiation of postembryonic development. To regulate lin-14 in the diverse cells affected by the heterochronic pathway, lin-4 must act in many or all cells that require the L1-to-L2 transition in developmental fate. Whether this widespread effect reflects broad lin-4 expression or movement of a regulatory signal remains unknown.

It is also not known whether lin-4 acts only once to convert lin-14 mRNA into a persistently inactive state, or whether continued lin-4 activity is required to maintain repression of LIN-14 synthesis. If lin-4 acts transiently, it might induce a stable change in lin-14 mRNA or in a protein complex associated with the lin-14 3' UTR. If continued activity is required, lin-4 RNA levels or activity should remain sufficient during later larval stages to prevent renewed LIN-14 synthesis. Measurements of lin-4L and lin-4S abundance during staged development, together with experimental manipulation of lin-4 expression, should clarify how the temporal decline in LIN-14 protein is generated.

A 3' UTR-Mediated Antisense Mechanism

Natural antisense RNAs are known to regulate target RNAs by affecting stability or translation, but the lin-4-lin-14 interaction may represent a distinct example of translational control through the 3' UTR. lin-4 probably does not regulate lin-14 primarily by altering mRNA abundance, because steady-state lin-14 mRNA levels are not appreciably changed during the period in which LIN-14 protein declines, nor are they strongly altered in lin-4 mutants. Thus lin-4 is more likely to affect the translatability, localization, or structure of lin-14 mRNA.

One possibility is that lin-4 RNA binds the lin-14 3' UTR in the cytoplasm and directly interferes with translation. Although many translational control mechanisms act at the 5' end of an mRNA, regulatory information in 3' UTRs is increasingly recognized as important for development. Pairing of lin-4 RNA with the 3' end of lin-14 mRNA could interfere with interactions between the 3' and 5' ends of the transcript, alter poly(A)-dependent translational stimulation, or recruit proteins that inhibit ribosome function. Alternatively, lin-4 might direct lin-14 mRNA to a subcellular compartment where it is poorly translated, or promote an mRNA modification that leaves abundance unchanged but reduces translational efficiency.

The conserved lin-4 sequences not predicted to pair with lin-14 are also noteworthy. In the proposed lin-4:lin-14 hybrid, these residues would be looped out. Their conservation suggests that they may bind proteins or contribute to a higher-order RNA structure required for lin-4 function. Such proteins might be essential for viability or might act redundantly, which could explain why additional heterochronic genes required specifically for lin-14 3' UTR regulation have not been identified genetically. Determining the cellular location of lin-4 RNAs and identifying associated proteins will be necessary to define the mechanism by which 3' UTR pairing inhibits LIN-14 protein synthesis.

Small RNAs as Developmental Regulators

lin-4 may be a member of a broader class of genes whose products are small regulatory RNAs rather than proteins. Other untranslated RNAs, including Xist and H19 RNAs, have been implicated in developmental processes, but they are much larger than lin-4L or lin-4S. Small RNAs also have important functions in RNA processing and editing, yet the putative active lin-4S RNA is unusually short even by comparison with many of these molecules. Its small size may allow highly specific recognition of a target mRNA through short complementary elements, while limiting the number of other interactions it can make.

The difficulty of identifying lin-4 alleles may reflect the small target size of this type of gene. Only one additional lin-4 allele, ma161, was recovered after a large noncomplementation screen, and that mutation changes a single conserved nucleotide in the small RNA sequence. Similar small RNA genes, particularly those located within introns of other genes, may be missed in conventional searches for protein-coding sequences or may be difficult to recover genetically unless mutations affect a very small critical region. The existence of lin-4 suggests that some developmental regulatory information may be encoded in short noncoding transcripts that are not readily recognized by standard criteria.

The regulation of lin-14 by lin-4 provides a mechanism for converting a temporal developmental signal into the stage-specific repression of a key regulatory protein. By targeting a defined 3' UTR through sequence complementarity, a small RNA can impose highly specific translational control without changing target mRNA abundance. This strategy may be especially useful in developmental pathways, where the timing of protein expression must be sharply controlled in many cells. If other small RNAs act similarly, then antisense interactions with 3' UTRs may represent a general means by which developmental genes are regulated posttranscriptionally.

text: The heterochronic gene lin-4 was successfully cloned and found to encode two small RNAs, rather than a protein. These small RNAs possess sequence complementarity to the 3' UTR of lin-14 mRNA, suggesting a novel mechanism of post-transcriptional regulation via antisense RNA interaction. This antisense interaction likely mediates the critical temporal downregulation of LIN-14 protein required for normal postembryonic developmental progression in C. elegans. The identification of lin-4 as a non-coding, small regulatory RNA introduces a new paradigm for understanding temporal developmental switches and highlights the potential for small RNAs to act as precise, stage-specific translational regulators in diverse organisms.

paper_title: The Dependence of Cell-Free Protein Synthesis in E coli upon Naturally Occurring or Synthetic Polyribonucleotides - 1961 - PNAS
display_title: The Dependence of Cell-Free Protein Synthesis in E coli upon Naturally Occurring or Synthetic Polyribonucleotides
title: The Dependence of Cell-Free Protein Synthesis in E coli upon Naturally Occurring or Synthetic Polyribonucleotides
text: A stable, cell-free system has been obtained from E. coli in which the amount of incorporation of amino acids into protein was dependent upon the addition of heat-stable template RNA preparations. Soluble RNA could not replace template RNA fractions. In addition, the amino acid incorporation required both ribosomes and 105,000 x g supernatant solution. The correlation between the amount of incorporation and the amount of added RNA suggested stoichiometric rather than catalytic activity of the template RNA. The template RNA-dependent amino acid incorporation also required ATP and an ATP-generating system, was stimulated by a complete mixture of L-amino acids, and was markedly inhibited by puromycin, chloramphenicol, and RNAase. Addition of a synthetic polynucleotide, polyuridylic acid, specifically resulted in the incorporation of L-phenylalanine into a protein resembling poly-L-phenylalanine. Polyuridylic acid appears to function as a synthetic template or messenger RNA. The implications of these findings are briefly discussed.
historical_context: In 1961, Marshall Nirenberg and J. Heinrich Matthaei published the groundbreaking results of their legendary "Poly-U experiment." Utilizing a cell-free *E. coli* extract, they proved that introducing synthetic poly-uracil (Poly-U) RNA successfully directed the synthesis of a polypeptide chain constructed entirely of the amino acid phenylalanine. This was the definitive breakthrough that cracked the genetic code, conclusively proving that the RNA sequence UUU explicitly codes for phenylalanine. This monumental paper led directly to Marshall Nirenberg being awarded the 1968 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine (shared with Har Gobind Khorana and Robert Holley). The Nobel committee honored them for their brilliant interpretation of the genetic code and its exact function in protein synthesis, a monumental discovery that decoded the molecular dictionary of life and serves as the absolute bedrock of modern biology.

title: The Dependence of Cell-Free Protein Synthesis in E coli upon Naturally Occurring or Synthetic Polyribonucleotides
text: A stable cell-free system from E. coli has been used to examine the role of added polyribonucleotides in amino acid incorporation into protein. Incorporation stimulated by ribosomal RNA required washed ribosomes, the 105,000 x g supernatant fraction, ATP, and an ATP-generating system, and was inhibited by RNAase, puromycin, and chloramphenicol, but not by DNAase. Soluble RNA did not replace the active RNA fraction, and fractionation experiments indicated that the activity was associated with high-molecular-weight RNA rather than soluble RNA or intact ribosomes. RNA from several sources, including yeast ribosomal RNA and tobacco mosaic virus RNA, was active. Polyuridylic acid caused a marked and specific incorporation of C14-phenylalanine into an acid-insoluble product, whereas other polynucleotides or uridylic acid derivatives were inactive. These results indicate that template RNA is required for cell-free protein synthesis and that polyuridylic acid functions as a synthetic messenger specifying phenylalanine incorporation.

title: The Dependence of Cell-Free Protein Synthesis in E coli upon Naturally Occurring or Synthetic Polyribonucleotides
text: We have developed a highly active and stable cell-free system from Escherichia coli capable of incorporating amino acids into protein at a rapid, energy-dependent rate. A novel and critical characteristic of this system is its absolute requirement for template RNA to direct amino acid incorporation, even when soluble RNA and ribosomes are abundant. While the addition of naturally occurring ribosomal or viral RNA robustly stimulates protein synthesis, we demonstrate that a synthetic polynucleotide can also serve as a functional template. Specifically, the addition of polyuridylic acid directs the highly specific incorporation of L-phenylalanine, resulting in the synthesis of a polypeptide resembling poly-L-phenylalanine. This process exhibits all the hallmarks of de novo protein synthesis and is completely dependent on both ribosomes and soluble factors, while being inhibited by ribonuclease, puromycin, and chloramphenicol. Our findings suggest that polyuridylic acid contains the precise genetic code for phenylalanine, providing a powerful experimental framework for deciphering the universal genetic code.

title: The Dependence of Cell-Free Protein Synthesis in E coli upon Naturally Occurring or Synthetic Polyribonucleotides
text: A stable cell-free system has been obtained from E. coli which incorporates C14-valine into protein at a rapid rate. It was shown that this apparent protein synthesis was energy-dependent, was stimulated by a mixture of L-amino acids, and was markedly inhibited by RNAase, puromycin, and chloramphenicol. The present communication describes a novel characteristic of the system, that is, a requirement for template RNA, needed for amino acid incorporation even in the presence of soluble RNA and ribosomes. It will also be shown that the amino acid incorporation stimulated by the addition of template RNA has many properties expected of de novo protein synthesis. Naturally occurring RNA as well as a synthetic polynucleotide were active in this system. The synthetic polynucleotide appears to contain the code for the synthesis of a "protein" containing only one amino acid. Part of these data have been presented in preliminary reports.

title: The Dependence of Cell-Free Protein Synthesis in E coli upon Naturally Occurring or Synthetic Polyribonucleotides
text: Previous studies have shown that cell-free preparations obtained from bacteria are capable of incorporating amino acids into protein under appropriate conditions. The nature of the component which determines the specificity and extent of this incorporation, however, has remained uncertain. Since DNA is removed or rendered ineffective in many such extracts, attention has been directed toward ribonucleic acid fractions which may carry genetic information from DNA to the protein-synthesizing system. It was therefore of interest to determine whether purified RNA preparations could restore amino acid incorporation in an E. coli extract depleted of endogenous activity, and whether the effect was due to soluble RNA, ribosomal RNA, or another RNA species. In addition, synthetic polyribonucleotides provide a means of testing whether a defined nucleotide sequence can direct the incorporation of a particular amino acid. The experiments described below examine these questions in a stable cell-free system.

title: The Dependence of Cell-Free Protein Synthesis in E coli upon Naturally Occurring or Synthetic Polyribonucleotides
text: The biochemical mechanism by which genetic information is translated into the specific amino acid sequences of proteins remains one of the central problems in biology. While it is generally accepted that deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA) harbors the genetic code, intermediate templates consisting of ribonucleic acid (RNA) are thought to direct the actual assembly of amino acids at the ribosomal site of protein synthesis. Previous studies have indicated a requirement for RNA in cell-free amino acid incorporation, yet the direct programming of specific protein sequences by defined RNA templates has not been conclusively demonstrated. In this communication, we describe a highly stable, cell-free system derived from Escherichia coli that exhibits a rigorous dependence on the addition of template RNA for the incorporation of amino acids into protein. We report that the addition of a synthetic polyribonucleotide, polyuridylic acid, specifically directs the synthesis of polyphenylalanine.

title: The Dependence of Cell-Free Protein Synthesis in E coli upon Naturally Occurring or Synthetic Polyribonucleotides
text:
In this investigation, we have demonstrated that template RNA is a requirement for cell-free amino acid incorporation. Addition of soluble RNA could not replace template RNA in this system. In addition, the density-gradient centrifugation experiments showed that the active fractions in the ribosomal RNA preparations sedimented much faster than soluble RNA. It should be noted that ribosomal RNA is qualitatively different from soluble RNA, since bases such as pseudouracil, methylated guanines, etc., found in soluble RNA, are not present in ribosomal RNA.

The bulk of the RNA in our ribosomal RNA fractions may be inactive as templates, for tobacco mosaic virus RNA was 20 times as active in stimulating amino acid incorporation as equivalent amounts of E. coli ribosomal RNA. In addition, preliminary fractionation of ribosomal RNA indicated that only a portion of the total RNA was active.

It should be emphasized that ribosomal RNA could not substitute for ribosomes, indicating that ribosomes were not assembled from the added RNA in toto. The function of ribosomal RNA remains an enigma, although at least part of the total RNA is thought to serve as templates for protein synthesis and has been termed "messenger" RNA. Alternatively, a part of the RNA may be essential for the synthesis of active ribosomes from smaller ribosomal particles.

Ribosomal RNA may be an aggregate of subunits which can dissociate after proper treatment. Phenol extraction of E. coli ribosomes yields two types of RNA molecules with S values of 23 and 16, equivalent to molecular weights of 1,000,000 and 560,000, respectively. These RNA species can be degraded by boiling to products having sedimentation coefficients of 13.1, 8.8, and 4.4. Although the sedimentation distributions of the latter preparations suggest a high degree of homogeneity among the molecules of each class, these observations do not eliminate the possibility that the subunits are linked to one another via covalent bonds. Preliminary evidence indicates that the subunits may be active in our system, since the supernatant solution obtained after boiling E. coli ribosomal RNA for 10 min and centrifugation was active. Examination of boiled ribosomal RNA showed a dispersed peak with a sedimentation coefficient of 4-8. This may be the same material found in the sucrose density-gradient experiment where a small peak of activity somewhat heavier than soluble RNA was usually noted.

In our system, at low concentrations of ribosomal RNA, amino acid incorporation into protein was proportional to the amount of ribosomal RNA added, suggesting a stoichiometric rather than a catalytic action of ribosomal RNA. In contrast, soluble RNA has been shown to act in a catalytic fashion. The results indicate that polyuridylic acid contains the information for the synthesis of a protein having many of the characteristics of poly-L-phenylalanine. This synthesis was very similar to the cell-free protein synthesis obtained when naturally-occurring template RNA was added, i.e., both ribosomes and 100,000 x g supernatant solutions were required, and the incorporation was inhibited by puromycin or chloramphenicol. One or more uridylic acid residues therefore appear to be the code for phenylalanine. Whether the code is of the singlet, triplet, etc., type has not yet been determined. Polyuridylic acid seemingly functions as a synthetic template or messenger RNA, and this stable, cell-free E. coli system may well synthesize any protein corresponding to meaningful information contained in added RNA.

The ratio between uridylic acid units of the polymer required and molecules of L-phenylalanine incorporated, in recent experiments, has approached the value of 1:1. Direct evidence for the number of uridylic acid residues forming the code for phenylalanine as well as for the eventual stoichiometric action of the template is not yet established. As polyuridylic acid codes the incorporation of L-phenylalanine, polycytidylic acid specifically mediates the incorporation of L-proline into a TCA-precipitable product.
historical_context: In 1961, Marshall Nirenberg and J. Heinrich Matthaei published the groundbreaking results of their legendary "Poly-U experiment." Utilizing a cell-free *E. coli* extract, they proved that introducing synthetic poly-uracil (Poly-U) RNA successfully directed the synthesis of a polypeptide chain constructed entirely of the amino acid phenylalanine. This was the definitive breakthrough that cracked the genetic code, conclusively proving that the RNA sequence UUU explicitly codes for phenylalanine. This monumental paper led directly to Marshall Nirenberg being awarded the 1968 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine (shared with Har Gobind Khorana and Robert Holley). The Nobel committee honored them for their brilliant interpretation of the genetic code and its exact function in protein synthesis, a monumental discovery that decoded the molecular dictionary of life and serves as the absolute bedrock of modern biology.

title: The Dependence of Cell-Free Protein Synthesis in E coli upon Naturally Occurring or Synthetic Polyribonucleotides
text:
The experiments described above indicate that a heat-stable RNA fraction is required for extensive amino acid incorporation in the cell-free system from E. coli. This requirement is not satisfied by soluble RNA, although soluble RNA is also needed for maximal activity. The active RNA fraction, therefore, appears to have a function different from that of amino acid acceptor RNA. Several observations suggest that this RNA acts as a template for protein synthesis. First, incorporation is proportional to the amount of added RNA at low concentrations, as would be expected if RNA molecules specify the formation of a limited amount of product. Secondly, the activity is destroyed by RNAase or alkali but not by trypsin, DNAase, or boiling. Thirdly, the reaction requires ribosomes, the 105,000 x g supernatant fraction, ATP and an ATP-generating system, and is inhibited by chloramphenicol and puromycin. These properties are those expected of protein synthesis rather than of nonspecific adsorption or exchange reactions.

The term ribosomal RNA has been used in this paper to designate the source of the RNA preparations. It should not be inferred from this terminology that the active material is necessarily identical with the structural RNA of ribosomes. The activity did not sediment with ribosomes, and preliminary fractionation showed that amino acid incorporation activity did not correspond simply with optical density at 260 mμ. It is possible that the active component is a minor RNA species extracted along with ribosomal RNA, or that a portion of the ribosomal RNA can acquire template activity after extraction. The greater activity of yeast ribosomal RNA and tobacco mosaic virus RNA also indicates that the E. coli system can respond to RNA molecules from other sources. These results are consistent with the view that the specificity of the product is determined principally by the added RNA rather than by the bacterial extract itself.

The experiments with polyuridylic acid provide the most direct evidence for this interpretation. Addition of this synthetic polynucleotide caused a marked incorporation of phenylalanine into a product which, after purification and hydrolysis, yielded radioactive phenylalanine. The reaction required ribosomes and the soluble enzyme fraction, was energy-dependent, and was inhibited by RNAase, puromycin, and chloramphenicol. Thus the incorporation stimulated by polyuridylic acid occurs through the same general mechanism as the incorporation stimulated by naturally occurring RNA preparations. The failure of UMP, UDP, UTP, polyadenylic acid, and mixed adenylic-uridylic acid polymers to replace polyuridylic acid shows that neither uracil nucleotides nor polyanionic character alone are sufficient for activity. The absence of activity in mixtures of polyuridylic and polyadenylic acids suggests further that the active polymer must be available in a single-stranded form.

Since omission of the other nineteen amino acids did not prevent phenylalanine incorporation, and since polyuridylic acid failed to stimulate the incorporation of the other amino acids examined, the simplest conclusion is that polyuridylic acid contains information which directs the incorporation of phenylalanine. The product has not yet been characterized completely, but its solubility behavior and the recovery of radioactive phenylalanine after acid hydrolysis are compatible with the formation of a polyphenylalanine-like material. These findings suggest that a sequence composed only of uridylic acid residues may specify a sequence composed only of phenylalanine residues.

The present data do not establish the length of the coding unit or the detailed mechanism by which the RNA template is read. They do, however, show that a defined synthetic polyribonucleotide can determine the amino acid incorporated in a cell-free protein-synthesizing system. This provides an experimental approach to the problem of the genetic code. By preparing polymers of known or partially known nucleotide composition and examining the amino acids incorporated in response to them, it should be possible to obtain direct information concerning the relationship between nucleotide sequence and amino acid sequence. The system described here therefore may be useful not only for the study of protein synthesis, but also for analysis of the chemical basis of genetic specificity.

paper_title: The Dependence of Cell-Free Protein Synthesis in E coli upon Naturally Occurring or Synthetic Polyribonucleotides - 1961 - PNAS

title: The Dependence of Cell-Free Protein Synthesis in E coli upon Naturally Occurring or Synthetic Polyribonucleotides
text:
The experiments presented in this study establish a robust, cell-free system from Escherichia coli that is capable of incorporating amino acids into protein at a rapid rate. The critical finding is that this incorporation is entirely dependent upon the addition of specific, high-molecular-weight RNA template fractions. This system provides the first direct biochemical evidence that RNA acts as the intermediate template, or "messenger," directing the sequence-specific synthesis of proteins.

Our results demonstrate that the amino acid incorporation observed in this cell-free system possesses all the requisite characteristics of de novo protein synthesis. The process requires the simultaneous presence of both ribosomes and a 105,000 x g supernatant fraction, relies on a continuous supply of ATP, and is stimulated by a complete complement of L-amino acids. Furthermore, the reaction is potently inhibited by known antagonists of protein synthesis, including chloramphenicol and puromycin, as well as by ribonuclease, confirming that intact RNA is essential for the synthetic process. The observation that DNAase does not inhibit the RNA-stimulated incorporation—whereas it abolishes incorporation in cruder extracts—proves that the added RNA functions downstream of DNA.

We have shown that ribosomal RNA preparations from both E. coli and yeast, as well as viral RNA from tobacco mosaic virus, can robustly stimulate amino acid incorporation. The stoichiometric, rather than catalytic, relationship between the amount of added RNA and the amount of protein synthesized strongly supports the concept that these RNA molecules are serving as direct physical templates for polypeptide assembly. Most significantly, however, we discovered that a completely synthetic polynucleotide, polyuridylic acid, can direct this system with absolute specificity. The addition of polyuridylic acid to the cell-free extract resulted in the exclusive and massive incorporation of L-phenylalanine, yielding a product that mimics poly-L-phenylalanine. This extraordinary finding demonstrates that the specific nucleotide sequence of the RNA template dictates the specific amino acid sequence of the resulting protein. The complete inactivity of mixed poly(A,U) polymers or double-stranded structures further underscores the requirement for a specific, single-stranded nucleotide sequence to convey this information.

The implication that polyuridylic acid functions as a synthetic messenger RNA, directing the synthesis of a polypeptide containing only a single amino acid, represents a critical breakthrough in our understanding of the genetic code. It provides direct experimental proof that a sequence composed entirely of uridylic acid residues contains the specific coding dictionary for the amino acid phenylalanine. This discovery not only confirms the messenger RNA hypothesis but also provides a universal biochemical assay for deciphering the entire genetic code. By utilizing synthetic polynucleotides of varying, defined compositions within this stable, cell-free system, it should now be possible to systematically determine the specific nucleotide sequences—the codons—that correspond to each of the twenty naturally occurring amino acids.

In conclusion, we have developed a stable, highly active cell-free system whose protein-synthetic activity is strictly governed by the addition of template RNA. The successful programming of this system with a synthetic polynucleotide to produce a defined polypeptide unequivocally establishes the flow of genetic information from RNA to protein. This methodology opens the door to the complete elucidation of the genetic code and provides a powerful new tool for investigating the fundamental mechanisms of translation and its regulation.

paper_title: Transposition of Native Chromatin for Fast and Sensitive Epigenomic Profiling of Open Chromatin, DNA-Binding Proteins, and Nucleosome Position - 2013 - Nature Methods
display_title: Transposition of Native Chromatin for Fast and Sensitive Epigenomic Profiling of Open Chromatin, DNA-Binding Proteins, and Nucleosome Position
title: Transposition of Native Chromatin for Fast and Sensitive Epigenomic Profiling of Open Chromatin, DNA-Binding Proteins, and Nucleosome Position
text: We describe an assay for transposase-accessible chromatin using sequencing (ATAC-seq), based on direct in vitro transposition of sequencing adaptors into native chromatin, as a rapid and sensitive method for integrative epigenomic analysis. ATAC-seq captures open chromatin sites using a simple two-step protocol with 500-50,000 cells and reveals the interplay between genomic locations of open chromatin, DNA-binding proteins, individual nucleosomes and chromatin compaction at nucleotide resolution. We discovered classes of DNA-binding factors that strictly avoided, could tolerate or tended to overlap with nucleosomes. Using ATAC-seq maps of human CD4+ T cells from a proband obtained on consecutive days, we demonstrated the feasibility of analyzing an individual's epigenome on a timescale compatible with clinical decision-making.
historical_context: In 2013, Jason Buenrostro, Paul Giresi, Howard Chang, William Greenleaf, and colleagues unveiled ATAC-seq (Assay for Transposase-Accessible Chromatin using sequencing). This highly elegant technique hijacked a hyperactive Tn5 transposase to simultaneously cut and tag open, physically accessible regions of chromatin with necessary sequencing adaptors. ATAC-seq successfully democratized epigenomics. Prior mapping methods like DNase-seq were notoriously difficult, mandated massive quantities of cells, and were highly sensitive to slight protocol deviations. ATAC-seq, by contrast, was incredibly fast, exquisitely sensitive, and required exceptionally few cells, making the routine profiling of the open chromatin landscape—the physical regulatory "switches" of the genome—accessible to practically any laboratory. It is now an essential, ubiquitous tool for mapping gene regulation in human health, development, and disease, particularly thriving at the single-cell level.

text: Eukaryotic gene regulation is encoded in chromatin accessibility, nucleosome organization and transcription factor occupancy, yet existing genome-wide assays require large cell numbers and separate, time-consuming protocols. Here we describe assay for transposase-accessible chromatin using sequencing (ATAC-seq), a rapid method in which adaptor-loaded Tn5 transposase preferentially inserts into accessible native chromatin. Local depletion of insertions over transcription factor motifs further provides footprints that infer factor occupancy genome wide. Applied to primary human blood cells, ATAC-seq generated personalized regulatory information on clinical timescales, enabling integrated analysis of chromatin state, nucleosome positioning and regulatory networks from limited samples.

title: Transposition of Native Chromatin for Fast and Sensitive Epigenomic Profiling of Open Chromatin, DNA-Binding Proteins, and Nucleosome Position
text:
Eukaryotic genomes are hierarchically packaged into chromatin , and the nature of this packaging plays a central role in gene regulation . Major insights into the epigenetic information encoded within the nucleoprotein structure of chromatin have come from high-throughput, genome-wide methods for separately assaying chromatin accessibility ('open chromatin') 4,5 , nucleosome positioning and transcription factor (TF) occupancy . Though they are powerful, published protocols for existing methods require millions of cells as starting material, involve complex and timeconsuming sample preparations, and cannot probe the interplay of nucleosome positioning, chromatin accessibility and TF binding simultaneously. These limitations give rise to three shortcomings. First, current methods can average over and 'drown out' heterogeneity in cellular populations. Second, cells must often be grown ex vivo to obtain sufficient material, and such conditions perturb the in vivo context and modulate the epigenetic state in unknown ways. Third, input requirements often prevent application of these assays to well-defined clinical samples, thereby precluding generation of 'personal epigenomes' on diagnostic timescales.

Here we report a robust and sensitive method for epigenomic profiling that can provide a multidimensional portrait of gene regulation. We used ATAC-seq to identify regions of open chromatin, identify nucleosome-bound and nucleosome-free positions in regulatory regions, and infer the positions of DNA-binding proteins using 'footprinting' in a B-cell line. We demonstrated that this method is compatible with clinical timescales and standard blood draws by observing the open-chromatin landscape of a healthy volunteer.
historical_context: In 2013, Jason Buenrostro, Paul Giresi, Howard Chang, William Greenleaf, and colleagues unveiled ATAC-seq (Assay for Transposase-Accessible Chromatin using sequencing). This highly elegant technique hijacked a hyperactive Tn5 transposase to simultaneously cut and tag open, physically accessible regions of chromatin with necessary sequencing adaptors. ATAC-seq successfully democratized epigenomics. Prior mapping methods like DNase-seq were notoriously difficult, mandated massive quantities of cells, and were highly sensitive to slight protocol deviations. ATAC-seq, by contrast, was incredibly fast, exquisitely sensitive, and required exceptionally few cells, making the routine profiling of the open chromatin landscape—the physical regulatory "switches" of the genome—accessible to practically any laboratory. It is now an essential, ubiquitous tool for mapping gene regulation in human health, development, and disease, particularly thriving at the single-cell level.

text:
The packaging of eukaryotic genomes into chromatin provides a central layer of gene regulation by modulating the access of transcription factors, polymerases and chromatin regulators to DNA. Regulatory elements such as promoters, enhancers and insulators are typically marked by increased chromatin accessibility, characteristic nucleosome organization and occupancy by sequence-specific DNA-binding proteins. Genome-wide methods that measure these properties, including DNase-seq, FAIRE-seq, MNase-seq and ChIP-seq, have transformed the study of gene regulation. However, each method generally interrogates only one feature of chromatin architecture, and many require millions of cells, long multistep protocols or biochemical manipulations that can limit their application to scarce primary samples.

We sought to develop a rapid and sensitive assay that could extract multiple layers of epigenomic information directly from native chromatin. Hyperactive Tn5 transposase can fragment DNA and simultaneously insert sequencing adaptors in vitro, producing libraries suitable for high-throughput sequencing. We reasoned that, in intact nuclei, the probability of transposase insertion would be shaped by chromatin accessibility: open regulatory regions should be readily tagmented, whereas nucleosome-bound or compacted chromatin should be relatively protected. In addition, the positions of insertions and the sizes of resulting paired-end fragments might encode information about DNA-binding factor occupancy and nucleosome organization.

Here we describe an assay for transposase-accessible chromatin using sequencing (ATAC-seq), a simple two-step method for epigenomic profiling from as few as 500 cells. We show that ATAC-seq identifies open chromatin, resolves nucleosome positions, reveals transcription-factor footprints and enables personalized regulatory analysis on clinically relevant timescales.

ATAC-seq successfully democratized epigenomics. Prior mapping methods like DNase-seq were notoriously difficult, mandated massive quantities of cells, and were highly sensitive to slight protocol deviations. ATAC-seq, by contrast, was incredibly fast, exquisitely sensitive, and required exceptionally few cells, making the routine profiling of the open chromatin landscape—the physical regulatory "switches" of the genome—accessible to practically any laboratory. It is now an essential, ubiquitous tool for mapping gene regulation in human health, development, and disease, particularly thriving at the single-cell level.

title: Transposition of Native Chromatin for Fast and Sensitive Epigenomic Profiling of Open Chromatin, DNA-Binding Proteins, and Nucleosome Position
text:
The spatial organization and dynamic state of chromatin are fundamental determinants of gene regulation, dictating the accessibility of the genome to the transcriptional machinery. Open chromatin regions, largely depleted of nucleosomes, act as regulatory hubs that bind transcription factors (TFs) and other regulatory proteins to orchestrate cellular programs. Consequently, mapping the landscape of accessible chromatin is essential for understanding the epigenetic control of cellular identity, development, and disease.

Existing methods for assaying chromatin accessibility, such as DNase I hypersensitive site sequencing (DNase-seq) and formaldehyde-assisted isolation of regulatory elements with sequencing (FAIRE-seq), have provided valuable genome-wide catalogs of regulatory elements. However, these techniques often require tens of millions of cells and involve complex, multi-step protocols with significant material loss, precluding their application to rare primary cell types or clinical samples. Furthermore, these methods generally assay single features of chromatin structure, requiring parallel techniques like MNase-seq or ChIP-seq to capture nucleosome positioning or specific TF binding.

To address these limitations, we have developed the Assay for Transposase-Accessible Chromatin using sequencing (ATAC-seq). This method relies on the direct in vitro transposition of sequencing adaptors into accessible chromatin regions using a hyperactive Tn5 transposase. ATAC-seq is a rapid, two-step protocol that can profile open chromatin, infer TF occupancy via footprinting, and map nucleosome positions simultaneously, requiring only 500 to 50,000 cells. Here, we validate ATAC-seq against established epigenomic methods and demonstrate its utility in defining patient-specific regulatory networks on a clinical timescale.

ATAC-seq successfully democratized epigenomics. Prior mapping methods like DNase-seq were notoriously difficult, mandated massive quantities of cells, and were highly sensitive to slight protocol deviations. ATAC-seq, by contrast, was incredibly fast, exquisitely sensitive, and required exceptionally few cells, making the routine profiling of the open chromatin landscape—the physical regulatory "switches" of the genome—accessible to practically any laboratory. It is now an essential, ubiquitous tool for mapping gene regulation in human health, development, and disease, particularly thriving at the single-cell level.

text:
ATAC-seq allows simultaneous interrogation of factor occupancy, nucleosome positions in regulatory sites, and chromatin accessibility genome wide by considering the position of insertion and the distribution of insert lengths captured during the transposition reaction. Although extant methods such as DNase-seq and MNase-seq can also provide this information, they each require separate assays with large cell numbers. ATAC-seq also provides insert-size fingerprints of biologically relevant genomic regions, which suggests that it captures information on chromatin compaction. We expect ATAC-seq to have broad applicability, to considerably add to the genomics toolkit and to improve our understanding of gene regulation, particularly when integrated with other powerful rare-cell techniques such as FACS or laser-capture microdissection and recent advancements in RNA-seq .

One potentially exciting application of ATAC-seq is to generate personal epigenomic profiles on a timescale compatible with clinical decision-making. We envision that when ATAC-seq is coupled with ongoing improvements to sequencing and analysis turnaround times, this technique will offer the possibility of a day-long turnaround time for a personal epigenomic map. Deeper analyses to generate global regulatory networks and other inferences will take additional time, and we anticipate that bioinformatic analyses-not the molecular biology or sequencing-will become the bottleneck of epigenomic studies in the future. As ATAC-seq is compatible with FACS, it may enable studies on carefully sorted and rare subpopulations from primary tissues. We expect that ATAC-seq can also be applied to study select cellular subpopulations at different points during development and aging and during the progression of human diseases, including cancer, autoimmunity and neuropsychiatric disorders.

text:
Here we have shown that direct transposition of native chromatin provides a rapid and sensitive route to integrative epigenomic profiling. ATAC-seq combines the experimental simplicity of tagmentation with the biological selectivity imposed by chromatin structure, generating sequencing-ready libraries from orders of magnitude fewer cells than existing chromatin-accessibility assays. From a single experiment, the resulting fragments report accessible regulatory DNA, nucleosome-associated and nucleosome-free regions, local spacing between nucleosomes and DNA-binding factors, and protected footprints consistent with factor occupancy.

The information content of ATAC-seq derives in part from the paired-end fragment-size distribution. Short insertions identify accessible sites and candidate factor-bound elements, whereas longer, phased fragments disclose nucleosome organization within regulatory chromatin. These measurements allowed us to distinguish chromatin states, recover canonical promoter architecture, and identify classes of factors with distinct relationships to adjacent nucleosomes. Such patterns suggest that the spatial coordination between transcription factors, chromatin remodelers and nucleosomes can be surveyed genome wide without separate assays for each component.

Several limitations remain. Transposase sequence bias, local chromatin compaction and cellular heterogeneity may influence insertion probability, and footprinting-based inference of factor binding should be interpreted together with motif quality, conservation and, when available, ChIP-seq validation. Nucleosome calls from fragment length are also most informative within accessible regions and may not replace genome-wide MNase-based maps of all nucleosomes.

Nevertheless, the speed and low input requirements of ATAC-seq make it well suited for samples that have been difficult to profile, including purified primary cell populations and serial clinical specimens. The ability to obtain a personalized regulatory map on a timescale compatible with medical decision-making suggests that open chromatin and factor-occupancy profiling may become practical components of functional genomics and, ultimately, precision medicine.
historical_context: In 2013, Jason Buenrostro, Paul Giresi, Howard Chang, William Greenleaf, and colleagues unveiled ATAC-seq (Assay for Transposase-Accessible Chromatin using sequencing). This highly elegant technique hijacked a hyperactive Tn5 transposase to simultaneously cut and tag open, physically accessible regions of chromatin with necessary sequencing adaptors. ATAC-seq successfully democratized epigenomics. Prior mapping methods like DNase-seq were notoriously difficult, mandated massive quantities of cells, and were highly sensitive to slight protocol deviations. ATAC-seq, by contrast, was incredibly fast, exquisitely sensitive, and required exceptionally few cells, making the routine profiling of the open chromatin landscape—the physical regulatory "switches" of the genome—accessible to practically any laboratory. It is now an essential, ubiquitous tool for mapping gene regulation in human health, development, and disease, particularly thriving at the single-cell level.

title: Transposition of Native Chromatin for Fast and Sensitive Epigenomic Profiling of Open Chromatin, DNA-Binding Proteins, and Nucleosome Position
text:
The ATAC-seq method described here represents a significant methodological advance for the field of epigenomics. By leveraging the hyperactive Tn5 transposase to simultaneously fragment and tag accessible DNA, ATAC-seq collapses the multi-day, complex protocols of DNase-seq and FAIRE-seq into a rapid, two-step process. Crucially, we demonstrate that this streamlined approach does not sacrifice sensitivity or specificity; ATAC-seq generates chromatin accessibility maps highly concordant with established methods but requires several orders of magnitude less input material.

Beyond simple accessibility profiling, the precise insertion patterns of Tn5 allow for the simultaneous extraction of multidimensional epigenetic data from a single assay. The characteristic insert size distribution uniquely reveals local nucleosome positioning, phasing, and compaction states. Furthermore, deeper sequencing yields nucleotide-resolution footprints of DNA-binding proteins, enabling the inference of transcription factor occupancy genome-wide without the need for target-specific antibodies.

The dramatic reduction in input requirements—down to 500 cells—opens new avenues for interrogating rare cell populations, such as stem cells, transient developmental intermediates, and specific clinical biopsies, which were previously inaccessible to epigenomic profiling. As a proof of principle, we generated a personal, allele-specific epigenomic map from a standard blood draw within a clinically relevant timeframe, accurately identifying the transcription factor network driving IL-2 expression in a human proband. This capability to construct patient-specific regulatory networks rapidly and from primary tissues underscores the potential of ATAC-seq to transition epigenomic analysis from basic research to precision medicine and clinical diagnostics.

title: Viable Offspring Derived from Fetal and Adult Mammalian Cells
text: Fertilization of mammalian eggs is followed by successive cell divisions and progressive differentiation, first into the early embryo and subsequently into all of the cell types that make up the adult animal. Transfer of a single nucleus at a specific stage of development, to an enucleated unfertilized egg, provided an opportunity to investigate whether cellular differentiation to that stage involved irreversible genetic modification. The first offspring to develop from a differentiated cell were born after nuclear transfer from an embryo-derived cell line that had been induced to become quiescent. Using the same procedure, we now report the birth of live lambs from three new cell populations established from adult mammary gland, fetus and embryo. The fact that a lamb was derived from an adult cell confirms that differentiation of that cell did not involve the irreversible modification of genetic material required for development to term. The birth of lambs from differentiated fetal and adult cells also reinforces previous speculation that by inducing donor cells to become quiescent it will be possible to obtain normal development from a wide variety of differentiated cells.
historical_context: In 1997, Ian Wilmut, Keith Campbell, and their team at the Roslin Institute published the staggering announcement that they had successfully cloned a mammal directly from an adult somatic cell, resulting in the birth of Dolly the sheep. By elegantly fusing an enucleated sheep egg with an adult mammary gland cell and utilizing somatic cell nuclear transfer (SCNT), they conclusively proved that the nucleus of a fully differentiated adult cell still harbored the complete, reversible genetic capacity to generate an entire, living organism. This paper forever changed biology and public consciousness. It decisively dismantled long-standing biological dogma regarding the irreversible nature of cellular differentiation and sparked intense global debates, sweeping ethical regulations, and the explosive, rapid growth of the modern stem cell and regenerative medicine fields.

text: Nuclear transplantation has shown that amphibian nuclei from differentiated adult cells can direct development to larval stages, but it has remained uncertain whether nuclei from differentiated mammalian cells can be reprogrammed to support development to term. We have previously obtained live lambs after transfer of nuclei from cultured embryonic cells induced into quiescence, suggesting that donor-cell-cycle state may influence the capacity of oocyte cytoplasm to reprogramme gene expression. Here we report the production of viable offspring after nuclear transfer from three cultured cell populations derived from a day-9 embryo, a day-26 fetus and adult mammary gland. Donor cells were made quiescent by serum starvation before fusion with enucleated metaphase-II oocytes. Reconstructed embryos developed to morulae or blastocysts, were transferred to recipient ewes, and gave rise to live lambs representing all three donor-cell sources. Microsatellite analysis confirmed that the lambs were derived from the respective nuclear donor populations. One lamb born from an adult mammary-gland cell is, to our knowledge, the first mammal to develop from a cell derived from adult tissue. These results show that the mammalian genome can remain developmentally totipotent after extensive differentiation.

text: It has long been debated whether a fully differentiated adult somatic nucleus can be reprogrammed to support complete development to term. Previous successes in mammalian cloning have been restricted to the use of early embryonic cells as nuclear donors. Here, we demonstrate that live, normal offspring can be generated using nuclei derived from both fetal and adult mammalian tissues. We established cell populations from an ovine day-9 embryo, a day-26 fetus, and the mammary gland of a 6-year-old adult ewe. Crucially, prior to nuclear transfer, these donor cells were induced to exit the growth phase and arrest in the G0 phase of the cell cycle through serum starvation. When these quiescent nuclei were fused with enucleated oocytes, they successfully supported full development. We report the birth of eight live lambs, including one derived from the adult mammary gland cell, whose genetic identity was confirmed by microsatellite analysis. These results prove that the genetic information of a differentiated adult cell retains complete totipotency. This breakthrough offers unprecedented opportunities for animal breeding, transgenics, and the study of cellular reprogramming and aging.

text: It has long been known that in amphibians, nuclei transferred from adult keratinocytes established in culture support development to the juvenile, tadpole stage. Although this involves differentiation into complex tissues and organs, no development to the adult stage was reported, leaving open the question of whether a differentiated adult nucleus can be fully reprogrammed. Previously we reported the birth of live lambs after nuclear transfer from cultured embryonic cells that had been induced into quiescence. We suggested that inducing the donor cell to exit the growth phase causes changes in chromatin structure that facilitate reprogramming of gene expression and that development would be normal if nuclei are used from a variety of differentiated donor cells in similar regimes. Here we investigate whether normal development to term is possible when donor cells derived from fetal or adult tissue are induced to exit the growth cycle and enter the G0 phase of the cell cycle before nuclear transfer.

text: In mammals, differentiation is normally accompanied by progressive restrictions in developmental potential, but the extent to which these restrictions reflect irreversible changes in genetic information has remained uncertain. Nuclear transfer provides a direct test of this question, because the nucleus of a donor cell must be reprogrammed by the cytoplasm of an enucleated oocyte to direct development of an entire animal. Previous studies have shown development after transfer of nuclei from early embryonic blastomeres, and live offspring have recently been obtained from an embryo-derived cell line induced to become quiescent before transfer. These results suggested that compatibility between donor and recipient cell-cycle stage may be critical for reprogramming. It was not known, however, whether nuclei from fetal or adult somatic cells could support normal development to term. We have therefore derived cell populations from a day-9 embryo, a day-26 fetus and adult mammary gland, induced them to quiescence, and used them as nuclear donors to enucleated sheep oocytes.

text:
During mammalian development, the fertilized egg undergoes progressive restrictions in developmental potential, ultimately giving rise to the specialized cell types comprising the adult organism. A fundamental question in biology is whether cellular differentiation involves irreversible genetic modifications. Nuclear transfer—inserting a donor nucleus into an enucleated unfertilized egg—provides a functional assay to test the developmental potential of a differentiated nucleus. While normal development has been achieved using nuclei from early embryonic blastomeres, the successful reprogramming of nuclei from adult tissues has proven exceptionally difficult.

Previously, we reported the birth of live sheep following nuclear transfer from a cultured embryonic cell line, demonstrating that cultured cells can remain totipotent if induced into a quiescent state. Building upon this strategy, we now investigate whether nuclei from advanced developmental stages can support full development. Here, we report the successful birth of live lambs derived by nuclear transfer from three distinct cell populations: an embryo-derived cell line, fetal fibroblasts, and a cell line derived from the mammary gland of an adult ewe.

This paper forever changed biology and public consciousness.
