text:
Development of embryos produced by nuclear transfer depends upon the maintenance of normal ploidy and creating the conditions for developmental regulation of gene expression. These responses are both influenced by the cell-cycle stage of donor and recipient cells and the interaction between them. A comparison of development of mouse and cattle embryos produced by nuclear transfer to oocytes or enucleated zygotes suggests that a greater proportion develop if the recipient is an oocyte. This may be because factors that bring about reprogramming of gene expression in a transferred nucleus are required for early development and are taken up by the pronuclei during development of the zygote.

If the recipient cytoplasm is prepared by enucleation of an oocyte at metaphase II, it is only possible to avoid chromosomal damage and maintain normal ploidy by transfer of diploid nuclei, but further experiments are required to define the optimum cell-cycle stage. Our studies with cultured cells suggest that there is an advantage if cells are quiescent. In earlier studies, donor cells were embryonic blastomeres that had not been induced into quiescence. Comparisons of the phases of the growth cycle showed that development was greater if donor cells were in mitosis or in the G1 phase of the cycle, rather than in S or G2 phases. Increased development using donor cells in G0, G1 or mitosis may reflect greater access for reprogramming factors present in the oocyte cycoplasm, but a direct comparison of these phases in the same cell population is required for a clearer understanding of the underlying mechanisms.

Together these results indicate that nuclei from a wide range of cell types should prove to be totipotent after enhancing opportunities for reprogramming by using appropriate combinations of these cell-cycle stages. In turn, the dissemination of the genetic improvement obtained within elite selection herds will be enhanced by limited replication of animals with proven performance by nuclear transfer from cells derived from adult animals. In addition, gene targeting in livestock should now be feasible by nuclear transfer from modified cell populations and will offer new opportunities in biotechnology. The techniques described also offer an opportunity to study the possible persistence and impact of epigenetic changes, such as imprinting and telomere shortening, which are known to occur in somatic cells during development and senescence, respectively.

The lamb born after nuclear transfer from a mammary gland cell is, to our knowledge, the first mammal to develop from a cell derived from an adult tissue. The phenotype of the donor cell is unknown. The primary culture contains mainly mammary epithelial (over 90%) as well as other differentiated cell types, including myoepithelial cells and fibroblasts. We cannot exclude the possibility that there is a small proportion of relatively undifferentiated stem cells able to support regeneration of the mammary gland during pregnancy. Birth of the lamb shows that during the development of that mammary cell there was no irreversible modification of genetic information required for development to term. This is consistent with the generally accepted view that mammalian differentiation is almost all achieved by systematic, sequential changes in gene expression brought about by interactions between the nucleus and the changing cytoplasmic environment.

title: Viable Offspring Derived from Fetal and Adult Mammalian Cells
text:
The birth of lambs after nuclear transfer from cells derived from embryo, fetus and adult tissue shows that the procedure is not restricted to the particular embryo-derived cell line used previously. Most importantly, the production of a viable lamb from mammary gland cells taken from a 6-year-old ewe demonstrates that at least some nuclei from an adult mammalian somatic cell can be reprogrammed by the cytoplasm of an enucleated oocyte to direct development to term. This result provides direct evidence that cellular differentiation in that donor cell had not been accompanied by irreversible genetic changes incompatible with the formation of a whole animal.

The present results extend earlier observations in amphibians and mammals by showing complete development from a cultured adult cell nucleus in a large domestic species. The identity of the lambs was supported by coat and breed characteristics and by microsatellite analysis, excluding the possibility that they arose from inadvertent mating or from the oocyte donor genotype. Because the mitochondrial genome is inherited from the recipient oocyte, these animals are not genetically identical at all loci to the nuclear donor, but their nuclear genotype is that of the donor cell population. The findings therefore support the view that the nuclear genome of differentiated cells can retain the information required for all tissues and organs, provided that appropriate reprogramming of gene expression is achieved.

The use of quiescent donor cells is likely to have been important. Serum deprivation induced cells to leave the growth cycle and enter G0 before fusion with the enucleated oocyte. We have suggested that this state may facilitate the coordination of donor nucleus and recipient cytoplasm, either by avoiding inappropriate cell-cycle interactions or by producing chromatin conditions more amenable to reprogramming. The success obtained with three independent cell populations is consistent with this interpretation, although it does not prove that G0 is uniquely required. Other differentiated cells, and cells arrested by other procedures, will have to be tested to define the range of nuclei capable of supporting development and the conditions that optimize their reprogramming.

Despite the birth of live offspring, development was inefficient. A high proportion of fetuses detected by ultrasound was subsequently lost, and four dead fetuses from embryo-derived cells were recovered late in gestation. Some fetal loss may have resulted from embryo manipulation, culture, transfer, or incomplete reprogramming of the donor nucleus. The abnormal liver development seen in two fetuses indicates that nuclear transfer can be associated with developmental defects, even when early development and implantation occur. By contrast, perinatal loss among lambs born alive was not greatly different from that observed in commercial sheep, and the surviving lambs appeared morphologically normal at birth. Continued observation will be required to establish growth, reproductive performance and long-term health.

These observations have several implications. Nuclear transfer from adult cells offers a route to reproduce valuable genotypes and to propagate animals carrying defined genetic modifications introduced into cultured cells. It also provides an experimental system for studying the mechanisms by which oocyte cytoplasm remodels chromatin and resets gene expression. The present study does not imply that all adult cells are equivalent donors, nor that cloned animals will always be normal. It does, however, show that the differentiated state of an adult mammalian cell is not necessarily a permanent barrier to development. The ability of the oocyte to re-establish embryonic potential in such a nucleus will now allow the limits and mechanisms of mammalian nuclear reprogramming to be examined directly.

title: Viable Offspring Derived from Fetal and Adult Mammalian Cells
text:
The birth of live lambs following the transfer of nuclei from cultured fetal and adult cells fundamentally challenges the long-standing biological dogma that cellular differentiation represents an irreversible modification of the genome. While previous studies in amphibians demonstrated that nuclei from adult cells could direct development to juvenile stages, the results presented here provide the first unequivocal proof that the genome of a terminally differentiated adult mammalian cell can be fully reprogrammed to direct the complete development of a fertile adult animal.

Central to this success was the deliberate induction of the donor cells into a quiescent state (G0) prior to nuclear transfer. We propose that causing the donor cells to exit the active cell cycle facilitates necessary changes in chromatin structure, stripping away tissue-specific transcription factors and rendering the DNA more amenable to reprogramming by maternal factors present in the enucleated recipient oocyte. This synchronization likely prevents the premature initiation of DNA replication before the donor nucleus can be appropriately remodeled, thereby avoiding the chromosomal abnormalities and embryonic lethality that have plagued earlier cloning attempts.

The successful derivation of viable offspring from three distinct cell populations—embryonic disc, fetal fibroblasts, and, most notably, adult mammary epithelium—suggests that this reprogramming capability is not restricted to a specific cell lineage or developmental stage. Although the overall efficiency of the procedure remains low, and the rate of perinatal loss was somewhat elevated compared to natural mating, the very existence of these animals proves that the barrier to totipotency is not insurmountable. Microsatellite analysis unequivocally confirmed that the genotype of each cloned lamb matched its respective donor cell line, ruling out any possibility of adventitious mating or contamination.

The implications of this technology are vast and transformative. In the realm of agriculture, the ability to clone mammals from cultured somatic cells will allow for the rapid dissemination of elite genetic traits and the targeted propagation of animals with superior production characteristics. More importantly, when combined with emerging techniques for precise genetic manipulation in culture, somatic cell nuclear transfer provides a viable route for the generation of transgenic livestock. Such animals could be engineered to secrete high-value human therapeutic proteins in their milk, or designed with tissues modified to resist hyperacute rejection for xenotransplantation.

Furthermore, these findings open entirely new avenues in fundamental developmental biology. By demonstrating that the epigenetic state of an adult cell can be reset to totipotency, this work provides a paradigm for studying the mechanisms of cellular programming and reprogramming. Future research must focus on elucidating the specific oocyte-derived factors responsible for this dramatic epigenetic remodeling. Understanding these mechanisms could eventually lead to strategies for directly reprogramming human cells in vitro, offering unprecedented possibilities for the development of personalized, histocompatible cells and tissues for regenerative medicine. Ultimately, the realization that somatic differentiation is reversible will profoundly influence our approach to genetics, developmental biology, and biotechnology.
